topics.title: Simple Rankine Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Simple Rankine Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simple Rankine Cycle (SRC)** is the **idealized thermodynamic cycle** that describes the operation of a **vapor-compression power plant** (commonly known as a **steam power plant**), where the working fluid (water/steam) undergoes a closed loop of phase changes to convert thermal energy into mechanical work.

The cycle is the **theoretical benchmark** for all real-world steam power generation systems and forms the foundation of Module 1 in the PBMET604 syllabus. It consists of **four internally reversible processes** executed in a closed sequence:

1. **Isentropic Compression** (Liquid Pump)
2. **Isobaric Heat Addition** (Steam Boiler)
3. **Isentropic Expansion** (Steam Turbine)
4. **Isobaric Heat Rejection** (Condenser)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiling Kettle on Wheels" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Recirculating Kettle-Powered Cart**

Imagine a sealed pipe loop carrying water, fitted with:
- A **kettle (boiler)** that boils water using fire,
- A **waterwheel (turbine)** that spins when high-pressure steam rushes through it,
- A **cooling sponge (condenser)** that condenses the spent steam back to water, and
- A **hand pump (feed pump)** that pushes the cold water back into the kettle.

As the cart moves, the kettle boils water → steam rushes through the waterwheel, turning the wheels → the cooled water is pumped back into the kettle → the cycle repeats endlessly.

- The **kettle** = Boiler (heat input $Q_{in}$)
- The **waterwheel** = Turbine (produces shaft work $W_T$)
- The **cooling sponge** = Condenser (heat rejection $Q_{out}$)
- The **hand pump** = Feed Pump (consumes work $W_P$)

> **Key takeaway:** Rankine cycle converts heat from fuel into rotational shaft work by exploiting the **boiling and condensing** of water.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Industrial) |
|---|---|---|
| Specific heat of water (liquid) | $C_p$ | **4.18 kJ/kg·K** |
| Latent heat of vaporization (at 100 °C) | $h_{fg}$ | **2257 kJ/kg** |
| Density of saturated liquid | $\rho_{f}$ | **1000 kg/m³** (approx.) |
| Condenser pressure (typical) | $P_1$ | **0.1 – 0.2 bar** |
| Boiler pressure (typical) | $P_2$ | **100 – 250 bar** |
| Turbine inlet temperature | $T_3$ | **500 – 600 °C** |

---

## 1.4 Schematic Representation of the Physical Plant

The plant has **four primary components**, each performing one thermodynamic process.

| Component | Function | Process Performed |
|---|---|---|
| **Boiler** | Adds heat $Q_{in}$ to convert liquid → superheated steam | 2 → 3 (Isobaric heat addition) |
| **Turbine** | Expands steam to produce shaft work $W_T$ | 3 → 4 (Isentropic expansion) |
| **Condenser** | Rejects heat $Q_{out}$ to condense steam → saturated liquid | 4 → 1 (Isobaric heat rejection) |
| **Pump** | Compresses saturated liquid → compressed liquid | 1 → 2 (Isentropic compression) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Ideal Processes — Detailed Breakdown

### Process 1 → 2 : Isentropic Compression (Feed Pump)

- **Start:** Saturated liquid at condenser pressure $P_1$ (state 1)
- **End:** Compressed liquid at boiler pressure $P_2$ (state 2)
- **Thermodynamic character:** Reversible adiabatic → **constant entropy**
- **Why compress?** Liquid must be pressurized before it can re-enter the boiler at high pressure
- **Specific volume** is essentially that of saturated liquid $v_1 = v_f @ P_1$

### Process 2 → 3 : Isobaric Heat Addition (Boiler)

- **Start:** Compressed liquid at $P_2$ (state 2)
- **End:** Superheated steam at $P_2$ and $T_3$ (state 3)
- **Thermodynamic character:** Constant pressure heat addition from furnace gases
- **Three sub-stages inside the boiler:**
  1. Sensible heating of liquid (2 → a)
  2. Evaporation at constant temperature (a → b)
  3. Superheating of vapor (b → 3)

### Process 3 → 4 : Isentropic Expansion (Turbine)

- **Start:** Superheated steam at $P_2, T_3$ (state 3)
- **End:** Wet steam (or saturated) at condenser pressure $P_1$ (state 4)
- **Thermodynamic character:** Reversible adiabatic → **constant entropy**
- **Produces shaft work** delivered to the generator

### Process 4 → 1 : Isobaric Heat Rejection (Condenser)

- **Start:** Wet steam at $P_1$ (state 4)
- **End:** Saturated liquid at $P_1$ (state 1)
- **Thermodynamic character:** Constant pressure heat rejection to cooling water
- **Closes the thermodynamic loop**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the cycle called "Simple"?** Because it has **only four components and four processes** — no reheat, no regeneration, no superheating (in the simplest variant). Modifications like superheat, reheat, and regeneration are taught in later modules.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **master reference** for solving any Simple Rankine Cycle numerical problem in KTU exams. Memorize these relations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pump work (per kg) | $W_P = h_2 - h_1 = v_1 \cdot (P_2 - P_1)$ | kJ/kg | $v_1 \approx v_f @ P_1$ |
| 2 | Turbine work (per kg) | $W_T = h_3 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | From steam tables |
| 3 | Heat added in boiler | $Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2$ | kJ/kg | |
| 4 | Heat rejected in condenser | $Q_{out} = h_4 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | |
| 5 | Net work output | $W_{net} = W_T - W_P = (h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)$ | kJ/kg | |
| 6 | Thermal efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$ | dimensionless | Always < 1 |
| 7 | Specific Steam Consumption | $SSC = \dfrac{3600}{W_{net}}$ | kg/kWh | Lower is better |
| 8 | Work ratio | $WR = \dfrac{W_{net}}{W_T} = 1 - \dfrac{W_P}{W_T}$ | dimensionless | Indicates pump work penalty |
| 9 | Quality at turbine exit | $x_4 = \dfrac{s_3 - s_f}{s_{fg}} \bigg\vert_{P_1}$ | dimensionless | From entropy balance $s_3 = s_4$ |
| 10 | Carnot equivalent (between $T_3$ & $T_1$) | $\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_1}{T_3}$ | dimensionless | **Upper limit** comparison |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute Value Convention:** In markdown tables, the absolute value notation $\vert x \vert$ is used (never the pipe symbol inside cells, as it breaks table rendering). When writing inline math, write $\vert x \vert$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Generation:** Nearly **80% of global electricity** is generated via Rankine-cycle-based plants (coal, nuclear, biomass, solar thermal, geothermal).
- **Marine Propulsion:** Nuclear submarines and aircraft carriers use Rankine cycles with pressurized water reactors.
- **Cogeneration:** Industrial plants recover condenser waste heat for district heating.
- **Solar Thermal Power:** Parabolic troughs and solar towers use Rankine cycles with molten salt or synthetic oils.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Pump Work Using Incompressible Liquid Approximation

The energy balance for the pump (steady-flow, adiabatic, negligible KE/PE change) gives the **steady-flow energy equation**:

$$
h_1 + W_P = h_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad W_P = h_2 - h_1
$$

Since the pump handles liquid water (essentially incompressible), the **enthalpy change** of an incompressible fluid is approximated as:

$$
h_2 - h_1 \approx v_1 (P_2 - P_1)
$$

### Step-by-Step Expansion

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{From the definition of enthalpy:} \quad h &= u + Pv \\[4pt]
\text{For an incompressible liquid:} \quad dh &= v \, dP + P \, dv \\[4pt]
\text{Since } dv &\approx 0 \text{ (incompressibility assumption)} \\[4pt]
\text{Therefore:} \quad dh &\approx v \, dP \\[4pt]
\text{Integrating from state 1 to state 2:} \quad \int_{1}^{2} dh &= \int_{1}^{2} v \, dP \\[4pt]
h_2 - h_1 &= v_1 (P_2 - P_1) \quad \text{(taking } v \approx v_1 = v_f @ P_1) \\[4pt]
\boxed{W_P = v_1 (P_2 - P_1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** Since $v_1$ is very small (order of $10^{-3}$ m³/kg), the pump work is typically only **1–2%** of turbine work — but **must not be ignored** in precise KTU numericals.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Turbine Work

The turbine is also a steady-flow device. Energy balance (per kg of steam):

$$
h_3 = h_4 + W_T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{W_T = h_3 - h_4}
$$

The exit enthalpy $h_4$ is **fixed by the isentropic condition** $s_3 = s_4$. So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_3 &= s_4 \\[4pt]
s_3 &= s_f @ P_1 + x_4 \cdot s_{fg} @ P_1 \\[4pt]
x_4 &= \dfrac{s_3 - s_f}{s_{fg}} \bigg\vert_{P_1} \\[4pt]
h_4 &= h_f @ P_1 + x_4 \cdot h_{fg} @ P_1
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Thermal Efficiency of Simple Rankine Cycle

By the **First Law of Thermodynamics applied to the cyclic integral**:

$$
\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W = W_{net}
$$

Since only the boiler and condenser exchange heat:

$$
Q_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} = W_{net}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{th} &= \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{Q_{in} - Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{(h_3 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2} \\[6pt]
&= 1 - \dfrac{h_4 - h_1}{h_3 - h_2} \\[6pt]
\boxed{\eta_{th} &= \dfrac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion Logic Used:** Heat input occurs **only** across the boiler (process 2→3), and heat output **only** across the condenser (process 4→1). The pump and turbine are adiabatic (no heat transfer), so they do not appear in the numerator/denominator of heat terms.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Specific Steam Consumption (SSC)

By definition, SSC is the mass of steam required to generate **1 kWh** of net work:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{net} \, \text{[kJ/kg]} \times m \, \text{[kg]} &= 1 \, \text{kWh} = 3600 \, \text{kJ} \\[4pt]
m \, (\text{kg of steam per kWh}) &= \dfrac{3600}{W_{net}} \\[4pt]
\boxed{SSC = \dfrac{3600}{W_{net}} \,\, \text{kg/kWh}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Operating Parameters

| Parameter Change | Effect on $\eta_{th}$ | Effect on $W_{net}$ | Engineering Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Boiler pressure $P_2$ | **Increases** | Increases | Higher material cost, thicker walls |
| ↑ Turbine inlet temp $T_3$ | **Increases** | Increases | Creep limits of blade alloys |
| ↓ Condenser pressure $P_1$ | **Increases** | Increases | Below ~0.04 bar requires vacuum pumps |
| Use superheat at state 3 | **Increases** | Increases | Avoids excessive moisture at turbine exit |
| Higher $x_4$ (dryness) | Neutral | Decreases slightly | Lower exit losses |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favorite Numerical Trick:** Always compute $h_4$ from **steam tables using entropy**, since the turbine exit state is generally a **mixture** at condenser pressure. Assuming saturated vapor at state 4 will give a wrong (higher) efficiency and is a common student mistake.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Adapted Mermaid)

The following diagram captures the **functional flow** of mass, energy, and work through the four components of a Simple Rankine Cycle plant.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[FURNACE Q IN FUEL] -->|Heat Q in| B[BOILER P2 T3]
    B -->|Superheated Steam h3 s3| C[TURBINE W shaft]
    C -->|Wet Steam h4 s4 x4| D[CONDENSER Q out P1]
    D -->|Saturated Liquid h1 v1| E[FEED PUMP W pump]
    E -->|Compressed Liquid h2 P2| B
    C -->|Mechanical Output| F[GENERATOR ELECTRICITY]
    D -->|Rejected Heat| G[COOLING TOWER WATER]

    style A fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#ffa500,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#45b7d1,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style E fill:#f9ca24,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#26de81,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#a29bfe,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- Red node = heat source (furnace)
- Orange node = boiler (isobaric heat addition)
- Teal node = turbine (work output)
- Blue node = condenser (heat rejection)
- Yellow node = pump (work input)
- Green node = generator (electrical output)
- Purple node = cooling tower (ultimate heat sink)

---

## 4.2 State Point Description Table (T-S Diagram Reference)

The cycle on a **T-S (Temperature–Entropy)** plane has the following characteristic shape:

| State | Location | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **State 1** | On saturated liquid line at $P_1$ | Pump inlet (condenser exit) |
| **State 2** | Slightly right of state 1, higher $T$ | Pump exit (compressed liquid) |
| **State 3** | Inside superheated region, high $T$ | Turbine inlet (boiler exit) |
| **State 4** | Inside two-phase region, low $T$ | Turbine exit (condenser inlet) |

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph T_S_PLOT [T S DIAGRAM STRUCTURE]
        S1["STATE 1 SAT LIQUID P1 LOW T"]
        S2["STATE 2 COMP LIQUID P2 HIGHER T"]
        S3["STATE 3 SUPERHEAT P2 HIGH T"]
        S4["STATE 4 WET STEAM P1 LOW T"]
        S1 -->|1 to 2 PUMP ISENTROPIC| S2
        S2 -->|2 to 3 BOILER ISOBARIC| S3
        S3 -->|3 to 4 TURBINE ISENTROPIC| S4
        S4 -->|4 to 1 CONDENSER ISOBARIC| S1
    end

    style S1 fill:#a8e6cf,stroke:#000
    style S2 fill:#dcedc1,stroke:#000
    style S3 fill:#ffd3b6,stroke:#000
    style S4 fill:#ffaaa5,stroke:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why T-S is preferred over P-V for vapor cycles:** In the two-phase region, the T-S plot has a well-defined dome shape that clearly demarcates wet steam, saturated, and superheated regions. This makes it the **standard reference** in KTU exam answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. Define Simple Rankine Cycle and list its four ideal processes. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The Simple Rankine Cycle is the **idealized thermodynamic cycle** for a steam power plant in which the working fluid (water/steam) undergoes a closed loop of phase change to convert heat into work.

The four ideal processes are:

1. **Process 1 → 2:** Isentropic compression of saturated liquid in the feed pump.
2. **Process 2 → 3:** Isobaric heat addition in the boiler (compressed liquid → superheated steam).
3. **Process 3 → 4:** Isentropic expansion of steam in the turbine (work output).
4. **Process 4 → 1:** Isobaric heat rejection in the condenser (wet steam → saturated liquid).

> **Examiner's Insight:** Students who omit "isentropic" for processes 1→2 and 3→4 lose 1 mark. Always specify the type of each process.

---

### Q2. Why is pump work often neglected in approximate efficiency calculations of the Rankine cycle? **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The pump handles **incompressible liquid water**, whose specific volume $v_1$ is extremely small (~$0.001$ m³/kg). Hence:

$$
W_P = v_1 (P_2 - P_1) \approx \text{very small}
$$

Typical values are $W_P \approx 5\text{–}10$ kJ/kg, while $W_T \approx 600\text{–}1000$ kJ/kg. The pump work is only **1–2%** of the turbine work, so for quick estimates it is neglected. However, **for KTU precise calculations, pump work must always be included** unless the question explicitly states "neglect pump work."

> **Examiner's Insight:** Saying "it is small" without quantifying loses marks. Always quote the **~1–2% ratio**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Module-end questions carry **14 marks** with **internal choice** (i.e., either OR). Solve any one of the following two question sets.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

In a simple Rankine cycle, the steam enters the turbine at **3 MPa, 350 °C**, and the condenser operates at **10 kPa**. Determine:

- **(a)** Quality of steam at the turbine exit, turbine work, pump work, and heat added in the boiler. **[7 Marks]**
- **(b)** Thermal efficiency and specific steam consumption (SSC) of the cycle. **[7 Marks]**

#### Given Data

- Boiler exit (state 3): $P_3 = 3$ MPa = 3000 kPa; $T_3 = 350$ °C
- Condenser pressure (state 1, 4): $P_1 = 10$ kPa

#### Steam Table Properties

**State 1 — Saturated liquid at 10 kPa:**

$$
h_1 = h_f = 191.81 \, \text{kJ/kg}; \quad v_1 = v_f = 0.001010 \, \text{m}^3/\text{kg}; \quad s_1 = s_f = 0.6492 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}
$$

**State 3 — Superheated steam at 3 MPa, 350 °C:**

$$
h_3 = 2988.1 \, \text{kJ/kg}; \quad s_3 = 6.7427 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}
$$

**State 2 — Compressed liquid (after pump):**

Pump work:

$$
W_P = v_1 (P_2 - P_1) = 0.001010 \times (3000 - 10) = 0.001010 \times 2990 = 3.02 \, \text{kJ/kg}
$$

$$
h_2 = h_1 + W_P = 191.81 + 3.02 = 194.83 \, \text{kJ/kg}
$$

**State 4 — Wet steam at 10 kPa (after isentropic turbine):**

For $P_1 = 10$ kPa: $s_f = 0.6492$, $s_{fg} = 7.4996$ kJ/kg·K, $h_f = 191.81$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg} = 2392.1$ kJ/kg.

Isentropic condition: $s_4 = s_3 = 6.7427$ kJ/kg·K

$$
x_4 = \dfrac{s_4 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \dfrac{6.7427 - 0.6492}{7.4996} = \dfrac{6.0935}{7.4996} = 0.8125
$$

$$
h_4 = h_f + x_4 \cdot h_{fg} = 191.81 + 0.8125 \times 2392.1 = 191.81 + 1943.58 = 2135.39 \, \text{kJ/kg}
$$

#### Part (a) Solution Summary [7 Marks]

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Quality at turbine exit $x_4$ | **0.8125** |
| Turbine work $W_T = h_3 - h_4 = 2988.1 - 2135.39$ | **852.71 kJ/kg** |
| Pump work $W_P$ | **3.02 kJ/kg** |
| Heat added $Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 = 2988.1 - 194.83$ | **2793.27 kJ/kg** |

#### Part (b) Solution [7 Marks]

Net work:

$$
W_{net} = W_T - W_P = 852.71 - 3.02 = 849.69 \, \text{kJ/kg}
$$

Thermal efficiency:

$$
\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{849.69}{2793.27} = 0.3042 = \mathbf{30.42\%}
$$

Specific Steam Consumption:

$$
SSC = \dfrac{3600}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{3600}{849.69} = \mathbf{4.237 \, kg/kWh}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT use $h_4 = h_g$ at condenser pressure** — this is a wet steam state and must be computed using $x_4$. Marks deducted: 2–3.
> 2. **Always include pump work** — even though small, omitting it costs 1 mark in this problem.
> 3. **Unit conversion** — 3 MPa must be converted to kPa (3000 kPa) when using SI steam tables. Marks deducted: 1.
> 4. **Show all steam-table values cited** — KTU expects you to write the table lookup explicitly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

A steam power plant operates on a simple ideal Rankine cycle. Steam enters the turbine at **80 bar, 500 °C**, and the condenser pressure is **0.1 bar**. Calculate:

- **(a)** Pump work, turbine work, and heat rejected in the condenser. **[7 Marks]**
- **(b)** Thermal efficiency of the cycle and the mass flow rate of steam required for **100 MW** net power output. **[7 Marks]**

#### Given Data

- $P_3 = 80$ bar = 8000 kPa; $T_3 = 500$ °C
- $P_1 = 0.1$ bar = 10 kPa
- Net power $W_{cycle} = 100$ MW = 100,000 kW

#### Steam Table Properties

**State 1 — Saturated liquid at 0.1 bar:**

$$
h_1 = 191.81 \, \text{kJ/kg}; \quad v_1 = 0.001010 \, \text{m}^3/\text{kg}; \quad s_1 = 0.6492 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}
$$

**State 3 — Superheated steam at 80 bar, 500 °C:**

$$
h_3 = 3398.3 \, \text{kJ/kg}; \quad s_3 = 6.7240 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}
$$

**State 2 — After pump:**

$$
W_P = v_1 (P_2 - P_1) = 0.001010 \times (8000 - 10) = 8.07 \, \text{kJ/kg}
$$

$$
h_2 = 191.81 + 8.07 = 199.88 \, \text{kJ/kg}
$$

**State 4 — After isentropic turbine at 0.1 bar:**

For 0.1 bar: $s_f = 0.6492$, $s_{fg} = 7.4996$, $h_f = 191.81$, $h_{fg} = 2392.1$ kJ/kg·K.

$$
x_4 = \dfrac{s_3 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \dfrac{6.7240 - 0.6492}{7.4996} = \dfrac{6.0748}{7.4996} = 0.8100
$$

$$
h_4 = 191.81 + 0.8100 \times 2392.1 = 191.81 + 1937.60 = 2129.41 \, \text{kJ/kg}
$$

#### Part (a) Solution Summary [7 Marks]

| Quantity | Calculation | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Pump work $W_P$ | $v_1(P_2 - P_1)$ | **8.07 kJ/kg** |
| Turbine work $W_T$ | $h_3 - h_4 = 3398.3 - 2129.41$ | **1268.89 kJ/kg** |
| Heat rejected $Q_{out}$ | $h_4 - h_1 = 2129.41 - 191.81$ | **1937.60 kJ/kg** |

#### Part (b) Solution [7 Marks]

Net work:

$$
W_{net} = W_T - W_P = 1268.89 - 8.07 = 1260.82 \, \text{kJ/kg}
$$

Thermal efficiency:

$$
\eta_{th} = \dfrac{1260.82}{3398.3 - 199.88} = \dfrac{1260.82}{3198.42} = \mathbf{0.3942 = 39.42\%}
$$

Mass flow rate:

$$
\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{W}_{net}}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{100{,}000 \, \text{kW}}{1260.82 \, \text{kJ/kg}} = \dfrac{100{,}000 \, \text{kJ/s}}{1260.82 \, \text{kJ/kg}} = \mathbf{79.32 \, kg/s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion from bar to kPa** — KTU steam tables are in kPa. Marks deducted: 1 if missed.
> 2. **Power unit conversion** — 100 MW must be converted to kJ/s (not kJ/h) for mass flow calculation.
> 3. **Quoting steam-table values explicitly** — Examiners expect you to write $h_3 = 3398.3$ kJ/kg *with reference*. Marks deducted: 0.5–1 for missing citation.
> 4. **Proper use of $Q_{out}$** — Some students write $Q_{in} - Q_{out}$; ensure $Q_{out}$ is computed separately for clarity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Simple Rankine Cycle**

- ✅ The **four processes** are: Pump (isentropic), Boiler (isobaric heat addition), Turbine (isentropic), Condenser (isobaric heat rejection).
- ✅ Working fluid is **water/steam** undergoing **closed-loop phase change**.
- ✅ The cycle is **internally reversible** but **externally irreversible** in real plants (heat transfer across finite ΔT).
- ✅ **Pump work formula**: $W_P = v_1(P_2 - P_1)$ — always use SI units (kPa, m³/kg) for consistency.
- ✅ **Turbine exit state** is found using **isentropic relation**: $s_3 = s_4$, then compute $x_4$ at $P_1$.
- ✅ **Thermal efficiency**: $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2}$
- ✅ **SSC**: $SSC = \dfrac{3600}{W_{net}}$ kg/kWh (lower is better — high efficiency → low SSC).
- ✅ **Quality at turbine exit** $x_4$ should ideally be **≥ 0.88** to avoid blade erosion in real turbines.
- ✅ **Higher $P_2$, higher $T_3$, lower $P_1$** → higher efficiency (subject to metallurgical and economic limits).
- ✅ **Carnot efficiency** between $T_3$ and $T_1$ is the **theoretical upper limit**; real Rankine is always lower.
- ✅ **Steam-table values must always be cited** in KTU answers — failing to do so costs 0.5–1 mark per sub-question.
- ✅ **Units**: Always convert bar → kPa, °C → K (only for absolute temperature ratios like Carnot efficiency).
- ✅ **Pump work is small (~1–2%) but mandatory** in KTU numericals unless explicitly told to neglect it.
- ✅ The **T-S diagram** is preferred over P-V for vapor cycles — practice drawing it for full-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Improvements in Rankine Cycles - Reheat and Regenerative Cycles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Improvements in Rankine Cycles - Reheat and Regenerative Cycles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Improvements in the Rankine Cycle** are thermodynamic modifications applied to the basic vapor power cycle to enhance its **thermal efficiency** and overcome the practical limitations of the simple Rankine cycle — namely, excessive turbine wetness at the exit and the irreversibility associated with adding heat to a liquid at low temperature.

The two principal improvement techniques mandated in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus are:

1. **Reheat Cycle** — Reduces turbine blade erosion by extracting steam partway through the turbine, re-heating it in a boiler, and expanding it further.
2. **Regenerative Cycle** — Improves cycle efficiency by bleeding (extracting) a portion of partially expanded steam from the turbine to heat the compressed liquid (feedwater) before it enters the boiler.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Coffee Cup Reheat" and the "Lukewarm Tea Pre-Warming":**

- **Reheat Cycle Analogy:** Imagine blowing up a long balloon in **two stages** instead of one. If you blow it continuously, your breath cools down and the balloon becomes floppy near the end. But if you pause, **reheat the air in your lungs**, and then continue blowing, you finish the balloon with stronger, drier air. This is what reheat does to steam — it **prevents excessive moisture** at the turbine exhaust.

- **Regenerative Cycle Analogy:** Imagine making tea. Instead of pouring *cold* milk straight into a boiling pot (which cools the pot and requires more gas to reheat), you first warm the milk using the *steam from the boiling pot itself* through a side coil. The pot loses less heat, and the milk enters already warm. This is what regenerative feedwater heating does — it **pre-warms the boiler feedwater using bled steam** from the turbine.

### 1.3 Limitations of the Simple Rankine Cycle (Why Improvements Are Needed)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three critical limitations drive the need for improvements:**

| Limitation | Root Cause | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Low thermal efficiency | Large temperature difference during heat addition (mean temperature of heat addition is low) | Higher fuel consumption per kWh |
| Excessive moisture in low-pressure turbine | Isentropic expansion ends in wet region (x < 0.88) | Blade erosion, efficiency loss |
| High heat rejection in condenser | Heat is rejected over a large temperature range | Irreversibility and waste |

### 1.4 Standard Reference States & Constants

> **[!NOTE]**
> - Steam quality at turbine exit should ideally remain **x ≥ 0.88 (88 %)** to prevent blade erosion.
> - Typical steam conditions: Boiler pressure **10–25 MPa**, Temperature **500–600 °C**.
> - **Universal gas constant, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - **Specific heat of superheated steam, c_p ≈ 2.1 kJ/(kg·K)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Reheat Rankine Cycle

#### 2.1.1 Operating Principle

In a reheat cycle, steam is expanded in the **High-Pressure (HP) turbine** only partially (typically to about one-fourth of its pressure range), then sent back to the boiler where it is **reheated at constant pressure** to (or near) the original turbine inlet temperature. The reheated steam is then expanded in a **Low-Pressure (LP) turbine** down to the condenser pressure.

**Why reheat is done:**
- It raises the **mean temperature of heat addition**, improving thermal efficiency.
- It keeps the steam **dry or slightly superheated** at the LP turbine exhaust, avoiding blade erosion.
- Modern power plants commonly use **double reheat** (two reheat stages).

#### 2.1.2 State-Point Description (Reheat Cycle)

| State | Location | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Boiler exit / HP turbine inlet | Superheated steam (high P, high T) |
| 2 | HP turbine exit / Reheater inlet | Partially expanded (medium P, lower T) |
| 3 | Reheater exit / LP turbine inlet | Reheated to original T (medium P, high T) |
| 4 | LP turbine exit / Condenser inlet | Fully expanded (low P, low T) |
| 5 | Condenser exit / Pump inlet | Saturated liquid (low P) |
| 6 | Pump exit / Boiler inlet | Compressed liquid (high P) |

#### 2.1.3 Energy Analysis (Per kg of steam leaving the boiler)

**Turbine Work Output:**
$$W_T = (h_1 - h_2) + (h_3 - h_4)$$

**Pump Work Input:**
$$W_P = h_6 - h_5 = v_5 (P_6 - P_5)$$

**Net Work Output:**
$$W_{net} = W_T - W_P$$

**Heat Added in Boiler + Reheater:**
$$Q_{in} = (h_1 - h_6) + (h_3 - h_2)$$

**Thermal Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{th,reheat} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{(h_1 - h_2) + (h_3 - h_4) - (h_6 - h_5)}{(h_1 - h_6) + (h_3 - h_2)}$$

### 2.2 The Regenerative Rankine Cycle

#### 2.2.1 Operating Principle

A fraction **y** of the steam is **extracted (bled)** from an intermediate stage of the turbine and routed into a **feedwater heater (FWH)**. This bled steam condenses and transfers its latent heat to the incoming feedwater, raising the feedwater temperature **before** it enters the boiler.

**Why regeneration is done:**
- It raises the **mean temperature of heat addition** in the boiler.
- It reduces the amount of heat supplied in the boiler (because the feedwater enters at a higher temperature).
- It improves the cycle thermal efficiency by **reducing irreversible heat transfer at low temperatures**.

#### 2.2.2 Types of Feedwater Heaters

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Focus:** Both types are tested — know the difference.

| Type | Construction | Mixing? | Extraction Pump? | Drain Handling |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Open FWH (De-aerator)** | Direct contact chamber | Yes — steam mixes with feedwater | No (throttled) | Drains mix with feedwater |
| **Closed FWH** | Surface-type heat exchanger | No — separated by tubes | Yes (drain pump) | Drains cascaded to next lower heater or condenser |

#### 2.2.3 State-Point Description (Single Open FWH Regenerative Cycle)

| State | Location | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Boiler exit / Turbine inlet | Superheated steam |
| 2 | Turbine extraction point | Steam at extraction pressure (P_ext) |
| 3 | Turbine exhaust / Condenser inlet | Steam at condenser pressure (P_c) |
| 4 | Condenser exit | Saturated liquid at P_c |
| 5 | Pump-1 exit (condensate pump) | Compressed liquid at P_ext |
| 6 | FWH exit (saturated liquid at P_ext) | After mixing with bled steam |
| 7 | Pump-2 exit (boiler feed pump) | Compressed liquid at boiler pressure P_b |

#### 2.2.4 Energy Analysis — Single Open FWH

**Energy Balance on the Open FWH (per kg of steam entering turbine):**

Let **y** = fraction of steam extracted at state 2.

$$\text{Energy In} = \text{Energy Out}$$

$$(1 - y)h_5 + y \cdot h_2 = 1 \cdot h_6$$

Solving for **y**:

$$y = \frac{h_6 - h_5}{h_2 - h_5}$$

**Net Work Output:**
$$W_{net} = (1 - y)(h_1 - h_3) - (1 - y)(h_4 - h_5) - (h_7 - h_6)$$

**Heat Added in Boiler:**
$$Q_{in} = (1 - y)(h_1 - h_7)$$

**Thermal Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{th,regen} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table consolidates ALL the must-know formulas for the KTU board exam. Memorize this layout.**

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Turbine work (Reheat) | $W_T = (h_1 - h_2) + (h_3 - h_4)$ | kJ/kg |
| Net work (Reheat) | $W_{net} = W_T - W_P$ | kJ/kg |
| Total heat added (Reheat) | $Q_{in} = (h_1 - h_6) + (h_3 - h_2)$ | kJ/kg |
| Thermal efficiency (Reheat) | $\eta_{th} = W_{net} \div Q_{in}$ | dimensionless |
| Extraction fraction (Open FWH) | $y = (h_6 - h_5) \div (h_2 - h_5)$ | dimensionless |
| Turbine work (Regen) | $W_T = (1-y)(h_1 - h_2) + (1-y)(h_2 - h_3)$ | kJ/kg |
| Net work (Regen) | $W_{net} = (1-y)(h_1 - h_3) - W_{pumps}$ | kJ/kg |
| Heat added (Regen) | $Q_{in} = (1-y)(h_1 - h_7)$ | kJ/kg |
| Pump work (approx.) | $W_P = v \Delta P / 1000$ | kJ/kg |
| Steam quality at exit | $x_4 = (s_4 - s_f) \div s_{fg}$ | dimensionless |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where these concepts are used in industry:**
> - All modern **coal-fired, nuclear, and combined-cycle power plants** employ **reheat + regenerative** cycles together. For example, a typical 500 MW Indian thermal plant uses **3 reheat stages** and **6–8 regenerative feedwater heaters** to achieve net plant efficiencies of 38–42 %.
> - **Regenerative Rankine cycles** are also used in **solar thermal power plants** and **organic Rankine cycle (ORC) geothermal plants** to maximize extraction efficiency from low-grade heat sources.
> - The **mean temperature of heat addition** concept is critical in designing **refrigeration cycles** (Carnot refrigerator analogue) — same thermodynamic principle.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Net Work and Thermal Efficiency of the Ideal Reheat Cycle

We consider **one kg of steam leaving the boiler** as the basis. All work and heat terms are per kg of this steam. Note that the **same 1 kg** passes through HP turbine, reheater, and LP turbine (no steam is extracted in the pure reheat cycle).

#### Step 1: HP Turbine Work (1 → 2, isentropic expansion in HP turbine)

$$W_{HP} = h_1 - h_2 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$

**Reasoning:** For a steady-flow adiabatic turbine, the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) with negligible kinetic and potential energy changes gives $w = h_{in} - h_{out}$.

#### Step 2: Reheater Heat Addition (2 → 3, constant-pressure heating in reheater)

$$Q_{reheat} = h_3 - h_2 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$

**Reasoning:** The reheater is essentially a boiler operating at intermediate pressure. Energy balance gives $q = \Delta h$ at constant pressure (no work, negligible $\Delta ke$ and $\Delta pe$).

#### Step 3: LP Turbine Work (3 → 4, isentropic expansion in LP turbine)

$$W_{LP} = h_3 - h_4 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$

#### Step 4: Total Turbine Work

$$W_T = W_{HP} + W_{LP} = (h_1 - h_2) + (h_3 - h_4)$$

#### Step 5: Condenser Heat Rejection (4 → 5, constant-pressure condensation)

$$Q_{out} = h_4 - h_5 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$

#### Step 6: Pump Work (5 → 6, isentropic compression of liquid)

For an incompressible liquid approximation:

$$W_P = h_6 - h_5 = v_5(P_6 - P_5)$$

**Reasoning:** For a liquid, $dh = v \, dP + T \, ds$. For isentropic compression ($ds=0$), $dh = v \, dP$, and for an incompressible liquid with constant $v$, integration gives $\Delta h = v \Delta P$.

#### Step 7: Boiler Heat Addition (6 → 1, constant-pressure heating + evaporation + superheating)

$$Q_{boiler} = h_1 - h_6 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$

#### Step 8: Total Heat Supplied

$$Q_{in} = Q_{boiler} + Q_{reheat} = (h_1 - h_6) + (h_3 - h_2)$$

#### Step 9: Net Work Output

$$W_{net} = W_T - W_P = (h_1 - h_2) + (h_3 - h_4) - (h_6 - h_5)$$

#### Step 10: Thermal Efficiency

By the First Law applied to the cyclic integral:

$$\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_{in} - Q_{out} = W_{net}$$

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{(h_1 - h_2) + (h_3 - h_4) - (h_6 - h_5)}{(h_1 - h_6) + (h_3 - h_2)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** The denominator has **two terms** in the reheat cycle (one for boiler, one for reheater) — this is the most common error KTU examiners penalize.

### 3.2 Derivation: Extraction Fraction y for a Single Open Feedwater Heater

Consider an **Open FWH** operating at pressure $P_{ext}$. The bleed steam enters at state 2 (extraction point) with enthalpy $h_2$ and condenses to saturated liquid at $P_{ext}$ with enthalpy $h_6$. The condensate from the condenser (state 5) is pumped up to $P_{ext}$ with enthalpy $h_5$ (note: $h_5$ is at $P_{ext}$ after pumping, NOT at $P_c$).

**Mass balance on the FWH (per kg of steam entering the main turbine):**

$$\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out} = 1 \text{ kg}$$

The FWH has two inlets and one outlet:

- Inlet 1: Condensate from pump 1 (after pump-1) = $(1-y)$ kg at enthalpy $h_5$
- Inlet 2: Bled steam from turbine = $y$ kg at enthalpy $h_2$
- Outlet: Mixed saturated liquid to boiler feed pump = $1$ kg at enthalpy $h_6$

**Energy balance on FWH (neglecting heat loss, no work):**

$$\text{Energy in} = \text{Energy out}$$

$$(1 - y) h_5 + y \cdot h_2 = (1) h_6$$

**Expanding and solving for y:**

$$h_5 - y \cdot h_5 + y \cdot h_2 = h_6$$

$$y(h_2 - h_5) = h_6 - h_5$$

$$\boxed{y = \frac{h_6 - h_5}{h_2 - h_5}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** $h_5$ is the enthalpy **after the condensate pump** (at $P_{ext}$), not the enthalpy at the condenser exit. Students frequently use $h_f$ at $P_c$ for $h_5$, which is **wrong** and costs 2–3 marks.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Model KTU Numerical — 14 marks)

**Problem Statement:**
Steam enters the HP turbine of a reheat-regenerative cycle at **8 MPa, 480 °C** (state 1). After expanding isentropically to **0.8 MPa** (state 2), it is reheated to **480 °C** (state 3) and then expanded isentropically in the LP turbine to **10 kPa** (state 4). One open feedwater heater operates at **0.8 MPa**, with steam bled from state 2. The condenser operates at **10 kPa**. Determine:
(a) The extraction fraction y.
(b) The thermal efficiency of the cycle.
(c) Compare the exit quality with and without reheat.

**Steam Table Data (interpolated from standard steam tables):**

| State | Pressure (MPa) | Temperature (°C) | Enthalpy h (kJ/kg) | Entropy s (kJ/kg·K) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 8.0 | 480 | 3348.4 | 6.6586 |
| 2 (sat. vap @ 0.8 MPa) | 0.8 | sat. vap | 2769.1 | 6.6628 |
| 3 (superheated @ 0.8 MPa, 480 °C) | 0.8 | 480 | 3359.7 | 7.8850 |
| 4 (wet @ 10 kPa) | 0.01 | — | 2340.0 (computed below) | 6.6586 |
| 5 (sat. liq @ 10 kPa) | 0.01 | 41.51 | 173.88 | — |
| 6 (sat. liq @ 0.8 MPa) | 0.8 | 170.4 | 720.87 | — |
| 7 (liq @ 8.0 MPa) | 8.0 | — | 728.75 (computed) | — |

#### Part (a): Extraction Fraction y

**Step 1 — Find $h_5$** (condensate at 10 kPa + pump 1 to 0.8 MPa):

Pump 1 work approximation: $W_{P1} = v_5(P_{ext} - P_c) = 0.001010 \times (800 - 10) = 0.798$ kJ/kg

$$h_5 = h_{f @ 10 kPa} + W_{P1} = 173.88 + 0.80 = 174.68 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Apply FWH energy balance:**

$$y = \frac{h_6 - h_5}{h_2 - h_5} = \frac{720.87 - 174.68}{2769.1 - 174.68}$$

$$y = \frac{546.19}{2594.42} = 0.2105$$

$$\boxed{y \approx 0.2105 \text{ (i.e., 21.05 % of steam is extracted)}}$$

#### Part (b): Thermal Efficiency

**Step 1 — Find $h_4$** (LP turbine exit at 10 kPa, isentropic from state 3):

At 10 kPa: $s_f = 0.6493$, $s_{fg} = 7.5009$, $s_g = 8.1502$ kJ/(kg·K)

$h_f = 191.83$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg} = 2392.8$ kJ/kg

Since $s_4 = s_3 = 7.8850 < s_g = 8.1502$, exit is wet:

$$x_4 = \frac{s_4 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{7.8850 - 0.6493}{7.5009} = 0.9653$$

$$h_4 = h_f + x_4 \cdot h_{fg} = 191.83 + 0.9653 \times 2392.8 = 2501.66 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Compute net work (per kg of steam leaving boiler):**

Turbine work per kg of steam passing through:

$w_T = (h_1 - h_2) + (1-y)(h_3 - h_4)$

$w_T = (3348.4 - 2769.1) + (0.7895)(3359.7 - 2501.66)$

$w_T = 579.3 + (0.7895)(858.04) = 579.3 + 677.48 = 1256.78$ kJ/kg

**Step 3 — Total pump work:**

$w_{P1} = 0.80$ kJ/kg (per kg of condensate)

$w_{P2} = v_6 (P_1 - P_{ext}) = 0.001115 \times (8000 - 800) = 8.03$ kJ/kg (per kg of feedwater leaving FWH)

Total pump work per kg of steam from boiler:

$w_P = w_{P2} + (1-y) w_{P1} = 8.03 + (0.7895)(0.80) = 8.03 + 0.63 = 8.66$ kJ/kg

**Step 4 — Net work:**

$$w_{net} = w_T - w_P = 1256.78 - 8.66 = 1248.12 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 5 — Heat added in boiler:**

$$q_{in} = (1-y)(h_1 - h_7)$$

where $h_7 = h_6 + w_{P2} = 720.87 + 8.03 = 728.90$ kJ/kg

$q_{in} = (0.7895)(3348.4 - 728.90) = (0.7895)(2619.50) = 2068.10$ kJ/kg

**Step 6 — Thermal efficiency:**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} = \frac{1248.12}{2068.10} = 0.6035$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{th} \approx 60.35\%}$$

#### Part (c): Comparison of Exit Quality

- **With reheat:** $x_4 = 0.9653$ (96.53 % — well above the 88 % limit) ✓
- **Without reheat** (single expansion from state 1 to 10 kPa): $s_4' = s_1 = 6.6586$

$x_4' = \frac{6.6586 - 0.6493}{7.5009} = 0.8008$ (80.08 % — **below** the safe 88 % limit) ✗

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The reheat cycle successfully raises the exit quality above the safe threshold, preventing LP turbine blade erosion.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Reheat Rankine Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Boiler1["BOILER 1\nP1 T1 high"]
    HPTurbine["HP TURBINE\nP1 to P2"]
    Reheater["REHEATER\nP2 T2 to T3"]
    LPTurbine["LP TURBINE\nP3 to P4"]
    Condenser1["CONDENSER\nP4 to P5"]
    Pump1["PUMP\nP5 to P1"]
    GeneratorA["GENERATOR\nelectrical output"]
    HPTurbine --> GeneratorA
    LPTurbine --> GeneratorA

    Boiler1 -->|state 1| HPTurbine
    HPTurbine -->|state 2| Reheater
    Reheater -->|state 3| LPTurbine
    LPTurbine -->|state 4| Condenser1
    Condenser1 -->|state 5| Pump1
    Pump1 -->|state 6| Boiler1
```

### 4.2 T-s Diagram: Reheat Rankine Cycle (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Dome["SATURATION DOME\n(saturation curve)"]
    subgraph CyclePath["Reheat Cycle Path on T-s"]
        direction TB
        s1["1: HP Turbine Inlet\nsuperheated, high T high s"]
        s2["2: HP Turbine Exit\nsat vapor or superheat"]
        s3["3: Reheater Exit\nsuperheated, P=P2, T=T1"]
        s4["4: LP Turbine Exit\nwet region"]
        s5["5: Condenser Exit\nsaturated liquid"]
        s6["6: Pump Exit\ncompressed liquid"]
        HeatIn1["Q boiler: 6 to 1"]
        HeatIn2["Q reheat: 2 to 3"]
        HeatOut["Q out: 4 to 5"]
        s6 -->|isobaric heat| s1
        s1 -->|isentropic| s2
        s2 -->|isobaric heat| s3
        s3 -->|isentropic| s4
        s4 -->|isobaric| s5
        s5 -->|isentropic pump| s6
    end
    s2 -.->|below dome or on dome| Dome
    s4 -.->|inside wet region| Dome
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Regenerative Rankine Cycle with Open FWH

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Boiler2["BOILER\nhigh P T"]
    MainTurbine["MAIN TURBINE\nthree sections"]
    Extraction["EXTRACTION\npoint at Pext"]
    OFWH["OPEN FEEDWATER HEATER\nmixing chamber at Pext"]
    BFP["BOILER FEED PUMP\nPext to Pboiler"]
    CondPump["CONDENSATE PUMP\nPc to Pext"]
    Condenser2["CONDENSER\nlow pressure"]
    GeneratorB["GENERATOR"]

    Boiler2 -->|state 1| MainTurbine
    MainTurbine --> GeneratorB
    MainTurbine -->|state 2 bleed| Extraction
    Extraction --> OFWH
    MainTurbine -->|state 3 exhaust| Condenser2
    Condenser2 -->|state 4| CondPump
    CondPump -->|state 5| OFWH
    OFWH -->|state 6 sat liquid| BFP
    BFP -->|state 7| Boiler2
```

### 4.4 h-s (Mollier) Diagram Schematic: Reheat Cycle Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Mollier["h-s MOLLIER DIAGRAM"]
    subgraph CyclePath2["Reheat Cycle Trajectory"]
        direction TB
        p1["1: high h, high s\nP=8 MPa T=480 C"]
        p2["2: medium h, same s as 1\nP=0.8 MPa, sat vapor"]
        p3["3: high h, higher s\nP=0.8 MPa T=480 C\nreheated"]
        p4["4: lowest h, same s as 3\nP=10 kPa, wet region"]
        p5["5: lowest h, sat liquid\nP=10 kPa"]
        p6["6: low h, compressed liq\nP=8 MPa"]
        p6 -->|constant P heating| p1
        p1 -->|vertical down isentropic| p2
        p2 -->|constant P heating right| p3
        p3 -->|vertical down isentropic| p4
        p4 -->|constant P condensation left| p5
        p5 -->|small slope up pump| p6
    end
    Mollier --- CyclePath2
```

### 4.5 Combined Reheat-Regenerative Cycle Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PlantLayout["COMBINED REHEAT + REGENERATIVE POWER PLANT"]
        direction LR
        B1["BOILER 1"]
        HPT["HP TURBINE"]
        RH["REHEATER"]
        IPT["IP TURBINE\nwith extraction"]
        LPT["LP TURBINE"]
        CND["CONDENSER"]
        CP["CONDENSATE PUMP"]
        DEAE["DEAERATOR\nopen FWH"]
        BFP2["BOILER FEED PUMP"]
        GEN["GENERATOR"]
        B1 -->|1| HPT
        HPT -->|2| RH
        RH -->|3| IPT
        IPT -->|extraction y| DEAE
        IPT -->|4| LPT
        LPT -->|5| CND
        CND -->|6| CP
        CP -->|7| DEAE
        DEAE -->|8| BFP2
        BFP2 -->|9| B1
        HPT --> GEN
        IPT --> GEN
        LPT --> GEN
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading Guide for T-s and h-s Diagrams (KTU Exam Tip):**
> - In the **reheat T-s diagram**, the expansion lines 1→2 and 3→4 are **vertical (constant entropy)**, and the heating lines 6→1 and 2→3 are **horizontal (constant pressure)**.
> - In the **h-s Mollier diagram**, expansion lines are vertical, but constant-pressure lines are **steep curves sloping up-right**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**What is the purpose of reheating steam in a Rankine cycle? Mention the effect of reheating on thermal efficiency and turbine blade erosion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Purpose of Reheating (1 mark):** Reheating is the process of expanding steam partially in a high-pressure (HP) turbine, then sending it back to the boiler to be re-superheated at constant pressure to (or near) the original turbine inlet temperature, before completing the expansion in a low-pressure (LP) turbine.

> **Effect on Thermal Efficiency (1 mark):** Reheating increases the **mean temperature of heat addition**, thereby increasing the **thermal efficiency** of the cycle. However, the gain is modest (typically 4–5 %) because additional heat must be supplied in the reheater.

> **Effect on Blade Erosion (1 mark):** Reheating keeps the LP turbine exit steam **dry or only slightly wet** (quality typically above 88 %), thereby **preventing blade erosion** due to water droplet impingement. This is often the *primary* practical motivation for reheat.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Distinguish between an open feedwater heater and a closed feedwater heater. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **[!NOTE]**
> **Distinction (2 marks):**

| Feature | Open FWH (De-aerator) | Closed FWH |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Direct contact mixing chamber | Shell-and-tube surface heat exchanger |
| Mixing | Bleed steam mixes directly with feedwater | Steam and feedwater separated by tube walls |
| Drain handling | Drains mix with feedwater (no separate pump) | Drains cascaded to next lower-pressure FWH or condenser (needs trap/pump) |
| Pressure of bleed steam | Same as feedwater inside FWH | Higher than feedwater (drives heat transfer) |
| Heat transfer | Direct (essentially isothermal at $T_{sat} @ P_{ext}$) | Indirect across tubes (feedwater may not reach saturation T) |

> **Advantage — Open FWH (0.5 mark):** Simpler construction, lower cost, no separate drain pump, also removes dissolved gases (de-aeration).
>
> **Advantage — Closed FWH (0.5 mark):** Bleed steam and feedwater are at different pressures — provides greater flexibility in cycle design and allows cascading of drains.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **thermal efficiency of an ideal reheat Rankine cycle** with a single reheat stage. Clearly state all assumptions and define each state-point. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steam power plant operates on an ideal reheat Rankine cycle. Steam enters the HP turbine at **15 MPa, 600 °C** and expands to **3 MPa**, where it is reheated to **600 °C**. The LP turbine exhausts to the condenser at **10 kPa**. Determine:
(i) The thermal efficiency of the cycle.
(ii) The quality of steam at the LP turbine exit.
(iii) The mass flow rate of steam required for a net power output of **100 MW**.

Given steam table data:

| State | P (MPa) | T (°C) | h (kJ/kg) | s (kJ/kg·K) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 15 | 600 | 3583.1 | 6.6796 |
| 2 | 3 | (sat vap) | 2994.3 | 6.5412 |
| 3 | 3 | 600 | 3682.3 | 7.5084 |
| 4 | 0.01 | (wet) | ? | 7.5084 |

Neglect pump work. Use $h_{f @ 10 kPa} = 191.83$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg @ 10 kPa} = 2392.8$ kJ/kg, $s_{f @ 10 kPa} = 0.6493$ kJ/kg·K, $s_{fg @ 10 kPa} = 7.5009$ kJ/kg·K. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a): 7 marks**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumptions (1 mark):**
> 1. All processes are internally reversible; turbines and pumps are isentropic.
> 2. No pressure drop in boiler, reheater, or condenser piping.
> 3. Condenser exit is saturated liquid; boiler and reheater exits are at the same temperature.
> 4. Kinetic and potential energy changes are negligible.
> 5. Single reheat stage (extension to multiple reheat is straightforward).

**State-Point Diagram (1 mark):**
Refer to Section 4.2 above — points 1 → 2 (HP expansion), 2 → 3 (reheat), 3 → 4 (LP expansion), 4 → 5 (condensation), 5 → 6 (pumping), 6 → 1 (boiler heating).

**Derivation (5 marks):** Per kg of steam leaving the boiler,

$$w_{HP} = h_1 - h_2 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

$$w_{LP} = h_3 - h_4 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

$$w_T = w_{HP} + w_{LP} = (h_1 - h_2) + (h_3 - h_4) \quad \text{(0.5 mark)}$$

$$q_{in} = (h_1 - h_6) + (h_3 - h_2) \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

$$q_{out} = h_4 - h_5 \quad \text{(0.5 mark)}$$

$$w_{net} = w_T - w_P \approx (h_1 - h_2) + (h_3 - h_4) \quad \text{(0.5 mark)}$$

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} = \frac{(h_1 - h_2) + (h_3 - h_4)}{(h_1 - h_6) + (h_3 - h_2)} \quad \text{(0.5 mark)}$$

---

**Model Solution — Part (b): 7 marks**

**(i) Thermal efficiency (3 marks):**

Neglecting pump work: $w_P \approx 0$, so $h_6 \approx h_5 = 191.83$ kJ/kg.

$$w_{HP} = 3583.1 - 2994.3 = 588.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Find $h_4$ at 10 kPa, $s_4 = s_3 = 7.5084$ kJ/(kg·K):

$x_4 = (7.5084 - 0.6493) / 7.5009 = 0.9143$

$h_4 = 191.83 + 0.9143 \times 2392.8 = 2379.4$ kJ/kg

$$w_{LP} = 3682.3 - 2379.4 = 1302.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$q_{in} = (3583.1 - 191.83) + (3682.3 - 2994.3) = 3391.27 + 688.0 = 4079.27 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{th} = \frac{588.8 + 1302.9}{4079.27} = \frac{1891.7}{4079.27} = 0.4638 \text{ or } 46.38\%}$$

**(ii) Exit quality (2 marks):**

$$\boxed{x_4 = 0.9143 \text{ (91.43 \%)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is well above the 88 % safe limit — reheat is effective.

**(iii) Mass flow rate (2 marks):**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{W}_{net}}{w_{net}} = \frac{100{,}000 \text{ kW}}{1891.7 \text{ kJ/kg}} = 52.86 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} \approx 52.86 \text{ kg/s}}$$

---

#### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the **working of a regenerative Rankine cycle with a single open feedwater heater**. Derive the expression for the **fraction of steam extracted (y)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A regenerative Rankine cycle uses an open feedwater heater operating at **0.5 MPa**. Steam enters the turbine at **4 MPa, 400 °C** and the condenser operates at **10 kPa**. The expansion in the turbine is isentropic. Calculate:
(i) The fraction of steam extracted.
(ii) The thermal efficiency of the cycle.

Given steam table data:

| State | P (MPa) | Condition | h (kJ/kg) | s (kJ/kg·K) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4.0 | superheated, 400 °C | 3213.6 | 6.7690 |
| 2 (extraction) | 0.5 | sat. vapor | 2748.7 | 6.8213 |
| 3 (LP exit) | 0.01 | wet | ? | 6.7690 |
| 4 (cond. exit) | 0.01 | sat. liquid | 191.83 | 0.6493 |
| 5 (after P-1) | 0.5 | comp. liquid | 192.33 (≈$h_4+v\Delta P$) | — |
| 6 (FWH exit) | 0.5 | sat. liquid | 640.23 | — |
| 7 (after BFP) | 4.0 | comp. liquid | 645.23 | — |

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a): 7 marks**

> **[!NOTE]**
> **Working Principle (3 marks):** In a regenerative cycle, a fraction $y$ of partially expanded steam is **bled (extracted)** from an intermediate turbine stage at the same pressure as the FWH. This bled steam condenses in the FWH, releasing its latent heat to the cold feedwater arriving from the condensate pump. The feedwater leaves the FWH as saturated liquid at $P_{ext}$ and is then pumped to the boiler pressure by the boiler feed pump (BFP). The remaining $(1-y)$ fraction of steam continues to expand in the LP turbine to the condenser pressure.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.3.

**Derivation of extraction fraction (3 marks):**

Mass balance on the FWH (per kg of steam entering main turbine):

$(1 - y)$ kg at $h_5$ (condensate after pump-1) + $y$ kg at $h_2$ (bled steam) = 1 kg at $h_6$ (saturated liquid leaving FWH).

$$(1 - y)h_5 + y \cdot h_2 = h_6$$

$$h_5 - y h_5 + y h_2 = h_6$$

$$y(h_2 - h_5) = h_6 - h_5$$

$$\boxed{y = \frac{h_6 - h_5}{h_2 - h_5}}$$

---

**Model Solution — Part (b): 7 marks**

**(i) Extraction fraction (3 marks):**

$$\boxed{y = \frac{640.23 - 192.33}{2748.7 - 192.33} = \frac{447.90}{2556.37} = 0.1752}$$

(17.52 % of steam is extracted)

**(ii) Thermal efficiency (4 marks):**

Find $h_3$ at 10 kPa with $s_3 = s_1 = 6.7690$ kJ/(kg·K):

$x_3 = (6.7690 - 0.6493)/7.5009 = 0.8163$

$h_3 = 191.83 + 0.8163 \times 2392.8 = 2145.16$ kJ/kg

**Turbine work per kg of steam entering turbine:**

$w_T = (h_1 - h_2) + (1-y)(h_2 - h_3)$

$w_T = (3213.6 - 2748.7) + (0.8248)(2748.7 - 2145.16)$

$w_T = 464.9 + (0.8248)(603.54) = 464.9 + 497.83 = 962.73$ kJ/kg

**Pump work per kg of steam entering turbine:**

$w_{P1} = h_5 - h_4 = 192.33 - 191.83 = 0.50$ kJ/kg (per kg of condensate)

$w_{P2} = h_7 - h_6 = 645.23 - 640.23 = 5.00$ kJ/kg (per kg leaving FWH)

Total per kg of steam from boiler:

$w_P = w_{P2} + (1-y) w_{P1} = 5.00 + (0.8248)(0.50) = 5.41$ kJ/kg

**Net work:**

$w_{net} = 962.73 - 5.41 = 957.32$ kJ/kg

**Heat added in boiler:**

$q_{in} = (1-y)(h_1 - h_7) = (0.8248)(3213.6 - 645.23) = (0.8248)(2568.37) = 2118.39$ kJ/kg

**Thermal efficiency:**

$$\boxed{\eta_{th} = \frac{957.32}{2118.39} = 0.4519 \text{ or } 45.19\%}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Reheat / Regenerative Cycle Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the reheater heat term in the denominator.** The denominator of $\eta_{th}$ for the reheat cycle is $(h_1 - h_6) + (h_3 - h_2)$, **NOT** just $(h_1 - h_6)$. *Penalty: 2 marks.*
>
> 2. **Confusing $h_5$ with $h_4$ in the FWH energy balance.** $h_5$ is the enthalpy **after** the condensate pump (at extraction pressure $P_{ext}$), not the enthalpy at the condenser exit. *Penalty: 2–3 marks.*
>
> 3. **Not applying the factor $(1-y)$ to the LP turbine work and condenser heat rejection.** Only the *un-extracted* fraction $(1-y)$ of steam passes through the LP turbine and condenser. *Penalty: 1–2 marks.*
>
> 4. **Using $(1-y)$ on pump work incorrectly.** The boiler feed pump (BFP) handles the *full* flow (1 kg basis) because feedwater passes through it after mixing in the FWH. Only the condensate pump handles $(1-y)$ flow. *Penalty: 1–2 marks.*
>
> 5. **Failing to specify assumptions** (e.g., neglecting pump work, ignoring KE/PE). The KTU marking scheme typically allocates **1 mark for assumptions**. *Penalty: 1 mark.*
>
> 6. **Wrong interpretation of "saturation dome" in the T-s diagram.** For a reheat cycle, the LP expansion 3→4 should *end inside the wet region* (not necessarily on the saturation curve). Drawing it incorrectly on the diagram costs **1 mark** in the diagram-evaluation component.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print This Section Before Exam):**

- **Reheat Cycle:**
  - ✓ Steam expanded in **HP turbine → reheated → expanded in LP turbine**.
  - ✓ Denominator of $\eta_{th}$ has **two heat addition terms**: boiler AND reheater.
  - ✓ Reheat **raises mean temperature of heat addition** → modest efficiency gain.
  - ✓ Primary benefit: **keeps LP exhaust quality above 88 %** (avoids blade erosion).
  - ✓ Modern plants use **double or triple reheat** for best efficiency.

- **Regenerative Cycle:**
  - ✓ Steam **bled (extracted)** from an intermediate turbine stage.
  - ✓ Bled steam heats the **feedwater** in a FWH.
  - ✓ **Extraction fraction**: $y = (h_6 - h_5)/(h_2 - h_5)$ for open FWH.
  - ✓ Open FWH: direct mixing, no drain pump; Closed FWH: surface type, needs drain pump.
  - ✓ Pump work has **two components**: condensate pump (handles $(1-y)$ flow) and BFP (handles full flow).

- **Combined Cycle (Reheat + Regeneration):**
  - ✓ Both improvements used together in modern plants.
  - ✓ Net plant efficiencies reach **38–42 %** in coal plants, **45–48 %** in combined-cycle gas plants.

- **Numerical Problem Strategy:**
  - ✓ Step 1 — Fix the state points using steam tables (1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 → 6 → 7).
  - ✓ Step 2 — Apply FWH energy balance to find $y$ (if regenerative).
  - ✓ Step 3 — Compute each work and heat term with the correct mass factor.
  - ✓ Step 4 — Apply $\eta_{th} = W_{net} / Q_{in}$.

- **Common Mistake to Avoid:**
  - ✓ For the reheat cycle, $Q_{in}$ = boiler heat + reheater heat (NOT just boiler heat).
  - ✓ For the regenerative cycle, the **condensate pump handles $(1-y)$ flow** but the **BFP handles full flow**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steam Boilers - Types of Boilers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Steam Boilers - Types of Boilers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Steam Boiler** is a closed pressure vessel (in most cases, a cylindrical shell) in which water is converted into saturated or superheated steam by the application of heat energy from the combustion of a fuel (solid, liquid, or gaseous) or from waste heat streams. The generated steam is then utilized for power generation, process heating, space heating, or driving auxiliary mechanical equipment in industries.

The pressure inside the boiler is generally above atmospheric pressure, and the steam generated is used for external work. The total heat gained by the steam (from feed water at a given temperature to steam at a given pressure and temperature) is termed the **"Boiler Evaporative Capacity"** or **"Equivalent Evaporation"**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBMET604 - Module 1):**
> A boiler is essentially a heat exchanger where the working fluid (water) undergoes a phase change from liquid to vapor. The fuel's chemical energy is first converted into thermal energy through combustion, which is then transferred to the water through the heating surface.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **pressure cooker on a gas stove** combined with a **kitchen chimney system**. The pressure cooker is your boiler shell. The water inside (like Dal being cooked) is heated by the burner (combustion chamber). Steam builds up inside, which is then piped out through a small nozzle (the steam pipe) to be used for cooking something else (turbine or process). The chimney (flue) carries away the hot exhaust gases.

Now scale this up to an industrial size: instead of a kitchen stove, you have a furnace firing coal, oil, or gas. Instead of a 5-liter cooker, you have a massive cylindrical vessel holding tons of water. The steam produced isn't for cooking rice — it spins a 200 MW turbine that lights up cities. That industrial pressure cooker is your **Steam Boiler**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Standard)

| Term | Definition | Typical Range |
|------|------------|---------------|
| **Boiler Capacity** | Quantity of steam generated per hour | 1 – 1000+ TPH |
| **Working Pressure** | Steam pressure at boiler outlet | 10 – 250 bar (modern) |
| **Evaporation Ratio** | Steam generated per kg of fuel | 8 – 14 kg steam/kg coal |
| **Thermal Efficiency** | Heat absorbed by water / Heat from fuel | 70 – 92% |
| **Heating Surface** | Total area exposed to hot gases for heat transfer | varies by capacity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard Definitions for KTU Exams:**
> - **Fire-Tube Boiler:** Hot gases flow inside the tubes, and water surrounds them.
> - **Water-Tube Boiler:** Water flows inside the tubes, and hot gases surround them.
> - **Boiler Horsepower (BHP):** The evaporation of 34.5 lb (≈15.65 kg) of water per hour from and at 100 °C (212 °F).
> - **1 TPH (Tonne Per Hour):** Evaporation of 1000 kg of water per hour.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept of a Steam Boiler

The working principle of a steam boiler is governed by the **First Law of Thermodynamics (Energy Conservation)**. The chemical energy stored in the fuel is released by combustion and transferred to water through the heating surface, producing steam.

The complete operational cycle has the following structured logical steps:

1. **Fuel Supply & Combustion:** The fuel (coal, oil, gas) is fed into the furnace/grate where it burns in the presence of air, releasing chemical energy as heat.
2. **Heat Transfer:** The hot combustion gases transfer their heat to the water/steam through the heating surface (tubes or shell).
3. **Water Circulation:** Cold feed water enters the boiler and circulates through the heating surface to absorb heat.
4. **Steam Generation:** When the water temperature reaches saturation temperature (corresponding to the boiler pressure), it begins to convert into steam.
5. **Steam Separation:** The steam-water mixture is separated in the steam drum; dry steam exits at the top.
6. **Exhaust:** Combustion gases leave through the chimney after transferring maximum heat.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why" and "How" Behind Each Step:**
> - **Why Combustion?** To convert chemical energy → thermal energy.
> - **How Heat Transfer Occurs?** Via **conduction** (through tube metal), **convection** (from gases to tube surface), and **radiation** (in the furnace).
> - **Why Circulation?** To bring cold water in contact with hot surfaces continuously, preventing overheating and scaling.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---------|-------------|---------------|
| $\dot{m}_s = \dfrac{Q}{h_g - h_f}$ | Steam mass flow rate from heat input | $kg/s$, $h$ in $kJ/kg$ |
| $E = \dfrac{\dot{m}_s (h_g - h_{f,100})}{2257}$ | Equivalent Evaporation (E) | $kg$ of water evaporated from & at 100°C per kg of fuel |
| $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_s (h_g - h_{f,feed})}{\dot{m}_f \times GCV}$ | Thermal Efficiency of Boiler | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| $E_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_s}{\dot{m}_f}$ | Actual Evaporation (kg steam/kg fuel) | $kg/kg$ |
| $\text{Boiler HP} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_s \text{ (lb/hr)}}{34.5}$ | Boiler Horsepower Definition | HP |
| $1 \text{ TPH} = 1000 \text{ kg/hr} \approx 62.7 \text{ BHP}$ | Tonne per Hour to Boiler HP | Conversion |
| $\dot{Q} = UA \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Heat Transfer Rate (LMTD method) | $W$, $U$ in $W/m^2K$ |
| $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log Mean Temperature Difference | $°C$ or $K$ |
| $P_{sat}$ vs $T_{sat}$ | Saturation Pressure-Temperature Relation | From Steam Tables |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Power Plants:** Water-tube boilers (Babcock & Wilcox, Benson) generate superheated steam at 250+ bar for Rankine cycle turbines.
> - **Sugar & Paper Industries:** Fire-tube boilers (Cochran, Lancashire) provide process steam at moderate pressures (10–15 bar).
> - **Marine Applications:** Locomotive-type boilers and packaged boilers are preferred for compactness and quick steam raising.
> - **Cogeneration/CHP:** Waste heat recovery boilers (WHRB) utilize exhaust gases from gas turbines or industrial processes.

### 2.3 Modern Classification Framework (KTU 2024 Perspective)

Steam boilers are classified based on multiple criteria:

- **Based on Tube Contents:** Fire-tube vs Water-tube
- **Based on Pressure:** Low (< 10 bar), Medium (10–40 bar), High (> 40 bar)
- **Based on Axis:** Vertical vs Horizontal
- **Based on Circulation:** Natural Circulation, Forced Circulation, Once-Through
- **Based on Fuel:** Solid fuel, Oil-fired, Gas-fired, Waste-heat
- **Based on Use:** Stationary, Portable, Marine, Locomotive

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Classification of Boilers (Exhaustive Breakdown)

#### A. FIRE-TUBE BOILERS

In **fire-tube boilers**, the hot combustion gases pass through the tubes, which are surrounded by water. The heat transfer occurs through the tube walls to the surrounding water.

**Common Types and Their Analytical Comparison:**

| S.No | Boiler Type | Capacity (TPH) | Working Pressure (bar) | Construction Notes |
|------|-------------|----------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Cochran Boiler** | 0.5 – 5 | 6.5 – 17.5 | Vertical, multi-tubular, fire-tube |
| 2 | **Lancashire Boiler** | 2 – 10 | 7 – 16 | Horizontal, internal furnace, 2 fire tubes |
| 3 | **Cornish Boiler** | 1 – 5 | 6 – 10 | Horizontal, single flue tube, shorter than Lancashire |
| 4 | **Locomotive Boiler** | 1 – 6 | 10 – 20 | Horizontal, multi-tubular, mobile |
| 5 | **Babcock & Wilcox (Fire-tube variant)** | 10 – 50 | 15 – 30 | Compact, shell-type fire-tube |
| 6 | **Vertical Fire-Tube** | 0.1 – 1 | 5 – 8 | Small capacity, vertical orientation |

**Key Characteristics of Fire-Tube Boilers:**

- **Pressure Limit:** Generally limited to ~25 bar due to large shell diameter
- **Heating Surface:** Located inside the cylindrical shell
- **Steam Output:** Limited to ~10 TPH typically
- **Maintenance:** Easier and cheaper
- **Response Time:** Slow (high thermal inertia)
- **Water Treatment:** Critical (high inventory of water inside)

#### B. WATER-TUBE BOILERS

In **water-tube boilers**, water circulates inside the tubes, and hot combustion gases flow over the outside of the tubes. The steam drum is at the top, and the mud drum is at the bottom.

**Common Types and Their Analytical Comparison:**

| S.No | Boiler Type | Capacity (TPH) | Working Pressure (bar) | Special Feature |
|------|-------------|----------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Babcock & Wilcox** | 20 – 200 | 30 – 100 | Bent water tubes, inclined headers |
| 2 | **Stirling Boiler** | 50 – 500 | 40 – 100 | Three-drum design, bent tubes |
| 3 | **La-Mont Boiler** | 10 – 100 | 80 – 180 | Forced circulation, centrifugal pump |
| 4 | **Benson Boiler** | 100 – 1000+ | 180 – 250 | Once-through, no circulation, critical pressure |
| 5 | **Velox Boiler** | 10 – 50 | 50 – 80 | Uses pressurized combustion |
| 6 | **Loeffler Boiler** | 30 – 100 | 100 – 150 | Uses part of steam for circulation |

**Key Characteristics of Water-Tube Boilers:**

- **Pressure Limit:** Can go beyond 250 bar (supercritical)
- **Heating Surface:** Distributed across many small tubes
- **Steam Output:** Up to 1000+ TPH in utility boilers
- **Maintenance:** More complex; requires skilled operators
- **Response Time:** Fast (low water inventory)
- **Water Treatment:** Less critical (smaller water volume)

#### C. SPECIAL BOILERS AND MODERN VARIANTS

| S.No | Boiler Type | Application | Key Feature |
|------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | **CFB (Circulating Fluidized Bed) Boiler** | Power plants, low-grade coal | Combustion at 850-900°C, limestone for SO₂ capture |
| 2 | **WHRB (Waste Heat Recovery Boiler)** | Cement, steel, process industries | No separate fuel; uses waste hot gases |
| 3 | **AFBC (Atmospheric Fluidized Bed Combustion)** | Small to medium power plants | Bed material is sand mixed with ash |
| 4 | **Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG)** | Combined cycle plants | Generates steam from gas turbine exhaust |
| 5 | **Electrostatic Precipitator-coupled Boilers** | High-ash coal applications | Combines combustion with emission control |

### 3.2 Detailed Working of a Cochran Boiler (Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough)

The **Cochran Boiler** is a vertical, multi-tubular, fire-tube boiler commonly used in small-scale industries and is a frequent KTU exam topic.

**Components and Their Functions:**

1. **Shell:** The outer cylindrical body made of steel plates, riveted or welded. It holds water and steam.
2. **Combustion Chamber:** Located at the bottom; a hemispherical dome where fuel is burned. The furnace is integrated into the shell.
3. **Fire Tubes:** A bundle of horizontal tubes (typically 60-160 tubes of 75mm diameter) inside the shell through which hot gases pass.
4. **Combustion Space:** The annular space between the shell and the fire-tube nest.
5. **Smoke Box:** Located at the top, where the exhaust gases collect before exiting through the chimney.
6. **Chimney:** Releases flue gases to the atmosphere.
7. **Mountings:** Includes safety valve, pressure gauge, water level indicator, feed check valve, steam stop valve, blow-off cock, fusible plug, manhole.
8. **Grate:** Located below the combustion chamber, where solid fuel is placed for burning.

**Operational Flow:**

$$\text{Fuel} \xrightarrow{\text{combustion}} \text{Hot Gases} \xrightarrow{\text{via fire tubes}} \text{Heat to Water} \xrightarrow{} \text{Steam}$$

- The fuel (typically coal) is fed onto the grate.
- Hot gases from combustion rise upward, enter the fire tubes, transfer heat to surrounding water.
- The cooled gases enter the smoke box, then exit through the chimney.
- Cold feed water enters through the check valve and surrounds the fire tubes.
- Steam accumulates at the top of the shell and is drawn off through the steam stop valve.

### 3.3 Detailed Working of a Babcock & Wilcox Boiler (Analytical Walkthrough)

The **Babcock & Wilcox Boiler** is a horizontal, water-tube, natural circulation boiler used widely in thermal power stations.

**Components:**

1. **Steam Drum (Upper Drum):** A large horizontal cylindrical drum that holds saturated steam and water mixture. It separates steam from water using cyclones or chevron separators.
2. **Water/Mud Drum (Lower Drum):** A smaller drum at the bottom that collects water and sediment/mud.
3. **Water Tubes:** Bent tubes connecting the upper and lower drums. Water circulates through them.
4. **Headers:** Distribution pipes that connect tubes in parallel.
5. **Superheater:** Tubes located in the path of hot gases, raising steam temperature above saturation.
6. **Furnace:** The combustion chamber at the front.
7. **Feed Water Inlet:** Cold feed water enters the steam drum.
8. **Baffles:** Brick or steel partitions to direct gas flow.

**Working (Natural Circulation Path):**

- Water from the steam drum descends through downcomers (large pipes outside the heated zone) to the mud drum.
- In the mud drum, the water is slightly heated, becomes less dense, and rises through the water tubes exposed to hot gases.
- The water-steam mixture returns to the steam drum, where steam separates and is drawn off.
- The natural density difference (cold water vs. hot water-steam mixture) drives the circulation — this is called **"thermo-siphon" or "natural circulation"**.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison: Fire-Tube vs Water-Tube Boilers

| Parameter | Fire-Tube Boiler | Water-Tube Boiler |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Water Location** | Outside the tubes (in shell) | Inside the tubes |
| **Hot Gas Location** | Inside the tubes | Outside the tubes |
| **Max Working Pressure** | ~25 bar | 250+ bar (supercritical) |
| **Steam Generation Rate** | Up to 10 TPH | Up to 1000+ TPH |
| **Shell Diameter** | Large (constrained) | Small (tubes carry water) |
| **Risk of Explosion** | Higher (large water inventory) | Lower (small water inventory) |
| **Time to Raise Steam** | Slow (1-3 hours) | Fast (5-30 minutes) |
| **Mobility** | Difficult | Easy (modular design) |
| **Water Treatment** | Critical (large water volume) | Less critical |
| **Suitability** | Small industries, process steam | Power plants, large industries |
| **Maintenance Cost** | Low | High |
| **Initial Cost** | Low | High |
| **Thermal Efficiency** | 75-85% | 85-92% |
| **Response to Load Change** | Slow | Fast |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always mention the **"high pressure — large capacity — fast response"** trio when justifying water-tube boiler selection for power plants.

### 3.5 Numerical Example: Equivalent Evaporation Calculation

**Problem:** A boiler produces 8000 kg/hr of dry saturated steam at 10 bar from feed water at 50°C. The coal consumption is 700 kg/hr with a GCV of 30,000 kJ/kg. Calculate:
(a) Actual evaporation
(b) Equivalent evaporation
(c) Boiler efficiency

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_s = 8000 \text{ kg/hr}$
- Boiler pressure = 10 bar
- $T_{feed} = 50°C$
- $\dot{m}_f = 700 \text{ kg/hr}$
- $GCV = 30,000 \text{ kJ/kg}$

**Step 1: From Steam Tables (at 10 bar):**
- $h_f = 762.81 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $h_g = 2778.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $h_{fg} = 2015.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- Saturation temperature at 10 bar = 179.9°C
- Enthalpy of water at 50°C: $h_{f,50} = 209.33 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- Latent heat at 100°C (for equivalent evaporation): $h_{fg,100} = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$

**Step 2: Actual Evaporation (kg steam per kg fuel):**

$$E_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_s}{\dot{m}_f} = \dfrac{8000}{700} = 11.43 \text{ kg steam/kg fuel}$$

**Step 3: Equivalent Evaporation (from & at 100°C basis):**

$$E = E_{\text{actual}} \times \dfrac{h_g - h_{f,feed}}{2257}$$

$$E = 11.43 \times \dfrac{2778.1 - 209.33}{2257}$$

$$E = 11.43 \times \dfrac{2568.77}{2257}$$

$$E = 11.43 \times 1.1381 = 13.0 \text{ kg steam (from & at 100°C)/kg fuel}$$

**Step 4: Boiler Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_s (h_g - h_{f,feed})}{\dot{m}_f \times GCV}$$

$$\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{8000 \times (2778.1 - 209.33)}{700 \times 30000}$$

$$\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{8000 \times 2568.77}{21,000,000} = \dfrac{20,550,160}{21,000,000}$$

$$\eta_{boiler} = 0.9785 \approx 97.85\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> This efficiency is unusually high (97.85%) because we used the **GCV of coal** directly. In real KTU problems, the heat input is often calculated using the **NCV** (Net Calorific Value) which accounts for latent heat lost in flue gases, giving a more realistic efficiency of 70-90%.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Tree of Steam Boilers

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[STEAM BOILERS] --> B[Fire-Tube Boilers]
    A --> C[Water-Tube Boilers]
    A --> D[Special Boilers]

    B --> B1[Cochran - Vertical Multi-Tubular]
    B --> B2[Lancashire - 2 Fire Tubes]
    B --> B3[Cornish - 1 Fire Tube]
    B --> B4[Locomotive - Mobile]
    B --> B5[Vertical Fire-Tube]

    C --> C1[Natural Circulation]
    C --> C2[Forced Circulation]
    C --> C3[Once-Through]

    C1 --> C1a[Babcock and Wilcox]
    C1 --> C1b[Stirling]
    C1 --> C1c[Three-Drum]

    C2 --> C2a[La-Mont]
    C2 --> C2b[Loeffler]

    C3 --> C3a[Benson]
    C3 --> C3b[Velox]
    C3 --> C3c[Supercritical Units]

    D --> D1[CFBC - Circulating Fluidized Bed]
    D --> D2[WHRB - Waste Heat Recovery]
    D --> D3[HRSG - Heat Recovery Steam Generator]
    D --> D4[AFBC - Atmospheric Fluidized Bed]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Water-Tube Boiler System

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[FUEL SUPPLY] --> B[FURNACE]
    B1[COMBUSTION AIR] --> B
    B --> C[HOT COMBUSTION GASES]
    C --> D[HEATING SURFACE - WATER TUBES]
    D --> E[STEAM DRUM]
    F[FEED WATER INLET] --> E
    E --> G[DRY SATURATED STEAM]
    G --> H[SUPERHEATER]
    H --> I[SUPERHEATED STEAM TO TURBINE]
    C --> J[ECONOMIZER]
    F --> J
    J --> D
    C --> K[PREHEATER AIR]
    B1 --> K
    C --> L[ASH HANDLING SYSTEM]
    C --> M[FLUE GAS TO CHIMNEY]
    M --> N[ESP / BAG FILTER]
    N --> O[STACK]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Working of a Cochran Boiler (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[COAL ON GRATE] --> B[COMBUSTION CHAMBER]
    B1[AIR SUPPLY] --> B
    B --> C[HOT GASES RISING]
    C --> D[FIRE TUBES - BUNDLE]
    D --> E[HEAT TRANSFER TO WATER]
    E --> F[STEAM RISES TO TOP]
    F --> G[STEAM OUTLET]
    C --> H[SMOKE BOX]
    H --> I[CHIMNEY]
    J[FEED WATER INLET] --> K[SHELL WATER SPACE]
    K --> E
    L[SAFETY VALVE] --> F
    M[WATER GAUGE] --> K
```

### 4.4 Comparative Architecture: Fire-Tube vs Water-Tube Boiler (Block View)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Fire-Tube Boiler Architecture
        FT1[Hot Gases in Tubes] --> FT2[Tube Wall Heat Transfer]
        FT2 --> FT3[Water Surrounds Tubes]
        FT3 --> FT4[Steam Rises to Top of Shell]
    end

    subgraph Water-Tube Boiler Architecture
        WT1[Water Inside Tubes] --> WT2[Tube Wall Heat Transfer]
        WT2 --> WT3[Hot Gases Outside Tubes]
        WT3 --> WT4[Steam-Water Mixture to Steam Drum]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams for KTU Exams:**
> When asked to draw a boiler diagram in the exam, use a clear **labeled block diagram** showing: Fuel → Combustion → Heat transfer path → Water circulation path → Steam output → Flue gas exit. Always include **mountings** (safety valve, pressure gauge, water gauge, fusible plug) and **accessories** (feed pump, economizer, superheater).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Differentiate between Fire-tube and Water-tube boilers in terms of (i) water and gas location, (ii) maximum working pressure, and (iii) application.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Fire-Tube Boiler | Water-Tube Boiler |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|
| **(i) Water & Gas Location** | Water is contained in the shell and surrounds the fire tubes; hot gases flow **inside** the tubes. | Water flows **inside** the tubes; hot gases flow **outside** the tubes, surrounding them. |
| **(ii) Maximum Working Pressure** | Limited to about **25 bar** because the large diameter shell cannot withstand high pressures. | Can withstand pressures of **250+ bar** (supercritical) because water is in small-diameter tubes. |
| **(iii) Application** | Used in **small-scale industries, sugar mills, paper mills, and process steam applications** where pressure requirement is moderate. | Used in **thermal power plants, large process industries, and high-pressure steam applications** (e.g., power generation turbines). |

*(3 marks: 1 mark each for the three aspects, with clear comparison)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Define "Equivalent Evaporation" and state its significance in boiler performance evaluation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Equivalent Evaporation (E) is defined as the **quantity of water (in kg) that would be evaporated per kg of fuel**, if the feed water were supplied at 100°C and steam generated at 100°C (i.e., from & at 100°C), under the assumption that the latent heat of vaporization is 2257 kJ/kg.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$E = \dfrac{\dot{m}_s \times (h_g - h_{f,\text{feed}})}{\dot{m}_f \times h_{fg,100}}$$

**Significance:**
1. It provides a **common standard** to compare boilers operating at different pressures and feed water temperatures.
2. It allows **direct comparison of efficiency** regardless of operating conditions.
3. It serves as the **basis for the Boiler Horsepower (BHP)** definition — 1 BHP = 34.5 lb of water evaporated from & at 100°C per hour.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark significance points)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 – KTU 2024 Pattern, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **Cochran boiler**. List at least four essential mountings used on it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A boiler generates **6000 kg/hr** of dry saturated steam at a pressure of **12 bar** from feed water entering at **45°C**. Coal is fired at the rate of **500 kg/hr** with a calorific value of **28000 kJ/kg**. Calculate the **equivalent evaporation** and **boiler efficiency** (use GCV basis). Comment on the obtained efficiency.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — Question A:**

**Part (a) — Construction and Working of Cochran Boiler [7 Marks]**

**Construction [3 Marks]:**
A Cochran boiler is a **vertical, multi-tubular, fire-tube boiler**. Its main parts are:

1. **Shell:** A vertical cylindrical steel shell, with a hemispherical top crown and a hemispherical bottom. It holds the water and steam.
