KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]** > **Q1.** Define *Most Probable Velocity* and *Root Mean Square Velocity* of a gas. State the relationship between them. **Model Answer:** The **most probable velocity** $C_{mp}$ of a gas is the speed possessed by the maximum number of molecules at a given temperature. It corresponds to the peak of the Maxwell speed distribution curve. The **root mean square velocity** $C_{rms}$ is the square root of the mean of the squares of the molecular speeds. It is the speed used in kinetic theory to relate temperature to average translational kinetic energy. $$\boxed{\,C_{mp} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2RT}{M}}, \qquad C_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3RT}{M}}\,}$$ The relationship is: $$C_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}}\; C_{mp} \approx 1.225\; C_{mp}$$ --- > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]** > **Q2.** Why is $C_{rms} > C_{bar} > C_{mp}$ for any gas at any temperature? **Model Answer:** Because the Maxwell distribution is **asymmetric (skewed toward higher speeds)** — it has a long tail on the right side representing a small but non-zero fraction of molecules with very high speeds. - $C_{mp}$ is the **mode** (peak) — biased toward low speeds because most molecules cluster near the peak. - $\bar{C}$ is the **mean** — slightly higher because the long right tail pulls the average upward. - $C_{rms}$ involves **squaring** speeds first, which gives **extra weight** to the high-speed tail. Hence the result is largest. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]` **(a)** Derive an expression for the **most probable velocity** of gas molecules using Maxwell's distribution law. *(7 marks)* **(b)** At what temperature will the RMS velocity of **oxygen** ($M = 32\ \text{kg/kmol}$) be equal to the mean velocity of **hydrogen** ($M = 2\ \text{kg/kmol}$) at **27 °C**? *(7 marks)* --- ### Model Solution to Question A #### Part (a) — Derivation **Step 1:** Write Maxwell's speed distribution: $$f(C) = 4\pi \left(\frac{M}{2\pi R T}\right)^{3/2} e^{-MC^2/2RT} \cdot C^2$$ Let $K = 4\pi \left(\dfrac{M}{2\pi R T}\right)^{3/2}$ — a constant. Then $f(C) = K \cdot e^{-MC^2/2RT} \cdot C^2$. **Step 2:** The most probable velocity occurs where $f(C)$ is **maximum**, i.e., $\dfrac{df}{dC} = 0$. $$\frac{df}{dC} = K\left[ e^{-MC^2/2RT}\cdot 2C + C^2 \cdot e^{-MC^2/2RT}\cdot \left(-\frac{MC}{RT}\right) \right] = 0$$ $$K \cdot e^{-MC^2/2RT} \cdot C \left[ 2 - \frac{MC^2}{RT} \right] = 0$$ Since $C \neq 0$: $$2 = \frac{MC_{mp}^2}{RT} \;\Rightarrow\; C_{mp} = \sqrt{\frac{2RT}{M}}$$ #### Part (b) — Numerical **Given:** - $M_{O_2} = 32\ \text{kg/kmol}$ - $M_{H_2} = 2\ \text{kg/kmol}$ - $T_{H_2} = 27 + 273 = 300\ \text{K}$ - Condition: $C_{rms,\, O_2}(T_x) = \bar{C}_{H_2}(300\ \text{K})$ **Step 1:** Compute $\bar{C}_{H_2}$ at 300 K: $$\bar{C}_{H_2} = \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 8.314 \times 1000 \times 300}{\pi \times 2}} = \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 8314 \times 300}{\pi \times 2}}$$ Wait — with M in kg/kmol, R must be **8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)**: $$\bar{C}_{H_2} = \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 8.314 \times 300}{\pi \times 2}} = \sqrt{\frac{19953.6}{6.2832}} = \sqrt{3175.7} \approx 56.35 \text{ m/s}$$ Hmm, that seems low. Let me recheck units: with $M = 2\ \text{kg/kmol}$ and $R = 8.314\ \text{kJ/(kmol·K)} = 8314\ \text{J/(kmol·K)}$: $$\bar{C}_{H_2} = \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 8314 \times 300}{\pi \times 2}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.996 \times 10^7}{6.2832}} = \sqrt{3.176 \times 10^6} \approx 1782\ \text{m/s}$$ That's the correct value (~1780 m/s at 300 K for H₂). **Step 2:** Set $C_{rms,\, O_2}(T_x) = 1782\ \text{m/s}$: $$\sqrt{\frac{3 \times 8.314 \times T_x}{32}} = 1782$$ $$\frac{3 \times 8.314 \times T_x}{32} = 1782^2 = 3.176 \times 10^6$$ $$T_x = \frac{3.176 \times 10^6 \times 32}{3 \times 8.314} = \frac{1.016 \times 10^8}{24.942} \approx 4.07 \times 10^6\ \text{K}$$ $$\boxed{T_x \approx 4.07 \times 10^6\ \text{K}}$$ > [!NOTE] > The extraordinarily high temperature arises because hydrogen, being the lightest gas, has extremely high velocities even at room temperature. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Internal Choice **(a)** Explain **Maxwell's law of distribution of molecular velocities**. Sketch the distribution curve and mark the positions of $C_{mp}$, $\bar{C}$ and $C_{rms}$. *(7 marks)* **(b)** The RMS velocity of nitrogen molecules at 0 °C is **511 m/s**. Compute: (i) Most probable velocity, (ii) Mean velocity, (iii) The temperature at which the RMS velocity becomes **doubled**. *(7 marks)* --- ### Model Solution to Question B #### Part (a) — Maxwell's Law Maxwell's law (1860) states that in a gas at thermal equilibrium, the **fraction of molecules** $dN/N$ having speeds between $C$ and $C+dC$ is: $$\frac{dN}{N} = 4\pi \left(\frac{M}{2\pi R T}\right)^{3/2} e^{-MC^2/2RT}\, C^2\, dC$$ The resulting $f(C)$ vs $C$ plot is a **bell-shaped asymmetric curve** with: - $C = 0$ at the origin (no molecules have zero speed) - Rises to a peak at $C = C_{mp}$ - Falls off gradually with a long right tail - Approaches zero as $C \to \infty$ **Curve sketch (Mermaid rendering):** ```mermaid graph LR subgraph xAxis["C axis"] z["C = 0"] mp["C_mp (Peak)"] bar["C_bar (Centroid)"] rms["C_rms (RMS)"] inf["C tends to infinity"] end subgraph yAxis["f C axis"] lo["Low f"] hi["High f"] end z --> mp --> bar --> rms --> inf ``` Mark on the curve in increasing order: $\;C_{mp} < \bar{C} < C_{rms}\;$. #### Part (b) — Numerical **Given:** $C_{rms} = 511\ \text{m/s}$ at $T_1 = 273\ \text{K}$. $M = 28\ \text{kg/kmol}$ (nitrogen). **Step 1 — Verify M:** $$C_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{3RT}{M}} \;\Rightarrow\; M = \frac{3RT}{C_{rms}^2} = \frac{3 \times 8.314 \times 273}{511^2} = \frac{6809.1}{261121} = 0.02608\ \text{kg/mol} = 26.08\ \text{kg/kmol} \approx 28$$ **Step 2 — Most Probable Velocity:** $$C_{mp} = C_{rms} \times \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} = 511 \times 0.8165 \approx 417.2\ \text{m/s}$$ **Step 3 — Mean Velocity:** $$\bar{C} = C_{rms} \times \sqrt{\frac{8}{3\pi}} = 511 \times \sqrt{0.8488} = 511 \times 0.9213 \approx 470.8\ \text{m/s}$$ **Step 4 — Temperature for doubled RMS velocity:** $$C_{rms}' = 2 C_{rms} \;\Rightarrow\; (C_{rms}')^2 = 4 C_{rms}^2$$ Since $C_{rms}^2 \propto T$: $$T_2 = 4 T_1 = 4 \times 273 = 1092\ \text{K} = 819\ \text{°C}$$ $$\boxed{C_{mp} \approx 417.2\ \text{m/s}, \quad \bar{C} \approx 470.8\ \text{m/s}, \quad T_2 = 1092\ \text{K}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Never confuse the three formulas** — $C_{mp}$ has factor 2, $\bar{C}$ has factor $8/\pi$, $C_{rms}$ has factor 3 under the root. > 2. **Always check units of R and M.** Mixing kg/mol with kJ/kmol (or vice versa) gives wrong answers by **1000×**. > 3. **Show the substitution step explicitly** when converting °C to K: K = °C + 273. Examiners allocate marks for this step. > 4. **Don't forget the factor of 1.128 / 1.085 / 1.225** — these ratio questions appear separately in 2-mark parts. > 5. **State assumptions** of kinetic theory before derivations — this alone is worth 1–2 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Points — Must Memorise** - ✅ Three molecular velocities in **ascending order**: $C_{mp} < \bar{C} < C_{rms}$. - ✅ Formulas: - $C_{mp} = \sqrt{2RT/M}$ - $\bar{C} = \sqrt{8RT/(\pi M)}$ - $C_{rms} = \sqrt{3RT/M}$ - ✅ All three depend on **temperature only**, not on pressure. - ✅ Ratio constants (pure numbers): $\bar{C}/C_{mp} = 2/\sqrt{\pi} \approx 1.128$; $C_{rms}/C_{mp} = \sqrt{3/2} \approx 1.225$; $C_{rms}/\bar{C} = \sqrt{3\pi/8} \approx 1.085$. - ✅ Universal gas constant: $R = 8.314\ \text{kJ/(kmol·K)}$ — pair with $M$ in kg/kmol. - ✅ Maxwell's distribution is **bell-shaped, asymmetric, with a long right tail**. - ✅ $C_{rms}$ is linked to **translational kinetic energy**: $\overline{KE}_{per\ molecule} = \dfrac{3}{2} k_B T = \dfrac{3}{2} \dfrac{R}{N_A} T$. - ✅ Pressure relation: $pV = \dfrac{1}{3} m n C_{rms}^2$ — basis of ideal gas law derivation. - ✅ Velocity of sound: $v = \sqrt{\gamma R T / M}$ — frequently asked companion question. - ✅ Assumptions of kinetic theory: large $N$, elastic collisions, random motion, Newtonian mechanics, negligible molecular volume. - ✅ Standard Gamma integrals used in derivation: $\int_0^\infty u\, e^{-u} du = 1$, $\int_0^\infty u^2 e^{-u} du = 2$. - ✅ Always convert °C → K before substituting in any velocity formula. - ✅ Sketches must show $C_{mp}$ at the **peak**, $\bar{C}$ slightly to its right, $C_{rms}$ further right — examiners check this order on the diagram.

topics.title: Effect of Friction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Effect of Friction # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of air-standard cycles, **friction** refers to the dissipative resistance offered by the working fluid (and the mechanical system) to the piston motion during compression and expansion strokes. The "Effect of Friction" is the systematic deviation of the **actual cycle** from the **ideal (frictionless) cycle** as represented on the P-V and T-S diagrams. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 1):** *Effect of Friction in air standard cycles — deviation between actual and ideal cycles, reduction in net work output, decrease in mean effective pressure, and the concept of polytropic processes with friction.* In an **ideal cycle**, the compression and expansion processes are assumed to be **reversible adiabatic (isentropic)**, following the relation $PV^\gamma = \text{constant}$. In an **actual cycle with friction**, the working fluid behaves as if it follows a **polytropic process** $PV^n = \text{constant}$, where the polytropic index $n \neq \gamma$: - During **compression (with friction):** $n_1 < \gamma$ (curve lies *above* the isentropic curve) - During **expansion (with friction):** $n_2 < \gamma$ (curve lies *below* the isentropic curve) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Stair-Climber With a Heavy Backpack" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a person climbing a staircase. > - **Without friction (ideal):** They walk smoothly up and down, conserving all energy. The energy going up equals the energy recovered coming down. > - **With friction (actual):** They are walking on a sticky surface that resists motion. Going *up* requires *more* effort (you push harder than gravity alone), and coming *down*, the stickiness *holds you back*, so you recover *less* energy than you put in. > - **Net result:** You are exhausted even though you returned to the same floor! The "lost" energy dissipated as heat to the sticky surface. > > Similarly, in an engine, the gas is "pushed harder" during compression (higher pressure) and "resists expansion" (lower pressure) — the **net work decreases** and the **efficiency drops**. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Specific heat ratio (air) | $\gamma$ | **1.4** | — | | Universal gas constant | $R$ | **8.314** | kJ/kg·K | | Polytropic index (compression, with friction) | $n_1$ | **1.30 – 1.35** | — | | Polytropic index (expansion, with friction) | $n_2$ | **1.30 – 1.35** | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Since friction generates heat that partially transfers back to the working fluid, the polytropic index $n$ is **always less than $\gamma$** for both compression and expansion in real cycles. --- ## 1.4 Why Friction Matters in Thermal Engineering The effect of friction is **not** a minor correction — it directly impacts: 1. **Net work output** ($W_{\text{net,actual}} < W_{\text{net,ideal}}$) 2. **Thermal efficiency** ($\eta_{\text{actual}} < \eta_{\text{ideal}}$) 3. **Mean effective pressure (MEP)** — a key indicator of engine power output per unit displacement 4. **Work ratio** — the fraction of expansion work that becomes useful net work 5. **Specific fuel consumption** — increases due to efficiency drop # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Ideal vs. Actual Cycle — Conceptual Breakdown In the **ideal Otto / Diesel / Dual cycle**, the working fluid undergoes: - **Reversible adiabatic (isentropic)** compression: $PV^\gamma = C$ - **Constant volume / constant pressure** heat addition - **Reversible adiabatic (isentropic)** expansion: $PV^\gamma = C$ - **Constant volume** heat rejection When **friction is present**, the fluid exhibits irreversibility. The compression and expansion processes are no longer isentropic — entropy is generated. ### Step-by-Step Logic of the Effect: **Step 1 — During Compression (Process 1→2):** - The piston pushes the gas up. Friction (fluid friction, wall friction, gas turbulence) **opposes** the piston motion. - The gas must be compressed to a *higher* pressure than ideal for the same volume ratio. - Result: $P_2^{\text{actual}} > P_2^{\text{ideal}}$ for the same $V_2$. - The compression curve is **steeper** (lies above the isentropic). **Step 2 — Heat Addition (Process 2→3):** - Heat is added at the same volume (Otto) or with some pressure rise (Diesel/Dual). - State 3 is reached at a higher pressure than ideal (because state 2 was higher). - $P_3^{\text{actual}} > P_3^{\text{ideal}}$ **Step 3 — During Expansion (Process 3→4):** - The expanding gas does work on the piston, but friction now **opposes** the piston motion in the opposite direction. - The expansion pressure drops *more* than ideal. - Result: $P_4^{\text{actual}} < P_4^{\text{ideal}}$ for the same $V_4$. - The expansion curve is **flatter** (lies below the isentropic). **Step 4 — Net Work Impact:** - Compression work *increases* (area under 1→2 increases). - Expansion work *decreases* (area under 3→4 decreases). - **Net work = Expansion work − Compression work** → Net work **decreases significantly**. **Step 5 — Efficiency Impact:** - The heat added remains similar, but the net work drops. - Therefore, $\eta_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net,actual}}}{Q_{\text{in,actual}}} < \eta_{\text{ideal}}$ ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **The following table is the AUTHORITATIVE reference for solving Effect of Friction problems in KTU University Examinations.** | # | Quantity | Ideal Cycle | Actual Cycle (with Friction) | Relationship | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Compression law | $PV^\gamma = C$ | $PV^{n_1} = C$, with $n_1 < \gamma$ | Polytropic with $n_1 \approx 1.30$ | | 2 | Expansion law | $PV^\gamma = C$ | $PV^{n_2} = C$, with $n_2 < \gamma$ | Polytropic with $n_2 \approx 1.30$ | | 3 | Compression work | $W_C = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{\gamma - 1}\left[r^{\gamma-1} - 1\right]$ | $W_C' = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{n_1 - 1}\left[r^{n_1-1} - 1\right]$ | $W_C' > W_C$ | | 4 | Expansion work | $W_E = \dfrac{P_3 V_3}{\gamma - 1}\left[1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}\right]$ | $W_E' = \dfrac{P_3 V_3}{n_2 - 1}\left[1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{n_2-1}}\right]$ | $W_E' < W_E$ | | 5 | Net work | $W_{\text{net}} = W_E - W_C$ | $W_{\text{net}}' = W_E' - W_C'$ | $W_{\text{net}}' < W_{\text{net}}$ | | 6 | Thermal efficiency | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ (Otto) | $\eta' = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{n_1-1}} \cdot \dfrac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$ | $\eta' < \eta$ | | 7 | Work ratio | $RW = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{W_E}$ | $RW' = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}'}{W_E'}$ | $RW' < RW$ | | 8 | MEP ratio | $\dfrac{\text{MEP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{MEP}_{\text{ideal}}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}'}{W_{\text{net}}}$ | Always less than 1 | $\text{MEP}_{\text{actual}} < \text{MEP}_{\text{ideal}}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL KTU Pattern:** When solving numerical problems, students are typically given the **per cent friction loss** or the **polytropic index $n$**. Always use the polytropic index for the *appropriate* process — $n_1$ for compression, $n_2$ for expansion. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Engine Design:** Engineers use friction-effect analysis to size pistons, choose cylinder materials, and design lubrication systems to minimize frictional losses. 2. **Performance Mapping:** The MEP ratio is plotted against compression ratio to find the **optimum compression ratio** for a real engine. 3. **Fuel Economy:** Understanding the efficiency drop due to friction directly informs strategies to reduce fuel consumption in SI and CI engines. 4. **Turbocharging and Supercharging:** Compensating for the lost work by forcing more air into the cylinder. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Compression Work With Friction **Starting Point:** The compression process follows the polytropic relation $PV^{n_1} = C$ for the actual (friction) case. The compression work per unit mass is given by: $$W_C = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{C}{V^{n_1}} \, dV$$ **Step 1:** Substitute and integrate: $$W_C = C \left[ \frac{V^{1-n_1}}{1 - n_1} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2}$$ **Step 2:** Evaluate the bounds: $$W_C = \frac{C}{1 - n_1} \left[ V_2^{1-n_1} - V_1^{1-n_1} \right]$$ **Step 3:** Use the relation $C = P_1 V_1^{n_1} = P_2 V_2^{n_1}$: $$W_C = \frac{P_1 V_1^{n_1}}{1 - n_1} \left[ V_2^{1-n_1} - V_1^{1-n_1} \right]$$ **Step 4:** Factor out $V_1^{1-n_1}$ and use the compression ratio $r = V_1/V_2$, so $V_2 = V_1/r$: $$W_C = \frac{P_1 V_1}{1 - n_1} \left[ \left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{1-n_1} - 1 \right]$$ $$W_C = \frac{P_1 V_1}{1 - n_1} \left[ r^{n_1 - 1} - 1 \right]$$ **Step 5:** Since $1 - n_1 = -(n_1 - 1)$, rewrite: $$\boxed{W_C = \frac{P_1 V_1}{n_1 - 1} \left[ r^{n_1 - 1} - 1 \right]}$$ > **Comparison with ideal:** For $n_1 = \gamma$, this reduces to the familiar $\dfrac{P_1 V_1}{\gamma - 1}\left[r^{\gamma-1} - 1\right]$. ## 3.2 Derivation: Expansion Work With Friction By an analogous procedure for the expansion process $3 \to 4$ with $PV^{n_2} = C$: **Step 1:** Expansion work is: $$W_E = \int_{V_3}^{V_4} P \, dV$$ **Step 2:** Following the same integration method: $$W_E = \frac{P_3 V_3^{n_2}}{1 - n_2} \left[ V_4^{1-n_2} - V_3^{1-n_2} \right]$$ **Step 3:** Factor and use $V_4/V_3 = r$: $$W_E = \frac{P_3 V_3}{1 - n_2} \left[ r^{1-n_2} - 1 \right]$$ **Step 4:** Since $r^{1-n_2} = 1/r^{n_2-1}$ and $1-n_2 = -(n_2-1)$: $$\boxed{W_E = \frac{P_3 V_3}{n_2 - 1} \left[ 1 - \frac{1}{r^{n_2-1}} \right]}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Net Work and Work Ratio With Friction **Step 1:** Net work with friction: $$W_{\text{net}}' = W_E' - W_C'$$ **Step 2:** Work ratio with friction: $$RW' = \frac{W_{\text{net}}'}{W_E'} = 1 - \frac{W_C'}{W_E'}$$ **Step 3:** Expressing the ratio in terms of polytropic indices: $$RW' = 1 - \frac{W_C'}{W_E'} = 1 - \frac{\dfrac{P_1 V_1}{n_1 - 1}\left[r^{n_1-1} - 1\right]}{\dfrac{P_3 V_3}{n_2 - 1}\left[1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{n_2-1}}\right]}$$ **Step 4:** Using the Otto-cycle relation $\dfrac{P_3 V_3}{P_1 V_1} = r^{\gamma - 1}$ (for the temperature-ignited case) and assuming $n_1 = n_2 = n$ for simplicity: $$RW' = 1 - r^{1 - \gamma + (n - 1)} \cdot \frac{1 - r^{-(n-1)}}{r^{n-1} - 1}$$ After algebraic simplification (with $n_1 = n_2 = n$): $$\boxed{RW' = 1 - r^{\gamma - n}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note:** For the ideal case ($n = \gamma$), $RW_{\text{ideal}} = 1 - r^0 = 0$... wait, this is the *limit* case. The ideal work ratio is obtained by computing $W_C/W_E$ with $\gamma$ first. > > For the ideal case: $RW_{\text{ideal}} = 1 - r^{1-\gamma}$. > For the actual case: $RW' = 1 - r^{1-\gamma} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\gamma - 1}{n - 1}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{r^{n-1} - 1}{r^{\gamma-1} - 1}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{1 - r^{-(\gamma-1)}}{1 - r^{-(n-1)}}\right)$ ## 3.4 Derivation: Thermal Efficiency With Friction (Otto Cycle) **Step 1:** Heat added (constant volume, $2 \to 3$): $$Q_{\text{in}} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2)$$ **Step 2:** Heat rejected (constant volume, $4 \to 1$): $$Q_{\text{out}} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$$ **Step 3:** Using polytropic relations, with $n_1$ for compression and $n_2$ for expansion: $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{n_1 - 1}, \quad T_3 = T_2 \cdot \frac{P_3}{P_2} \quad (\text{constant } V)$$ $$T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{r}\right)^{n_2 - 1}$$ **Step 4:** Net work: $$W_{\text{net}}' = Q_{\text{in}} - Q_{\text{out}}$$ **Step 5:** Efficiency: $$\boxed{\eta_{\text{actual}} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}}$$ > **Key comparison:** For the ideal Otto cycle, $\eta_{\text{ideal}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$. The friction-affected efficiency uses $n$ instead of $\gamma$, giving a *lower* value because $n < \gamma \Rightarrow r^{n-1} < r^{\gamma-1}$. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Percentage Loss Due to Friction For a quick KTU-style numerical answer, the **percentage loss in net work** due to friction is: $$\% \text{ Loss} = \frac{W_{\text{net,ideal}} - W_{\text{net,actual}}}{W_{\text{net,ideal}}} \times 100$$ Similarly for MEP: $$\frac{\text{MEP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{MEP}_{\text{ideal}}} = \frac{W_{\text{net,actual}}}{W_{\text{net,ideal}}}$$ These ratios are **always less than 1** in the presence of friction. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 P-V Diagram: Ideal vs Actual Otto Cycle With Friction > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** The following Mermaid flowchart visually maps the P-V diagram regions, illustrating the *relative position* of ideal (dashed) vs actual (solid) curves. The **shaded regions** highlight the lost work area. ```mermaid flowchart TB A["P-V DIAGRAM: EFFECT OF FRICTION
Comparison of Ideal vs Actual Otto Cycle"] --> B["PROCESS 1-2 COMPRESSION
Ideal: Isentropic curve (gamma)
Actual: Polytropic curve (n1 less than gamma)
Actual lies ABOVE ideal"] A --> C["PROCESS 2-3 HEAT ADDITION
Constant Volume
State 3-actual is HIGHER than 3-ideal"] A --> D["PROCESS 3-4 EXPANSION
Ideal: Isentropic curve (gamma)
Actual: Polytropic curve (n2 less than gamma)
Actual lies BELOW ideal"] A --> E["PROCESS 4-1 HEAT REJECTION
Constant Volume
State 4-actual is LOWER than 4-ideal"] B --> F["RESULT: INCREASED COMPRESSION WORK
Area 1-2-prime-2 is the EXTRA work input"] D --> G["RESULT: DECREASED EXPANSION WORK
Area 3-4-prime-4 is the LOST work output"] F --> H["NET EFFECT
W_net-actual LESS THAN W_net-ideal
Area enclosed REDUCES"] G --> H H --> I["CONSEQUENCES
1. Lower Thermal Efficiency
2. Lower Mean Effective Pressure
3. Lower Work Ratio
4. Higher Fuel Consumption"] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Friction's Cascade Impact ```mermaid flowchart LR A["FRICTION
Fluid + Mechanical
Irreversibility"] --> B["DEVIATION FROM ISENTROPIC
n less than gamma"] B --> C["COMPRESSION CURVE SHIFTS UP
Higher P2"] B --> D["EXPANSION CURVE SHIFTS DOWN
Lower P4"] C --> E["W_compression INCREASES"] D --> F["W_expansion DECREASES"] E --> G["W_net DECREASES"] F --> G G --> H["MEP DECREASES"] G --> I["eta THERMAL DECREASES"] G --> J["Work Ratio DECREASES"] H --> K["ENGINE POWER LOSS"] I --> L["FUEL ECONOMY WORSENS"] J --> M["CYCLE PERFORMANCE DEGRADES"] ``` ## 4.3 T-S Diagram Comparison (Text-Based Schematic) ``` T | | 3 (actual) * <- Higher T3 due to higher P2 and constant V | / \ | 3 (ideal)* \ | / \ | 2 (actual)* \ 4 (ideal) * <- T4 slightly higher than ideal T4 | \ / (because some friction heat stays in gas) | 2 (ideal) * \ / | \ * 4 (actual) <- Lower T4 (lost work to friction) | \ | | \| * 1 | \| |___________________*________ S 1 ``` > **T-S Interpretation:** > - The compression curve **1→2** (actual) is to the **right** of ideal (entropy increases due to friction). > - The expansion curve **3→4** (actual) is also to the **right** of ideal. > - The area between the two cycles on the T-S diagram represents the **irreversibility (lost work)**. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Define the effect of friction on an air-standard cycle. How does it alter the compression and expansion processes compared to the ideal cycle?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > The effect of friction in an air-standard cycle refers to the **irreversibilities** present in the actual compression and expansion processes, which deviate from the ideal reversible adiabatic (isentropic) processes. > > - **Compression with friction:** The process deviates from $PV^\gamma = C$ to $PV^{n_1} = C$ with $n_1 < \gamma$. The actual compression curve lies **above** the ideal curve, meaning **higher pressure** is required to reach the same volume. This **increases compression work**. > > - **Expansion with friction:** The process follows $PV^{n_2} = C$ with $n_2 < \gamma$. The actual expansion curve lies **below** the ideal curve, giving **lower pressure** during expansion. This **decreases expansion work**. > > **Net result:** Net work output, thermal efficiency, and mean effective pressure all **decrease** compared to the ideal cycle. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Explain the concept of 'work ratio' with and without friction.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Work ratio (RW)** is defined as the ratio of net work output to the gross expansion work: > > $$RW = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{W_{\text{expansion}}}$$ > > - **Without friction (ideal cycle):** $RW_{\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{W_E - W_C}{W_E} = 1 - \dfrac{W_C}{W_E}$. Typical value for Otto cycle with $r = 8$ is around **0.55–0.65**. > > - **With friction (actual cycle):** $RW_{\text{actual}} < RW_{\text{ideal}}$ because friction **increases** $W_C$ and **decreases** $W_E$, both effects reducing the ratio. > > This reduction in work ratio is a direct measure of how much the cycle is degraded by irreversibilities. --- ## PART B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern) ### ✅ Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model] **An air-standard Otto cycle has a compression ratio of 8:1 and operates between pressure limits of 1 bar and 40 bar. The compression and expansion processes follow the polytropic law with index $n_1 = n_2 = 1.32$ due to friction. Calculate:** - **(a)** [7 Marks] The compression work, expansion work, and net work per kg of air with friction. - **(b)** [7 Marks] The percentage reduction in net work due to friction, and the ratio of actual to ideal mean effective pressure. **Given:** $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K, $P_1 = 1$ bar, $P_2$ (ideal) corresponds to $r = 8$. --- **Solution:** **Part (a):** We first determine state points. **Step 1 — State 2 (ideal, for reference):** $$P_2^{\text{ideal}} = P_1 \cdot r^\gamma = 1 \times 8^{1.4} = 18.38 \text{ bar}$$ For the actual cycle with friction, state 2 reaches the same volume (since compression ratio is the same) but higher pressure. However, in the standard KTU problem statement, we are given that the cycle "operates between 1 bar and 40 bar," meaning the *actual* maximum pressure is 40 bar. **Step 2 — Recompute state 2 using the actual polytropic law:** Given $P_2^{\text{actual}} = 40$ bar at $V_2 = V_1/8$: $$P_1 V_1^{n_1} = P_2 V_2^{n_1}$$ $$\frac{V_1}{V_2} = r = 8, \quad \frac{P_2}{P_1} = r^{n_1} = 8^{1.32} = 16.51$$ So $P_2 = 16.51$ bar (if $P_1 = 1$ bar). The "40 bar" represents the peak pressure after heat addition at constant volume. **Step 3 — Compression work with friction:** $$W_C' = \frac{P_1 V_1}{n_1 - 1}\left[r^{n_1-1} - 1\right]$$ Take $V_1 = 1$ m³ (basis), $P_1 = 10^5$ Pa: $$W_C' = \frac{10^5 \times 1}{1.32 - 1}\left[8^{0.32} - 1\right] = \frac{10^5}{0.32}\left[1.95 - 1\right]$$ $$W_C' = \frac{10^5 \times 0.95}{0.32} = 296.88 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 4 — Expansion work with friction:** State 3: $P_3 = 40$ bar at $V_3 = V_2 = 0.125$ m³. $$W_E' = \frac{P_3 V_3}{n_2 - 1}\left[1 - \frac{1}{r^{n_2-1}}\right]$$ $$W_E' = \frac{40 \times 10^5 \times 0.125}{0.32}\left[1 - \frac{1}{1.95}\right] = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{0.32} \times 0.487$$ $$W_E' = 1.5625 \times 10^6 \times 0.487 = 761.72 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 5 — Net work with friction:** $$W_{\text{net}}' = W_E' - W_C' = 761.72 - 296.88 = 464.84 \text{ kJ}$$ **Part (b): Percentage reduction in net work:** **Step 6 — Compute ideal net work (using $\gamma = 1.4$):** For the ideal cycle with $P_1 = 1$ bar and reaching $P_2 = 40$ bar (we use the same peak pressure for fair comparison): $$P_2^{\text{ideal}} = 1 \times 8^{1.4} = 18.38 \text{ bar}$$ To compare on the same basis, we use the *same heat input* — the peak pressure $P_3 = 40$ bar with constant volume heat addition: $$T_3 = T_2 \times \frac{P_3}{P_2} \quad \text{(constant } V\text{)}$$ For ideal case: $T_2^{\text{ideal}} = T_1 \cdot 8^{0.4} = T_1 \times 2.297$ Heat added (same for both): $Q_{\text{in}} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2)$ For the ideal case, $P_3 = 40$ bar, $P_2 = 18.38$ bar, so $T_3/T_2 = 40/18.38 = 2.176$. **Step 7 — Compute ideal compression work:** $$W_C^{\text{ideal}} = \frac{P_1 V_1}{\gamma - 1}\left[r^{\gamma-1} - 1\right] = \frac{10^5}{0.4}\left[8^{0.4} - 1\right] = \frac{10^5 \times 1.297}{0.4} = 324.25 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 8 — Compute ideal expansion work:** The expansion ratio is the same ($r = 8$), and $P_3 = 40$ bar, $V_3 = 0.125$ m³: $$W_E^{\text{ideal}} = \frac{40 \times 10^5 \times 0.125}{0.4}\left[1 - \frac{1}{8^{0.4}}\right] = \frac{5 \times 10^5 \times 0.703}{0.4} = 878.75 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 9 — Ideal net work:** $$W_{\text{net}}^{\text{ideal}} = 878.75 - 324.25 = 554.50 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 10 — Percentage reduction:** $$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{554.50 - 464.84}{554.50} \times 100 = \frac{89.66}{554.50} \times 100 = 16.17\%$$ **Step 11 — MEP ratio:** $$\frac{\text{MEP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{MEP}_{\text{ideal}}} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}'}{W_{\text{net}}^{\text{ideal}}} = \frac{464.84}{554.50} = 0.838 \text{ (or 83.8\%)}$$ --- ### ✅ Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] **An ideal Diesel cycle operates with a compression ratio of 17:1 and a cut-off ratio of 2:1. Due to mechanical and fluid friction, the polytropic indices for compression and expansion are $n_1 = 1.33$ and $n_2 = 1.30$ respectively. Taking $P_1 = 1$ bar and $T_1 = 320$ K, calculate:** - **(a)** [7 Marks] The thermal efficiency of the actual cycle with friction, and compare it with the ideal cycle efficiency. - **(b)** [7 Marks] The actual net work per kg of air and the reduction in mean effective pressure due to friction. **Given:** $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K. --- **Solution:** **Part (a):** **Step 1 — Compute temperatures with friction:** $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{n_1 - 1} = 320 \times 17^{0.33} = 320 \times 2.456 = 785.92 \text{ K}$$ $$T_3 = T_2 \cdot \rho^{n_2'} \quad \text{where } \rho = 2, \; n_2' = \frac{n_2 - 1}{n_2} \text{? No — for constant pressure, } T_3 = T_2 \cdot \rho$$ Wait — for constant pressure heat addition (Diesel), $T_3/T_2 = \rho = 2$ (independent of friction, as the process is isobaric): $$T_3 = 785.92 \times 2 = 1571.84 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $T_4$ with friction expansion:** $$T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\rho \cdot r}\right)^{n_2 - 1} \cdot \rho^{(n_2 - 1) - 0}$$ Actually, for the Diesel cycle, $V_4 = V_1$, and $V_3 = \rho V_2 = \rho V_1/r$, so $V_4/V_3 = r/\rho$. $$T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{V_3}{V_4}\right)^{n_2 - 1} = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{\rho}{r}\right)^{n_2 - 1}$$ $$T_4 = 1571.84 \times \left(\frac{2}{17}\right)^{0.30} = 1571.84 \times (0.1176)^{0.30} = 1571.84 \times 0.515 = 809.5 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3 — Actual efficiency:** $$\eta_{\text{actual}} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2} \cdot \frac{C_v}{C_p} \quad \text{?}$$ Wait — for a Diesel cycle, the heat addition is at constant pressure, so: $$Q_{\text{in}} = C_p(T_3 - T_2), \quad Q_{\text{out}} = C_v(T_4 - T_1)$$ $$\eta_{\text{actual}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \frac{C_v(T_4 - T_1)}{C_p(T_3 - T_2)}$$ $$\eta_{\text{actual}} = 1 - \frac{0.718 \times (809.5 - 320)}{1.005 \times (1571.84 - 785.92)}$$ $$\eta_{\text{actual}} = 1 - \frac{0.718 \times 489.5}{1.005 \times 785.92} = 1 - \frac{351.46}{789.85} = 1 - 0.445 = 0.555 \text{ or } 55.5\%$$ **Step 4 — Ideal Diesel efficiency (for comparison):** For the ideal cycle, $T_2^{\text{ideal}} = 320 \times 17^{0.4} = 320 \times 3.117 = 997.4$ K, $T_3^{\text{ideal}} = 997.4 \times 2 = 1994.9$ K. $$T_4^{\text{ideal}} = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{\rho}{r}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = 1994.9 \times (0.1176)^{0.4} = 1994.9 \times 0.435 = 867.8 \text{ K}$$ $$\eta_{\text{ideal}} = 1 - \frac{0.718 \times (867.8 - 320)}{1.005 \times (1994.9 - 997.4)} = 1 - \frac{0.718 \times 547.8}{1.005 \times 997.5}$$ $$\eta_{\text{ideal}} = 1 - \frac{393.32}{1002.49} = 1 - 0.392 = 0.608 \text{ or } 60.8\%$$ **Result:** $\eta_{\text{actual}} = 55.5\%$ vs $\eta_{\text{ideal}} = 60.8\%$ — a drop of **5.3 percentage points**. **Part (b):** **Step 5 — Net work per kg of air with friction:** $$W_{\text{net}}' = Q_{\text{in}} - Q_{\text{out}} = 789.85 - 351.46 = 438.39 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 6 — Ideal net work:** $$W_{\text{net}}^{\text{ideal}} = 1002.49 - 393.32 = 609.17 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 7 — MEP ratio (or net work ratio):** $$\frac{\text{MEP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{MEP}_{\text{ideal}}} = \frac{438.39}{609.17} = 0.7197 \text{ (or 71.97\%)}$$ **Step 8 — Percentage reduction in MEP:** $$\% \text{ Reduction in MEP} = (1 - 0.7197) \times 100 = 28.03\%$$ --- ## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU EXAMS:** > > 1. **Mixing up $n_1$ and $n_2$:** Students often use the same polytropic index for both processes. In the *real world*, compression friction index ($n_1$) and expansion friction index ($n_2$) are **different**. **Always use the values given in the problem.** > > 2. **Forgetting to use $n$ in the exponent correctly:** The exponent is $n - 1$ (for compression) and $-(n-1)$ (for expansion). Using $\gamma - 1$ instead of $n - 1$ will yield a 50% inflated work calculation and lose **all 7 marks** in the work sub-part. > > 3. **Heat addition process is process-specific:** Otto = constant volume (use $C_v$), Diesel = constant pressure (use $C_p$), Dual = both. **Do NOT use $C_v$ for Diesel cycle heat addition — this is a very common error.** > > 4. **Sign convention:** Compression work is **work done ON the gas** (positive on the system), expansion work is **work done BY the gas**. Net work = Expansion work − Compression work. **Do not subtract in the wrong order.** > > 5. **Units check:** $P$ in Pa, $V$ in m³ → Work in Joules. Divide by 1000 to get kJ. Inconsistency here is a **silent killer** of partial marks. > > 6. **Comparing on the same basis:** When asked for "percentage reduction," you MUST compute the ideal net work *for the same heat input* or *same peak pressure* as the actual cycle. Comparing with a *different* heat input gives meaningless numbers. --- ## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — "Effect of Friction"** - **Friction** in air-standard cycles is modeled as a **deviation from isentropic** to **polytropic** processes, with $n_1, n_2 < \gamma$. - **Compression with friction:** Curve shifts **upward** → $W_C$ **increases**. - **Expansion with friction:** Curve shifts **downward** → $W_E$ **decreases**. - **Net work decreases** → **Thermal efficiency decreases** → **MEP decreases** → **Work ratio decreases**. - **Polytropic index $n$** is **always less than $\gamma$** for both compression and expansion in real cycles (typically $n \approx 1.30$). - **Compression work formula:** $W_C = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{n_1 - 1}\left[r^{n_1-1} - 1\right]$ - **Expansion work formula:** $W_E = \dfrac{P_3 V_3}{n_2 - 1}\left[1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{n_2-1}}\right]$ - **Net work with friction:** $W_{\text{net}}' = W_E' - W_C'$ - **MEP ratio with friction:** $\dfrac{\text{MEP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{MEP}_{\text{ideal}}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}'}{W_{\text{net}}} < 1$ - **Otto cycle heat:** Use $C_v$ for both $Q_{\text{in}}$ and $Q_{\text{out}}$ (constant volume processes). - **Diesel cycle heat:** $Q_{\text{in}} = C_p(T_3 - T_2)$ (constant pressure), $Q_{\text{out}} = C_v(T_4 - T_1)$. - **Friction generates entropy:** On the T-S diagram, both actual compression and expansion curves lie to the **right** of the ideal curves, with the area between representing **lost work (irreversibility)**. - **Real-world mitigation:** Lubrication, low-friction coatings, optimal piston ring design, and precision machining all help reduce the polytropic deviation from $\gamma$. - **KTU trick question alert:** If $n_1 = \gamma$ is given, friction exists only in the *expansion* process (or vice versa). Always read the problem statement carefully.

topics.title: Nozzle Efficiency and Its Effects - Simple Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nozzle Efficiency and Its Effects - Simple Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nozzle Efficiency (η_N):** It is defined as the ratio of the **actual kinetic energy** acquired by the steam at the exit of the nozzle to the **isentropic (or ideal) kinetic energy** that would have been acquired if the expansion had been perfectly isentropic (frictionless, reversible, adiabatic) from the same initial state to the same exit pressure.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\eta_N = \dfrac{\text{Actual KE at exit}}{\text{Isentropic KE at exit}} = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{2} m V_2^2}{m (h_1 - h_{2s})} = \dfrac{V_2^2}{2 (h_1 - h_{2s}) \times 1000}$$

Or equivalently in terms of enthalpies:

$$\eta_N = \dfrac{h_1 - h_2'}{h_1 - h_{2s}} = \dfrac{\Delta h_{\text{actual}}}{\Delta h_{\text{isentropic}}}$$

Where:
- $h_1$ = Enthalpy of steam at nozzle inlet (kJ/kg)
- $h_{2s}$ = Isentropic enthalpy at nozzle exit (kJ/kg) — assuming perfect expansion
- $h_2'$ = Actual enthalpy at nozzle exit (kJ/kg) — always higher than $h_{2s}$
- $V_2$ = Actual exit velocity (m/s)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Reality Check:** In an *ideal* nozzle, steam expands isentropically, and the **entire** enthalpy drop converts into kinetic energy. In a **real** nozzle, friction, turbulence, and shock losses cause the actual exit velocity to be **lower** than the ideal one. The ratio quantifying this shortfall is precisely the *nozzle efficiency*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the top of a smooth water slide (initial high pressure steam), and you slide down to the bottom (exit of the nozzle). The slide represents the **isentropic** (frictionless) case — you reach the maximum possible speed at the bottom.

Now imagine the slide has rough patches, sharp bends, and a few bumps. You will reach the bottom, but **slower**, because some of your potential energy was lost as heat (friction) and noise (turbulence). The nozzle efficiency is simply the ratio of *how fast you actually arrived* to *how fast you would have arrived on the perfect slide*.

- **Ideal isentropic nozzle** = Perfectly smooth water slide (100% efficiency).
- **Real nozzle** = Rough water slide (typically **85%–95%** efficient for well-designed nozzles, lower in poor designs).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Typical Nozzle Efficiency Range for well-designed steam nozzles:** **85% to 95%** (often taken as **90%** for design calculations in KTU problems).
> - **Critical Pressure Ratio for Steam:** $\left(\dfrac{p_2}{p_1}\right)_{\text{crit}} = 0.546$ (governed by $\gamma = 1.3$ for saturated steam).
> - **Discharge Coefficient (C_d):** Typically **0.95 to 0.98** — accounts for the vena contracta effect, which *indirectly* reduces effective flow area and apparent efficiency.
> - **Universal Gas Constant for Steam:** $R = \dfrac{8.314}{18.02} = 0.4615$ kJ/(kg·K) — used only for superheated regions.

## 1.4 Real-World Context (Where This Concept Matters)

| Application | Role of Nozzle Efficiency |
|---|---|
| **Steam Turbines** (KTU Module 1) | Determines exit velocity of steam, directly affects **blade work output** and **overall turbine efficiency**. |
| **Rocket Nozzles** (De Laval) | Deviation from isentropic flow due to boundary layer, divergence losses. |
| **Wind Tunnels** | Calibration of supersonic test sections requires accurate η_N. |
| **Steam Injectors / Ejectors** | Efficiency governs momentum transfer in jet pumps. |
| **Gas Turbine Combustor Liners** | Convergent-divergent cooling nozzles rely on η_N to predict thrust. |
| **Industrial Steam Ejectors** | Used to create vacuum; efficiency sets achievable vacuum level. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why is Nozzle Efficiency Less Than 100%? — Causes of Loss

The following physical phenomena cause the actual kinetic energy at the nozzle exit to be **less than** the ideal isentropic value:

- **Frictional Losses** between steam molecules and nozzle walls (skin friction, boundary layer development).
- **Turbulence and Eddy Formation** as steam turns at the throat region.
- **Shock Waves** in the diverging section if the pressure ratio is poorly matched (especially in supersaturated flow).
- **Divergence Loss** — when flow fails to follow the designed divergence angle perfectly.
- **Supersaturation / Wilson Line Effect** — when high-pressure superheated steam expands too rapidly through a nozzle, the steam **fails to condense** at the saturation line, remaining in a metastable supersaturated state. This causes the actual enthalpy drop to be **less than** the isentropic one, and the **exit velocity is lower** than predicted by isentropic tables/Mollier chart.

> [!NOTE]
> **Supersaturation Effect (Important for KTU Theory Questions):** In high-pressure stages of a steam turbine where the steam initially has **high superheat**, the time spent in the nozzle is too short for the moisture droplets to form (condensation nuclei require finite time). Steam continues to expand as if it were a **dry gas** (constant specific heat) along a path *below* the saturation curve on the Mollier chart, and only later spontaneously condenses. The **Wilson line** represents the locus where condensation finally occurs. The expansion along the supersaturated path has a **lower enthalpy drop** and hence **lower kinetic energy** than the standard isentropic path.

## 2.2 Energy Balance for a Nozzle (Steady Flow Energy Equation — SFEE)

Applying the **SFEE** between inlet (1) and exit (2), neglecting potential energy change and heat transfer (adiabatic):

$$h_1 + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2} = h_2 + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2} \quad \text{(per unit mass, J/kg)}$$

For an **ideal isentropic nozzle** (η_N = 1):

$$\dfrac{V_2^2}{2} = h_1 - h_{2s} \quad (\text{assuming } V_1 \approx 0)$$

For a **real nozzle** (η_N < 1):

$$\dfrac{V_2^{'2}}{2} = h_1 - h_2' = \eta_N (h_1 - h_{2s})$$

Thus the **actual exit velocity** is:

$$V_2' = \sqrt{2 \times 1000 \times \eta_N (h_1 - h_{2s})} \;\; \text{m/s}$$

> The factor **1000** converts kJ/kg → J/kg.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet / High-Yield Equation Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the EXACT equations required to solve any KTU numerical problem on Nozzle Efficiency.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nozzle Efficiency (definition) | $\eta_N = \dfrac{V_2^{'2}}{2 \times 1000 \times (h_1 - h_{2s})} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_2'}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$ | dimensionless | Always ≤ 1 |
| 2 | Actual Enthalpy Drop | $\Delta h_{\text{act}} = \eta_N \cdot \Delta h_s = \eta_N (h_1 - h_{2s})$ | kJ/kg | Used in exit velocity calculation |
| 3 | Ideal Exit Velocity | $V_{2s} = 44.72 \sqrt{h_1 - h_{2s}}$ | m/s | When $\Delta h$ in kJ/kg |
| 4 | Actual Exit Velocity | $V_2' = 44.72 \sqrt{\eta_N (h_1 - h_{2s})}$ | m/s | Real nozzle case |
| 5 | Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m} = C_d \cdot \dfrac{A_2 \cdot V_2'}{v_2}$ | kg/s | $C_d$ = discharge coeff. (0.95–0.98) |
| 6 | Throat Velocity (sonic) | $V_{\text{throat}} = \sqrt{\gamma R T_{\text{throat}}}$ | m/s | Equal to local speed of sound |
| 7 | Exit Area (from continuity) | $A_2 = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot v_2}{V_2'}$ | m² | Inverse of mass flow rate formula |
| 8 | % Loss of KE | $\%\,\text{Loss} = (1 - \eta_N) \times 100$ | % | Reduction from isentropic value |
| 9 | % Reduction in Exit Velocity | $\%\,\text{Reduction} = (1 - \sqrt{\eta_N}) \times 100$ | % | From $V_2' = V_{2s}\sqrt{\eta_N}$ |
| 10 | Loss in KE (per kg) | $\text{KE Loss} = (1 - \eta_N)(h_1 - h_{2s})$ | kJ/kg | Represents the inefficiency |

> **Conversion Reminder:** $V = \sqrt{2 \Delta h_{\text{act}}} = \sqrt{2 \times 1000 \times \Delta h_{\text{act}}(\text{kJ/kg})} = 44.72 \sqrt{\Delta h_{\text{act}}(\text{kJ/kg})}$ m/s

## 2.4 Effects of Reduced Nozzle Efficiency — Real-World Engineering Implications

- **Reduced Exit Velocity** → Lower momentum → Reduced blade force → **Lower work output per stage**.
- **Increased Exit Enthalpy** ($h_2' > h_{2s}$) → Wasted thermal energy → **Lower thermal efficiency** of the cycle.
- **Lower Mass Flow Rate** (if back pressure is fixed and area is fixed) → Reduced power output.
- **Increased Heat Loss to Walls** (friction dissipates KE as heat) → Local heating, possible thermal stress on nozzle blades.
- **Supersaturation Loss** (for high-pressure stages) → Condensation shock may occur later, causing **erosion of blades** downstream.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Exit Velocity and Nozzle Efficiency

We start with the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** applied to an adiabatic nozzle with negligible inlet velocity:

$$h_1 = h_2 + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2000} \quad \text{(with } h \text{ in kJ/kg and } V \text{ in m/s)}$$

**Step 1:** Rearranging for kinetic energy at exit:

$$\dfrac{V_2^2}{2000} = h_1 - h_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_2 = \sqrt{2000(h_1 - h_2)}$$

> The factor is **2000** (not 2000 in SI), because we convert kJ/kg → J/kg by multiplying by 1000, and the kinetic energy term is $\frac{1}{2}V^2$, so $\frac{V^2}{2 \times 1000} = \frac{V^2}{2000}$.

**Step 2:** For the *ideal isentropic* case (no losses), exit enthalpy is $h_{2s}$:

$$V_{2s} = \sqrt{2000(h_1 - h_{2s})}$$

**Step 3:** For the *actual* case (with losses), the actual enthalpy drop is reduced by $\eta_N$:

$$h_1 - h_2' = \eta_N (h_1 - h_{2s})$$

**Step 4:** The actual exit velocity is therefore:

$$V_2' = \sqrt{2000 \, \eta_N (h_1 - h_{2s})}$$

**Step 5:** Taking the ratio:

$$\dfrac{V_2'}{V_{2s}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2000 \, \eta_N (h_1 - h_{2s})}}{\sqrt{2000 (h_1 - h_{2s})}} = \sqrt{\eta_N}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_2' = V_{2s} \cdot \sqrt{\eta_N}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a critical KTU result:** A nozzle efficiency of 0.90 (90%) yields an exit velocity of $V_2' = 0.9487 \cdot V_{2s}$, i.e., only **~5% reduction** in velocity despite a **10% energy loss**. This non-linear relationship is often tested.

## 3.2 Solved Numerical Problem #1 (Model KTU Pattern — Standard Type)

**Problem Statement:** Steam enters a nozzle at $p_1 = 10$ bar, $h_1 = 3050$ kJ/kg, and expands isentropically to $p_2 = 2$ bar where $h_{2s} = 2700$ kJ/kg. If the nozzle efficiency is **90%**, determine:
- (a) The actual enthalpy at exit.
- (b) The actual exit velocity of steam.
- (c) The percentage loss of kinetic energy due to nozzle inefficiency.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Data:**
- Inlet pressure: $p_1 = 10$ bar
- Inlet enthalpy: $h_1 = 3050$ kJ/kg
- Exit pressure: $p_2 = 2$ bar
- Isentropic exit enthalpy: $h_{2s} = 2700$ kJ/kg
- Nozzle efficiency: $\eta_N = 0.90$

**Step 1: Compute Isentropic Enthalpy Drop**

$$\Delta h_s = h_1 - h_{2s} = 3050 - 2700 = 350 \;\; \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2: (a) Actual Enthalpy at Exit**

The actual enthalpy drop is:

$$\Delta h_{\text{act}} = \eta_N \cdot \Delta h_s = 0.90 \times 350 = 315 \;\; \text{kJ/kg}$$

The actual exit enthalpy is therefore:

$$h_2' = h_1 - \Delta h_{\text{act}} = 3050 - 315 = 2735 \;\; \text{kJ/kg}$$

> **Result:** $h_2' = 2735$ kJ/kg (35 kJ/kg higher than $h_{2s}$, representing the lost energy).

**Step 3: (b) Actual Exit Velocity**

Using $V_2' = 44.72 \sqrt{\Delta h_{\text{act}}}$ (with $\Delta h$ in kJ/kg):

$$V_2' = 44.72 \times \sqrt{315} = 44.72 \times 17.748 = 793.7 \;\; \text{m/s}$$

> **Result:** $V_2' \approx 793.7$ m/s

**Verification using the alternate formula** $V_2' = V_{2s} \sqrt{\eta_N}$:

$$V_{2s} = 44.72 \times \sqrt{350} = 44.72 \times 18.708 = 836.6 \;\; \text{m/s}$$

$$V_2' = 836.6 \times \sqrt{0.90} = 836.6 \times 0.9487 = 793.7 \;\; \text{m/s} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4: (c) Percentage Loss of Kinetic Energy**

$$\%\,\text{KE Loss} = (1 - \eta_N) \times 100 = (1 - 0.90) \times 100 = 10\%$$

> **Result:** The kinetic energy is reduced by **10%**, even though the actual exit velocity is reduced by only **~5.13%** (i.e., $1 - 0.9487 = 0.0513$).

## 3.3 Solved Numerical Problem #2 (Effect on Mass Flow Rate — Extended Type)

**Problem Statement:** A steam nozzle has a throat area of $0.001 \text{ m}^2$ and exit area of $0.0025 \text{ m}^2$. Steam enters at $p_1 = 8$ bar, $h_1 = 3000$ kJ/kg. Isentropic enthalpy at exit (for $p_2 = 2$ bar) is $h_{2s} = 2680$ kJ/kg, and specific volume at exit is $v_2 = 0.9$ m³/kg. If $\eta_N = 0.85$, determine:
- (a) The isentropic exit velocity and actual exit velocity.
- (b) The mass flow rate at exit, assuming discharge coefficient $C_d = 0.96$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Data:**
- $\Delta h_s = h_1 - h_{2s} = 3000 - 2680 = 320$ kJ/kg
- $\eta_N = 0.85$, $C_d = 0.96$, $v_2 = 0.9$ m³/kg

**Step 1: (a) Isentropic Exit Velocity**

$$V_{2s} = 44.72 \times \sqrt{320} = 44.72 \times 17.889 = 800.0 \;\; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2: Actual Exit Velocity**

$$\Delta h_{\text{act}} = 0.85 \times 320 = 272 \;\; \text{kJ/kg}$$

$$V_2' = 44.72 \times \sqrt{272} = 44.72 \times 16.492 = 737.5 \;\; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3: (b) Mass Flow Rate**

$$\dot{m} = C_d \cdot \dfrac{A_2 \cdot V_2'}{v_2} = 0.96 \times \dfrac{0.0025 \times 737.5}{0.9}$$

$$\dot{m} = 0.96 \times \dfrac{1.8438}{0.9} = 0.96 \times 2.049 = 1.967 \;\; \text{kg/s}$$

> **Result:** $\dot{m} \approx 1.97$ kg/s

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effects of Nozzle Efficiency on Key Performance Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table summarizes HOW changing η_N affects each downstream parameter. This is the *Engineering Judgment* skill KTU examiners test.**

| Parameter | Effect of ↓ η_N (efficiency decreases) | Quantitative Relation |
|---|---|---|
| Actual enthalpy drop $\Delta h_{\text{act}}$ | **Decreases** | $\Delta h_{\text{act}} = \eta_N \cdot \Delta h_s$ (linear) |
| Exit velocity $V_2'$ | **Decreases** | $V_2' \propto \sqrt{\eta_N}$ (square-root) |
| % KE loss | **Increases** | $(1 - \eta_N) \times 100$ |
| Mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ | **Decreases** (for fixed $A_2, p_2$) | $\dot{m} \propto V_2' \propto \sqrt{\eta_N}$ |
| Blade work output $W_b$ | **Decreases** | $W_b = \dot{m} \cdot V_2' (V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})/g$ |
| Exit enthalpy $h_2'$ | **Increases** | $h_2' = h_1 - \eta_N (h_1 - h_{2s})$ |
| Stage efficiency $\eta_{\text{stage}}$ | **Decreases** | Propagated through blade efficiency |
| Heat absorbed by walls | **Increases** | Friction → dissipation |
| Supersaturation risk | **Increases** | Higher $\Delta p$ and faster expansion |
| Overall cycle efficiency $\eta_{\text{cycle}}$ | **Decreases** | Cascade effect through all stages |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Define nozzle efficiency. Why is the actual exit velocity in a steam nozzle less than the isentropic exit velocity?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1 Mark):** Nozzle efficiency $\eta_N$ is defined as the ratio of the actual kinetic energy at the exit of the nozzle to the isentropic kinetic energy at the same exit pressure:

$$\eta_N = \dfrac{h_1 - h_2'}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$$

**Reasons for Actual Velocity Being Lower (2 Marks):** The actual exit velocity is less than the isentropic velocity because of the following losses inside the nozzle:

- **Frictional resistance** between steam and nozzle walls (skin friction) — converts some KE into heat.
- **Turbulence and eddy formation** at the throat — dissipates energy as heat.
- **Shock wave formation** in the diverging portion when flow deviates from design conditions.
- **Supersaturation effect** — when high-superheat steam expands too rapidly, condensation is delayed, leading to a lower enthalpy drop and hence lower exit velocity.

These losses cause the actual enthalpy drop $(h_1 - h_2')$ to be less than the isentropic enthalpy drop $(h_1 - h_{2s})$, and since $V_2 = \sqrt{2000 \Delta h}$, the actual velocity is correspondingly lower.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]`

**Explain the supersaturation phenomenon in a steam nozzle. How does it affect nozzle efficiency?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Explanation (2 Marks):** Supersaturation occurs when high-pressure superheated steam expands rapidly through a converging-diverging nozzle. Due to the very short residence time inside the nozzle, water molecules do not get sufficient time to condense at the saturation line, so the steam continues to expand as a **dry gas** (with constant specific heat) along a path lying *below* the saturation curve on the Mollier (h-s) diagram. The steam reaches a **metastable supersaturated state**, and condensation finally occurs spontaneously when the **Wilson line** is crossed.

**Effect on Nozzle Efficiency (1 Mark):** Supersaturation reduces the actual enthalpy drop compared to the isentropic value, because the steam behaves more like a perfect gas with a *higher* effective specific heat in the metastable region. As a result, the **actual exit velocity is lower** than predicted by standard steam tables, and the **nozzle efficiency decreases**. This is particularly significant in the **high-pressure stages** of steam turbines (typically the first 2–3 stages).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — With Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Standard Application Type** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a)** Define nozzle efficiency. Derive the relationship between actual exit velocity, isentropic exit velocity, and nozzle efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Steam enters a nozzle at $p_1 = 15$ bar with enthalpy $h_1 = 3020$ kJ/kg. The steam expands isentropically to $p_2 = 1.5$ bar where $h_{2s} = 2620$ kJ/kg. If the nozzle efficiency is **88%**, calculate:
- (i) The actual enthalpy drop.
- (ii) The actual exit velocity.
- (iii) The percentage loss of kinetic energy.
- (iv) The percentage reduction in exit velocity compared to the isentropic value. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Nozzle efficiency $\eta_N$ is the ratio of the actual kinetic energy at the nozzle exit to the kinetic energy that would be obtained under isentropic (frictionless) expansion from the same inlet conditions to the same exit pressure:

$$\eta_N = \dfrac{V_2'^2 / 2}{V_{2s}^2 / 2} = \dfrac{V_2'^2}{V_{2s}^2}$$

**Derivation (5 Marks):** Applying the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** to the nozzle (adiabatic, no work, negligible inlet velocity, no elevation change):

$$h_1 = h_2 + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2000} \quad \text{(with } h \text{ in kJ/kg)}$$

For **isentropic** expansion:

$$h_1 - h_{2s} = \dfrac{V_{2s}^2}{2000} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{2s} = \sqrt{2000(h_1 - h_{2s})}$$

For **actual** expansion (with losses):

$$h_1 - h_2' = \dfrac{V_2'^2}{2000} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_2' = \sqrt{2000(h_1 - h_2')}$$

By definition of nozzle efficiency:

$$\eta_N = \dfrac{h_1 - h_2'}{h_1 - h_{2s}} = \dfrac{V_2'^2 / 2000}{V_{2s}^2 / 2000} = \dfrac{V_2'^2}{V_{2s}^2}$$

Taking the square root:

$$\boxed{\,V_2' = V_{2s} \sqrt{\eta_N}\,}$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $h_1 = 3020$ kJ/kg, $h_{2s} = 2620$ kJ/kg, $\eta_N = 0.88$

**(i) Actual Enthalpy Drop (2 Marks):**

$$\Delta h_{\text{act}} = \eta_N (h_1 - h_{2s}) = 0.88 \times (3020 - 2620)$$

$$\Delta h_{\text{act}} = 0.88 \times 400 = 352 \;\; \text{kJ/kg}$$

**(ii) Actual Exit Velocity (2 Marks):**

$$V_2' = 44.72 \sqrt{\Delta h_{\text{act}}} = 44.72 \times \sqrt{352}$$

$$V_2' = 44.72 \times 18.762 = 839.0 \;\; \text{m/s}$$

**(iii) Percentage Loss of Kinetic Energy (1.5 Marks):**

$$\%\,\text{KE Loss} = (1 - \eta_N) \times 100 = (1 - 0.88) \times 100 = 12\%$$

**(iv) Percentage Reduction in Exit Velocity (1.5 Marks):**

$$\dfrac{V_2'}{V_{2s}} = \sqrt{\eta_N} = \sqrt{0.88} = 0.9381$$

$$\%\,\text{Reduction} = (1 - 0.9381) \times 100 = 6.19\%$$

**Final Answers:**
- Actual enthalpy drop = **352 kJ/kg**
- Actual exit velocity = **839.0 m/s**
- % Loss of KE = **12%**
- % Reduction in exit velocity = **6.19%**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Effects & Supersaturation Type** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**(a)** With the help of a Mollier diagram, explain the **supersaturation phenomenon** in a steam nozzle. Discuss its effect on the nozzle efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A convergent-divergent steam nozzle receives steam at $p_1 = 20$ bar, $h_1 = 3100$ kJ/kg and expands to $p_2 = 0.1$ bar. The isentropic exit enthalpy is $h_{2s} = 2300$ kJ/kg and the actual exit enthalpy (with losses) is $h_2' = 2360$ kJ/kg. Determine:
- (i) The nozzle efficiency.
- (ii) The isentropic and actual exit velocities.
- (iii) The kinetic energy loss per kg of steam.
- (iv) The isentropic and actual mass flow rates, given the exit area $A_2 = 0.003$ m², specific volume at exit $v_2 = 13.5$ m³/kg, and discharge coefficient $C_d = 0.95$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Mollier Diagram Description (3 Marks):** On a Mollier (h-s) diagram:
- The inlet state **Point 1** lies in the superheated region (high pressure, high superheat).
- The **isentropic expansion line** is a vertical line (constant entropy) from Point 1 down to Point 2s, which lies inside the two-phase region (wet steam).
- The **actual expansion path** with supersaturation deviates from the vertical: entropy increases slightly, and the path lies *below* the saturation curve, inside the supersaturated region.
- The **Wilson line** is the locus where condensation finally begins spontaneously — beyond this point, the actual path rejoins the normal wet-steam region.

**Supersaturation Phenomenon (2 Marks):** When high-pressure, high-superheat steam expands rapidly in a convergent-divergent nozzle, the residence time is too short (microseconds) for condensation nuclei to form and grow into droplets. As a result, the steam **fails to condense** at the saturation boundary, and continues to expand as a **dry supersaturated vapour**, behaving like a perfect gas with constant specific heat $c_p$. Condensation finally occurs when the steam state crosses the **Wilson line**, beyond which droplets form and entropy rises sharply.

**Effect on Nozzle Efficiency (2 Marks):** The actual enthalpy drop along the supersaturated path is **less** than the isentropic value (because the effective specific heat is higher in the metastable region). Hence:
- The actual exit velocity is **lower** than predicted by isentropic tables.
- The **nozzle efficiency is reduced** — typically by 2% to 5% in high-pressure stages.
- Once condensation begins (at the Wilson line), a **condensation shock** forms inside the nozzle, causing additional losses and possible **erosion of downstream rotor blades**.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $h_1 = 3100$ kJ/kg, $h_{2s} = 2300$ kJ/kg, $h_2' = 2360$ kJ/kg, $A_2 = 0.003$ m², $v_2 = 13.5$ m³/kg, $C_d = 0.95$

**(i) Nozzle Efficiency (1.5 Marks):**

$$\eta_N = \dfrac{h_1 - h_2'}{h_1 - h_{2s}} = \dfrac{3100 - 2360}{3100 - 2300} = \dfrac{740}{800} = 0.925 = 92.5\%$$

**(ii) Isentropic and Actual Exit Velocities (2 Marks):**

$$V_{2s} = 44.72 \sqrt{800} = 44.72 \times 28.284 = 1264.9 \;\; \text{m/s}$$

$$V_2' = V_{2s} \sqrt{\eta_N} = 1264.9 \times \sqrt{0.925} = 1264.9 \times 0.9618 = 1216.6 \;\; \text{m/s}$$

**(iii) Kinetic Energy Loss per kg (1.5 Marks):**

$$\text{KE Loss} = (1 - \eta_N) \cdot \Delta h_s = (1 - 0.925) \times 800 = 0.075 \times 800 = 60 \;\; \text{kJ/kg}$$

**(iv) Mass Flow Rates (2 Marks):**

Isentropic (ideal) mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{isen}} = \dfrac{A_2 \cdot V_{2s}}{v_2} = \dfrac{0.003 \times 1264.9}{13.5} = \dfrac{3.795}{13.5} = 0.281 \;\; \text{kg/s}$$

Actual mass flow rate (with discharge coefficient):

$$\dot{m}_{\text{act}} = C_d \cdot \dfrac{A_2 \cdot V_2'}{v_2} = 0.95 \times \dfrac{0.003 \times 1216.6}{13.5}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{act}} = 0.95 \times \dfrac{3.650}{13.5} = 0.95 \times 0.2704 = 0.257 \;\; \text{kg/s}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Nozzle efficiency = **92.5%**
- Isentropic exit velocity = **1264.9 m/s**
- Actual exit velocity = **1216.6 m/s**
- KE loss per kg = **60 kJ/kg**
- Ideal mass flow rate = **0.281 kg/s**
- Actual mass flow rate = **0.257 kg/s**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Mistakes on this Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing 2000 vs 2 in the velocity formula:** The correct expression is $V = \sqrt{2000 \cdot \Delta h}$ (when $\Delta h$ is in kJ/kg). Using $\sqrt{2 \Delta h}$ without the **1000** conversion factor yields a velocity off by a factor of $\sqrt{1000} \approx 31.6$. The shortcut formula $V = 44.72 \sqrt{\Delta h}$ is derived from $\sqrt{2000} = 44.72$ — memorize this carefully.
>
> 2. **Mixing up $\eta_N$ and the ratio of velocities:** A nozzle efficiency of 90% does **NOT** mean the exit velocity is 90% of isentropic — it is $\sqrt{0.90} = 94.87\%$ of isentropic. This is one of the most common conceptual errors.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the discharge coefficient $C_d$ in mass flow rate problems:** When the problem mentions vena contracta, friction at inlet, or gives $C_d$ explicitly, students often use $A_2$ directly without multiplying by $C_d$. This results in an **inflated** mass flow rate, and **2 marks** are lost.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the supersaturation correction in high-pressure stages:** When the inlet pressure is **above 30 bar** with high superheat, students should mention the **Wilson line** effect. Failing to do so in Part (a) theory questions results in partial credit loss.
>
> 5. **Using $h_1 - h_{2s}$ (isentropic) directly to compute actual velocity** when $\eta_N$ is given. Always convert to **actual enthalpy drop** first: $\Delta h_{\text{act}} = \eta_N (h_1 - h_{2s})$, then plug into $V = 44.72\sqrt{\Delta h_{\text{act}}}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Revision Notes — Read this 30 minutes before the KTU exam.**

- [x] **Nozzle efficiency** $\eta_N = \dfrac{h_1 - h_2'}{h_1 - h_{2s}} = \dfrac{V_2'^2}{V_{2s}^2}$ — *always dimensionless, always ≤ 1*.
- [x] **Actual exit velocity** $V_2' = V_{2s} \cdot \sqrt{\eta_N} = 44.72 \sqrt{\eta_N (h_1 - h_{2s})}$ m/s.
- [x] **% Loss of KE** $= (1 - \eta_N) \times 100$ — this is a **linear** function of $\eta_N$.
- [x] **% Reduction in exit velocity** $= (1 - \sqrt{\eta_N}) \times 100$ — this is a **non-linear (square-root)** function of $\eta_N$.
- [x] **KE loss per kg** $= (1 - \eta_N)(h_1 - h_{2s})$ kJ/kg.
- [x] **Mass flow rate formula:** $\dot{m} = C_d \cdot \dfrac{A_2 \cdot V_2'}{v_2}$ where $C_d \approx 0.95$ to $0.98$.
- [x] **Typical η_N values:** Well-designed nozzles = 85%–95%; in KTU problems, **90% is the standard assumed value** unless stated otherwise.
- [x] **Supersaturation (Wilson line) effect:** Reduces actual enthalpy drop in high-pressure stages (typically first 2–3 stages of a turbine). Causes condensation shock and blade erosion.
- [x] **Conversion of units in velocity formula:** $V = \sqrt{2 \times 1000 \times \Delta h (\text{kJ/kg})} = 44.72 \sqrt{\Delta h (\text{kJ/kg})}$ m/s. The constant **44.72** comes from $\sqrt{2000}$.
- [x] **Exit enthalpy is HIGHER than isentropic** in a real nozzle: $h_2' > h_{2s}$. The difference $(h_2' - h_{2s})$ represents the energy lost to friction and turbulence.
- [x] **Actual exit velocity is LOWER than isentropic** in a real nozzle: $V_2' < V_{2s}$.
- [x] **Critical pressure ratio** for saturated steam $= 0.546$; if $p_2/p_1 < 0.546$, use a **convergent-divergent** nozzle.
- [x] **Reduction in velocity vs reduction in KE is NOT equal:** A 10% reduction in KE corresponds to only ~5.13% reduction in velocity — this is a **favorite KTU conceptual question**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supersaturated Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Supersaturated Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Supersaturated Flow** (also known as **Metastable Flow** or **Supercooled Vapor Flow**) is a thermodynamic phenomenon occurring in nozzles and diffusers of turbomachines (particularly in **steam turbines** and **refrigeration expansion devices**) where a vapor expands rapidly to such low pressures that its **actual pressure falls below the saturation pressure corresponding to its actual temperature**, yet **condensation of the vapor does not initiate**. The vapor temporarily remains in a gaseous state despite being thermodynamically unstable.

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology:
> **Supersaturation**: A state in which the **actual pressure of the expanding vapor (P) is less than the saturation pressure (P_s)** corresponding to the actual temperature of the vapor. The ratio **P_s / P > 1.0** defines the degree of supersaturation.

Mathematically, the condition is expressed as:

$$\text{Degree of Supersaturation} = \frac{P_s}{P} > 1.0$$

where:
- $P_s$ = Saturation pressure corresponding to actual temperature
- $P$ = Actual (static) pressure of the vapor during expansion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Supersaturated flow is a critical non-equilibrium phenomenon studied under **Nozzle and Diffuser Flow (Vapour Power Cycles)**. Students MUST understand the **Wilson Line**, **Supercooling**, and their impact on **nozzle efficiency and exit kinetic energy**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Explanation

Imagine a crowded classroom where students (water vapor molecules) are highly energetic and bouncing around rapidly. The teacher announces a sudden "exam pressure drop" (rapid expansion in a nozzle). Theoretically, some students should immediately sit down and become calm (condense into liquid droplets). However, due to the **sudden nature** of the announcement and the **absence of nucleation centers** (dust, ions, or pre-existing liquid droplets), the students **continue standing and moving chaotically** for a while before they finally settle down.

This delayed settling is **supersaturation**:
- The classroom is "too empty" (pressure too low) for the energy level (temperature) of the students.
- Equilibrium is **not yet achieved**.
- The system is in a **metastable equilibrium**.

### Real-World Examples:
1. **High-altitude aircraft wing tips** – Vapor suddenly expands over the wing, sometimes creating supersaturated regions.
2. **Steam turbine nozzles** – The very first rows of blades experience steam that has not yet started condensing, even though the pressure is well below saturation.
3. **Cloud chambers in physics** – Supersaturated vapor used to detect charged particles.
4. **Refrigerant expansion valves** – Rapid throttling can lead to metastable states.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range/Value | Units |
|-----------|--------|--------------------|----|
| Degree of Supersaturation | $P_s / P$ | **1.0 to ~2.0** | Dimensionless |
| Degree of Supercooling | $\Delta T = T_s - T$ | **0 to 12 K** (steam) | K or °C |
| Critical Supersaturation (steam) | $P_s / P$ | **≈ 1.58 to 1.84** | Dimensionless |
| Typical Wilson Line Location (Steam) | — | **x ≈ 0.85 to 0.95** dryness fraction | — |
| Condensation Lag (Time) | $t_{lag}$ | **Microseconds to milliseconds** | s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Threshold:** For dry saturated steam, condensation does **not** start automatically when the state line crosses the saturation line. The actual condensation initiates only when the supersaturation ratio exceeds a **critical value** (typically around **1.58 to 1.84** for pure steam), beyond which spontaneous condensation nuclei are formed.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism – Why Does Supersaturation Occur?

The phenomenon arises from a combination of **thermodynamic instability** and **kinetic lag**. The key contributing factors are:

1. **Extreme Rapidity of Expansion**: In high-velocity nozzle flow, residence time in the low-pressure region is on the order of **microseconds (10⁻⁶ s)**. The vapor molecules physically do not have time to organize into liquid droplets.

2. **Absence of Condensation Nuclei**: Condensation in vapors is a **heterogeneous process** requiring:
   - Pre-existing liquid droplets
   - Dust particles, ions, or charged surfaces
   - Sufficient molecular clustering (homogeneous nucleation)
   
   In clean, dry, superheated steam flowing through a polished turbine nozzle, these nuclei are largely **absent**.

3. **Surface Tension Effects**: For a tiny embryonic droplet to form, the **internal pressure must exceed the external pressure** by an amount $\Delta P = 2\sigma / r$ (Laplace pressure). For very small radii, this required overpressure becomes impractically large.

4. **High Molecular Velocities**: At turbine inlet temperatures, molecules have high kinetic energy, delaying molecular clustering.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Supersaturated Expansion

The sequence of events during expansion in a nozzle:

- **Step 1 — Initial State**: Dry saturated or slightly superheated steam enters the nozzle at point A with state $(P_1, T_1)$ where $P_1 = P_{s1}$.
- **Step 2 — Isentropic Expansion (Ideal)**: Theoretically, the steam follows the isentropic line ABC, where at point B it would cross the saturation curve and condensation would begin.
- **Step 3 — Continued Expansion Without Condensation**: In reality, the steam **does not** begin condensing at B. It continues expanding isentropically along **AB'D'** where it lies in the **superheated region** of the Mollier diagram but at pressures *lower* than the saturation pressure for its actual temperature.
- **Step 4 — Wilson Line (Point D)**: Condensation finally begins at the **Wilson Line**, located at a dryness fraction between **0.85 to 0.95**. From D onwards, the flow follows the **Wilson Line** (not the isentropic line) towards the exhaust.
- **Step 5 — Final State**: The exit condition may be wetter than predicted by the equilibrium isentropic expansion.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{Supersaturation Ratio} = P_s / P$ | Ratio of saturation pressure to actual pressure | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{Degree of Supercooling} = T_s - T$ | Temperature lag below saturation | K |
| 3 | $\dfrac{P_s - P}{P_s} = \dfrac{h_s - h}{h_{fg}}$ | Energy balance across the Wilson line (approximate) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\dot{m} = \rho A V = \dfrac{A V}{v}$ | Mass flow rate continuity | kg/s |
| 5 | $\dfrac{dV}{V} = -\dfrac{dA}{A}\left[\dfrac{1 - (V^2/c^2)}{1 + (V^2/c^2)}\right]^{-1}$ | Quasi-one-dimensional nozzle flow | — |
| 6 | $V_{exit} = \sqrt{2(h_1 - h_2)}$ | Kinetic energy from enthalpy drop | m/s |
| 7 | $\eta_{nozzle} = \dfrac{V_{actual}^2}{V_{isentropic}^2} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$ | Nozzle efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $P_s \cdot v_s = R \cdot T_s$ | Ideal gas relation for saturated vapor | — |
| 9 | $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f$ | Latent heat of vaporization | kJ/kg |
| 10 | $r_c = \dfrac{2\sigma T_s}{\rho_l h_{fg} (T_s - T)}$ | Critical droplet radius (Kelvin equation) | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** In equations involving absolute values (e.g., $\vert V^2/c^2 \vert$), use **\vert** or **\mid** in your answer scripts to avoid breaking LaTeX table syntax in digital submissions.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The understanding of supersaturation is **critically important** in the following domains:

1. **Steam Turbine Design**: The first-stage nozzles are often **sized assuming supersaturated flow** in the divergent section, leading to a measurable increase in specific volume and reduction in mass flow rate.
2. **Condenser Sizing**: Since condensation occurs later than predicted, the condenser must handle vapor that arrives slightly **drier** (lower actual quality) than the isentropic prediction.
3. **Refrigeration**: The **Joule-Thomson expansion valve** can produce metastable refrigerant states, affecting evaporator performance.
4. **Wind Tunnels & Supersonic Aerodynamics**: Moisture-laden air may form condensation shocks (e.g., **Prandtl–Meyer expansion fans**), which is precisely the supersaturation phenomenon visualized in **Schlieren photography**.
5. **Cloud Physics & Meteorology**: The Wilson line concept is essentially the laboratory foundation of cloud formation physics.
