The textual description is provided for clarity:** **Single-Stage / Rateau Stage — Outlet Triangle:** - Draw a horizontal baseline of length $U$ (blade velocity vector, to the right). - From the *left* end of the baseline, draw vector $V_1$ (or $V_{w1}$) at a small angle $\alpha_1$ (typically 20°) *above* the horizontal — this is the absolute jet velocity. - From the *right* end of the baseline, draw vector $V_{r2}$ at angle $\beta_2$ *below* the horizontal — this is the *relative* exit velocity. - Close the triangle: $V_1$ (left tip) → drop baseline of $U$ → draw $V_{r2}$ from right tip down-and-left to meet the same point. - The vertical drop from $V_1$ tip to the $U$ baseline gives the whirl component $V_{w2}$ of the exit absolute velocity $V_2$. **Curtis Second-Row Triangle:** - Same construction, but $V_3$ (entering second row) is drawn from the *same* baseline of $U$ (since the moving row has the same $U$). - The fixed guide blade merely redirects $V_2$ to $V_3$ without changing the *kinetic energy* level. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]` > **Define compounding of steam turbines. Name the three principal methods with one identifying feature of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Compounding is the technique of extracting the total available enthalpy drop of steam in a steam turbine in *multiple successive stages* (each consisting of a nozzle + moving blade combination) so as to keep the *blade speed within practical limits* while achieving high overall efficiency. The three principal methods are: 1. **Pressure Compounding (Rateau):** Total pressure (enthalpy) drop is split into *n equal stages*; blade speed is reduced by a factor of $\sqrt{n}$. 2. **Velocity Compounding (Curtis):** Total pressure drop occurs *once* in a single nozzle; the high jet velocity is absorbed in *two or more rows* of moving blades separated by fixed guide blades. 3. **Pressure–Velocity Compounding (Curtis–Rateau):** A *hybrid* design where a Curtis stage is followed by several Rateau stages — used in large utility turbines. *(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three methods with features.)* --- ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]` > **In a Rateau turbine with 5 pressure stages, the total enthalpy drop is 600 kJ/kg. If the nozzle coefficient is 0.96 and the speed ratio is 0.46, determine (i) the jet velocity per stage and (ii) the mean blade speed.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** $$h_{g,\text{per stage}} = \frac{600}{5} = 120 \ \text{kJ/kg}$$ $$V_1 = C_v \cdot 44.72 \cdot \sqrt{h_g} = 0.96 \times 44.72 \times \sqrt{120} = 0.96 \times 44.72 \times 10.954 = 470.4 \ \text{m/s}$$ $$U = 0.46 \times 470.4 = 216.4 \ \text{m/s}$$ *(Award 1 mark for $h_g$, 1 mark for $V_1$, 1 mark for $U$.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]` > **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the principle of **pressure compounding** in a steam turbine. Derive the expression for **optimum blade speed** in an $n$-stage pressure-compounded turbine. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A 4-stage pressure-compounded impulse turbine develops 1500 kW at 3000 rpm. The total isentropic enthalpy drop is 800 kJ/kg. Steam flow rate is 5 kg/s. The blade speed ratio is 0.45, nozzle coefficient is 0.95, and the moving-blade angle is 25°. Determine (i) the mean blade diameter, (ii) the work developed per stage, and (iii) the blade efficiency. Take $\alpha_1 = 0°$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):** In pressure compounding, the total pressure (enthalpy) drop $H_g$ available between the boiler and condenser pressures is *divided into a number of equal stages* $n$. Each stage consists of a *fixed nozzle* followed by a *single row of moving blades*. Steam expands progressively: pressure drops in discrete steps at each nozzle, while the velocity magnitude after each stage remains nearly constant. This is the **Rateau** design. **Velocity Triangle (sketch description for 2 marks):** - Inlet absolute velocity $V_1$ at angle $\alpha_1$ to the blade-speed vector $U$. - Relative inlet velocity $V_{r1}$ at angle $\beta_1$. - Relative exit velocity $V_{r2}$ at angle $\beta_2$ (where $V_{r2} = K \cdot V_{r1}$). - Absolute exit velocity $V_2$ with whirl component $V_{w2}$. **Derivation (2 marks):** Total enthalpy drop is partitioned: $$h_g = \frac{H_g}{n}$$ Jet velocity per stage: $$V_1 = C_v \cdot \sqrt{2 \times 1000 \times h_g} = \frac{C_v \cdot \sqrt{2 \times 1000 \times H_g}}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{V_{1,\text{single}}}{\sqrt{n}}$$ At the optimum speed ratio $\rho = U/V_1 = 1/2$: $$\boxed{\,U_n = \frac{V_1}{2} = \frac{C_v \cdot \sqrt{2000 \cdot H_g}}{2\sqrt{n}}\,}$$ This shows that **blade speed reduces as $\sqrt{n}$**, enabling the use of safe rotational speeds for large enthalpy drops. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Given:** $n = 4$, $H_g = 800$ kJ/kg, $\dot m = 5$ kg/s, $N = 3000$ rpm, $\phi = 0.45$, $C_v = 0.95$, $\beta_2 = 25°$, $\alpha_1 = 0°$. **Step 1 — Enthalpy drop per stage:** $$h_g = \frac{800}{4} = 200 \ \text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2 — Jet velocity per stage:** $$V_1 = 0.95 \times 44.72 \times \sqrt{200} = 0.95 \times 44.72 \times 14.142 = 600.85 \ \text{m/s}$$ **Step 3 — Mean blade speed:** $$U = 0.45 \times 600.85 = 270.4 \ \text{m/s}$$ **Step 4 — Mean blade diameter:** $$U = \frac{\pi D N}{60} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = \frac{60 U}{\pi N} = \frac{60 \times 270.4}{\pi \times 3000} = \frac{16224}{9424.78} = 1.722 \ \text{m}$$ $$\boxed{D = 1.722 \ \text{m}}$$ **Step 5 — Work per stage:** With $\alpha_1 = 0°$ and $K_{b2} \approx 0.9$ (assume standard; if not given, take $K = 1$ for an *ideal* solution): For the *ideal* (no-friction) case, $V_{r1} = V_1 - U$ and $V_{r2} = V_{r1}$ (so $V_{w2} = V_1 - 2U$): $$V_{w1} = V_1 = 600.85 \ \text{m/s}, \quad V_{w2} = 600.85 - 2(270.4) = 60.05 \ \text{m/s}$$ $$W_{\text{stage}} = \frac{U(V_{w1} + V_{w2})}{1000} = \frac{270.4 \times (600.85 + 60.05)}{1000} = \frac{270.4 \times 660.9}{1000} = 178.7 \ \text{kJ/kg}$$ $$\boxed{W_{\text{stage}} = 178.7 \ \text{kJ/kg}}$$ **Step 6 — Blade efficiency:** $$\eta_b = \frac{W_{\text{stage}}}{h_g} = \frac{178.7}{200} = 0.8935 = 89.35\%$$ $$\boxed{\eta_b \approx 89.4\%}$$ --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]` > **(a)** Explain with velocity diagrams the principle of **velocity compounding (Curtis stage)** in a steam turbine. Compare it with pressure compounding in any **four** aspects. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** In a 2-row velocity-compounded impulse stage, the nozzle supplies steam at 30° to the direction of blade motion with a velocity of 600 m/s. The mean blade speed is 180 m/s. The angles of the moving blades are: $\beta_1 = \beta_2 = 30°$ and the blade friction coefficient is 0.9. Determine (i) the work developed per kg of steam, (ii) the axial thrust on the blades, and (iii) the blade efficiency. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Principle (4 marks):** In a **Curtis stage**, the entire pressure drop of the steam occurs in a *single nozzle*. The high-velocity jet then impinges on the *first row of moving blades*, where a part of its kinetic energy is converted into mechanical work. The steam, now at a lower absolute velocity, is then *redirected by a row of fixed guide blades* (reversing blades) into the *second row of moving blades*, where the remaining kinetic energy is extracted. The fixed guide blades do not cause any pressure drop — they only change the direction of flow. The pressure throughout the Curtis stage (after the main nozzle) remains essentially constant. **Velocity Triangle — First Row (sketch description):** - Inlet: $V_1$ at $\alpha_1 = 30°$ (given in part b); $V_{r1} = V_1\cos\alpha_1 - U$; the triangle is closed with the baseline $U$. - Outlet: $V_{r2} = K \cdot V_{r1}$; $V_{w2} = V_{r2}\cos\beta_2 - U$. **Velocity Triangle — Second Row (sketch description):** - The fixed guide blade redirects $V_2$ to give $V_3$ (essentially at the same magnitude if the guide blade is frictionless, else $V_3 = K_f \cdot V_2$). - The second-row inlet triangle is similar to the first-row inlet triangle, but with a smaller $V_3$. **Comparison Table (3 marks for 4 well-explained points):** | Aspect | Pressure Compounding | Velocity Compounding | |--------|----------------------|----------------------| | Pressure drop location | Distributed over $n$ nozzles | Single nozzle only | | Velocity at moving-blade inlet | Same in every stage | Decreases from row to row | | Number of moving rows for same output | $n$ rows of *equal* work | Only 2 (or 3) rows | | Blade speed reduction | $U_1/\sqrt{n}$ | $U_1/\sqrt{2}$ (for 2 rows) | | Specific work per stage | Low | High | | Stage efficiency | Higher (≈ 88–92%) | Lower (≈ 70–80%) | | Use | Utility HP/IP | Marine, governing | #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Given:** $V_1 = 600$ m/s, $\alpha_1 = 30°$, $U = 180$ m/s, $\beta_1 = \beta_2 = 30°$, $K = 0.9$. **Step 1 — Velocity Components at First-Row Inlet:** $$V_{w1} = V_1 \cos\alpha_1 = 600 \times \cos 30° = 600 \times 0.8660 = 519.6 \ \text{m/s}$$ $$V_{f1} = V_1 \sin\alpha_1 = 600 \times \sin 30° = 600 \times 0.5 = 300 \ \text{m/s}$$ $$V_{r1}^2 = V_1^2 + U^2 - 2V_1 U \cos\alpha_1 = 600^2 + 180^2 - 2 \times 600 \times 180 \times 0.8660$$ $$V_{r1}^2 = 360000 + 32400 - 187056 = 205344 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{r1} = 453.15 \ \text{m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Velocity Components at First-Row Exit:** $$V_{r2} = K \cdot V_{r1} = 0.9 \times 453.15 = 407.84 \ \text{m/s}$$ $$V_{w2} = V_{r2}\cos\beta_2 - U = 407.84 \times \cos 30° - 180 = 407.84 \times 0.8660 - 180 = 353.19 - 180 = 173.19 \ \text{m/s}$$ **Step 3 — Absolute Velocity at First-Row Exit:** $$V_{f2} = V_{r2}\sin\beta_2 = 407.84 \times \sin 30° = 407.84 \times 0.5 = 203.92 \ \text{m/s}$$ $$V_2^2 = V_{w2}^2 + V_{f2}^2 = 173.19^2 + 203.92^2 = 29995 + 41583 = 71578 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_2 = 267.5 \ \text{m/s}$$ **Step 4 — Second-Row Inlet (assuming ideal fixed guide, $K_f = 1$):** $$V_3 = V_2 = 267.5 \ \text{m/s}, \quad V_{w3} \approx V_3 = 267.5 \ \text{m/s} \quad (\text{at } \alpha_2 = 0°)$$ **Step 5 — Work per kg:** $$W = \frac{U}{1000}\left(V_{w1} + V_{w2} + V_{w3}\right) = \frac{180}{1000}\left(519.6 + 173.19 + 267.5\right)$$ $$W = 0.18 \times 960.29 = 172.85 \ \text{kJ/kg}$$ $$\boxed{W \approx 172.9 \ \text{kJ/kg}}$$ **Step 6 — Axial Thrust:** The axial thrust is the net axial force on the blades, computed from the rate of change of axial momentum per kg/s. The axial velocity components are $V_{f1}$ and $V_{f2}$: $$F_a = \dot m (V_{f1} - V_{f2}) \quad \text{(for first row; per kg, } F_a/\dot m = V_{f1} - V_{f2}\text{)}$$ For the *first* moving row, the axial force per kg/s is: $$\frac{F_a}{\dot m} = V_{f1} + V_{f2} = 300 + 203.92 = 503.92 \ \text{N per kg/s}$$ If the problem expects *total* for both rows with symmetric exit (i.e., $V_{f2}$ in row 1 = $V_{f1}$ in row 2, then second row exits at $V_{f4}$ in the same direction): $$F_{a,\text{total}} = \dot m \left[(V_{f1} + V_{f2}) + (V_{f3} + V_{f4})\right]$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU mark-allocation note:** The question asks for "axial thrust on the blades" — if no mass flow is given, the convention is to give the force per kg/s of steam. The complete first-row axial thrust per kg/s is: > $$F_a/\dot m = (V_{f1} - V_{f4}) + (V_{f3} - V_{f2}) \ \text{N per kg/s}$$ > With the *full Curtis* analysis (assuming second row exit axial component equals the second row inlet axial component by symmetry), the answer reduces to $V_{f1} + V_{f2} = 503.9$ N/(kg/s). **Step 7 — Blade Efficiency:** The kinetic energy input is: $$\text{KE}_{\text{input}} = \frac{V_1^2}{2000} = \frac{600^2}{2000} = 180 \ \text{kJ/kg}$$ $$\eta_b = \frac{W}{\text{KE}_{\text{input}}} = \frac{172.85}{180} = 0.9603 = 96.03\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** Two recurring mistakes in Curtis-stage problems: > 1. **Forgetting the third whirl term** $V_{w3}$ in the work expression. Many students write $W = U(V_{w1} + V_{w2})/1000$, which is the *single-stage* formula and gives a 3–4 mark penalty. > 2. **Using the wrong reference for efficiency.** The denominator is the *kinetic energy supplied by the nozzle* ($V_1^2 / 2000$ in kJ/kg), *not* the total available enthalpy $H_g$. The latter is used in *stage* efficiency, not *blade* efficiency. > 3. **Sign of $V_{w2}$**: If $K \cos\beta_2 < U/V_{r1}$ (a very slow-running wheel), $V_{w2}$ becomes *negative*, which physically means the steam exits with a *reverse* whirl — this is the start of *reaction* behaviour, an entirely different regime. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision Punchlist — Compounding of Turbines** - ☐ **Definition:** Compounding = multi-stage extraction of total enthalpy drop to keep blade speed safe. - ☐ **Three methods:** Pressure (Rateau), Velocity (Curtis), Pressure–Velocity (Curtis–Rateau). - ☐ **Pressure compounding:** $n$ stages, each absorbs $H_g/n$; $U_n = U_1/\sqrt{n}$; identical velocity triangles per stage. - ☐ **Velocity compounding (2-row):** Single nozzle, two moving rows separated by fixed guide blade; $U = U_1/\sqrt{2}$. - ☐ **Curtis work formula:** $W = U(V_{w1} + V_{w2} + V_{w3})/1000$ — *three* whirl terms, *not two*. - ☐ **Optimum speed ratio (ideal):** $\rho = U/V_1 = 1/2$ for maximum blade efficiency. - ☐ **Maximum blade efficiency (ideal):** $\eta_{b,\max} = (1 + K\cos\beta_2)/2$. - ☐ **Reheat factor:** ≥ 1, applicable to multi-stage turbines only; typical value **1.03–1.08**. - ☐ **Typical blade angles:** Nozzle $\alpha_1 = 20°$, moving-blade $\beta_1 = \beta_2 = 20°$–$30°$ (symmetric blades). - ☐ **Standard friction coefficients:** $K_{b1}, K_{b2}, K_f = 0.85$–$0.95$ (use 0.9 if not given). - ☐ **Numerical constants:** $g = 9.81$ m/s²; $V_1 = 44.72\sqrt{h_g}$ in m/s when $h_g$ in kJ/kg. - ☐ **Power equation:** $P = \dot m \times W_{\text{stage}} \times n$ in kW (for total output). - ☐ **Curtis use case:** Governing stages, marine turbines, low-rpm high-power units. - ☐ **Rateau use case:** Utility HP/IP sections, high-efficiency large central power stations. - ☐ **Curtis–Rateau use case:** 500 MW+ supercritical units; combines high specific output with high efficiency. - ☐ **Common mistake to avoid:** Confusing *stage efficiency* ($\eta_{\text{stage}} = \eta_b \cdot \eta_N$) with *blade efficiency* ($\eta_b = W_{\text{stage}}/h_g$). - ☐ **Common mistake to avoid:** Using $H_g$ (total drop) as the denominator for *blade* efficiency — use $h_g$ (per-stage drop) for Rateau; use $V_1^2/2000$ (nozzle KE) for Curtis. - ☐ **Velocity triangle rule:** The relative velocity *entering* the moving blade is $V_{r1}$, and the *relative velocity leaving* is $V_{r2} = K \cdot V_{r1}$ — these two are on the *same* side of the triangle (the side opposite to $V_1$ and $V_2$).

topics.title: Velocity Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Velocity Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Velocity Diagrams in Turbomachinery

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> *Velocity diagrams (or velocity triangles) are graphical vector representations of the absolute and relative velocities of the working fluid (steam/gas) as it enters and leaves the moving blades of a turbomachine. They depict the kinematic interaction between the fluid jet and the rotating blade, enabling the analytical determination of blade efficiency, work output, and energy transfer.*

### 1.1 The Three Fundamental Velocity Vectors

A complete velocity diagram for a single turbomachine stage is constructed from **three principal vectors**:

1. **Absolute Velocity (V)** — The velocity of the fluid (steam jet) with respect to a **stationary (earth-fixed) reference frame**. Denoted as $V_1$ at inlet and $V_2$ at outlet.
2. **Blade Velocity (U)** — The linear velocity of the rotating blade at the mean radius, equal to $\dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$, where $D$ is the mean blade diameter and $N$ is the rotational speed in **rpm**.
3. **Relative Velocity (V_r)** — The velocity of the fluid **relative to the moving blade**. Obtained by vector subtraction: $V_r = V - U$.

### 1.2 The Two Critical Angles

| Symbol | Angle | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| $\alpha$ | **Nozzle (Flow) Angle** | Angle made by the absolute velocity $V$ with the plane of blade motion (tangential direction) |
| $\beta$ | **Blade Angle** | Angle made by the relative velocity $V_r$ with the plane of blade motion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Convention:** Subscript **1** always denotes **inlet** conditions; subscript **2** denotes **outlet** conditions. Angles are measured from the direction of blade motion. Always maintain consistency — every KTU answer script that mixes up $\alpha$ and $\beta$ loses a guaranteed 2 marks.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spinning Car Wash Brush"

Imagine you are holding a garden hose and spraying water onto a spinning car-wash brush (the blades).

* If you stand still, the water hits the brush with velocity $V$ (the **absolute velocity**).
* The brush bristles are themselves moving sideways with velocity $U$ (the **blade velocity**).
* From the brush's own frame of reference, the water approaches it with a *combined* velocity that is the vector difference — this is the **relative velocity** $V_r$.
* After the water bounces off, it leaves the brush at some other velocity, again as a combination of the blade's motion and the water's own motion.

The velocity triangle is simply a **geometry paper-and-pencil way** of doing exactly this vector arithmetic — and it tells the engineer how much kinetic energy got transferred from steam to blade (i.e., **work done**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Units:**
> * Velocity: **m/s** (SI)
> * Rotational speed $N$: **rpm** (revolutions per minute)
> * Blade diameter $D$: **m**
> * Blade velocity $U = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ in **m/s**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Constructing an Inlet Velocity Triangle

The inlet triangle is built when steam leaves the **nozzle** and is about to strike the **moving blade**. Follow this strict order:

1. Draw a **horizontal reference line** representing the direction of blade motion.
2. From the origin, draw the **blade velocity vector $U_1$** along this horizontal line.
3. From the same origin, draw the **absolute velocity $V_1$** at an angle $\alpha_1$ (the nozzle angle) above the horizontal.
4. Complete the triangle by drawing $V_{r1}$ from the **tip of $U_1$** to the **tip of $V_1$**. This closing side represents $V_{r1}$, and the angle it makes with the reversed direction of $U_1$ is $\beta_1$ (the blade inlet angle).

> [!TIP]
> **Why we close the triangle this way:** The vector equation $V_1 = U_1 + V_{r1}$ must be satisfied. Starting at the origin with $U_1$ then adding $V_{r1}$ must land exactly at the tip of $V_1$ — this is the **triangle rule of vector addition**.

## 2.2 Outlet Velocity Triangle

The outlet triangle is constructed analogously:

1. From a new origin, draw $U_2$ horizontally (same magnitude if mean diameter is unchanged, $U_1 = U_2$).
2. The relative velocity $V_{r2}$ leaves the blade at the **blade outlet angle $\beta_2$**, so draw it at angle $\beta_2$ from the (reversed) blade motion direction.
3. The closing vector from the **tip of $V_{r2}$** to the **origin** is the absolute outlet velocity $V_2$, making angle $\alpha_2$ with the horizontal.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master every equation below — at least one of these appears in every KTU ESE Part B question on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | Blade velocity from diameter and rpm |
| 2 | $V_1 = U_1 + V_{r1}$ | Inlet velocity triangle (vector form) |
| 3 | $V_2 = U_2 + V_{r2}$ | Outlet velocity triangle (vector form) |
| 4 | $V_{w1} = V_1 \cos \alpha_1$ | Inlet **whirl** component (tangential to motion) |
| 5 | $V_{w2} = V_2 \cos \alpha_2$ | Outlet **whirl** component |
| 6 | $V_{f1} = V_1 \sin \alpha_1$ | Inlet **flow** component (axial/radial) |
| 7 | $V_{f2} = V_2 \sin \alpha_2$ | Outlet **flow** component |
| 8 | $V_{r1} \cos \beta_1 = V_1 \cos \alpha_1 - U_1$ | Resolving along blade motion direction |
| 9 | $V_{r1} \sin \beta_1 = V_1 \sin \alpha_1$ | Resolving perpendicular to blade motion |
| 10 | $W = \dfrac{U \, (V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})}{g}$ | **Work done per kg of steam** (Euler's Turbine Equation) |
| 11 | $\eta_{\text{blade}} = \dfrac{2 \, U \, (V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})}{V_1^{\,2}}$ | Blade (kinematic) efficiency |
| 12 | $\eta_{\text{diagram}} = \dfrac{\text{Ideal work}}{\text{Kinetic energy at inlet}} = \dfrac{2 \, U \, V_{w1}}{V_1^{\,2}}$ | Diagram efficiency (for 0° outlet angle, ideal case) |
| 13 | $\phi = \dfrac{V_f}{U}$ | Flow coefficient (non-dimensional) |
| 14 | $\psi = \dfrac{2 \, U \, V_{w1}}{V_1^{\,2}}$ | Work (loading) coefficient |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Convention for Work Equation:** Use **'+'** when $\alpha_2 < 90°$ (i.e., $V_{w2}$ is in the **same direction** as $U$ — outlet whirl is *positive*). Use **'−'** when $\alpha_2 > 90°$ (i.e., $V_{w2}$ is **opposite** to $U$ — outlet whirl is *negative*). Getting this sign wrong is the single most common 2-mark deduction in KTU scripts.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Velocity diagrams are not just academic drawings. They are used by:

* **Steam turbine designers (BHEL, Siemens, GE)** to optimize blade profiles for maximum power extraction.
* **Aircraft engine (turbojet/turboprop) designers** to compute thrust-specific fuel consumption.
* **Hydraulic turbine engineers** to design Francis, Kaplan, and Pelton buckets.
* **Compressor aerodynamicists** to size axial-flow and centrifugal compressor stages.

> [!NOTE]
> Without an accurate velocity diagram, the engineer cannot predict whether the steam will hit the moving blade at its designed **stagnation point**, whether it will enter the blade smoothly without shock, or whether the leaving velocity will carry away useful energy as **kinetic energy leaving loss**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Euler's Turbomachine Equation from Velocity Triangles

> **Goal:** Derive $W = \dfrac{U (V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})}{g}$ rigorously from first principles.

### Step 1 — Apply Newton's Second Law in the Tangential Direction

For a mass $m$ of steam entering tangentially with velocity $V_{w1}$ and leaving with velocity $V_{w2}$, the change in angular momentum about the axis of rotation must equal the torque $T$ imparted to the blades over time $t$.

$$
T = \dfrac{m}{t} \left( V_{w1} \, r_1 \, \pm \, V_{w2} \, r_2 \right)
$$

where $r_1$ and $r_2$ are the radii at inlet and outlet (mean radii for the blade row).

### Step 2 — Express Torque in Terms of Work Done

Work done per unit time (power) is torque multiplied by angular velocity $\omega$:

$$
P = T \cdot \omega
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $r \omega = U$

Since the linear blade velocity at any radius $r$ is $U = r \omega$, we substitute $r_1 \omega = U_1$ and $r_2 \omega = U_2$. For a stage where the mean diameter is constant, $U_1 = U_2 = U$.

$$
P = \dfrac{m}{t} \, U \, \left( V_{w1} \, \pm \, V_{w2} \right)
$$

### Step 4 — Work Done per kg of Steam

Since power $P$ is energy per unit time, and mass flow rate is $m/t$, the **specific work** (work per kg) is:

$$
W = \dfrac{P}{\dot{m}} = U \left( V_{w1} \pm V_{w2} \right)
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Mechanical Work in kgf·m/kg

In engineering (gravitational) units, we divide by $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$
W = \dfrac{U \, (V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})}{g} \quad \text{[kgf·m/kg of steam]}
$$

This is **Euler's Turbomachine Equation**, and the velocity triangle is its geometric foundation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Blade Efficiency from Velocity Diagrams

### Step 1 — Define Blade Efficiency

Blade efficiency is the ratio of **work done on the blade** to the **kinetic energy supplied by the nozzle**:

$$
\eta_{\text{blade}} = \dfrac{\text{Work done per kg}}{\text{KE supplied per kg}} = \dfrac{U(V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})/g}{\dfrac{V_1^{\,2}}{2g}}
$$

### Step 2 — Simplify

The factor $g$ cancels:

$$
\eta_{\text{blade}} = \dfrac{2 \, U \, (V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})}{V_1^{\,2}}
$$

### Step 3 — Maximum Blade Efficiency Condition

To maximize $\eta_{\text{blade}}$, we set $\dfrac{d\eta}{dU} = 0$. After differentiation (assume $\alpha_2 = 90°$ for the **symmetrical (ideal) blade** case), we obtain:

$$
U_{\text{opt}} = \dfrac{V_1 \cos \alpha_1}{2} = \dfrac{V_{w1}}{2}
$$

The corresponding **maximum blade efficiency** is:

$$
\eta_{\text{blade, max}} = \cos^2 \alpha_1
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the famous result: **maximum blade efficiency depends only on the nozzle angle $\alpha_1$** and is independent of the blade speed. KTU loves to ask for the derivation of this condition in 14-mark problems.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Key Geometric Relations for Single-Stage Impulse Turbine

Below is the complete set of relations you can read directly off a velocity triangle. All are obtained by resolving vectors along the tangential (horizontal) and axial (vertical) directions.

| Quantity | Tangential Component | Axial Component | Magnitude (Pythagoras) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Absolute inlet velocity $V_1$ | $V_1 \cos \alpha_1$ | $V_1 \sin \alpha_1$ | $\sqrt{V_{w1}^{\,2} + V_{f1}^{\,2}}$ |
| Absolute outlet velocity $V_2$ | $V_2 \cos \alpha_2$ | $V_2 \sin \alpha_2$ | $\sqrt{V_{w2}^{\,2} + V_{f2}^{\,2}}$ |
| Relative inlet velocity $V_{r1}$ | $V_{r1} \cos \beta_1$ | $V_{r1} \sin \beta_1$ | $\sqrt{(V_{w1}-U)^2 + V_{f1}^{\,2}}$ |
| Relative outlet velocity $V_{r2}$ | $V_{r2} \cos \beta_2$ | $V_{r2} \sin \beta_2$ | $\sqrt{(U \pm V_{w2})^2 + V_{f2}^{\,2}}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the symbol $\pm$ inside a magnitude expression must be interpreted as the **absolute value** since we are taking a square root of a squared term. For board answers, write $\vert U - V_{w2} \vert$ or $\vert U + V_{w2} \vert$ explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Functional Flow of Velocity Diagram Analysis

The diagram below maps the **logical workflow** a KTU student must follow when solving any velocity-triangle problem, from the given data to the final efficiency.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[StepA: Read Given Data] --> B[StepB: Compute Blade Velocity U = piDN slash 60]
    B --> C[StepC: Resolve V1 into Whirl Vw1 and Flow Vf1]
    C --> D[StepD: Construct Inlet Velocity Triangle]
    D --> E[StepE: Apply Blade Angle Constraint at Inlet beta1]
    E --> F[StepF: Solve for Relative Velocity Vr1 and Vr2]
    F --> G[StepG: Construct Outlet Velocity Triangle]
    G --> H{DecisionH: Is alpha2 acute or obtuse}
    H -->|alpha2 less than 90| I[StepI: Use PLUS sign in Work Equation]
    H -->|alpha2 greater than 90| J[StepJ: Use MINUS sign in Work Equation]
    I --> K[StepK: Compute Work Done per kg]
    J --> K
    K --> L[StepL: Compute Blade Efficiency eta blade]
    L --> M[StepM: State Final Answer with Units]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Inlet and Outlet Triangle Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INLET_TRI
        X1[Origin Point O] --> X2[Vector U1 horizontal]
        X1 --> X3[Vector V1 at angle alpha1]
        X2 --> X4[Vector Vr1 closing the triangle]
        X3 --> X4
    end
    subgraph OUTLET_TRI
        Y1[Origin Point P] --> Y2[Vector U2 horizontal]
        Y1 --> Y3[Vector Vr2 at angle beta2]
        Y2 --> Y4[Vector V2 closing the triangle]
        Y3 --> Y4
    end
    INLET_TRI --> OUTLET_TRI
```

## 4.3 Description of the Physical Drawing (For Board Exam Sketches)

For your KTU answer-script, sketch the **two triangles side-by-side**, sharing the same horizontal baseline. Use the following conventions:

* **Solid arrows** for absolute velocities $V_1$, $V_2$.
* **Dashed arrows** for relative velocities $V_{r1}$, $V_{r2}$.
* **Double-line arrows** for blade velocity $U$.
* Label **all six angles**: $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \beta_1, \beta_2$ — and indicate the **blade exit angle** $\theta$ if different from $\beta_2$.
* Show the **direction of rotation** with a curved arrow so the examiner can verify your sign convention.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** A neatly drawn velocity triangle with all angles labeled **earns an automatic 1–2 marks** for presentation, even before the calculation is checked. Do not skip it.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define velocity triangle and list the velocities involved in it.**

**Model Answer:**
A velocity triangle is a vector diagram showing the absolute, relative, and blade velocities of a fluid as it enters and leaves a turbomachine blade row.

The velocities involved are:
* $V$ — absolute velocity of the fluid (m/s).
* $U$ — linear velocity of the blade (m/s).
* $V_r$ — relative velocity of the fluid with respect to the moving blade (m/s).

The vector relation is $V = U + V_r$.

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State Euler's turbomachine equation. What does the term $V_w$ represent?**

**Model Answer:**
Euler's turbomachine equation (per kg of fluid per second) is:

$$
W = \dfrac{U \, (V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})}{g} \quad \text{kgf·m/kg}
$$

$V_w$ is the **whirl component** of the absolute velocity, i.e., the component of $V$ in the **direction of blade motion**. For inlet, $V_{w1} = V_1 \cos \alpha_1$; for outlet, $V_{w2} = V_2 \cos \alpha_2$.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU ESE, every Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers an internal choice. Below, both alternatives are worked out fully.

### **Question B1 (Choice A)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**An impulse turbine receives steam at a velocity of 600 m/s from the nozzle. The nozzle angle is 20°. The blade speed is 200 m/s. The blade friction factor is 0.9 and the blades are symmetrical. Find:**
**(a) The relative velocity at inlet and outlet of the blade. (7 marks)**
**(b) The work done per kg of steam and the blade efficiency. (7 marks)**

#### **Solution:**

**Given Data:**
$V_1 = 600$ m/s, $\alpha_1 = 20°$, $U = 200$ m/s, friction factor $k = 0.9$, symmetrical blades ($\beta_1 = \beta_2$, $\alpha_2 = 90°$).

**(a) Relative Velocity Calculation:**

Inlet whirl component:
$$
V_{w1} = V_1 \cos \alpha_1 = 600 \times \cos 20° = 600 \times 0.9397 = 563.82 \text{ m/s}
$$

Inlet flow component:
$$
V_{f1} = V_1 \sin \alpha_1 = 600 \times \sin 20° = 600 \times 0.3420 = 205.21 \text{ m/s}
$$

Relative velocity at inlet — tangential component:
$$
V_{w1} - U = 563.82 - 200 = 363.82 \text{ m/s}
$$

Magnitude of relative velocity at inlet:
$$
V_{r1} = \sqrt{(V_{w1} - U)^2 + V_{f1}^{\,2}} = \sqrt{(363.82)^2 + (205.21)^2}
$$

$$
V_{r1} = \sqrt{132{,}365 + 42{,}111} = \sqrt{174{,}476} = 417.7 \text{ m/s}
$$

Since the blades are symmetrical, $V_{r2} = k \cdot V_{r1}$:
$$
V_{r2} = 0.9 \times 417.7 = 375.9 \text{ m/s}
$$

For symmetrical blades with $\alpha_2 = 90°$, the flow component at outlet equals the flow component at inlet:
$$
V_{f2} = V_{f1} = 205.21 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(b) Work Done and Blade Efficiency:**

Since $\alpha_2 = 90°$, we have $V_{w2} = 0$, so we use the **minus sign** (actually becomes $V_{w2}=0$):

$$
W = \dfrac{U \cdot V_{w1}}{g} = \dfrac{200 \times 563.82}{9.81} = \dfrac{112{,}764}{9.81}
$$

$$
W = 11{,}494 \text{ kgf·m/kg} \approx 11{,}494 \times 9.81 = 112{,}758 \text{ J/kg} = 112.76 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

Blade efficiency:
$$
\eta_{\text{blade}} = \dfrac{2 \, U \, V_{w1}}{V_1^{\,2}} = \dfrac{2 \times 200 \times 563.82}{(600)^2} = \dfrac{225{,}528}{360{,}000} = 0.6266
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\text{blade}} = 62.66\%}
$$

---

### **Question B2 (Choice B)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a) With the help of a velocity triangle, derive an expression for the work done per kg of steam in an impulse turbine. (7 marks)**
**(b) A single-stage impulse turbine has a mean blade speed of 175 m/s and a nozzle angle of 30°. The absolute velocity of steam leaving the nozzle is 600 m/s. If the velocity of steam leaving the blade is in the axial direction (i.e., $\alpha_2 = 90°$), determine: (i) the blade angles at inlet and outlet, (ii) the work done per kg of steam, and (iii) the blade efficiency. (7 marks)**

#### **Solution:**

**(a) Derivation** — Already done exhaustively in Section 3.1 above. Briefly:
* Apply Newton's law: tangential force $F_t = \dot{m}(V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})$.
* Multiply by $U$ to get power; divide by $\dot{m}$ to get specific work.
* Final result:

$$
W = \dfrac{U(V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})}{g}
$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $U = 175$ m/s, $\alpha_1 = 30°$, $V_1 = 600$ m/s, $\alpha_2 = 90°$.

**(i) Blade angles:**

Inlet whirl:
$$
V_{w1} = V_1 \cos \alpha_1 = 600 \times \cos 30° = 600 \times 0.8660 = 519.6 \text{ m/s}
$$

Inlet flow:
$$
V_{f1} = V_1 \sin \alpha_1 = 600 \times \sin 30° = 600 \times 0.5 = 300 \text{ m/s}
$$

Inlet blade angle $\beta_1$:
$$
\tan \beta_1 = \dfrac{V_{f1}}{V_{w1} - U} = \dfrac{300}{519.6 - 175} = \dfrac{300}{344.6}
$$

$$
\beta_1 = \tan^{-1}(0.8706) = 41.04°
$$

Outlet: since $\alpha_2 = 90°$, the relative velocity makes an angle $\beta_2$ such that the axial component equals $V_{f2} = V_{f1} = 300$ m/s (continuity), and the tangential component is $-U = -175$ m/s (in reversed direction):

$$
\tan \beta_2 = \dfrac{300}{175} = 1.714
$$

$$
\beta_2 = \tan^{-1}(1.714) = 59.74°
$$

**(ii) Work Done:**
Since $\alpha_2 = 90°$, $V_{w2} = 0$:

$$
W = \dfrac{U \cdot V_{w1}}{g} = \dfrac{175 \times 519.6}{9.81} = \dfrac{90{,}930}{9.81} = 9269 \text{ kgf·m/kg}
$$

$$
W = 9269 \times 9.81 = 90{,}929 \text{ J/kg} = 90.93 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**(iii) Blade Efficiency:**
$$
\eta_{\text{blade}} = \dfrac{2 \, U \, V_{w1}}{V_1^{\,2}} = \dfrac{2 \times 175 \times 519.6}{360{,}000} = \dfrac{181{,}860}{360{,}000} = 0.5052
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\text{blade}} = 50.52\%}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Marks-Loss Traps in Velocity Diagram Problems:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in Euler's equation** — forgetting that $V_{w2}$ becomes *negative* when $\alpha_2 > 90°$. Use a clear sketch to decide.
> 2. **Inlet vs. outlet blade diameter** — if inlet and outlet mean diameters differ (rare but possible in Curtis stage), $U_1 \neq U_2$. KTU tests this every 2–3 years.
> 3. **Confusing $V_2$ with $V_{r2}$** — the absolute outlet velocity is **not** the relative outlet velocity. Both must be drawn and labelled distinctly.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert to kgf·m/kg** — final answer must be in engineering units when $g$ is used. Numerical answer in J/kg without the division by $g$ loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Not drawing the diagram** — every 14-mark question mandates the velocity triangle. Skipping the diagram is an automatic 2-mark deduction per KTU valuation key.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **Velocity triangle** = graphical vector addition of $V$, $U$, and $V_r$.
> - **Subscript 1 = inlet; subscript 2 = outlet.** Always.
> - **Blade velocity** $U = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ in m/s.
> - **Whirl component** $V_w = V \cos \alpha$ (tangential); **Flow component** $V_f = V \sin \alpha$ (axial).
> - **Relative velocity magnitude** $V_r = \sqrt{(V_w \pm U)^2 + V_f^{\,2}}$.
> - **Euler's equation** $W = \dfrac{U(V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})}{g}$ — **plus** if $\alpha_2 < 90°$, **minus** if $\alpha_2 > 90°$.
> - **Blade efficiency** $\eta_{\text{blade}} = \dfrac{2 U (V_{w1} \pm V_{w2})}{V_1^{\,2}}$.
> - **Maximum blade efficiency** = $\cos^2 \alpha_1$, achieved at $U = \dfrac{V_1 \cos \alpha_1}{2}$.
> - **Symmetrical blade assumption** → $\beta_1 = \beta_2$, $\alpha_2 = 90°$, $V_{r2} = k \cdot V_{r1}$ (where $k$ = friction factor, typically 0.85–0.95).
> - **Always draw the diagram** before starting any calculation — this is your marks insurance.
> - **Units check**: SI velocities in m/s, work in J/kg (SI) or kgf·m/kg (engineering); efficiency is dimensionless in % or fraction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Work Done
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Work Done

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In engineering thermodynamics, **Work Done (W)** is defined as the *energy transferred* across the boundary of a closed system to its surroundings by the action of a pressure difference or a force acting through a distance. It is a **path function** (its magnitude depends on the process path followed between the initial and final states) and a **mode of energy transfer** that results from a generalized force moving through a generalized displacement.

For a **quasi-static (reversible) process** in a closed system (also called the *simple compressible system*), the infinitesimal boundary work is given by the fundamental differential form:

$$dW = P \, dV$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Sign Convention (Clausius / Engineering Convention):**
> - **Work done BY the system on the surroundings = POSITIVE** (e.g., gas expanding and pushing a piston outward).
> - **Work done ON the system by the surroundings = NEGATIVE** (e.g., surroundings compressing the gas).
> - The symbol **W** in this entire module denotes work done **BY** the system unless explicitly stated otherwise.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a bicycle pump with its nozzle sealed — the air inside is trapped. When you push the pump handle inward, **you** are doing work **on** the gas (compressing it). The gas resists by pushing back on the piston. Now flip the picture: if the nozzle were opened and the gas were allowed to rush out, the expanding gas would push the piston **outward** — the gas itself is doing work **on** the piston. 

The "boundary" of the gas is the moving piston face. As that face moves, the gas transfers energy across it. That transferred energy is what we call **thermodynamic work**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Work vs. Shaft Work vs. Flow Work — three different things!**
> 1. **Boundary Work (PdV work):** Energy transfer due to volume change against a moving boundary (the piston above).
> 2. **Shaft Work (Wₛ):** Energy transfer via a rotating shaft (think turbine, compressor, propeller shaft).
> 3. **Flow Work (Flow Energy):** Energy required to push a fluid *into* or *out of* an open system control volume. Equals **P·v** per unit mass.
>
> For the rest of this module, the unqualified term "work done" refers specifically to **boundary / displacement work** in **closed systems**.

## 1.3 Units, Symbols & Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SI Units:** Joule (**J**) or kilojoule (**kJ**).
> **English Units:** Btu (1 Btu ≈ **1055 J**).
> **Specific Work (w):** kJ/kg (work per unit mass of working fluid).
> **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** **101.325 kPa** (≈ 1 bar = **100 kPa**).
> **Universal Gas Constant:** **R̄ = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Derivation Logic — The "Why" Behind $dW = P\,dV$

Consider a frictionless piston–cylinder arrangement holding a gas at pressure **P**. Let the piston face have area **A**. The force exerted by the gas on the piston is:

$$F = P \times A$$

If the piston moves outward by an infinitesimal distance **dx**, the work done by the gas is:

$$dW = F \cdot dx = (P \cdot A) \cdot dx$$

But the change in volume is $dV = A \cdot dx$, therefore:

$$dW = P \, dV$$

This is the **single most important equation** in this entire module. For any *finite* (reversible) process between states 1 and 2, the total work is the area under the process curve on a **P–V diagram**:

$$W_{1-2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

## 2.2 Why Work is a Path Function

Because the integral $\int P\,dV$ evaluates the *area* under a specific curve, two different paths connecting the same states 1 and 2 will sweep out *different areas* and therefore yield *different work values*. Heat **Q** shares this same path-dependence, which is why both W and Q are **not** properties of a system — unlike temperature, pressure, and volume, which are.

> [!NOTE]
> **Properties** depend only on the state. **Path functions** (W, Q) depend on how the system got there.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Closed System, Ideal Gas)

| # | Process | Path Relation | Work Done W (kJ) | Special Case |
|---|---------|---------------|------------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Constant Volume (Isochoric)** | $V = \text{const}$ | $W = 0$ | No boundary movement |
| 2 | **Constant Pressure (Isobaric)** | $P = \text{const}$ | $W = P(V_2 - V_1)$ | Rectangle on P–V diagram |
| 3 | **Isothermal (Ideal Gas)** | $PV = \text{const}$ | $W = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = mRT \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$ | Ln of volume ratio |
| 4 | **Polytropic** | $PV^n = \text{const}$ | $W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n - 1}$ | Valid for $n \neq 1$ |
| 5 | **Adiabatic (Reversible)** | $PV^\gamma = \text{const}$ | $W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{\gamma - 1} = \dfrac{mR(T_1 - T_2)}{\gamma - 1}$ | Set $n = \gamma$ in polytropic |
| 6 | **Free Expansion** | $P_{\text{ext}} = 0$ | $W = 0$ | No resistance, no work |

> **Legend:** $\gamma = C_p / C_v$ (ratio of specific heats), $n$ = polytropic index, $m$ = mass in kg, $R$ = specific gas constant in kJ/(kg·K).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Boundary work calculations are at the heart of:
- **Internal Combustion Engines** — Otto and Diesel cycle work output.
- **Steam Power Plants** — Rankine cycle turbine work and pump work.
- **Refrigeration & HVAC** — Vapour compression cycle compressor work.
- **Pneumatic & Hydraulic Systems** — Cylinder work for lifting and actuation.

Without an accurate handle on boundary work, an engineer cannot size a compressor, predict engine torque, or estimate fuel economy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Work Done in an Isothermal Process (Ideal Gas)

**Starting Point — Ideal Gas Law & Isothermal Constraint**

For an ideal gas, the equation of state is $PV = mRT$. An isothermal process requires $T = \text{const}$, so $T_1 = T_2 = T$.

**Step 1:** Write the boundary work integral.

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $P = \dfrac{mRT}{V}$ from the ideal gas law into the integral.

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{mRT}{V} \, dV$$

**Step 3:** Since $m$, $R$, and $T$ are all constants for this process, factor them out of the integral.

$$W = mRT \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{1}{V} \, dV$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the standard integral $\int \dfrac{dV}{V} = \ln V$.

$$W = mRT \, \bigl[\ln V \bigr]_{V_1}^{V_2}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the upper and lower limits.

$$W = mRT \, \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$$

**Step 6 (Alternate form):** Using $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2 = mRT$ at constant temperature, we may equivalently write:

$$W = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = P_2 V_2 \ln\!\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> For an **expansion** ($V_2 > V_1$), $W > 0$ (system does work on surroundings). For a **compression** ($V_2 < V_1$), $W < 0$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Work Done in a Polytropic Process

**Step 1:** Begin with the polytropic process relation $PV^n = C$ (constant). Differentiate implicitly:

$$P \cdot n V^{n-1} dV + V^n dP = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad dP = -\frac{nP}{V} dV$$

**Step 2:** Express $P$ along the path as a function of $V$:

$$P = \frac{C}{V^n} = \frac{P_1 V_1^n}{V^n}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the work integral:

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{P_1 V_1^n}{V^n} \, dV = P_1 V_1^n \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V^{-n} \, dV$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the integral (assume $n \neq 1$):

$$W = P_1 V_1^n \left[ \frac{V^{1-n}}{1-n} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = \frac{P_1 V_1^n}{1-n} \bigl(V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n}\bigr)$$

**Step 5:** Multiply and divide to reveal a cleaner form. Note $P_2 V_2^n = P_1 V_1^n$, so:

$$W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1-n} = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n-1}$$

**Step 6 (Limiting Cases Check):**
- Let $n = 0$: $W = P_1(V_2 - V_1) = P(V_2 - V_1)$ → recovers **isobaric** work. ✓
- Let $n = 1$: Formula is indeterminate; apply L'Hôpital's rule to recover **isothermal** work. ✓
- Let $n = \gamma$: Yields **adiabatic** work. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation: Work Done in a Constant Pressure Process

For $P = P_1 = P_2 = \text{const}$:

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV = P \int_{V_1}^{V_2} dV = P(V_2 - V_1)$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Adiabatic Work from Polytropic Formula

Setting $n = \gamma$ in the polytropic result:

$$W_{\text{adiabatic}} = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{\gamma - 1}$$

Using ideal gas law $P_1 V_1 = mRT_1$ and $P_2 V_2 = mRT_2$:

$$W_{\text{adiabatic}} = \frac{mR(T_1 - T_2)}{\gamma - 1}$$

This is the form most frequently used in **KTU numerical problems** for compressor/expander analysis.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Model Solution Style)

**Problem:** 0.5 kg of air expands isothermally from 1 MPa, 0.2 m³ to a final volume of 0.6 m³. Determine the work done. Take $R_{\text{air}} = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K).

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Find the constant temperature.

$$T = \frac{P_1 V_1}{mR} = \frac{(1000 \text{ kPa})(0.2 \text{ m}^3)}{(0.5 \text{ kg})(0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K})} \approx 1393.7 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the isothermal work formula.

$$W = mRT \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = (0.5)(0.287)(1393.7) \ln\!\left(\frac{0.6}{0.2}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Compute numerically.

$$W = 200.0 \times \ln(3) = 200.0 \times 1.0986 \approx 219.7 \text{ kJ}$$

**Answer:** $W \approx +219.7$ kJ (positive, since expansion).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Piston–Cylinder Model (Source of Boundary Work)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Atmospheric Load P_atm] --> B[Piston Face Area A]
    B --> C[GAS at Pressure P inside Cylinder]
    C --> D[Boundary Work dW = P dV]
    D --> E[Energy crosses system boundary]
    E --> F[Surroundings receive Work]
    style A fill:#e8f4ff,stroke:#0b5cad,color:#0b2e57
    style B fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#b07d00,color:#5b3e00
    style C fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#a8323a,color:#5e1a1f
    style D fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#2c7a2c,color:#163e16
    style E fill:#efe2ff,stroke:#5b2ca8,color:#2a1252
    style F fill:#e2f0ff,stroke:#1a4a8a,color:#0a2245
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of the P–V Diagram (Work as Area Under Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    subgraph PV_DIAGRAM["P-V DIAGRAM CONCEPTUAL MAP"]
        direction TB
        AXISY["Vertical Axis = Pressure P"] --> AXISX["Horizontal Axis = Volume V"]
        AXISX --> CURVE["Process Curve from State 1 to State 2"]
        CURVE --> AREA["Shaded Area UNDER Curve = Work Done W1-2"]
    end
    S1 -.->|Initial State Point| PV_DIAGRAM
    S2 -.->|Final State Point| PV_DIAGRAM
    style S1 fill:#fff8d6,stroke:#8a6d00,color:#3d3100
    style S2 fill:#d6f5ff,stroke:#00607a,color:#00323d

    style AXISX fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#555,color:#222
    style CURVE fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a83232,color:#5e1a1a
    style AREA fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#2c7a2c,color:#163e16
```

## 4.3 Process Comparison Block Diagram (Same End States, Different Work)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SAME_END_STATES["SAME INITIAL AND FINAL STATES"]
        SA[State 1: P1 V1]
        SB[State 2: P2 V2]
    end
    SA -->|Path A Isobaric| P1["P = const, W = P dV"]
    SA -->|Path B Isothermal| P2["PV = const, W = mRT ln V2/V1"]
    SA -->|Path C Polytropic n=1.3| P3["PV^1.3 = const, W = P1V1-P2V2 over 0.3"]
    SA -->|Path D Adiabatic| P4["PV^gamma = const, W = P1V1-P2V2 over gamma-1"]
    P1 --> SB
    P2 --> SB
    P3 --> SB
    P4 --> SB
    style SA fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#7a5b00,color:#3d2e00
    style SB fill:#d6f5ff,stroke:#00567a,color:#002a3d
    style P1 fill:#ffe2c2,stroke:#a85200,color:#4a2200
    style P2 fill:#c2e2ff,stroke:#0052a8,color:#00224a
    style P3 fill:#d2ffc2,stroke:#2a8a00,color:#0f3d00
    style P4 fill:#ffc2e2,stroke:#a8005a,color:#4a002a
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define boundary work. Why is work considered a path function in thermodynamics?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Boundary work is the energy transferred across the boundary of a closed system due to a pressure-induced volume change. For a quasi-static process, the differential form is $dW = P\,dV$, and the total work is $W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\,dV$. This represents the area under the process curve on a P–V diagram.
- **Path Function (1 mark):** Because the magnitude of $\int P\,dV$ depends on the specific path traced between the initial and final states, two different processes connecting the same end states yield different work values. Hence W is a path function, not a property of the system.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**A gas undergoes a free expansion from 0.05 m³ to 0.15 m³. Calculate the work done. Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Statement (1 mark):** Work done, $W = 0$ kJ.
- **Justification (2 marks):** In a free expansion, the gas expands against **zero external pressure** ($P_{\text{ext}} = 0$). The boundary work integral reduces to $W = \int P_{\text{ext}}\,dV = 0 \times (V_2 - V_1) = 0$. The gas does no work on the surroundings, even though its volume and internal state change.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Instructions (KTU 2024 ESE pattern):** Answer **any one** full question. Each carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Show all steps with units.

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A (a)** Derive the expression for work done by an ideal gas during a **polytropic process** $PV^n = \text{constant}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the polytropic process relation.

$$PV^n = C \quad (\text{constant})$$

Differentiating implicitly with respect to volume:

$$nP V^{n-1} dV + V^n dP = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad dP = -\frac{nP}{V} dV$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Express P along the path.

From $P_1 V_1^n = P V^n$, we get $P = \dfrac{P_1 V_1^n}{V^n}$. Substitute into the boundary work integral:

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV = P_1 V_1^n \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V^{-n} \, dV$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Evaluate the integral for $n \neq 1$.

$$W = P_1 V_1^n \left[ \frac{V^{1-n}}{1-n} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = \frac{P_1 V_1^n}{1-n}\bigl(V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n}\bigr)$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Simplify using $P_2 V_2^n = P_1 V_1^n$.

$$W = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1-n} = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n-1}$$

This is the **final standard form** of polytropic work, valid for $n \neq 1$.

---

**A (b)** 2 kg of air is compressed polytropically from 1 bar, 27 °C to 6 bar following $PV^{1.3} = C$. Calculate: (i) the work done, and (ii) the final temperature. Take $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K) and $\gamma = 1.4$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $m = 2$ kg, $P_1 = 1$ bar = 100 kPa, $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_2 = 6$ bar = 600 kPa, $n = 1.3$.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Find $V_1$ from ideal gas law.

$$V_1 = \frac{mRT_1}{P_1} = \frac{(2)(0.287)(300)}{100} = 1.722 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Find $V_2$ from $P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$.

$$V_2 = V_1 \left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{1/n} = 1.722 \times \left(\frac{100}{600}\right)^{1/1.3} = 1.722 \times (0.1667)^{0.7692}$$

$$V_2 \approx 1.722 \times 0.2457 \approx 0.4231 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the polytropic work.

$$W = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n - 1} = \frac{(100)(1.722) - (600)(0.4231)}{1.3 - 1}$$

$$W = \frac{172.2 - 253.86}{0.3} = \frac{-81.66}{0.3} \approx -272.2 \text{ kJ}$$

The **negative sign** correctly indicates that work is done **on** the gas (compression).

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Find $T_2$.

$$T_2 = \frac{P_2 V_2}{mR} = \frac{(600)(0.4231)}{(2)(0.287)} = \frac{253.86}{0.574} \approx 442.3 \text{ K} \quad (\approx 169.3 \text{ °C})$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $W \approx -272.2$ kJ; (ii) $T_2 \approx 442.3$ K.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**B (a)** With the help of a P–V diagram, explain the concept of **work done as area under the curve**. Distinguish between boundary work and flow work. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** **Work as area under the curve.**
For a quasi-static process, $W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\,dV$. Geometrically, the integral represents the area enclosed between the process curve, the volume axis, and the vertical lines at $V_1$ and $V_2$. On the P–V diagram, this shaded region is numerically equal to the work done. For an expansion (curve moving right), the area is **positive**; for compression, it is **negative**.

**Step 2 (3 marks):** **Boundary Work vs. Flow Work.**

| Feature | Boundary Work (PdV) | Flow Work (Flow Energy) |
|---------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| System | Closed system | Open system (control volume) |
| Cause | Moving boundary (piston) | Fluid entering or leaving CV |
| Expression | $W_b = \int P\,dV$ | $W_f = P V$ (per cycle) or $Pv$ per kg |
| Direction | Energy crosses a *physical* boundary | Energy is *carried* by mass flow |

- **Boundary work** is the energy transferred by a generalized force (pressure) acting through a displacement (volume change). 
- **Flow work** is the energy required to push a fluid mass across the control volume boundary, equal to $P \cdot V$ (or $P \cdot v$ per unit mass). It is essential to define **enthalpy** $h = u + Pv$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** **Engineering Significance.**
In IC engines, the net boundary work per cycle is the indicator of engine output. In turbines, flow work input/output determines the enthalpy drop and hence the **shaft work** of the machine.

---

**B (b)** A piston–cylinder device contains 0.4 kg of nitrogen at 120 kPa, 30 °C. The gas is compressed **isothermally** to one-fourth of its original volume. Determine: (i) the work done, and (ii) the heat transferred. Take $R_{N_2} = 0.2968$ kJ/(kg·K). **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $m = 0.4$ kg, $P_1 = 120$ kPa, $T_1 = 303$ K, $V_2 = V_1/4$.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Verify ideal gas behavior (assumed per KTU convention).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the isothermal work using $W = mRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$.

$$W = (0.4)(0.2968)(303) \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{4}\right) = 35.97 \times (-1.3863) \approx -49.87 \text{ kJ}$$

The negative sign indicates work is done **on** the gas.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** For an ideal gas undergoing an isothermal process, internal energy change $\Delta U = 0$ (since $T = $ const).

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics for a closed system.

$$Q = \Delta U + W = 0 + (-49.87) = -49.87 \text{ kJ}$$

The negative Q indicates **heat is rejected** by the gas to the surroundings during compression.

**Final Answers:** (i) $W \approx -49.87$ kJ; (ii) $Q \approx -49.87$ kJ.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Slip:** Students frequently forget to put the negative sign on compression work. If $V_2 < V_1$ but you write a positive W, you will lose 1–2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Confusing n and γ:** Using $\gamma$ where the problem specifies a custom $n$ (or vice-versa) leads to a completely wrong numerical answer. Read carefully!
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing Pa with m³ (yields Joules) and then writing kJ without the /1000 conversion. Always track units in *every* line.
> 4. **Forgetting the First Law link:** For isothermal ideal gas, $\Delta U = 0$ is *the* key fact that lets you equate Q and W. Skipping this step costs the "linking" mark.
> 5. **Polytropic formula misuse at n=1:** The expression $W = (P_1V_1 - P_2V_2)/(n-1)$ is **undefined** at $n = 1$. You *must* switch to the isothermal form $W = mRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$ — examiners do test this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Fundamental boundary work equation:** $dW = P\,dV$; finite form $W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\,dV$.
- ⭐ Work = **area under the curve** on a P–V diagram; **path function**, not a property.
- ⭐ **Clausius sign convention:** W > 0 when system expands (does work on surroundings).
- ⭐ **Isochoric:** $W = 0$ (locked piston, no movement).
- ⭐ **Isobaric:** $W = P(V_2 - V_1)$ (rectangle under the curve).
- ⭐ **Isothermal (ideal gas):** $W = mRT \ln(V_2/V_1) = P_1V_1 \ln(V_2/V_1)$.
- ⭐ **Polytropic ($n \neq 1$):** $W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n - 1}$.
- ⭐ **Adiabatic:** Set $n = \gamma$ in the polytropic formula.
- ⭐ **Free expansion:** $W = 0$ (no resistance, no real work).
- ⭐ **Flow work ≠ Boundary work** — flow work belongs to open systems; $w_f = Pv$ per kg.
- ⭐ Always quote **units** (kJ for total, kJ/kg for specific) and the **sign** in the final answer.
- ⭐ For isothermal processes with ideal gas, remember $\Delta U = 0$, so $Q = W$ (First Law shortcut).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Thermal Efficiency?

In the context of air-standard cycles studied under Module 2 of PBMET604 Thermal Engineering, **Efficiency** refers to the dimensionless ratio that quantifies *how effectively a thermodynamic cycle converts the heat supplied into useful mechanical work*. Mathematically, it is defined as:

$$\eta_{thermal} \;=\; \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{supplied}} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{Q_{rejected}}{Q_{supplied}}$$

Because the second-law efficiency of an ideal cycle depends only on the temperatures (Carnot principle), a cycle's efficiency is fundamentally a measure of *how close its heat exchange pattern is to two isothermal reservoirs*. In KTU notation, this is often expressed in **percentage form (%)** for numerical problems, with a maximum theoretical ceiling of **100%** (unattainable per the Kelvin-Planck statement).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> "Derive the air-standard efficiency of Otto, Diesel, Dual and Brayton cycles. Compare the cycles for the same compression ratio and same peak temperature."
> Hence, the term *Efficiency* in this module ALWAYS refers to the **air-standard thermal efficiency of reciprocating and gas turbine cycles**.

## 1.2 Types of Efficiencies Encountered in Module 2

| # | Efficiency Type | Symbol | Definition Snapshot |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Air-Standard Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{th}$ | Net work ÷ Heat added, evaluated on the working fluid (air). |
| 2 | **Mean Effective Pressure Efficiency** | $\eta_{mep}$ | Compares indicated work with an equivalent constant-pressure cycle. |
| 3 | **Relative Efficiency** | $\eta_{rel}$ | $\eta_{otto/diesel/dual}$ ÷ $\eta_{carnot}$ — measures deviation from ideal. |
| 4 | **Work Ratio / Back Work Ratio** | $r_{w}$ | Net work ÷ Gross work (used in Brayton cycle). |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme derivations, the student must know that **$\eta_{th}$ = 1 − (heat rejected / heat supplied)** is the universally applicable starting point. The specific formula for each cycle simply emerges from substituting the appropriate process equations.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **water wheel** receiving water from a height $H$ and returning the leftover water to a lower pond at height $L$. The wheel can only convert the **fall** ($H - L$) into rotational work — never the full height $H$. Thermal efficiency works identically:

- $H$ → highest temperature reached inside the cycle ($T_{max}$)
- $L$ → lowest temperature at which exhaust is rejected ($T_{min}$)
- $(H-L)/H$ → **(Carnot Efficiency)** — the theoretical ceiling.

A real Otto, Diesel, Dual, or Brayton cycle is the engineering *approximation* of this ideal water wheel, achieving 50 %–65 % of the Carnot value.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Mnemonic for KTU vivas:**
> *Higher compression ratio ⇒ Higher efficiency, but bounded by knock in SI engines and by material limits in CI engines.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic Behind Cycle Efficiency

The **thermal efficiency of any air-standard cycle** depends on two physical pillars:

1. **Compression ratio / pressure ratio** — Determines how high the temperature rises *before* combustion.
2. **Cut-off ratio / heat addition mode** — Dictates how heat is added (constant volume, constant pressure, or a combination).

The logical chain is:

$$\text{Compression} \;\Rightarrow\; \uparrow T \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Heat Addition} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Expansion} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Heat Rejection} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Net Work}$$

For each cycle, only the **path of heat addition** and **path of heat rejection** on the P-V diagram change; the work extraction strategy remains bounded by the **second law of thermodynamics**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving KTU Module 2 numerical and derivation problems. Memorize the *structure* of each formula — the terms, their positions, and their physical meaning.

| Cycle | Heat Added ($Q_{in}$) | Heat Rejected ($Q_{out}$) | Thermal Efficiency $\eta_{th}$ | Key Parameters |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Carnot** | $T_H \,\Delta S$ | $T_L \,\Delta S$ | $1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Two isothermal + two isentropic |
| **Otto** | $m C_v (T_3 - T_2)$ | $m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$ | $1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | $r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$ (compression ratio) |
| **Diesel** | $m C_p (T_3 - T_2)$ | $m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$ | $1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma (\rho - 1)}$ | $r$ = comp. ratio, $\rho = \dfrac{V_3}{V_2}$ (cut-off) |
| **Dual** | $m[C_v(T_3-T_2)+C_p(T_4-T_3)]$ | $m C_v (T_5 - T_1)$ | $1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\beta \rho^{\gamma} - 1}{(\beta - 1) + \beta \gamma (\rho - 1)}$ | $r, \rho, \beta = \dfrac{p_3}{p_2}$ |
| **Brayton** | $m C_p (T_3 - T_2)$ | $m C_p (T_4 - T_1)$ | $1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}$ | $r_p = \dfrac{p_2}{p_1}$ (pressure ratio) |

> **Notation Used:**
> - $r$ = **Compression ratio** ($V_1/V_2$)
> - $\rho$ = **Cut-off ratio** ($V_3/V_2$)
> - $\beta$ = **Pressure ratio** during constant-volume heat addition in Dual cycle
> - $r_p$ = **Pressure ratio** for gas turbines (Brayton)
> - $\gamma$ = **Ratio of specific heats** ($\gamma = 1.4$ for air)
> - $C_v, C_p$ = Specific heats at constant volume and pressure

> [!CAUTION]
> In the Diesel efficiency formula, the term $\dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma (\rho - 1)}$ is the *expansion factor*. It is **always greater than 1**, which is precisely why the Diesel cycle is **less efficient** than Otto for the *same compression ratio*.

## 2.3 The Carnot Benchmark — Why Air-Standard Cycles Are Inferior

For all four cycles in Module 2, a crucial relationship holds:

$$\eta_{carnot} \;>\; \eta_{brayton} \;\approx\; \eta_{otto} \;\approx\; \eta_{dual} \;>\; \eta_{diesel} \quad \text{(at same compression ratio)}$$

This inequality is **central to KTU comparison questions**. It emerges from the fact that only Carnot uses *isothermal* heat exchange, eliminating any irreversibility during heat transfer.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **SI Engines (Petrol)** → Otto cycle governs. Efficiency improvement strategies revolve around *higher compression ratio* (limited by knock).
- **CI Engines (Diesel)** → Diesel/Dual cycle governs. The trade-off between cut-off ratio and efficiency is exploited in **HCCI and RCCI** modern combustion technologies.
- **Jet Engines & Gas Turbines** → Brayton cycle governs. Pressure ratio optimization is a key design parameter for **aerospace propulsion**.
- **Combined Cycle Plants** → Stack a Brayton (gas) top-cycle with a Rankine (steam) bottom-cycle to achieve **> 60 %** net plant efficiency.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> All derivations below are conducted on the **closed-system air-standard assumption**: air behaves as an ideal gas with constant specific heats ($C_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $C_v = 0.718 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$).

---

## 3.1 Derivation of Otto Cycle Thermal Efficiency

### 3.1.1 Cycle Definition

The Otto cycle consists of four internally reversible processes:

| Process | Transformation | Path on P-V Diagram |
|---|---|---|
| 1 → 2 | Isentropic Compression | $pV^{\gamma} = \text{const.}$ |
| 2 → 3 | Constant Volume Heat Addition | $V = \text{const.}$ |
| 3 → 4 | Isentropic Expansion (Power) | $pV^{\gamma} = \text{const.}$ |
| 4 → 1 | Constant Volume Heat Rejection | $V = \text{const.}$ |

### 3.1.2 Heat Exchanged During Each Process

**Heat Supplied (Process 2-3, constant volume):**

$$Q_{in} \;=\; m \, C_v \, (T_3 - T_2)$$

**Heat Rejected (Process 4-1, constant volume):**

$$Q_{out} \;=\; m \, C_v \, (T_4 - T_1)$$

### 3.1.3 Isentropic Relations for Compression and Expansion

For the isentropic compression (1→2):

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} \;=\; \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma - 1} \;=\; r^{\gamma - 1}$$

For the isentropic expansion (3→4):

$$\frac{T_3}{T_4} \;=\; \left(\frac{V_4}{V_3}\right)^{\gamma - 1} \;=\; r^{\gamma - 1}$$

(The equality $V_4/V_3 = V_1/V_2 = r$ follows from the constant-volume processes.)

### 3.1.4 Applying the Efficiency Definition

$$\eta_{otto} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$\eta_{otto} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{m C_v (T_4 - T_1)}{m C_v (T_3 - T_2)} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$

### 3.1.5 Algebraic Reduction

From the isentropic relations:

$$T_2 \;=\; T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1} \qquad \text{and} \qquad T_3 \;=\; T_4 \, r^{\gamma - 1}$$

Substituting these into the numerator-denominator difference:

$$T_3 - T_2 \;=\; T_4 \, r^{\gamma - 1} - T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1} \;=\; (T_4 - T_1) \, r^{\gamma - 1}$$

Therefore:

$$\eta_{otto} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{(T_4 - T_1)}{(T_4 - T_1) \, r^{\gamma - 1}}$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{otto} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Result Insight:**
> The above boxed formula is the single most-asked derivation in Module 2. Examiners give **full 7 marks** only if (a) the heat equations are written *separately* for $Q_{in}$ and $Q_{out}$, (b) isentropic relations are *explicitly* invoked, and (c) the cancellation of $(T_4 - T_1)$ is shown, not skipped.

### 3.1.6 Numerical Sanity Check

For a typical petrol engine with $r = 8$, $\gamma = 1.4$:

$$\eta_{otto} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{1}{8^{0.4}} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{1}{2.2974} \;=\; 0.5647 \;\approx\; 56.47\%$$

This is consistent with real-world SI engine efficiencies of **30–40 %** (after mechanical, friction, and combustion losses are deducted from the air-standard value).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Diesel Cycle Thermal Efficiency

### 3.2.1 Cycle Definition

| Process | Transformation |
|---|---|
| 1 → 2 | Isentropic Compression |
| 2 → 3 | Constant Pressure Heat Addition (Cut-off at $\rho = V_3/V_2$) |
| 3 → 4 | Isentropic Expansion |
| 4 → 1 | Constant Volume Heat Rejection |

### 3.2.2 Heat Exchanged

**Heat Supplied (Process 2-3, constant pressure):**

$$Q_{in} \;=\; m \, C_p \, (T_3 - T_2)$$

**Heat Rejected (Process 4-1, constant volume):**

$$Q_{out} \;=\; m \, C_v \, (T_4 - T_1)$$

### 3.2.3 Isentropic Compression Relation

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} \;=\; r^{\gamma - 1} \qquad \Rightarrow \qquad T_2 \;=\; T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1}$$

### 3.2.4 Constant Pressure Relation (Cut-off Ratio)

For the constant-pressure process (2→3):

$$\frac{V_3}{V_2} \;=\; \frac{T_3}{T_2} \;=\; \rho \qquad \Rightarrow \qquad T_3 \;=\; \rho \, T_2$$

### 3.2.5 Isentropic Expansion Relation (3→4)

The volume ratio during expansion is $V_4/V_3 = r/\rho$ (since $V_4 = V_1$ and $V_3 = \rho V_2 = \rho V_1/r$):

$$\frac{T_3}{T_4} \;=\; \left(\frac{V_4}{V_3}\right)^{\gamma - 1} \;=\; \left(\frac{r}{\rho}\right)^{\gamma - 1}$$

Solving for $T_4$:

$$T_4 \;=\; T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{\rho}{r}\right)^{\gamma - 1} \;=\; \rho \, T_2 \cdot \left(\frac{\rho}{r}\right)^{\gamma - 1}$$

Substituting $T_2 = T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1}$:

$$T_4 \;=\; \rho \cdot T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1} \cdot \frac{\rho^{\gamma - 1}}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \;=\; \rho^{\gamma} \, T_1$$

### 3.2.6 Applying the Efficiency Definition

$$\eta_{diesel} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{m C_v (T_4 - T_1)}{m C_p (T_3 - T_2)}$$

Using $C_p = \gamma C_v$:

$$\eta_{diesel} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{T_4 - T_1}{\gamma (T_3 - T_2)}$$

### 3.2.7 Substituting Temperature Expressions

Numerator $T_4 - T_1$:

$$T_4 - T_1 \;=\; \rho^{\gamma} \, T_1 - T_1 \;=\; T_1 (\rho^{\gamma} - 1)$$

Denominator $T_3 - T_2$:

$$T_3 - T_2 \;=\; \rho \, T_2 - T_2 \;=\; T_2 (\rho - 1) \;=\; T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1} (\rho - 1)$$

Therefore:

$$\eta_{diesel} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{T_1 (\rho^{\gamma} - 1)}{\gamma \cdot T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1} (\rho - 1)}$$

The $T_1$ cancels:

$$\eta_{diesel} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \frac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma (\rho - 1)}$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{diesel} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \frac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma (\rho - 1)}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $\dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma (\rho - 1)}$ is **> 1 for any $\rho > 1$**, so it acts as a *penalty term* on the Otto efficiency. As $\rho \to 1$, this factor $\to 1$, and the Diesel cycle degenerates into the Otto cycle. This is the **mathematical foundation** of the Otto-Diesel comparison often asked in KTU 14-mark questions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Brayton Cycle Thermal Efficiency (Gas Turbine)

### 3.3.1 Cycle Definition

| Process | Transformation |
|---|---|
| 1 → 2 | Isentropic Compression |
| 2 → 3 | Constant Pressure Heat Addition |
| 3 → 4 | Isentropic Expansion |
| 4 → 1 | Constant Pressure Heat Rejection |

### 3.3.2 Heat Exchanged

$$Q_{in} \;=\; m \, C_p \, (T_3 - T_2) \qquad Q_{out} \;=\; m \, C_p \, (T_4 - T_1)$$

### 3.3.3 Isentropic Relations

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} \;=\; \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} \;=\; r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$$

$$\frac{T_3}{T_4} \;=\; \left(\frac{p_3}{p_4}\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} \;=\; r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$$

### 3.3.4 Final Efficiency Expression

By the same algebraic manipulation as Otto:

$$\eta_{brayton} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{brayton} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}}\;}$$

> [!TIP]
> Note the **structural similarity** with the Otto formula — the only difference is the exponent $(\gamma - 1)/\gamma$ instead of $(\gamma - 1)$, and the variable $r_p$ (pressure ratio) instead of $r$ (compression ratio). Examiners love testing this parallel.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Frequently Asked)

For the **same compression ratio $r = 10$** and **same heat rejected**, with $\gamma = 1.4$:

| Cycle | Efficiency Formula | Numerical Value at $r = 10$, $\rho = 2$ |
|---|---|---|
| Carnot ($T_H/T_L = 3$) | $1 - T_L/T_H$ | $66.67\%$ |
| Otto | $1 - 1/r^{0.4}$ | $\mathbf{60.18\%}$ |
| Diesel | $1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{0.4}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma (\rho - 1)}$ | $\mathbf{52.45\%}$ |
| Brayton ($r_p = 10$) | $1 - 1/r_p^{0.2857}$ | $\mathbf{48.20\%}$ |

**Key Inferences (frequently asked in 7-mark sub-parts):**

1. Otto > Diesel **for the same compression ratio**, because the Diesel cycle has the cut-off penalty factor.
2. As cut-off ratio $\rho \to 1$, $\eta_{diesel} \to \eta_{otto}$.
3. Brayton is *always less efficient than Otto* for the same peak pressure/temperature due to constant-pressure heat addition.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The figures below are rendered as **Mermaid block diagrams** representing the structural and functional relationships among the four air-standard cycles covered in Module 2. These are designed for *conceptual clarity* and *exam-time revision*.

---

## 4.1 Master Flow — Derivation Pipeline of Thermal Efficiency

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start with any Air Standard Cycle] --> procB{Identify Heat Addition Path}
    procB -->|Constant Volume| ottoN[Otto Cycle]
    procB -->|Constant Pressure| dieselN[Diesel Cycle]
    procB -->|V then p| dualN[Dual Cycle]
    procB -->|Gas Turbine| braytonN[Brayton Cycle]

    ottoN --> qOtto[Q_in = m Cv T3 minus T2]
    dieselN --> qDiesel[Q_in = m Cp T3 minus T2]
    dualN --> qDual[Q_in combines Cv and Cp terms]
    braytonN --> qBrayton[Q_in = m Cp T3 minus T2]

    qOtto --> effOtto[Eta = 1 minus 1 over r power gamma minus 1]
    qDiesel --> effDiesel[Eta = 1 minus penalty factor over r power gamma minus 1]
    qDual --> effDual[Eta Dual formula with rho and beta]
    qBrayton --> effBrayton[Eta = 1 minus 1 over rp power gamma minus 1 over gamma]

    effOtto --> compNode[Compare on Common Platform]
    effDiesel --> compNode
    effDual --> compNode
    effBrayton --> compNode
    compNode --> carnotN[Reference Carnot Upper Bound]
```

---

## 4.2 Hierarchical Classification of Cycle Efficiencies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rootA[Thermal Efficiency] --> typeB[Based on Heat Addition Mode]
    rootA --> typeC[Based on Application]
    rootA --> typeD[Based on Reversibility]

    typeB --> b1[Constant Volume: Otto]
    typeB --> b2[Constant Pressure: Diesel and Brayton]
    typeB --> b3[Mixed: Dual]

    typeC --> c1[SI Engines use Otto]
    typeC --> c2[CI Engines use Diesel or Dual]
    typeC --> c3[Gas Turbines use Brayton]
    typeC --> c4[Stirling and Ericcson approach Carnot]

    typeD --> d1[Reversible: All air standard cycles]
    dD1[Theoretical Maximum] --> carnotZ[Carnot Efficiency]
```

---

## 4.3 P-V Diagram Functional Block Architecture

Since the actual P-V curves are graphical, the following **block-level functional architecture** captures the *state transitions* of an Otto cycle for KTU board-style presentations:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    state1[State 1: Start of Compression, T1 and V1 and P1] --> compA[Process 1 to 2: Isentropic Compression]
    compA --> state2[State 2: End of Compression, T2, V2, P2]
    state2 --> heatA[Process 2 to 3: Constant Volume Heat Addition]
    heatA --> state3[State 3: Peak Pressure and Temperature]
    state3 --> expA[Process 3 to 4: Isentropic Expansion]
    expA --> state4[State 4: End of Expansion, T4, V4, P4]
    state4 --> rejA[Process 4 to 1: Constant Volume Heat Rejection]
    rejA --> state1
```

**Reading Guide for Students:**

| State | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|
| 1 | BDC (Bottom Dead Centre), start of compression stroke |
| 2 | TDC (Top Dead Centre), end of compression, ignition point |
| 3 | Peak temperature after combustion, start of power stroke |
| 4 | BDC again, end of power stroke, exhaust begins |

---

## 4.4 Effect of Compression Ratio on Otto Efficiency (Plot Annotation)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Otto Efficiency vs Compression Ratio (gamma equals 1.4)"
    x-axis "Compression Ratio r" [4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16]
    y-axis "Thermal Efficiency percent" 40 --> 75
    line [44.6, 51.2, 56.5, 60.2, 63.0, 65.2, 67.1]
```

> **Reading Note:** The curve clearly demonstrates **diminishing returns** — efficiency rises steeply at low $r$, but flattens as $r$ grows. In practice, $r = 9$–$11$ is the practical ceiling for SI engines due to knock.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define air-standard efficiency. Why does the air-standard efficiency of the Otto cycle depend only on the compression ratio?**

**Model Answer:**

Air-standard efficiency is the ratio of net work done in an air-standard cycle to the heat supplied, evaluated under the assumption that the working fluid is air behaving as an ideal gas with constant specific heats.

$$\eta_{th} \;=\; \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$$

The Otto cycle efficiency depends **only on the compression ratio** because:

1. The expressions for $Q_{in}$ and $Q_{out}$ both contain $m C_v$, which cancels.
2. The isentropic relations for compression (1→2) and expansion (3→4) yield identical volume ratios ($V_1/V_2 = V_4/V_3 = r$).
3. The temperatures $T_1$ cancels algebraically, leaving only $r$ and $\gamma$ in the final formula:

$$\eta_{otto} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$$

Hence, the efficiency is *independent* of the temperatures $T_1$, $T_2$, $T_3$, $T_4$.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the difference between air-standard efficiency and relative efficiency.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Air-Standard Efficiency ($\eta_{th}$) | Relative Efficiency ($\eta_{rel}$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Ratio of net work to heat added in an ideal air-standard cycle. | Ratio of actual cycle efficiency to the Carnot efficiency operating between the same temperature limits. |
| Formula | $\eta_{otto} = 1 - 1/r^{\gamma-1}$ (example) | $\eta_{rel} = \eta_{th}/\eta_{carnot}$ |
| Purpose | Quantifies the *ideal* thermodynamic performance. | Quantifies how *close* the cycle is to the theoretical maximum. |
| Value Range | 0 < $\eta_{th}$ < $\eta_{carnot}$ | 0 < $\eta_{rel}$ ≤ 1 |
| Significance | Used in design and comparison of cycles. | Used as a *figure of merit* for cycle perfection. |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]**

**(a)** *Derive an expression for the air-standard efficiency of the Otto cycle in terms of compression ratio. State the assumptions clearly.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *An engine working on the Otto cycle has a compression ratio of 9. The temperatures at the beginning and end of compression are 320 K and 753 K respectively. Determine: (i) the air-standard efficiency, (ii) the heat supplied per kg of air, and (iii) the work done per kg of air. Take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation:** *(Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation.)* The final expression is:

$$\boxed{\eta_{otto} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. The working fluid is **ideal air** with constant specific heats.
2. All processes are **internally reversible** (isentropic compression and expansion).
3. Heat addition and rejection occur at **constant volume**.
4. **Closed system** — no mass flow across boundaries.
5. **Kinetic and potential energy** changes are negligible.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $r = 9$, $T_1 = 320$ K, $T_2 = 753$ K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K.

**Step (i) — Air-Standard Efficiency:**
