| 1 | **Watt** | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g}$, so $h = \dfrac{g}{\omega^2}$ | $\dfrac{2\omega_1 - \omega_2}{\omega}$ — poor |
| 2 | **Porter (without friction)** | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g + \dfrac{F_s}{m}}$ | Better than Watt |
| 3 | **Porter (with friction)** | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{\omega^2 r \pm F_f/m}{g + F_s/m}$ | Hysteresis band = ±Friction |
| 4 | **Hartnell** | $\omega^2 = \dfrac{2k}{m\,(r_1 + r_2)}\left(\dfrac{a}{r_1} + 1\right) - \dfrac{2c}{m\,(r_1 + r_2)}$ | Excellent |
| 5 | **Nozzle Governing — Power steps** | $P_n = n \cdot \dfrac{P_{total}}{N}$ where $n$ = nozzles open, $N$ = total | Stepped |
| 6 | **Sleeve lift in Hartnell** | $h = \dfrac{(r_2 - r_1) \, (S_1 - S_2)}{2\,(S_1 + S_2 - 2W)}$ | Spring-controlled |

### Notation for Hartnell
- $r_1, r_2$ = minimum and maximum radius of rotation (m)
- $S_1, S_2$ = spring force at $r_1$ and $r_2$ (N)
- $k$ = spring stiffness (N/m)
- $c$ = initial spring compression (N)
- $W$ = weight of sleeve + equivalent (N)
- $a$ = bell-crank lever arm ratio

### Boundary Conditions and Units

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Angular velocities are usually expressed in **N (RPM)** for engineering calculations. Conversion: $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s.
> - All forces in **N (Newtons)**, masses in **kg**, lengths in **metres**.
> - Sensitiveness of a governor = $\dfrac{N_2 - N_1}{N}$ where $N_2$ = max equilibrium speed, $N_1$ = min equilibrium speed.
> - **Stability condition** (Watt/Porter): $\dfrac{h}{\omega^2} = \dfrac{dh}{d\omega^2}$ → $h = \dfrac{g}{\omega^2}$ (Watt) or $h = \dfrac{g + F_s/m}{\omega^2}$ (Porter).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Turbogenerator speed regulation:** A 500 MW unit running at 3000 RPM must hold frequency at 50 ± 0.5 Hz.
- **Safety:** A centrifugal overspeed trip at 110% rated RPM shuts the throttle in <0.1 s to prevent catastrophic blade failure.
- **Grid code compliance:** Indian Electricity Grid Code (IEGC) mandates droop characteristic — Hartnell governor with adjustable droop (typically 4–5%).
- **Combined with Electro-Hydraulic Control (EHC):** Modern systems use centrifugal pilot for speed sensing → electrical signal → hydraulic servo → nozzle/throttle valve motion.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Height of a Watt Governor

### Statement
*Show that the height of a Watt governor is independent of the geometry of the arms and equals $g/\omega^2$.*

### Free-Body Setup
A simple Watt governor has two balls attached to rigid arms of length $L$, pivoted at the top of the spindle. There is **no sleeve, no spring, no friction**.

**Variables:**
- $m$ = mass of each ball (kg)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity of spindle (rad/s)
- $r$ = horizontal radius of ball = $L \sin\theta$ (m)
- $h$ = vertical height from pivot to ball centre = $L \cos\theta$ (m)
- $\theta$ = arm angle with vertical (rad)

### Force Diagram
Two forces act on the ball:
1. **Weight $W = mg$** vertically downward.
2. **Centrifugal force $F_c = m\omega^2 r$** horizontally outward.

The rigid arm can only exert a force **along its own length**, so the resultant of $W$ and $F_c$ must lie along the arm.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Identify the direction of the arm from the geometry.
The arm makes angle $\theta$ with the vertical, so:
$$\tan\theta = \dfrac{r}{h} \quad \text{(geometry of right triangle)}$$

**Step 2:** For equilibrium of the ball, the line of action of the resultant of $W$ and $F_c$ must coincide with the arm. Therefore:
$$\tan\theta = \dfrac{F_c}{W} = \dfrac{m\omega^2 r}{mg}$$

$$\tan\theta = \dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g}$$

**Step 3:** But from geometry (Step 1), we also have $\tan\theta = r/h$. Equating the two expressions:

$$\dfrac{r}{h} = \dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g}$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $r$ from both sides (valid for $r \neq 0$):

$$\dfrac{1}{h} = \dfrac{\omega^2}{g}$$

**Step 5:** Invert both sides to obtain the governing height:

$$\boxed{h = \dfrac{g}{\omega^2}}$$

### Physical Interpretation
- The height is **independent of arm length $L$**, the **mass $m$**, and the **angle $\theta$**.
- Higher rotational speed → smaller height → balls fly out (visual confirmation).
- At $\omega = 0$, $h \to \infty$ — the governor is "fully extended downward" with no rotation.

### Numerical Verification
Let $N = 60$ RPM, then $\omega = 2\pi \times 60 / 60 = 2\pi \approx 6.283$ rad/s.
$$h = \dfrac{9.81}{(6.283)^2} = \dfrac{9.81}{39.48} \approx 0.248 \text{ m} = 248 \text{ mm}$$

At $N = 300$ RPM, $\omega = 31.416$ rad/s:
$$h = \dfrac{9.81}{(31.416)^2} = \dfrac{9.81}{986.96} \approx 0.00994 \text{ m} \approx 10 \text{ mm}$$

→ As speed rises by 5×, height shrinks by 25× (consistent with $\omega^2$ dependence).

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Equilibrium of a Porter Governor (With Sleeve Friction)

### Statement
*Derive the equilibrium equation for a Porter governor in the presence of sleeve friction.*

### Setup
A Porter governor is a Watt governor with a **central sleeve** of weight $W_s = m_s g$ sliding on the spindle. The sleeve exerts a downward pull $F_s$ on the ball via the lower arm. Friction $F_f$ opposes sleeve motion (acts upward when sleeve rises, downward when sleeve descends).

**Variables (additional):**
- $m_s$ = mass of central sleeve (kg)
- $F_s = m_s g$ = load on sleeve (N)
- $F_f$ = friction force at sleeve = $\mu \cdot F_s$ (N), where $\mu$ is the friction coefficient at the spindle
- $\alpha$ = angle of lower arm with vertical

### Force Resolution

**Horizontal equilibrium of ball:**
$$F_{s1} \sin\theta + F_{s2} \sin\alpha = m\omega^2 r \quad \text{...(A)}$$

**Vertical equilibrium of ball:**
$$F_{s1} \cos\theta - F_{s2} \cos\alpha - mg - F_{s2}^{eff} = 0$$

But more conveniently, consider equilibrium of the **sleeve + ball system vertically**. The total downward load on the spindle from one ball via the lower arm is the vertical component of the lower-arm tension. By resolving along the lower arm at the sleeve pivot:

$$F_{s2} \cos\alpha = mg + F_s + F_f \quad \text{(for sleeve rising, friction acts downward on sleeve → upward on ball)}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Take moments about the spindle axis for the **lower arm**.
The ball is at radius $r$, height $h_2$ (pivot to ball along lower arm if pivot is at sleeve). Force balance on the ball in vertical direction:
$$F_{s2} \cos\alpha = mg + \dfrac{F_s \pm F_f}{2}$$
(The factor of 2 accounts for two balls sharing the sleeve load.)

For each ball, the effective vertical load is:
$$mg + \dfrac{F_s \pm F_f}{2}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the vertical equilibrium (for each ball, considering the total downward force):
$$F_{s1} \cos\theta = mg + \dfrac{F_s \pm F_f}{2} + F_{s2} \cos\alpha$$
(But $F_{s1}\cos\theta$ is balanced by $F_{s2}\cos\alpha$ + ball weight + sleeve share.)

Standard approach (using effective vertical load $P$):

$$P = mg + \dfrac{F_s \pm F_f}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the same arm-coincidence principle:
$$\tan\theta = \dfrac{\text{Horizontal force}}{\text{Vertical force}} = \dfrac{m\omega^2 r}{P}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $P$:
$$\tan\theta = \dfrac{m\omega^2 r}{mg + \dfrac{F_s \pm F_f}{2}}$$

**Step 5:** Multiply numerator and denominator by 2:
$$\tan\theta = \dfrac{2m\omega^2 r}{2mg + F_s \pm F_f}$$

**Step 6:** Final Porter Governor Equilibrium Equation:

$$\boxed{\tan\theta = \dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g + \dfrac{F_s}{2m}} \pm \dfrac{F_f}{2m\,g_{\text{eff}}}}$$

Or, more commonly written (per ball, after simplification):

$$\boxed{\tan\theta = \dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g + \dfrac{F_s + F_f}{2m}} \quad \text{(sleeve rising)}}$$

$$\boxed{\tan\theta = \dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g + \dfrac{F_s - F_f}{2m}} \quad \text{(sleeve falling)}}$$

### Critical Discussion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **± sign** with $F_f$ produces the **hysteresis band** (also called the **dead zone**) of the governor:
> - Sleeve rising (load decreasing, speed rising): friction opposes motion → **higher** centrifugal force needed → **higher** speed required to lift the sleeve.
> - Sleeve falling (load increasing, speed falling): friction assists motion → **lower** speed needed to drop the sleeve.
> - The difference between these two speeds defines the **insensitiveness** of the governor.

This is why **Hartnell governors** (spring-loaded, negligible friction) are preferred for high-precision turbine speed control.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Hartnell Governor: Spring Force vs. Sleeve Lift

### Statement
*Derive the relation between angular velocity $\omega$, sleeve lift $h$, and spring force for a Hartnell governor.*

### Setup
A Hartnell governor has:
- **Two flyballs** attached to **bell-crank levers** pivoted on the spindle frame.
- The upper arm is at angle $\theta$ to the vertical.
- The lower arm carries a roller that contacts a sleeve; the roller lifts/lowers the sleeve.
- A **calibrated helical spring** of stiffness $k$ and initial compression $c$ (i.e., pre-load) pushes the sleeve down.
- Sleeve movement is amplified by the **lever ratio** $a = \dfrac{\text{vertical arm}}{\text{horizontal arm}}$ of the bell crank.

**Variables:**
- $r_1$ = minimum radius of rotation (sleeve at lowest position)
- $r_2$ = maximum radius of rotation (sleeve at highest position)
- $S_1$ = spring force at minimum radius position (N)
- $S_2$ = spring force at maximum radius position (N)
- $W$ = effective weight of sleeve assembly (N)
- $a$ = lever ratio of bell crank (dimensionless)
- $h = r_2 - r_1$ = total sleeve lift (m)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Spring stiffness in terms of $S_1$, $S_2$, $r_1$, $r_2$.**
As sleeve moves up by amount $h$, the balls move outward by $(r_2 - r_1)$. The bell-crank lever amplifies this by factor $(1 + 1/a)$ or similar, depending on geometry.

For a standard Hartnell geometry where the bell crank has ratio $a$ between the ball-end and sleeve-end:
$$\text{Sleeve lift} = \left(\dfrac{a + 1}{a}\right)(r_2 - r_1)$$

Spring compression increases linearly with sleeve lift. If $S_1$ corresponds to minimum radius and $S_2$ to maximum:
$$\text{Change in spring force} = k \cdot h$$
$$S_2 - S_1 = k \cdot h$$

**Step 2: Spring stiffness.**
$$k = \dfrac{S_2 - S_1}{h} = \dfrac{S_2 - S_1}{r_2 - r_1}$$

**Step 3: Equilibrium of the flyball at intermediate radius $r$.**
At any intermediate sleeve position (radius $r$), the spring force is:
$$S = S_1 + k(r - r_1) = S_1 + \dfrac{(S_2 - S_1)(r - r_1)}{r_2 - r_1}$$

The vertical force on the ball (combining weight, sleeve load via lever, and spring restoring force) is:
$$P = mg + \dfrac{S + W}{2 \, \text{(lever ratio correction)}}$$

For a bell-crank with horizontal arm of length $y$ and vertical arm of length $x$, ratio $a = y/x$.

**Step 4: Standard Hartnell Form.**
Combining the horizontal centrifugal force with the vertical restoring force, and noting that the lever amplifies the centrifugal force's effect on the sleeve:

$$\boxed{\omega^2 = \dfrac{S_1 + S_2 - 2W}{m\,(r_1 + r_2)} \cdot \dfrac{2(a+1)}{a}}$$

where $a$ is the lever ratio.

An equivalent and commonly-quoted form:
$$\omega^2 = \dfrac{2k}{m(r_1 + r_2)} \left(1 + \dfrac{a}{r_1}\right) - \dfrac{2c}{m(r_1 + r_2)}$$

where $c$ is the initial spring compression force.

### Numerical Example — Hartnell Governor
**Given:**
- $m = 5$ kg per ball
- $r_1 = 0.10$ m, $r_2 = 0.15$ m
- $S_1 = 200$ N, $S_2 = 400$ N
- $W = 50$ N, $a = 2$

**Find:** Range of speed (min to max).

**Solution:**

Compute the coefficient:
$$\dfrac{2(a+1)}{a} = \dfrac{2(2+1)}{2} = 3$$

Numerator term:
$$S_1 + S_2 - 2W = 200 + 400 - 100 = 500 \text{ N}$$

At **minimum radius** ($r = r_1$):
$$\omega_1^2 = \dfrac{500 \times 3}{5 \times (0.10 + 0.15)} = \dfrac{1500}{1.25} = 1200$$
$$\omega_1 = \sqrt{1200} \approx 34.64 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$N_1 = \dfrac{34.64 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 330.7 \text{ RPM}$$

At **maximum radius** ($r = r_2$):
$$\omega_2^2 = \dfrac{500 \times 3}{5 \times (0.15 + 0.15)} = \dfrac{1500}{1.50} = 1000$$
$$\omega_2 = \sqrt{1000} \approx 31.62 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$N_2 = \dfrac{31.62 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 302.0 \text{ RPM}$$

**Sleeve lift (h):**
$$h = r_2 - r_1 = 0.15 - 0.10 = 0.05 \text{ m} = 50 \text{ mm}$$

**Spring stiffness:**
$$k = \dfrac{S_2 - S_1}{h} = \dfrac{400 - 200}{0.05} = 4000 \text{ N/m}$$

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 4 — Nozzle Governing: Power Steps and Valve Sequencing

### Statement
*Show that with $N$ nozzles grouped under $n$ control valves, the turbine delivers $n+1$ discrete power levels, with step magnitude $\Delta P = P_{\text{rated}}/N$.*

### Concept
In nozzle governing, the first stage of an impulse turbine has $N$ nozzles arranged around the periphery (e.g., $N = 12$ for a typical 50 MW unit). A single rotary control valve with $n$ cam lobes can open **$1, 2, 3, \dots, n$** groups of nozzles in stepped fashion.

### Power Steps
- **Step 0:** All nozzles closed → $P = 0$.
- **Step 1:** 1 valve open → $P = P_1$.
- **Step 2:** 2 valves open → $P = 2 P_1$.
- …
- **Step $N$:** All valves open → $P = P_{\text{rated}}$.

So power increment per step:
$$\Delta P = \dfrac{P_{\text{rated}}}{N}$$

If a partial step is required (for smoother regulation), **throttle governing** is superimposed — the partially-open valve additionally throttles its admitted steam.

### Number of Distinct Power Levels
A governor with $N$ nozzles can produce at most $N + 1$ discrete power levels. In practice, valves overlap by ~10% to avoid the dead-zone of transition (this is called **overlap or partial overlap**).

### Numerical Example
**Given:**
- Rated power: $P_{\text{rated}} = 10{,}000$ kW
- Total nozzles: $N = 10$
- Grouped into 5 cam lobes (2 nozzles per group)

**Find:** Power at each step.

**Solution:**
$$\Delta P = \dfrac{10{,}000}{10} = 1000 \text{ kW per nozzle}$$

| Step | Nozzles Open | Power (kW) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 2 | 2000 |
| 2 | 4 | 4000 |
| 3 | 6 | 6000 |
| 4 | 8 | 8000 |
| 5 | 10 | 10,000 |

For fine control, a **throttle valve** is mounted on each cam-port so that within a single step, continuous variation of ~±10% is possible.

---

## 3.5 Sensitiveness of a Governor

The **sensitiveness** of a governor is defined as the ratio of the mean speed range to the mean speed:

$$\text{Sensitiveness} = \dfrac{N_2 - N_1}{N_{\text{mean}}}$$

where $N_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{N_1 + N_2}{2}$.

- **Watt governor**: sensitiveness ≈ 1/15 to 1/20 (poor).
- **Porter governor**: ≈ 1/40 to 1/50 (moderate).
- **Hartnell governor**: ≈ 1/100 to 1/200 (excellent).
- **Hydraulic/EHC servo**: ≈ 1/500 (industrial standard for steam turbines).

A **stable governor** requires that increasing speed causes the sleeve to rise (positive feedback for control), but the **droop characteristic** must be positive — speed rises slightly with load decrease.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a centrifugal governor. State its functions in a steam turbine system.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **centrifugal governor** is a mechanical speed-sensing device that uses the centrifugal force developed by rotating flyweights to produce a mechanical displacement proportional to the square of the angular velocity of the shaft.

**Functions in a steam turbine:**
1. **Speed Sensing:** Converts shaft speed into a sleeve displacement (feedback signal).
2. **Load Sharing:** Through the droop characteristic, enables parallel operation of multiple generator units on the grid.
3. **Overspeed Protection:** Triggers an emergency trip if speed exceeds 110% of rated (e.g., loss of load).

[3 marks]

---

### Q2. What is nozzle governing? How does it differ from throttle governing?
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Nozzle governing** is a method of regulating steam flow in an impulse turbine by **varying the number of nozzles** through which steam is admitted, using a multi-port cam-operated valve. Each step opens or closes a discrete group of nozzles.

**Difference from throttle governing:**

| Feature | Throttle Governing | Nozzle Governing |
|---|---|---|
| Steam flow control | Continuous throttling of single path | Stepped admission of nozzle groups |
| Stage efficiency | Falls at part load (over-expansion) | Maintained at each step (no throttling) |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex cam/valve |
| Application | Small/medium turbines | Large multi-nozzle turbines |

[3 marks]

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing $\tan\theta = \omega^2 r / g$ for a *Porter* governor (forgetting the sleeve load $F_s$). Always check the type first.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the **lever ratio $a$** in Hartnell calculations — this costs 2–3 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In nozzle-governing problems, students often confuse *valve positions* with *nozzle count*. Always state **N = total nozzles, n = nozzles per group**.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Unit mismatches — convert RPM to rad/s **before** substituting into $\omega^2$. Marks are deducted for using N directly in $\omega^2$ formulas.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Centrifugal Governor Focus
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the equilibrium height of a **Watt governor** and show that it is independent of arm length. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **Porter governor** has each ball of mass 6 kg, arms each of length 250 mm, top arm angle 30° with vertical, bottom arm angle 40° with vertical. Sleeve mass = 40 kg, friction coefficient at sleeve = 0.1. Find the equilibrium speed when (i) sleeve is rising, (ii) sleeve is falling. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation of Watt Governor Height**

Refer to **Section 3.1 Derivation 1** above for the complete step-by-step derivation.

**Quick Recap (Key Steps):**
1. Force on ball: $W = mg$ (down), $F_c = m\omega^2 r$ (outward).
2. Arm direction: $\tan\theta = r/h$.
3. Equilibrium: $\tan\theta = F_c/W = \omega^2 r/g$.
4. Equating: $r/h = \omega^2 r/g \Rightarrow h = g/\omega^2$.

**Conclusion:** $h$ depends only on $\omega$ and $g$, not on $m$, $L$, or $\theta$. ✓

[7 marks — awarded for: 1 mark FBD, 2 marks force resolution, 2 marks equating, 1 mark conclusion, 1 mark for independence statement]

---

**Part (b) — Porter Governor Numerical**

**Given:**
- $m = 6$ kg (each ball), $L = 250$ mm = 0.25 m
- $\theta = 30°$ (top arm), $\alpha = 40°$ (bottom arm)
- $m_s = 40$ kg, $\mu = 0.1$
- Two balls (factor of 2 in sleeve load sharing)

**Step 1: Compute geometric quantities.**
$$r = L \sin\theta = 0.25 \sin 30° = 0.25 \times 0.5 = 0.125 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Sleeve load per ball.**
$$F_s = \dfrac{m_s g}{2} = \dfrac{40 \times 9.81}{2} = 196.2 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Friction force per ball.**
$$F_f = \dfrac{\mu \cdot m_s \cdot g}{2} = \dfrac{0.1 \times 40 \times 9.81}{2} = 19.62 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Equilibrium equation for sleeve rising (friction adds to effective vertical load):**
$$\tan\theta = \dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g + \dfrac{F_s + F_f}{2m}}$$

$$\tan 30° = \dfrac{0.5774}{1 + \dfrac{196.2 + 19.62}{2 \times 6 \times 9.81}}$$

$$0.5774 = \dfrac{\omega^2 \times 0.125}{9.81 + \dfrac{215.82}{117.72}}$$

$$0.5774 = \dfrac{\omega^2 \times 0.125}{9.81 + 1.834}$$

$$0.5774 = \dfrac{\omega^2 \times 0.125}{11.644}$$

$$\omega^2 = \dfrac{0.5774 \times 11.644}{0.125} = \dfrac{6.724}{0.125} = 53.79$$

$$\omega_{\text{rise}} = \sqrt{53.79} = 7.334 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$N_{\text{rise}} = \dfrac{7.334 \times 60}{2\pi} = 70.03 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 5: Equilibrium for sleeve falling (friction subtracts):**
$$\tan\theta = \dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g + \dfrac{F_s - F_f}{2m}}$$

$$0.5774 = \dfrac{\omega^2 \times 0.125}{9.81 + \dfrac{196.2 - 19.62}{117.72}}$$

$$0.5774 = \dfrac{\omega^2 \times 0.125}{9.81 + \dfrac{176.58}{117.72}}$$

$$0.5774 = \dfrac{\omega^2 \times 0.125}{9.81 + 1.500}$$

$$0.5774 = \dfrac{\omega^2 \times 0.125}{11.310}$$

$$\omega^2 = \dfrac{0.5774 \times 11.310}{0.125} = \dfrac{6.531}{0.125} = 52.25$$

$$\omega_{\text{fall}} = \sqrt{52.25} = 7.228 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$N_{\text{fall}} = \dfrac{7.228 \times 60}{2\pi} = 69.02 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 6: Range and sensitiveness.**
$$\Delta N = 70.03 - 69.02 = 1.01 \text{ RPM}$$

$$\text{Sensitiveness} = \dfrac{1.01}{69.52} \approx 0.0145 \text{ or } 1.45\%$$

[7 marks — awarded for: 2 marks setup, 2 marks sleeve load and friction, 2 marks solving both cases, 1 mark interpretation]

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Nozzle Governing Focus
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **principle and working of nozzle governing** in a steam turbine. What are its advantages over throttle governing? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 6 MW impulse turbine has 12 nozzles in the first-stage ring. It is fitted with a nozzle-governing valve that admits steam to groups of 2, 4, and 6 nozzles in three discrete steps. Calculate the power delivered at each step and the per-nozzle flow if the rated steam consumption is 5 kg/s per nozzle. Take inlet steam enthalpy = 3200 kJ/kg and exit enthalpy from stage = 2700 kJ/kg. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Principle and Working of Nozzle Governing**

**Principle:** In an impulse turbine, the total power is the sum of the power developed in each nozzle. By **selectively admitting steam to groups of nozzles**, the total power can be varied in discrete steps without changing the working pressure ratio in any *individual* nozzle (which would happen in throttling).

**Working:**
1. The first-stage nozzle ring is divided into $N$ nozzles (typically arranged symmetrically).
2. A single rotary **cam-operated valve** is mounted at the steam chest.
3. The valve has $n$ ports, each connected to a group of $n_k$ nozzles.
4. As the valve rotates under governor control, it admits steam to **1, 2, 3, ..., N nozzles in steps**.
5. At each step, the admitted nozzles operate at **design pressure ratio** → no throttling loss → high part-load efficiency.
6. The remaining (closed) nozzles carry no steam and develop no power.

**Advantages over throttle governing:**
1. **Higher part-load efficiency:** Nozzles operate at design conditions even at part load.
2. **Less steam wastage** from throttling losses (reheat factor and stage efficiency preserved).
3. **Faster response** at large load changes (because full-pressure steam is admitted instantly).
4. **Better suited for large turbines** with many nozzles.
5. **Disadvantages:** More complex valve mechanism, stepped (non-continuous) power output.

**Diagram (Description):**
- Steam chest → rotary valve (with cam profile) → ports to nozzle groups → nozzles → blades.
- Governor linkage actuates valve rotation.

[7 marks — awarded for: 2 marks principle, 2 marks working description, 2 marks advantages, 1 mark diagram]

---

**Part (b) — Numerical on Nozzle Governing**

**Given:**
- $P_{\text{rated}} = 6$ MW = 6000 kW
- Total nozzles: $N = 12$
- Valve admits in three steps: 2, 4, 6 nozzles
- Rated steam per nozzle: $\dot{m}_n = 5$ kg/s
- $h_1 = 3200$ kJ/kg, $h_2 = 2700$ kJ/kg

**Step 1: Enthalpy drop per nozzle (design):**
$$\Delta h = h_1 - h_2 = 3200 - 2700 = 500 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2: Power per nozzle (rated):**
$$P_n = \dot{m}_n \cdot \Delta h = 5 \times 500 = 2500 \text{ kW} = 2.5 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 3: Total rated power check:**
$$P_{\text{rated}} = N \times P_n = 12 \times 2.5 = 30 \text{ MW}$$

**Note:** The problem statement says 6 MW, but with the given $\dot{m}_n$ and $\Delta h$, the calculated rated power is 30 MW. This is a typical KTU-style ambiguity. We will present the **per-step power** based on the actual nozzle count and data:

**Step 4: Power at each step:**

| Step | Nozzles Open | Total Steam Flow (kg/s) | Power (kW) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 10 | $10 \times 500 = 5000$ |
| 2 | 4 | 20 | $20 \times 500 = 10{,}000$ |
| 3 | 6 | 30 | $30 \times 500 = 15{,}000$ |
| Rated | 12 | 60 | $60 \times 500 = 30{,}000$ |

**Step 5: Per-nozzle specific steam consumption (SSCN):**
$$\text{SSCN} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta h} = \dfrac{1}{500} = 0.002 \text{ kg/kJ} = 2 \text{ kg/kWh (after unit conversion)}$$

Wait — let me recompute properly:
$$1 \text{ kWh} = 3600 \text{ kJ}$$
$$\text{SSCN} = \dfrac{1 \text{ kg/s}}{2500 \text{ kW}} \times 3600 = \dfrac{3600}{2500} = 1.44 \text{ kg/kWh per nozzle}$$

**Step 6: Overall specific steam consumption at rated load:**
$$\text{SSC}_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{N \dot{m}_n}{P_{\text{rated}}} = \dfrac{60 \text{ kg/s}}{30{,}000 \text{ kW}} = 0.002 \text{ kg/kW·s}$$

Converted: $0.002 \times 3600 = 7.2 \text{ kg/kWh}$.

**Conclusion:** Nozzle governing delivers power in **discrete steps of 5 MW** (for 2-nozzle groups), with each step maintaining design efficiency.

[7 marks — awarded for: 2 marks enthalpy drop, 2 marks per-step power, 1 mark specific steam consumption, 1 mark efficiency discussion, 1 mark presentation]

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Points for Last-Minute KTU Revision:**

### A. Centrifugal Governor Essentials

- ✅ **Watt Governor:** $h = g/\omega^2$ — independent of arm length, mass, and angle.
- ✅ **Porter Governor:** Includes sleeve load $F_s$ and friction $F_f$ — produces a hysteresis band (insensitiveness).
- ✅ **Hartnell Governor:** Spring-loaded, bell-crank lever — used in **all modern steam turbines**; negligible friction.
- ✅ **Centrifugal force** always equals $m\omega^2 r$ and acts **horizontally outward**.
- ✅ **Governing equation pattern:** $\tan\theta = \dfrac{\omega^2 r}{g_{\text{eff}}}$ where $g_{\text{eff}}$ includes sleeve load and friction.
- ✅ **Stability:** A governor is stable if sleeve rises with rising speed (i.e., $dh/d\omega^2 > 0$).
- ✅ **Sensitiveness:** Lower value = better governor. Hartnell is best among mechanical governors.
- ✅ **Overspeed trip** at 110% of rated speed is **mandatory** for all steam turbines (per Indian Boiler Regulations).

### B. Nozzle Governing Essentials

- ✅ Nozzle governing varies the **number of effective nozzles**, NOT the pressure drop across them.
- ✅ Each step admits steam at **full boiler pressure** → no throttling loss → high part-load efficiency.
- ✅ **Number of power levels** = number of nozzle groups + 1 (for zero-admission).
- ✅ **Overlap:** Valves overlap by ~10% at transition to avoid momentary dead zones.
- ✅ **Combined governing:** Large turbines use **throttle + nozzle** together for fine control within each step.
- ✅ **Best suited for Curtis and Rateau** impulse turbines (multi-nozzle first stage).
- ✅ **Not applicable to reaction turbines** (no discrete nozzle rings — continuous blade passages).

### C. Unit Conversion Reminders

- ✅ $\omega \text{ (rad/s)} = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ where $N$ is in RPM.
- ✅ 1 kW = 1 kJ/s; 1 kWh = 3600 kJ.
- ✅ Force in Newtons = mass (kg) × 9.81 m/s² (use $g = 9.81$ unless otherwise specified).

### D. Numerical Problem Pattern Recognition

| Given | Find | Formula to use |
|---|---|---|
| $\omega, g$ | $h$ (Watt) | $h = g/\omega^2$ |
| $m, L, F_s, F_f$ | $N_{\text{rise/fall}}$ (Porter) | $\tan\theta = \omega^2 r / [g + (F_s \pm F_f)/(2m)]$ |
| $r_1, r_2, S_1, S_2, a, W, m$ | $N_1, N_2$ (Hartnell) | $\omega^2 = \dfrac{2(a+1)(S_1 + S_2 - 2W)}{a \, m (r_1 + r_2)}$ |
| $N$ nozzles, $\Delta h$, $\dot{m}_n$ | Power steps | $P_{\text{step}} = n \cdot \dot{m}_n \cdot \Delta h$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy:** In KTU ESE, questions on this topic are **almost guaranteed** once a year. Always draw the FBD, label every force with magnitude and direction, then proceed algebraically. Marks are awarded for **methodology**, not just the final number.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Standard Cycles - Analysis and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Air Standard Cycles - Analysis and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Air Standard Cycle** is an idealized thermodynamic cycle in which the working fluid is assumed to behave as a perfect gas (air) with constant specific heats, and the entire cycle is modeled on the **P-V (Pressure-Volume)** and **T-S (Temperature-Entropy)** planes. Real internal combustion engines and gas turbines deviate from actual cycles due to fuel combustion, chemical reactions, heat losses, and variable specific heats. The air standard cycle abstracts these complexities by replacing the actual combustion process with an equivalent **heat addition from an external source**, enabling purely thermodynamic analysis of engine performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 focuses on the analysis of **Otto Cycle, Diesel Cycle, Dual (Limited Pressure / Sabathe) Cycle**, and **Brayton Cycle**, along with their **Air Standard Efficiencies**, **Mean Effective Pressure (MEP)**, and **performance comparisons**.

### 1.2 Core Assumptions of Air Standard Cycles (The "Air Standard" Hypotheses)

To make real engine analysis mathematically tractable, KTU-approved air standard analysis relies on these assumptions:

1. **Perfect Gas Behavior:** The working fluid (air) obeys the ideal gas equation $PV = mRT$.
2. **Constant Specific Heats:** $C_p$ and $C_v$ remain constant throughout the cycle (evaluated at room temperature $T_1 = 300\text{ K}$).
3. **Closed System:** No working fluid enters or leaves the system; a fixed mass of air undergoes the cycle.
4. **All Processes are Internally Reversible:** Frictionless, quasi-equilibrium processes.
5. **Combustion Replaced by Heat Addition:** Heat is added from a constant-temperature (isothermal) or constant-volume (isochoric) or constant-pressure (isobaric) external source.
6. **Exhaust Replaced by Heat Rejection:** Heat is rejected to a constant-temperature sink.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Bouncing Piston" Analogy:**
> Imagine a bicycle pump sealed at one end. If you push the handle quickly (compression) and release, the air inside heats up. If you then imagine placing a tiny heater inside to add heat, expanding the air (power stroke), and finally cooling it back down to room temperature before the next cycle begins — that *imagined* sequence of compression, heating, expansion, and cooling is precisely an **Air Standard Cycle**. The actual spark ignition or fuel injection is replaced by a controlled external heater so we can use pure thermodynamic math.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants for KTU Calculations

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314\text{ kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$
- **Specific Heat of Air at Constant Volume:** $C_v = 0.718\text{ kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$
- **Specific Heat of Air at Constant Pressure:** $C_p = 1.005\text{ kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$
- **Ratio of Specific Heats:** $\gamma = C_p / C_v = 1.4$
- **Gas Constant for Air:** $R = C_p - C_v = 0.287\text{ kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If a problem does not specify which $C_p$, $C_v$, or $\gamma$ to use, you **MUST** use the standard air values above. If values are given in the problem statement, always use the provided values.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Major Air Standard Cycles

#### 2.1.1 Otto Cycle (Constant Volume Heat Addition)
Represents the ideal cycle for **Spark Ignition (SI) engines** (petrol engines).

| Process | Description | Thermodynamic Process |
|---|---|---|
| 1-2 | Isentropic Compression | Adiabatic Reversible |
| 2-3 | Heat Addition at Constant Volume | Isochoric |
| 3-4 | Isentropic Expansion | Adiabatic Reversible |
| 4-1 | Heat Rejection at Constant Volume | Isochoric |

#### 2.1.2 Diesel Cycle (Constant Pressure Heat Addition)
Represents the ideal cycle for **Compression Ignition (CI) engines** (diesel engines).

| Process | Description | Thermodynamic Process |
|---|---|---|
| 1-2 | Isentropic Compression | Adiabatic Reversible |
| 2-3 | Heat Addition at Constant Pressure | Isobaric |
| 3-4 | Isentropic Expansion | Adiabatic Reversible |
| 4-1 | Heat Rejection at Constant Volume | Isochoric |

#### 2.1.3 Dual Cycle (Limited Pressure / Sabathe Cycle)
Represents the modern **high-speed CI engine** with both constant volume and constant pressure heat addition.

| Process | Description | Thermodynamic Process |
|---|---|---|
| 1-2 | Isentropic Compression | Adiabatic Reversible |
| 2-3 | Heat Addition at Constant Volume | Isochoric (fraction $\beta$) |
| 3-4 | Heat Addition at Constant Pressure | Isobaric (fraction $\gamma_{cut}$) |
| 4-5 | Isentropic Expansion | Adiabatic Reversible |
| 5-1 | Heat Rejection at Constant Volume | Isochoric |

#### 2.1.4 Brayton Cycle (Gas Turbine Cycle)
Represents the ideal cycle for **gas turbine power plants** and **jet propulsion**.

| Process | Description | Thermodynamic Process |
|---|---|---|
| 1-2 | Isentropic Compression | Adiabatic Reversible |
| 2-3 | Heat Addition at Constant Pressure | Isobaric |
| 3-4 | Isentropic Expansion | Adiabatic Reversible |
| 4-1 | Heat Rejection at Constant Pressure | Isobaric |

### 2.2 Key Performance Parameters

#### 2.2.1 Compression Ratio ($r$ or $r_k$)
Ratio of the **maximum volume** (before compression) to the **minimum volume** (after compression):
$$r = r_k = \frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{V_{\max}}{V_{\min}}$$

#### 2.2.2 Cut-off Ratio ($r_c$)
Ratio of volumes at the end and start of constant-pressure heat addition:
$$r_c = \frac{V_4}{V_3}$$

#### 2.2.3 Pressure Ratio ($r_p$)
Ratio of pressures at the end and start of constant-volume heat addition:
$$r_p = \frac{P_3}{P_2}$$

#### 2.2.4 Expansion Ratio ($r_e$)
Ratio of volumes for the isentropic expansion process:
$$r_e = \frac{V_5}{V_4}$$

#### 2.2.5 Mean Effective Pressure (MEP)
A hypothetical constant pressure that, if applied throughout the power stroke, would produce the same net work as the actual cycle:
$$\text{MEP} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{V_1 - V_2} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{V_1\left(1 - \frac{1}{r}\right)}$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Exam Note:** Memorize the efficiency formulas and the relationships between state points. Most 14-mark derivations require these. Use $\vert$ instead of `|` to denote absolute values in formulas.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Otto Cycle Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Diesel Cycle Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{Diesel}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma \cdot (r_c - 1)}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Dual Cycle Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{Dual}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{\beta \cdot r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{\left(\beta - 1\right) + \gamma \cdot \beta \cdot \left(r_c - 1\right)}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Brayton Cycle Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{Brayton}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Heat Added (Otto) | $Q_{\text{in}} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2)$ | kJ |
| 6 | Heat Added (Diesel) | $Q_{\text{in}} = m C_p (T_3 - T_2)$ | kJ |
| 7 | Net Work Output | $W_{\text{net}} = Q_{\text{in}} - Q_{\text{out}}$ | kJ |
| 8 | Mean Effective Pressure | $\text{MEP} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{V_{\text{swept}}} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{V_1 - V_2}$ | kPa |
| 9 | Specific Volume at State 1 | $v_1 = \frac{R T_1}{P_1}$ | m³/kg |
| 10 | Isentropic Relation (Ideal Gas) | $T_2/T_1 = (V_1/V_2)^{\gamma-1} = r^{\gamma-1}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Isentropic Pressure Relation | $P_2/P_1 = (V_1/V_2)^{\gamma} = r^{\gamma}$ | dimensionless |

Where:
- $\beta = r_p = P_3/P_2$ (pressure ratio for dual cycle)
- $r_c = V_4/V_3$ (cut-off ratio for diesel/dual)
- $r = V_1/V_2$ (compression ratio)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Otto Cycle** analysis underpins the design of petrol car engines, where high compression ratios improve efficiency but are limited by **knock**.
- **Diesel Cycle** analysis drives heavy-duty truck, locomotive, and marine engine design, with higher compression ratios allowing higher efficiencies.
- **Dual Cycle** is the practical model for modern high-speed diesel engines where fuel injection timing causes a hybrid combustion profile.
- **Brayton Cycle** governs gas turbine design for jet aircraft propulsion, power generation, and industrial drives where compactness and high power-to-weight ratios are critical.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Otto Cycle Air Standard Efficiency

**Given:** Ideal Otto cycle with states 1, 2, 3, 4 undergoing 1→2 isentropic compression, 2→3 constant-volume heat addition, 3→4 isentropic expansion, 4→1 constant-volume heat rejection.

**Goal:** Derive $\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$.

---

**Step 1 — Define Air Standard Efficiency**

By definition, thermal efficiency is the ratio of net work output to heat input:
$$\eta = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$$

This row converts the verbal efficiency definition into the standard closed-cycle formula using $W_{\text{net}} = Q_{\text{in}} - Q_{\text{out}}$.

---

**Step 2 — Express Heat Added $Q_{\text{in}}$ (Process 2→3)**

Heat addition occurs at **constant volume**, so the appropriate specific heat is $C_v$:
$$Q_{\text{in}} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2)$$

This row uses the first law for a closed system at constant volume: $\delta Q = m C_v dT$, integrated from state 2 to state 3.

---

**Step 3 — Express Heat Rejected $Q_{\text{out}}$ (Process 4→1)**

Heat rejection occurs at **constant volume** during the exhaust process:
$$Q_{\text{out}} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$$

This row applies the same first-law logic for process 4→1, noting that $T_4 > T_1$ so $Q_{\text{out}}$ is positive as a magnitude of rejected heat.

---

**Step 4 — Substitute into Efficiency Expression**
$$\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{m C_v (T_4 - T_1)}{m C_v (T_3 - T_2)} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$

The mass $m$ and $C_v$ cancel since both processes are at constant volume.

---

**Step 5 — Apply Isentropic Relations for Processes 1→2 and 3→4**

For isentropic process 1→2 (compression):
$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma-1} = r^{\gamma-1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$$

For isentropic process 3→4 (expansion):
$$\frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{V_4}{V_3}\right)^{\gamma-1}$$

In the Otto cycle, $V_4 = V_1$ and $V_3 = V_2$, therefore:
$$\frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma-1} = r^{\gamma-1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r^{\gamma-1}}$$

This row substitutes the geometric fact that the volumes at states 4 and 1 are equal (BDC) and at states 3 and 2 are equal (TDC), allowing the same compression ratio $r$ to govern both isentropic processes.

---

**Step 6 — Express Temperature Differences in Terms of $r$, $T_1$, and $T_3$**

Rearrange the two expressions to express all temperatures relative to $T_1$ and $T_3$:
$$T_2 - T_1 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} - T_1 = T_1 \left(r^{\gamma-1} - 1\right)$$
$$T_3 - T_4 = T_3 - \frac{T_3}{r^{\gamma-1}} = T_3 \left(1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}\right) = \frac{T_3 \left(r^{\gamma-1} - 1\right)}{r^{\gamma-1}}$$

This row algebraically isolates the differences needed in the efficiency expression.

---

**Step 7 — Substitute into Efficiency Formula**
$$\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{T_1 \left(r^{\gamma-1} - 1\right)}{\dfrac{T_3 \left(r^{\gamma-1} - 1\right)}{r^{\gamma-1}}}$$

The common factor $(r^{\gamma-1} - 1)$ cancels:
$$\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{T_1}{T_3} \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$$

---

**Step 8 — Express $T_1/T_3$ Using Isentropic Expansion**

From step 5, $T_3 = T_4 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$. Combining with the constant-volume process $T_4/T_1 = T_3/T_2$, we get:
$$\frac{T_1}{T_3} = \frac{1}{r^{2(\gamma-1)}} \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{r^{2(\gamma-1)}}$$

Wait — re-derive cleanly: in the Otto cycle, by the property of the closed cycle and isentropic processes:
$$\frac{T_3}{T_2} = \frac{T_4}{T_1} \quad \text{(since both equal } r^{\gamma-1}\text{)}$$

So the temperature ratio simplifies as:
$$\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2} = 1 - \frac{T_1 (T_4/T_1 - 1)}{T_2 (T_3/T_2 - 1)} = 1 - \frac{T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} - T_1}{T_2 (r^{\gamma-1} - 1)} \cdot \frac{1}{1}$$

Substituting $T_2 = T_1 r^{\gamma-1}$:
$$\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{T_1 (r^{\gamma-1} - 1)}{T_1 r^{\gamma-1} (r^{\gamma-1} - 1)} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$$

---

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}}$$

This derivation is the cornerstone for Module 3 and the same logical chain (with minor variations for $r_c$ or $r_p$) yields the Diesel and Dual cycle formulas.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Cycle Comparisons at Fixed Compression Ratio

| Parameter | Otto | Diesel | Dual | Brayton |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Heat Addition Mode | Constant $V$ | Constant $P$ | Both | Constant $P$ |
| Heat Rejection Mode | Constant $V$ | Constant $V$ | Constant $V$ | Constant $P$ |
| Independent Variables | $r$ | $r$, $r_c$ | $r$, $r_p$, $r_c$ | $r_p$ |
| Efficiency at same $r$ | Highest | Lowest | In-between | N/A (different geometry) |
| Application | SI engines | Slow CI engines | Fast CI engines | Gas turbines |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** "For the same compression ratio and heat input, which cycle has the highest efficiency?" — Answer: **Otto**, because constant-volume heat addition produces the highest peak temperature, maximizing $T_3$.

### 3.3 Mean Effective Pressure (MEP) Derivation Summary

For Otto cycle, after deriving $W_{\text{net}}$ per unit mass:
$$w_{\text{net}} = q_{\text{in}} \cdot \eta_{\text{Otto}} = C_v (T_3 - T_2) \left(1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}\right)$$

Using $T_2 = T_1 r^{\gamma-1}$ and ideal gas law:
$$\text{MEP} = \frac{w_{\text{net}}}{v_1 - v_2} = \frac{w_{\text{net}}}{v_1 (1 - 1/r)} = \frac{P_1 r}{(\gamma - 1)(r - 1)} \cdot \left[\frac{T_3}{T_1} r^{-(\gamma-1)} - 1\right] \left[1 - r^{-(\gamma-1)}\right]$$

The key engineering insight: **higher MEP means more compact engine producing more work per unit volume** — critical for automotive and aerospace applications.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Air Standard Cycle Analysis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real Engine Data] --> B{Choose Ideal Cycle Model}
    B -->|SI Petrol Engine| C[Otto Cycle]
    B -->|Slow CI Diesel| D[Diesel Cycle]
    B -->|Fast CI Diesel| E[Dual Cycle]
    B -->|Gas Turbine| F[Brayton Cycle]
    C --> G[Apply Air Standard Assumptions]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute State Point Temperatures and Pressures]
    H --> I[Calculate Heat Added Qin]
    H --> J[Calculate Heat Rejected Qout]
    I --> K[Compute Net Work Wnet]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Evaluate Air Standard Efficiency]
    L --> M[Compute Mean Effective Pressure MEP]
    M --> N[Performance Comparison and Selection]
```

### 4.2 P-V and T-S Diagram Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PV_DIAGRAM["P-V PLANE"]
        P1[State 1 BDC Start] -->|Isentropic Compression 1 to 2| P2[State 2 TDC Pre Heat]
        P2 -->|Constant Volume Heat Add 2 to 3| P3[State 3 TDC Post Heat]
        P3 -->|Isentropic Expansion 3 to 4| P4[State 4 BDC End]
        P4 -->|Constant Volume Heat Reject 4 to 1| P1
    end
    subgraph TS_DIAGRAM["T-S PLANE"]
        T1[State 1 Low T High S] -->|Isentropic Vertical 1 to 2| T2[State 2 Med T Med S]
        T2 -->|Constant Volume Heat Add 2 to 3| T3[State 3 High T High S]
        T3 -->|Isentropic Vertical 3 to 4| T4[State 4 Med T Med S]
        T4 -->|Constant Volume Heat Reject 4 to 1| T1
    end
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram of Heat Addition Modes Across Cycles

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Air Standard Cycle Heat Addition] --> B{Heat Addition Mode}
    B -->|Constant Volume| C[Otto Cycle]
    B -->|Constant Pressure| D[Diesel or Brayton Cycle]
    B -->|Mixed Constant V then P| E[Dual or Limited Pressure Cycle]
    C --> F[Highest Peak Temperature]
    D --> G[Moderate Peak Temperature]
    E --> H[Intermediate Peak Temperature]
    F --> I[Highest Air Standard Efficiency]
    G --> J[Lower Efficiency but Practical CI]
    H --> K[Best Match for Modern High Speed CI]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block schematic maps the logical flow of the air standard analysis. In the P-V plane, the area enclosed by 1-2-3-4-1 represents the **net work output** of the cycle. In the T-S plane, the area enclosed by 1-2-3-4-1 represents the **net heat input** (since net work equals net heat in a closed cycle).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define Air Standard Cycle. State the assumptions made for the air standard cycle analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

An air standard cycle is an idealized thermodynamic cycle in which a fixed mass of air (assumed to behave as a perfect gas with constant specific heats) undergoes a closed sequence of processes. The actual combustion and exhaust processes of a real engine are replaced by equivalent heat addition and heat rejection from external sources so that the cycle can be analyzed using pure thermodynamic principles.

**Assumptions:**
1. The working fluid is a perfect gas (air) with constant specific heats $C_p$ and $C_v$.
2. The mass of air in the system remains constant throughout the cycle.
3. All processes are internally reversible.
4. The ideal gas equation $PV = mRT$ holds at every state point.
5. Combustion is replaced by heat addition from an external source at constant volume, constant pressure, or a combination.

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Define Mean Effective Pressure (MEP) and state its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

Mean Effective Pressure (MEP) is defined as the hypothetical constant pressure which, if maintained throughout the power stroke, would produce the same net work as the actual variable-pressure cycle:
$$\text{MEP} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{V_{\text{swept}}} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{V_1 - V_2}$$

**Significance:**
- MEP is a **figure of merit** for comparing engines of different sizes.
- A higher MEP indicates that the engine produces more work per unit displaced volume, meaning a more compact and lighter engine for the same power output.
- MEP is independent of the number of cylinders, making it ideal for performance benchmarking.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the air standard efficiency of an Otto cycle in terms of compression ratio. (7 marks)

**(b)** An Otto cycle has a compression ratio of 8:1. At the beginning of compression, the air is at $100\text{ kPa}$ and $27^{\circ}\text{C}$. The maximum temperature during the cycle is $2000\text{ K}$. Determine:
- (i) The air standard efficiency
- (ii) The net work output per kg of air
- (iii) The mean effective pressure (MEP)

Assume $C_v = 0.718\text{ kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$, and $R = 0.287\text{ kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

The derivation follows Section 3.1 above. Key steps:

$$\eta_{\text{Otto}} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$$

$$Q_{\text{in}} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2), \quad Q_{\text{out}} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$$

Using isentropic relations $T_2 = T_1 r^{\gamma-1}$ and $T_4 = T_3 / r^{\gamma-1}$:

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}}$$

This is independent of the heat added — efficiency depends only on $r$ and $\gamma$.

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $r = 8$, $T_1 = 300\text{ K}$, $P_1 = 100\text{ kPa}$, $T_3 = 2000\text{ K}$.

**(i) Air Standard Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{1}{8^{1.4-1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{8^{0.4}}$$

Calculate $8^{0.4}$:
$$\ln(8^{0.4}) = 0.4 \times \ln 8 = 0.4 \times 2.0794 = 0.8318$$
$$8^{0.4} = e^{0.8318} = 2.2974$$

Therefore:
$$\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.2974} = 1 - 0.4353 = 0.5647 = 56.47\%$$

**(ii) Net Work Output per kg:**

First, calculate $T_2$ using isentropic relation:
$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} = 300 \times 2.2974 = 689.21\text{ K}$$

Heat added:
$$Q_{\text{in}} = C_v (T_3 - T_2) = 0.718 \times (2000 - 689.21) = 0.718 \times 1310.79 = 941.15\text{ kJ/kg}$$

Net work:
$$W_{\text{net}} = \eta \times Q_{\text{in}} = 0.5647 \times 941.15 = 531.46\text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(iii) Mean Effective Pressure:**

Specific volume at state 1:
$$v_1 = \frac{R T_1}{P_1} = \frac{0.287 \times 300}{100} = 0.861\text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

Volume at state 2:
$$v_2 = \frac{v_1}{r} = \frac{0.861}{8} = 0.1076\text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

Swept volume:
$$v_1 - v_2 = 0.861 - 0.1076 = 0.7534\text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

MEP:
$$\text{MEP} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{v_1 - v_2} = \frac{531.46}{0.7534} = 705.42\text{ kPa} \approx 705.4\text{ kPa}$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the air standard efficiency of a Diesel cycle in terms of compression ratio and cut-off ratio. (7 marks)

**(b)** A Diesel cycle operates with a compression ratio of 18:1 and a cut-off ratio of 2.5. The conditions at the start of compression are $100\text{ kPa}$ and $30^{\circ}\text{C}$. Determine:
- (i) The temperatures at all four state points
- (ii) The air standard efficiency
- (iii) The MEP

Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005\text{ kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$, $C_v = 0.718\text{ kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$, $R = 0.287\text{ kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

For the Diesel cycle with states 1→2 isentropic compression, 2→3 constant-pressure heat addition, 3→4 isentropic expansion, 4→1 constant-volume heat rejection:

Heat added at constant pressure:
$$Q_{\text{in}} = m C_p (T_3 - T_2)$$

Heat rejected at constant volume:
$$Q_{\text{out}} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$$

Efficiency:
$$\eta_{\text{Diesel}} = 1 - \frac{C_v (T_4 - T_1)}{C_p (T_3 - T_2)} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$

For isentropic compression 1→2:
$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$$

For isentropic expansion 3→4:
$$\frac{T_4}{T_3} = \left(\frac{V_3}{V_4}\right)^{\gamma-1} = \left(\frac{1}{r_c}\right)^{\gamma-1}$$

So $T_4 = T_3 / r_c^{\gamma-1}$.

Substituting and simplifying:
$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{Diesel}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma (r_c - 1)}}$$

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $r = 18$, $r_c = 2.5$, $T_1 = 303\text{ K}$, $P_1 = 100\text{ kPa}$.

**(i) Temperatures at all four states:**

**State 1:** $T_1 = 303\text{ K}$.

**State 2** (after isentropic compression):
$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} = 303 \times 18^{0.4}$$

Compute $18^{0.4}$:
$$\ln(18) = 2.8904, \quad 0.4 \times 2.8904 = 1.1562$$
$$18^{0.4} = e^{1.1562} = 3.177$$

So $T_2 = 303 \times 3.177 = 962.6\text{ K}$.

**State 3** (after constant-pressure heat addition):
For constant-pressure process: $V_3/V_2 = T_3/T_2 = r_c$, so:
$$T_3 = T_2 \times r_c = 962.6 \times 2.5 = 2406.5\text{ K}$$

**State 4** (after isentropic expansion):
$$T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r_c^{\gamma-1}} = \frac{2406.5}{2.5^{0.4}}$$

Compute $2.5^{0.4}$:
$$\ln(2.5) = 0.9163, \quad 0.4 \times 0.9163 = 0.3665$$
$$2.5^{0.4} = e^{0.3665} = 1.4427$$

So $T_4 = 2406.5 / 1.4427 = 1668.2\text{ K}$.

**(ii) Air Standard Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{\text{Diesel}} = 1 - \frac{1}{18^{0.4}} \cdot \frac{2.5^{1.4} - 1}{1.4 \times (2.5 - 1)}$$

Compute $2.5^{1.4}$:
$$2.5^{1.4} = 2.5 \times 2.5^{0.4} = 2.5 \times 1.4427 = 3.6068$$

Substituting:
$$\eta_{\text{Diesel}} = 1 - \frac{1}{3.177} \cdot \frac{3.6068 - 1}{1.4 \times 1.5} = 1 - 0.3148 \cdot \frac{2.6068}{2.1}$$
$$= 1 - 0.3148 \times 1.2413 = 1 - 0.3908 = 0.6092 = 60.92\%$$

**(iii) Mean Effective Pressure:**

Heat added per kg:
$$q_{\text{in}} = C_p (T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (2406.5 - 962.6) = 1.005 \times 1443.9 = 1451.1\text{ kJ/kg}$$

Net work per kg:
$$w_{\text{net}} = \eta \times q_{\text{in}} = 0.6092 \times 1451.1 = 884.0\text{ kJ/kg}$$

Specific volumes:
$$v_1 = \frac{R T_1}{P_1} = \frac{0.287 \times 303}{100} = 0.8696\text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$
$$v_2 = \frac{v_1}{r} = \frac{0.8696}{18} = 0.04831\text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

Swept volume:
$$v_1 - v_2 = 0.8696 - 0.04831 = 0.8213\text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

MEP:
$$\text{MEP} = \frac{884.0}{0.8213} = 1076.4\text{ kPa} \approx 1076\text{ kPa}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong compression ratio usage:** In Diesel cycle, $r$ applies to process 1→2 and $r_c$ applies to 2→3. Mixing them yields wrong $T_2$ or $T_3$.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency:** Temperatures MUST be in Kelvin. Pressure in kPa for MEP to come out in kPa directly.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert cut-off ratio to volume ratio:** $r_c = V_4/V_3 = V_3/V_2$ (since $V_3 = V_2$ in Diesel cycle constant-pressure process).
> 4. **Using $C_p$ instead of $C_v$ (or vice versa)** for heat addition/rejection depending on process.
> 5. **Neglecting the $1/r^{\gamma-1}$ factor in efficiency formulas:** Many students write only the cut-off ratio term and forget the compression ratio factor.
> 6. **Sign convention errors:** $Q_{\text{out}}$ is positive (a magnitude); ensure the formula structure preserves sign.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Air Standard Cycle = idealized model** with constant $C_p$, $C_v$, perfect gas, no chemical reactions.
- **Otto Cycle** (SI engines): constant-$V$ heat addition, efficiency $= 1 - 1/r^{\gamma-1}$.
- **Diesel Cycle** (CI engines): constant-$P$ heat addition, efficiency $= 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma (r_c - 1)}$.
- **Dual Cycle** (modern CI): mixed heat addition with parameters $r$, $r_p$, $r_c$.
- **Brayton Cycle** (gas turbines): all processes at constant pressure except isentropic compression/expansion.
- **Higher compression ratio = higher efficiency** for Otto and Brayton.
- **For fixed $r$ and $Q_{\text{in}}$**: Otto > Dual > Diesel in efficiency.
- **MEP** is a true performance indicator independent of engine size.
- **Always use SI units**: T in K, P in kPa, V in m³, gives W in kJ, MEP in kPa.
- **Standard air properties** to memorize: $C_p = 1.005$, $C_v = 0.718$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 0.287$.
- **Isentropic relations** are your workhorse: $TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{const}$, $TP^{(1-\gamma)/\gamma} = \text{const}$, $PV^{\gamma} = \text{const}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Otto Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Otto Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Otto Cycle** is the idealized **air-standard thermodynamic cycle** that models the operation of a **spark-ignition (SI) reciprocating internal combustion engine**. It serves as the theoretical benchmark against which the real performance of petrol/gasoline engines is compared. The cycle consists of **four internally reversible processes**: two isentropic (adiabatic reversible) processes and two isochoric (constant volume) processes.

The Otto Cycle was conceptualized by **Nikolaus Otto** in 1876 and remains the foundational analytical model for all spark-ignition engines. In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic is classified under **Module 3: Air Standard Cycles**, which directly tests the student's grasp of idealized gas cycles used to estimate the upper-limit performance of real IC engines.

### 1.2 The Four Processes (KTU Curriculum Definition)

| Process Number | Process Name | Thermodynamic Character |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 → 2 | **Isentropic Compression** | Reversible Adiabatic Compression |
| 2 → 3 | **Constant Volume Heat Addition** | Isochoric Heat Input ($Q_{in}$) |
| 3 → 4 | **Isentropic Expansion** | Reversible Adiabatic Expansion |
| 4 → 1 | **Constant Volume Heat Rejection** | Isochoric Heat Rejection ($Q_{out}$) |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine a Bicycle Pump (Cycle Pump) used carefully:** When you **push the handle down rapidly**, the air inside gets compressed and becomes hot — this is the **isentropic compression**. Now imagine that compressed hot air is inside a sealed chamber with a tiny **spark plug**. The spark instantly adds heat, raising the temperature and pressure sharply at constant volume — this is the **constant volume heat addition**. The high-pressure gas then **pushes a piston back down rapidly**, doing useful work and cooling as it expands — this is the **isentropic expansion**. Finally, the exhaust valve opens, allowing the spent gases to release their remaining heat to the atmosphere at constant volume — this is the **constant volume heat rejection**. Then the cycle repeats.

### 1.4 Fundamental Assumptions of the Air-Standard Otto Cycle

For the KTU examination, students must explicitly state these **six assumptions** whenever analyzing the Otto Cycle:

1. The working fluid is a **perfect gas** (ideal gas) — specifically **air** — with constant specific heats evaluated at **room temperature (≈ 300 K)**.
2. All processes are **internally reversible**; friction and turbulence are neglected.
3. The **compression and expansion processes are adiabatic** (no heat transfer with the surroundings).
4. **No chemical reaction** occurs inside the system; heat is added/rejected from an external source (this is the *air-standard* assumption, replacing actual combustion).
5. The specific heats $C_p$ and $C_v$ remain **constant** throughout the cycle.
6. The cycle is **closed**; the same working fluid is reused repeatedly.

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> For air (working fluid in KTU Otto Cycle problems):
> - **Gas constant:** $R = 0.287 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}$
> - **Specific heat at constant volume:** $C_v = 0.718 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}$
> - **Specific heat at constant pressure:** $C_p = 1.005 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}$
> - **Ratio of specific heats:** $\gamma = \dfrac{C_p}{C_v} = 1.4$ (for diatomic air)
> - **Air-standard cycle reference temperature:** $T_1 = 300 \, \text{K}$
> - **Atmospheric pressure (initial condition):** $p_1 = 100 \, \text{kPa}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Process-by-Process Logical Breakdown

#### **Process 1 → 2: Isentropic Compression**

- **Why:** The piston moves from Bottom Dead Centre (BDC) to Top Dead Centre (TDC). Because the motion is rapid, there is insufficient time for heat exchange, making the process adiabatic.
- **How:** The gas is compressed, raising its pressure and temperature. The entropy remains constant ($s_1 = s_2$).
- **Mathematical Character:** $pV^\gamma = \text{constant}$ and $TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{constant}$.
- **Compression Ratio ($r$):** Defined as the ratio of maximum to minimum volume:
$$r = \frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{V_{\max}}{V_{\min}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **compression ratio $r$** is the single most important parameter in the Otto Cycle. In KTU problems, $r$ typically ranges between **7 and 12** for practical SI engines.

#### **Process 2 → 3: Constant Volume Heat Addition**

- **Why:** In a real SI engine, combustion occurs almost instantaneously when the spark plug fires near TDC. Modeling this as an instantaneous heat addition at constant volume is the defining feature of the Otto Cycle.
- **How:** Heat $Q_{in}$ is supplied from an external source, raising temperature from $T_2$ to $T_3$ without any change in volume.
- **Mathematical Character:** $\dfrac{p}{T} = \text{constant}$ (Gay-Lussac's Law for ideal gas at constant V).

#### **Process 3 → 4: Isentropic Expansion (Power Stroke)**

- **Why:** The high-pressure, high-temperature gas drives the piston from TDC back to BDC, producing useful work. Again, the process is rapid and adiabatic.
- **How:** Gas expands and does work on the piston. Pressure and temperature both decrease.
- **Mathematical Character:** $pV^\gamma = \text{constant}$; $TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{constant}$.

#### **Process 4 → 1: Constant Volume Heat Rejection**

- **Why:** At BDC, the exhaust valve opens (or modeled as such), and the cycle's residual heat $Q_{out}$ is rejected to the surroundings at constant volume, returning the system to its initial state.
- **How:** Heat is removed at constant $V$, bringing temperature from $T_4$ back to $T_1$.
- **Mathematical Character:** $\dfrac{p}{T} = \text{constant}$.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table constitutes the complete formula set required for KTU 2024 Otto Cycle numerical problems.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Compression Ratio | $r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$ | Dimensionless; typical: **7 to 12** |
| 2 | Temperature after Compression | $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$ | K; uses $r^{\gamma-1}$ |
| 3 | Temperature after Heat Addition | $T_3 = T_2 \cdot \dfrac{Q_{in}}{m \cdot C_v \cdot (T_3 - T_2)}$ rephrased as $T_3 = T_2 \cdot \rho$ where $\rho = \dfrac{T_3}{T_2}$ is the **Explosion Ratio** | K |
| 4 | Temperature after Expansion | $T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{r}\right)^{\gamma-1} = \dfrac{T_3}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ | K |
| 5 | Pressure Ratio (isentropic) | $\dfrac{p_2}{p_1} = r^{\gamma}$, $\dfrac{p_3}{p_2} = \dfrac{T_3}{T_2}$, $\dfrac{p_4}{p_3} = \left(\dfrac{1}{r}\right)^{\gamma}$ | All dimensionless |
| 6 | Heat Added | $Q_{in} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2)$ | kJ |
| 7 | Heat Rejected | $Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$ | kJ |
| 8 | Net Work Done | $W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} = m C_v [(T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1)]$ | kJ |
| 9 | **Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ | Dimensionless (× 100 for %) |
| 10 | Mean Effective Pressure | $MEP = \dfrac{W_{net}}{V_1 - V_2}$ | kPa |
| 11 | Specific Heat Relationship | $C_p - C_v = R$; $\gamma = \dfrac{C_p}{C_v}$ | Constants for air |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Formula:** The thermal efficiency of the Otto Cycle depends **ONLY** on the compression ratio $r$ and the ratio of specific heats $\gamma$. It is **independent of the heat added**. This is a frequently asked conceptual question in KTU examinations.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Otto Cycle analysis is indispensable in:

- **Automotive Industry:** Designing and benchmarking petrol/gasoline engines for cars, motorcycles, and light aircraft.
- **Performance Optimization:** Higher $r$ → higher efficiency, but limited by **knocking** (auto-ignition), which is why modern engines use fuel octane ratings.
- **Refrigeration Analogy:** The reversed Otto Cycle (also called the **Bell-Coleman Cycle**) is used for air refrigeration systems — a related concept tested in KTU Module 5.
- **Combined Cycle Plants:** Although the Brayton and Rankine cycles dominate, Otto Cycle principles guide small-scale gas engine power generation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the Otto Cycle Thermal Efficiency

**Starting Point:** The thermal efficiency $\eta_{th}$ of any heat engine cycle is defined as the ratio of net work output to heat input.

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$$

**Step 1:** By the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to a closed cycle, the net work output equals the net heat transfer:

$$W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$$

**Step 2:** Therefore, the efficiency can be written as:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{Q_{in} - Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$$

**Step 3:** Heat is added only during process 2 → 3 at constant volume. For an ideal gas:

$$Q_{in} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2)$$

**Step 4:** Heat is rejected only during process 4 → 1 at constant volume. For an ideal gas:

$$Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute Steps 3 and 4 into Step 2:

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{m C_v (T_4 - T_1)}{m C_v (T_3 - T_2)} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$

**Step 6:** For **isentropic processes 1→2 and 3→4**, the following temperature-volume relationships hold (since $TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{const}$):

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma-1} = r^{\gamma-1}$$

$$\frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{V_4}{V_3}\right)^{\gamma-1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma-1} = r^{\gamma-1}$$

(Note: $V_1 = V_4$ and $V_2 = V_3$ since processes 2→3 and 4→1 occur at constant volume.)

**Step 7:** From the relations in Step 6:

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} \quad \text{and} \quad T_3 = T_4 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$$

**Step 8:** Therefore:

$$T_3 - T_2 = r^{\gamma-1}(T_4 - T_1)$$

**Step 9:** Substituting Step 8 back into the efficiency equation:

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{r^{\gamma-1}(T_4 - T_1)}$$

**Step 10:** Canceling the common term $(T_4 - T_1)$:

$$\boxed{\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Mean Effective Pressure (MEP)

**Step 1:** MEP is the constant pressure that, if acting on the piston over the full stroke, would produce the same net work as the actual cycle.

$$MEP = \frac{W_{net}}{V_{swept}} = \frac{W_{net}}{V_1 - V_2}$$

**Step 2:** Since $V_1 = r V_2$, we have:

$$V_1 - V_2 = V_2(r - 1) = \frac{V_1}{r}(r - 1)$$

**Step 3:** The net work from the efficiency equation:

$$W_{net} = \eta_{otto} \cdot Q_{in} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}\right) m C_v (T_3 - T_2)$$

**Step 4:** Substituting and simplifying for an ideal gas ($pV = mRT$):

$$MEP = \frac{p_1 r}{(\gamma - 1)(r - 1)} \left[ r^{\gamma-1} - 1 \right] \left[ \frac{T_3}{T_2} - 1 \right]$$

The MEP formula is a high-yield topic for 14-mark KTU problems where students must compute work per unit volume.

### 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Standard KTU Format)

> **Problem Statement:** An engine working on the Otto Cycle has a compression ratio of **8**. At the beginning of the compression stroke, the pressure and temperature of air are **100 kPa** and **300 K** respectively. The heat added during the cycle is **800 kJ/kg**. Take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $C_v = 0.718 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}$. Determine:
> (a) The pressures and temperatures at all four salient points.
> (b) The thermal efficiency of the cycle.
> (c) The net work done per kg of air.

**Given Data:**
- $r = 8$, $p_1 = 100 \, \text{kPa}$, $T_1 = 300 \, \text{K}$
- $Q_{in} = 800 \, \text{kJ/kg}$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_v = 0.718 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}$

#### Solution:

**Step A — State 1 (Initial State):**
$$p_1 = 100 \, \text{kPa}, \quad T_1 = 300 \, \text{K}, \quad V_1 = \frac{mRT_1}{p_1}$$

**Step B — State 2 (After Isentropic Compression):**
$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} = 300 \times 8^{0.4}$$

Computing $8^{0.4}$: $\ln(8^{0.4}) = 0.4 \times \ln(8) = 0.4 \times 2.0794 = 0.8318$, so $8^{0.4} = e^{0.8318} = 2.2974$

$$T_2 = 300 \times 2.2974 = 689.22 \, \text{K}$$

$$p_2 = p_1 \cdot r^{\gamma} = 100 \times 8^{1.4} = 100 \times 8 \times 8^{0.4} = 100 \times 8 \times 2.2974 = 1837.9 \, \text{kPa}$$

**Step C — State 3 (After Heat Addition):**
$$T_3 = T_2 + \frac{Q_{in}}{C_v} = 689.22 + \frac{800}{0.718} = 689.22 + 1114.21 = 1803.43 \, \text{K}$$

$$p_3 = p_2 \cdot \frac{T_3}{T_2} = 1837.9 \times \frac{1803.43}{689.22} = 1837.9 \times 2.6167 = 4809.7 \, \text{kPa}$$

**Step D — State 4 (After Isentropic Expansion):**
$$T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r^{\gamma-1}} = \frac{1803.43}{2.2974} = 784.98 \, \text{K}$$

$$p_4 = p_3 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{r}\right)^{\gamma} = 4809.7 \times \frac{1}{8^{1.4}} = \frac{4809.7}{18.379} = 261.7 \, \text{kPa}$$

**Step E — Thermal Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.2974} = 1 - 0.4353 = 0.5647$$

$$\eta_{otto} = 56.47\%$$

**Step F — Net Work Done:**
$$Q_{out} = C_v (T_4 - T_1) = 0.718 \times (784.98 - 300) = 0.718 \times 484.98 = 348.22 \, \text{kJ/kg}$$

$$W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} = 800 - 348.22 = 451.78 \, \text{kJ/kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** $W_{net} = \eta_{otto} \times Q_{in} = 0.5647 \times 800 = 451.76 \, \text{kJ/kg}$ ✓ (matches within rounding).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Compression Ratio on Efficiency

| Compression Ratio ($r$) | $r^{\gamma-1}$ | Efficiency $\eta = 1 - 1/r^{\gamma-1}$ | Practical Feasibility |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 4 | 1.741 | **42.6%** | Very low — poor engine |
| 6 | 2.048 | **51.2%** | Low — early engines |
| 8 | 2.297 | **56.5%** | Standard petrol engines |
| 10 | 2.512 | **60.2%** | High-performance SI engines |
| 12 | 2.703 | **63.0%** | Near-knock limit for petrol |
| 14 | 2.877 | **65.2%** | Requires high-octane fuel |
| 20 | 3.314 | **69.8%** | Theoretical only — knock limit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Otto Cycle efficiency increases with $r$, but **diminishing returns** occur at high values. Doubling $r$ from 5 to 10 improves efficiency by less than 10%. This is a key reason engineers don't indefinitely increase $r$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Otto Cycle Process Flow

This block diagram maps the **functional interaction** of the four processes, the working fluid state transitions, and the energy exchanges that constitute the Otto Cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["STATE 1: P1 T1 V1 - BDC Initial"] -->|Isentropic Compression Wc| B["STATE 2: P2 T2 V2 - TDC Compressed"]
    B -->|Heat Added Qin at Constant Volume| C["STATE 3: P3 T3 V3 - TDC After Combustion"]
    C -->|Isentropic Expansion We| D["STATE 4: P4 T4 V4 - BDC Expanded"]
    D -->|Heat Rejected Qout at Constant Volume| A
    B -.->|Qin from external source| E["External Heat Source"]
    D -.->|Qout to sink| F["External Heat Sink"]
    G["Net Work Wnet"] -->|Wnet Qin minus Qout| H["Output Shaft / Piston"]
    H -.->|Feedback for next cycle| A
```

**Interpretation of the Block Diagram:**
- The **solid arrows** denote the working fluid's thermodynamic state transitions.
- The **dotted arrows** denote energy transfers with the surroundings.
- The cycle is **closed** (returns to State 1), confirming the cyclic nature of the Otto process.

### 4.2 P-V Diagram Schematic — Otto Cycle on Indicated Plane

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["P-axis Vertical"] --> A2["V-axis Horizontal"]
    B1["State 1 - Bottom Right Vmax"] -->|Process 1-2 Isentropic Compression Curve Upward Leftward| B2["State 2 - Top Left Vmin"]
    B2 -->|Process 2-3 Constant Volume Vertical Upward| B3["State 3 - Top Left but Higher P"]
    B3 -->|Process 3-4 Isentropic Expansion Curve Downward Rightward| B4["State 4 - Bottom Right but Higher P than 1"]
    B4 -->|Process 4-1 Constant Volume Vertical Downward| B1
    C1["Enclosed Area inside curve equals Net Work Wnet"]
```

**Schematic Reading Guide:**
- Process 1→2 and 3→4 are **adiabatic curves** (steeper than isothermal).
- Process 2→3 and 4→1 are **vertical lines** (constant volume).
- The area enclosed by 1-2-3-4 represents $W_{net}$.

### 4.3 T-S Diagram Schematic — Otto Cycle on Temperature-Entropy Plane

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["State 1: T1 low S1"] -->|Process 1-2 Vertical line up isentropic| S2["State 2: T2 higher S same"]
    S2 -->|Process 2-3 Curve rightward heat addition at constant V| S3["State 3: T3 highest S higher"]
    S3 -->|Process 3-4 Vertical line down isentropic| S4["State 4: T4 lower S same as S3"]
    S4 -->|Process 4-1 Curve leftward heat rejection at constant V| S1
    Q1["Area under 2-3 equals Qin"]
    Q2["Area under 4-1 equals Qout"]
    Q3["Area enclosed equals Wnet"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** On a T-S diagram, the area enclosed by the cycle equals the **net heat transfer** $Q_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$, which by the First Law equals the **net work done**. This is a standard 3-mark conceptual question.

### 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — Otto vs. Diesel vs. Dual Cycles

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O1["OTTO CYCLE"] --> O2["Constant Volume Heat Addition"]
    O2 --> O3["SI Engine Petrol"]
    D1["DIESEL CYCLE"] --> D2["Constant Pressure Heat Addition"]
    D2 --> D3["CI Engine Diesel"]
    DC1["DUAL CYCLE"] --> DC2["Mixed Constant V then Constant P"]
    DC2 --> DC3["Modern High Speed Engines"]
    COM1["Common Air Standard Assumptions"]
    COM1 --> O1
    COM1 --> D1
    COM1 --> DC1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Otto, Diesel, and Dual cycles share identical **adiabatic compression and expansion** processes. The **only distinction** lies in the manner of heat addition. This comparative understanding is essential for KTU Module 3 questions that ask students to differentiate between these three cycles.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Q1.** Define the Otto Cycle and name the four processes involved. Why is it called an "air-standard" cycle?
>
> **Model Answer:** The Otto Cycle is an idealized air-standard thermodynamic cycle that models a spark-ignition reciprocating internal combustion engine. The four processes are: (1) Isentropic compression (1→2), (2) Constant volume heat addition (2→3), (3) Isentropic expansion (3→4), and (4) Constant volume heat rejection (4→1). It is called an *air-standard* cycle because the actual combustion process is replaced by an equivalent heat transfer to a fixed mass of air (treated as an ideal gas with constant specific heats), thereby simplifying the thermodynamic analysis.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q2.** "The thermal efficiency of the Otto Cycle depends only on the compression ratio." Justify this statement with the relevant formula.
>
> **Model Answer:** The thermal efficiency of the Otto Cycle is given by:
> $$\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$$
> From this expression, it is clear that the efficiency depends **solely** on the compression ratio $r$ and the ratio of specific heats $\gamma$ (a constant for air). It is independent of the amount of heat added $Q_{in}$ or the initial temperature $T_1$. Higher compression ratios yield higher thermal efficiencies.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice Format

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Otto Cycle Analysis**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the air-standard thermal efficiency of the Otto Cycle, stating clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Working fluid is air, treated as an ideal gas with constant specific heats.
2. All processes are internally reversible.
3. Compression (1→2) and expansion (3→4) are isentropic.
4. Heat addition (2→3) and heat rejection (4→1) occur at constant volume.
5. No chemical reaction; the cycle is closed.

**Derivation:**

Efficiency is defined as:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$$

For constant volume heat transfer processes:

$$Q_{in} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2) \quad \text{and} \quad Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$$

Therefore:

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$

For the isentropic processes, $TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{const}$:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = r^{\gamma-1} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{T_3}{T_4} = r^{\gamma-1}$$

Combining these gives $T_3 - T_2 = r^{\gamma-1}(T_4 - T_1)$, which upon substitution yields:

$$\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$$

**(b)** An Otto Cycle engine has a compression ratio of 9. At the start of compression, the air is at **100 kPa** and **40 °C**. The maximum temperature in the cycle is **2000 K**. Determine: (i) the temperatures and pressures at all four points, (ii) the heat added, (iii) the net work done per kg, and (iv) the thermal efficiency. Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_v = 0.718 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $r = 9$, $p_1 = 100 \, \text{kPa}$, $T_1 = 313 \, \text{K}$, $T_3 = 2000 \, \text{K}$.

Compute $r^{\gamma-1} = 9^{0.4} = 2.4082$ and $r^{\gamma} = 9^{1.4} = 21.6735$.

**(i) State Points:**

$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} = 313 \times 2.4082 = 753.77 \, \text{K}$

$p_2 = p_1 \cdot r^{\gamma} = 100 \times 21.6735 = 2167.35 \, \text{kPa}$

$p_3 = p_2 \cdot \dfrac{T_3}{T_2} = 2167.35 \times \dfrac{2000}{753.77} = 5751.47 \, \text{kPa}$

$T_4 = \dfrac{T_3}{r^{\gamma-1}} = \dfrac{2000}{2.4082} = 830.49 \, \text{K}$

$p_4 = p_3 \cdot \dfrac{T_4}{T_3} = 5751.47 \times \dfrac{830.49}{2000} = 2388.14 \, \text{kPa}$

Wait — correcting the Step D calculation using $p_4 = p_1 \cdot r^{\gamma} \cdot \dfrac{T_4}{T_3 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}}$ is unnecessary; using $p_4 = p_3 / r^{\gamma}$:

$p_4 = \dfrac{5751.47}{21.6735} = 265.37 \, \text{kPa}$

**(ii) Heat Added:**

$$Q_{in} = C_v (T_3 - T_2) = 0.718 \times (2000 - 753.77) = 0.718 \times 1246.23 = 894.79 \, \text{kJ/kg}$$

**(iii) Net Work:**

$$Q_{out} = C_v (T_4 - T_1) = 0.718 \times (830.49 - 313) = 0.718 \times 517.49 = 371.56 \, \text{kJ/kg}$$

$$W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} = 894.79 - 371.56 = 523.23 \, \text{kJ/kg}$$

**(iv) Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{523.23}{894.79} = 0.5848 = 58.48\%$$

**Verification using direct formula:** $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{9^{0.4}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{2.4082} = 1 - 0.4152 = 0.5848 = 58.48\%$ ✓

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice**

**(a)** Compare the Otto Cycle and the Diesel Cycle with respect to: (i) processes, (ii) efficiency expressions, (iii) limiting compression ratio, and (iv) practical application. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer Table:**

| Parameter | Otto Cycle | Diesel Cycle |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Heat Addition | Constant volume (2→3) | Constant pressure (2→3) |
| Heat Rejection | Constant volume (4→1) | Constant volume (4→1) |
| Compression | Isentropic | Isentropic |
| Expansion | Isentropic | Isentropic |
| Efficiency | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(r_c - 1)}$ where $r_c$ = cutoff ratio |
| Limiting $r$ | Limited by knock (~12) | Higher possible (~20) since no knock |
| Application | Spark-ignition petrol engines | Compression-ignition diesel engines |
| Fuel | Gasoline (volatile) | Diesel (less volatile) |

**(b)** An air-standard Otto Cycle has a mean effective pressure of **8 bar** and a compression ratio of **8**. If the pressure at the beginning of compression is **1 bar**, calculate the heat added per kg of air. Take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $R = 0.287 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $MEP = 800 \, \text{kPa}$, $r = 8$, $p_1 = 100 \, \text{kPa}$.

The MEP formula relates net work to swept volume:

$$MEP = \frac{W_{net}}{V_1 - V_2} = \frac{W_{net}}{V_1 (1 - 1/r)} = \frac{W_{net} \cdot r}{V_1(r-1)}$$

Therefore:

$$W_{net} = \frac{MEP \cdot V_1 (r-1)}{r}$$

Now, $V_1 = \dfrac{RT_1}{p_1}$ per kg of air.

From efficiency: $W_{net} = \eta_{otto} \cdot Q_{in}$

$\eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{8^{0.4}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{2.2974} = 0.5647$

Setting up the equality:

$$0.5647 \cdot Q_{in} = \frac{800 \times (R T_1 / 100) \times 7}{8}$$

$$0.5647 \cdot Q_{in} = 7 \cdot R T_1 = 7 \times 0.287 \times 300 = 602.7 \, \text{kJ/kg}$$

$$Q_{in} = \frac{602.7}{0.5647} = 1067.3 \, \text{kJ/kg}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** KTU strictly deducts marks if $C_v$ is used in $\text{kJ/kg·K}$ but temperature is in °C. Always convert °C to K by adding 273.
> 2. **Misidentifying $r$:** Students often confuse the compression ratio as $V_2/V_1$. The correct definition is $V_1/V_2$, where $V_1 > V_2$.
> 3. **Using $C_p$ instead of $C_v$:** Heat transfer at constant volume uses $C_v$, not $C_p$. This single error costs full marks in Part B.
