> 4. **Skipping assumptions:** KTU valuators award marks specifically for stating the six air-standard assumptions in any derivation question. Skipping them = loss of 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Pressure ratio confusion:** Some students compute $p_3/p_2$ as $T_2/T_3$. The correct is $p_3/p_2 = T_3/T_2$ for constant volume.

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for the Otto Cycle:**

- **Definition:** Idealized air-standard cycle for SI engines; 2 isentropic + 2 constant-volume processes.
- **Compression Ratio:** $r = V_1/V_2$ — the **single most critical design parameter**.
- **Master Formula:** $\eta_{otto} = 1 - 1/r^{\gamma-1}$ — independent of heat added.
- **Air Constants to Memorize:** $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_v = 0.718$, $C_p = 1.005$, $R = 0.287$ (all in kJ/kg·K).
- **Process 1→2:** $T_2 = T_1 r^{\gamma-1}$; $p_2 = p_1 r^{\gamma}$.
- **Process 2→3:** Constant volume — $p/T = \text{const}$; $Q_{in} = m C_v(T_3 - T_2)$.
- **Process 3→4:** $T_4 = T_3 / r^{\gamma-1}$; $p_4 = p_3 / r^{\gamma}$.
- **Process 4→1:** Constant volume heat rejection — $Q_{out} = m C_v(T_4 - T_1)$.
- **Efficiency Behavior:** Increases with $r$ but **diminishing returns** at high values; limited by **knock** in real engines.
- **Comparison Hook:** Otto = constant V heat addition; Diesel = constant P; Dual = both.
- **Practical Range:** Real SI engines operate between $r = 7$ and $r = 12$.
- **Work Formula:** $W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} = m C_v[(T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1)]$.
- **Diagrams to Master:** Both P-V and T-S diagrams; area enclosed = $W_{net}$.
- **First Law Checkpoint:** Always verify $W_{net} = \eta \times Q_{in}$ for sanity-check on numerical answers.
- **Units Vigilance:** Pressure in kPa, temperature in K, work in kJ/kg, MEP in kPa.
- **Reversibility Note:** Air-standard assumption means **internally reversible** processes only — no friction, no turbulence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** For 14-mark Part B questions, structure your answer in **four segments**: (1) Data listing with units, (2) Assumptions, (3) Stepwise derivation/calculation, (4) Final boxed answer with verification. Examiners reward clarity and systematic presentation as much as numerical accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diesel Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Diesel Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diesel Cycle (Constant Pressure Cycle):** The Diesel cycle is the **idealized air-standard thermodynamic cycle** that models the operation of a **Compression Ignition (CI) reciprocating engine**. It consists of four internally reversible processes applied to a fixed mass of air acting as the working fluid: (1) isentropic compression, (2) **constant-pressure** heat addition, (3) isentropic expansion, and (4) constant-volume heat rejection.

The Diesel cycle was proposed by **Rudolf Diesel in 1892** as a more efficient alternative to the Otto cycle. Unlike the spark-ignition engine, fuel is injected directly into the hot compressed air charge, which auto-ignites due to the high temperature reached at the end of compression.

> [!NOTE]
> **Air-Standard Assumptions (Mandatory for Analysis):**
> 1. The working fluid is **air**, which behaves as an **ideal gas** throughout the cycle.
> 2. All processes are internally reversible.
> 3. The combustion process is replaced by heat addition from an external source.
> 4. The exhaust process is replaced by heat rejection to the surroundings.
> 5. Specific heats ($c_p$ and $c_v$) are assumed **constant** and evaluated at room temperature.
> 6. No chemical reaction takes place — energy is added/rejected as heat only.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are **pumping a bicycle pump** rapidly with your thumb blocking the outlet. The air inside gets very hot — this is exactly what happens inside a diesel engine cylinder during the compression stroke. Now, picture a **spray bottle of perfume**: when you squeeze the trigger, liquid is forced out at constant pressure through the nozzle. Similarly, in a diesel engine, fuel is injected at a rate that maintains **constant pressure** inside the cylinder as combustion occurs.

**Plain English Summary:** Air is squeezed (compressed) to very high pressure and temperature → fuel is sprayed in at a controlled rate so the pressure stays roughly constant while heat is added → the hot, high-pressure gas pushes the piston down (power stroke) → finally, heat is dumped out so the cycle can restart.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Constant Pressure?** In a real diesel engine, fuel injection begins at top dead center and continues for a finite crank angle. The rate of fuel injection is carefully metered by the injector so that the mass of gas burned is balanced by the increasing volume due to piston motion — this naturally produces a near-**constant-pressure combustion** process, which is the defining signature of the Diesel cycle.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific heat ratio of air | $\gamma$ | **1.4** | dimensionless |
| Gas constant for air | $R$ | **0.287** | kJ/kg·K |
| Specific heat at constant pressure | $c_p$ | **1.005** | kJ/kg·K |
| Specific heat at constant volume | $c_v$ | **0.718** | kJ/kg·K |
| Universal gas constant | $\bar{R}$ | **8.314** | kJ/kmol·K |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Four Processes of the Diesel Cycle

The cycle operates between four key states: **State 1** (BDC start of compression), **State 2** (TDC end of compression), **State 3** (end of heat addition), and **State 4** (BDC end of expansion).

### Process 1→2: Isentropic Compression
- The piston moves from Bottom Dead Center (BDC) to Top Dead Center (TDC).
- The air is compressed rapidly; no heat transfer occurs (Q = 0).
- Pressure and temperature rise sharply.
- Governing relations:

$$P_2/P_1 = (V_1/V_2)^{k} = r^{k}$$

$$T_2/T_1 = (V_1/V_2)^{k-1} = r^{k-1}$$

where **r** = Compression Ratio = $V_1/V_2$.

### Process 2→3: Constant-Pressure Heat Addition
- At TDC, fuel is injected and burns, raising temperature at **constant pressure**.
- Volume increases from $V_2$ to $V_3$ (piston moves down).
- The **Cut-off Ratio** ($\rho$) is defined as: $\rho = V_3/V_2$
- Governing relations:

$$P_3 = P_2$$

$$T_3/T_2 = V_3/V_2 = \rho$$

### Process 3→4: Isentropic Expansion (Power Stroke)
- The hot, high-pressure gas expands and pushes the piston to BDC.
- This is the **work-producing stroke** of the cycle.
- Governing relations:

$$P_4/P_3 = (V_3/V_4)^{k} = (\rho/r)^{k}$$

$$T_4/T_3 = (V_3/V_4)^{k-1} = (\rho/r)^{k-1}$$

### Process 4→1: Constant-Volume Heat Rejection
- At BDC, the exhaust valve opens and heat is rejected to return the working fluid to its initial state 1.
- Volume remains constant at $V_1 = V_4$.
- Governing relations:

$$V_4 = V_1$$

$$Q_{out} = m \cdot c_v \cdot (T_4 - T_1)$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — 90% of numerical problems in KTU exams are derived from these formulas.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compression Ratio | $r = V_1/V_2$ | Ratio of maximum to minimum volume |
| 2 | Cut-off Ratio | $\rho = V_3/V_2$ | Expansion ratio during heat addition |
| 3 | Expansion Ratio | $r_e = V_4/V_3 = r/\rho$ | Volume ratio during expansion |
| 4 | Heat Added | $Q_{in} = m c_p (T_3 - T_2)$ | Constant-pressure process |
| 5 | Heat Rejected | $Q_{out} = m c_v (T_4 - T_1)$ | Constant-volume process |
| 6 | **Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{k-1}} \left[ \dfrac{\rho^{k} - 1}{k(\rho - 1)} \right]$ | **The Diesel Cycle Efficiency Equation** |
| 7 | Mean Effective Pressure | $\text{MEP} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{(V_1 - V_2)}$ | Specific work output |
| 8 | Net Work | $W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$ | First Law application |
| 9 | Air-Standard Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{T_4 - T_1}{k(T_3 - T_2)}$ | Alternative form using temperatures |

## 2.3 The 'Why' Behind the Efficiency Formula

**Why does longer cut-off reduce efficiency?**

Looking at the efficiency formula, $\eta_{th}$ **decreases** as the cut-off ratio $\rho$ **increases**. This is because:
- A longer cut-off means more fuel is burned → more heat is added.
- However, the heat addition happens at a lower average temperature because expansion begins earlier.
- This is a fundamental trade-off: in a Diesel cycle, **efficiency is sacrificed for the ability to use higher compression ratios** (since there is no premature detonation/knock as in SI engines).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Diesel cycle analysis underpins the design of **truck engines, marine propulsion, diesel locomotives, and standby generators**. Modern common-rail direct-injection diesels achieve thermal efficiencies of **40–45%**, far surpassing Otto-cycle gasoline engines (~25–35%) — a direct consequence of the higher compression ratios (16:1 to 22:1) made possible by the constant-pressure combustion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Thermal Efficiency of the Diesel Cycle

We begin with the definition of thermal efficiency:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$$

**Step 1: Express Heat Added.** During process 2→3 (constant pressure):

$$Q_{in} = m c_p (T_3 - T_2)$$

**Step 2: Express Heat Rejected.** During process 4→1 (constant volume):

$$Q_{out} = m c_v (T_4 - T_1)$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the efficiency equation.**

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{m c_v (T_4 - T_1)}{m c_p (T_3 - T_2)} = 1 - \frac{1}{k} \cdot \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$

**Step 4: Express all temperatures in terms of $T_1$ using process relationships.**

From process 1→2 (isentropic): $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{k-1}$

From process 2→3 (constant P): $T_3 = T_2 \cdot \rho = T_1 \cdot r^{k-1} \cdot \rho$

From process 3→4 (isentropic): 

$$\frac{T_4}{T_3} = \left(\frac{V_3}{V_4}\right)^{k-1} = \left(\frac{\rho}{r}\right)^{k-1}$$

Therefore:

$$T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{\rho}{r}\right)^{k-1} = T_1 \cdot r^{k-1} \cdot \rho \cdot \left(\frac{\rho}{r}\right)^{k-1} = T_1 \cdot \rho^{k}$$

**Step 5: Compute the numerator and denominator terms.**

$$T_4 - T_1 = T_1 \rho^{k} - T_1 = T_1 (\rho^{k} - 1)$$

$$T_3 - T_2 = T_1 r^{k-1} \rho - T_1 r^{k-1} = T_1 r^{k-1} (\rho - 1)$$

**Step 6: Substitute back into the efficiency expression.**

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{k} \cdot \frac{T_1 (\rho^{k} - 1)}{T_1 r^{k-1} (\rho - 1)}$$

**Step 7: Simplify.**

$$\boxed{\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{k-1}} \left[ \frac{\rho^{k} - 1}{k(\rho - 1)} \right]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical Diesel cycle efficiency** formula. The term $\dfrac{\rho^{k} - 1}{k(\rho - 1)}$ is often called the **"cut-off correction factor"** and always has a value greater than 1, which is why Diesel efficiency is always **less** than Otto efficiency for the same compression ratio.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mean Effective Pressure (MEP)

The MEP represents the **constant pressure** that, if applied throughout the power stroke, would produce the same net work as the actual cycle.

**Step 1: Net work from the cycle.** $W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$

**Step 2: Displaced volume.** $V_s = V_1 - V_2$

**Step 3: Definition of MEP.**

$$\text{MEP} = \frac{W_{net}}{V_1 - V_2}$$

Expanding using heat quantities:

$$\text{MEP} = \frac{m c_p (T_3 - T_2) - m c_v (T_4 - T_1)}{V_1 - V_2}$$

Using the ideal gas law $PV = mRT$ and dividing numerator and denominator by $m$:

$$\text{MEP} = \frac{P_1 r \left[ k r^{k-1}(\rho - 1) - (r^{k-1} \rho^{k} - 1) \right]}{(r - 1)(T_1/T_1)}$$

The standard textbook form is:

$$\text{MEP} = \frac{P_1 r \left[ k \rho^{k-1}(\rho - 1) - (\rho^{k} - 1/r^{k-1}) \right]}{(r - 1)(k - 1)}$$

For a **solved KTU problem**, MEP is typically evaluated numerically after substituting all known values.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Solved KTU Pattern Problem

**Problem Statement (Model Question):** An ideal Diesel cycle has a compression ratio of **18:1** and the heat added at constant pressure is such that the cut-off ratio is **1.6**. The conditions at the beginning of compression are **100 kPa** and **27°C**. Determine:
1. The thermal efficiency of the cycle.
2. The mean effective pressure.

Take $k = 1.4$ for air.

### Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $r = 18$, $\rho = 1.6$, $P_1 = 100$ kPa, $T_1 = 27 + 273 = 300$ K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K, $k = 1.4$.

**Part (a): Thermal Efficiency**

Using the Diesel efficiency formula:

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{k-1}} \left[ \frac{\rho^{k} - 1}{k(\rho - 1)} \right]$$

Compute $r^{k-1}$:

$$r^{k-1} = (18)^{0.4}$$

Using logarithms: $\ln(18^{0.4}) = 0.4 \times \ln(18) = 0.4 \times 2.8904 = 1.1562$

$$18^{0.4} = e^{1.1562} \approx 3.177$$

Compute $\rho^{k}$:

$$\rho^{k} = (1.6)^{1.4}$$

$\ln(1.6^{1.4}) = 1.4 \times \ln(1.6) = 1.4 \times 0.4700 = 0.6580$

$$1.6^{1.4} = e^{0.6580} \approx 1.931$$

Compute the bracket term:

$$\frac{\rho^{k} - 1}{k(\rho - 1)} = \frac{1.931 - 1}{1.4 \times (1.6 - 1)} = \frac{0.931}{0.84} \approx 1.108$$

Therefore:

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1.108}{3.177} = 1 - 0.3488 = 0.6512$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{th} \approx 65.12\%}$$

**Part (b): Mean Effective Pressure**

First, find $V_1$ and $V_2$. Using $m = 1$ kg (per kg basis):

$$V_1 = \frac{R T_1}{P_1} = \frac{0.287 \times 300}{100} = 0.861 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

$$V_2 = \frac{V_1}{r} = \frac{0.861}{18} = 0.04783 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

Compute temperatures:

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{k-1} = 300 \times 3.177 = 953.1 \text{ K}$$

$$T_3 = T_2 \cdot \rho = 953.1 \times 1.6 = 1524.96 \text{ K}$$

$$T_4 = T_1 \cdot \rho^{k} = 300 \times 1.931 = 579.3 \text{ K}$$

Heat added and rejected:

$$Q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (1524.96 - 953.1) = 1.005 \times 571.86 = 574.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$Q_{out} = c_v (T_4 - T_1) = 0.718 \times (579.3 - 300) = 0.718 \times 279.3 = 200.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Net work:

$$W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} = 574.8 - 200.5 = 374.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Displaced volume:

$$V_1 - V_2 = 0.861 - 0.04783 = 0.8132 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

MEP:

$$\text{MEP} = \frac{374.3}{0.8132} = 460.3 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MEP} \approx 460.3 \text{ kPa} = 4.603 \text{ bar}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 P-V and T-S Diagrams of the Diesel Cycle

The following Mermaid block renders the **schematic flow** of the four processes with their state transitions, complementing the standard P-V and T-S diagrams that students must draw by hand during exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["STATE 1: BDC Start P1 V1 T1 Low P Low T"] --> B["PROCESS 1 to 2: Isentropic Compression"]
    B --> C["STATE 2: TDC End of Compression P2 High V2 Small T2 High"]
    C --> D["PROCESS 2 to 3: CONSTANT PRESSURE Heat Addition"]
    D --> E["STATE 3: End of Combustion P3 equals P2 V3 Larger T3 Highest"]
    E --> F["PROCESS 3 to 4: Isentropic Expansion Power Stroke"]
    F --> G["STATE 4: BDC End of Expansion P4 Low V4 equals V1 T4 Mid"]
    G --> H["PROCESS 4 to 1: CONSTANT VOLUME Heat Rejection"]
    H --> A
```

### Hand-Drawn Reference Sketches (For Exam Answer Sheets)

**P-V Diagram Characteristics:**
- Curve **1→2**: Steep rising curve (isentropic compression)
- Line **2→3**: Horizontal line to the right (constant pressure expansion)
- Curve **3→4**: Descending curve (isentropic expansion)
- Line **4→1**: Vertical line down (constant volume heat rejection)
- The enclosed area represents **net work output**

**T-S Diagram Characteristics:**
- Curve **1→2**: Steep rising curve (isentropic, vertical line on T-S)
- Line **2→3**: Rising curve along constant pressure line
- Curve **3→4**: Falling vertical curve (isentropic)
- Line **4→1**: Horizontal line along constant volume line (heat rejection)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Always label both P-V AND T-S diagrams on the same answer sheet. State the heat transfer direction (Q_in = arrow right/up on T-S; Q_out = arrow left/down) and clearly mark the four corner states (1, 2, 3, 4). Failure to draw BOTH diagrams results in **2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Real CI Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INTAKE["Air Intake System"]
        AIR["Atmospheric Air P1 T1"] --> FILT["Air Filter"]
        FILT --> COMP["Turbocharger Compressor Optional"]
    end

    subgraph COMPRESS["Compression Phase"]
        COMP --> CYL["Cylinder Piston at BDC"]
        CYL --> SQUEEZE["Isentropic Compression 1 to 2"]
    end

    subgraph COMBUST["Combustion Phase"]
        SQUEEZE --> INJ["Fuel Injector at TDC"]
        INJ --> BURN["Constant Pressure Heat Addition 2 to 3"]
    end

    subgraph POWER["Power Phase"]
        BURN --> EXPAND["Isentropic Expansion 3 to 4"]
        EXPAND --> CRANK["Crankshaft Rotation Net Work Out"]
    end

    subgraph EXHAUST["Exhaust Phase"]
        CRANK --> VALVE["Exhaust Valve Opens"]
        VALVE --> COOL["Constant Volume Heat Rejection 4 to 1"]
    end
```

### Diagram Annotations

| Component | Real Engine Equivalent | Cycle Process |
|---|---|---|
| Air Filter + Intake | Air entry at BDC | State 1 establishment |
| Piston Compression | Mechanical compression | Process 1→2 |
| Fuel Injector | CI fuel system | Process 2→3 initiation |
| Combustion Chamber | Constant P heat addition | Process 2→3 |
| Power Piston Down | Expansion work | Process 3→4 |
| Exhaust Valve + Cooling | Heat rejection | Process 4→1 |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Conceptual Definition `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define the air-standard Diesel cycle. Why is the heat addition process at constant pressure in a CI engine?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** The air-standard Diesel cycle is an idealized thermodynamic cycle consisting of four reversible processes: isentropic compression (1-2), constant-pressure heat addition (2-3), isentropic expansion (3-4), and constant-volume heat rejection (4-1), with air as the working fluid.
- **Constant Pressure Justification (1 mark):** In a CI engine, fuel is injected over a finite crank angle. The injector is designed to deliver fuel at a rate that matches the rate of volume increase due to piston motion, maintaining pressure nearly constant during combustion.

### Question A2: Formula and Concept `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Write the expression for the air-standard efficiency of a Diesel cycle and explain why its efficiency is always lower than that of the Otto cycle for the same compression ratio.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Formula (2 marks):** $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{k-1}} \left[ \dfrac{\rho^{k} - 1}{k(\rho - 1)} \right]$
- **Comparison Explanation (1 mark):** The factor $\dfrac{\rho^{k} - 1}{k(\rho - 1)} > 1$ for all finite cut-off ratios. This factor equals 1 only when $\rho = 1$ (no heat addition). Hence, Diesel efficiency is always less than Otto efficiency $\eta_{otto} = 1 - 1/r^{k-1}$ at the same compression ratio. However, Diesel engines can use much higher compression ratios (15:1 to 22:1) than Otto engines (8:1 to 12:1) without knock, leading to higher overall practical efficiency.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 3 Choice 1]`

**Q:** An ideal Diesel cycle operates with air at the beginning of compression at **95 kPa** and **20°C**. The compression ratio is **18:1**, and the maximum temperature in the cycle is **1800 K**. Determine:
**(a)** The pressure and temperature at all four corners of the cycle. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The thermal efficiency, net work per kg of air, and mean effective pressure. **(7 marks)**

Take $k = 1.4$, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.

#### Model Solution:

**Given:** $P_1 = 95$ kPa, $T_1 = 293$ K, $r = 18$, $T_3 = 1800$ K

**Part (a): State Points**

**State 1:** $P_1 = 95$ kPa, $T_1 = 293$ K

**State 2 (Isentropic Compression):**

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot r^{k} = 95 \times (18)^{1.4}$$

Computing $18^{1.4}$: $\ln(18^{1.4}) = 1.4 \times 2.8904 = 4.0466 \Rightarrow 18^{1.4} = 57.18$

$$P_2 = 95 \times 57.18 = 5432 \text{ kPa} \approx 54.32 \text{ bar}$$

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{k-1} = 293 \times 3.177 = 931 \text{ K}$$

**State 3 (Constant Pressure):** Given $T_3 = 1800$ K, $P_3 = P_2 = 5432$ kPa

Cut-off ratio:

$$\rho = \frac{T_3}{T_2} = \frac{1800}{931} = 1.933$$

**State 4 (Isentropic Expansion):**

$$T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{\rho}{r}\right)^{k-1} = 1800 \times \left(\frac{1.933}{18}\right)^{0.4}$$

$$\frac{1.933}{18} = 0.1074$$

$(0.1074)^{0.4}$: $\ln(0.1074^{0.4}) = 0.4 \times \ln(0.1074) = 0.4 \times (-2.232) = -0.8928$

$$0.1074^{0.4} = e^{-0.8928} = 0.4094$$

$$T_4 = 1800 \times 0.4094 = 737 \text{ K}$$

$$P_4 = P_3 \cdot \left(\frac{\rho}{r}\right)^{k} = 5432 \times 0.4094 \times \frac{0.1074}{1} $$

Alternatively, use ideal gas law: $P_4 = P_1 \cdot \frac{T_4}{T_1} = 95 \times \frac{737}{293} = 239$ kPa

**Summary of State Points (7 marks):**
| State | P (kPa) | T (K) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 95 | 293 |
| 2 | 5432 | 931 |
| 3 | 5432 | 1800 |
| 4 | 239 | 737 |

**Part (b): Efficiency, Work, MEP**

**Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{k-1}} \cdot \frac{\rho^{k} - 1}{k(\rho - 1)}$$

$$\rho^{k} = (1.933)^{1.4}$$

$\ln(1.933^{1.4}) = 1.4 \times \ln(1.933) = 1.4 \times 0.6592 = 0.9229$

$$\rho^{k} = 2.516$$

$$\frac{\rho^{k} - 1}{k(\rho - 1)} = \frac{2.516 - 1}{1.4 \times (1.933 - 1)} = \frac{1.516}{1.306} = 1.161$$

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1.161}{3.177} = 1 - 0.3655 = 0.6345 = 63.45\%$$

**Net Work per kg:**

$$Q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (1800 - 931) = 873.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$Q_{out} = c_v (T_4 - T_1) = 0.718 \times (737 - 293) = 318.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} = 873.3 - 318.9 = 554.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Mean Effective Pressure:**

$$V_1 = \frac{RT_1}{P_1} = \frac{0.287 \times 293}{95} = 0.8852 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

$$V_2 = \frac{V_1}{18} = 0.04918 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

$$V_1 - V_2 = 0.8360 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

$$\text{MEP} = \frac{554.4}{0.8360} = 663.2 \text{ kPa} \approx 6.63 \text{ bar}$$

**Final Answer Summary (7 marks):**
- $\eta_{th} = 63.45\%$
- $W_{net} = 554.4$ kJ/kg
- $\text{MEP} = 663.2$ kPa

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 3 Choice 2]`

**Q:** A Diesel engine has a compression ratio of **20:1** and the heat supplied per kg of air is **1800 kJ/kg**. The pressure and temperature at the beginning of compression are **1 bar** and **300 K** respectively. Determine:
**(a)** The maximum pressure and temperature in the cycle. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The thermal efficiency and the work done per kg of air. **(7 marks)**

Take $k = 1.4$, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.

#### Model Solution:

**Given:** $r = 20$, $Q_{in} = 1800$ kJ/kg, $P_1 = 100$ kPa, $T_1 = 300$ K

**Part (a): Maximum Pressure and Temperature**

**State 2 (Isentropic Compression):**

$$r^{k-1} = 20^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 20} = e^{0.4 \times 2.9957} = e^{1.1983} = 3.314$$

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{k-1} = 300 \times 3.314 = 994.2 \text{ K}$$

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot r^{k} = 100 \times 20^{1.4}$$

$20^{1.4} = 20 \times 3.314 = 66.29$

$$P_2 = P_{max} = 100 \times 66.29 = 6629 \text{ kPa} \approx 66.29 \text{ bar}$$

**State 3 (Maximum Temperature):**

Using $Q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_2)$:

$$1800 = 1.005 \times (T_3 - 994.2)$$

$$T_3 - 994.2 = \frac{1800}{1.005} = 1791.0$$

$$T_3 = 2785.2 \text{ K}$$

Since $P_3 = P_2 = 6629$ kPa (constant pressure), the **maximum temperature** is $T_3 = 2785.2$ K and **maximum pressure** is $P_2 = P_3 = 6629$ kPa.

**Summary (7 marks):** $P_{max} = 6629$ kPa, $T_{max} = 2785.2$ K

**Part (b): Efficiency and Net Work**

Cut-off ratio:

$$\rho = \frac{T_3}{T_2} = \frac{2785.2}{994.2} = 2.802$$

Compute $\rho^{k} = (2.802)^{1.4}$:

$\ln(2.802^{1.4}) = 1.4 \times \ln(2.802) = 1.4 \times 1.0305 = 1.4427$

$$\rho^{k} = 4.231$$

Efficiency:

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{3.314} \times \frac{4.231 - 1}{1.4 \times (2.802 - 1)}$$

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{3.314} \times \frac{3.231}{1.4 \times 1.802}$$

$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{3.314} \times \frac{3.231}{2.523} = 1 - \frac{1.281}{3.314} = 1 - 0.3866 = 0.6134$$

$$\eta_{th} = 61.34\%$$

Net work:

$$W_{net} = \eta_{th} \times Q_{in} = 0.6134 \times 1800 = 1104.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Summary (7 marks):** $\eta_{th} = 61.34\%$, $W_{net} = 1104.1$ kJ/kg

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units of temperature:** Always convert °C to K before applying any gas law or efficiency formula. A single °C error cascades throughout.
> 2. **Mixing up cut-off ratio with expansion ratio:** Cut-off $\rho = V_3/V_2$ occurs DURING heat addition. Expansion ratio $r_e = V_4/V_3$ occurs DURING expansion.
> 3. **Using wrong process equation:** Students often mistakenly use the Otto cycle formula $\eta = 1 - 1/r^{k-1}$ for the Diesel cycle. **Mark penalty: full marks of the part.**
> 4. **Not drawing both P-V and T-S diagrams:** In 14-mark derivations, the absence of either diagram attracts a **2-mark deduction** even if the numerical answer is correct.
> 5. **Rounding intermediate results:** Always retain 4–5 significant digits in intermediate calculations to avoid cumulative rounding error in the final answer.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Cycle Identity:** Diesel cycle = Isentropic Compression + Constant-Pressure Heat Addition + Isentropic Expansion + Constant-Volume Heat Rejection.
- **Two Key Ratios:** Compression Ratio $r = V_1/V_2$ and Cut-off Ratio $\rho = V_3/V_2$.
- **Master Formula:** $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{k-1}} \left[ \dfrac{\rho^{k} - 1}{k(\rho - 1)} \right]$
- **Effect of $r$:** Higher compression ratio → higher efficiency (just like Otto cycle).
- **Effect of $\rho$:** Higher cut-off ratio → **lower** efficiency (more heat is added at lower average temperature).
- **State Relations to Memorize:**
  * $T_2 = T_1 r^{k-1}$, $P_2 = P_1 r^{k}$
  * $T_3 = T_2 \rho$, $P_3 = P_2$
  * $T_4 = T_1 \rho^{k}$, $P_4 = P_1 \rho^{k}/\rho$ ... wait: $T_4 = T_3 (\rho/r)^{k-1} = T_1 \rho^{k}$
- **Heat Transfer:**
  * $Q_{in} = m c_p (T_3 - T_2)$ — use $c_p$ for constant pressure
  * $Q_{out} = m c_v (T_4 - T_1)$ — use $c_v$ for constant volume
- **Air Properties:** $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.
- **Diagrams:** Always sketch both P-V and T-S diagrams with labeled axes, processes, and four state points (1, 2, 3, 4).
- **Comparison with Otto:** For the same $r$, Diesel < Otto efficiency. But Diesel can use much higher $r$ → overall higher efficiency in practice.
- **MEP Formula:** $\text{MEP} = W_{net} / (V_1 - V_2)$ — evaluate per kg basis using $V_1 = RT_1/P_1$.
- **Always use SI units:** P in kPa or Pa, T in K, V in m³, W in kJ.
- **Watch the difference between SI engine (Otto) and CI engine (Diesel):** The defining feature is the **constant pressure** heat addition in the Diesel cycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dual Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Dual Cycle # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Dual Cycle (Limited Pressure / Mixed / Sabathe Cycle):** A theoretical air-standard thermodynamic cycle that models the working of **modern high-speed compression-ignition (CI) engines** and **high-speed spark-ignition (SI) engines** in which combustion occurs partly at **constant volume** and partly at **constant pressure**. The cycle consists of **five (5) distinct reversible processes** executed by a fixed mass of working fluid (air) enclosed in a piston-cylinder assembly. The two-stage heat addition (constant volume followed by constant pressure) is the defining feature that places this cycle between the pure **Otto Cycle (constant volume heat addition)** and the pure **Diesel Cycle (constant pressure heat addition)**. ### Process Sequence (The Five Processes): | Process | Operation | Thermodynamic Type | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 → 2 | Compression of air | Adiabatic / Isentropic | | 2 → 3 | Heat addition (part 1) | **Constant Volume** | | 3 → 4 | Heat addition (part 2) | **Constant Pressure** | | 4 → 5 | Expansion of gases | Adiabatic / Isentropic | | 5 → 1 | Heat rejection | **Constant Volume** | ### Key Dimensional Parameters: - **Compression Ratio ($r$):** Ratio of volumes before and after compression. $$r = \frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{V_5}{V_2}$$ - **Cut-off Ratio ($r_c$):** Ratio of volumes before and after constant pressure heat addition. $$r_c = \frac{V_4}{V_3}$$ - **Pressure Ratio ($r_p$):** Ratio of pressures during constant volume heat addition. $$r_p = \frac{P_3}{P_2}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why is it called the "Dual Cycle"?** Because heat is added in **two modes simultaneously** — first rapidly at constant volume (modeling the instantaneous combustion of premixed charge) and then progressively at constant pressure (modeling the controlled combustion of the fuel injected later). This dual-mode addition closely approximates real engine behavior. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition > [!TIP] > **The Cooking Analogy (Perfect for KTU Viva!):** > Imagine you are boiling water in a **pressure cooker** to cook rice. First, you close the lid and the gas burns very quickly, causing an **instant pressure spike** (this is the **constant volume** part — like the fuel ignites almost instantaneously). Then, as more gas burns, the pressure stays high but the volume (puffiness of the cooker) keeps increasing slowly (this is the **constant pressure** part — like sustained combustion while the piston is still moving down). Finally, you open the valve and the steam (exhaust gases) rushes out, pressure drops instantly back to atmospheric (this is **constant volume heat rejection**). ### Real Engine Mapping: In an actual high-speed **diesel engine** (compression ratio $r = 14$ to $22$): - The fuel injected doesn't all burn at once. A fraction burns almost instantly (raising pressure sharply) — this is the **constant volume** part. - The remaining fuel burns as the piston descends, with the pressure remaining roughly steady — this is the **constant pressure** part. - Therefore, the **Dual Cycle is a more accurate model** of the real cycle than either Otto or Diesel alone. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Air-Standard Assumptions** (mandatory for all air-standard cycle analyses in KTU): > 1. Working fluid is **air**, behaving as an **ideal gas** throughout the cycle. > 2. All processes are internally reversible; the compression and expansion are adiabatic (isentropic). > 3. Specific heats ($C_p$, $C_v$) and the ratio $\gamma$ remain **constant** during the cycle (cold air-standard assumption). > 4. The cycle is **closed** — same air is reused after heat rejection. > 5. Kinetic and potential energy changes of the fluid are negligible. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > The following standard values are used universally in KTU 2024 numerical problems: > - **Ratio of specific heats:** $\gamma = 1.4$ (for air) > - **Gas constant:** $R = 0.287 \ \text{kJ/kg·K}$ > - **$C_v$ for air:** $0.718 \ \text{kJ/kg·K}$ > - **$C_p$ for air:** $1.005 \ \text{kJ/kg·K}$ > - **Atmospheric conditions (NTP):** $P_1 = 101.325 \ \text{kPa}$, $T_1 = 298 \ \text{K}$ (often rounded to 300 K in problems). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Process Analysis — The "Why" and "How" ### Process 1→2: Isentropic Compression - **What happens:** The piston moves from Bottom Dead Center (BDC) to Top Dead Center (TDC), compressing the air adiabatically. No heat transfer with surroundings. - **Why:** In real engines, compression stroke is fast (~10–20 ms), so there is insufficient time for significant heat loss. The temperature rises drastically (e.g., from 300 K to ~900 K at $r = 16$). - **Mathematical relations:** $$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma-1} = r^{\gamma-1} \quad ; \quad \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma} = r^{\gamma}$$ ### Process 2→3: Constant Volume Heat Addition (Part 1) - **What happens:** Heat $Q_{V}$ is added at constant volume ($V_2 = V_3$). Pressure rises sharply from $P_2$ to $P_3$. - **Why:** Represents the fraction of fuel that ignites nearly instantaneously, causing a sudden pressure rise while the piston is momentarily at TDC. - **Mathematical relations:** $$\frac{T_3}{T_2} = \frac{P_3}{P_2} = r_p \quad ; \quad Q_V = m C_v (T_3 - T_2)$$ ### Process 3→4: Constant Pressure Heat Addition (Part 2) - **What happens:** Heat $Q_p$ is added at constant pressure ($P_3 = P_4$). Volume expands from $V_3$ to $V_4$ as the piston moves down. - **Why:** Represents the remaining fuel that burns progressively as the piston descends, with the regulator (governor) maintaining approximately constant pressure. - **Mathematical relations:** $$\frac{T_4}{T_3} = \frac{V_4}{V_3} = r_c \quad ; \quad Q_p = m C_p (T_4 - T_3)$$ ### Process 4→5: Isentropic Expansion - **What happens:** The high-pressure gases expand adiabatically from $V_4$ to $V_5$ ($V_5 = V_1$), doing work on the piston (power stroke). - **Why:** This is where useful work is extracted. The gases cool rapidly as they expand. - **Mathematical relations:** $$\frac{T_5}{T_4} = \left(\frac{V_4}{V_5}\right)^{\gamma-1} = \left(\frac{r_c}{r}\right)^{\gamma-1}$$ ### Process 5→1: Constant Volume Heat Rejection - **What happens:** Heat $Q_R$ is rejected at constant volume ($V_5 = V_1$). Pressure drops from $P_5$ back to $P_1$. - **Why:** Represents the exhaust process (modeled as instantaneous pressure drop with the heat carried away by exhaust gases). - **Mathematical relations:** $$Q_R = m C_v (T_5 - T_1)$$ --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (HIGH-YIELD FOR EXAMS) > [!IMPORTANT] > **MASTER THIS TABLE — It is the core of every Dual Cycle numerical problem in KTU.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | Compression ratio | $r = V_1 / V_2$ | Dimensionless, always $> 1$ | | 2 | Cut-off ratio | $r_c = V_4 / V_3$ | Dimensionless, typically $1.05$ to $2.0$ | | 3 | Pressure (explosion) ratio | $r_p = P_3 / P_2$ | Dimensionless, typically $1.5$ to $2.0$ | | 4 | Temperature at state 2 | $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$ | K | | 5 | Temperature at state 3 | $T_3 = T_2 \cdot r_p$ | K | | 6 | Temperature at state 4 | $T_4 = T_3 \cdot r_c$ | K | | 7 | Temperature at state 5 | $T_5 = T_1 \cdot r_p \cdot r_c^{\gamma}$ | K (Derivation in §3) | | 8 | Total Heat Supplied | $Q_S = m C_v (T_3 - T_2) + m C_p (T_4 - T_3)$ | kJ | | 9 | Total Heat Rejected | $Q_R = m C_v (T_5 - T_1)$ | kJ | | 10 | **Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{dual}} = 1 - \dfrac{r_p \cdot r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{r^{\gamma-1} \left[ (r_p - 1) + \gamma \cdot r_p (r_c - 1) \right]}$ | Dimensionless | | 11 | Net Work Output | $W_{\text{net}} = Q_S - Q_R$ | kJ | | 12 | Mean Effective Pressure | $\text{MEP} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{V_1 - V_2} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{V_1(1 - 1/r)}$ | kPa | ### Special-Case Reductions (Exam Favorites!): > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Trick:** Setting specific parameters to 1 will reduce the dual cycle to Otto or Diesel. > - If $r_p = 1$ (no constant volume heating): The cycle reduces to the **Diesel Cycle**. > - If $r_c = 1$ (no constant pressure heating): The cycle reduces to the **Otto Cycle**. > - If both $r_p = 1$ and $r_c = 1$: No heat addition — efficiency becomes 0. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The Dual Cycle is **not just a textbook concept** — it directly informs real engine design: 1. **Modern Diesel Engine Design:** High-speed diesels (in cars, trucks, locomotives) operate closer to the Dual Cycle than the pure Diesel Cycle. Engineers use dual cycle analysis to optimize fuel injection timing — the earlier the injection, the larger the $r_p$ component; the later, the larger the $r_c$ component. 2. **HCCI Engines (Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition):** The next-generation low-emission engines have combustion profiles that are best modeled by the dual cycle with very high $r_p$. 3. **Power Output Estimation:** Using the **MEP (Mean Effective Pressure)** derived from the dual cycle, engineers can size the engine displacement needed to deliver a required power output for a given fuel energy input. 4. **Emission Control:** The peak temperature $T_3$ directly correlates with **NOx formation**. Dual cycle analysis helps determine the maximum allowable $r_p$ to keep $T_3$ below the NOx threshold. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Air-Standard Efficiency of the Dual Cycle > [!NOTE] > The derivation below is the **most important derivation** for KTU Thermal Engineering Module 3. Every step is written explicitly with logical justifications. ### **STEP 1: Set Up the Energy Balance** For a cyclic process, the **First Law of Thermodynamics** gives: $$W_{\text{net}} = Q_{\text{net}} = Q_S - Q_R$$ Therefore, the thermal efficiency is: $$\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_S} = 1 - \frac{Q_R}{Q_S}$$ ### **STEP 2: Express the Heat Supplied ($Q_S$)** Heat is added in two stages (process 2→3 at constant volume, process 3→4 at constant pressure): $$ \begin{aligned} Q_S &= Q_{V} + Q_{p} \\ Q_S &= m \cdot C_v \cdot (T_3 - T_2) + m \cdot C_p \cdot (T_4 - T_3) \end{aligned} $$ Factor out $m \cdot C_v$ using $C_p = \gamma \cdot C_v$: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_S &= m \cdot C_v \left[ (T_3 - T_2) + \gamma \cdot (T_4 - T_3) \right] \end{aligned} $$ Substitute $T_3 = r_p \cdot T_2$ and $T_4 = r_c \cdot T_3 = r_p \cdot r_c \cdot T_2$: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_S &= m \cdot C_v \left[ (r_p \cdot T_2 - T_2) + \gamma \cdot (r_p \cdot r_c \cdot T_2 - r_p \cdot T_2) \right] \\ &= m \cdot C_v \cdot T_2 \left[ (r_p - 1) + \gamma \cdot r_p (r_c - 1) \right] \end{aligned} $$ Now substitute $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_S &= m \cdot C_v \cdot T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} \cdot \left[ (r_p - 1) + \gamma \cdot r_p (r_c - 1) \right] \end{aligned} $$ ### **STEP 3: Express the Heat Rejected ($Q_R$)** Heat is rejected at constant volume in process 5→1: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_R = m \cdot C_v \cdot (T_5 - T_1) \end{aligned} $$ ### **STEP 4: Determine $T_5$ in terms of $T_1$, $r$, $r_c$, $r_p$** From the isentropic expansion process 4→5: $$\frac{T_5}{T_4} = \left(\frac{V_4}{V_5}\right)^{\gamma-1}$$ We know $V_5 = V_1$ and $V_4 = r_c \cdot V_3 = r_c \cdot V_2$. Since $V_1 = r \cdot V_2$: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{V_4}{V_5} &= \frac{r_c \cdot V_2}{V_1} = \frac{r_c \cdot V_2}{r \cdot V_2} = \frac{r_c}{r} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{T_5}{T_4} &= \left(\frac{r_c}{r}\right)^{\gamma-1} \\ T_5 &= T_4 \cdot \left(\frac{r_c}{r}\right)^{\gamma-1} \end{aligned} $$ Now substitute $T_4 = T_3 \cdot r_c = T_2 \cdot r_p \cdot r_c = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} \cdot r_p \cdot r_c$: $$ \begin{aligned} T_5 &= T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} \cdot r_p \cdot r_c \cdot \left(\frac{r_c}{r}\right)^{\gamma-1} \\ &= T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} \cdot r_p \cdot r_c \cdot r_c^{\gamma-1} \cdot r^{-(\gamma-1)} \\ &= T_1 \cdot r_p \cdot r_c \cdot r_c^{\gamma-1} \\ &= T_1 \cdot r_p \cdot r_c^{\gamma} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Result:** $\boxed{T_5 = T_1 \cdot r_p \cdot r_c^{\gamma}}$ — this is a frequently asked intermediate result in KTU exams. ### **STEP 5: Substitute $T_5$ into the Heat Rejection Expression** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_R &= m \cdot C_v \cdot (T_1 \cdot r_p \cdot r_c^{\gamma} - T_1) \\ Q_R &= m \cdot C_v \cdot T_1 \cdot (r_p \cdot r_c^{\gamma} - 1) \end{aligned} $$ ### **STEP 6: Form the Ratio $Q_R / Q_S$** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{Q_R}{Q_S} &= \frac{m \cdot C_v \cdot T_1 \cdot (r_p \cdot r_c^{\gamma} - 1)}{m \cdot C_v \cdot T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} \cdot \left[ (r_p - 1) + \gamma \cdot r_p (r_c - 1) \right]} \\ \frac{Q_R}{Q_S} &= \frac{r_p \cdot r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{r^{\gamma-1} \cdot \left[ (r_p - 1) + \gamma \cdot r_p (r_c - 1) \right]} \end{aligned} $$ ### **STEP 7: Final Efficiency Equation** $$ \begin{aligned} \boxed{\eta_{\text{dual}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \left[ \frac{r_p \cdot r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{(r_p - 1) + \gamma \cdot r_p (r_c - 1)} \right]} \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **Validity Check:** Setting $r_p = 1$ (i.e., $P_2 = P_3$) recovers the **Diesel efficiency formula**. Setting $r_c = 1$ (i.e., $V_3 = V_4$) recovers the **Otto efficiency formula**. This is the ultimate verification of the derived expression. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Parameters on Dual Cycle Efficiency > [!NOTE] > The table below summarizes the trends that KTU examiners frequently test via "Discuss the effect of..." or "Compare..." type questions. | Parameter Increase | Effect on $\eta_{\text{dual}}$ | Physical Reasoning | |:---|:---:|:---| | Compression ratio $r$ ↑ | **Increases** | Higher $T_2$ → more work extracted in expansion (dominant effect) | | Pressure ratio $r_p$ ↑ | **Decreases** | More heat added at constant volume → higher $T_3$ → higher $Q_R$ | | Cut-off ratio $r_c$ ↑ | **Decreases** | More heat added at constant pressure → higher $T_4$ and $T_5$ → higher $Q_R$ | | $\gamma$ ↑ (e.g., monoatomic gas) | **Increases** | Better heat-to-work conversion (Carnot-like benefit) | | Heat supplied $Q_S$ (at fixed $r$) | **Depends on $r_p, r_c$** | The way heat is added matters more than the amount | --- ## 3.3 Mean Effective Pressure (MEP) — Complete Derivation The MEP relates the work output of the cycle to the swept volume of the engine: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MEP} &= \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{V_s} = \frac{Q_S - Q_R}{V_1 - V_2} \\ \text{MEP} &= \frac{m \cdot C_v \cdot T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} \left[ (r_p - 1) + \gamma r_p (r_c - 1) \right] - m \cdot C_v \cdot T_1 (r_p r_c^{\gamma} - 1)}{V_1 (1 - 1/r)} \end{aligned} $$ Factoring $m$ using the ideal gas law $P_1 V_1 = m R T_1 \Rightarrow m T_1 = P_1 V_1 / R$ and $C_v = R/(\gamma - 1)$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MEP} &= \frac{P_1}{(\gamma - 1) \cdot (1 - 1/r)} \left[ r^{\gamma} \left( (r_p - 1) + \gamma r_p (r_c - 1) \right) - (r_p r_c^{\gamma} - 1) \right] \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **Exam Shortcut:** Most KTU problems give the **work output** directly, so use $\text{MEP} = W_{\text{net}} / (V_1 - V_2)$ directly without re-deriving the long expression. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 P-V and T-S Diagrams of the Dual Cycle (Mermaid Block Schematic) > [!NOTE] > The schematic below maps the **functional/sequential flow** of the five processes on the P-V plane, including all critical state points. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define the Dual Cycle. Why is it also called the Limited Pressure cycle?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Dual Cycle is an air-standard thermodynamic cycle consisting of **five processes**: isentropic compression (1→2), constant volume heat addition (2→3), constant pressure heat addition (3→4), isentropic expansion (4→5), and constant volume heat rejection (5→1). It is called the **Limited Pressure cycle** because the peak pressure $P_3$ is **limited** by the strength of the engine's mechanical components. In real high-speed engines, the pressure cannot rise indefinitely after combustion starts, so part of the heat is added at constant pressure instead. This "limits" the peak pressure compared to a pure Otto cycle with the same compression ratio and heat input. > [!NOTE] > **Mark distribution:** Definition (1 mark), 5 processes listed (1 mark), Reason for "Limited Pressure" name (1 mark). --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Distinguish between the Otto, Diesel, and Dual cycles in terms of heat addition.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Cycle | Heat Addition Mode | Typical Use | |:---|:---|:---| | Otto | Entirely at **constant volume** | Spark-ignition (SI) engines | | Diesel | Entirely at **constant pressure** | Slow-speed CI engines | | Dual | **Partly at constant volume + partly at constant pressure** | High-speed CI engines | The Dual Cycle is essentially a **combination of Otto and Diesel cycles**, modeling the real combustion process of high-speed engines more accurately than either pure cycle. > [!NOTE] > **Mark distribution:** Table / list with all 3 cycles (2 marks), brief explanation (1 mark). --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module 3 Internal Choice Format** — KTU provides two full 14-mark questions and the student answers one. The structure below mirrors this pattern exactly. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **An air-standard dual cycle has a compression ratio of 16 and a cut-off ratio of 1.5. The pressure ratio during constant volume heat addition is 1.6. If the initial conditions are 100 kPa and 300 K, determine:** **(a)** The temperatures and pressures at all the five cardinal points of the cycle. **[7 marks]** **(b)** The thermal efficiency, net work output per kg of air, and the mean effective pressure. **[7 marks]** **Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005 \ \text{kJ/kg·K}$, $C_v = 0.718 \ \text{kJ/kg·K}$, and $R = 0.287 \ \text{kJ/kg·K}$.** --- #### **Solution to Part (a) — Temperatures and Pressures [7 marks]** **Given:** $r = 16$, $r_c = 1.5$, $r_p = 1.6$, $P_1 = 100 \ \text{kPa}$, $T_1 = 300 \ \text{K}$. **State 1:** $T_1 = 300 \ \text{K}$, $P_1 = 100 \ \text{kPa}$ **State 2** (Isentropic compression): $$ \begin{aligned} T_2 &= T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} = 300 \times 16^{0.4} \\ 16^{0.4} &= 2^{4 \times 0.4} = 2^{1.6} = 3.0314 \\ T_2 &= 300 \times 3.0314 = 909.43 \ \text{K} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} P_2 &= P_1 \cdot r^{\gamma} = 100 \times 16^{1.4} = 100 \times 48.503 = 4850.3 \ \text{kPa} \end{aligned} $$ **State 3** (Constant volume heating): $$ \begin{aligned} T_3 &= T_2 \cdot r_p = 909.43 \times 1.6 = 1455.1 \ \text{K} \\ P_3 &= P_2 \cdot r_p = 4850.3 \times 1.6 = 7760.5 \ \text{kPa} \end{aligned} $$ **State 4** (Constant pressure heating): $$ \begin{aligned} T_4 &= T_3 \cdot r_c = 1455.1 \times 1.5 = 2182.65 \ \text{K} \\ P_4 &= P_3 = 7760.5 \ \text{kPa} \end{aligned} $$ **State 5** (Isentropic expansion): $$ \begin{aligned} T_5 &= T_1 \cdot r_p \cdot r_c^{\gamma} = 300 \times 1.6 \times 1.5^{1.4} \\ 1.5^{1.4} &= e^{1.4 \ln 1.5} = e^{1.4 \times 0.4055} = e^{0.5676} = 1.7641 \\ T_5 &= 300 \times 1.6 \times 1.7641 = 846.8 \ \text{K} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} P_5 &= P_4 \cdot \left(\frac{V_4}{V_5}\right)^{\gamma} = P_4 \cdot \left(\frac{r_c}{r}\right)^{\gamma} \\ P_5 &= 7760.5 \times \left(\frac{1.5}{16}\right)^{1.4} = 7760.5 \times (0.09375)^{1.4} \\ (0.09375)^{1.4} &= e^{1.4 \ln 0.09375} = e^{1.4 \times (-2.367)} = e^{-3.314} = 0.03649 \\ P_5 &= 7760.5 \times 0.03649 = 283.2 \ \text{kPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Summary Table:** | State | Temperature (K) | Pressure (kPa) | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 300.00 | 100.0 | | 2 | 909.43 | 4850.3 | | 3 | 1455.10 | 7760.5 | | 4 | 2182.65 | 7760.5 | | 5 | 846.80 | 283.2 | --- #### **Solution to Part (b) — Efficiency, Work, MEP [7 marks]** **Heat Supplied:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_S &= m C_v (T_3 - T_2) + m C_p (T_4 - T_3) \\ Q_S/m &= 0.718 \times (1455.1 - 909.43) + 1.005 \times (2182.65 - 1455.1) \\ &= 0.718 \times 545.67 + 1.005 \times 727.55 \\ &= 391.79 + 731.19 = 1122.98 \ \text{kJ/kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Heat Rejected:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_R/m &= C_v (T_5 - T_1) = 0.718 \times (846.8 - 300) \\ &= 0.718 \times 546.8 = 392.6 \ \text{kJ/kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Thermal Efficiency:** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{\text{dual}} &= 1 - \frac{Q_R}{Q_S} = 1 - \frac{392.6}{1122.98} = 1 - 0.3496 = 0.6504 \\ \boxed{\eta_{\text{dual}} &= 65.04\%} \end{aligned} $$ **Net Work Output:** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{\text{net}}/m &= Q_S - Q_R = 1122.98 - 392.6 = 730.38 \ \text{kJ/kg} \\ \boxed{W_{\text{net}}/m &= 730.38 \ \text{kJ/kg}} \end{aligned} $$ **Mean Effective Pressure:** Specific volume at state 1: $$ \begin{aligned} v_1 &= \frac{R T_1}{P_1} = \frac{0.287 \times 300}{100} = 0.861 \ \text{m}^3/\text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ Specific volume at state 2: $$ \begin{aligned} v_2 &= v_1 / r = 0.861 / 16 = 0.05381 \ \text{m}^3/\text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ Swept volume: $$ \begin{aligned} v_1 - v_2 &= 0.861 - 0.05381 = 0.8072 \ \text{m}^3/\text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ MEP: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MEP} &= \frac{W_{\text{net}}/m}{v_1 - v_2} = \frac{730.38}{0.8072} \\ \boxed{\text{MEP} &= 904.79 \ \text{kPa}} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **An air-standard dual cycle operates with the following data:** - **Pressure and temperature at the beginning of compression:** $90 \ \text{kPa}$ and $40^\circ\text{C}$ - **Compression ratio:** $r = 18$ - **Heat added at constant volume:** $600 \ \text{kJ/kg}$ - **Heat added at constant pressure:** $400 \ \text{kJ/kg}$ **Determine:** **(a)** The maximum pressure and temperature in the cycle. **[7 marks]** **(b)** The thermal efficiency of the cycle and the work output per kg of air. **[7 marks]** **Take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $C_v = 0.718 \ \text{kJ/kg·K}$.** --- #### **Solution to Part (a) — Max Pressure and Temperature [7 marks]** **Given:** $P_1 = 90 \ \text{kPa}$, $T_1 = 40 + 273 = 313 \ \text{K}$, $r = 18$, $Q_V = 600 \ \text{kJ/kg}$, $Q_p = 400 \ \text{kJ/kg}$. **State 2 (Isentropic compression):** $$ \begin{aligned} T_2 &= T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} = 313 \times 18^{0.4} \\ 18^{0.4} &= e^{0.4 \ln 18} = e^{0.4 \times 2.8904} = e^{1.1562} = 3.178 \\ T_2 &= 313 \times 3.178 = 994.7 \ \text{K} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} P_2 &= P_1 \cdot r^{\gamma} = 90 \times 18^{1.4} \\ 18^{1.4} &= e^{1.4 \ln 18} = e^{1.4 \times 2.8904} = e^{4.0466} = 57.20 \\ P_2 &= 90 \times 57.20 = 5148 \ \text{kPa} \end{aligned} $$ **State 3 (Constant volume heating):** Using $Q_V = C_v (T_3 - T_2)$: $$ \begin{aligned} T_3 &= T_2 + \frac{Q_V}{C_v} = 994.7 + \frac{600}{0.718} = 994.7 + 835.65 = 1830.35 \ \text{K} \\ \boxed{T_3 &= 1830.4 \ \text{K} \ \text{(Maximum Temperature)}} \end{aligned} $$ **Pressure ratio:** $$ \begin{aligned} r_p &= \frac{P_3}{P_2} = \frac{T_3}{T_2} = \frac{1830.35}{994.7} = 1.840 \end{aligned} $$ **Maximum pressure:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_3 &= P_2 \cdot r_p = 5148 \times 1.840 = 9472.3 \ \text{kPa} \\ \boxed{P_3 &= 9472.3 \ \text{kPa} \ \text{(Maximum Pressure)}} \end{aligned} $$ **State 4 (Constant pressure heating):** Using $Q_p = C_p (T_4 - T_3)$ with $C_p = \gamma C_v = 1.4 \times 0.718 = 1.005 \ \text{kJ/kg·K}$: $$ \begin{aligned} T_4 &= T_3 + \frac{Q_p}{C_p} = 1830.35 + \frac{400}{1.005} = 1830.35 + 398.01 = 2228.36 \ \text{K} \end{aligned} $$ **Cut-off ratio:** $$ \begin{aligned} r_c &= \frac{V_4}{V_3} = \frac{T_4}{T_3} = \frac{2228.36}{1830.35} = 1.2175 \end{aligned} $$ --- #### **Solution to Part (b) — Efficiency and Work Output [7 marks]** **State 5 temperature:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_5 &= T_1 \cdot r_p \cdot r_c^{\gamma} = 313 \times 1.840 \times 1.2175^{1.4} \\ 1.2175^{1.4} &= e^{1.4 \ln 1.2175} = e^{1.4 \times 0.1970} = e^{0.2758} = 1.3175 \\ T_5 &= 313 \times 1.840 \times 1.3175 = 758.9 \ \text{K} \end{aligned} $$ **Heat Rejected:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_R &= C_v (T_5 - T_1) = 0.718 \times (758.9 - 313) = 0.718 \times 445.9 = 320.2 \ \text{kJ/kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Total Heat Supplied:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_S &= Q_V + Q_p = 600 + 400 = 1000 \ \text{kJ/kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Thermal Efficiency:** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{\text{dual}} &= 1 - \frac{Q_R}{Q_S} = 1 - \frac{320.2}{1000} = 0.6798 \\ \boxed{\eta_{\text{dual}} &= 67.98\%} \end{aligned} $$ **Work Output:** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{\text{net}} &= Q_S - Q_R = 1000 - 320.2 = 679.8 \ \text{kJ/kg} \\ \boxed{W_{\text{net}} &= 679.8 \ \text{kJ/kg}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!** > > **Common mistakes students make (and lose 2–3 marks each):** > > 1. **Forgetting the $r_c^{\gamma}$ term in $T_5$:** Many students incorrectly use $T_5 = T_4 \cdot (V_4/V_5)^{\gamma-1}$ but compute the volume ratio as $V_4/V_1$ instead of $V_4/V_5 = r_c/r$. The correct expression is $T_5 = T_1 \cdot r_p \cdot r_c^{\gamma}$ — memorize this. > > 2. **Mixing $C_p$ and $C_v$:** Constant volume processes use $C_v$; constant pressure processes use $C_p$. Substituting $C_v$ for $C_p$ in heat addition at constant pressure gives a wrong answer. > > 3. **MEP denominator error:** The denominator is $V_1 - V_2$ (swept volume), NOT $V_1$ alone. Some students forget the $V_2$ term. > > 4. **Unit inconsistency in $P_1 V_1 = mRT_1$:** Always use $P$ in kPa, $V$ in m³, $T$ in K, and $R$ in kJ/kg·K (NOT J/kg·K). Mixing units will give a wrong MEP. > > 5. **Not stating air-standard assumptions:** In derivation questions, KTU awards 1 mark specifically for **listing the air-standard assumptions**. Always begin with this. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Dual Cycle** ### **🔹 Core Concepts to Memorize:** - ✅ The Dual Cycle has **5 processes** (1→2: isentropic comp, 2→3: const V heat, 3→4: const P heat, 4→5: isentropic exp, 5→1: const V heat rej). - ✅ **Three key parameters:** $r$ (compression ratio), $r_c$ (cut-off ratio), $r_p$ (pressure/explosion ratio). - ✅ The cycle reduces to **Otto** when $r_c = 1$, and to **Diesel** when $r_p = 1$. - ✅ Heat is added in **two stages** — that's why it's "dual". ### **🔹 Critical Formulas to Memorize:** - ✅ $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$ - ✅ $P_2 = P_1 \cdot r^{\gamma}$ - ✅ $T_3 = T_2 \cdot r_p$ - ✅ $T_4 = T_3 \cdot r_c$ - ✅ **$T_5 = T_1 \cdot r_p \cdot r_c^{\gamma}$** (most-missed formula) - ✅ $\eta_{\text{dual}} = 1 - \dfrac{r_p r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{r^{\gamma-1} [(r_p - 1) + \gamma r_p (r_c - 1)]}$ - ✅ $\text{MEP} = W_{\text{net}} / (V_1 - V_2)$ ### **🔹 Trends to Remember:** - ✅ Increasing $r$ → **higher** efficiency (always, in absence of other changes). - ✅ Increasing $r_p$ or $r_c$ → **lower** efficiency (more heat added, but more rejected). - ✅ For the **same compression ratio and heat input**, the Dual cycle efficiency lies between Otto and Diesel. ### **🔹 Numerical Problem Pattern (14-Mark Standard):** 1. Calculate $T_2$ using $r^{\gamma-1}$. 2. Calculate $P_2$ using $r^{\gamma}$. 3. Calculate $T_3$ and $P_3$ using $r_p$. 4. Calculate $T_4$ using $r_c$. 5. Calculate $T_5$ using $r_p \cdot r_c^{\gamma}$. 6. Calculate $Q_S$ and $Q_R$. 7. Calculate $\eta$ from $1 - Q_R/Q_S$. 8. Calculate $W_{\text{net}} = Q_S - Q_R$. 9. Calculate MEP using $v_1 = RT_1/P_1$ and $v_2 = v_1/r$. ### **🔹 Air-Standard Assumptions (Always State Before Solving):** - ✅ Ideal gas, constant $C_p$ and $C_v$, all processes internally reversible, closed system, negligible KE/PE changes. ### **🔹 Key Differences (Exam Favorites):** - ✅ **Otto:** all heat at const V → high $T_3$ → high peak pressure. - ✅ **Diesel:** all heat at const P → lower peak pressure for same $r$. - ✅ **Dual:** combines both → peak pressure controlled, more realistic for high-speed engines. > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Tip:** If a problem gives you $Q_V$ and $Q_p$ directly (not $r_p$ and $r_c$), use the heat equations to find $T_3$ and $T_4$ first, then compute $r_p$ and $r_c$ as ratios of temperatures. **Question B above** demonstrates this exact technique!

topics.title: Atkinson Cycle (No Numerical Problems and Derivations)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Atkinson Cycle (No Numerical Problems and Derivations)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Atkinson Cycle (Air-Standard Definition):** The Atkinson cycle is a theoretical, idealised **internal combustion thermodynamic cycle** in which the **expansion ratio is intentionally greater than the compression ratio**. It is achieved by allowing the working fluid to expand over a larger volume than that over which it was originally compressed, thereby extracting a greater portion of the thermal energy as useful work. The cycle was patented by the British engineer **James Atkinson** in **1882**.

In an ideal air-standard analysis, the Atkinson cycle consists of **four distinct processes** applied to a perfect gas operating within a closed system:

- **Process 1 → 2:** Reversible adiabatic (Isentropic) Compression
- **Process 2 → 3:** Constant Volume Heat Addition
- **Process 3 → 4:** Reversible adiabatic (Isentropic) Expansion
- **Process 4 → 1:** Constant Volume Heat Rejection

The defining geometric feature of the cycle on a **P–V diagram** is that point **4 lies at a volume *below* point 1** (i.e., $V_4 < V_1$), so the expansion stroke is geometrically longer than the compression stroke.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Distinction (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**
> - **Compression Ratio ($r_c$)** = $V_1 / V_2$ — fixed by TDC geometry
> - **Expansion Ratio ($r_e$)** = $V_4 / V_2$ — made deliberately larger than $r_c$
> - **Cut-off Ratio for Expansion:** The working fluid continues to push the piston *past* the original BDC, recovering more work.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Slow Breath" Principle

Imagine you are **blowing up a balloon** and then letting it deflate:

- **Otto Cycle Analogy:** You blow air in fast (compression), and let it escape fast through the same nozzle (expansion). Quick in, quick out. Symmetric.
- **Atkinson Cycle Analogy:** You blow air in fast through a small nozzle (compression), but let the air escape through a **much larger opening** so it has more time and distance to push against the surroundings (expansion). The same amount of air, but you extracted more useful "push" from it.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** The Atkinson cycle is essentially the engineering embodiment of the principle: *"Don't waste the hot, expanded gas — let it keep pushing for as long as it still has pressure to give."* This is why **Toyota's hybrid powertrains** prominently brand their engines as "Atkinson Cycle" engines, even though mechanically they are still 4-stroke engines with clever **valve-timing tricks** to mimic the cycle.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance

- **Inventor:** James Atkinson (1882)
- **Cycle Type:** **Theoretical / Idealised** internal combustion cycle
- **Modern User:** Toyota, Hyundai, Kia, Ford (mild hybrids) — using **delayed intake-valve closing** to approximate the cycle.
- **Theoretical Thermal Efficiency:** **Higher** than the Otto cycle at the *same compression ratio*.
- **Practical Power Density:** **Lower** than an equivalent Otto engine.
- **Key Trade-off:** Efficiency vs. Power Output.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Processes — Detailed Logical Breakdown

The Atkinson cycle is analysed as an **air-standard cycle** with the following assumptions:
- The working fluid is a **perfect gas** with constant specific heats ($C_p$, $C_v$).
- All processes are **internally reversible**.
- The kinetic and potential energy changes are negligible.
- **Compression and expansion are isentropic** (reversible adiabatic, no heat transfer).

#### Process 1 → 2: Isentropic Compression
- The piston moves from **Bottom Dead Centre (BDC)** to **Top Dead Centre (TDC)**.
- The gas is compressed **rapidly**, so no heat is exchanged with the surroundings ($Q = 0$).
- Both **pressure and temperature rise sharply**.
- Governing relations involve the isentropic relation for an ideal gas.

#### Process 2 → 3: Constant Volume Heat Addition
- The piston is momentarily at TDC; the volume is held constant.
- Heat $Q_s$ is supplied from an external source (simulating combustion in an SI engine).
- **Pressure rises sharply**, temperature rises from $T_2$ to $T_3$.
- Work done = 0 (since $dV = 0$).

#### Process 3 → 4: Isentropic Expansion (the Defining Step)
- The piston moves from TDC down to a position **below the original BDC** (i.e., $V_4 < V_1$).
- The gas does work on the piston as it expands, pressure and temperature both fall.
- Crucially, **the expansion ratio $r_e$ exceeds the compression ratio $r_c$**.
- This is the cycle's signature feature — **more work is extracted per unit of heat supplied**.

#### Process 4 → 1: Constant Volume Heat Rejection
- The gas is cooled at constant volume from state 4 back to state 1.
- This simulates the exhaust and induction processes in a real engine.
- Heat $Q_r$ is rejected to the surroundings.

### 2.2 Why the Cycle "Works" — The Engineering Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core "Why":** In an ideal Otto cycle, the gas is **forced to stop expanding at the original BDC**, even though it still contains usable pressure-energy. This energy is *wasted* in the exhaust stroke. The Atkinson cycle **lets the gas keep pushing** until its pressure falls closer to atmospheric, recovering the otherwise wasted energy as useful work.

**Consequence on the T–S Diagram:**
- The heat-rejection curve (4 → 1) sits at a **lower temperature** than in an Otto cycle.
- Lower $Q_r$ for the same $Q_s$ → **higher thermal efficiency** by definition ($\eta = 1 - Q_r/Q_s$).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Stated, Not Derived)

> [!NOTE]
> Per the topic constraint, formulas are **listed without derivations**. Each formula is in the standard form expected in a KTU ESE answer script.

#### Governing Geometric Ratios

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Condition |
|----------|--------|---------|-----------|
| Compression Ratio | $r_c$ | $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$ | Always $r_c > 1$ |
| Expansion Ratio | $r_e$ | $\dfrac{V_4}{V_2}$ | $r_e > r_c$ in Atkinson |
| Pressure Ratio | $r_p$ | $\dfrac{P_3}{P_2}$ | Heat-addition ratio |
| Volume at State 4 | $V_4$ | $\dfrac{V_2 \cdot r_e}{1}$ | Geometric reference |

#### Isentropic Process Relations

| Process | Temperature Relation | Pressure Relation |
|---------|----------------------|-------------------|
| 1 → 2 (Isentropic Compression) | $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_c^{\gamma - 1}$ | $P_2 = P_1 \cdot r_c^{\gamma}$ |
| 3 → 4 (Isentropic Expansion) | $T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{r_e}\right)^{\gamma - 1}$ | $P_4 = P_3 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{r_e}\right)^{\gamma}$ |

#### Heat Transfer Quantities (per unit mass)

| Quantity | Formula | Sign Convention |
|----------|---------|-----------------|
| Heat Supplied | $q_s = C_v \cdot (T_3 - T_2)$ | Positive (into system) |
| Heat Rejected | $q_r = C_v \cdot (T_4 - T_1)$ | Negative (out of system) |
| Net Work Output | $w_{net} = q_s - q_r$ | Positive for power cycle |

#### Thermal Efficiency (Closed Form)

$$
\eta_{Atkinson} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2} \cdot \frac{1}{r_c^{\gamma - 1}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Efficiency Comparison Trick (KTU Examiner's Favourite):** At the *same* $r_c$, $T_1$, $T_3$, and using the same fuel/air mixture, the Atkinson cycle always yields $\eta_{Atkinson} > \eta_{Otto}$ because $T_4$ in the Atkinson cycle is **lower** (the gas expanded further and cooled more).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Automotive Hybrid Powertrains** | Toyota THS, Hyundai TMED, Honda i-MMD — all use valve-timing to realise Atkinson cycle at part-load. |
| **Stirling-Adjacent Research** | Modern free-piston engines and HCCI prototypes use Atkinson geometry to push efficiency boundaries. |
| **Range Extenders (EVs)** | BMW i3 REx, Chevrolet Volt — small Atkinson-cycle engines act as on-board generators. |
| **Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs)** | Lightweight Atkinson-based engines for endurance optimisation. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Process-by-Process Analysis & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Scope Note (As Per Topic Constraint):** This section presents the *process-level analytical framework* and comparison matrices for the Atkinson cycle. **No algebraic derivations or numerical problems** are included, in line with the user instruction.

### 3.1 Analytical Framework: Process-by-Process Insight

| Process | State Change | Energy Interaction | KTU Conceptual Takeaway |
|---------|--------------|--------------------|-------------------------|
| 1 → 2 | $V$ decreases, $P \uparrow$, $T \uparrow$ | $Q = 0$, $W$ done **on** gas | Compression work is *stored* as internal energy. |
| 2 → 3 | $V$ constant, $P \uparrow\uparrow$, $T \uparrow\uparrow$ | $Q_s$ added, $W = 0$ | Combustion-equivalent heat addition at TDC. |
| 3 → 4 | $V$ increases (overshoots BDC), $P \downarrow$, $T \downarrow$ | $Q = 0$, $W$ done **by** gas | **The signature step** — more work is extracted. |
| 4 → 1 | $V$ constant, $P \downarrow$, $T \downarrow$ | $Q_r$ rejected, $W = 0$ | Exhaust-equivalent cooling; $T_4 < T_4^{Otto}$. |

### 3.2 The Otto vs. Atkinson Analytical Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is the single most exam-relevant block for Module 3.** Examiners frequently frame a 7-mark sub-question as *"Compare Otto and Atkinson cycles."*

| Parameter | Otto Cycle | Atkinson Cycle | Engineering Implication |
|-----------|------------|----------------|-------------------------|
| Compression Stroke Length | Equal to expansion | **Shorter** than expansion | Piston travels asymmetrically |
| Compression Ratio $r_c$ | $V_1 / V_2$ | $V_1 / V_2$ (same) | Geometric cap by cylinder head |
| Expansion Ratio $r_e$ | $V_1 / V_2 = r_c$ | $V_4 / V_2 > r_c$ | **The defining difference** |
| State 4 Volume | $V_4 = V_1$ (Otto returns to BDC) | $V_4 < V_1$ (extends past BDC) | Asymmetric loop on P–V |
| Heat Rejection Temperature | $T_1$ (at higher pressure) | **Lower** than Otto for same $T_1$ | $\eta$ improves |
| Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{Otto} = 1 - 1/r_c^{\gamma-1}$ | $\eta_{Atk} > \eta_{Otto}$ at same $r_c$ | Efficiency gain |
| Power Density (per litre) | Higher | **Lower** | Trade-off vs. efficiency |
| Exhaust Gas Temperature | Higher | **Lower** | Heat-recovery potential |
| Mechanical Complexity | Standard 4-stroke | Needs **long-stroke** or **VVT** | Higher cost |
| Practical Realisation | Standard SI engine | Hybrid powertrains, range-extenders | Modern niche |

### 3.3 The Variable Valve Timing Trick (Modern Realisation)

> [!TIP]
> **Why KTU Examiners Love This Topic:** Modern cars do not physically use a long-stroke crank for the Atkinson cycle. Instead, they use **VVT (Variable Valve Timing)** with **delayed intake-valve closing (Late Intake Valve Closing — LIVC)**:
> - During the intake stroke, the intake valve remains open *past BDC* of the intake.
> - Some of the fresh charge is pushed *back* into the intake manifold, reducing the effective trapped mass.
> - Net effect: a *smaller effective compression ratio* with a *larger effective expansion ratio* — the Atkinson signature.

This realisation makes the Atkinson cycle achievable in a **conventional 4-stroke engine geometry**, which is why nearly every modern hybrid uses it.

### 3.4 The Miller Cycle Connection (Conceptual Bridge)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module 3 often pairs Atkinson with the **Miller Cycle**. They are *conceptual cousins*:
> - **Atkinson:** $r_e > r_c$, achieved by *extended expansion*.
> - **Miller:** $r_e > r_c$, achieved by *shortened effective compression* (early or late intake-valve closing on a turbocharged engine).
> - Both yield the same efficiency benefit; the mechanical pathway differs. Examiners often expect students to state this contrast.

### 3.5 Limitations of the Atkinson Cycle (Examiner's Favourite "Discuss" Angle)

1. **Reduced Power Output:** Because the engine is "breathing" less fresh charge per cycle, peak power falls.
2. **Pulsating Torque:** Realisation through long-stroke cranks causes non-uniform torque delivery.
3. **Mechanical Packaging:** A longer-stroke engine is physically taller, complicating vehicle integration.
4. **Sub-optimal at High Loads:** The cycle's efficiency advantage *vanishes* at wide-open throttle; hence it is best suited to **part-load hybrid operation**.
5. **Complex Valve Actuation:** Modern VVT systems add cost, calibration burden, and oil-consumption concerns.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 P–V and T–S Process Flow (Atkinson Cycle Architecture)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **thermodynamic process sequence** of the air-standard Atkinson cycle. The flow reads clockwise around the cycle on a standard P–V diagram, with state numbers corresponding to the academic notation used in Sections 1–3.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["STATE 1: BDC Start, Low P, High V, Temp T1"]:::startNode
    B["PROCESS 1 to 2: ISENTROPIC COMPRESSION - Q equals 0, P rises, T rises, V decreases to V2"]:::compNode
    C["STATE 2: TDC After Compression, High P, Small V, Temp T2"]:::tdcNode
    D["PROCESS 2 to 3: CONSTANT VOLUME HEAT ADDITION - Qs added, P rises sharply, T rises to T3, V constant"]:::heatInNode
    E["STATE 3: Peak Pressure, Volume V2, Temp T3"]:::peakNode
    F["PROCESS 3 to 4: ISENTROPIC EXPANSION LONG STROKE - Q equals 0, P falls, T falls, V decreases to V4 less than V1"]:::expNode
    G["STATE 4: BELOW ORIGINAL BDC, Low P, V4 less than V1, Temp T4 less than Otto case"]:::lowNode
    H["PROCESS 4 to 1: CONSTANT VOLUME HEAT REJECTION - Qr rejected, P falls, T falls to T1, V constant"]:::heatOutNode
    I["WORK OUTPUT: W equals Qs minus Qr, greater than Otto at same rc"]:::workNode

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> A
    G --> I

    classDef startNode fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1f7a3a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef compNode fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#a37c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tdcNode fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#a14d00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef heatInNode fill:#ffadad,stroke:#a30000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef peakNode fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#7a0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef expNode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003e7a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lowNode fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#3a1f7a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef heatOutNode fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#1f3a7a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef workNode fill:#caffbf,stroke:#1f7a3a,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Comparison: Otto vs Atkinson Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OTTO["OTTO CYCLE ENGINE BLOCK"]
        O1["INTAKE VALVE: Closes at BDC"]
        O2["PISTON: Symmetric up-down travel"]
        O3["EXPANSION: Ends exactly at BDC"]
        O4["EFFICIENCY: Standard"]
        O1 --> O2 --> O3 --> O4
    end

    subgraph ATK["ATKINSON CYCLE ENGINE BLOCK"]
        A1["INTAKE VALVE: Closes LATE past BDC VVT"]
        A2["PISTON: Effective compression reduced"]
        A3["EXPANSION: Extends past BDC"]
        A4["EFFICIENCY: Higher than Otto at same rc"]
        A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4
    end

    OTTO -. COMPARISON .-> ATK
    ATK -. SAME FUEL SAME COMPRESSION .-> OTTO

    classDef ottoBlock fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#a14d00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef atkBlock fill:#caffbf,stroke:#1f7a3a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class O1,O2,O3,O4 ottoBlock
    class A1,A2,A3,A4 atkBlock
```

### 4.3 Schematic Interpretation Guide

| Element in Diagram | Represents in the Real Engine |
|--------------------|-------------------------------|
| State 1 → 2 arc | Compression stroke of the SI engine |
| State 2 → 3 vertical line | Combustion event at TDC (constant volume approximation) |
| State 3 → 4 arc (extended) | Power stroke extended past original BDC |
| State 4 → 1 vertical line | Exhaust blow-down + cooling (constant volume approximation) |
| Delayed intake-valve closure (VVT block) | Modern mechanical route to Atkinson behaviour |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*Define the Atkinson cycle. What distinguishes it from the Otto cycle? (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The Atkinson cycle is an ideal air-standard thermodynamic cycle for internal combustion engines in which the **expansion ratio is greater than the compression ratio**, achieved by allowing the working fluid to expand over a volume larger than that over which it was initially compressed.

**Distinction from Otto cycle:**
- In an **Otto cycle**, the expansion ratio equals the compression ratio ($r_e = r_c$), so the expansion ends exactly at the original BDC.
- In an **Atkinson cycle**, $r_e > r_c$, so the expansion continues past the original BDC, recovering additional work that the Otto cycle discards.
- Result: The Atkinson cycle has a **higher thermal efficiency** than the Otto cycle at the *same compression ratio*, but a **lower power density** per unit displacement.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for distinction, 1 for consequence)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional]**
*List any three practical limitations of the Atkinson cycle. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Reduced power output** per litre of displacement because the effective trapped charge is lower (in VVT realisations) or because the cylinder does less work per cycle.
2. **Pulsating and non-uniform torque delivery** if realised through long-stroke geometry.
3. **Mechanical complexity and cost** — Variable Valve Timing (VVT) systems with Late Intake Valve Closing add calibration, oil-consumption, and durability concerns.
4. (Optional 4th) **Sub-optimal at high loads** — the cycle's efficiency advantage diminishes at wide-open throttle.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per valid limitation, max 3)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat **P–V diagram**, describe the four processes of the air-standard Atkinson cycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **Otto and Atkinson cycles** on the basis of: (i) compression and expansion ratios, (ii) thermal efficiency, (iii) power output, and (iv) exhaust temperature. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — State the four processes clearly (2 marks):**
1. **Process 1 → 2:** Reversible adiabatic (isentropic) compression from BDC to TDC.
2. **Process 2 → 3:** Constant volume heat addition ($Q_s$ supplied).
3. **Process 3 → 4:** Reversible adiabatic (isentropic) expansion, with $V_4 < V_1$.
4. **Process 4 → 1:** Constant volume heat rejection ($Q_r$ rejected).

**Step 2 — Draw the P–V diagram (3 marks):**
- Plot a closed loop with state 1 at lower-right, state 2 at upper-left (TDC), state 3 just above state 2 (constant volume heat addition), and state 4 *below* state 1 (extended expansion).
- Label all four states and the curve segments clearly. The asymmetry of the loop ($V_4 < V_1$) is the diagram's signature feature.

**Step 3 — State the key relations (2 marks):**
- Compression ratio $r_c = V_1 / V_2$
- Expansion ratio $r_e = V_4 / V_2$ with $r_e > r_c$
- $q_s = C_v(T_3 - T_2)$, $q_r = C_v(T_4 - T_1)$
- $w_{net} = q_s - q_r$

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

| Parameter | Otto Cycle | Atkinson Cycle |
|-----------|------------|----------------|
| (i) Compression and Expansion Ratios | $r_e = r_c$ (symmetric) | $r_e > r_c$ (asymmetric) |
| (ii) Thermal Efficiency | $\eta = 1 - 1/r_c^{\gamma-1}$ | **Higher** than Otto at same $r_c$ |
| (iii) Power Output | Higher per unit displacement | **Lower** per unit displacement |
| (iv) Exhaust Temperature | Higher | **Lower** (gas expanded further) |

**Concluding statement (1 mark):** The Atkinson cycle trades power for efficiency, making it ideal for part-load hybrid applications where BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption) matters more than peak output.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **concept of Variable Valve Timing (VVT)** as a means of approximating the Atkinson cycle in modern spark-ignition engines. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **modern automotive applications** of the Atkinson cycle. Why is it especially favoured in **hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs)**? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Define VVT (2 marks):**
Variable Valve Timing refers to engine valvetrain systems that can dynamically adjust the **timing**, **duration**, and in some cases the **lift** of the intake and exhaust valves, controlled electronically by the ECU.

**Step 2 — Explain Late Intake Valve Closing (LIVC) (3 marks):**
In LIVC, the intake valve remains open *past* the BDC of the intake stroke. A portion of the fresh charge is pushed back into the intake manifold. This:
- Reduces the **effective trapped mass** → lowers the effective compression ratio.
- The piston then compresses *less* charge to TDC, but the subsequent expansion pushes the *same* (now hot) gases over the *full* stroke.

**Step 3 — Link to Atkinson signature (2 marks):**
The net effect is **$r_e > r_c$ at the system level**, mimicking the Atkinson cycle geometry without requiring a long-stroke crank. Toyota's VVT-i, Honda's i-VTEC, and Hyundai's CVVD all use variants of this principle.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — List real-world applications (3 marks):**
- Toyota Prius, Camry Hybrid, Corolla Hybrid (THS-II system).
- Hyundai Ioniq, Kia Niro (TMED system).
- Honda Insight, Accord Hybrid (i-MMD).
- Range-extender engines in BMW i3 REx and Chevrolet Volt.

**Step 2 — Why HEVs favour Atkinson (3 marks):**
1. **Part-load operation:** HEV engines mostly run at low/medium loads where Atkinson efficiency gains are maximal.
2. **Electric motor compensates for power deficit:** The hybrid electric machine covers the torque hole, so the engine's lower power density is not a penalty.
3. **BSFC optimisation:** Atkinson cycle achieves minimum BSFC at typical cruising conditions, which dominates real-world fuel economy.

**Step 3 — Closing remark (1 mark):**
The Atkinson cycle is therefore a **system-level enabler** of modern hybrid powertrains — its limitation in power is *engineered around* by electrification, while its efficiency is *amplified* by regenerative braking.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Atkinson Questions**
> 1. **Confusing Atkinson with Diesel Cycle:** Students sometimes think Atkinson = constant pressure heat addition (it is NOT — it is constant volume). State heat-addition process explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting $V_4 < V_1$:** The defining geometric feature of the P–V diagram is that point 4 is *below* point 1 in volume. Drawing it equal to V1 will be marked wrong.
> 3. **Claiming Atkinson is always more efficient:** It is *theoretically* more efficient than Otto at the *same $r_c$*. With a *smaller* effective $r_c$ (VVT mode), the comparison is not direct — note the qualifier.
> 4. **Ignoring the power-density trade-off:** A "Discuss" question that praises efficiency without acknowledging the power penalty loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **No mention of VVT mechanism:** A "Modern realisation" answer without the LIVC explanation is incomplete.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
