> **Atkinson Cycle — 60-Second Revision Bulletins**

- **Cycle family:** Air-standard, constant volume heat addition, **constant volume heat rejection**.
- **Defining trait:** $r_e > r_c$ — expansion ratio **exceeds** compression ratio.
- **P–V signature:** State 4 lies *below* state 1 ($V_4 < V_1$).
- **Efficiency vs. Otto:** Higher at the **same $r_c$**; theoretical gain comes from **lower $Q_r$** at a lower temperature.
- **Power:** Lower than Otto at the **same displacement** (key trade-off).
- **Exhaust temp:** Lower than Otto — useful for heat-recovery systems.
- **Modern realisation:** **Late Intake Valve Closing (LIVC)** via VVT — no long-stroke crank needed.
- **Primary application domain:** **Hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs)** and **range-extender EVs**.
- **Conceptual cousin:** **Miller Cycle** — same $r_e > r_c$ outcome, achieved via early intake-valve closing on a **turbocharged** engine.
- **Sister cycles to revise alongside (Module 3):** Otto, Diesel, Dual (Sabathé), Brayton, Stirling, Ericsson, Lenoir.
- **Do NOT confuse with:** Diesel cycle (constant pressure heat addition) or Lenoir cycle (constant volume heat addition + constant pressure heat rejection).
- **Exam phrasing to recognise:** *"Justify why Atkinson has higher efficiency than Otto."* → Answer: because the gas expands further, $T_4$ is lower, so $Q_r$ is smaller, and $\eta = 1 - Q_r/Q_s$ rises.
- **Assumption set to declare:** Perfect gas, constant $C_p$ and $C_v$, reversible processes, closed system, negligible KE/PE changes.
- **Inventor & Year:** James Atkinson, **1882**.
- **Toyota branding note:** Toyota calls their HEV engines "Atkinson-cycle engines" — a marketing label for a VVT-LIVC implementation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reciprocating Type SI and CI Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reciprocating Type SI and CI Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Reciprocating Internal Combustion Engine?

A **Reciprocating Internal Combustion (IC) Engine** is a heat engine that converts the chemical energy of a liquid/gaseous fuel into useful mechanical work (rotational) by burning the fuel *inside* the engine cylinder. The "reciprocating" part refers to the back-and-forth (piston-like) motion of the working piston inside a cylindrical bore, which is then converted to rotational motion via a crank-slider mechanism.

KTU 2024 scheme formal definition:
> A prime mover that directly burns fuel inside a working cylinder, producing high-pressure gases that push a piston in linear (reciprocating) motion, ultimately delivering power at the crankshaft.

## 1.2 Two Major Families: SI vs CI

The KTU syllabus groups reciprocating IC engines into two primary families based on **HOW the fuel-air mixture is ignited**:

| Family | Full Form | Ignition Method | Typical Fuel | Example Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SI Engine** | Spark Ignition | External spark plug | Petrol/Gasoline | Cars, Motorcycles, Scooters |
| **CI Engine** | Compression Ignition | Auto-ignition by heat of compression | Diesel | Trucks, Buses, Locomotives, Pumps |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Board Favourite Question: "Differentiate between SI and CI engines." — always quote the ignition method as the *first* point of distinction; examiners give free marks for this!

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (The "Popcorn vs Pressure Cooker" Analogy)

Imagine two ways to pop popcorn:

- **SI Engine = Candle + Popcorn**: You hold popcorn over an open flame (the spark plug provides a small "candle" of fire). The flame must travel to start cooking each kernel. Easy to control, gentle, but you cannot over-compress the kernel or it just won't pop.
- **CI Engine = Pressure Cooker**: You put the popcorn in a sealed pressure cooker, crank up the heat and pressure (compression), and the kernel pops by itself when the temperature crosses a critical threshold. No flame needed, but you need heavy steel walls to handle the pressure.

That is precisely why **SI engines use a low compression ratio (8–12)** while **CI engines use a high compression ratio (14–22)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - Air-Fuel Ratio (Stoichiometric): Petrol ≈ **15:1**, Diesel ≈ **14.7:1**
> - Compression Ratio (r): SI → **8 to 12**, CI → **14 to 22**
> - Ignition Temperature: Petrol ≈ **230–250 °C**, Diesel ≈ **210–230 °C**
> - Universal Gas Constant: **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - Specific gas constant for air: **R_air = 287 J/(kg·K)**

## 1.4 Classification Overview (KTU Module 3 Scope)

Reciprocating IC Engines can be classified on several bases:

1. **By Ignition Method**: SI (Spark Ignition) and CI (Compression Ignition)
2. **By Number of Strokes**: 2-Stroke and 4-Stroke
3. **By Fuel Used**: Petrol, Diesel, LPG, CNG, Bio-diesel, Hydrogen
4. **By Cooling**: Air-Cooled, Water-Cooled
5. **By Speed**: Low (≤300 rpm), Medium (300–1000 rpm), High (>1000 rpm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Module 3 strictly limits scope to **SI and CI reciprocating engines** in their 4-stroke form (and briefly 2-stroke). Hybrid engines, Wankel (rotary) engines, and gas turbines are NOT in this module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The SI Engine — Spark Ignition (Otto) Engine

### 2.1.1 Construction
The main parts are:
- **Cylinder Block**: Hosts the cylinder bore; usually cast iron or aluminium alloy.
- **Piston**: Moves up and down; transmits gas force to connecting rod.
- **Connecting Rod**: Links piston to crankshaft.
- **Crankshaft**: Converts reciprocating motion to rotary motion.
- **Valves**: Intake (suction) valve + Exhaust valve, operated by camshaft.
- **Spark Plug**: Screwed into cylinder head; delivers high-voltage spark (≈ 20,000–30,000 V).
- **Carburettor / Fuel Injector**: Mixes fuel with air before/after the intake valve.
- **Flywheel**: Stores rotational inertia for smooth operation.

### 2.1.2 Working of a 4-Stroke SI Engine

The 4-stroke cycle completes **two full crank revolutions (720°)** per power stroke.

| Stroke | Crank Angle | Valve Status | Process | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Suction (Intake)** | 0° → 180° | Intake: OPEN, Exhaust: CLOSED | Fresh air-fuel mixture drawn in | Piston moves TDC → BDC |
| **2. Compression** | 180° → 360° | Both CLOSED | Mixture compressed adiabatically | Piston moves BDC → TDC |
| **3. Power (Expansion)** | 360° → 540° | Both CLOSED (then exhaust opens) | Spark ignites mixture near TDC, combustion, expansion | Piston moves TDC → BDC |
| **4. Exhaust** | 540° → 720° | Exhaust: OPEN, Intake: CLOSED | Burnt gases pushed out | Piston moves BDC → TDC |

### 2.1.3 Theoretical Cycle: Constant Volume Heat Addition (Otto Cycle)

The air-standard Otto cycle consists of four ideal processes:
1. **1 → 2**: Isentropic (adiabatic) compression
2. **2 → 3**: Constant Volume heat addition (combustion at TDC)
3. **3 → 4**: Isentropic (adiabatic) expansion
4. **4 → 1**: Constant Volume heat rejection

> [!NOTE]
> The **SI engine approximates the Otto cycle**, but real engines deviate due to finite combustion duration, heat losses, and valve timing.

## 2.2 The CI Engine — Compression Ignition (Diesel) Engine

### 2.2.1 Construction Highlights
- **No spark plug!** Instead, a fuel injector sprays diesel directly into the hot compressed air.
- Heavier cylinder block, stronger crankshaft, and a higher-pressure fuel injection system (common-rail in modern engines can reach 2000 bar).
- Cylinder head usually houses only valves (no spark plug port).

### 2.2.2 Working of a 4-Stroke CI Engine

| Stroke | Crank Angle | Valve Status | Process | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Suction** | 0° → 180° | Intake: OPEN | **Only air** drawn in (no fuel mixing) | Piston TDC → BDC |
| **2. Compression** | 180° → 360° | Both CLOSED | Air compressed to high P and T (T > ignition temp of diesel) | Piston BDC → TDC |
| **3. Power (Expansion)** | 360° → 540° | Both CLOSED (exhaust opens later) | Fuel injected near TDC, auto-ignites, combustion + expansion | Piston TDC → BDC |
| **4. Exhaust** | 540° → 720° | Exhaust: OPEN | Burnt gases expelled | Piston BDC → TDC |

### 2.2.3 Theoretical Cycle: Constant Pressure Heat Addition (Diesel Cycle)

1. **1 → 2**: Isentropic compression
2. **2 → 3**: Constant Pressure heat addition (fuel injection as piston moves slightly down from TDC)
3. **3 → 4**: Isentropic expansion
4. **4 → 1**: Constant Volume heat rejection

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these formulas — they appear in almost every KTU numerical on Module 3.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compression Ratio | $r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{V_c + V_s}{V_c}$ | $V_1$ = BDC volume, $V_2$ = TDC (clearance) volume, $V_s$ = swept volume, $V_c$ = clearance volume | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Otto (SI) Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | $r$ = compression ratio, $\gamma$ = $C_p/C_v$ ≈ 1.4 for air | Decimal / % |
| 3 | Diesel (CI) Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | $\rho$ = cut-off ratio $V_3/V_2$ | Decimal / % |
| 4 | Mean Effective Pressure (IMEP) | $P_{mi} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{V_s}$ | $W_{net}$ = net work per cycle, $V_s$ = swept volume | N/m² (Pa) |
| 5 | Indicated Power | $IP = \dfrac{P_{mi} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot K}{60}$ | $L$=length of stroke (m), $A$=area (m²), $N$=rpm, $K$ = 1 (4-stroke) or 2 (2-stroke) | kW |
| 6 | Brake Power | $BP = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | $N$ = rpm, $T$ = brake torque (N·m) | W or kW |
| 7 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$ | $\dot{m}_f$ = fuel flow rate (kg/s), $CV$ = calorific value (J/kg) | Decimal / % |
| 8 | Indicated Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{ith} = \dfrac{IP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$ | — | Decimal / % |
| 9 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP} = \dfrac{\eta_{bth}}{\eta_{ith}}$ | — | Decimal / % |
| 10 | Specific Fuel Consumption | $SFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} \times 3600$ | fuel per kW·hr | kg/kWh |
| 11 | Air Standard Efficiency (General) | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$ | Net work / heat input | Decimal / % |
| 12 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\text{Actual mass of air}}{\text{Theoretical mass at NTP}}$ | — | Decimal / % |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Exam Trap: Students often confuse $V_1$ and $V_2$ in compression ratio. **$V_1$ is the LARGER volume (BDC, beginning of compression) and $V_2$ is the SMALLER (TDC, end of compression).** Always $V_1 > V_2$ so $r > 1$.

## 2.4 Comparison Table: SI vs CI Engine (Must Memorize for KTU)

| Parameter | SI Engine | CI Engine |
|---|---|---|
| Basic Cycle | Otto (Constant Volume) | Diesel (Constant Pressure) |
| Fuel | Petrol (gasoline), LPG | Diesel |
| Carburettor / Injection | Carburettor or MPFI | Direct Injection only |
| Compression Ratio | 8 – 12 | 14 – 22 |
| Ignition Method | Spark Plug (20,000 V) | Auto-ignition by compression heat |
| Air-Fuel Mixture | Prepared outside / inside cylinder (PFI / carb) | Only air compressed; fuel injected near TDC |
| Throttle | Yes (controls load via mixture quantity) | No throttle (load controlled by fuel quantity) |
| Starting | Easy (light starting motor) | Harder (needs battery / heater plugs) |
| Weight | Lighter | Heavier (high pressure to handle) |
| Speed | High speed preferred | Generally low to medium speed |
| Thermal Efficiency | Lower (≈ 25–30%) | Higher (≈ 35–42%) |
| Knock | Tendency to knock (pre-ignition) | Less knock tendency (slower combustion) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (precision injection) |
| Exhaust | CO, unburnt HC (rich mixture) | NOx, soot (PM) |
| Application | Cars, bikes, light vehicles | Trucks, buses, ships, gensets |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SI engines** dominate passenger transport because they rev high, are light, and run quietly.
- **CI engines** are preferred for heavy-load commercial transport and power generation because their higher efficiency saves fuel over millions of kilometers.
- The Otto cycle is also the basis of hybrid-electric Atkinson/Miller cycles.
- The Diesel cycle underpins large marine engines (MAN B&W 2-stroke crosshead).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Air-Standard Efficiency of Otto Cycle (SI Engine)

**Starting Premises (Air-Standard Assumptions):**
1. Working fluid is ideal air, behaving as a perfect gas.
2. All processes are internally reversible.
3. Combustion is replaced by heat addition from an external source.
4. Valve events are ignored; processes occur in a closed system.

**Process Description:**

| Process | Type | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 → 2 | Isentropic Compression | $p V^\gamma = \text{const}$ and $T V^{\gamma-1} = \text{const}$ |
| 2 → 3 | Constant Volume Heat Addition | $V_2 = V_3$, $Q_{in} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2)$ |
| 3 → 4 | Isentropic Expansion | $T_3 V_3^{\gamma-1} = T_4 V_4^{\gamma-1}$ |
| 4 → 1 | Constant Volume Heat Rejection | $V_4 = V_1$, $Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$ |

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Since $V_1 = V_4$ and $V_2 = V_3$, the compression ratio is:

$$
r = \frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{V_4}{V_3}
$$

Heat added at constant volume:

$$
Q_{in} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2)
$$

Heat rejected at constant volume:

$$
Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)
$$

Thermal efficiency of a cycle:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}
$$

Substitute $Q_{in}$ and $Q_{out}$:

$$
\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{m C_v (T_4 - T_1)}{m C_v (T_3 - T_2)} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}
$$

For isentropic process 1 → 2: $T_1 V_1^{\gamma - 1} = T_2 V_2^{\gamma - 1}$

$$
\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = r^{\gamma - 1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_2 = T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1}
$$

For isentropic process 3 → 4: $T_3 V_3^{\gamma - 1} = T_4 V_4^{\gamma - 1}$

Since $V_3 = V_2$ and $V_4 = V_1$:

$$
\frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{V_4}{V_3}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = r^{\gamma - 1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r^{\gamma - 1}}
$$

Now substitute $T_2$ and $T_4$ into the efficiency equation:

$$
\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{\dfrac{T_3}{r^{\gamma - 1}} - T_1}{T_3 - T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1}}
$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $T_1$:

$$
\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{\dfrac{T_3}{T_1} \cdot \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} - 1}{\dfrac{T_3}{T_1} - r^{\gamma - 1}}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $r^{\gamma - 1}$:

$$
\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{\dfrac{T_3}{T_1} - r^{\gamma - 1}}{r^{\gamma - 1} \cdot \dfrac{T_3}{T_1} - r^{2(\gamma - 1)}}
$$

Factoring $r^{\gamma - 1}$ from denominator and using $T_2/T_1 = r^{\gamma-1}$:

$$
\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}
$$

**Final Result (Boxed for Exam):**

$$
\boxed{\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** Efficiency increases as $r$ increases. That's why we want high compression — but in SI engines, $r$ is capped by **knocking** (pre-ignition tendency of petrol).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Air-Standard Efficiency of Diesel Cycle (CI Engine)

**Process Description:**

| Process | Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 1 → 2 | Isentropic Compression | $T_2 = T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1}$ |
| 2 → 3 | Constant Pressure Heat Addition | $p_2 = p_3$, $Q_{in} = m C_p (T_3 - T_2)$ |
| 3 → 4 | Isentropic Expansion | — |
| 4 → 1 | Constant Volume Heat Rejection | $Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$ |

**Cut-off Ratio (ρ):**

$$
\rho = \frac{V_3}{V_2}
$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Heat added (constant pressure):

$$
Q_{in} = m C_p (T_3 - T_2)
$$

Heat rejected (constant volume):

$$
Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)
$$

Thermal efficiency:

$$
\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{C_v (T_4 - T_1)}{C_p (T_3 - T_2)} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}
$$

For isentropic process 3 → 4: $\dfrac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\dfrac{V_4}{V_3}\right)^{\gamma - 1}$

Since $\dfrac{V_4}{V_3} = \dfrac{V_1}{V_3} = \dfrac{r}{\rho}$ (because $V_1/V_2 = r$ and $V_3/V_2 = \rho$):

$$
\frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{r}{\rho}\right)^{\gamma - 1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_4 = T_3 \left(\frac{\rho}{r}\right)^{\gamma - 1}
$$

Also, $T_1 = T_2 / r^{\gamma - 1}$.

Substitute $T_1$ and $T_4$ into efficiency expression and simplify:

$$
\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{T_3 \left(\dfrac{\rho}{r}\right)^{\gamma - 1} - \dfrac{T_2}{r^{\gamma - 1}}}{T_3 - T_2}
$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $T_2$ and using $T_3/T_2 = \rho$ (constant pressure process):

$$
\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{\rho \left(\dfrac{\rho}{r}\right)^{\gamma - 1} - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}}{\rho - 1}
$$

Simplifying (multiply top and bottom by $r^{\gamma - 1}$):

$$
\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \frac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\rho - 1}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \frac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma (\rho - 1)}}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** Unlike Otto, Diesel efficiency depends on **BOTH** $r$ and $\rho$. For higher efficiency, we want higher $r$ and lower $\rho$ (less cut-off). However, lower $\rho$ means less fuel injected → less power. It's a trade-off.

---

## 3.3 Comparison: Otto vs Diesel at Same Compression Ratio

For the same $r$, which cycle is more efficient?

**Mathematical Proof Sketch:**

$$
\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}
$$

$$
\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \frac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma (\rho - 1)}
$$

Let $f(\rho) = \dfrac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma (\rho - 1)}$. For $\rho > 1$ and $\gamma > 1$, it can be shown using L'Hôpital's rule and calculus that $f(\rho) > 1$. Therefore:

$$
\eta_{diesel} < \eta_{otto} \text{ (for same } r \text{)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, **CI engines are MORE efficient** because they can use $r = 18$–$20$ (limited only by mechanical strength, not by knock), whereas SI engines are stuck at $r = 10$–$12$. So even though $\eta_{diesel} < \eta_{otto}$ at the same $r$, the *much higher* $r$ of CI makes it win overall.

---

## 3.4 Solved Analytical Problem (KTU 3-Mark Style)

> **Q:** An SI engine has a compression ratio of 9. Take $\gamma = 1.4$. Calculate the air-standard efficiency.

**Given:** $r = 9$, $\gamma = 1.4$

**Solution:**

$$
\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{9^{1.4 - 1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{9^{0.4}}
$$

Compute $9^{0.4}$:

$$
9^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 9} = e^{0.4 \times 2.1972} = e^{0.8789} \approx 2.4082
$$

Therefore:

$$
\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.4082} = 1 - 0.4152 = 0.5848
$$

**Answer:** $\eta_{otto} \approx 58.48\%$

> [!NOTE]
> Real SI engines achieve only 25–30% efficiency due to mechanical, heat, and pumping losses. The Otto cycle is a *theoretical upper bound*.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a 4-Stroke SI Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Air Intake Filter] --> B[Carburettor / Fuel Injector]
    B --> C[Throttle Valve]
    C --> D[Intake Manifold]
    D --> E[Intake Valve OPEN]
    E --> F[Cylinder with Piston]
    F --> G[Spark Plug Fires at TDC]
    G --> H[Combustion and Expansion]
    H --> I[Piston Pushes Crankshaft]
    I --> J[Flywheel Stores Energy]
    J --> K[Exhaust Valve OPEN]
    K --> L[Exhaust Manifold]
    L --> M[Muffler and Tailpipe]
    M --> N[Atmosphere]
    F --> O[Water Jacket Cooling]
    O --> P[Radiator and Coolant Pump]
    P --> F
    I --> Q[Timing Chain / Belt]
    Q --> R[Camshaft]
    R --> E
    R --> K
```

## 4.2 Otto Cycle — p-V and T-S Schematic Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph pV_Diagram["P-V DIAGRAM (OTTO)"]
        pV1["Point 1: BDC, low P high V"]:::stateNode
        pV2["Point 2: TDC, high P low V"]:::stateNode
        pV3["Point 3: TDC, very high P low V"]:::stateNode
        pV4["Point 4: BDC, medium P high V"]:::stateNode
        pV1 --Isentropic Compression 1 to 2--> pV2
        pV2 --Constant V Heat Addition 2 to 3--> pV3
        pV3 --Isentropic Expansion 3 to 4--> pV4
        pV4 --Constant V Heat Rejection 4 to 1--> pV1
    end
    subgraph TS_Diagram["T-S DIAGRAM (OTTO)"]
        ts1["Point 1: Low T Low S"]:::stateNode
        ts2["Point 2: High T Mid S"]:::stateNode
        ts3["Point 3: Very High T High S"]:::stateNode
        ts4["Point 4: Mid T Highest S"]:::stateNode
        ts1 --Compression 1 to 2--> ts2
        ts2 --Heat Added 2 to 3--> ts3
        ts3 --Expansion 3 to 4--> ts4
        ts4 --Heat Rejected 4 to 1--> ts1
    end
    classDef stateNode fill:#e8f4f8,stroke:#2b6cb0,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Process Flow: 4-Stroke SI Engine — Stroke Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Engine Crankshaft Rotates]) --> s1{Stroke 1: Intake}
    s1 -->|Intake Valve Open| intake[Fresh Air-Fuel Mixture Enters Cylinder]
    intake -->|Piston TDC to BDC| s2{Stroke 2: Compression}
    s2 -->|Both Valves Closed| compress[Mixture Compressed to TDC]
    compress -->|Spark Plug Fires| ignition[Combustion and High Pressure]
    ignition --> s3{Stroke 3: Power}
    s3 -->|Piston TDC to BDC| power[Hot Gases Expand and Drive Piston]
    power --> s4{Stroke 4: Exhaust}
    s4 -->|Exhaust Valve Open| exhaust[Burnt Gases Expelled to Atmosphere]
    exhaust --> s1
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block Architecture: SI vs CI Working Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SI["SI ENGINE - OTTO CYCLE"]
        SI1[Intake: Air + Fuel Mixed]
        SI2[Compression of Mixture]
        SI3[Spark Ignition at TDC]
        SI4[Power: Constant Volume Combustion]
        SI1 --> SI2 --> SI3 --> SI4
    end
    subgraph CI["CI ENGINE - DIESEL CYCLE"]
        CI1[Intake: Only Air]
        CI2[Compression of Air Only]
        CI3[Fuel Injection Near TDC]
        CI4[Auto-Ignition + Constant Pressure Combustion]
        CI1 --> CI2 --> CI3 --> CI4
    end
    style SI fill:#fff7e6,stroke:#b7791f
    style CI fill:#e6fffa,stroke:#2c7a7b
```

## 4.5 Combustion Process Schematic in SI Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ignition Lag Phase: 0 to 1 ms] --> B[Flame Kernel Formation]
    B --> C[Flame Propagation Across Chamber]
    C --> D[Peak Pressure and Post Combustion]
    D --> E[Burnt Gas Expansion Phase]
    A:::phaseNode
    B:::phaseNode
    C:::phaseNode
    D:::phaseNode
    E:::phaseNode
    classDef phaseNode fill:#fef5e7,stroke:#af601a,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU students must be able to draw a **labeled p-V diagram of Otto and Diesel cycles** by hand in exams. Always label: 1-2 (compression), 2-3 (heat addition), 3-4 (expansion), 4-1 (heat rejection), with $\gamma$, $r$, $\rho$ values where applicable.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Q:** Differentiate between SI and CI engines based on ignition method, compression ratio, and fuel used.

**Model Answer (3 marks — allocate 1 mark per point):**

| Basis | SI Engine | CI Engine |
|---|---|---|
| **Ignition Method** | Spark plug generates a high-voltage spark to ignite the air-fuel mixture near TDC. | The heat generated by compressing air alone raises its temperature above the auto-ignition temperature of diesel; fuel is injected and self-ignites. |
| **Compression Ratio** | Low, between **8 and 12**, limited by knocking tendency of petrol. | High, between **14 and 22**, limited by mechanical strength of engine parts. |
| **Fuel Used** | Volatile fuels like petrol, ethanol, LPG, CNG. | Less volatile fuels like diesel, biodiesel, heavy oils. |

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Q:** What is the difference between the Otto cycle and the Diesel cycle?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. The **Otto cycle** represents the ideal working of an SI engine in which heat is added at **constant volume**. The Diesel cycle represents the ideal working of a CI engine in which heat is added at **constant pressure**. (1.5 marks)
2. In the Otto cycle, the **compression ratio is limited to ≈ 10** due to knock, and the thermal efficiency depends **only on $r$**. In the Diesel cycle, the compression ratio is high (≈ 18), but efficiency depends on **both $r$ and the cut-off ratio $\rho$**. (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> KTU ESE Rule: A 14-mark question typically has 2 sub-parts: (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the air-standard efficiency of the Otto cycle and explain why the compression ratio in SI engines is limited. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-stroke SI engine has a compression ratio of 8.5 and operates on the air-standard Otto cycle. If $\gamma = 1.4$, calculate:
- (i) Air-standard thermal efficiency
- (ii) If the indicated power is 50 kW and brake power is 45 kW, find the mechanical efficiency and brake thermal efficiency given CV = 44,000 kJ/kg and fuel consumption = 12 kg/hr. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation of Otto Efficiency:**

(For detailed derivation, see **Section 3.1** above. Students are expected to present the 4-process cycle and reach the final equation.)

**Key Steps to Write in Exam (7 marks distribution):**
- (2 marks) State air-standard assumptions + describe 4 processes clearly with a p-V diagram.
- (3 marks) Derive $T_2 = T_1 r^{\gamma-1}$, $T_4 = T_3 / r^{\gamma-1}$.
- (1 mark) Substitute into $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$ to reach final boxed result $\eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$.
- (1 mark) Explain why compression ratio is limited: high $r$ raises end-of-compression temperature $T_2$, causing **auto-ignition (knock)** before the spark fires, leading to power loss and engine damage. Petrol's auto-ignition temperature (≈ 250°C) is easily reached at $r \approx 12$.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $r = 8.5$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $IP = 50$ kW, $BP = 45$ kW, $\dot{m}_f = 12$ kg/hr, $CV = 44{,}000$ kJ/kg.

**(i) Air-standard thermal efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{8.5^{0.4}}
$$

Compute $8.5^{0.4}$:

$$
8.5^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 8.5} = e^{0.4 \times 2.1401} = e^{0.8560} \approx 2.3534
$$

$$
\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.3534} = 1 - 0.4249 = 0.5751
$$

**Answer:** $\eta_{otto} \approx 57.51\%$ (3 marks)

**(ii) Mechanical Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{IP} = \frac{45}{50} = 0.90 = 90\%
$$

**Answer:** $\eta_{mech} = 90\%$ (1 mark)

**Brake Thermal Efficiency:**

Convert fuel flow to kg/s:

$$
\dot{m}_f = \frac{12}{3600} = 0.00333 \text{ kg/s}
$$

Heat input rate:

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times CV = 0.00333 \times 44{,}000 = 146.67 \text{ kW}
$$

Brake thermal efficiency:

$$
\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \frac{45}{146.67} = 0.3068
$$

**Answer:** $\eta_{bth} \approx 30.68\%$ (3 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat p-V and T-S diagram, explain the working of a 4-stroke CI engine. Compare it with a 4-stroke SI engine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-stroke CI engine working on the Diesel cycle has a compression ratio of 18 and a cut-off ratio of 1.6. Take $\gamma = 1.4$. Calculate:
- (i) Air-standard efficiency
- (ii) Compare it with an Otto cycle having the same compression ratio and comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — 4-Stroke CI Engine Description (7 marks):**

**Working of 4-Stroke CI Engine (3 marks):**

- **Suction Stroke (0° to 180°):** Intake valve opens; **only air** is drawn into the cylinder (no fuel mixing). Piston moves from TDC to BDC.
- **Compression Stroke (180° to 360°):** Both valves close. Air is compressed to a very high pressure and temperature. By the time piston reaches TDC, the air temperature exceeds the ignition temperature of diesel (≈ 210–230 °C).
- **Power Stroke (360° to 540°):** Fuel injector sprays finely atomized diesel into the hot compressed air just before TDC. The fuel **auto-ignites** due to the high temperature, and combustion continues at approximately constant pressure as fuel injection continues for a brief crank angle. The high-pressure gases push the piston from TDC to BDC.
- **Exhaust Stroke (540° to 720°):** Exhaust valve opens; burnt gases are expelled. Piston moves from BDC to TDC.

**p-V Diagram (1.5 marks) + T-S Diagram (1 mark):** Draw labeled 4-point diagram (1, 2, 3, 4) with constant-pressure heat addition.

**Comparison with 4-Stroke SI Engine (1.5 marks):**

| Basis | SI Engine | CI Engine |
|---|---|---|
| Fuel inducted | Air + Fuel | Only Air |
| Ignition | Spark Plug | Auto-ignition |
| Combustion Type | Constant Volume | Constant Pressure |
| Compression Ratio | 8–12 | 14–22 |
| Thermal Efficiency | Lower | Higher |

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $r = 18$, $\rho = 1.6$, $\gamma = 1.4$.

**(i) Diesel cycle efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \frac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma (\rho - 1)}
$$

Compute $r^{\gamma - 1} = 18^{0.4}$:

$$
18^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 18} = e^{0.4 \times 2.8904} = e^{1.1562} \approx 3.177
$$

Compute $\rho^\gamma = 1.6^{1.4}$:

$$
1.6^{1.4} = e^{1.4 \ln 1.6} = e^{1.4 \times 0.4700} = e^{0.658} \approx 1.931
$$

Now:

$$
\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{3.177} \cdot \frac{1.931 - 1}{1.4 \times (1.6 - 1)} = 1 - \frac{0.3148}{3.177} \cdot \frac{0.931}{0.84}
$$

$$
= 1 - 0.0991 \times 1.1083 = 1 - 0.1098 = 0.8902
$$

Hmm, that seems high. Let me recompute carefully:

$$
\frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} = \frac{1}{3.177} = 0.3148
$$

$$
\frac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)} = \frac{1.931 - 1}{1.4 \times 0.6} = \frac{0.931}{0.84} = 1.1083
$$

Product: $0.3148 \times 1.1083 = 0.3489$

$$
\eta_{diesel} = 1 - 0.3489 = 0.6511
$$

**Answer:** $\eta_{diesel} \approx 65.11\%$ (3 marks)

**(ii) Otto cycle efficiency at same $r$:**

$$
\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{3.177} = 1 - 0.3148 = 0.6852
$$

**Answer:** $\eta_{otto} \approx 68.52\%$ (2 marks)

**Comment (2 marks):** At the *same* compression ratio, the Otto cycle gives higher efficiency ($\approx 68.5\%$) than the Diesel cycle ($\approx 65.1\%$) because the constant-volume heat addition process is thermodynamically more efficient. However, in practice, CI engines operate at much higher $r$ than SI engines (since they are not knock-limited), so their *actual* efficiency exceeds that of SI engines.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong compression ratio notation:** Always $r = V_1/V_2$ where $V_1 > V_2$. Confusing these gives negative efficiency in logs — instant 2-mark penalty.
> 2. **Confusing $\rho$ with $r$:** Cut-off ratio $\rho = V_3/V_2$ applies ONLY to the Diesel cycle. Otto cycle does NOT have a $\rho$ — students who write $r = V_3/V_2$ in Otto derivations lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting $\gamma$ in Diesel cycle:** The factor $\gamma$ in the denominator of $\dfrac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ is frequently missed. This alone can give an answer that is 40% wrong.
> 4. **Units of fuel flow:** KTU numericals always give $\dot{m}_f$ in kg/hr but formulas use kg/s. Always divide by 3600.
> 5. **Drawing p-V diagrams without labels:** Examiners give 0.5–1 free mark for a clean, labeled diagram. Omit it at your own risk.
> 6. **Confusing 2-stroke vs 4-stroke constants:** In $IP$ formula, $K = 1$ for 4-stroke and $K = 2$ for 2-stroke. Many students hardcode $K = 1$ without reading the question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 3: Reciprocating SI and CI Engines)**

- [x] An **IC engine** burns fuel *inside* the cylinder; reciprocating means a piston moves up and down.
- [x] **SI** = Spark Ignition (petrol); **CI** = Compression Ignition (diesel).
- [x] SI engines use a **spark plug**; CI engines use **auto-ignition** by high compression heat.
- [x] SI compression ratio: **8–12**; CI compression ratio: **14–22**.
- [x] 4-stroke engine = **720° per cycle**; 2-stroke engine = **360° per cycle**.
- [x] **Otto cycle** = constant volume heat addition (theoretical basis of SI).
- [x] **Diesel cycle** = constant pressure heat addition (theoretical basis of CI).
- [x] **Otto efficiency:** $\eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$.
- [x] **Diesel efficiency:** $\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$.
- [x] For same $r$: Otto > Diesel; in practice, CI wins because it uses much higher $r$.
- [x] **SI throttle** controls load via mixture quantity; **CI** has no throttle (load controlled by fuel injection quantity).
- [x] **SI knock** = premature auto-ignition due to high $T_2$ → limits $r$.
- [x] **CI noise** = high-pressure combustion "diesel knock" (different mechanism from SI knock).
- [x] **IP** uses $P_{mi}$ and crank speed; **BP** uses torque and crank speed.
- [x] **Mechanical efficiency:** $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP} = \dfrac{\eta_{bth}}{\eta_{ith}}$.
- [x] **SFC** = fuel consumed per unit brake power output (kg/kWh).
- [x] **Air-standard efficiency** is a *theoretical upper bound*; real engines are 50–60% of this value.
- [x] Always convert fuel flow from kg/hr to kg/s before substitution.
- [x] Always draw and label the p-V and T-S diagrams — it's free marks.
- [x] Memorize the SI vs CI comparison table — it's a guaranteed 7-mark question in KTU.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ideal and Actual Cycle for IC Engines - Deviation from Ideal Cycle and Associated Factors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ideal and Actual Cycle for IC Engines - Deviation from Ideal Cycle and Associated Factors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Internal Combustion (IC) engines, the **Ideal Cycle** (also called the **Air Standard Cycle**) is a theoretical thermodynamic cycle in which an ideal gas (assumed to behave as a perfect gas with constant specific heats) undergoes idealized processes to convert heat into work. The actual working fluid is replaced by air, and all combustion-related complexities are abstracted into instantaneous heat addition.

The **Actual Cycle** represents the real thermodynamic processes occurring inside a working engine, where the working fluid is a complex mixture of air, fuel vapor, and combustion products, and the processes are subject to mechanical, thermal, fluid dynamic, and chemical irreversibilities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PBMET604 - Module 3):**
> The *Ideal Cycle* is an idealized thermodynamic model that simplifies the analysis of reciprocating IC engines. It assumes:
> (a) The working fluid is a **fixed mass of ideal gas** (air) with constant specific heats.
> (b) All processes are **internally reversible** (no friction, no turbulence losses).
> (c) Heat addition and rejection occur at **constant volume or constant pressure** (depending on cycle type).
> (d) Compression and expansion are **isentropic** (adiabatic + reversible).

The deviation between the ideal and actual cycle arises because, in reality, an IC engine experiences finite time for combustion, heat transfer to cylinder walls, mechanical friction, blowdown losses, incomplete combustion, variation of specific heats with temperature, and many other irreversibilities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examinations:** The ideal cycle provides an **upper bound** on the performance of an actual engine. The deviation from this ideal represents the cumulative effect of *real-world losses*, and the quantification of these deviations is critical for engine design and performance evaluation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **baking a cake using a perfect recipe** (the *ideal cycle*). The recipe states: preheat oven to exactly **180 °C**, bake for **exactly 30 minutes**, use ingredients at exactly **room temperature**, and assume no heat escapes the oven walls.

In reality (the *actual cycle*):
- The oven temperature **fluctuates** around 180 °C (loss due to heat transfer to surroundings).
- The baking time is **not perfectly uniform** because of door-opening and imperfect heat distribution.
- The ingredients have **variable moisture content** and are not at the same starting temperature.
- The cake **cools down** when you take it out (heat rejection is not instantaneous).
- Some heat is **lost** to the oven walls and surrounding air.

Similarly, in an actual IC engine, the combustion process is not instantaneous, heat escapes through the cylinder walls, friction dissipates mechanical energy, and the fuel-air mixture does not behave as a perfect gas. **The ideal cycle is the perfect recipe; the actual cycle is the cake you actually bake** — it is *good*, but *not perfect*. The deviation is what we call **cycle losses** or **deviation factors**.

> [!TIP]
> A useful way to remember: **The ideal cycle is a "paper engine"** — it exists only on paper for analytical convenience. The actual cycle is the **"metal engine"** that you can hear, feel, and smell in a real workshop.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard values are universally accepted in KTU 2024 scheme thermal engineering analysis:

- **Ratio of specific heats for air:** $\gamma = 1.4$ (at standard conditions, $C_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $C_v = 0.718 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$)
- **Gas constant for air:** $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_0 = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
- **Standard atmospheric temperature:** $T_0 = 288.15 \text{ K}$
- **Mechanical equivalent of heat:** $J = 4.186 \text{ J/cal}$ (calorie-based problems)
- **Isentropic process relation:** $PV^\gamma = \text{constant}$, $TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{constant}$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board examinations, students are expected to use $\gamma = 1.4$ unless the problem statement specifically mentions variable specific heats or provides an alternate value. Always state the assumption clearly at the beginning of your solution to score the first easy mark.

## 1.4 The Four Classic Air Standard Cycles

The KTU 2024 syllabus (PBMET604 Module 3) emphasizes the following four ideal cycles, each corresponding to a class of real engines:

| Ideal Cycle | Real Engine | Heat Addition | Heat Rejection |
|-------------|-------------|---------------|----------------|
| **Otto Cycle** | Spark Ignition (SI) Petrol Engine | Constant Volume | Constant Volume |
| **Diesel Cycle** | Compression Ignition (CI) Diesel Engine | Constant Pressure | Constant Volume |
| **Dual (Limited Pressure) Cycle** | Modern High-Speed CI Engine | Constant Volume + Constant Pressure | Constant Volume |
| **Brayton (Joule) Cycle** | Gas Turbine Engine | Constant Pressure | Constant Pressure |

Each of these cycles approximates the *real* processes in a different manner, and each has its own characteristic deviation from the corresponding **actual cycle**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions of the Air Standard Cycle

For the analysis of any ideal cycle, the following **six foundational assumptions** are made. These are the *axioms* upon which the entire theoretical framework rests:

1. **Working fluid is air** (a fixed mass, treated as a perfect gas), and it behaves as an ideal gas throughout the cycle. The products of combustion are replaced by an equivalent mass of air.
2. **Specific heats ($C_p$ and $C_v$) remain constant** during the entire cycle. This is the *cold air standard assumption*.
3. **All processes are internally reversible** (no internal irreversibilities such as friction, turbulence, or sudden pressure drops).
4. **Heat addition and heat rejection occur from external sources/sinks** at constant volume or constant pressure, depending on the cycle.
5. **Compression and expansion processes are isentropic** (reversible adiabatic). The polytropic index equals $\gamma$.
6. **No change in the chemical composition of the working fluid** during the cycle. The fluid is treated as a single-component system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These assumptions convert a *complex, time-dependent, non-equilibrium real cycle* into a *simple, equilibrium, closed-system ideal cycle* that can be analyzed using the First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics.

## 2.2 Why the Actual Cycle Deviates from the Ideal Cycle

The deviation is rooted in the breakdown of each of the above assumptions in a real engine. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly categorizes these into **five major loss groups**:

### 2.2.1 Mechanical Losses
- **Friction between piston and cylinder walls** (especially at top dead center where sliding velocity is zero and oil film is thin).
- **Friction in bearings** (main bearings, connecting rod bearings, camshaft).
- **Windage losses** (the piston whipping through air-oil mist inside the crankcase).
- **Drive losses** for auxiliary systems: cooling fan, water pump, oil pump, alternator, distributor, power steering pump (in automotive engines).

> [!CAUTION]
> Mechanical losses are not *cycle losses* in the strict thermodynamic sense, but they reduce the **brake (output) power** of the engine, thereby reducing the *brake thermal efficiency* $\eta_{bt}$ compared to the *indicated thermal efficiency* $\eta_{it}$ which is closer to the ideal.

### 2.2.2 Heat Transfer Losses (Thermal Losses)
- **Heat loss to the cylinder walls** during combustion and expansion. The high-temperature gases transfer heat to the relatively cooler water-cooled (or air-cooled) cylinder head, liner, and piston crown.
- **Heat loss during compression** from the residual gases and from the cylinder walls back to the fresh charge.
- **Incomplete heat transfer** during the very short time available for combustion (typically 10° to 40° of crank angle).

> [!NOTE]
> This is one of the **most significant deviations** in real engines. The ideal cycle assumes **adiabatic** processes, but in reality, every process has finite heat transfer across the cylinder boundary.

### 2.2.3 Combustion-Related Losses
- **Incomplete combustion:** Not all fuel is oxidized. Some carbon becomes CO instead of CO₂; some hydrogen becomes H₂O and unburned hydrocarbons leave through the exhaust.
- **Time loss (combustion duration):** Combustion is not instantaneous. It occurs over 30°–60° of crank rotation, while the ideal cycle assumes it occurs at a single instant (constant volume or constant pressure).
- **Dissociation of combustion products** at high temperatures: CO₂ → CO + ½O₂, H₂O → H₂ + ½O₂. This absorbs energy that would otherwise be available for work.
- **Variations in specific heats** with temperature and composition: $C_p$ and $C_v$ increase significantly above 1000 K. The constant-$\gamma$ assumption breaks down.

### 2.2.4 Gas Exchange (Blowdown) Losses
- **Exhaust blowdown loss:** When the exhaust valve opens, the high-pressure cylinder contents rush out to the low-pressure exhaust manifold, doing no useful work.
- **Gas pumping loss:** The fresh charge must be pushed into the cylinder against exhaust gas back pressure.
- **Valve timing losses:** Early or late intake/exhaust valve opening and closing deviate from the ideal 180° (half-cycle) gas exchange assumed in the ideal cycle.
- **Residual gas fraction:** A small fraction of exhaust gas remains in the clearance volume and dilutes the fresh charge, reducing the effective compression ratio and altering combustion.

### 2.2.5 Fluid Dynamic and Process Irreversibilities
- **Turbulence and throttling losses** in the intake manifold, valve ports, and combustion chamber.
- **Non-isentropic compression and expansion:** The actual polytropic index $n$ is **less than $\gamma$** for compression (typically 1.32–1.38) and expansion (typically 1.25–1.32), because heat transfer to the walls makes the process less adiabatic.
- **Leakage past piston rings, valves, and gaskets.**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the key formulas used to quantify the **deviation** between actual and ideal cycles. These are the equations a KTU 2024 student must memorize for the End Semester Examination (ESE).

| # | Quantity | Ideal Formula | Actual / Real-World Note |
|---|----------|---------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Otto cycle efficiency | $\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ | $r$ = compression ratio; valid for ideal gas, constant $\gamma$ |
| 2 | Diesel cycle efficiency | $\eta_{Diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \left[ \dfrac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)} \right]$ | $\rho$ = cut-off ratio; correction for constant pressure heat addition |
| 3 | Dual cycle efficiency | $\eta_{Dual} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\beta \gamma^\gamma - 1}{(\beta - 1) + \gamma \beta (\rho - 1)}$ | $\beta$ = pressure ratio, $\rho$ = cut-off ratio |
| 4 | Brayton cycle efficiency | $\eta_{Brayton} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ | $r_p$ = pressure ratio |
| 5 | Air standard efficiency (general) | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$ | All temperatures in **Kelvin** (mandatory) |
| 6 | Isentropic process | $T_2 / T_1 = (V_1 / V_2)^{\gamma - 1} = (P_2 / P_1)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$ | Used for processes 1-2 and 3-4 |
| 7 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP} = \dfrac{\eta_{bt}}{\eta_{it}}$ | $BP$ = brake power, $IP$ = indicated power |
| 8 | Relative efficiency | $\eta_{rel} = \dfrac{\eta_{it,actual}}{\eta_{air,standard}}$ | Quantifies deviation between actual and ideal |
| 9 | Overall (brake) efficiency | $\eta_{bt} = \eta_{rel} \times \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{air}$ | Cascade product of all three efficiencies |
| 10 | Polytropic process (actual) | $PV^n = \text{const}$, where $n < \gamma$ | Replaces isentropic in actual cycle |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Pattern Tip:** When asked to "compare the actual and ideal cycle," examiners expect a **quantitative expression of the deviation**, such as the **relative efficiency $\eta_{rel}$** which is typically **0.50 to 0.85** for a well-tuned production engine. Always present your final answer in **percentage (%)** form, e.g., $\eta_{rel} = 65\%$, to be safe.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The analysis of deviation from the ideal cycle is **not merely an academic exercise**. It has profound practical utility:

- **Engine calibration and tuning:** Knowing which losses dominate (heat transfer vs friction vs incomplete combustion) allows engineers to target specific design changes (e.g., lower compression ratio, better insulation, advanced ignition timing).
- **Emissions control:** The same deviations (incomplete combustion, dissociation, residual gases) are the *root cause* of emissions such as CO, unburned hydrocarbons (UHC), and NOx. Modern catalytic converters, EGR systems, and direct injection strategies are direct responses to these deviations.
- **Materials selection:** Heat transfer losses and high peak temperatures drive the choice of materials for pistons (forged aluminum, cast iron), cylinder heads (aluminum alloys), and valves (Inconel, sodium-cooled exhaust valves).
- **Computational fluid dynamics (CFD) validation:** The air standard cycle serves as the **baseline** against which sophisticated 3D CFD and chemical kinetics simulations are validated.
- **Hybrid and electric vehicle transition:** A clear understanding of cycle losses shows engineers the *theoretical maximum* extractable from internal combustion, motivating the search for alternatives such as hybrid powertrains and fuel cells.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **exhaustive mathematical derivations** that KTU 2024 examiners frequently test. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly so that a student can reproduce it in the examination hall for full marks.

## 3.1 Derivation: Air Standard Efficiency of the Otto Cycle (Theoretical Foundation)

The **Otto cycle** is the ideal cycle for the SI (Spark Ignition) petrol engine. It consists of four internally reversible processes:
- **Process 1-2:** Isentropic compression
- **Process 2-3:** Constant volume heat addition
- **Process 3-4:** Isentropic expansion
- **Process 4-1:** Constant volume heat rejection

**Step 1 — State the heat transfers using the First Law for a closed system.**

For each constant-volume process, the heat transfer equals the change in internal energy (no work done at constant volume).

$$Q_{in} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2)$$

$$Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$$

This is the heat absorbed during combustion (process 2-3) and the heat rejected during exhaust (process 4-1), respectively.

**Step 2 — Apply the isentropic process relations to eliminate the intermediate temperatures.**

For the isentropic compression process 1-2, the ideal gas relation gives:

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}$$

where $r = V_1 / V_2$ is the **compression ratio** (a dimensionless quantity, always $> 1$).

For the isentropic expansion process 3-4, with the same compression ratio (since $V_4 / V_3 = V_1 / V_2 = r$):

$$T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{r}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = \frac{T_3}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute these temperature relations into the efficiency equation.**

The thermal efficiency is defined as the net work output divided by the heat input, which equals one minus the ratio of heat rejected to heat added.

$$\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$$

Substituting the expressions derived in Step 1:

$$\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{m C_v (T_4 - T_1)}{m C_v (T_3 - T_2)} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$

**Step 4 — Factor out the common temperature terms to obtain the compact closed-form expression.**

Factor $T_1$ from the numerator and $T_2$ from the denominator:

$$\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{T_1 \left(\frac{T_4}{T_1} - 1\right)}{T_2 \left(\frac{T_3}{T_2} - 1\right)}$$

Now substitute the isentropic relations: $T_3 / T_2 = (T_3 / T_1) \cdot (T_1 / T_2)$. Using $T_2 / T_1 = r^{\gamma - 1}$ and $T_4 / T_3 = (1/r)^{\gamma - 1}$, we get $T_4 / T_1 = T_3 / T_1 \cdot (1/r)^{\gamma - 1}$. Combining and simplifying:

$$\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{T_1}{T_2} \cdot \frac{(T_4 / T_3) - 1}{(T_3 / T_2) - 1} \cdot \frac{T_3}{T_2} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$$

**Step 5 — Final Result (boxed for KTU valuation).**

$$\boxed{\eta_{Otto, \text{ideal}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** The Otto cycle efficiency depends **only on the compression ratio $r$** and the **ratio of specific heats $\gamma$**, and is **independent of the heat added or the load**. This is why a higher compression ratio always improves efficiency, subject to the knock limit in SI engines.

## 3.2 Derivation: Diesel Cycle Efficiency

The **Diesel cycle** has constant pressure heat addition (instead of constant volume). Let $r$ be the compression ratio and $\rho$ be the **cut-off ratio** $\rho = V_3 / V_4$ (which represents the volume ratio during the constant pressure heat addition phase).

**Step 1 — Heat transfers at constant pressure and constant volume.**

$$Q_{in} = m C_p (T_3 - T_2) \quad \text{(constant pressure heat addition)}$$

$$Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1) \quad \text{(constant volume heat rejection)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the isentropic relations.**

For the isentropic compression 1-2: $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}$.

For the isentropic expansion 3-4: Using $V_4 / V_3 = \rho$ and the relation $V_4 = V_1 / r$ and $V_3 = V_2 \cdot \rho = V_1 \cdot \rho / r$, we obtain:

$$T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{V_3}{V_4}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\rho}\right)^{\gamma - 1} \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}$$

Wait — let us re-derive carefully. $V_3 / V_4 = \rho$, and the expansion ratio from 3 to 4 is $V_4 / V_3 = 1/\rho$. So:

$$T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\rho}\right)^{\gamma - 1}$$

This is correct. But we also need to express $T_3$ in terms of $T_2$ using the constant pressure process 2-3: $V_3 / V_2 = T_3 / T_2$ (since $P$ is constant), so:

$$T_3 = T_2 \cdot \frac{V_3}{V_2} = T_2 \cdot \rho \cdot \frac{r}{r} = T_2 \cdot \rho$$

**Step 3 — Combine and substitute into the efficiency formula.**

$$\eta_{Diesel} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{C_v (T_4 - T_1)}{C_p (T_3 - T_2)}$$

Using $C_p / C_v = \gamma$ and the temperature relations:

$$T_4 - T_1 = \frac{T_3}{\rho^{\gamma - 1}} - T_1 = \frac{T_2 \cdot \rho}{\rho^{\gamma - 1}} - \frac{T_2}{r^{\gamma - 1}} = T_2 \left( \rho^{2 - \gamma} - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \right)$$

$$T_3 - T_2 = T_2 \rho - T_2 = T_2 (\rho - 1)$$

After algebraic simplification (multiplying numerator and denominator by appropriate factors and using $\gamma = C_p / C_v$):

$$\eta_{Diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \frac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma (\rho - 1)}$$

**Step 4 — Final Result.**

$$\boxed{\eta_{Diesel, \text{ideal}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \left[ \frac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma (\rho - 1)} \right]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The bracketed term is always **greater than 1** for any finite $\rho > 1$, so the Diesel cycle efficiency is **always less than the Otto cycle efficiency** at the same compression ratio. However, the Diesel cycle can operate at *higher* $r$ (since there is no knock), which more than compensates in practice.

## 3.3 Derivation: Actual Cycle Polytropic Deviation

The most commonly asked derivation in the KTU 2024 ESE is the **polytropic compression and expansion** used to model the actual cycle. The ideal isentropic process assumes $PV^\gamma = \text{const}$; the actual process uses $PV^n = \text{const}$ where $1 < n < \gamma$.

**Step 1 — Start with the ideal-gas relation for the compression process.**

$$P_1 V_1 = m R T_1, \quad P_2 V_2 = m R T_2$$

**Step 2 — Use the polytropic relation $P V^n = \text{const}$.**

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(n - 1)/n} = \left( \frac{V_1}{V_2} \right)^{n - 1}$$

For an **ideal (isentropic)** process, $n = \gamma$, giving $T_{2,\text{ideal}} / T_1 = r^{\gamma - 1}$.

For an **actual (polytropic)** process, $n < \gamma$, giving $T_{2,\text{actual}} / T_1 = r^{n - 1}$.

**Step 3 — Quantify the deviation in compression temperature.**

$$\Delta T_{compression} = T_{2,\text{ideal}} - T_{2,\text{actual}} = T_1 \left( r^{\gamma - 1} - r^{n - 1} \right)$$

Since $n < \gamma$, we have $r^{n - 1} < r^{\gamma - 1}$, so $\Delta T > 0$. This means **the actual compression reaches a lower temperature than ideal**, which is intuitively correct: the heat loss to the walls prevents the gas from reaching the ideal isentropic temperature.

**Step 4 — Convert to efficiency deviation.**

The relative efficiency of compression alone is:

$$\eta_{rel,\text{compression}} = \frac{r^{n - 1}}{r^{\gamma - 1}} = r^{(n - \gamma)} = \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - n}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Mistake:** Students often write $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^n$ instead of $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{n-1}$. The correct exponent is $n - 1$, not $n$. Always re-derive from $TV^{n-1} = \text{const}$ to be sure.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Losses to Physical Phenomena

The following table links each ideal-cycle assumption to its corresponding real-world deviation, the resulting **loss mechanism**, and the **approximate magnitude** in a modern well-tuned engine.

| Assumption in Ideal Cycle | Real-World Deviation | Loss Mechanism | Approximate Magnitude |
|---------------------------|----------------------|----------------|----------------------|
| Isentropic compression ($n = \gamma$) | Heat loss to cylinder walls | Reduced compression temperature and pressure | $\eta_{rel} \approx 0.95$ |
| Instantaneous heat addition at constant V | Finite combustion duration; pressure rise is gradual | Lost work due to non-instantaneous pressure rise | $\eta_{rel} \approx 0.90$ |
| Isentropic expansion ($n = \gamma$) | Heat loss to walls; blowdown | Reduced expansion work; exhaust blowdown | $\eta_{rel} \approx 0.85$ |
| Constant-volume heat rejection | Open cycle; exhaust at atmospheric pressure | Lost work of expansion below atmospheric | $\eta_{rel} \approx 0.80$ |
| Working fluid = air, constant $C_p, C_v$ | Variable specific heats; dissociation | Reduced available energy at high $T$ | $\eta_{rel} \approx 0.95$ |
| Complete combustion | Incomplete combustion; CO formation | Energy remains in unburned fuel | $\eta_{rel} \approx 0.97$ |
| No mechanical friction | Bearing, piston, valve train friction | Reduced brake power | $\eta_{mech} \approx 0.85$ |
| No auxiliary drives | Fan, pump, alternator losses | Additional power consumption | $\eta_{aux} \approx 0.90$ |
| **Overall (cascade product)** | — | — | $\eta_{bt} \approx 0.20 - 0.35$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Insight:** When asked "Why is the actual efficiency less than the ideal efficiency?", the model answer is to list **at least four** of the above loss mechanisms with brief explanations, then present the **overall (brake) efficiency** as a cascade product: $\eta_{bt} = \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{rel} \times \eta_{air}$. This three-part decomposition is a favorite KTU examiner pattern.

## 3.5 Worked Example: Quantitative Deviation Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
An SI engine operates on the Otto cycle with a compression ratio of $r = 8$ and develops an actual indicated thermal efficiency of $\eta_{it,actual} = 50\%$. The air standard efficiency of the Otto cycle is $\eta_{Otto} = 56.5\%$ (computed using $\gamma = 1.4$). Calculate the **relative efficiency** and identify the major loss factors.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the air standard efficiency using the Otto formula.**

$$\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{8^{0.4}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.297} = 1 - 0.4353 = 0.5647 \approx 56.47\%$$

**Step 2 — Compute the relative efficiency (the quantitative measure of deviation).**

$$\eta_{rel} = \frac{\eta_{it,actual}}{\eta_{Otto}} = \frac{0.50}{0.5647} = 0.8855 \approx 88.55\%$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the deviation.**

The actual indicated efficiency is **88.55%** of the ideal. The **11.45% deficit** is attributed to:
- Finite combustion duration (~3–4%)
- Heat loss to walls (~3–4%)
- Incomplete combustion (~2%)
- Variable specific heats (~2%)

**Step 4 — If the mechanical efficiency is $\eta_{mech} = 0.85$, compute the brake thermal efficiency.**

$$\eta_{bt} = \eta_{it,actual} \times \eta_{mech} = 0.50 \times 0.85 = 0.425 = 42.5\%$$

Alternatively, using the cascade formula:

$$\eta_{bt} = \eta_{Otto} \times \eta_{rel} \times \eta_{mech} = 0.5647 \times 0.8855 \times 0.85 = 0.425$$

Both methods agree, confirming the calculation.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Ideal vs Actual Cycle Process Mapping

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **process-by-process mapping** between the ideal Otto cycle and the actual cycle in a real SI engine. This is a KTU 2024 high-yield diagram for visualization questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fresh Charge<br/>Intake Stroke] --> B[Valve Closure<br/>Intake Valve Closes]
    B --> C{Ideal vs Actual<br/>Comparison}
    C --> D1[Ideal Process 1 to 2<br/>Isentropic Compression<br/>PV raised to gamma equals const]
    C --> D2[Actual Process 1 to 2<br/>Polytropic Compression<br/>PV raised to n equals const<br/>n equals 1.32 to 1.38]
    D1 --> E1[Ideal State 2<br/>High T and High P]
    D2 --> E2[Actual State 2<br/>Lower T and Lower P than Ideal]
    E1 --> F1[Ideal Process 2 to 3<br/>Constant Volume Heat Addition<br/>Q in from Combustion]
    E2 --> F2[Actual Process 2 to 3<br/>Finite Duration Combustion<br/>30 to 60 deg Crank Angle<br/>Heat Loss to Walls]
    F1 --> G1[Ideal State 3<br/>Peak T and Peak P]
    F2 --> G2[Actual State 3<br/>Lower Peak T and Peak P]
    G1 --> H1[Ideal Process 3 to 4<br/>Isentropic Expansion<br/>Work Output on Piston]
    G2 --> H2[Actual Process 3 to 4<br/>Polytropic Expansion<br/>n equals 1.25 to 1.32<br/>Heat Loss and Blowdown]
    H1 --> I1[Ideal State 4<br/>Low T and Low P]
    H2 --> I2[Actual State 4<br/>Higher T and Higher P than Ideal]
    I1 --> J1[Ideal Process 4 to 1<br/>Constant Volume Heat Rejection]
    I2 --> J2[Actual Process 4 to 1<br/>Blowdown and Exhaust<br/>Open Cycle]
    J1 --> K1[Cycle Closes<br/>Net Work W net equals Q in minus Q out]
    J2 --> K2[Net Indicated Work<br/>Reduced due to all Losses]
    K1 --> L1[Ideal Efficiency equals 1 minus 1 over r raised to gamma minus 1]
    K2 --> L2[Actual Efficiency equals Relative Eff times Ideal Eff times Mech Eff]
    L1 --> M[Quantified Deviation<br/>Losses due to Heat, Friction, Combustion, Gas Exchange]
    L2 --> M
```

## 4.2 P-V and T-S Diagram Comparison

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **schematic comparison** of the P-V diagram between the ideal and actual cycles. (Note: A true P-V diagram requires curved isentropes; the Mermaid block below is a structural functional architecture flow representing the comparative geometry, suitable for KTU diagram-based questions.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IDEAL_CYCLE[Ideal Otto Cycle on P-V Diagram]
        A1[State 1: BDC Low P Low V] -->|Isentropic Compression| A2[State 2: TDC High P Low V]
        A2 -->|Constant Volume Heat In| A3[State 3: TDC Higher P Same V]
        A3 -->|Isentropic Expansion| A4[State 4: BDC Lower P High V]
        A4 -->|Constant Volume Heat Out| A1
    end
    subgraph ACTUAL_CYCLE[Actual SI Engine Cycle on P-V Diagram]
        B1[State 1: Intake Closes] -->|Polytropic Compression n equals 1.35| B2[State 2: Ignition Point]
        B2 -->|Finite Duration Heat Release| B3[State 3: Peak Pressure]
        B3 -->|Polytropic Expansion n equals 1.28| B4[State 4: Blowdown Begins]
        B4 -->|Exhaust Valve Opens Pressure Drops| B5[Exhaust Stroke at Atmospheric P]
    end
    ACTUAL_CYCLE -.->|Deviation Sources| IDEAL_CYCLE
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of Loss Cascade

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **cascade of efficiency losses** as energy flows from the chemical energy of fuel to useful mechanical work at the crankshaft.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Chemical Energy of Fuel Q fuel] --> Q1[Combustion Losses<br/>Incomplete Combustion<br/>Dissociation]
    Q1 --> Q2[Heat Transfer Losses<br/>To Cylinder Walls<br/>To Coolant and Oil]
    Q2 --> Q3[Indicated Energy Available<br/>Equal to Air Standard Q in times Relative Eff]
    Q3 --> Q4[Mechanical Losses<br/>Friction in Bearings<br/>Piston Rings and Valves]
    Q4 --> Q5[Brake Energy at Crankshaft<br/>Useful Output Work]
    Q5 --> Q6[Driven Accessories<br/>Cooling Fan, Water Pump<br/>Alternator, Oil Pump]
    Q6 --> R[Net Useful Work at Wheels<br/>After Transmission Losses]
    P --> S1[Step 1: Chemical to Thermal]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2: Thermal to Mechanical]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Mechanical to Useful]
    S3 --> R
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Diagram Strategy:** The Mermaid block diagrams above are formatted as **functional architecture flows** because physical P-V or T-S diagrams with curved isentropes cannot be rendered in Mermaid syntax. In the KTU examination, students are expected to draw the *actual* P-V and T-S diagrams on graph paper, with curved isentropes and clear state-point labels (1, 2, 3, 4). The Mermaid diagrams above serve as a *conceptual bridge* between the mathematical model and the physical drawing.
