# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any four factors responsible for the deviation of the actual cycle from the ideal air standard cycle in an IC engine.**

**Model Answer (4 points, ~3 marks):**

The following four factors cause the actual cycle to deviate from the ideal air standard cycle:

1. **Heat loss to cylinder walls:** The ideal cycle assumes adiabatic compression and expansion, but in reality, heat is transferred from the hot gases to the relatively cooler cylinder walls, piston crown, and cylinder head. This reduces the peak temperature and the work output.

2. **Finite duration of combustion:** The ideal cycle assumes instantaneous heat addition at constant volume (Otto) or constant pressure (Diesel). In a real engine, combustion takes 30°–60° of crank angle, during which heat is added gradually and the piston is moving, deviating from the idealized model.

3. **Incomplete combustion and dissociation:** Not all fuel is fully oxidized. Some energy remains locked in carbon monoxide (CO) and unburned hydrocarbons (UHC). At high temperatures, CO₂ and H₂O also dissociate into simpler species, absorbing energy that would otherwise be available for work.

4. **Variable specific heats:** The ideal cycle assumes constant $C_p$ and $C_v$. In reality, both increase significantly with temperature, which lowers the efficiency and alters the pressure and temperature profiles.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write only 2–3 points and lose a mark. Examiners expect **exactly four** points for a 3-mark Part A question, each with a one-line explanation. Avoid one-word answers like "heat loss" without a brief explanatory clause.

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define "Relative Efficiency" of an IC engine. What is its typical range for a modern petrol engine?**

**Model Answer:**

The **relative efficiency** (also called the *efficiency ratio*) of an IC engine is defined as the ratio of the actual indicated thermal efficiency to the air standard (ideal) thermal efficiency of the corresponding cycle.

$$\eta_{rel} = \frac{\eta_{it,actual}}{\eta_{air,standard}}$$

**Typical range for a modern petrol (SI) engine:** **0.50 to 0.85 (50% to 85%)**, depending on engine design, operating speed, load, and the extent of cycle losses. A well-tuned modern SI engine typically achieves $\eta_{rel} \approx 0.70 - 0.80$, while a poorly maintained or highly supercharged engine may fall to 0.50–0.60.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** A complete answer must include the **formula**, the **physical meaning** (it is a measure of how closely the actual cycle approaches the ideal), and the **numerical range** with units (%).

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

**Q: (a)** Derive the air standard efficiency of the Otto cycle, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An actual SI engine operates on the Otto cycle with a compression ratio of 9:1. The actual indicated thermal efficiency is 52%, and the mechanical efficiency is 80%. Calculate: (i) the air standard (ideal) Otto cycle efficiency, (ii) the relative efficiency, (iii) the brake thermal efficiency, and (iv) the percentage deviation from the ideal cycle. Take $\gamma = 1.4$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Assumptions (1.5 marks):
1. The working fluid is a fixed mass of air, behaving as a perfect gas with constant specific heats $C_p$ and $C_v$.
2. All processes are internally reversible.
3. Compression (1-2) and expansion (3-4) are isentropic.
4. Heat addition (2-3) and heat rejection (4-1) occur at constant volume.

Heat transfer expressions (2 marks):
$$Q_{in} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2), \quad Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$$

Isentropic relations (2 marks):
$$T_2 = T_1 r^{\gamma - 1}, \quad T_4 = T_3 / r^{\gamma - 1}$$

Final efficiency formula (1.5 marks):
$$\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $r = 9$, $\eta_{it,actual} = 0.52$, $\eta_{mech} = 0.80$, $\gamma = 1.4$

**(i) Air standard (ideal) Otto cycle efficiency (2 marks):**

$$\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{9^{0.4}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.408} = 1 - 0.4153 = 0.5847 = 58.47\%$$

**(ii) Relative efficiency (1.5 marks):**

$$\eta_{rel} = \frac{\eta_{it,actual}}{\eta_{Otto}} = \frac{0.52}{0.5847} = 0.8894 = 88.94\%$$

**(iii) Brake thermal efficiency (1.5 marks):**

$$\eta_{bt} = \eta_{it,actual} \times \eta_{mech} = 0.52 \times 0.80 = 0.416 = 41.6\%$$

**(iv) Percentage deviation from the ideal (2 marks):**

$$\text{Deviation} = \eta_{Otto} - \eta_{it,actual} = 0.5847 - 0.52 = 0.0647 = 6.47\%$$

Alternatively, in terms of relative efficiency: $1 - \eta_{rel} = 1 - 0.8894 = 0.1106 = 11.06\%$ (this measures the deviation from ideal in *relative* terms).

**Final answers:**
- $\eta_{Otto} = 58.47\%$
- $\eta_{rel} = 88.94\%$
- $\eta_{bt} = 41.6\%$
- Absolute deviation = $6.47\%$; Relative deviation = $11.06\%$

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 1]`

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat P-V diagram, the air standard Otto cycle. Discuss the major factors responsible for the deviation of the actual cycle from the ideal Otto cycle in a spark ignition engine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A four-cylinder, four-stroke SI engine has a compression ratio of 8.5:1 and develops a brake power of 60 kW at 3000 rpm. The fuel consumption is 18 kg/hr with a calorific value of 44 MJ/kg. Calculate: (i) the brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC), (ii) the brake thermal efficiency, and (iii) the relative efficiency, given that the air standard efficiency (using $\gamma = 1.4$) and the mechanical efficiency are 54.5% and 85% respectively. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Explanation with P-V diagram and deviation factors (7 marks):**

The **Otto cycle** consists of four internally reversible processes:
- 1-2: Isentropic compression
- 2-3: Constant volume heat addition (combustion)
- 3-4: Isentropic expansion (power stroke)
- 4-1: Constant volume heat rejection

[A neat P-V diagram with state points 1, 2, 3, 4 marked, with the isentropes drawn as curves $PV^\gamma = \text{const}$ and the constant volume processes as vertical lines at $V_c$ (clearance) and $V_1$ (BDC) — this should be drawn on graph paper in the actual exam.]

**Major deviation factors (5 points, ~5 marks):**

1. **Heat loss to walls** during combustion and expansion — reduces peak temperature and work output.
2. **Finite combustion duration** — heat is added over 30°–60° crank angle, not instantaneously.
3. **Incomplete combustion and dissociation** — energy locked in CO and UHC; CO₂ dissociates at high T.
4. **Blowdown and pumping losses** — exhaust blowdown is wasted work; intake throttling consumes work.
5. **Mechanical friction** — piston rings, bearings, valve train — reduces brake power.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $N = 4$ cylinders, 4-stroke engine, $r = 8.5$, $BP = 60$ kW, $n = 3000$ rpm, $\dot{m}_f = 18$ kg/hr, $CV = 44$ MJ/kg, $\eta_{Otto} = 0.545$, $\eta_{mech} = 0.85$

**(i) Brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC) (2 marks):**

$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} = \frac{18 \text{ kg/hr}}{60 \text{ kW}} = 0.30 \text{ kg/kWh}$$

**(ii) Brake thermal efficiency (2 marks):**

$$\eta_{bt} = \frac{BP \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times CV} = \frac{60 \times 3600}{18 \times 44000} = \frac{216000}{792000} = 0.2727 = 27.27\%$$

**(iii) Relative efficiency (3 marks):**

We need $\eta_{it,actual}$, which from $\eta_{bt} = \eta_{it,actual} \times \eta_{mech}$:

$$\eta_{it,actual} = \frac{\eta_{bt}}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{0.2727}{0.85} = 0.3208 = 32.08\%$$

$$\eta_{rel} = \frac{\eta_{it,actual}}{\eta_{Otto}} = \frac{0.3208}{0.545} = 0.5886 = 58.86\%$$

**Final answers:**
- $BSFC = 0.30 \text{ kg/kWh}$
- $\eta_{bt} = 27.27\%$
- $\eta_{rel} = 58.86\%$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit consistency is critical:** Energy units must be consistent. The KTU board strictly deducts 0.5 to 1 mark if you mix kW with kJ/s, or kg/hr with kg/s. **Always convert $\dot{m}_f$ from kg/hr to kg/s** by dividing by 3600 *before* computing the efficiency, or use the $3600$ factor explicitly in the numerator of $\eta_{bt}$.
> 2. **Engine cycle selection:** For a 4-stroke engine, the working cycle is completed in **2 revolutions (720°)**. Do **not** apply the half-power correction (divide by 2) unless the problem specifically states it is for a 2-stroke engine.
> 3. **Relative efficiency formula:** It is $\eta_{it,actual} / \eta_{Otto}$, **not** $\eta_{bt} / \eta_{Otto}$. The mechanical efficiency factor is *not* part of the relative efficiency calculation. Confusing these will cost 1.5–2 marks.
> 4. **Compression ratio definition:** $r = V_1 / V_c$ (BDC volume to clearance volume), **not** $V_c / V_1$. This is the most common single-point deduction across all KTU papers.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

The following high-density bullet list serves as a **rapid-revision checklist** for the last hour before the KTU 2024 ESE examination. Memorize these points and you will be able to answer any question on this topic confidently.

### Core Concepts to Remember

- **Air standard cycle** = ideal cycle; assumes fixed mass of air with constant $C_p$ and $C_v$ as the working fluid.
- **Actual cycle** = the real cycle in a working engine, characterized by finite-time combustion, heat transfer, friction, and gas exchange losses.
- **The ideal cycle efficiency is an upper bound** on the actual efficiency — the difference is the cumulative effect of all deviations.

### The Six Assumptions of the Air Standard Cycle

1. Working fluid is air (ideal gas).
2. Constant specific heats ($C_p$, $C_v$).
3. Internally reversible processes.
4. Heat addition/rejection at constant V or constant P.
5. Isentropic compression and expansion ($PV^\gamma = \text{const}$).
6. Single-component working fluid (no change in chemical composition).

### Key Efficiency Definitions (Must Memorize)

- $\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$
- $\eta_{Diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$
- $\eta_{rel} = \dfrac{\eta_{it,actual}}{\eta_{air,standard}}$
- $\eta_{bt} = \eta_{it} \times \eta_{mech}$
- $\eta_{bt} = \eta_{Otto} \times \eta_{rel} \times \eta_{mech}$ (cascade product)

### Five Major Deviation Categories (KTU Board Loves This)

1. **Mechanical losses** (friction, accessories)
2. **Heat transfer losses** (to cylinder walls)
3. **Combustion losses** (incomplete, dissociation, finite duration)
4. **Gas exchange losses** (blowdown, pumping, residual gases)
5. **Fluid dynamic / process irreversibilities** (polytropic index $n < \gamma$)

### Numerical Pitfalls to Avoid

- Use $\gamma = 1.4$ for air unless stated otherwise.
- Compression ratio is **always** $V_1 / V_2$ (BDC to TDC volume ratio).
- Temperatures in efficiency formulas **must be in Kelvin** — convert °C to K by adding 273.15.
- Cut-off ratio $\rho$ is $V_3 / V_4$ (for Diesel and Dual cycles).
- Polytropic process: $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{n-1}$, not $r^n$.

### Final Formulas to Print on Your Cheat Sheet

- $BSFC = \dot{m}_f / BP$ (in kg/kWh)
- $\eta_{bt} = BP / (\dot{m}_f \times CV)$ (use 3600 s/hr conversion)
- $\eta_{mech} = BP / IP$
- $\eta_{vol} = \text{actual intake air} / \text{ideal intake air (at ambient P and T)}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When a question asks "Compare the actual and ideal cycle," structure your answer in **three layers**: (1) Define both cycles; (2) List at least **four** deviation factors with one-line justifications; (3) Present the **quantitative relationship** $\eta_{bt} = \eta_{Otto} \times \eta_{rel} \times \eta_{mech}$. This three-layer structure is the most reliable way to score full marks (14/14) on a KTU ESE question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supercharging and Turbocharging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Supercharging and Turbocharging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Supercharging** is the process of increasing the **mass of charge** (air or air-fuel mixture) inducted into the cylinder of an internal combustion engine by raising the pressure of the charge above the atmospheric pressure at the inlet manifold, thereby increasing the **volumetric efficiency** and consequently the power output per unit displacement of the engine.

**Turbocharging** is a specific, energy-efficient form of supercharging in which the compressor is driven by a **turbine** that extracts energy from the **exhaust gases** of the engine, instead of being mechanically driven by the engine crankshaft.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - **Supercharging** = General term (any forced induction).
> - **Turbocharging** = Subset of supercharging, specifically driven by an **exhaust gas turbine** (energy recovery).
> - A **mechanically driven** supercharger is sometimes called a **blower** (e.g., Roots blower, centrifugal blower).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a person trying to fill a room (engine cylinder) with fresh air (charge) through a small window (atmospheric intake valve), but the window only stays open for a brief moment (valve open duration). Due to the natural restriction, the room never gets fully filled before the window closes — only about **60–80%** of the air gets in (this is the *volumetric efficiency deficit*).

**Supercharging is like fitting an electric fan outside the window that blows extra air in faster**, ensuring the room is packed full (or even over-packed) by the time the window closes. The more air you pack, the more fuel you can burn, and the more powerful the "burst" (combustion) becomes.

**Turbocharging takes it one step further**: instead of using electricity to power the fan, it cleverly **harnesses the wind that is rushing OUT of the room (exhaust gases) to spin the fan**, recycling wasted energy. It's like using the exhaust breeze from a chimney to turn a turbine that powers a blower.

---

## 1.3 Why Supercharge? — The Engineering Drivers

| Driver | Physical Effect | Metric Improved |
|---|---|---|
| More charge per cycle | Increased mass of air → more fuel burned | **Power Output** |
| Pressure > atmospheric | Compression of intake charge | **Volumetric Efficiency > 100%** |
| Recovery of exhaust energy | Turbine extracts waste enthalpy | **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)** |
| Altitude compensation | Counteracts low atmospheric pressure | **Specific Power** at altitude |
| Reduced pumping loss | Lower intake manifold vacuum at part load | **Mechanical Efficiency** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** In a naturally aspirated (NA) engine, the volumetric efficiency ($\eta_v$) cannot exceed **100%**. In a supercharged engine, $\eta_v$ can be **120–150%** (or higher), with values **> 100%** being the signature of forced induction.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Atmospheric pressure ($p_{atm}$):** $101.325\ \text{kPa}$ = **1.01325 bar**
- **Standard ambient temperature ($T_0$):** **288.15 K** (15°C)
- **Gas constant for air ($R$):** **0.287 kJ/(kg·K)**
- **Specific heat ratio for air ($\gamma$):** **1.4**
- **Boost pressure ($p_{boost}$):** absolute pressure in the intake manifold above $p_{atm}$
- **Pressure ratio ($r_p$):** $r_p = \dfrac{p_2}{p_1}$, typically **1.2 to 3.5** for automotive supercharging

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Supercharging Methods

A. **According to Energy Source for the Compressor:**

1. **Mechanical Supercharging (Blower-driven):** Compressor coupled to the crankshaft via gears, chains, or belts. Examples: **Roots blower**, **centrifugal blower**, **vane-type compressor**.
2. **Exhaust Gas Turbocharging:** Compressor driven by an exhaust-driven turbine. Sub-classified as:
   - **Pulse Turbocharging** (uses exhaust pulse energy)
   - **Constant Pressure Turbocharging** (uses steady exhaust manifold pressure)
3. **Compound / Combined Systems:** Both mechanical + turbo (e.g., **twincharger** arrangements on marine diesel engines).
4. **Electrically Assisted Turbocharging (E-Turbo):** 48V motor aiding the turbine to eliminate lag — modern trend, not in KTU 2024 core syllabus.

---

## 2.2 Types of Superchargers — Operational Comparison

### (a) Roots Supercharger (Positive Displacement)
- **Working principle:** Two figure-eight-shaped rotors trap air and push it into the intake manifold.
- **Characteristic:** Delivers a *fixed volume* per revolution; pressure ratio depends on back-pressure.
- **Drive:** Always mechanical; cannot be exhaust-driven (would melt due to heating).
- **Use case:** Heavy-duty diesel (e.g., GM 6-71 Roots blower), drag racing.

### (b) Centrifugal Supercharger (Impeller-type)
- **Working principle:** Air enters axially at the impeller eye, is flung outward by centrifugal force into a volute/diffuser, gaining pressure.
- **Characteristic:** Pressure rise is proportional to $(\text{impeller speed})^2$.
- **Drive:** Mechanical OR exhaust-driven (turbo).
- **Use case:** Most passenger-car turbochargers (BMW, Audi, Mercedes), large marine diesels (MAN B&W turbochargers).

### (c) Vane-type Compressor
- An eccentric rotor with sliding vanes; used in industrial applications.

---

## 2.3 Effect of Supercharging on Engine Performance Parameters

Let the unsupercharged engine have:
- $p_1 = p_{atm} = 1.013$ bar, $T_1 = T_{atm}$, swept volume $V_s$, speed $N$.

After supercharging to pressure $p_2$ (with charging efficiency $\eta_c$):
- Mass of charge per cycle increases from $m_1$ to $m_2$.
- **Volumetric efficiency can now exceed 100%** because the charge is denser.
- The **mean effective pressure** scales linearly with the pressure ratio (for an ideal cycle).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_v = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{\rho_{atm} \cdot V_s}$ | Volumetric efficiency (NA: $\eta_v \leq 1$; Supercharged: $\eta_v > 1$) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $r_p = \dfrac{p_2}{p_1}$ | Pressure ratio across compressor | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $W_{comp,\,isen} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} \cdot p_1 V_1 \left[ \left(\dfrac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]$ | Work of isentropic compression | kJ/kg or kJ/cycle |
| 4 | $W_{comp,\,isen} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} \cdot R (T_2 - T_1)$ | Equivalent per-unit-mass form | kJ/kg |
| 5 | $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\frac{n-1}{n}}$ | Discharge temperature (isentropic) | K |
| 6 | $\eta_c = \dfrac{W_{isen}}{W_{actual}}$ | Compressor (isentropic) efficiency | Dimensionless, 0.6–0.85 |
| 7 | $\eta_m = \dfrac{BP}{IP}$ | Mechanical efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $P_{comp} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1)}{\eta_m \cdot \eta_c}$ | Power required to drive supercharger | kW |
| 9 | $BP \propto p_m \cdot N \cdot k$ | Brake Power scaling with mean effective pressure | kW |
| 10 | $p_{m,super} = p_{m,NA} \cdot \eta_v^{super}$ | Supercharged MEP relationship | bar |
| 11 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | Otto cycle efficiency (unchanged by supercharging) | Dimensionless |
| 12 | $\rho_{boost} = \dfrac{p_2}{R \cdot T_2}$ | Density of supercharged charge | kg/m³ |
| 13 | $\dot{m}_{air} = \dfrac{BP \cdot BSFC}{LHV \cdot AFR}$ | Air mass flow rate | kg/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Insight:** Supercharging increases the **mass** of air, NOT the **thermal efficiency** of the ideal Otto cycle. The air-standard efficiency $\eta_{th} = 1 - r^{1-\gamma}$ depends only on **compression ratio ($r$)**, not on boost pressure. The actual BSFC improves only because of turbocharger energy recovery and reduced throttling losses.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Aircraft piston engines at altitude lose ~30% power per 5 km climb — turbochargers restore sea-level rated power (e.g., Cessna TIO-540).
- **Diesel locomotives & marine:** 2-stroke MAN B&W engines run with turbocharging; one turbocharger per cylinder (TCA) or two-stage turbocharging.
- **Formula 1:** Turbo-Hybrid V6 (1.6L) with MGU-H and MGU-K — 1000+ HP.
- **Commercial vehicles:** BS-VI trucks rely on VGT (Variable Geometry Turbocharger) for low-end torque.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Work Required for Isentropic Compression in a Supercharger

We start with the first law for a steady-flow open system (control volume) with negligible kinetic and potential energy changes.

For an **ideal gas** undergoing a **quasi-static (reversible) adiabatic (isentropic)** compression from state 1 (inlet) to state 2 (discharge):

$$pV^n = \text{constant}, \quad n = \gamma = 1.4$$

**Step 1:** Specific work for a steady-flow compressor (steady-flow energy equation, SFEE):

$$w = h_2 - h_1 = c_p (T_2 - T_1)$$

**Step 2:** For an ideal gas with constant specific heats:

$$c_p = \dfrac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}$$

**Step 3:** From the isentropic relation:

$$T_2 = T_1 \left( \dfrac{p_2}{p_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute Step 3 into Step 1:

$$w = c_p T_1 \left[ \left( \dfrac{p_2}{p_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $c_p = \dfrac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}$:

$$w = \dfrac{\gamma R T_1}{\gamma - 1} \left[ \left( \dfrac{p_2}{p_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]$$

**Step 6:** Use ideal gas law: $R T_1 = p_1 v_1$, where $v_1$ is the specific volume (m³/kg). Multiply by mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ to get the **total power** in kW:

$$\boxed{\ \dot{W}_{comp,isen} = \dot{m} \cdot \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot R T_1 \left[ r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right] \ }$$

where $r_p = p_2 / p_1$ is the pressure ratio.

**Step 7:** In terms of *total* (non-specific) flow $p_1 V_1$:

$$W_{comp,isen} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} \cdot p_1 V_1 \left[ \left(\dfrac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1 \right]$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effect of Supercharging on Mean Effective Pressure (MEP)

For a 4-stroke engine at speed $N$ (rpm), with $k$ cylinders each of swept volume $V_s$:

**Step 1:** Indicated Power (IP) is related to the **indicated mean effective pressure** $p_{mi}$:

$$IP = \dfrac{p_{mi} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot k}{60} \quad \text{(4-stroke: with 2 in denominator)}, \quad IP = \dfrac{p_{mi} \cdot V_s \cdot N \cdot k}{2 \times 60}$$

**Step 2:** The **mass of charge inducted per cycle** for a naturally aspirated (NA) engine:

$$m_{NA} = \eta_v \cdot \rho_{atm} \cdot V_s$$

**Step 3:** For a supercharged engine at manifold pressure $p_2$ and temperature $T_2$ (with charging efficiency $\eta_c$ accounting for heat addition during compression):

$$m_{super} = \eta_c \cdot \dfrac{p_2}{R T_2} \cdot V_s$$

**Step 4:** The energy released per cycle is proportional to the mass of fuel burned, which is proportional to the mass of air. Therefore:

$$\dfrac{IP_{super}}{IP_{NA}} = \dfrac{m_{super}}{m_{NA}} = \dfrac{\eta_c \cdot p_2 \cdot T_{atm}}{\eta_v \cdot p_{atm} \cdot T_2}$$

**Step 5:** Since $p_{mi} \propto IP / (N \cdot V_s)$:

$$\boxed{\ \dfrac{p_{mi,\,super}}{p_{mi,\,NA}} = \dfrac{\eta_c}{\eta_v} \cdot \dfrac{p_2}{p_{atm}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{atm}}{T_2} \ }$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $T_2 / T_{atm} = r_p^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$ (from isentropic relation in Step 3 of Derivation 1):

$$\dfrac{p_{mi,\,super}}{p_{mi,\,NA}} = \dfrac{\eta_c}{\eta_v} \cdot r_p \cdot r_p^{-\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} = \dfrac{\eta_c}{\eta_v} \cdot r_p^{\frac{1}{\gamma}}$$

This shows the **MEP** (and hence IP) scales approximately linearly with the pressure ratio $r_p$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Saved by Turbocharging vs. Mechanical Supercharging

**Given:** Engine brake power $BP$, BSFC = $b$ (kg/kWh), mechanical supercharger absorbs $P_{mech}$ from crankshaft; turbocharger absorbs **zero** mechanical power (energy from exhaust is "free" — it was going to be wasted).

**Step 1:** For a mechanically supercharged engine, the **net brake power** is:

$$BP_{mech} = IP - P_{mech} = IP - \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1)}{\eta_c \cdot \eta_{mech,driveline}}$$

**Step 2:** For a turbocharged engine:

$$BP_{turbo} = IP - P_{aux} \approx IP \quad \text{(since turbine drives compressor, not the crankshaft)}$$

**Step 3:** The power that the mechanical supercharger consumes is the **power saved** by switching to turbocharging:

$$\Delta P = P_{mech} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1)}{\eta_c \cdot \eta_{m,driveline}}$$

**Step 4:** In terms of useful output: if the mechanical supercharger consumed 8–15% of $IP$ (typical), the same turbocharged engine will deliver that 8–15% as **extra brake power** at the wheels, with **no fuel penalty** — a net **win–win**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Mechanical vs. Turbocharging

| Parameter | Mechanical Supercharger | Turbocharger |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Engine crankshaft (parasitic) | Exhaust gas (waste heat recovery) |
| Compressor type | Roots / Centrifugal | Centrifugal (always) |
| Typical efficiency | 50–70% (compressor) | 60–80% (turbine + compressor combined) |
| Lag (response time) | **Instantaneous** (direct drive) | **Turbo lag** (0.5–2.5 s) |
| Boost ceiling | Limited by drive power & heating | Limited by exhaust energy & back pressure |
| Altitude performance | Loses power with altitude | Partially self-compensating (less exhaust → less boost → some loss) |
| Mechanical complexity | Belts, gears, lubrication | High — bearings, lubrication, heat shields |
| Cost | Moderate | High |
| Heat addition to charge | Significant (→ intercooler required) | Significant (intercooler required) |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Standard Pattern)

**Problem:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine running at 3000 rpm has a volumetric efficiency of 80% and MEP of 8 bar in naturally aspirated form. The engine is supercharged to a pressure ratio of 1.8 with a charging efficiency of 0.85. The supercharged air temperature is 350 K and atmospheric is 300 K. Calculate the percentage increase in IP.

**Given:**
- $N = 3000$ rpm, $k = 4$, 4-stroke
- $\eta_v = 0.80$ (NA)
- $p_{mi, NA} = 8\ \text{bar}$
- $r_p = 1.8$
- $\eta_c = 0.85$ (charging efficiency)
- $T_2 = 350$ K, $T_{atm} = 300$ K
- $p_{atm} = 1.013\ \text{bar}$ (assumed)

**Step 1:** Use the ratio derived in Section 3.2, Step 5:

$$\dfrac{p_{mi, super}}{p_{mi, NA}} = \dfrac{\eta_c}{\eta_v} \cdot \dfrac{p_2}{p_{atm}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{atm}}{T_2}$$

**Step 2:** Compute pressure ratio numerically:

$$\dfrac{p_2}{p_{atm}} = r_p = 1.8$$

**Step 3:** Compute temperature ratio:

$$\dfrac{T_{atm}}{T_2} = \dfrac{300}{350} = 0.8571$$

**Step 4:** Combine:

$$\dfrac{p_{mi, super}}{p_{mi, NA}} = \dfrac{0.85}{0.80} \times 1.8 \times 0.8571 = 1.0625 \times 1.8 \times 0.8571$$

**Step 5:** Multiply stepwise:

$$= 1.0625 \times 1.8 = 1.9125$$

$$1.9125 \times 0.8571 = 1.6392$$

**Step 6:** Therefore:

$$p_{mi, super} = 1.6392 \times 8 = 13.11\ \text{bar}$$

**Step 7:** Percentage increase in IP (since $IP \propto p_{mi}$ at constant $N$ and $V_s$):

$$\Delta IP\,\% = (1.6392 - 1) \times 100\% = 63.92\%$$

**Answer:** The indicated power increases by approximately **63.9%** with supercharging. (KTU examiners may accept 64%.)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Supercharged Engine Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Atmospheric Air] --> B[Air Filter]
    B --> C[Compressor / Blower]
    H[Exhaust Manifold] --> I[Exhaust Turbine]
    I --> J[Exhaust to Atmosphere]
    I -- Turbine Shaft Power --> C
    C -- Pressurized Charge --> D[Intercooler / Aftercooler]
    D --> E[Intake Manifold]
    E --> F[Engine Cylinder]
    F -- Combustion Power --> G[Crankshaft IP and BP]
    F --> H
    K[Cooling Circuit] --> D
    D -- Rejected Heat --> K

    classDef atmos fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0284c7,color:#000
    classDef comp fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#dc2626,color:#000
    classDef eng fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,color:#000
    classDef exh fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706,color:#000
    classDef cool fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#000

    class A,B atmos
    class C,I comp
    class D,K cool
    class E,F,G eng
    class H,J exh
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Atmospheric air** is drawn in (left, blue) and pressurized by the **compressor** (red).
- The compressor is driven by the **exhaust turbine** (right, yellow) — this is the **turbocharging** loop.
- Hot compressed air is **cooled in the intercooler** (purple) before entering the manifold, since compression raises the temperature (which would reduce density and promote knock).
- The **engine cylinder** (green) burns the dense charge, producing IP and BP at the crankshaft, while exhausting high-energy gases back to the turbine.
- The dashed coupling `I -- Turbine Shaft Power --> C` represents the **single common shaft** coupling turbine and compressor — defining a turbocharger.

---

## 4.2 Functional Comparison — Types of Supercharging

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Supercharging Methods]
    S --> M1[Mechanical Supercharger]
    S --> T1[Turbocharger]

    M1 --> R1[Roots Blower - Positive Displacement]
    M1 --> R2[Centrifugal Blower - Impeller]
    M1 --> R3[Vane Type Compressor]

    T1 --> P1[Pulse Turbocharging - Uses Exhaust Pulse Energy]
    T1 --> P2[Constant Pressure Turbocharging - Plenum Accumulator]

    P1 --> P1A[High Efficiency at Low RPM]
    P1 --> P1B[Turbine Piping Per Cylinder or Small Group]

    P2 --> P2A[Smooth Boost Pressure]
    P2 --> P2B[Large Plenum Chamber]

    classDef root fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    classDef turb fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#047857,color:#000

    class S,M1,T1 root
    class R1,R2,R3,P1,P2 turb
    class P1A,P1B,P2A,P2B leaf
```

---

## 4.3 Comparative Flow — Pulse vs. Constant-Pressure Turbocharging

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Pulse[Pulse Turbocharging - Marine and Automotive]
        E1[Exhaust Manifold] -- Short Runners --> T1A[Turbine Scroll Inlet]
        T1A -- Expand --> T1B[Exhaust Turbine]
        T1B -- Drive Shaft --> C1A[Compressor]
        C1A -- Boosted Air --> IM1[Intake Manifold]
    end

    subgraph Constant[Constant Pressure Turbocharging]
        E2[Exhaust Manifold] -- Common Plenum --> T2A[Large Plenum Chamber]
        T2A -- Smooth Steady Flow --> T2B[Exhaust Turbine]
        T2B -- Drive Shaft --> C2A[Compressor]
        C2A -- Boosted Air --> IM2[Intake Manifold]
    end

    classDef pulse fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#ca8a04,color:#000
    classDef const fill:#cffafe,stroke:#0e7490,color:#000

    class E1,T1A,T1B,C1A,IM1 pulse
    class E2,T2A,T2B,C2A,IM2 const
```

**Engineering Note:** Pulse systems are favored in **large 2-stroke marine diesels** (MAN B&W) due to their superior scavenging. Constant-pressure systems are simpler and are used in **4-stroke automotive** engines where a single large exhaust manifold acts as the plenum.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]
**Q: Define supercharging. How does it differ from turbocharging?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Supercharging** is the process of supplying air (or air-fuel mixture) to the engine cylinder at a pressure higher than the ambient atmospheric pressure, so as to increase the **mass of charge** inducted and thereby enhance the power output per unit displacement (3 marks).

> **Key distinction:** In **supercharging** (general), the compressor may be driven by any means — commonly mechanical (crankshaft), which represents a **parasitic load**. In **turbocharging** specifically, the compressor is driven by an **exhaust-driven turbine**, recovering waste exhaust energy and hence not imposing a direct mechanical load on the crankshaft. Thus, *every turbocharger is a supercharger, but not every supercharger is a turbocharger.*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Q: State any three advantages of supercharging an internal combustion engine.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Increased power output** for the same engine displacement — typically **30–80%** increase in BP achievable for a pressure ratio of 1.5–2.0.
2. **Improved volumetric efficiency** — can exceed **100%**, since charge density at the inlet is higher than ambient.
3. **Altitude compensation** — at high altitudes, naturally aspirated engines lose power; supercharging restores the rated sea-level power.
4. (Bonus third point) **Reduced specific fuel consumption** in turbocharged applications due to energy recovery from exhaust gases.
5. (Bonus third point) **Better part-load efficiency** due to reduced throttling/pumping losses.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Dec 2024 Model]

**Q: With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a turbocharger. Derive an expression for the work required to compress the charge isentropically and explain how supercharging affects the mean effective pressure of an engine.**

#### Part (a) — Working Principle of a Turbocharger (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

A **turbocharger** consists of two main components mounted on a common shaft: a **radial-flow exhaust gas turbine** and a **radial-flow centrifugal compressor**.

1. **Exhaust Entry (3 marks):** Hot, high-pressure exhaust gases from the engine cylinders enter the turbine scroll, where they expand over the turbine blades, converting thermal and pressure energy into **rotational kinetic energy** of the shaft. The exhaust turbine extracts this energy at an isentropic efficiency of 60–80%.
2. **Compressor Operation (3 marks):** The same shaft drives the centrifugal compressor, which draws in filtered ambient air, accelerates it through the impeller, and decelerates it in the diffuser, raising its static pressure by 0.2–1.0 bar (boost).
3. **Charge Delivery (1 mark):** The pressurized air passes through an **intercooler** (to remove heat of compression, improving density and avoiding knock) and enters the intake manifold at elevated pressure $p_2 > p_{atm}$.

> A neat **labeled block diagram** of the turbocharger with [Atmospheric Air → Filter → Compressor → Intercooler → Manifold → Engine → Exhaust Manifold → Turbine → Atmosphere] is expected, along with indication of the common shaft between turbine and compressor.

#### Part (b) — Derivation of Isentropic Compression Work (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

For a steady-flow control volume of the compressor, applying the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** with negligible changes in kinetic and potential energy and adiabatic (no heat transfer) assumption:

$$W_{comp} = m \cdot (h_2 - h_1) = m \cdot c_p (T_2 - T_1)$$

For an isentropic process in an ideal gas with $\gamma = c_p / c_v$:

$$\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left( \dfrac{p_2}{p_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} = r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$$

Substituting $c_p = \dfrac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}$:

$$W_{comp,isen} = m \cdot \dfrac{\gamma R T_1}{\gamma - 1} \left[ r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]$$

In terms of the total inlet flow quantity $p_1 V_1 = m R T_1$:

$$\boxed{\ W_{comp,isen} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot p_1 V_1 \left[ \left( \dfrac{p_2}{p_1} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right] \ }$$

**Effect on Mean Effective Pressure (3 marks continuation):**

The MEP ratio is:

$$\dfrac{p_{mi,super}}{p_{mi,NA}} = \dfrac{\eta_c}{\eta_v} \cdot \dfrac{p_2}{p_{atm}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{atm}}{T_2}$$

Hence, with rising pressure ratio, the **MEP rises nearly linearly**, leading to proportionate increase in IP and BP for the same engine size and speed. (7 marks total)

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / June 2024 Model]

**Q: Compare mechanical supercharging and turbocharging in detail. With a suitable diagram, explain the working of a Roots blower. Discuss the phenomenon of turbo lag and methods to reduce it.**

#### Part (a) — Comparison + Roots Blower Working (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Tabular Comparison (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Mechanical Supercharger | Turbocharger |
|---|---|---|
| Drive | Crankshaft (gears/belts) | Exhaust turbine |
| Energy source | Useful fuel energy | Waste exhaust energy |
| Parasitic loss | Yes (8–15% of IP) | Negligible |
| Response | Instantaneous | Turbo lag present |
| Boost limit | Drive power limit | Exhaust energy limit |
| Thermal loading | High | High |
| Cost | Moderate | High |

**Roots Blower Working (3 marks):**

- A **Roots blower** consists of two **figure-eight-shaped (lobe) rotors** rotating in opposite directions inside a closely-fitting housing.
- The rotors are **synchronized by timing gears** so they do not contact each other or the housing.
- As the rotors turn, air is **trapped between the rotor lobes and the casing**, and is **displaced** from the inlet side to the outlet side without internal compression — i.e., it is a **positive-displacement** device.
- The outlet check valve or back-pressure in the intake manifold provides the pressure rise. Compression occurs in the **plenum**, not inside the blower itself.
- It is **always mechanically driven** because the trapped air during compression heats up; an exhaust-driven Roots blower would suffer from thermal failure.

#### Part (b) — Turbo Lag and Mitigation (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Turbo Lag (2 marks):**
Turbo lag is the **delay between the throttle demand and the corresponding rise in boost pressure** — typically 0.5–2.5 seconds in a conventional turbocharged engine. It occurs because:
- The turbine and compressor have rotational inertia ($I \cdot \alpha$).
- At low engine speeds, **exhaust mass flow is low**, hence insufficient turbine torque to spool up.
- The driver demands boost before the exhaust can supply it.

**Methods to Reduce Turbo Lag (5 marks):**

1. **Variable Geometry Turbocharger (VGT):** Adjustable guide vanes vary the effective $A/R$ ratio with engine speed, maintaining turbine efficiency across the range.
2. **Twin-Scroll Turbocharger:** Split exhaust pulses feed separate scrolls; pulse energy is preserved and used earlier.
3. **Bypass / Wastegate Control:** Limits maximum boost at high RPM; indirectly improves low-end response.
4. **Electrically Assisted Turbo (E-Turbo):** A 48V (or 12V) electric motor on the shaft pre-spools the compressor at low RPM — used in modern Audi SQ5, Mercedes EQ Boost.
5. **Anti-lag System (ALS) in motorsport:** Combustion is intentionally delayed to keep exhaust gas temperature and pressure high, sustaining turbine speed between gear shifts.
6. **Smaller, low-inertia turbines (e.g., ball-bearing shafts) and reduced bearing friction** allow faster spool-up.
7. **Compound charging:** A small mechanical supercharger fills the low-RPM gap, while the turbocharger takes over at higher RPM.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing "supercharging" with "turbocharging" in definitions** — Examiners award full marks only when the *general vs. specific* distinction is made explicit. (–1 to –2 marks if missed.)
> 2. **Forgetting the charging efficiency $\eta_c$ in MEP derivations** — Many students use $\eta_c = 1$ which overstates the boost benefit. Always state $\eta_c$ in the assumption block.
> 3. **Using $\gamma = 1.4$ for charged air after heating** — If the charge is *significantly* heated (e.g., after a small Roots blower), use $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K and treat $\gamma$ as effectively lower (≈1.33–1.38).
> 4. **Neglecting turbine efficiency** in turbocharger questions — The energy balance $W_t \cdot \eta_t = W_c / \eta_c$ is the most common derivation trap; the work *recovered* from the exhaust is the turbine work, not the enthalpy drop.
> 5. **Power ratio vs. MEP ratio confusion** — $IP \propto p_{mi} \cdot N \cdot V_s$. If $N$ changes (e.g., a turbocharger enables higher rated speed), the BP increase is **not** purely the MEP ratio.
> 6. **Drawing the Roots blower with rotors touching** — The figure-eight lobes must be shown with a small clearance and synchronized by external timing gears, not touching.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Supercharging & Turbocharging**

- **Definition:** Supercharging = forced induction; Turbocharging = exhaust-driven forced induction.
- **Volumetric Efficiency:** In NA: $\eta_v \leq 1$; In supercharged: $\eta_v$ can be **1.2 to 1.5** (or higher).
- **Compressor Power (isentropic):** $W = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} p_1 V_1 \left[ r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]$ — **memorize this exact form** for KTU derivations.
- **Pressure Ratio $r_p$:** Typically 1.2–3.5 for road vehicles; up to 4–5 in heavy-duty diesels.
- **Boost Pressure Formula:** $p_{boost} = p_2 - p_{atm}$.
- **MEP Ratio:** $\dfrac{p_{mi,super}}{p_{mi,NA}} = \dfrac{\eta_c}{\eta_v} \cdot r_p \cdot \dfrac{T_{atm}}{T_2}$.
- **Thermal Efficiency:** Unchanged by supercharging in the ideal Otto cycle — depends on compression ratio $r$, not on $r_p$.
- **Roots Blower:** Positive displacement, mechanically driven only, no internal compression, lobes timed by external gears.
- **Centrifugal Compressor:** Velocity rise → diffuser pressure rise; pressure ∝ $N^2$ of impeller.
- **Turbo Lag:** Inertia + low exhaust flow at low RPM → boost response delay.
- **Mitigation Methods:** VGT, twin-scroll, E-turbo, smaller turbine, anti-lag, compound supercharging.
- **Intercooler / Aftercooler:** Mandatory for high $r_p$ — every 10°C drop in charge temp ≈ 3% increase in density.
- **Real-world KTU numbers to remember:**
    - Aircraft turbocharger restores 100% of sea-level power at 5 km altitude.
    - Modern passenger-car turbos achieve BSFC ~ 220 g/kWh (vs. ~ 280 g/kWh NA petrol).
    - Pulse turbocharging is preferred for 2-stroke marine diesels (MAN B&W, Wartsila).
- **Common Comparison Pairs KTU asks:**
    1. Roots blower vs. Centrifugal blower.
    2. Mechanical supercharger vs. Turbocharger.
    3. Pulse vs. Constant-pressure turbocharging.
- **Energy Recovery Logic (must-know):** Turbine extracts ~ 8–15% of fuel energy that would otherwise be wasted → net BSFC improvement in turbocharged engines.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engine Testing and Performance of SI and CI Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Engine Testing and Performance of SI and CI Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engine Testing** is the experimental determination of the actual performance characteristics of an internal combustion (IC) engine under varying operating conditions (load, speed, fuel, cooling) using a dynamometer and standardized instrumentation. **Performance of SI and CI Engines** refers to the quantitative evaluation of the engine's power output, efficiency, fuel consumption, and heat distribution using the First Law of Thermodynamics applied to an open system (control volume).

The performance of any IC engine is quantified by parameters that measure **how effectively** the chemical energy of the fuel is converted into **useful mechanical work** at the crankshaft, while accounting for unavoidable thermodynamic and mechanical losses.

### 1.2 Key Performance Parameters (KTU Syllabus Glossary)

| Symbol | Parameter | Physical Meaning |
|--------|-----------|------------------|
| $IP$ | Indicated Power | Power developed inside the cylinder from gas pressure |
| $BP$ | Brake Power | Useful power available at the crankshaft |
| $FP$ | Friction Power | Power lost to friction and pumping ($FP = IP - BP$) |
| $\eta_{mech}$ | Mechanical Efficiency | Ratio $BP/IP$ |
| $\eta_{th}$ | Thermal Efficiency | Fraction of fuel energy converted to work |
| $\eta_{vol}$ | Volumetric Efficiency | Effectiveness of air induction |
| $BSFC$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | Fuel consumed per unit brake power |
| $p_{im}$ | Indicated Mean Effective Pressure | Average constant pressure giving same work |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Engine as a Marathon Runner"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine an engine as a marathon runner carrying a heavy backpack of fuel.**
> - The **fuel's chemical energy** is the total calories in the runner's body.
> - **Indicated Power (IP)** is the energy spent in just moving the legs (the theoretical "push" the muscles generate).
> - **Brake Power (BP)** is the net forward motion — after the runner loses energy to sweat, joint friction, and breathing (friction).
> - **Mechanical efficiency** is the ratio of forward motion to leg-muscle effort.
> - **Thermal efficiency** is the ratio of forward motion to the food's total calories (most energy leaves as body heat — analogous to exhaust and cooling losses in the engine).

This analogy makes it clear why no engine can be **100% efficient** — every step involves unavoidable losses to surroundings (cooling, exhaust, friction).

## 1.4 Why Engine Testing is Performed

1. **Validation of design** — confirming theoretical predictions.
2. **Comparative evaluation** — benchmarking SI vs CI, fuels, modifications.
3. **Emission and economy assessment** — meeting regulatory norms.
4. **Calibration** of governor and fuel injection equipment.
5. **Heat balance** for designing cooling and exhaust systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Engine testing forms a **14-mark compulsory question** in most years under the module "Performance of IC Engines." Mastery of indicator diagrams, Morse test, and heat balance is essential.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Open System Energy Balance

An IC engine cylinder is modeled as an **open system (control volume)** executing the **Otto / Diesel / Dual cycle**. Applying the steady-flow energy equation between inlet (1) and exhaust (2):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{in} - \dot{W}_{net} &= \dot{m}_f \cdot CV + \dot{m}_a \cdot h_{a1} - \dot{m}_{exh} \cdot h_{exh}
\end{aligned}
$$

For engine testing, the **performance parameters** are derived from measurable quantities (force, time, distance, fuel mass, cooling water flow, exhaust gas temperature).

## 2.2 Power Calculations — The Three Pillars

### 2.2.1 Indicated Power (IP)
Measured from the **indicator diagram** (area represents work per cycle).

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{cycle} &= p_{im} \cdot L \cdot A \\
IP &= \frac{p_{im} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot K}{60} \quad \text{[kW]}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $p_{im}$ = indicated mean effective pressure, **in kPa** (or **bar**, with conversion $1\ \text{bar} = 100\ \text{kPa}$)
- $L$ = stroke length in **m**
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of piston in **m²**
- $N$ = crank speed in **rpm**
- $K$ = number of cylinders
- For a 2-stroke engine, multiply by 2 (one working stroke per revolution).

### 2.2.2 Brake Power (BP)
Measured using a **dynamometer** (rope-brake, hydraulic, eddy-current, or electrical).

$$
\begin{aligned}
BP &= \frac{2\pi N T}{60 \times 1000} \quad \text{[kW]} \quad \text{(T in N·m)} \\
BP &= \frac{2\pi N (F - F_0) R}{60 \times 1000} \quad \text{(rope-brake, F in N, R in m)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.2.3 Friction Power (FP)
Cannot be measured directly; obtained as the difference:

$$
\begin{aligned}
FP = IP - BP
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Morse Test** is a special technique to measure FP experimentally by **cutting out** cylinders one at a time and observing the change in BP. It is a favourite KTU 14-mark question.

## 2.3 Efficiencies — Quantifying Conversion Quality

### 2.3.1 Mechanical Efficiency

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{IP} = 1 - \frac{FP}{IP}
\end{aligned}
$$

Typical values: **80–90%** for well-maintained engines.

### 2.3.2 Indicated Thermal Efficiency

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{ith} = \frac{IP \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times CV} = \frac{IP}{IP + \text{Losses}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.3.3 Brake Thermal Efficiency

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times CV}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.3.4 Volumetric Efficiency

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{vol} = \frac{\text{Actual mass of air inducted}}{\text{Theoretical mass (swept volume} \times \text{air density)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{vol} = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\rho_a \cdot V_s \cdot \frac{N}{60} \cdot \frac{K}{2}} \quad (\text{for 4-stroke})
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.3.5 Relative Efficiency (Efficiency Ratio)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{rel} = \frac{\eta_{th,actual}}{\eta_{th,air-standard}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.3.6 Air-Standard Efficiency (Reference values)
- **Otto cycle:** $\eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$
- **Diesel cycle:** $\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$
- **Dual cycle:** combination of the above.

## 2.4 Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)

$$
\begin{aligned}
ISFC &= \frac{\dot{m}_f}{IP} \quad \text{[kg/kWh]} \\
BSFC &= \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} \quad \text{[kg/kWh]}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SI engines:** BSFC ≈ **0.30–0.40 kg/kWh**. **CI engines:** BSFC ≈ **0.22–0.28 kg/kWh** — this is why diesel is more economical.

## 2.5 Heat Balance Sheet — Energy Accounting

The First Law requires that **all fuel energy must be accounted for**. A heat balance sheet is a 100% breakdown:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{fuel} &= \dot{Q}_{BP} + \dot{Q}_{cooling} + \dot{Q}_{exhaust} + \dot{Q}_{unaccounted}
\end{aligned}
$$

Each term in **kJ/s (kW)** or **% of fuel energy**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{fuel} &= \frac{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}{3600} \\
\dot{Q}_{BP} &= BP \\
\dot{Q}_{cooling} &= \dot{m}_w c_{pw} (T_{w,out} - T_{w,in}) \\
\dot{Q}_{exhaust} &= \dot{m}_{exh} c_{pe} (T_{exh} - T_{amb})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> $\dot{Q}_{unaccounted}$ is typically **5–10%** and includes radiation, incomplete combustion, and unburnt hydrocarbons.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All absolute-value symbols in tables use `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown syntax.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $IP = \dfrac{p_{im} L A N K}{60}$ | Indicated Power | kW |
| 2 | $BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60 \times 1000}$ | Brake Power (T in N·m) | kW |
| 3 | $FP = IP - BP$ | Friction Power | kW |
| 4 | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP}$ | Mechanical Efficiency | — |
| 5 | $\eta_{ith} = \dfrac{IP \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$ | Indicated Thermal Efficiency | — |
| 6 | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$ | Brake Thermal Efficiency | — |
| 7 | $\eta_{rel} = \dfrac{\eta_{ith}}{\eta_{air\text{-}std}}$ | Relative Efficiency | — |
| 8 | $BSFC = \dfrac{3600 \dot{m}_f}{BP}$ | Brake SFC | kg/kWh |
| 9 | $ISFC = \dfrac{3600 \dot{m}_f}{IP}$ | Indicated SFC | kg/kWh |
| 10 | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{2 \dot{m}_a}{\rho_a V_s N K}$ (4-stroke) | Volumetric Efficiency | — |
| 11 | $BP_{motor} - BP_{engine} = \text{Morse correction}$ | Morse Test | kW |
| 12 | $\dot{Q}_{cool} = \dot{m}_w c_{pw} \Delta T$ | Cooling Heat Loss | kW |
| 13 | $\dot{Q}_{exh} = \dot{m}_e c_{pe} (T_e - T_a)$ | Exhaust Heat Loss | kW |
| 14 | $\eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ | Otto Efficiency | — |
| 15 | $p_{im} = \dfrac{\text{Area of indicator diagram}}{L}$ | Mean Effective Pressure | kPa |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SI (Petrol) Engines** — high-speed, lightweight applications (motorcycles, cars). Higher BSFC, lower $\eta_{bth}$, throttle governing used.
- **CI (Diesel) Engines** — heavy-duty, high compression, fuel efficient. Used in trucks, ships, locomotives, gensets. Quantity governing used.
- Heat balance data is used to design **radiators** (cooling), **turbochargers** (exhaust energy recovery), and **emission control systems**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Indicated Power from Mean Effective Pressure

**Starting Point:** Work done per cycle per cylinder equals the area under the indicator (P-V) diagram.

**Step 1 — Define work per cycle:**
The work done by gas on the piston during one complete cycle is the area enclosed by the indicator diagram.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{cycle} &= \oint p \, dV \quad \text{[J/cycle]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Introduce mean effective pressure:**
Replace the irregular indicator diagram with an equivalent rectangle whose height is $p_{im}$ and base is the swept volume $V_s = L \cdot A$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{cycle} &= p_{im} \cdot V_s = p_{im} \cdot L \cdot A
\end{aligned}
$$

where $p_{im}$ is in Pa (or N/m²), $L$ in m, $A$ in m², giving $W_{cycle}$ in Joules.

**Step 3 — Number of cycles per second:**
For a **4-stroke engine**, one cycle = 2 revolutions. So cycles per second = $N/120$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{cycle} &= \frac{N}{120} \quad \text{[cycles/s]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Power per cylinder:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IP_{cyl} &= W_{cycle} \cdot n_{cycle} = \frac{p_{im} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N}{120} \quad \text{[W]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Multi-cylinder extension:**
For $K$ identical cylinders:

$$
\begin{aligned}
IP &= K \cdot IP_{cyl} = \frac{p_{im} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot K}{120} \quad \text{[W]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to kW and 4-stroke simplification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IP \ [\text{kW}] &= \frac{p_{im}\ [\text{kPa}] \cdot L\ [\text{m}] \cdot A\ [\text{m}^2] \cdot N \cdot K}{60{,}000} \\
\text{or equivalently}\quad IP \ [\text{kW}] &= \frac{p_{im}\ [\text{bar}] \cdot L\ [\text{m}] \cdot A\ [\text{cm}^2] \cdot N \cdot K}{6 \times 10^5}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For 2-stroke engines:** replace the factor 120 by 60 (one cycle per revolution), giving $\dfrac{p_{im} L A N K}{60}$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Brake Power from Torque Measurement

**Step 1 — Work done per revolution:**
A torque $T$ applied through one full revolution ($2\pi$ radians) does work:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{rev} &= T \cdot 2\pi \quad \text{[J]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Revolutions per second:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{rev} &= \frac{N}{60} \quad \text{[rev/s]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BP &= W_{rev} \cdot n_{rev} = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} \quad \text{[W]} \\
BP \ [\text{kW}] &= \frac{2\pi N T}{60 \times 1000} \quad \text{with } T \text{ in N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Rope-Brake Special Form:**
For a rope brake, $T = (F - F_0) \cdot R$ where $(F - F_0)$ is the net effective load (N) and $R$ is the brake drum radius (m):

$$
\begin{aligned}
BP \ [\text{kW}] &= \frac{2\pi N (F - F_0) R}{60 \times 1000}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Morse Test (Friction Power)

**Concept:** Run the engine with **all cylinders firing** → measure $BP_{all}$. Then "cut out" one cylinder (spark plug disabled for SI; fuel supply cut for CI) and measure the new BP. The cylinder that is cut out now runs as a **pump** (drawing friction power only).

**Step 1 — Cut out cylinder 1:**

The remaining $(K-1)$ cylinders produce BP = $BP_1$. Meanwhile, the disabled cylinder contributes zero IP but consumes friction power $FP_1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
BP_1 &= BP_{all} - FP_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad FP_1 = BP_{all} - BP_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Cut out each cylinder sequentially:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FP_1 &= BP_{all} - BP_1 \\
FP_2 &= BP_{all} - BP_2 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
FP_K &= BP_{all} - BP_K
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total friction power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FP &= FP_1 + FP_2 + \cdots + FP_K \\
FP &= K \cdot BP_{all} - (BP_1 + BP_2 + \cdots + BP_K)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Indicated power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IP &= BP_{all} + FP
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** Friction power of each cylinder remains constant regardless of which cylinders are firing. This is the standard assumption for KTU problems.

## 3.4 Worked Example 1 — IP, BP, Efficiencies

**Problem:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has bore = 80 mm, stroke = 100 mm, runs at 3000 rpm. The indicator diagram gives $p_{im} = 8.5$ bar. Rope-brake diameter = 1 m, dead load = 50 N, net load = 350 N. Fuel consumption = 10 kg/hr, CV = 44,000 kJ/kg. Find IP, BP, $\eta_{mech}$, $\eta_{bth}$, BSFC.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Geometry:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi}{4}(0.080)^2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2 \\
L &= 0.100\ \text{m} \\
K &= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Indicated Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IP &= \frac{p_{im} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot K}{60{,}000} \\
IP &= \frac{8.5 \times 100 \times 0.100 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \times 3000 \times 4}{60{,}000} \\
IP &= \frac{8.5 \times 100 \times 0.100 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \times 12{,}000}{60{,}000} \\
IP &= \frac{5127.5}{60{,}000} = 0.08546\ \text{kW} \quad ???
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction — Using bar × cm² units for cleaner KTU computation:**
> $p_{im} = 8.5$ bar, $L = 10$ cm, $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(8)^2 = 50.27$ cm².

$$
\begin{aligned}
IP &= \frac{p_{im} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot K}{6 \times 10^5} \\
IP &= \frac{8.5 \times 10 \times 50.27 \times 3000 \times 4}{6 \times 10^5} \\
IP &= \frac{51{,}275{,}400}{600{,}000} = 85.46\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Brake Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= (F - F_0) \cdot R = (350 - 50) \times 0.5 = 150\ \text{N·m} \\
BP &= \frac{2\pi \times 3000 \times 150}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{2{,}827{,}433}{60{,}000} = 47.12\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Mechanical Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{mech} &= \frac{BP}{IP} = \frac{47.12}{85.46} = 0.5514 = 55.14\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Brake Thermal Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_f &= \frac{10}{3600}\ \text{kg/s} \\
\eta_{bth} &= \frac{BP \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV} = \frac{47.12 \times 3600}{(10/3600) \times 44{,}000} \\
\eta_{bth} &= \frac{47.12 \times 3600}{122.22} = \frac{169{,}632}{122.22} \\
\eta_{bth} &= 1387 \quad \text{(WRONG UNITS!)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit correction:** $\dot{m}_f = 10\ \text{kg/hr} = \dfrac{10}{3600}\ \text{kg/s} = 2.778 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/s}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{bth} &= \frac{47.12}{2.778 \times 10^{-3} \times 44{,}000} = \frac{47.12}{122.22} = 0.3856 = 38.56\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — BSFC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BSFC &= \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} = \frac{2.778 \times 10^{-3}}{47.12} \times 3600 = 0.212\ \text{kg/kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering sanity check:** BSFC ≈ 0.21 kg/kWh is too low for an SI engine — typical SI range is 0.30–0.40. The unusually high $\eta_{bth}$ confirms an inconsistent problem data; in KTU, such problems are accepted as-is with the computed values.

## 3.5 Worked Example 2 — Heat Balance Sheet

**Problem:** A single-cylinder diesel engine: BP = 7.5 kW, $\eta_{mech}$ = 80%, fuel = 2 kg/hr, CV = 43 MJ/kg. Cooling water: 12 kg/hr, inlet 25°C, outlet 60°C. Exhaust gas temp = 320°C, ambient = 27°C, $c_{pe}$ = 1.005 kJ/kg·K, air-fuel ratio = 18, $c_{pw}$ = 4.18 kJ/kg·K.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Fuel energy input:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{fuel} &= \dot{m}_f \cdot CV = \frac{2}{3600} \times 43{,}000 = 23.89\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Brake heat equivalent (work):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{BP} &= BP = 7.5\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Cooling heat loss:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{cool} &= \dot{m}_w c_{pw} \Delta T \\
\dot{Q}_{cool} &= \frac{12}{3600} \times 4.18 \times (60 - 25) \\
\dot{Q}_{cool} &= 3.333 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.18 \times 35 = 0.4876\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Indicated Power (from $\eta_{mech}$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IP &= \frac{BP}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{7.5}{0.80} = 9.375\ \text{kW} \\
\dot{Q}_{IP} - \dot{Q}_{BP} &= FP = 1.875\ \text{kW} \quad \text{(also a loss)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Exhaust mass flow:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{exh} &= \dot{m}_f (1 + A/F) = \frac{2}{3600}(1 + 18) = \frac{38}{3600} = 0.01056\ \text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Exhaust heat loss:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{exh} &= \dot{m}_{exh} c_{pe} (T_{exh} - T_{amb}) \\
\dot{Q}_{exh} &= 0.01056 \times 1.005 \times (320 - 27) \\
\dot{Q}_{exh} &= 0.01056 \times 1.005 \times 293 = 3.110\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Unaccounted loss (radiation, etc.):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{unaccounted} &= \dot{Q}_{fuel} - \dot{Q}_{BP} - \dot{Q}_{cool} - \dot{Q}_{exh} - FP \\
\dot{Q}_{unaccounted} &= 23.89 - 7.5 - 0.4876 - 3.110 - 1.875 \\
\dot{Q}_{unaccounted} &= 10.92\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Heat Balance Table:**

| Item | Energy (kW) | % of Fuel Energy |
|------|-------------|------------------|
| Brake Power | 7.500 | 31.40 |
| Friction Power (lost to oil) | 1.875 | 7.85 |
| Cooling Water | 0.488 | 2.04 |
| Exhaust Gas | 3.110 | 13.02 |
| Unaccounted (radiation) | 10.917 | 45.69 |
| **Total** | **23.890** | **100.00** |

> [!WARNING]
> **High unaccounted loss (45%) is unrealistic** — likely due to insufficient data on jacket losses. In KTU problems, accept computed values and present them in the table.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.1 Engine Testing Setup — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    B --> C[Dynamometer]
    C --> D[Load Cell / Weighing Scale]

    A --> G[Cooling Water Outlet]
    A --> H[Exhaust Gas Outlet]
    A --> I[Engine Indicator]
    C --> J[Tachometer]
    C --> K[Thermocouples]

    E --> M[Fuel Measuring Cylinder]

    N --> O[Planimeter for Area]
    O --> P[p_im Calculation]
```

    C --> H[To Output Shaft]

```

```

## 4.4 Morse Test Procedure Flowchart

flowchart TD
    A[Start: All Cylinders Firing] --> B[Measure BP_all]
    B --> C[Cut Out Cylinder 1]
    C --> D[Measure BP_1]
    D --> E[Re-engage Cylinder 1]
    E --> F[Cut Out Cylinder 2]
    F --> G[Measure BP_2]
    G --> H[Repeat for All K Cylinders]
    H --> I[FP_1 = BP_all - BP_1]
    H --> J[FP_2 = BP_all - BP_2]
    H --> K[FP_K = BP_all - BP_K]
    I --> L[Total FP = Sum of all FP_i]
    J --> L
    K --> L
