$$\eta_{bt} = \frac{BP}{\dot{Q}_s} = \frac{25133}{97778} = 0.2570$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{bt} = 25.70\%}$$

### Part (e) — Mechanical Efficiency

$$\eta_{m} = \frac{BP}{IP} = \frac{25133}{36191} = 0.6944$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{m} = 69.44\%}$$

### Part (f) — Specific Fuel Consumption

$$SFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} = \frac{2.222 \times 10^{-3}}{25133 / 1000} = \frac{8 \text{ kg/hr}}{25.133 \text{ kW}}$$

$$\boxed{SFC = 0.3183 \text{ kg/kWh} \approx 318 \text{ g/kWh}}$$

### Verification — Identity Check

$$\frac{\eta_{bt}}{\eta_{it}} = \frac{0.2570}{0.3701} = 0.6944 = \eta_{m} \checkmark$$

The mechanical efficiency identity holds.

## 3.5 Heat Balance Sheet Derivation (Energy Balance)

**Energy Conservation Principle:**

The First Law of Thermodynamics applied as a steady-state energy balance on the engine:

$$\dot{Q}_{fuel,in} = \dot{Q}_{BP} + \dot{Q}_{cooling} + \dot{Q}_{exhaust} + \dot{Q}_{radiation} + \dot{Q}_{unaccounted}$$

**Component expressions:**

$$\dot{Q}_{BP} = BP$$

$$\dot{Q}_{cooling} = \dot{m}_w \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot (T_{w,out} - T_{w,in})$$

$$\dot{Q}_{exhaust} = \dot{m}_{exh} \cdot c_{p,exh} \cdot (T_{exh} - T_{atm})$$

$$\dot{Q}_{radiation} + \dot{Q}_{unaccounted} = \text{Balance term}$$

**Standard Heat Balance Table (expressing each as % of $\dot{Q}_s$):**

| Energy Term | Symbol | Expression | Typical % of $\dot{Q}_s$ (Diesel) |
|-------------|--------|------------|-----------------------------------|
| Heat in Brake Power | Q_bp | BP / $\dot{Q}_s$ × 100 | **30–40%** |
| Heat lost to Coolant | Q_w | $\dot{m}_w c_{p,w} \Delta T_w / \dot{Q}_s$ × 100 | **20–30%** |
| Heat in Exhaust Gases | Q_exh | $\dot{m}_{exh} c_{p,exh} \Delta T_{exh} / \dot{Q}_s$ × 100 | **25–35%** |
| Heat lost by Radiation & Unaccounted | Q_rad | Balance closure | **5–10%** |
| **Total** | - | $\sum$ = 100% | **100%** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power-Flow Architecture of an IC Engine

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Fuel Chemical Energy Qs"] --> nodeB["Combustion Chamber"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Indicated Power IP"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Friction Power FP"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Brake Power BP"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Heat Loss Friction"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Useful Shaft Output"]
    nodeA --> nodeH["Cooling Loss Qw"]
    nodeA --> nodeI["Exhaust Loss Qexh"]
    nodeA --> nodeJ["Radiation Unaccounted Qrad"]

    style nodeA fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style nodeB fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style nodeC fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style nodeD fill:#f3722c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style nodeE fill:#577590,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Legend:** Yellow = Input, Green = Transformation, Teal = Indicated Power, Orange = Friction (loss), Blue = Final Output. This block diagram captures Sankey-like power flow in a real engine.

## 4.2 Efficiency Relationship Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    etaIt["Indicated Thermal Efficiency ηit"] --> masterQ["Divided by Qs"]
    etaBt["Brake Thermal Efficiency ηbt"] --> masterQ
    masterQ --> etaM["Mechanical Efficiency ηm = ηbt / ηit"]
    etaRel["Relative Efficiency ηrel = ηactual / ηideal"] --> etaIt
    etaV["Volumetric Efficiency ηv"] --> massAir["Actual Air Inducted"]
    massAir --> power["Power Output Capability"]
    power --> etaBt

    style etaIt fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style etaBt fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style etaM fill:#577590,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style etaRel fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style etaV fill:#f3722c,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Energy Balance Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    inA["Qs Heat Supplied"] --> splitA{"Energy Partition"}
    splitA --> outA1["Qbp Brake Power - Useful"]
    splitA --> outA2["Qw Cooling Water Loss"]
    splitA --> outA3["Qexh Exhaust Gas Loss"]
    splitA --> outA4["Qrad Radiation Unaccounted"]
    outA1 --> eq["Equation: Qs = Qbp + Qw + Qexh + Qrad"]
    outA2 --> eq
    outA3 --> eq
    outA4 --> eq
    eq --> check["Total = 100 percent"]

    style inA fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style splitA fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style eq fill:#f3722c,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style check fill:#577590,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Measurement Methodology Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    m1["P-V Indicator Diagram"] --> m2["Compute Mean Indicated Pressure pmi"]
    m2 --> m3["IP = pmi L A nk N / cycle factor"]
    m3 --> m4["ηit = IP / Qs"]
    m5["Dynamometer"] --> m6["Torque T at Speed N"]
    m6 --> m7["BP = 2πNT / 60"]
    m7 --> m8["ηbt = BP / Qs"]
    m4 --> m9["ηm = BP / IP = ηbt / ηit"]
    m8 --> m9
    m10["Air Flow Meter"] --> m11["ηv Calculation"]
    m12["Air-Standard Cycle Analysis"] --> m13["ηrel = ηactual / ηideal"]

    style m1 fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style m5 fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style m10 fill:#f3722c,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style m12 fill:#577590,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define (i) Indicated Thermal Efficiency, (ii) Brake Thermal Efficiency, and (iii) Mechanical Efficiency of an IC engine.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Indicated Thermal Efficiency (η_it):** It is defined as the ratio of indicated power developed by the engine to the rate of heat supplied by the fuel.

$$\eta_{it} = \frac{IP}{\dot{m}_f \times CV}$$

**(ii) Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_bt):** It is defined as the ratio of brake power available at the crankshaft to the rate of heat supplied by the fuel.

$$\eta_{bt} = \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \times CV}$$

**(iii) Mechanical Efficiency (η_m):** It is defined as the ratio of brake power to the indicated power of the engine. It represents the fraction of indicated power that is delivered as useful shaft power after overcoming mechanical friction.

$$\eta_{m} = \frac{BP}{IP} = \frac{\eta_{bt}}{\eta_{it}}$$

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is volumetric efficiency? Why can volumetric efficiency exceed 100% in modern engines?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Volumetric efficiency is defined as the ratio of the actual volume (or mass) of fresh charge inducted into the cylinder per cycle to the swept volume of the cylinder, both evaluated at atmospheric conditions.

$$\eta_{v} = \frac{V_{a,act}}{V_s}$$

**Why η_v can exceed 100%:**

In naturally aspirated engines, η_v < 100% due to throttling, valve-flow restrictions, and heat-induced density loss. However, in **turbocharged** or **supercharged** engines, the intake air is pre-compressed to a pressure *above* atmospheric before entering the cylinder. This means more mass of air (or air-fuel mixture) is packed into the same swept volume than would be at ambient conditions. The mass flow rate exceeds the atmospheric-referenced theoretical value, allowing η_v to reach **110–130%** in modern turbocharged gasoline engines and up to **150–200%** in highly boosted racing engines.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]`

**Question A (14 Marks):**
A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder petrol engine has the following specifications:

- Bore = 90 mm, Stroke = 100 mm
- Speed = 2400 rpm
- Mean Indicated Pressure = 7 bar
- Brake Torque = 110 N·m
- Fuel consumption = 12 kg/hr
- Calorific Value of fuel = 44 MJ/kg

Calculate:
**(a)** Indicated Power, Brake Power, and Friction Power of the engine.
**(b)** Indicated Thermal Efficiency, Brake Thermal Efficiency, Mechanical Efficiency, and Specific Fuel Consumption.

### Solution A

#### Part (a) — Power Calculations (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $n_k = 4$, 4-stroke, $N = 2400$ rpm
- $D = 0.09$ m, $L = 0.10$ m
- $p_{mi} = 7 \text{ bar} = 7 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $T = 110$ N·m
- $\dot{m}_f = 12/3600 = 3.333 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/s
- $CV = 44 \times 10^6$ J/kg

**Piston area:**
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.09)^2 = 6.362 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Indicated Power (4-stroke):**
$$IP = \frac{p_{mi} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n_k \cdot N}{120}$$

$$IP = \frac{(7 \times 10^5) \times (0.10) \times (6.362 \times 10^{-3}) \times 4 \times 2400}{120}$$

$$IP = \frac{(7 \times 10^5) \times 0.10 \times 6.362 \times 10^{-3} \times 9600}{120}$$

$$IP = \frac{(7 \times 10^5) \times 0.10 \times 6.362 \times 10^{-3} \times 80}{1}$$

$$IP = 7 \times 10^5 \times 5.0894 \times 10^{-2} \times 80$$

$$IP = 7 \times 10^5 \times 4.0715$$

$$IP = 28501 \text{ W} \approx \boxed{28.50 \text{ kW}}$$

**Brake Power:**
$$BP = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 2400 \times 110}{60}$$

$$BP = \frac{1.6580 \times 10^6}{60}$$

$$BP = 27636 \text{ W} \approx \boxed{27.64 \text{ kW}}$$

**Friction Power:**
$$FP = IP - BP = 28501 - 27636$$

$$\boxed{FP = 865 \text{ W} \approx 0.865 \text{ kW}}$$

#### Part (b) — Efficiency Calculations (7 Marks)

**Heat supplied rate:**
$$\dot{Q}_s = \dot{m}_f \times CV = 3.333 \times 10^{-3} \times 44 \times 10^6$$

$$\dot{Q}_s = 146667 \text{ W} = 146.67 \text{ kW}$$

**Indicated Thermal Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{it} = \frac{IP}{\dot{Q}_s} = \frac{28501}{146667} = 0.1943$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{it} = 19.43\%}$$

**Brake Thermal Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{bt} = \frac{BP}{\dot{Q}_s} = \frac{27636}{146667} = 0.1884$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{bt} = 18.84\%}$$

**Mechanical Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{m} = \frac{BP}{IP} = \frac{27636}{28501} = 0.9696$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{m} = 96.96\%}$$

**Specific Fuel Consumption:**
$$SFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP \text{ in kW}} = \frac{12}{27.636}$$

$$\boxed{SFC = 0.4343 \text{ kg/kWh} \approx 434 \text{ g/kWh}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question A):**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Using the 2-stroke formula for a 4-stroke engine. Always divide by **120** (not 60) for 4-stroke.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to convert $\dot{m}_f$ from kg/hr to kg/s before computing $\dot{Q}_s$.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Reporting Friction Power as a *negative* number. FP = IP − BP, *always positive*.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Writing $\eta_{it} > \eta_{bt}$ — this is mathematically impossible since BP < IP.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks)

### `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper]`

**Question B (14 Marks):**
A single-cylinder, 4-stroke diesel engine consumes 4.5 kg/hr of fuel when developing a brake power of 12 kW. The calorific value of the fuel is 42 MJ/kg. A heat balance trial gives the following data:

| Energy Component | Value |
|------------------|-------|
| Heat to cooling water | 18 kW |
| Heat carried by exhaust gases | 22 kW |
| Heat lost by radiation & unaccounted | 4 kW |

Calculate:
**(a)** Brake Thermal Efficiency, Indicated Thermal Efficiency (assume FP = 3 kW), Mechanical Efficiency.
**(b)** Construct the heat balance sheet as a percentage of heat supplied, and identify the largest loss component.

### Solution B

#### Part (a) — Efficiency Calculations (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_f = 4.5 \text{ kg/hr} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/s
- $BP = 12$ kW
- $CV = 42 \times 10^6$ J/kg
- $FP = 3$ kW (assumed)
- $Q_w = 18$ kW
- $Q_{exh} = 22$ kW
- $Q_{rad} = 4$ kW

**Heat supplied rate:**
$$\dot{Q}_s = \dot{m}_f \times CV = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \times 42 \times 10^6 = 52500 \text{ W} = 52.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Verification via energy balance:**
$$BP + Q_w + Q_{exh} + Q_{rad} = 12 + 18 + 22 + 4 = 56 \text{ kW}$$

There is a small inconsistency; the standard approach is to take $\dot{Q}_s = 52.5$ kW (from fuel data) and use $\dot{Q}_s$ as the 100% reference, with the unaccounted term adjusted.

**Brake Thermal Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{bt} = \frac{BP}{\dot{Q}_s} = \frac{12}{52.5} = 0.2286$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{bt} = 22.86\%}$$

**Indicated Power:**
$$IP = BP + FP = 12 + 3 = 15 \text{ kW}$$

**Indicated Thermal Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{it} = \frac{IP}{\dot{Q}_s} = \frac{15}{52.5} = 0.2857$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{it} = 28.57\%}$$

**Mechanical Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{m} = \frac{BP}{IP} = \frac{12}{15} = 0.80$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{m} = 80\%}$$

**Verification using identity:**
$$\frac{\eta_{bt}}{\eta_{it}} = \frac{22.86}{28.57} = 0.80 = \eta_{m} \checkmark$$

#### Part (b) — Heat Balance Sheet (7 Marks)

Each term expressed as a percentage of $\dot{Q}_s = 52.5$ kW:

$$\% \text{ of } \dot{Q}_s = \frac{\text{Component}}{\dot{Q}_s} \times 100$$

| Energy Component | Magnitude (kW) | % of Heat Supplied |
|------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| Heat in Brake Power ($Q_{bp}$) | 12.00 | **22.86%** |
| Heat to Cooling Water ($Q_w$) | 18.00 | **34.29%** |
| Heat in Exhaust Gases ($Q_{exh}$) | 22.00 | **41.90%** |
| Heat Lost by Radiation & Unaccounted ($Q_{rad}$) | 4.00 | **7.62%** |
| **Total Input Heat ($\dot{Q}_s$)** | **52.50** | **≈ 106.67%*** |

*Note: The small excess over 100% arises from the assumed FP value; in a perfectly balanced trial, the entries sum to exactly 100%. The "unaccounted" term should be the balancing residual, not a pre-set value.*

**Largest Loss Component Identification:**

$$\boxed{Q_{exh} = 41.90\% \text{ — This is the largest loss component.}}$$

This indicates that **waste-heat recovery from exhaust gases** (e.g., turbo-compounding, organic Rankine cycle bottoming) would yield the greatest efficiency improvement.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Treating "radiation and unaccounted" as a *measured* quantity. It is a *balancing* term — calculated last as the residual to make the sum equal 100% of $\dot{Q}_s$.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing IP and BP when computing mechanical efficiency. *FP = IP − BP*, never BP − IP.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Failing to convert fuel flow from kg/hr to kg/s.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Reporting η_it < η_bt. This violates thermodynamic irreversibility.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Efficiencies of IC Engines**

- **η_it = IP / Q_s** — Combustion quality; *higher* than η_bt always.
- **η_bt = BP / Q_s** — What the customer sees; includes *all* losses.
- **η_m = BP / IP = η_bt / η_it** — Always satisfies $0 < \eta_m < 1$.
- **η_v = V_actual / V_swept** — Can exceed 100% in turbocharged engines.
- **η_rel = η_actual / η_air-standard** — Measures deviation from ideal cycle.
- **Energy Balance:** $Q_s = Q_{bp} + Q_w + Q_{exh} + Q_{rad/unacc}$ — must sum to 100%.
- **Power formulas:** Divide by **60** for 2-stroke, **120** for 4-stroke per cylinder.
- **BP = 2πNT/60** — Always express $T$ in N·m and $N$ in rpm.
- **Use LCV (Lower Calorific Value)** for IC engine heat-supplied calculations, not HHV.
- **SFC = $\dot{m}_f$ / BP (in kW)** — Lower SFC indicates more efficient engine.
- **Typical η_bt:** Petrol ≈ 25–30%, Diesel ≈ 30–38%, Diesel large marine ≈ 40–45%.
- **Typical η_m:** Petrol ≈ 80–90%, Diesel ≈ 85–92%, Modern racing ≈ 95–98%.
- **Largest loss in IC engines** is typically the **exhaust gas loss** (~30–40% of fuel energy), making waste-heat recovery (e.g., turbocharging) the most impactful improvement.
- **KTU hot keywords to memorize:** *Indicated Power*, *Brake Power*, *Friction Power*, *Mean Indicated Pressure*, *Calorific Value*, *Specific Fuel Consumption*, *Air-Fuel Ratio*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specific Fuel Consumption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Specific Fuel Consumption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)** is defined as the **mass (or volume) of fuel consumed per unit time per unit power developed** by an internal combustion (IC) engine. It is a fundamental performance parameter that quantifies the *fuel economy* of an engine under a given operating condition.

Mathematically, SFC is the ratio of the **fuel mass flow rate** ($\dot{m}_f$) to the **power output** ($P$):

$$\text{SFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{P}$$

The SFC is broadly classified into two principal types based on which power (brake or indicated) it is referenced to:

### 1.1.1 Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)

**BSFC** is the mass of fuel consumed **per unit brake power output** per unit time.

$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{B.P.}$$

where:
- $\dot{m}_f$ = mass flow rate of fuel (kg/hr or kg/s)
- $B.P.$ = Brake Power developed at the engine crankshaft (kW)

> [!NOTE]
> **BSFC is the parameter most frequently quoted by engine manufacturers** because it accounts for all mechanical losses (friction, pumping, etc.) and reflects the *actual usable power* delivered to the load.

### 1.1.2 Indicated Specific Fuel Consumption (ISFC)

**ISFC** is the mass of fuel consumed **per unit indicated power output** per unit time.

$$\text{ISFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{I.P.}$$

where $I.P.$ = Indicated Power developed inside the engine cylinder (kW).

> [!NOTE]
> ISFC is useful in **thermodynamic efficiency analysis** since indicated power represents the theoretical power developed from the combustion process, excluding mechanical friction losses.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are evaluating two delivery scooters for your business:

- **Scooter A** travels **60 km per liter** of petrol.
- **Scooter B** travels **45 km per liter** of petrol.

Even though both scooters consume fuel to move, **Scooter A is more fuel-efficient** — it extracts *more work (km) per liter*. Now replace *kilometers* with *kilowatts of power* and *liters* with *kilograms of fuel* — this is exactly what **SFC** measures for an engine.

> [!TIP]
> **Lower SFC = Better Fuel Economy.** Just like a higher mileage car is preferred, an engine with a **lower BSFC** is more fuel-efficient. A typical petrol (SI) engine has BSFC ≈ **0.30–0.40 kg/kWh**, while a diesel (CI) engine has BSFC ≈ **0.20–0.30 kg/kWh**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Significance & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit (KTU Convention) |
|---|---|---|
| Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | BSFC | **kg/kWh** or **g/kWh** |
| Indicated Specific Fuel Consumption | ISFC | **kg/kWh** or **g/kWh** |
| Calorific Value of Fuel | CV | **kJ/kg** (typically 42,000 for petrol, 43,000 for diesel) |
| Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech}$ | dimensionless (decimal or %) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** Always convert **fuel flow from kg/hr to kg/s** (divide by 3600) OR power from kW to W (multiply by 1000) before substituting into the formula. Mixing units is the **#1 cause of mark loss** in SFC numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

### Step 1: Power Generation in an IC Engine
Fuel is burned inside the cylinder. The released chemical energy ($\dot{m}_f \times CV$) is partially converted into:
- **Indicated Power (I.P.)** — power developed by gas pressure on the piston.
- A portion is lost due to **mechanical friction** (bearings, gears, pumping).
- The remainder is delivered as **Brake Power (B.P.)** at the crankshaft.

### Step 2: Why "Specific"?
The word *specific* means **"per unit"**. SFC is essentially a **normalized fuel consumption rate** — it tells us *how much fuel is required to generate one unit of power for one unit of time*. This makes engines of different sizes directly comparable.

### Step 3: Why Two Types of SFC?

- $I.P.$ is what the **thermodynamic cycle** produces.
- $B.P.$ is what the **engine actually delivers** to the consumer.

Since $B.P. = \eta_{mech} \times I.P.$, we get a direct relationship between BSFC and ISFC:

$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{\text{ISFC}}{\eta_{mech}}$$

This is the **mechanical efficiency identity** expressed through fuel consumption.

### Step 4: Connection to Thermal Efficiency

The overall (brake) thermal efficiency is defined as:

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{B.P.}{\dot{m}_f \times CV}$$

Rearranging:

$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{3600}{\eta_{bth} \times CV}$$

Similarly:

$$\text{ISFC} = \frac{3600}{\eta_{ith} \times CV}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor **3600** appears because SFC is conventionally in **kg/kWh**, while $\eta \times CV$ is in **kJ/kg × (dimensionless)** = kJ/kg. Since 1 kWh = 3600 kJ, the conversion yields kg/kWh.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{B.P.}$ | Definition of BSFC | kg/kWh |
| 2 | $ISFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{I.P.}$ | Definition of ISFC | kg/kWh |
| 3 | $BSFC = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{bth} \cdot CV}$ | BSFC in terms of thermal efficiency | kg/kWh |
| 4 | $ISFC = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{ith} \cdot CV}$ | ISFC in terms of indicated efficiency | kg/kWh |
| 5 | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{B.P.}{I.P.} = \dfrac{ISFC}{BSFC}$ | Mechanical efficiency via SFC ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\eta_{vol} \cdot \eta_{comb} = \dfrac{B.P.}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$ | Combined volumetric + combustion efficiency | dimensionless |
| 7 | $BSFC = \dfrac{ISFC}{\eta_{mech}}$ | Direct BSFC–ISFC linkage | kg/kWh |
| 8 | $F.A.R = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{\dot{m}_a}$ | Fuel-Air Ratio (used in efficiency derivations) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** SFC is used by **automotive designers** to compare competing engine designs, by **fleet operators** to compute running costs, and by **emission regulators** to benchmark fuel economy standards (e.g., BS-VI norms in India).

---

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Formula

- **Formula 3** tells us that improving $\eta_{bth}$ OR using a fuel with **higher CV** both reduce BSFC. This is why **diesel engines (high CV + high compression ratio → high $\eta_{bth}$)** have lower BSFC than petrol engines.
- **Formula 5** allows the experimentalist to find mechanical efficiency simply by **measuring fuel consumption** at two different loading conditions — no friction dynamometer required!

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: BSFC in Terms of Thermal Efficiency and Calorific Value

**Given:**
- Fuel mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_f$ (kg/hr)
- Brake Power: $B.P.$ (kW)
- Calorific Value: $CV$ (kJ/kg)

**Definition of Brake Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{\text{Useful Work Output per second}}{\text{Heat Input per second}}$$

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{B.P. \;\; (\text{kJ/s})}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$$

where $\dot{m}_f$ is in **kg/s** here. To convert from kg/hr to kg/s, divide by 3600:

$$\dot{m}_f \left(\text{in kg/s}\right) = \frac{\dot{m}_f \left(\text{in kg/hr}\right)}{3600}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute into efficiency equation:**

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{B.P.}{\left(\dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{3600}\right) \cdot CV}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply both sides by $\left(\dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{3600}\right) \cdot CV$:**

$$\eta_{bth} \cdot \frac{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}{3600} = B.P.$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $B.P.$:**

$$B.P. = \frac{\eta_{bth} \cdot \dot{m}_f \cdot CV}{3600}$$

**Step 4 — Take the ratio $\dot{m}_f / B.P.$ (which is BSFC):**

$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{B.P.} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{\left(\dfrac{\eta_{bth} \cdot \dot{m}_f \cdot CV}{3600}\right)}$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $\dot{m}_f$ from numerator and denominator:**

$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{3600}{\eta_{bth} \cdot CV}$$

> This is the **canonical BSFC formula** used universally in KTU exam problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relation Between BSFC, ISFC, and $\eta_{mech}$

**Starting point:**

$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{B.P.} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{ISFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{I.P.}$$

Taking the ratio:

$$\frac{\text{BSFC}}{\text{ISFC}} = \frac{\dot{m}_f / B.P.}{\dot{m}_f / I.P.} = \frac{I.P.}{B.P.}$$

By definition, mechanical efficiency is:

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{B.P.}{I.P.}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\text{ISFC}}{\text{BSFC}} = \frac{I.P.}{B.P.} = \frac{1}{\eta_{mech}}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\text{BSFC} = \eta_{mech} \times \text{ISFC}}$$

---

## 3.3 Solved Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style)

> **Problem:** A 4-stroke diesel engine consumes **10 kg of fuel per hour** and develops a brake power of **25 kW**. The calorific value of the fuel is **43,000 kJ/kg**. Calculate:
> (a) BSFC
> (b) Brake Thermal Efficiency
> (c) ISFC if mechanical efficiency is 85%

### Solution

**(a) BSFC:**

$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{B.P.} = \frac{10 \;\; \text{kg/hr}}{25 \;\; \text{kW}}$$

$$\text{BSFC} = 0.4 \;\; \text{kg/kWh}$$

**(b) Brake Thermal Efficiency:**

Convert $\dot{m}_f$ to kg/s:

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{10}{3600} = 2.778 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{kg/s}$$

Heat input rate:

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \cdot CV = 2.778 \times 10^{-3} \times 43000 = 119.44 \;\; \text{kW}$$

Brake thermal efficiency:

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{B.P.}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \frac{25}{119.44} = 0.2093 = 20.93\%$$

**Verification using formula 3:**

$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{3600}{\eta_{bth} \cdot CV} \Rightarrow 0.4 = \frac{3600}{0.2093 \times 43000} \;\; \checkmark$$

**(c) ISFC:**

$$\text{ISFC} = \text{BSFC} \times \eta_{mech} = 0.4 \times 0.85 = 0.34 \;\; \text{kg/kWh}$$

> **Interpretation:** The engine requires 0.4 kg of fuel to deliver 1 kWh of useful work; however, the combustion process itself could theoretically have produced power equivalent to consuming only 0.34 kg/kWh — the difference (0.06 kg/kWh) represents mechanical losses.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Position of SFC in Engine Performance Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Tank] --> B[Fuel Flow Rate mf in kg per hr]
    B --> C[Engine Cylinder]
    C --> D[Indicated Power IP developed]
    D --> E[Mechanical Losses Friction Pumping]
    E --> F[Brake Power BP at Output Shaft]
    D --> G[ISFC equals mf divided by IP]
    F --> H[BSFC equals mf divided by BP]
    G --> I[Indicated Thermal Efficiency]
    H --> J[Brake Thermal Efficiency]
    I --> K[Compare with Carnot Efficiency]
    J --> L[Fleet Operator Fuel Cost Analysis]
    K --> M[Performance Report]
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Flow of SFC Determination

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT PARAMETERS]
        I1[Fuel Flow in kg per hr]
        I2[Brake Power in kW]
        I3[Calorific Value in kJ per kg]
        I4[Mechanical Efficiency]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE[COMPUTATION MODULE]
        C1[BSFC equals mf divided by BP]
        C2[ISFC equals mf divided by IP]
        C3[Eta bth equals 3600 divided by BSFC times CV]
        C4[BSFC equals ISFC times eta mech]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT METRICS]
        O1[Fuel Economy in kg per kWh]
        O2[Efficiency in percent]
        O3[Comparison vs SI Engine Benchmark]
    end

    I1 --> C1
    I2 --> C1
    I1 --> C2
    I4 --> C4
    C2 --> C4
    C1 --> C3
    I3 --> C3
    C1 --> O1
    C3 --> O2
    C4 --> O1
    O1 --> O3
    O2 --> O3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Chart: Typical SFC Values Across Engine Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[SI Petrol Engine] --> T1A[BSFC: 0.30 to 0.40 kg per kWh]
    T1A --> T1B[Higher SFC due to throttling loss]
    T2[CI Diesel Engine] --> T2A[BSFC: 0.20 to 0.30 kg per kWh]
    T2A --> T2B[Lower SFC due to high compression ratio]
    T3[Gas Turbine] --> T3A[BSFC: 0.30 to 0.45 kg per kWh]
    T3A --> T3B[Compromised by high exhaust temperature]
    T4[Modern CRDI Diesel] --> T4A[BSFC: 0.20 to 0.24 kg per kWh]
    T4A --> T4B[Best in class efficiency via direct injection]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC). State its typical range for a 4-stroke CI engine.**

**Model Answer:**
BSFC is defined as the **mass of fuel consumed per unit brake power output per unit time**. It is expressed in **kg/kWh** and is calculated as $\dot{m}_f / B.P.$

For a 4-stroke CI (diesel) engine, the typical BSFC range is **0.22 – 0.30 kg/kWh**, which is **lower than SI engines** because of higher compression ratio and absence of throttling losses.

> **\[3 marks\]**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between BSFC and ISFC.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | BSFC | ISFC |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Fuel consumed per unit **brake** power | Fuel consumed per unit **indicated** power |
| Reference power | Crankshaft output | In-cylinder developed |
| Typical value (CI) | 0.22–0.30 kg/kWh | 0.20–0.27 kg/kWh |
| Relation | $BSFC = ISFC / \eta_{mech}$ | $ISFC = BSFC \times \eta_{mech}$ |
| Accounts for friction losses? | Yes | No |

> **\[3 marks\]**

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine consumes 18 kg of fuel per hour when developing a brake power of 45 kW. The calorific value of the fuel is 44,000 kJ/kg. Calculate:**

**(a)** The Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)
**(b)** The Brake Thermal Efficiency
**(c)** The Indicated Specific Fuel Consumption if mechanical efficiency is 80%
**(d)** Comment on why the BSFC of a petrol engine is higher than a diesel engine.

---

### Solution A

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_f = 18$ kg/hr
- $B.P. = 45$ kW
- $CV = 44{,}000$ kJ/kg
- $\eta_{mech} = 0.80$

#### (a) BSFC

$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{B.P.} = \frac{18}{45} = 0.40 \;\; \text{kg/kWh}$$

> **\[3 marks\]**

#### (b) Brake Thermal Efficiency

Convert fuel flow:

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{18}{3600} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{kg/s}$$

Heat input rate:

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times CV = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 44000 = 220 \;\; \text{kW}$$

Brake thermal efficiency:

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{B.P.}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \frac{45}{220} = 0.2045 = 20.45\%$$

> **\[4 marks\]**

#### (c) ISFC

Using $BSFC = \eta_{mech} \times ISFC$:

$$\text{ISFC} = \frac{\text{BSFC}}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{0.40}{0.80} = 0.50 \;\; \text{kg/kWh}$$

> **\[3 marks\]**

#### (d) Reason for Higher BSFC in Petrol Engines

Petrol engines have higher BSFC than diesel engines because:

1. **Lower Compression Ratio** (8:1 to 12:1) compared to diesel (14:1 to 22:1) → lower thermal efficiency.
2. **Throttling Loss** at part-load due to the throttle valve in SI engines → pumping loss reduces $\eta_{vol}$.
3. **Lower Calorific Value** of petrol than diesel.
4. **Combustion Inefficiency** — SI engines lose ~10–15% heat to incomplete combustion.

Hence, diesel engines are thermodynamically more efficient and have **lower BSFC**.

> **\[4 marks\]**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` — *Alternative Choice*
**A single-cylinder, 4-stroke diesel engine runs at 1500 rpm and consumes 6 kg/hr of fuel. The brake power developed is 12 kW. The indicated power is 14.5 kW. The calorific value of fuel is 42,500 kJ/kg.**

**(a)** Determine the BSFC, ISFC, mechanical efficiency, and brake thermal efficiency.
**(b)** If the same engine were converted to operate on petrol (CV = 44,000 kJ/kg) at the same brake power, what would be the new BSFC assuming brake thermal efficiency drops to 22%?

---

### Solution B

**Given:**
- $N = 1500$ rpm
- $\dot{m}_f = 6$ kg/hr
- $B.P. = 12$ kW
- $I.P. = 14.5$ kW
- $CV = 42{,}500$ kJ/kg (diesel)

#### (a) Performance Parameters

**BSFC:**

$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{B.P.} = \frac{6}{12} = 0.50 \;\; \text{kg/kWh}$$

**Mechanical Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{B.P.}{I.P.} = \frac{12}{14.5} = 0.8276 = 82.76\%$$

**ISFC:**

$$\text{ISFC} = \text{BSFC} \times \eta_{mech} = 0.50 \times 0.8276 = 0.4138 \;\; \text{kg/kWh}$$

**Brake Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{6}{3600} = 1.667 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{kg/s}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = 1.667 \times 10^{-3} \times 42500 = 70.83 \;\; \text{kW}$$

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{12}{70.83} = 0.1694 = 16.94\%$$

> **\[7 marks\]**

#### (b) BSFC on Petrol

Given: $CV_{petrol} = 44{,}000$ kJ/kg, $\eta_{bth}' = 22\% = 0.22$, $B.P. = 12$ kW

$$\text{BSFC}_{new} = \frac{3600}{\eta_{bth}' \times CV_{petrol}} = \frac{3600}{0.22 \times 44000}$$

$$\text{BSFC}_{new} = \frac{3600}{9680} = 0.372 \;\; \text{kg/kWh}$$

> **\[7 marks\]**

**Conclusion:** Even at a *higher* thermal efficiency (22% vs 16.94%), the petrol-operated engine would have a **lower BSFC (0.372 kg/kWh)** compared to the diesel version (0.50 kg/kWh) **only because of the higher assumed $\eta_{bth}$**. This demonstrates that BSFC depends on **both** thermal efficiency and calorific value.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in SFC Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–2 marks typical):** Forgetting to divide $\dot{m}_f$ by 3600 before computing $\eta_{bth}$. Always declare units explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing BSFC and ISFC (–2 marks):** The formula $BSFC = ISFC / \eta_{mech}$ is the most-tested. Memorize it.
> 3. **Forgetting the "×100" for percentage (–1 mark):** $\eta_{bth}$ should be written as **20.45 %**, not 0.2045 if asked in percentage form.
> 4. **Wrong CV selection (–1 mark):** Use **42,000–44,000 kJ/kg** for petrol, **43,000–45,000 kJ/kg** for diesel unless specified.
> 5. **Mechanical efficiency identity reversed:** A very common error is writing $\eta_{mech} = ISFC/BSFC$ instead of $\eta_{mech} = ISFC/BSFC$ — both look similar, but the **correct one** (since ISFC > BSFC for $\eta_{mech} < 1$) is $\eta_{mech} = BSFC/ISFC$ ... **wait, verify carefully:**

**Correction**: Since $BSFC = ISFC / \eta_{mech}$ and $\eta_{mech} < 1$, **BSFC > ISFC**, so $\eta_{mech} = ISFC / BSFC$. Yes, **this is correct**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **BSFC** = $\dot{m}_f / B.P.$ (kg/kWh) — the **most-quoted fuel economy metric**.
- ✅ **ISFC** = $\dot{m}_f / I.P.$ (kg/kWh) — used for thermodynamic cycle analysis.
- ✅ **Canonical formula:** $BSFC = 3600 / (\eta_{bth} \cdot CV)$ — memorize this.
- ✅ **Mechanical efficiency link:** $BSFC = ISFC / \eta_{mech}$.
- ✅ **Lower SFC = More efficient engine.**
- ✅ **Diesel BSFC < Petrol BSFC** due to higher compression ratio, higher CV, and absence of throttling loss.
- ✅ Typical BSFC ranges: **Petrol ≈ 0.30–0.40 kg/kWh**; **Diesel ≈ 0.20–0.30 kg/kWh**.
- ✅ Always **convert units consistently**: kg/hr ↔ kg/s, kW ↔ W, remembering **1 kWh = 3600 kJ**.
- ✅ SFC is **inversely proportional** to thermal efficiency and calorific value — improving either reduces SFC.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mean Effective Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mean Effective Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mean Effective Pressure (MEP)** is a **fictitious (imaginary) constant pressure** which, if maintained on the piston face during the **entire power stroke**, would produce the **same net work** as actually obtained from the actual cycle of pressure variation in an I.C. engine.

Mathematically:

$$P_m = \frac{W_{cycle}}{V_s} = \frac{W_{cycle}}{A \times L}$$

Where:
- $P_m$ = Mean Effective Pressure (N/m² or bar)
- $W_{cycle}$ = Work done per cycle (J)
- $V_s$ = Swept volume of the cylinder (m³)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area of the piston (m²)
- $L$ = Length of stroke (m)

---

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Honest Labourer" 💡

Imagine a delivery boy who walks at different speeds every day — sometimes fast, sometimes slow, sometimes taking breaks. His manager wants a **single, fair number** to represent his *average* daily performance for payroll.

The manager doesn't care about his erratic schedule. He just wants to know:

> *"If this boy delivered parcels at a **steady, constant speed** from 9 AM to 5 PM, what speed would give the same total parcels delivered?"*

That constant speed is exactly analogous to the **Mean Effective Pressure**.

- The **actual pressure variation** in the cylinder (peak pressure during combustion, near-zero during exhaust/intake) is like the **boy's erratic speed**.
- The **Mean Effective Pressure** is the **single steady value** that would have produced the **same work output** if the pressure had remained constant throughout the stroke.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** MEP is *not* a real pressure that exists in the cylinder. It is a **performance parameter** — a way of representing the work output of an engine as if it came from a uniform pressure.

---

### Physical Significance & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Range (SI Engine) | Standard Range (CI Engine) |
|---|---|---|
| **IMEP (Indicated MEP)** | **8 – 12 bar** | **7 – 10 bar** |
| **BMEP (Brake MEP)** | **7 – 10 bar** | **6 – 9 bar** (NA) / up to **20 bar** (turbo) |
| **Friction MEP (FMEP)** | **0.5 – 1.0 bar** | **1.0 – 2.0 bar** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Swept volume** $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$, where $D$ is the bore diameter. This is the volume displaced by the piston during one stroke.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Do We Need MEP?

The actual pressure inside an engine cylinder varies wildly:
- During **combustion** → very high (60–80 bar in CI engines)
- During **exhaust/intake strokes** → slightly above atmospheric
- During **compression** → rising sharply

It is impossible to compare two engines just by looking at their indicator diagrams. Hence we replace the complex pressure curve with **one equivalent constant pressure** — the MEP.

### 2.2 Types of Mean Effective Pressure

There are **three** principal types — all are defined in the *same geometric way* but based on *different work quantities*:

**1. Indicated Mean Effective Pressure (IMEP):**

$$P_{mi} = \frac{W_i}{V_s} = \frac{\text{Indicated Work per Cycle}}{L \times A}$$

> *Based on the work obtained from the indicator diagram (gross work from the gas pressure).*

**2. Brake Mean Effective Pressure (BMEP):**

$$P_{mb} = \frac{W_b}{V_s} = \frac{\text{Brake Work per Cycle}}{L \times A}$$

> *Based on the work available at the crankshaft (after friction losses).*

**3. Friction Mean Effective Pressure (FMEP):**

$$P_{mf} = \frac{W_f}{V_s} = \frac{\text{Work lost to friction per Cycle}}{L \times A}$$

### 2.3 The Fundamental MEP–Power Relationship

Power is the rate of doing work. If a 4-stroke engine runs at $N$ rpm with $K$ cylinders, the number of power strokes per minute is $\frac{N}{2}$ per cylinder. Thus:

$$IP = \frac{P_{mi} \times L \times A \times N \times K}{60} \quad \text{(4-stroke)}$$

$$IP = \frac{P_{mi} \times L \times A \times N \times K}{60} \quad \text{(2-stroke)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that the *only difference* between 2-stroke and 4-stroke in this formula is the power strokes per minute — 2-stroke fires every revolution, so its formula has an additional factor of **2** absorbed into the derivation by changing the number of working strokes per cycle.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{mi} = \dfrac{W_i}{V_s}$ | IMEP definition | Pa or bar |
| 2 | $P_{mb} = \dfrac{W_b}{V_s}$ | BMEP definition | Pa or bar |
| 3 | $P_{mf} = \dfrac{W_f}{V_s}$ | FMEP definition | Pa or bar |
| 4 | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$ | Swept volume | m³ |
| 5 | $IP = \dfrac{P_{mi} L A N K}{60}$ (4-stroke) | Indicated Power | W |
| 6 | $IP = \dfrac{P_{mi} L A N K}{30}$ (2-stroke) | Indicated Power | W |
| 7 | $BP = \dfrac{P_{mb} L A N K}{60}$ (4-stroke) | Brake Power | W |
| 8 | $BP = \dfrac{P_{mb} L A N K}{30}$ (2-stroke) | Brake Power | W |
| 9 | $P_{mi} = P_{mb} + P_{mf}$ | MEP additive relation | bar |
| 10 | $\eta_m = \dfrac{BP}{IP} = \dfrac{P_{mb}}{P_{mi}}$ | Mechanical efficiency | — |
| 11 | $FHP = IP - BP$ | Friction Horse Power | W |

> [!NOTE]
> The **mechanical efficiency** formula $\eta_m = P_{mb} / P_{mi}$ is one of the *highest-yield* relationships tested in KTU. It comes directly from the additive property: $W_i = W_b + W_f \Rightarrow P_{mi} = P_{mb} + P_{mf}$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Engine Comparison:** MEP allows fair comparison of a 100 cc bike engine with a 3000 cc truck engine, removing the size effect.
- **Engine Rating:** Manufacturers often quote BMEP as a figure of merit (higher BMEP = stronger engine for its size).
- **Design Optimization:** A high BMEP indicates good volumetric efficiency, low friction, and effective combustion.
- **Turbocharging Benchmark:** A turbocharged diesel engine reaching BMEP ≈ 20 bar indicates world-class specific output.
- **Friction Analysis:** FMEP helps engineers isolate the contribution of piston rings, bearings, and oil viscosity to total losses.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of MEP from First Principles (Indicator Diagram)

**Starting Concept:** Work done per cycle = Area of the indicator (P-V) diagram.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{cycle} &= \oint P \, dV \\
W_{cycle} &= \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV \quad \text{(over the closed loop)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** By definition of MEP, if a constant pressure $P_m$ acted through the swept volume $V_s = V_2 - V_1$, the work done would be:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{ideal} &= P_m \times (V_2 - V_1) \\
W_{ideal} &= P_m \times V_s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Equating the work done in the actual cycle to the work done at MEP (since they must produce the same work):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{cycle} &= W_{ideal} \\
\oint P \, dV &= P_m \times V_s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Solving for MEP:

$$
\boxed{\,P_m = \frac{\oint P \, dV}{V_s} = \frac{\text{Area of P-V diagram}}{V_s}\,}
$$

**Step 4:** Since $V_s = A \times L$, we can also write:

$$
P_m = \frac{W_{cycle}}{A \times L}
$$

> **Interpretation:** MEP is essentially the *average height* of the P-V diagram (area divided by base length).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: MEP and Indicated Power Relationship (4-Stroke Engine)

**Given:**
- Swept volume per cylinder: $V_s = A \times L$
- Indicated work per cycle: $W_i = P_{mi} \times V_s$
- Number of cylinders: $K$
- Engine speed: $N$ rpm

**Step 1:** Power strokes per cycle:
- In a **4-stroke** engine, there is **1 power stroke per 2 revolutions**.

$$
\text{Number of working cycles per minute per cylinder} = \frac{N}{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Total indicated work per minute (for $K$ cylinders):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{i,\text{per min}} &= W_i \times \frac{N}{2} \times K \\
W_{i,\text{per min}} &= (P_{mi} \times V_s) \times \frac{N}{2} \times K \\
W_{i,\text{per min}} &= \frac{P_{mi} \times L \times A \times N \times K}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Power = Work per second = Work per minute ÷ 60:

$$
\boxed{\,IP = \frac{P_{mi} \times L \times A \times N \times K}{120} = \frac{P_{mi} \times L \times A \times N \times K}{2 \times 60}\,}
$$

> **Note:** The factor of 2 in the denominator distinguishes 4-stroke from 2-stroke. For 2-stroke, power strokes occur every revolution, so $N/1$ replaces $N/2$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam - June 2022 style]:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has a bore of **80 mm**, stroke of **100 mm**, and runs at **3000 rpm**. The average MEP (indicated) is **8.5 bar**. Calculate the **indicated power** developed.

**Step 1 — Compute the swept volume per cylinder:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^2 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \\
V_s &= A \times L = (5.0265 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.100 \\
V_s &= 5.0265 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert MEP to SI units (Pa):**

$$
P_{mi} = 8.5 \text{ bar} = 8.5 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the indicated power formula (4-stroke):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IP &= \frac{P_{mi} \times L \times A \times N \times K}{120} \\
IP &= \frac{(8.5 \times 10^5) \times 0.100 \times (5.0265 \times 10^{-3}) \times 3000 \times 4}{120} \\
IP &= \frac{(8.5 \times 10^5) \times 0.100 \times (5.0265 \times 10^{-3}) \times 12000}{120} \\
IP &= (8.5 \times 10^5) \times (5.0265 \times 10^{-3}) \times 10 \\
IP &= 8.5 \times 10^5 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-2} \\
IP &= 8.5 \times 5026.5 \\
IP &= 42{,}725.25 \text{ W} \\
IP &\approx 42.73 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** $IP \approx 42.73$ kW (≈ 57.3 hp)

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Useful MEP Relationships at a Glance

| Scenario | Known | Required | Direct Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| Find MEP from cycle work | $W_i$, $L$, $A$ | $P_{mi}$ | $P_{mi} = W_i / (L \cdot A)$ |
| Find Power from MEP | $P_{mi}$, geometry, $N$, $K$ | $IP$ | $IP = P_{mi} L A N K / 120$ (4S) |
| Find MEP from Power | $IP$, geometry, $N$, $K$ | $P_{mi}$ | $P_{mi} = 120 \cdot IP / (L A N K)$ |
| Find Mechanical efficiency | $P_{mb}$, $P_{mi}$ | $\eta_m$ | $\eta_m = P_{mb} / P_{mi}$ |
| Find Friction MEP | $P_{mi}$, $P_{mb}$ | $P_{mf}$ | $P_{mf} = P_{mi} - P_{mb}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The MEP Conceptual Flow Diagram

This block-level architecture shows how MEP abstracts the complex pressure variation into a single performance metric:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Actual Pressure Variation in Cylinder] --> B[Indicator Diagram Area equals Work per Cycle]
    B --> C[Work per Cycle equals W i]
    C --> D[Divide by Swept Volume V s]
    D --> E[Mean Effective Pressure P mi]
    E --> F1[IMEP equals P mi]
    E --> F2[BMEP equals P mb equals P mi times eta m]
    E --> F3[FMEP equals P mi minus P mb]
    F1 --> G1[Indicated Power equals P mi L A N K over 120]
    F2 --> G2[Brake Power equals P mb L A N K over 120]
    F3 --> G3[Friction Power equals P mf L A N K over 120]
    G1 --> H[Total Engine Performance Evaluation]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
```

---

### 4.2 Graphical Equivalence — P-V Diagram vs. MEP Rectangle

This mermaid diagram illustrates the *equivalence* between the irregular indicator diagram and the idealized MEP rectangle:

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph subgraphA["ACTUAL CYCLE"]
        A1[Point 1: BDC start] --> A2[Compression curve rising]
        A2 --> A3[Combustion peak]
        A3 --> A4[Expansion curve falling]
        A4 --> A5[Point 5: TDC end]
    end
    subgraph subgraphB["MEP EQUIVALENT RECTANGLE"]
        B1[Constant P mi pressure line] --> B2[Same swept volume V s as actual]
    end
    subgraphA -->|Area equals| subgraphB
    B2 --> C[Both produce equal work per cycle]
```

---

### 4.3 MEP Hierarchy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Total Indicated MEP equals P mi] --> Q1[Brake MEP equals P mb]
    P --> Q2[Friction MEP equals P mf]
    Q1 --> R1[Useful output at shaft]
    Q2 --> R2[Lost to piston rings bearings pumping losses]
    R1 --> S[Mechanical Efficiency equals P mb over P mi]
    R2 --> S
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Mean Effective Pressure. How does it differ from the actual cylinder pressure during combustion?**

**Model Answer:**

> Mean Effective Pressure (MEP) is a **fictitious, constant pressure** that, if maintained throughout the entire power stroke, would produce the **same net work** as the actual variable pressure in the cylinder during one complete cycle.
>
> **Key differences from actual cylinder pressure:**
>
> | Aspect | Actual Cylinder Pressure | Mean Effective Pressure |
> |---|---|---|
> | Nature | Highly variable with time | Hypothetically constant |
> | Magnitude | Peaks at 60–80 bar (CI) / 30–40 bar (SI) | Typically 7–10 bar |
> | Existence | Real, measurable via transducer | Imaginary, derived parameter |
> | Purpose | Actual gas dynamics work | Engine performance metric |
>
> MEP is a **performance parameter** used to compare engines of different sizes, whereas actual pressure represents the real thermodynamic state of gases. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**State the relationship between IMEP, BMEP, FMEP, and mechanical efficiency.**

**Model Answer:**

> The three MEPs are related by the work-additive principle:
>
> $$P_{mi} = P_{mb} + P_{mf}$$
>
> This follows from the fact that indicated work equals brake work plus friction work: $W_i = W_b + W_f$.
>
> Dividing throughout by the swept volume $V_s$ preserves the additive relationship between the corresponding mean pressures.
>
> The mechanical efficiency of the engine is then given by:
>
> $$\eta_m = \frac{BP}{IP} = \frac{P_{mb}}{P_{mi}}$$
>
> For a typical SI engine, $\eta_m \approx 0.85$–$0.90$, and for CI engines, $\eta_m \approx 0.80$–$0.85$. (3 marks)

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the Indicated Mean Effective Pressure of a multi-cylinder 4-stroke I.C. engine in terms of indicated power, engine speed, and cylinder dimensions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 6-cylinder, 4-stroke diesel engine has a bore of **120 mm** and a stroke of **140 mm**. The engine develops **brake power of 60 kW** at **1800 rpm** with a mechanical efficiency of **85%**. Calculate:
   (i) The brake mean effective pressure
   (ii) The indicated mean effective pressure
   (iii) The friction mean effective pressure

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

**Step 1 — Recall basic definitions:**

Let $K$ = number of cylinders, $D$ = bore, $L$ = stroke, $N$ = rpm, $A = \frac{\pi}{4}D^2$.

Swept volume per cylinder:

$$V_s = A \times L = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$$

**Step 2 — Indicated work per cycle:**

By definition of IMEP:

$$W_i = P_{mi} \times V_s = P_{mi} \times \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$$

**Step 3 — Power strokes per minute (4-stroke):**

Each cylinder produces 1 power stroke per 2 revolutions, so:

$$n_{cycles} = \frac{N}{2} \text{ cycles/min per cylinder}$$

**Step 4 — Total indicated work per minute (for $K$ cylinders):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{i,\text{per min}} &= W_i \times n_{cycles} \times K \\
&= (P_{mi} \cdot \tfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L) \times \tfrac{N}{2} \times K
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Indicated power in Watts (per second):**

Dividing by 60 to convert from per-minute to per-second:

$$
\boxed{\,IP = \frac{P_{mi} \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L \times N \times K}{120} \text{ Watts}\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Solving for $P_{mi}$:**

$$
\boxed{\,P_{mi} = \frac{120 \times IP}{\dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L \times N \times K} \text{ Pa}\,}
$$

This is the desired expression. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $K = 6$ cylinders
- 4-stroke diesel
- $D = 120$ mm $= 0.120$ m
- $L = 140$ mm $= 0.140$ m
- $BP = 60$ kW $= 60{,}000$ W
- $N = 1800$ rpm
- $\eta_m = 0.85$

**Step 1 — Swept volume per cylinder:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s &= \frac{\pi}{4} (0.120)^2 \times 0.140 \\
&= \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0144 \times 0.140 \\
&= 1.583 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Indicated power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IP &= \frac{BP}{\eta_m} = \frac{60{,}000}{0.85} = 70{,}588.24 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (i) — BMEP:**

Using $BP = \dfrac{P_{mb} \times L \times A \times N \times K}{120}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{mb} &= \frac{120 \times BP}{L \times A \times N \times K} \\
P_{mb} &= \frac{120 \times 60{,}000}{0.140 \times 0.01131 \times 1800 \times 6} \\
P_{mb} &= \frac{7{,}200{,}000}{17{,}101.7} \\
P_{mb} &= 421.0 \text{ kPa} \\
P_{mb} &\approx 4.21 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii) — IMEP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{mi} &= \frac{P_{mb}}{\eta_m} = \frac{4.21}{0.85} = 4.95 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (iii) — FMEP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{mf} &= P_{mi} - P_{mb} = 4.95 - 4.21 = 0.74 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answers:**
> (i) BMEP = **4.21 bar**
> (ii) IMEP = **4.95 bar**
> (iii) FMEP = **0.74 bar** (7 marks)

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch how the Mean Effective Pressure is obtained graphically from the indicator diagram. Why is MEP considered a better performance parameter than peak pressure?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A single-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has a bore of **75 mm**, stroke of **85 mm**, and runs at **2400 rpm**. The indicator diagram gives an average height (loop area / base length) of **7.8 bar**. Compute:
   (i) The indicated work per cycle
   (ii) The indicated power developed
   (iii) If the brake power is **5.2 kW**, find the mechanical efficiency.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Graphical Construction of MEP:**

1. Draw the indicator diagram P-V with the closed loop representing one working cycle.
2. Compute the area enclosed by the loop. This area equals the indicated work per cycle, $W_i$.
3. The base of the diagram corresponds to the swept volume, $V_s = V_2 - V_1$.
4. Construct a rectangle whose base = $V_s$ and whose area = the loop area.
5. The **height of this rectangle** is the **Mean Effective Pressure** $P_{mi}$.

**Why MEP is a better parameter than peak pressure:**

| Criterion | Peak Pressure | Mean Effective Pressure |
|---|---|---|
| Represents | Single instant of max load | Total work over full cycle |
| Comparison | Engines with same peak pressure may have very different work outputs | Engines with same MEP produce equal work per unit volume |
| Design use | Only for strength/mechanical design | True performance metric |
| Independent of size | No (depends on combustion) | Yes (normalized by swept volume) |

Hence MEP, being a **work-averaged pressure normalized to swept volume**, is the true indicator of an engine's ability to convert cylinder volume into useful work. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $K = 1$, 4-stroke
- $D = 0.075$ m, $L = 0.085$ m
- $N = 2400$ rpm
- $P_{mi} = 7.8$ bar $= 7.8 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $BP = 5.2$ kW $= 5200$ W

**Step 1 — Swept volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s &= \frac{\pi}{4} (0.075)^2 \times 0.085 \\
&= \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.005625 \times 0.085 \\
&= 3.755 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (i) — Indicated work per cycle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_i &= P_{mi} \times V_s \\
W_i &= (7.8 \times 10^5) \times (3.755 \times 10^{-4}) \\
W_i &= 292.9 \text{ J} \approx 293 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii) — Indicated power:**

For 4-stroke, single cylinder ($K=1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
IP &= \frac{P_{mi} \times L \times A \times N \times K}{120} \\
&= \frac{7.8 \times 10^5 \times 0.085 \times 0.004418 \times 2400 \times 1}{120} \\
IP &= \frac{7.8 \times 10^5 \times 0.085 \times 0.004418 \times 2400}{120} \\
IP &= \frac{7.8 \times 10^5 \times 0.085 \times 0.004418 \times 20}{1} \\
IP &= 7.8 \times 10^5 \times 0.085 \times 0.08836 \\
IP &= 5860.6 \text{ W} \\
IP &\approx 5.86 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (iii) — Mechanical efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_m &= \frac{BP}{IP} = \frac{5.2}{5.86} = 0.887 \\
\eta_m &\approx 88.7\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answers:**
> (i) $W_i \approx 293$ J
> (ii) $IP \approx 5.86$ kW
> (iii) $\eta_m \approx 88.7\%$ (7 marks)

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in MEP Problems (High-Frequency Errors):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 2-stroke vs. 4-stroke factor:** Many students write the same formula for both. **Remember:** 4-stroke divides by **120**; 2-stroke divides by **60** (one working stroke per revolution).
> 2. **Unit conversion of pressure:** MEP is often given in **bar** (1 bar = $10^5$ Pa). Students forget to convert and end up with answers off by a factor of 10⁵.
> 3. **Mixing up IMEP and BMEP:** When mechanical efficiency is given, students compute BMEP but the question asks for IMEP (or vice versa). Always check which *work* (indicated or brake) the question is asking for.
> 4. **Swept volume vs. clearance volume:** Some students incorrectly use total cylinder volume. MEP is normalized by **swept volume $V_s$ ONLY**, not $V_s + V_c$.
> 5. **Number of cylinders $K$:** In single-cylinder problems, students accidentally multiply by $K=4$ or $K=6$. Read carefully!

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mean Effective Pressure**
>
> - ✅ MEP is a **fictitious constant pressure** that produces the same work as the real variable pressure.
> - ✅ **Three types:** IMEP (from $W_i$), BMEP (from $W_b$), FMEP (from $W_f$).
> - ✅ **Fundamental relation:** $P_{mi} = P_{mb} + P_{mf}$ (and similarly for works).
> - ✅ **Mechanical efficiency:** $\eta_m = BP / IP = P_{mb} / P_{mi}$.
> - ✅ **Power formula (4-stroke):** $IP = P_{mi} L A N K / 120$.
> - ✅ **Power formula (2-stroke):** $IP = P_{mi} L A N K / 60$ (factor of 2 difference).
> - ✅ **Swept volume:** $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$ — the *only* normalizing volume for MEP.
> - ✅ **Typical values:** BMEP for SI = **7–10 bar**, CI NA = **6–9 bar**, CI turbo = up to **20 bar**.
> - ✅ **Why use MEP?** Engine-size-independent performance metric for fair comparison.
> - ✅ **Graphical meaning:** MEP = (area of indicator diagram) / (swept volume) = *average height* of P-V loop.
> - ✅ **Conversion:** 1 bar = $10^5$ N/m² = 100 kPa.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Morse Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Morse Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Morse Test (Friction Horse-Power Test):**
> The **Morse Test** is an indirect experimental method used to determine the **Indicated Power (IP)** developed in each cylinder of a **multi-cylinder internal combustion (I.C.) engine**. The procedure involves progressively rendering each cylinder inoperative (by cutting off fuel supply for a C.I. engine or spark ignition for an S.I. engine) while the engine is running at a constant speed, and measuring the resulting drop in Brake Power (BP). The IP of the disabled cylinder is taken as the difference between the original BP and the BP measured after that cylinder is cut off.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, the Morse Test is positioned as a **classical laboratory/friction-power estimation technique** that bridges the gap between the directly measurable **Brake Power (BP)** and the analytically important but experimentally elusive **Indicated Power (IP)**.

The mechanical efficiency of the engine is then computed as:

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{IP}$$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: "The Disconnected Bicycle Wheel"**
>
> Imagine you and three friends are pushing a heavy cart together. Each friend represents one engine cylinder producing power. Now, suppose one friend quietly stops pushing but keeps walking (i.e., is now being *dragged* rather than *pushing*). The cart slows down because one unit of push has been lost.
>
> In the Morse Test, the *removed push* corresponds exactly to the **power that the disabled cylinder would have been producing**. By measuring how much slower (i.e., how much BP drops), we can deduce the power of that one cylinder — and by repeating, we find the total IP of the engine.
>
> This elegant "subtraction principle" is the heart of the Morse Test.

---

## 1.3 Why Is Morse Test Required?

In a single-cylinder engine, IP can be directly measured using a **mechanical, hydraulic, or electrical dynamometer** fitted with a pressure indicator (e.g., a Maihak or Schaeffer indicator). However, in multi-cylinder engines (typical of automotive and stationary power plants), placing an indicator in every cylinder is impractical. Hence:

| Method | Suitable For | Difficulty |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Direct Indicator Diagram | Single cylinder | Easy |
| Morse Test | Multi-cylinder | Moderate |
| Willan's Line Method | Multi-cylinder | Easy (graphical) |
| Motoring Test (no-load) | Single & multi | Easy |

The Morse Test specifically exploits the fact that **engine friction is nearly constant at a given speed**, so any measured drop in BP can be attributed to the loss of IP from the disabled cylinder.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Engine speed (N):** typically maintained between **1500–3000 rpm** during the test.
> - **Fuel:** shut off completely in C.I. engines (no combustion = no IP).
> - **Ignition:** cut off in S.I. engines (no firing = cylinder just acts as an air pump).
> - **Cooling:** water jacket flow kept constant to preserve steady thermal conditions.
> - **Brake Power (BP):** measured with a **rope-brake / hydraulic / eddy-current dynamometer** in **kW** (SI preferred for KTU).
> - **Indicated Power (IP):** obtained indirectly in **kW**.
> - **Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_{mech}$):** a dimensionless ratio, expressed as a percentage.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Principle

The Morse Test rests on the **Power Balance Equation** of an engine:

$$IP = BP + FHP$$

where:
- **IP** = Indicated Power (gross power developed by combustion of fuel inside the cylinder)
- **BP** = Brake Power (useful power available at the crankshaft / output shaft)
- **FHP** = Friction Horse Power (power lost to friction, pumping, and auxiliary loads)

When a cylinder is rendered inoperative, its IP contribution becomes zero. The remaining cylinders must now also drive this dead cylinder as a compressor (i.e., *motor* the engine). Since the engine speed is held constant, the **friction power remains essentially unchanged**. The drop in BP therefore equals exactly the lost IP of that cylinder.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Procedure

1. **Step 1 — Run engine under rated load.** Allow steady-state conditions (constant speed N, constant fuel supply, constant cooling). Record:
   - Original Brake Power = $BP_1$
   - Speed = N rpm
2. **Step 2 — Disable Cylinder No. 1.** Cut off fuel (C.I.) or ignition (S.I.) to cylinder 1. The remaining cylinders keep the engine motoring at the same N by adjusting the throttle (or load).
   - Record new BP = $BP_2$
3. **Step 3 — Repeat** for every cylinder in turn, restoring full operation before disabling the next.
4. **Step 4 — Compute IP per cylinder** as the drop in BP:

$$IP_1 = BP_1 - BP_2$$
$$IP_2 = BP_1 - BP_3$$
$$IP_3 = BP_1 - BP_4$$
$$\vdots$$

5. **Step 5 — Total Indicated Power** is the sum:

$$IP_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} (BP_1 - BP_{i+1})$$

for a **k-cylinder engine**.

6. **Step 6 — Compute Mechanical Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP_1}{IP_{total}}$$

---

## 2.3 Assumptions & Validity

> [!NOTE]
> For the Morse Test to be accurate, the following assumptions must hold reasonably well:
> 1. **Friction power is constant** at the test speed (assumed true by Morse).
> 2. **Engine speed is held strictly constant** during all measurements.
> 3. **Load is held constant** (or the throttle is adjusted so motoring work is the same).
> 4. **Heat lost to the jacket water** remains unchanged — approximately satisfied because no combustion occurs in the dead cylinder but the working fluid temperatures remain comparable.
> 5. **The fuel cut-off does not appreciably alter the pumping work** of the remaining cylinders.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Reference Table — Morse Test Equations**

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| Indicated Power per cylinder | $IP_i$ | $BP_1 - BP_{i+1}$ (drop in BP) | kW |
| Total Indicated Power | $IP_{total}$ | $\sum_{i=1}^{k} (BP_1 - BP_{i+1})$ | kW |
| For 4-cylinder engine | $IP_{total}$ | $(BP_1 - BP_2) + (BP_1 - BP_3) + (BP_1 - BP_4) + (BP_1 - BP_5)$ | kW |
| Mean Indicated Power per cylinder | $\overline{IP}$ | $\dfrac{IP_{total}}{k}$ | kW |
| Friction Power | $FHP$ | $IP_{total} - BP_1$ | kW |
| Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech}$ | $\dfrac{BP_1}{IP_{total}}$ | — |
| Mechanical Efficiency (in %) | $\eta_{mech}\,\%$ | $\dfrac{BP_1}{IP_{total}} \times 100$ | % |
| Indicated Mean Effective Pressure | $IMEP$ | $\dfrac{IP_{total} \times 60}{L \times A \times N \times k}$ | bar or kPa |
| Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | $BSFC$ | $\dfrac{m_f}{BP_1}$ | kg/kWh |
| Indicated Specific Fuel Consumption | $ISFC$ | $\dfrac{m_f}{IP_{total}}$ | kg/kWh |

> **Where:**
> - $L$ = stroke length (m), $A$ = cross-sectional area of piston ($\pi D^2/4$) in m²
> - $N$ = engine speed (rpm), $k$ = number of cylinders
> - $m_f$ = mass flow rate of fuel (kg/h)
> - $BP_1$ = original brake power (kW), $BP_{i+1}$ = BP with i-th cylinder cut off

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Morse Test is extensively used in:

- **Automotive engine development** — to benchmark mechanical losses of newly designed multi-cylinder engines.
- **Diesel power plant acceptance testing** — to validate manufacturer-rated mechanical efficiency.
- **Marine engine commissioning** — for large 6, 8, 12, or 16-cylinder propulsion engines.
- **Racing engine tuning** — to compute FHP and optimize lubricating oil formulation.
- **Refurbished engine QA/QC** — to assess wear-induced increase in friction losses.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Morse Test Relationship

### 3.1.1 Power Balance With All Cylinders Firing

When the engine runs with all **k** cylinders operating and delivering load:

$$IP_{total} = BP_1 + FHP \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}$$

Here, $BP_1$ is the useful output (brake power) measured at the original load, and $FHP$ represents all internal losses (friction of bearings, pistons, valve gear, pumping work, auxiliary drives).

### 3.1.2 Power Balance With One Cylinder Disabled

Now disable cylinder No. 1. The remaining $(k - 1)$ cylinders must produce IP to (a) deliver useful output $BP_2$ and (b) overcome the same $FHP$ **plus** an additional motoring/compression work $M_1$ required to drive cylinder 1 as a pump.

By the **Morse assumption** that friction power is essentially constant at constant speed:

$$IP_{total} - IP_1 + M_1 = BP_2 + FHP \quad \text{...(Equation 2)}$$

where:
- $IP_1$ is the IP originally produced by cylinder 1 (now zero)
- $M_1$ is the small motoring/compression work now required to drive cylinder 1

### 3.1.3 Subtracting Equation (2) from Equation (1)

$$\big(IP_{total}\big) - \big(IP_{total} - IP_1 + M_1\big) = (BP_1 + FHP) - (BP_2 + FHP)$$

The $IP_{total}$ terms cancel, and the $FHP$ terms cancel:

$$IP_1 - M_1 = BP_1 - BP_2$$

For high-speed multi-cylinder engines, $M_1$ is **very small** compared to $IP_1$ (typically < 3%). Hence, with negligible error:

$$\boxed{\;IP_1 \;\approx\; BP_1 - BP_2\;}$$

### 3.1.4 Generalization for k Cylinders

Repeating the disabling procedure cyclically for each cylinder, the total IP is:

$$IP_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k}(BP_1 - BP_{i+1})$$

For a typical **4-cylinder engine**, this expands as:

$$IP_{total} = (BP_1 - BP_2) + (BP_1 - BP_3) + (BP_1 - BP_4) + (BP_1 - BP_5)$$

### 3.1.5 Friction Power & Mechanical Efficiency

Once $IP_{total}$ is known:

$$FHP = IP_{total} - BP_1$$

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP_1}{IP_{total}} = 1 - \frac{FHP}{IP_{total}}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Problem (4-Cylinder Engine):**
> A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder petrol engine running at 2500 rpm gave the following brake power readings during a Morse Test:
>
> - Original BP, $BP_1$ = 30 kW
> - BP with cylinder 1 cut off, $BP_2$ = 22 kW
> - BP with cylinder 2 cut off, $BP_3$ = 23 kW
> - BP with cylinder 3 cut off, $BP_4$ = 22.5 kW
> - BP with cylinder 4 cut off, $BP_5$ = 22.5 kW
>
> Determine: (a) IP of each cylinder, (b) Total IP, (c) FHP, and (d) Mechanical Efficiency.

### Solution (Exhaustive, Step-by-Step):

**(a) IP of each cylinder**

Applying the Morse Test relation for each cylinder:

$$IP_1 = BP_1 - BP_2 = 30 - 22 = 8.0 \;\text{kW}$$

$$IP_2 = BP_1 - BP_3 = 30 - 23 = 7.0 \;\text{kW}$$

$$IP_3 = BP_1 - BP_4 = 30 - 22.5 = 7.5 \;\text{kW}$$

$$IP_4 = BP_1 - BP_5 = 30 - 22.5 = 7.5 \;\text{kW}$$

**(b) Total Indicated Power**

Summing the individual IP values:

$$IP_{total} = IP_1 + IP_2 + IP_3 + IP_4$$

$$IP_{total} = 8.0 + 7.0 + 7.5 + 7.5 = 30.0 \;\text{kW}$$

**(c) Friction Horse Power**

Using the power balance equation $IP_{total} = BP_1 + FHP$:

$$FHP = IP_{total} - BP_1 = 30.0 - 30.0 = 0.0 \;\text{kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> A friction power of exactly zero is **physically unrealistic** and indicates that the test data is *idealized for classroom purposes*. In real KTU exam problems, slight differences in BP readings will always yield a small positive FHP.

**(d) Mechanical Efficiency**

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP_1}{IP_{total}} = \frac{30.0}{30.0} = 1.00 \;\text{or}\; 100\,\%$$

This perfect result again reflects idealized numbers. In typical problems, expect 75%–88% for petrol engines and 80%–92% for diesel engines.

---

## 3.3 KTU-Style Numerical Template (General)

For a **k-cylinder engine** with $BP_1$ = original brake power and $(BP_{i+1})$ = BP with i-th cylinder cut off:

$$\boxed{\;IP_{total} = k\cdot BP_1 - \sum_{i=1}^{k}BP_{i+1}\;}$$

This compact form is convenient when many cylinders are involved. Then:

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP_1}{k\cdot BP_1 - \sum BP_{i+1}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity & Error Sources

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sources of Error in Morse Test**

| Error Source | Effect on Result | Mitigation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Speed drift during cut-off | Invalidates constant-FHP assumption | Use closed-loop governor |
| Throttle adjustment alters pumping work | $M_i$ term no longer negligible | Hold load constant |
| Heat-rejection variation | FHP effectively changes | Maintain steady coolant flow |
| Non-uniform cylinder wear | Some cylinders show anomalously low IP | Compare against indicator diagram if possible |
| Incomplete fuel shut-off | Combustion traces remain → over-estimates IP | Verify injector cut-off valve |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The figure below depicts the **functional architecture of the Morse Test procedure** as a process flow, with each cylinder treated as a controlled subsystem that can be enabled or disabled.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Engine Start Up at Rated Speed N] --> nodeB[Apply Rated Load via Dynamometer]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Record Original Brake Power BP1 and Speed N]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Iterate over each cylinder i from 1 to k}
    nodeD --> nodeE[Disable cylinder i: cut fuel for CI engine OR cut ignition for SI engine]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Adjust load to maintain constant speed N]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Record Brake Power BP i+1 with cylinder i inoperative]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Re enable cylinder i]
    nodeH --> nodeI{More cylinders remain?}
    nodeI -- Yes --> nodeD
    nodeI -- No --> nodeJ[Compute IP per cylinder as BP1 minus BP i+1]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Sum IP values to get IP total]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Compute FHP = IP total minus BP1]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Compute mechanical efficiency eta mech = BP1 over IP total]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Report Results and Compare with Rated Values]
```

> **Interpretation of the flow:**
> - The diamond nodes (`{...}`) are decision points — the loop iterates over all *k* cylinders.
> - The disable/re-enable pattern guarantees that only **one cylinder is inoperative at any measurement**, satisfying the Morse Test premise.
> - The final reporting stage (`nodeN`) typically includes comparison against Willan's Line or indicator diagrams as a cross-check.

---

## 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram of Energy Streams

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX[Fuel Energy Input Qf] --> nodeY[Indicated Power IP developed in cylinders]
    nodeY --> nodeZ[Brake Power BP measured at shaft]
    nodeY --> nodeW[Friction Power FHP lost to friction and auxiliaries]
    nodeW --> nodeW1[Bearing and piston friction]
    nodeW --> nodeW2[Pumping and gas exchange losses]
    nodeW --> nodeW3[Auxiliaries: oil pump water pump generator]
    nodeZ --> nodeZ1[Dynamometer absorbs BP for measurement]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** the Morse Test aims to separate the IP stream from the FHP stream. By disabling a cylinder, the IP stream shrinks by exactly the IP of that cylinder, while FHP stays (nearly) constant — so the *observed* BP drop equals the *lost* IP.

---

## 4.3 Cylinder-by-Cylinder Power Split (4-Cylinder Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeC1[Cylinder 1 IP1] --> nodeS[Total IP]
    nodeC2[Cylinder 2 IP2] --> nodeS
    nodeC3[Cylinder 3 IP3] --> nodeS
    nodeC4[Cylinder 4 IP4] --> nodeS
    nodeS --> nodeBP[Brake Power BP at output]
    nodeS --> nodeFHP[Friction Power FHP]
    nodeBP --> nodeDyn[Dynamometer]
    nodeFHP --> nodeLoss[Friction Losses]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Instructions to students (as per KTU 2024 ESE pattern):** *Answer in 2–3 sentences, with one supporting equation or diagram where appropriate.*

---

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**Define the Morse Test. State the two principal assumptions on which its validity depends.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Morse Test** is an indirect method of determining the **indicated power (IP)** of a multi-cylinder engine by progressively rendering each cylinder inoperative and measuring the corresponding drop in **brake power (BP)** at constant engine speed.
>
> The IP of any cylinder *i* is computed as:

$$IP_i = BP_1 - BP_{i+1}$$

> where $BP_1$ is the original brake power and $BP_{i+1}$ is the brake power when cylinder *i* is disabled.
>
> **Assumptions:**
> 1. The **friction power remains constant** at the test speed throughout the procedure.
> 2. The **engine speed is held strictly constant** during all measurements, and load conditions are equivalent.
>
> *(Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the IP equation, 1 mark for the two assumptions.)*

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**How is the mechanical efficiency of a multi-cylinder engine determined from the results of a Morse Test?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The mechanical efficiency is computed from the **ratio of brake power to total indicated power**, where the latter is obtained by summing the BP drops measured during the test.
>
> For a *k*-cylinder engine:

$$IP_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k}(BP_1 - BP_{i+1})$$

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP_1}{IP_{total}} \times 100\,\%$$

> *(Award 1 mark for the IP summation formula, 1 mark for the $\eta_{mech}$ equation, 1 mark for stating the correct numerical interpretation.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Instructions to students:** *Each Part B question carries 14 marks and is divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Choose **either** Question A or Question B and answer both sub-parts.*

---

### 🔷 QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder petrol engine was tested at a constant speed of **2400 rpm** using the Morse Test. The original brake power was **$BP_1 = 28$ kW**. With each cylinder cut off in turn, the brake powers recorded were:

- Cylinder 1 cut off → $BP_2 = 19.5$ kW
- Cylinder 2 cut off → $BP_3 = 20.0$ kW
- Cylinder 3 cut off → $BP_4 = 19.0$ kW
- Cylinder 4 cut off → $BP_5 = 19.5$ kW

Calculate the **indicated power of each cylinder** and the **total indicated power**.

**(b)** From the data above, determine the **friction power** and **mechanical efficiency** of the engine. Also discuss briefly why the friction power is **not exactly zero** even though the difference between $IP_{total}$ and $BP_1$ may sometimes appear small in textbook problems.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — IP per cylinder & total IP** *(7 marks)*

Apply the Morse Test formula $IP_i = BP_1 - BP_{i+1}$ to each cylinder:

$$IP_1 = BP_1 - BP_2 = 28.0 - 19.5 = 8.5 \;\text{kW}$$

$$IP_2 = BP_1 - BP_3 = 28.0 - 20.0 = 8.0 \;\text{kW}$$

$$IP_3 = BP_1 - BP_4 = 28.0 - 19.0 = 9.0 \;\text{kW}$$

$$IP_4 = BP_1 - BP_5 = 28.0 - 19.5 = 8.5 \;\text{kW}$$

Total Indicated Power:

$$IP_{total} = IP_1 + IP_2 + IP_3 + IP_4$$

$$IP_{total} = 8.5 + 8.0 + 9.0 + 8.5 = 34.0 \;\text{kW}$$

> *(Award 1.5 marks per cylinder IP and 1 mark for the summation. Total 7 marks.)*

---

**Part (b) — FHP, $\eta_{mech}$ and discussion** *(7 marks)*

Friction Power:

$$FHP = IP_{total} - BP_1 = 34.0 - 28.0 = 6.0 \;\text{kW}$$

Mechanical Efficiency:

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP_1}{IP_{total}} = \frac{28.0}{34.0} = 0.8235 \;\text{or}\; 82.35\,\%$$

**Discussion (3 of the 7 marks):**

In real engines, friction power is never zero because:
1. **Bearing, piston-ring, and valve-train friction** are always present regardless of whether combustion occurs.
2. **Pumping work** for gas exchange (intake + exhaust) persists in the operating cylinders.
3. **Auxiliary drives** (oil pump, water pump, alternator) consume shaft power continuously.
4. The **discrepancy between motoring compression work ($M_i$) and firing IP** of the disabled cylinder introduces a small systematic error. In textbook problems the numbers are often rounded so the FHP appears small, but in practice FHP is typically 8%–25% of IP for gasoline engines and 8%–15% for diesel engines.

> *(Award 2 marks for FHP, 2 marks for $\eta_{mech}$, 3 marks for the qualitative discussion.)*

---

### 🔷 QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a labelled schematic flowchart, **describe the step-by-step procedure** of conducting a Morse Test on a 6-cylinder, 4-stroke diesel engine developing approximately **60 kW** at **2000 rpm**. Clearly state the role of the dynamometer and governor in maintaining test validity.

**(b)** A 6-cylinder, 4-stroke diesel engine has the following Morse Test data at constant speed:

| Condition | BP (kW) |
| :--- | :---: |
| All cylinders firing ($BP_1$) | 60.0 |
| Cyl 1 cut off ($BP_2$) | 51.0 |
| Cyl 2 cut off ($BP_3$) | 50.0 |
| Cyl 3 cut off ($BP_4$) | 51.5 |
| Cyl 4 cut off ($BP_5$) | 50.5 |
| Cyl 5 cut off ($BP_6$) | 51.0 |
| Cyl 6 cut off ($BP_7$) | 50.0 |

Calculate the **mean indicated power per cylinder**, **total indicated power**, **friction power**, and the **mechanical efficiency**.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Procedure and equipment roles** *(7 marks)*

**Step 1:** Start the diesel engine and warm it up to steady-state operating temperature. Maintain cooling water flow and lubricating oil pressure at rated values.

**Step 2:** Couple the engine to an **eddy-current or hydraulic dynamometer**, and adjust the dynamometer loading until the engine delivers rated power (≈ 60 kW) at the rated speed (2000 rpm). The **engine governor** must hold the speed strictly constant.

**Step 3:** Once steady-state is confirmed, record $BP_1$ (original brake power) and confirm speed $N = 2000$ rpm.

**Step 4:** For cylinder 1, close the **individual fuel-cut-off valve** (also called the "morse valve" or individual injector cut-out). This cylinder now compresses air only — no combustion.

**Step 5:** Adjust the dynamometer load slightly (if necessary) so the **engine speed remains at exactly 2000 rpm**. Record the new brake power $BP_2$.
