**Step 6:** Re-open the fuel supply to cylinder 1. Repeat **Step 4–Step 5** for cylinders 2 through 6 sequentially, recording $BP_3$ through $BP_7$.

**Step 7:** Compute IP per cylinder as $IP_i = BP_1 - BP_{i+1}$, sum to get $IP_{total}$, then calculate $FHP$ and $\eta_{mech}$.

**Role of the dynamometer:** Acts as the variable load that absorbs engine output. Allows BP measurement via torque and speed.

**Role of the governor:** Holds the engine speed **strictly constant** so that FHP remains essentially unchanged across all measurements — this is the central assumption of the Morse Test.

> *(Award 2 marks for procedure, 2 marks for equipment roles, 2 marks for the assumption linkage, 1 mark for diagram description.)*

---

**Part (b) — Calculations** *(7 marks)*

**IP of each cylinder:**

$$IP_1 = 60.0 - 51.0 = 9.0 \;\text{kW}$$

$$IP_2 = 60.0 - 50.0 = 10.0 \;\text{kW}$$

$$IP_3 = 60.0 - 51.5 = 8.5 \;\text{kW}$$

$$IP_4 = 60.0 - 50.5 = 9.5 \;\text{kW}$$

$$IP_5 = 60.0 - 51.0 = 9.0 \;\text{kW}$$

$$IP_6 = 60.0 - 50.0 = 10.0 \;\text{kW}$$

**Total IP:**

$$IP_{total} = 9.0 + 10.0 + 8.5 + 9.5 + 9.0 + 10.0 = 56.0 \;\text{kW}$$

**Mean IP per cylinder:**

$$\overline{IP} = \frac{IP_{total}}{6} = \frac{56.0}{6} = 9.333 \;\text{kW}$$

**Friction Power:**

$$FHP = IP_{total} - BP_1 = 56.0 - 60.0 = -4.0 \;\text{kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> A **negative FHP is physically impossible**. This indicates an inconsistency in the test data — most likely the dynamometer reading was not properly zeroed, or the engine speed drifted slightly during the cuts. In an actual exam, if you obtain a negative FHP, **state explicitly** that the result is non-physical and identify the likely experimental cause. Do not silently report it.

**Mechanical Efficiency (using corrected IP estimate from mean):**

If we discard the negative-FHP anomaly and use the mean IP × 6 = 56 kW as a robust estimate:

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP_1}{IP_{total}} = \frac{60.0}{56.0} \approx 1.071 \;\text{or}\; 107\,\%$$

This result is also > 100 %, again pointing to **experimental error**. A proper Morse Test on a healthy diesel engine should give $\eta_{mech}$ in the range **80–90%**. The "ideal" version of this problem would have $IP_{total}$ slightly larger than $BP_1$, e.g., $IP_{total} = 70$ kW giving $\eta_{mech} = 85.7\%$.

> *(Award 1 mark per IP calculation × 6 = 2 marks for IP block; 1 mark for total IP; 1 mark for mean IP; 1 mark for FHP; 2 marks for $\eta_{mech}$ and the discussion of the non-physical result.)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in Morse Test Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to restore full operation** between cylinder cuts — the procedure demands that only *one* cylinder is inoperative at a time. Mixing readings from simultaneous cuts loses full marks.
> 2. **Mixing up $BP_1$ (original)** with $BP_{i+1}$ (BP after cut-off) — students often invert the subtraction and obtain a *negative* IP, which is marked wrong.
> 3. **Ignoring the role of the governor** — without emphasizing *why* constant speed is essential, students lose the conceptual marks tied to the FHP-constancy assumption.
> 4. **Quoting $\eta_{mech}$ as a decimal instead of a percentage** — KTU valuation expects $\eta_{mech}$ as a fraction (e.g., 0.85) **and** optionally as a percent (85%). Always state both.
> 5. **Reporting negative FHP without comment** — silence is treated as a conceptual error. Always interpret anomalous results.
> 6. **Saying "FHP is constant at all loads"** — strictly, FHP is taken as constant at *constant speed*, not constant load. This is a frequently tested distinction.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Morse Test**
>
> ✅ The Morse Test is an **indirect method** to determine IP of multi-cylinder engines.
>
> ✅ It is **based on the constant-FHP assumption** at constant engine speed.
>
> ✅ **Procedure:** Run engine at rated load → cut off fuel/ignition to one cylinder → maintain speed → record BP → repeat for every cylinder.
>
> ✅ **Core formula:** $IP_i = BP_1 - BP_{i+1}$ *(drop in BP equals IP of the disabled cylinder).*
>
> ✅ **Total IP:** $IP_{total} = \sum IP_i = k \cdot BP_1 - \sum BP_{i+1}$.
>
> ✅ **Friction Power:** $FHP = IP_{total} - BP_1$.
>
> ✅ **Mechanical Efficiency:** $\eta_{mech} = BP_1 / IP_{total}$ (typically 75–88% for petrol, 80–92% for diesel).
>
> ✅ **Mandatory equipment:** (i) Engine with individual fuel/ignition cut-off, (ii) Dynamometer for BP measurement, (iii) Speed governor to hold N constant, (iv) Coolant & lubricating-oil systems.
>
> ✅ **Errors to avoid:** speed drift, partial fuel shut-off, throttle-induced pumping changes, ignoring the motoring work term $M_i$.
>
> ✅ **Typical exam numbers:** 4-cylinder (most common), 6-cylinder (industrial), 8-cylinder (advanced).
>
> ✅ **Related methods** (often asked in Module 3 comparison questions): *Willan's Line Method* (graphical, requires fuel-rate vs. BP curve extrapolation) and *Motoring Test* (engine driven by an external motor at rated speed; FHP is the input power).
>
> ✅ **Exam-favorite phrasing:** *"Compare the Morse Test with the Motoring Test"* or *"Discuss the limitations of the Morse Test."* — both expect explicit discussion of the constant-FHP assumption and the small error introduced by the motoring/compression work $M_i$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Balance Test and Retardation Test - Simple Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heat Balance Test and Retardation Test - Simple Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Heat Balance Test — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Balance Test (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> The Heat Balance Test is an experimental performance evaluation of an Internal Combustion (IC) engine in which the **total heat energy supplied by the fuel** is quantitatively accounted for by adding up the useful work output (Brake Power) and all measurable heat losses (cooling water, exhaust gas, and radiation). It is governed by the **First Law of Thermodynamics** (Energy Conservation): *Energy can neither be created nor destroyed, only converted from one form to another.*

Mathematically, the heat balance is expressed as a percentage of heat input:

$$Q_{\text{fuel}} = Q_{\text{BP}} + Q_{\text{water}} + Q_{\text{exhaust}} + Q_{\text{radiation}}$$

where every term is expressed as a percentage of the calorific energy input per unit time.

## 1.2 The Retardation (Morse) Test — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Retardation Test / Morse Test (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> The Retardation Test, commonly known as the **Morse Test**, is an experimental method to determine the **Frictional Power (FP)** of an IC engine by utilizing the engine's own **flywheel as a dynamometer**. The engine is first motored to a rated speed, then the fuel supply is cut off, and the rate of deceleration of the flywheel is observed. From the moment of inertia and deceleration, the frictional power is calculated.

Once FP is known, the **Indicated Power (IP)** is recovered as:

$$IP = BP + FP$$

$$\eta_{\text{mechanical}} = \frac{BP}{IP} = \frac{BP}{BP + FP}$$

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a Tea Kettle on a Gas Stove (Heat Balance Analogy):**
> Imagine boiling 1 litre of water. The energy from the gas (Heat Input) goes into three places:
> 1. Heating the water to boiling and turning it to steam (Useful work / BP)
> 2. Heating the metal kettle body (Heat lost to cooling water jacket)
> 3. The hot steam and gases escaping up the chimney (Heat lost in exhaust)
> 4. A small bit warms the kitchen air (Radiation losses)
>
> A Heat Balance Test simply **adds up all these energy destinations** and confirms they sum to the gas energy supplied. If the numbers don't add up, the test reveals inefficiencies!

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a Spinning Bicycle Wheel (Retardation Analogy):**
> Spin a bicycle wheel fast and let go. It slows down due to axle friction and air drag. By measuring **how fast it slows down**, and knowing the wheel's mass distribution (moment of inertia), you can calculate **how much frictional torque** is acting. The Morse test does exactly this — but for a whole engine flywheel.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard values are universally used in KTU numerical problems and must be memorized:

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Specific heat of water | $C_{pw}$ | **4.18 kJ/kg·K** (or 4.1868) |
| Specific heat of exhaust gas | $C_{pg}$ | **1.005 kJ/kg·K** |
| Air-fuel ratio (for petrol) | A/F | **15:1** (stoichiometric) |
| Density of air (NTP) | $\rho_{air}$ | **1.293 kg/m³** |
| Density of exhaust gas (approx.) | $\rho_{gas}$ | **1.18 kg/m³** |
| Gas constant (universal) | $R$ | **8.314 kJ/kmol·K** |
| Molecular weight of air | $M$ | **28.97 kg/kmol** |
| Specific heat ratio (air) | $\gamma$ | **1.4** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Heat Balance Test — Component-by-Component Theory

The Heat Balance Test is performed on an engine under steady-state operating conditions. The following measurements are recorded:

1. **Fuel consumption rate** ($\dot{m}_f$) in kg/s or kg/min
2. **Calorific Value of fuel** (CV) in kJ/kg
3. **Cooling water flow rate** ($\dot{m}_w$) in kg/s
4. **Cooling water temperatures**: inlet $T_c$, outlet $T_w$ (in °C or K)
5. **Exhaust gas mass flow rate** ($\dot{m}_g$) in kg/s
6. **Exhaust gas temperature** $T_g$ and ambient air temperature $T_a$
7. **Brake Power** (BP) in kW

### 2.1.1 Heat Supplied by Fuel (Heat Input)

$$Q_{\text{fuel}} = \dot{m}_f \times CV \quad [\text{kW or kJ/s}]$$

**Why this formula?** Every kilogram of fuel releases a fixed amount of chemical energy equal to its calorific value when burned completely. The rate of energy input is therefore the rate of mass burning times the energy released per kg.

### 2.1.2 Heat Equivalent of Brake Power (Useful Work)

$$Q_{BP} = BP \quad [\text{kW}]$$

**Why?** Mechanical work, when expressed in SI units, has units of kJ/s = kW, which is dimensionally identical to heat rate. This is the **useful heat** successfully converted to work.

### 2.1.3 Heat Lost to Cooling Water

$$Q_{water} = \dot{m}_w \times C_{pw} \times (T_w - T_c) \quad [\text{kW}]$$

**How?** The water jacket absorbs heat from the cylinder walls (which are heated by combustion). This heat is removed by circulating water, and the temperature rise of the water is a direct measure of the heat absorbed.

### 2.1.4 Heat Lost in Exhaust Gases

The exhaust gas leaves at high temperature, carrying away sensible heat:

$$Q_{exhaust} = \dot{m}_g \times C_{pg} \times (T_g - T_a) \quad [\text{kW}]$$

**Mass flow of exhaust gas** is calculated from air consumption:

$$\dot{m}_g = \dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f \approx \dot{m}_a \quad (\text{since } \dot{m}_f \ll \dot{m}_a)$$

where $\dot{m}_a$ is calculated from volumetric air measurement:

$$\dot{m}_a = \rho_{air} \times \dot{V}_a = \rho_{air} \times A_c \times v_{air}$$

### 2.1.5 Heat Lost by Radiation, Convection & Unaccounted

This is the **balancing figure** that closes the energy equation:

$$Q_{rad} = Q_{\text{fuel}} - (Q_{BP} + Q_{water} + Q_{exhaust})$$

**Why does it exist?** It accounts for incomplete combustion, unmeasured surface convection losses from hot engine parts, and any experimental error.

## 2.2 Retardation (Morse) Test — Theoretical Basis

### 2.2.1 Physical Principle

When the fuel supply is cut off, the engine's flywheel acts as the only energy source. The **kinetic energy** of the flywheel is progressively dissipated against the engine's internal friction (piston rings, bearings, valve mechanism, etc.).

### 2.2.2 Governing Equation of Motion

Applying Newton's second law for rotation:

$$T_f = I \times \alpha = I \times \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

where $I$ is the **moment of inertia** of the flywheel in kg·m², and $\alpha$ is the angular deceleration in rad/s².

The **frictional power** dissipated is:

$$FP = T_f \times \omega = I \times \alpha \times \omega \quad [\text{Watts}]$$

### 2.2.3 Derivation of Standard Deceleration Formula

Let the engine speed drop from $N_1$ to $N_2$ rpm in time $t$ seconds:

- Initial angular velocity: $\omega_1 = \dfrac{2\pi N_1}{60}$
- Final angular velocity: $\omega_2 = \dfrac{2\pi N_2}{60}$
- Average angular velocity: $\omega_{avg} = \dfrac{\pi(N_1 + N_2)}{60}$
- Angular deceleration: $\alpha = \dfrac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{t} = \dfrac{2\pi(N_1 - N_2)}{60\,t}$

Substituting:

$$FP = I \times \frac{2\pi(N_1 - N_2)}{60\,t} \times \frac{\pi(N_1 + N_2)}{60}$$

$$\boxed{FP = \frac{\pi^2 \, I \, (N_1^2 - N_2^2)}{900 \, t} \quad [\text{Watts}]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for KTU Examinations:**
> If $I$ is given in kg·m², $N$ in rpm, and $t$ in seconds, the formula above yields **Watts**. Divide by 1000 to get kW. Some textbooks use slightly different constants — always verify the expected unit from the given problem data.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{\text{fuel}} = \dot{m}_f \times CV$ | Heat input rate | kW |
| 2 | $Q_{BP} = BP$ | Heat converted to brake work | kW |
| 3 | $Q_{water} = \dot{m}_w \times C_{pw} \times (T_w - T_c)$ | Heat lost to jacket water | kW |
| 4 | $Q_{exhaust} = \dot{m}_g \times C_{pg} \times (T_g - T_a)$ | Heat lost in exhaust | kW |
| 5 | $Q_{rad} = Q_{\text{fuel}} - (Q_{BP} + Q_{water} + Q_{exhaust})$ | Radiation / unaccounted loss | kW |
| 6 | $FP = \dfrac{\pi^2 \, I \, (N_1^2 - N_2^2)}{900 \, t}$ | Frictional power from retardation | W |
| 7 | $FP \text{ (kW)} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \, I \, (N_1^2 - N_2^2)}{900\,000 \, t}$ | Same in kW | kW |
| 8 | $IP = BP + FP$ | Indicated power | kW |
| 9 | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP}$ | Mechanical efficiency | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\eta_{brake\,th} = \dfrac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \times CV}$ | Brake thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\eta_{indicated\,th} = \dfrac{IP}{\dot{m}_f \times CV}$ | Indicated thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\dot{m}_a = \rho_{air} \times A \times v$ | Air mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 13 | $\dot{m}_g = \dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f$ | Exhaust mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 14 | $\%\text{Heat share} = \dfrac{Q_i}{Q_{\text{fuel}}} \times 100$ | Heat distribution percentage | % |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this topic critical for practicing engineers?**
> - **Engine Manufacturers (Tata Motors, Mahindra, Ashok Leyland):** Use heat balance tests to optimize cooling system sizing, design efficient exhaust manifolds, and minimize fuel consumption.
> - **Energy Auditors:** Identify wasted energy streams in diesel generator sets and large industrial engines.
> - **Emission Control Designers:** Radiation and exhaust heat loss fractions guide placement of catalytic converters and EGR systems.
> - **Mechanical Design Engineers:** Morse test-derived friction data informs the sizing of crankshaft bearings, piston rings, and lubrication systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example #1 — Heat Balance Test

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Model):**
> The following data were recorded during a heat balance test on a 4-stroke diesel engine:
>
> - Fuel consumption: $\dot{m}_f = 10 \text{ kg/hr}$
> - Calorific value: $CV = 42{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$
> - Brake Power: $BP = 25 \text{ kW}$
> - Cooling water flow rate: $\dot{m}_w = 600 \text{ kg/hr}$
> - Cooling water inlet temperature: $T_c = 25°\text{C}$
> - Cooling water outlet temperature: $T_w = 65°\text{C}$
> - Air consumption: $\dot{m}_a = 240 \text{ kg/hr}$
> - Exhaust gas temperature: $T_g = 400°\text{C}$
> - Ambient temperature: $T_a = 30°\text{C}$
>
> **Draw up the heat balance sheet on a percentage basis.**

### Step 1: Convert All Rates to kW (kJ/s) Basis

**Fuel rate in kg/s:**

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{10}{3600} = 2.778 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Water rate in kg/s:**

$$\dot{m}_w = \frac{600}{3600} = 0.1667 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Air rate in kg/s:**

$$\dot{m}_a = \frac{240}{3600} = 0.06667 \text{ kg/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** Since 1 hour = 3600 seconds, dividing all flow rates by 3600 converts the per-hour rates to per-second rates — necessary to obtain power in kW (which is kJ/s).

### Step 2: Calculate Heat Input from Fuel

$$Q_{\text{fuel}} = \dot{m}_f \times CV$$

$$Q_{\text{fuel}} = 2.778 \times 10^{-3} \times 42{,}000$$

$$Q_{\text{fuel}} = 116.67 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 3: Heat Equivalent of Brake Power

$$Q_{BP} = 25 \text{ kW} \quad \text{(given directly)}$$

### Step 4: Heat Lost to Cooling Water

$$Q_{water} = \dot{m}_w \times C_{pw} \times (T_w - T_c)$$

$$Q_{water} = 0.1667 \times 4.18 \times (65 - 25)$$

$$Q_{water} = 0.1667 \times 4.18 \times 40$$

$$Q_{water} = 27.87 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 5: Mass Flow Rate of Exhaust Gas

$$\dot{m}_g = \dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f$$

$$\dot{m}_g = 0.06667 + 2.778 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\dot{m}_g = 0.06944 \text{ kg/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we add fuel mass to air:** Combustion converts the fuel (which was stored in the cylinder as liquid) into hot combustion products that exit as exhaust. The total mass exiting equals the mass entering (air) plus the mass burned (fuel), by conservation of mass.

### Step 6: Heat Lost in Exhaust Gases

$$Q_{exhaust} = \dot{m}_g \times C_{pg} \times (T_g - T_a)$$

$$Q_{exhaust} = 0.06944 \times 1.005 \times (400 - 30)$$

$$Q_{exhaust} = 0.06944 \times 1.005 \times 370$$

$$Q_{exhaust} = 25.82 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 7: Heat Lost by Radiation / Unaccounted (Balance)

$$Q_{rad} = Q_{\text{fuel}} - (Q_{BP} + Q_{water} + Q_{exhaust})$$

$$Q_{rad} = 116.67 - (25 + 27.87 + 25.82)$$

$$Q_{rad} = 116.67 - 78.69$$

$$Q_{rad} = 37.98 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 8: Convert All Heat Components to Percentage of Input

$$\% Q_i = \frac{Q_i}{Q_{\text{fuel}}} \times 100$$

| Component | Heat (kW) | Percentage |
|---|---|---|
| Heat input from fuel | 116.67 | 100.00 % |
| Heat equivalent of BP | 25.00 | 21.43 % |
| Heat lost to cooling water | 27.87 | 23.89 % |
| Heat lost in exhaust gas | 25.82 | 22.13 % |
| Heat lost by radiation | 37.98 | 32.55 % |
| **TOTAL** | **116.67** | **100.00 %** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Verification Trick:** The percentages must sum to **exactly 100%** (within ±0.1% accounting for rounding). If they don't, recheck your arithmetic — the test is failed otherwise!

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example #2 — Retardation (Morse) Test

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Model):**
> In a retardation test on a 4-stroke petrol engine, the following observations were recorded:
>
> - Moment of inertia of flywheel: $I = 2.5 \text{ kg·m}^2$
> - Initial speed (when fuel cut off): $N_1 = 2400 \text{ rpm}$
> - Final speed: $N_2 = 1800 \text{ rpm}$
> - Time to decelerate from $N_1$ to $N_2$: $t = 12 \text{ seconds}$
> - Brake Power during normal running: $BP = 18 \text{ kW}$
>
> **Determine:**
> (a) Frictional Power
> (b) Indicated Power
> (c) Mechanical Efficiency
> (d) Indicated Thermal Efficiency, if fuel consumption is 7 kg/hr with CV = 44,000 kJ/kg

### Step 1: Apply the Frictional Power Formula

$$FP = \frac{\pi^2 \, I \, (N_1^2 - N_2^2)}{900 \, t}$$

**Calculate $N_1^2 - N_2^2$:**

$$N_1^2 - N_2^2 = (2400)^2 - (1800)^2$$

$$= 5{,}760{,}000 - 3{,}240{,}000$$

$$= 2{,}520{,}000 \text{ rpm}^2$$

**Substitute into the formula:**

$$FP = \frac{\pi^2 \times 2.5 \times 2{,}520{,}000}{900 \times 12}$$

$$FP = \frac{9.8696 \times 2.5 \times 2{,}520{,}000}{10{,}800}$$

$$FP = \frac{62{,}179{,}440}{10{,}800}$$

$$FP = 5757.36 \text{ W} = 5.757 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** $\pi^2 \approx 9.8696$. The 900 in the denominator arises from $(60 \times 60)/4 = 900$, combining the two factors of 60 in the rpm-to-rad/s conversion of both $\alpha$ and $\omega$. This is why the formula uses 900 in the denominator.

### Step 2: Calculate Indicated Power

$$IP = BP + FP$$

$$IP = 18 + 5.757$$

$$IP = 23.757 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Mechanical Efficiency

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{IP}$$

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{18}{23.757}$$

$$\eta_{mech} = 0.7577 = 75.77\%$$

### Step 4: Calculate Indicated Thermal Efficiency

**First, find heat input rate:**

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{7}{3600} = 1.944 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$Q_{\text{fuel}} = 1.944 \times 10^{-3} \times 44{,}000 = 85.56 \text{ kW}$$

**Now, indicated thermal efficiency:**

$$\eta_{ith} = \frac{IP}{Q_{\text{fuel}}}$$

$$\eta_{ith} = \frac{23.757}{85.56}$$

$$\eta_{ith} = 0.2777 = 27.77\%$$

### Final Answer Summary

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Frictional Power (FP) | 5.757 kW |
| Indicated Power (IP) | 23.757 kW |
| Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_{mech}$) | 75.77 % |
| Indicated Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{ith}$) | 27.77 % |

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example #3 — Heat Balance with Retardation Data

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Model - Combined):**
> A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine of bore 80 mm and stroke 100 mm develops a brake power of 30 kW at 3000 rpm. A retardation test gave the following: $I = 1.5 \text{ kg·m}^2$, the engine decelerated from 3150 rpm to 2850 rpm in 8 seconds. The fuel consumption is 9 kg/hr, CV = 44,000 kJ/kg, and the engine uses 15:1 air-fuel ratio by mass. Cooling water flow is 540 kg/hr, with inlet at 30°C and outlet at 75°C. Exhaust temperature is 420°C and ambient is 32°C. **Draw the complete heat balance sheet.**

### Step 1: Compute Frictional Power (Morse Test)

$$N_1^2 - N_2^2 = (3150)^2 - (2850)^2 = 9{,}922{,}500 - 8{,}122{,}500 = 1{,}800{,}000$$

$$FP = \frac{\pi^2 \times 1.5 \times 1{,}800{,}000}{900 \times 8} = \frac{9.8696 \times 1.5 \times 1{,}800{,}000}{7200}$$

$$FP = \frac{26{,}647{,}920}{7200} = 3701 \text{ W} = 3.701 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 2: Indicated Power

$$IP = 30 + 3.701 = 33.701 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 3: Heat Input Rate

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{9}{3600} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$Q_{\text{fuel}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 44{,}000 = 110 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 4: Heat Lost to Cooling Water

$$\dot{m}_w = \frac{540}{3600} = 0.15 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$Q_{water} = 0.15 \times 4.18 \times (75 - 30) = 0.15 \times 4.18 \times 45 = 28.215 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 5: Heat Lost in Exhaust

**Air flow rate:** $\dot{m}_a = 15 \times \dot{m}_f = 15 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0375 \text{ kg/s}$

**Exhaust flow rate:** $\dot{m}_g = \dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f = 0.0375 + 0.0025 = 0.04 \text{ kg/s}$

$$Q_{exhaust} = 0.04 \times 1.005 \times (420 - 32) = 0.04 \times 1.005 \times 388 = 15.598 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 6: Heat Lost by Radiation

$$Q_{rad} = 110 - (33.701 + 28.215 + 15.598) = 110 - 77.514 = 32.486 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 7: Complete Heat Balance Sheet (Percentage Basis)

| Item | Heat (kW) | % of Input |
|---|---|---|
| Heat input (fuel) | 110.000 | 100.00 % |
| Indicated power (IP) | 33.701 | 30.64 % |
| Heat to cooling water | 28.215 | 25.65 % |
| Heat in exhaust gas | 15.598 | 14.18 % |
| Heat by radiation/unaccounted | 32.486 | 29.53 % |
| **TOTAL** | **110.000** | **100.00 %** |

> [!TIP]
> **Note on IP in heat balance:** In some KTU problems, "Heat equivalent of IP" is used in the heat balance sheet (when only IP is known and BP is not separately given). Always re-read the problem — the heat balance can use either BP or IP as the useful work term, depending on which power was measured.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

    nodeB -->|Measure Time t for N1 to N2| nodeC["RECORD Initial Speed N1 rpm"]

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Q: Define Heat Balance Test for an IC engine. List the four main components of the heat balance.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Definition:** A Heat Balance Test is an experimental procedure carried out on an IC engine to quantitatively account for the **distribution of the total heat energy supplied by the fuel** into useful work output and various heat losses. It is based on the **First Law of Thermodynamics (Energy Conservation)**.
>
> **The four main components of a heat balance sheet are:**
> 1. **Heat supplied by the fuel:** $Q_{\text{fuel}} = \dot{m}_f \times CV$
> 2. **Heat equivalent of Brake Power (useful work):** $Q_{BP} = BP$
> 3. **Heat lost to the cooling water (jacket water):** $Q_{water} = \dot{m}_w \times C_{pw} \times (T_w - T_c)$
> 4. **Heat carried away by exhaust gases:** $Q_{exhaust} = \dot{m}_g \times C_{pg} \times (T_g - T_a)$
> 5. **Heat lost by radiation, convection and unaccounted** (balancing figure): $Q_{rad} = Q_{\text{fuel}} - (Q_{BP} + Q_{water} + Q_{exhaust})$
>
> All components are expressed as **percentages of the total heat input**, ensuring the total sums to 100%.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: What is the Retardation Test? Write the formula used to calculate frictional power from this test.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Definition:** The **Retardation Test (Morse Test)** is an experimental method used to determine the **frictional power of an IC engine** by allowing the flywheel to decelerate freely after the fuel supply is cut off, and measuring the rate of speed drop. The flywheel itself acts as a built-in dynamometer.
>
> **Formula for Frictional Power:**
>
> $$FP = \frac{\pi^2 \, I \, (N_1^2 - N_2^2)}{900 \, t}$$
>
> where $I$ = moment of inertia of flywheel in kg·m², $N_1$ and $N_2$ are initial and final speeds in rpm, and $t$ is the time in seconds.
>
> Once FP is known, **Indicated Power** $IP = BP + FP$, and **Mechanical Efficiency** $\eta_{mech} = BP/IP$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Q: A four-stroke single-cylinder diesel engine was tested under steady-state conditions, and the following data were recorded during a heat balance test:**
- Fuel consumption: 8.5 kg/hr
- Calorific value: 43,000 kJ/kg
- Brake power developed: 22 kW
- Cooling water flow rate: 540 kg/hr
- Cooling water temperatures: inlet 28°C, outlet 70°C
- Air consumption: 220 kg/hr
- Exhaust gas temperature: 380°C
- Ambient temperature: 30°C

**Required:**
**(a)** Calculate the **heat lost to cooling water** and **heat lost in exhaust gases** in kW. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Draw the **complete heat balance sheet** on a percentage basis, including radiation losses. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Given Data (converted to SI units per second):**

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{8.5}{3600} = 2.361 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_w = \frac{540}{3600} = 0.15 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_a = \frac{220}{3600} = 0.06111 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Exhaust mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m}_g = \dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f = 0.06111 + 0.002361 = 0.06347 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Heat lost to cooling water:**

$$Q_{water} = \dot{m}_w \times C_{pw} \times (T_w - T_c)$$

$$Q_{water} = 0.15 \times 4.18 \times (70 - 28)$$

$$Q_{water} = 0.15 \times 4.18 \times 42 = 26.33 \text{ kW}$$

**Heat lost in exhaust gases:**

$$Q_{exhaust} = \dot{m}_g \times C_{pg} \times (T_g - T_a)$$

$$Q_{exhaust} = 0.06347 \times 1.005 \times (380 - 30)$$

$$Q_{exhaust} = 0.06347 \times 1.005 \times 350 = 22.33 \text{ kW}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Total heat input from fuel:**

$$Q_{\text{fuel}} = 2.361 \times 10^{-3} \times 43{,}000 = 101.52 \text{ kW}$$

**Heat equivalent of brake power:** $Q_{BP} = 22 \text{ kW}$ (given)

**Heat lost by radiation / unaccounted:**

$$Q_{rad} = Q_{\text{fuel}} - (Q_{BP} + Q_{water} + Q_{exhaust})$$

$$Q_{rad} = 101.52 - (22 + 26.33 + 22.33) = 101.52 - 70.66 = 30.86 \text{ kW}$$

**Heat Balance Sheet (Percentage Basis):**

| Item | Heat (kW) | % of Input |
|---|---|---|
| Heat supplied by fuel | 101.52 | 100.00 % |
| Heat equivalent of BP | 22.00 | 21.67 % |
| Heat lost to cooling water | 26.33 | 25.94 % |
| Heat lost in exhaust gases | 22.33 | 22.00 % |
| Heat lost by radiation/unaccounted | 30.86 | 30.39 % |
| **TOTAL** | **101.52** | **100.00 %** |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: A retardation test was performed on a 4-stroke single-cylinder petrol engine with the following observations:**
- Moment of inertia of flywheel: $I = 1.8 \text{ kg·m}^2$
- Engine speed dropped from 2700 rpm to 2100 rpm in 10 seconds
- Brake power during normal running: 20 kW
- Fuel consumption: 6 kg/hr
- Calorific value: 44,500 kJ/kg

**Required:**
**(a)** Calculate the **frictional power**, **indicated power**, and **mechanical efficiency** of the engine. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Find the **brake thermal efficiency** and **indicated thermal efficiency** of the engine, and comment on the engine's performance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Frictional Power**

$$N_1^2 - N_2^2 = (2700)^2 - (2100)^2 = 7{,}290{,}000 - 4{,}410{,}000 = 2{,}880{,}000$$

$$FP = \frac{\pi^2 \times 1.8 \times 2{,}880{,}000}{900 \times 10}$$

$$FP = \frac{9.8696 \times 1.8 \times 2{,}880{,}000}{9000}$$

$$FP = \frac{51{,}162{,}000}{9000} = 5684.7 \text{ W} = 5.685 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2: Indicated Power**

$$IP = BP + FP = 20 + 5.685 = 25.685 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Mechanical Efficiency**

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{IP} = \frac{20}{25.685} = 0.7786 = 77.86\%$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Heat input rate**

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{6}{3600} = 1.667 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$Q_{\text{fuel}} = 1.667 \times 10^{-3} \times 44{,}500 = 74.17 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2: Brake thermal efficiency**

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{Q_{\text{fuel}}} = \frac{20}{74.17} = 0.2697 = 26.97\%$$

**Step 3: Indicated thermal efficiency**

$$\eta_{ith} = \frac{IP}{Q_{\text{fuel}}} = \frac{25.685}{74.17} = 0.3464 = 34.64\%$$

**Step 4: Performance Comment**

The mechanical efficiency of 77.86% is **typical for a well-maintained small petrol engine**. The indicated thermal efficiency of 34.64% is reasonable for the Otto cycle. The difference between indicated and brake thermal efficiencies (≈ 7.67%) represents the frictional loss — primarily from piston rings, crankshaft bearings, valve train, and auxiliary drives (oil pump, water pump, generator). A higher mechanical efficiency indicates **better lubrication, lower ring wear, and reduced bearing friction**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error (Most Common!):** Failing to convert kg/hr to kg/s. This causes a 3600× error in heat values. **Always divide by 3600 first.**
> 2. **Exhaust Mass Flow Error:** Students often use $\dot{m}_a$ instead of $\dot{m}_g = \dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f$ in the exhaust heat equation. The fuel mass does enter the exhaust.
> 3. **Temperature Difference Mistake:** Using $T_w$ and $T_c$ in °C gives the same numerical difference as in Kelvin — that part is safe. But never mix °C and K in gas constant calculations.
> 4. **Retardation Formula Constant:** Using 900 in the denominator for kW without adjusting. The formula $FP = \pi^2 I (N_1^2 - N_2^2)/900t$ gives **Watts** (with I in kg·m², N in rpm, t in seconds). Divide by 1000 for kW.
> 5. **Sign Convention:** Radiation loss must be **positive** (it is a loss). If your calculation gives a negative $Q_{rad}$, it indicates either arithmetic error or that you've included BP twice.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exams:**

- [ ] **Heat Balance Equation** (must memorize verbatim): $Q_{\text{fuel}} = Q_{BP} + Q_{water} + Q_{exhaust} + Q_{rad}$
- [ ] **Specific heat of water** = 4.18 kJ/kg·K (always — sometimes 4.1868 in precise problems)
- [ ] **Specific heat of exhaust gas** = 1.005 kJ/kg·K (assume same as air)
- [ ] **Air-fuel ratio** for stoichiometric petrol = 15:1; for diesel ≈ 18:1
- [ ] **Exhaust mass flow** = air flow + fuel flow (not just air flow!)
- [ ] **Percentage conversion** is essential — examiner awards marks for the percentage table
- [ ] **Morse/Retardation formula** = $\pi^2 I (N_1^2 - N_2^2)/(900t)$ in **Watts** with SI consistent units
- [ ] **IP = BP + FP** is the cornerstone relationship — once FP is found, IP follows
- [ ] **$\eta_{mech}$ = BP/IP** — typically 75% to 90% for healthy engines
- [ ] **$\eta_{bth}$ = BP/($\dot{m}_f \times CV$)** — typically 20% to 35% for petrol, 30% to 45% for diesel
- [ ] **$\eta_{ith}$ = IP/($\dot{m}_f \times CV$)** — slightly higher than $\eta_{bth}$
- [ ] **Assumption in Retardation Test:** Frictional torque remains **constant** over the small speed range $N_1$ to $N_2$ — this is a standard KTU assumption
- [ ] **Order of magnitude check:** For a small engine with I = 1-2 kg·m², FP typically lies in 4-10 kW range
- [ ] **Verification:** Heat balance percentages **must sum to 100%** (or very close due to rounding)
- [ ] **Retardation Test Limitations:** Cannot be done with very low moment-of-inertia flywheels; assumes constant friction; needs accurate time measurement
- [ ] **Difference between Heat Balance and Morse Test:** Heat Balance finds **how energy is distributed**; Morse Test finds **how much power is lost to internal friction**
- [ ] **In KTU, you will likely be given one or the other problem type, not both combined** — but Example 3 (Section 3.3) shows how to integrate both in a single comprehensive question
- [ ] **Always show units throughout** — partial credit is given for correct methodology even if the final number is wrong
- [ ] **For heat balance percentage problems**, present the final answer in a **table** — examiners love well-organized answers
- [ ] **For retardation problems**, draw the **free body diagram** of the decelerating flywheel if the problem allows — adds marks
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuels for IC Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fuels for IC Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FUELS FOR IC ENGINES — Canonical Definition**
> A *fuel* for an Internal Combustion (IC) engine is a chemical substance (solid, liquid, or gaseous) that, upon controlled combustion with an oxidizer (typically atmospheric oxygen), releases **thermal energy** in the form of heat. This heat is subsequently converted into **mechanical work** inside the engine cylinder through the thermodynamic expansion of high-temperature gases. For SI engines and CI engines alike, the fuel must satisfy strict physico-chemical property windows (volatility, cetane/octane rating, calorific value, viscosity, etc.) to ensure **smooth ignition**, **complete combustion**, and **minimum exhaust emissions**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine your kitchen LPG cylinder. The liquefied petroleum gas (a mixture of **butane** and **propane**) escapes, mixes with air inside your gas stove, and a spark (from the auto-igniter) sets it ablaze. The heat boils your water. Now scale that tiny flame up to the size of a fist — and enclose it inside a sealed steel cylinder with a piston. That is exactly how an IC engine works: **fuel + air + ignition = expanding gas → pushing a piston → rotary motion.**

The *trick* is that different engines prefer different "kinds of fire":
- A **petrol (SI) engine** likes fuel that *resists* spontaneous explosion (high octane number).
- A **diesel (CI) engine** likes fuel that *self-ignites easily* under high compression (high cetane number).

### 1.3 Classification of IC Engine Fuels (At a Glance)

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Primary States of Matter for IC Engine Fuels**

| State | Examples | Typical Engine Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Solid** | Coal, Wood, Biomass, Charcoal | Historical / experimental gas producers |
| **Liquid** | Petrol (Gasoline), Diesel, Kerosene, Alcohols, Biodiesel | **SI & CI Engines (Primary)** |
| **Gaseous** | CNG, LPG, Hydrogen, Biogas, Producer Gas | SI Engines (CNG/LPG), Dual-fuel CI |

### 1.4 Essential Properties — Definitions Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Fuel Property Definitions (Exam-Relevant)**

- **Calorific Value (CV):** The amount of heat released per unit mass (or volume) of fuel upon **complete combustion**. Units: **kJ/kg** (mass basis) or **kJ/m³** (volume basis).
- **Flash Point:** The *lowest* temperature at which fuel vapor produces a momentary flash (ignites) when an external flame is brought near it. **Critical for safety storage.**
- **Fire Point:** The temperature at which the vapor burns continuously for at least **5 seconds**.
- **Cloud Point & Pour Point:** The temperatures at which wax crystals first appear (cloud) and the fuel ceases to flow (pour) — vital for cold-climate diesel operation.
- **Cetane Number (CN):** Measure of **ignition quality** of diesel fuel (CI engines). Higher CN = shorter ignition delay.
- **Octane Number (ON):** Measure of **knock resistance** of petrol (SI engines). Higher ON = greater resistance to detonation.
- **Viscosity:** Resistance to flow; affects **spray atomization** in injectors.
- **Specific Gravity:** Density relative to water at standard conditions (**15.6 °C**).
- **Carbon Residue (Conradson):** Tendency of fuel to form carbon deposits in combustion chamber.
- **Sulphur Content:** Impurity; contributes to **acid rain** and engine corrosion.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework: Why Fuel Properties Govern Engine Behavior

The IC engine is essentially a **chemical-to-mechanical transducer**. The thermodynamic efficiency (Carnot-limited) and actual indicated/thermal efficiency depend heavily on how *cleanly and completely* the fuel-air mixture burns. Therefore, every fuel property is a *design constraint* that engineers must respect.

#### 2.1.1 Calorific Value — The Energy Currency

Two types are distinguished:
- **Higher Calorific Value (HCV / Gross CV):** Heat released assuming **all water vapor in products condenses** to liquid (latent heat recovered).
- **Lower Calorific Value (LCV / Net CV):** Heat released when water leaves as **vapor** (latent heat NOT recovered). For IC engines, **LCV is the practically useful value** because exhaust gases always leave at >100 °C.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Relationship:**
> $$ \text{LCV} = \text{HCV} - 9 \times H \times 2442 \; \text{kJ/kg} $$
> where $H$ is the **mass fraction of hydrogen** in the fuel (decimal), and the factor 2442 kJ/kg is the latent heat of vaporization of water.

#### 2.1.2 Octane Number (ON) — The SI Engine's Primary Metric

- Determined by **CFR Engine Test** (Cooperative Fuel Research).
- **Reference Fuels:** Iso-octane (C₈H₁₈) assigned **ON = 100** (high knock resistance). n-Heptane (C₇H₁₆) assigned **ON = 0** (knocks violently).
- A fuel with **ON = X** knocks the same as a blend of X% iso-octane + (100−X)% n-heptane.

#### 2.1.3 Cetane Number (CN) — The CI Engine's Primary Metric

- **Reference Fuels:** n-Cetane (C₁₆H₃₄) assigned **CN = 100** (ignites rapidly). α-Methylnaphthalene assigned **CN = 0** (very poor ignition).
- A fuel with **CN = Y** behaves like Y% cetane + (100−Y)% α-methylnaphthalene.

> [!WARNING]
> **Inverse Trend:** ON and CN are roughly *inversely related*. A fuel with high CN typically has *low* ON, and vice versa. Aromatics (benzene, toluene) have high ON but very low CN.

#### 2.1.4 Knock Resistance — Physical Explanation

- **SI Knock (Detonation):** After spark ignition, the end-gas (unburnt mixture ahead of flame front) may auto-ignite due to high temperature/pressure, causing two flame fronts to collide → **sharp pressure spike** → "pinging" sound. Higher ON = greater resistance.
- **CI Engine "Diesel Knock":** Caused by **excessive ignition delay** — too much fuel accumulates before combustion starts, then burns all at once → rough running. Higher CN = shorter delay = smoother combustion.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Save This Table for Last-Minute Revision**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{LCV} = \text{HCV} - 9H \cdot h_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | Net energy available in engine |
| 2 | $\text{Air-Fuel Ratio (Stoich.)} = \dfrac{11.6 \, C}{1} + \dfrac{34.8(H - O/8)}{1} + \dfrac{4.35 \, S}{1}$ (mass) | kg air/kg fuel | Combustion calculations |
| 3 | $\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{indicated}}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LCV}}$ | dimensionless | Engine efficiency |
| 4 | $\text{Mean Effective Pressure} = \dfrac{W_{\text{indicated}}}{V_s}$ | kPa | Performance comparison |
| 5 | $\text{Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{W_{\text{brake}}}$ | kg/kWh | Economy metric |
| 6 | $\text{ON} \leftrightarrow \text{CN Trade-off}$ | — | Fuel selection logic |
| 7 | $\rho_{\text{fuel}} = \text{SG} \times \rho_{\text{water}}$ (at 15.6 °C) | kg/m³ | Volume ↔ mass conversion |
| 8 | $\text{Volumetric CV} = \text{Mass CV} \times \rho_{\text{fuel}}$ | kJ/m³ | Comparing gaseous/liquid fuels |
| 9 | $\text{DuLong's Formula for HCV: } \text{HCV} = 33800\,C + 144200\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 9410\,S$ | kJ/kg | Theoretical CV from ultimate analysis |
| 10 | $\text{Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP)}$ | kPa | Volatility index for petrol |

> **Notation:** $C, H, O, S$ = mass fractions of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, sulphur in fuel (ultimate analysis). $h_{fg} = 2442$ kJ/kg (latent heat of water at 25 °C).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries** blend multiple hydrocarbon streams to hit *target octane numbers* (e.g., Regular 91, Premium 95 in India).
- **Additive chemistry** (MTBE, ethanol, TEL — now banned in many countries) raises ON.
- **Cold-flow improvers** in diesel suppress wax crystallization to lower **pour point**.
- **Biodiesel (FAME)** offers high CN (~50–60) but poor low-temperature flow — limiting factor for cold climates.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio from Ultimate Analysis

Consider 1 kg of fuel containing mass fractions $C$, $H$, $O$, $S$ of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, sulphur respectively, where:
$$ C + H + O + S + N + \text{Ash} = 1 $$

Assume complete combustion. The combustion reactions (per kg of each element) are:

**Carbon oxidation:**
$$ \text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad;\quad \text{Mass ratio} = \frac{32}{12} = \dfrac{8}{3} $$

**Hydrogen oxidation:**
$$ 2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad;\quad \text{Mass ratio} = \dfrac{32}{4} = 8 $$

**Sulphur oxidation:**
$$ \text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2 \quad;\quad \text{Mass ratio} = \dfrac{32}{32} = 1 $$

**Oxygen correction:** A fraction of fuel-bound oxygen is *already* available, so external O₂ required is reduced. Oxygen present in fuel = $O$ kg, equivalent O₂ = $O/32$ kg-mol × 32 = $O$ kg of O₂ bound, but it appears as O atoms in the fuel — the available "free" oxygen equivalent is $O/8$ kg of O₂ (since 1 kg-mol O₂ = 32 kg, and the fuel carries O atoms at 16 kg/kg-mol; thus $O$ kg of O corresponds to $O/8$ kg of O₂ effectively usable externally... correct via the 8 in denominator).

**Mass of O₂ required per kg of fuel:**
$$ m_{O_2} = \frac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O $$

Air is approximately 23.2% O₂ by mass (or 21% by volume), so mass of air:
$$ m_{\text{air,stoich}} = \frac{m_{O_2}}{0.232} = 4.31 \cdot m_{O_2} $$

**Therefore, the stoichiometric mass A/F ratio:**
$$ (A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{4.31 \left( \frac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O \right)}{1} $$

Simplifying numerically:
$$ (A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = 11.6\,C + 34.8\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 4.35\,S \quad \text{[kg air / kg fuel]} $$

**Engineering justification of coefficients:**
- $4.31 \times \frac{8}{3} = 11.49 \approx 11.6$ (carbon coefficient)
- $4.31 \times 8 = 34.48 \approx 34.8$ (hydrogen coefficient, with oxygen correction)
- $4.31 \times 1 = 4.31 \approx 4.35$ (sulphur coefficient)

> **Worked Example — Typical Petrol (Gasoline):** Ultimate analysis by mass: C = 0.85, H = 0.15, O = 0, S = 0.
> $$ (A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = 11.6(0.85) + 34.8(0.15) = 9.86 + 5.22 = 15.08 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel} $$
> Matches the textbook value of ~14.7–15 for gasoline.

### 3.2 Derivation: HCV from DuLong's Formula (for solid/liquid fuels)

Starting from the heats of combustion of the elements (per kg) and the stoichiometric O₂ requirements:

$$ \text{HCV} = 33800\,C + 144200\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 9410\,S \quad [\text{kJ/kg}] $$

**Coefficient origins (per kg of each element):**
- C → CO₂ releases **33800 kJ/kg of C** (since 12 kg C → 44 kg CO₂, and standard enthalpy of CO₂ formation ≈ −393.5 kJ/mol, hence 393.5×1000/12 ≈ 32792; refined to 33800 with mean bond energies).
- H → H₂O releases **144200 kJ/kg of H** (2 kg H → 18 kg H₂O; 285.8 kJ/mol × 1000 / 2 ≈ 142900, refined to 144200).
- O correction: every 8 kg of O in fuel *displaces* 1 kg-mol of O₂, equivalent to $144200/8 = 18025$ kJ/kg-O saved → term $144200(O/8)$ subtracted.
- S → SO₂ releases **9410 kJ/kg of S** (32 kg S → 64 kg SO₂; ~297 kJ/mol × 1000/32 ≈ 9281, refined).

**Worked Example — Coal with** $C=0.78, H=0.05, O=0.10, S=0.01$:
$$ \text{HCV} = 33800(0.78) + 144200(0.05 - 0.10/8) + 9410(0.01) $$
$$ = 26364 + 144200(0.05 - 0.0125) + 94.1 $$
$$ = 26364 + 144200(0.0375) + 94.1 $$
$$ = 26364 + 5407.5 + 94.1 = 31865.6 \;\text{kJ/kg} $$

This is realistic for a **bituminous coal** (typical HCV: 25 000–35 000 kJ/kg).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: SI vs CI Engine Fuel Requirements

| Property | SI Engine (Petrol) | CI Engine (Diesel) |
|---|---|---|
| Ignition trigger | Spark plug | Auto-ignition by compression |
| Key rating | **Octane Number** (≥ 91 India) | **Cetane Number** (45–55) |
| Volatility | High (good vaporization) | Moderate (forms spray) |
| Viscosity | Low | Moderate (lubricates injectors) |
| Sulphur | < 50 ppm (BS-VI) | < 10 ppm (BS-VI) |
| Density | 720–780 kg/m³ | 820–860 kg/m³ |
| Flash Point | < –30 °C (safety hazard) | > 55 °C (safer storage) |
| Fire Point | Above flash | Above flash by ~5–10 °C |

### 3.4 Comparative Merits of Alternative Fuels

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU 2024:** The syllabus places increasing emphasis on **sustainable and alternative fuels** for IC engines.

| Fuel | CN | ON | LCV (kJ/kg) | Merits | Demerits |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Petrol** | 8–14 | 90–98 | 44 000 | High power, smooth | CO₂ emissions, finite |
| **Diesel** | 45–55 | < 25 | 42 500 | High efficiency | NOx, particulates |
| **CNG** | — | 120–130 | 50 000 (vol) | Clean, cheap | Tank volume, range anxiety |
| **LPG** | — | 100 | 46 000 | Clean | Cold-start issues |
| **Ethanol** | — | 108 | 26 900 | Renewable | Low energy density, corrosion |
| **Biodiesel (FAME)** | 50–60 | — | 37 000 | Renewable, lubricity | Poor cold flow, NOₓ rise |
| **Hydrogen** | — | > 130 | 120 000 | Zero CO₂ | Storage, knock tendency, cost |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PBMET604]**
**Define Calorific Value. Differentiate between HCV and LCV. Why is LCV preferred in IC engine calculations?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Calorific Value (1 mark):** The amount of heat released per unit mass (or volume) of a fuel upon *complete combustion* with oxygen, under standard conditions (25 °C, 101.325 kPa). Units: kJ/kg or kJ/m³.
> - **HCV vs LCV (1 mark):** HCV assumes water vapor in products *condenses* (latent heat recovered). LCV assumes water leaves as *vapor* (latent heat not recovered). Numerically, **LCV = HCV − 9 × H × 2442 kJ/kg**.
> - **Why LCV in IC engines (1 mark):** In a real engine, exhaust gases always leave at temperatures **above 100 °C** (typically 400–700 °C), so the water vapor *cannot condense* inside the cylinder. The latent heat of vaporization is therefore *not recovered* as useful work → LCV is the practically available energy.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PBMET604]**
**Define Cetane Number. State its significance for CI engines and explain how it is determined.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Definition (1 mark):** Cetane Number is a measure of the **ignition quality** of a diesel fuel — specifically the *shortness of the ignition delay* period in a CI engine. Higher CN → faster auto-ignition → smoother combustion.
> - **Significance (1 mark):** A diesel fuel with insufficient CN (typically < 40) causes **diesel knock**, rough engine operation, hard starting in cold weather, and excessive smoke. A fuel with very high CN (> 55) may cause incomplete combustion and power loss. **Optimal CN for road diesel: 45–55.**
> - **Determination (1 mark):** Measured in a **CFR (Cooperative Fuel Research) single-cylinder test engine** under standard conditions. The test fuel's ignition delay is matched against blends of two reference fuels: **n-cetane (CN = 100)** and **α-methylnaphthalene (CN = 0)**. The CN of the test fuel = volume % of n-cetane in the matching blend.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Fuel Analysis Problem**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, PBMET604 Model Paper]**

> A sample of coal has the following ultimate analysis by mass:
> Carbon = 78 %, Hydrogen = 5 %, Oxygen = 10 %, Sulphur = 2 %, Nitrogen = 1.5 %, Ash = 3.5 %.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the **stoichiometric air required** for complete combustion of 1 kg of this coal. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Determine the **Higher and Lower Calorific Values** of the coal using DuLong's formula. Discuss why LCV is used for IC engine efficiency calculations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(a) — Stoichiometric Air (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Identify mass fractions (per 1 kg of fuel):
$$ C = 0.78, \; H = 0.05, \; O = 0.10, \; S = 0.02 $$
(Nitrogen and ash do not participate in combustion.)

**Step 2:** Use the stoichiometric A/F formula:
$$ (A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = 11.6\,C + 34.8\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 4.35\,S $$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values:
$$ (A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = 11.6(0.78) + 34.8\left(0.05 - \dfrac{0.10}{8}\right) + 4.35(0.02) $$

**Step 4:** Evaluate each term:
- $11.6 \times 0.78 = 9.048$
- $34.8 \times (0.05 - 0.0125) = 34.8 \times 0.0375 = 1.305$
- $4.35 \times 0.02 = 0.087$

**Step 5:** Sum the terms:
$$ (A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = 9.048 + 1.305 + 0.087 = 10.44 \;\text{kg air / kg fuel} $$

**Step 6:** State the answer:
$$ \boxed{m_{\text{air,stoich}} = 10.44 \;\text{kg of air per kg of coal} } $$

> **Examiner's note (1 mark):** Many students forget the *oxygen correction* term $O/8$. Always check if the fuel contains chemically bound oxygen (coal, biomass, alcohols) and *subtract* the corresponding O₂ requirement.

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(b) — Calorific Values (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Apply DuLong's formula for HCV:
$$ \text{HCV} = 33800\,C + 144200\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 9410\,S \quad[\text{kJ/kg}] $$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:
$$ \text{HCV} = 33800(0.78) + 144200(0.05 - 0.0125) + 9410(0.02) $$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:
- $33800 \times 0.78 = 26364$ kJ/kg
- $144200 \times 0.0375 = 5407.5$ kJ/kg
- $9410 \times 0.02 = 188.2$ kJ/kg

**Step 4:** Sum:
$$ \text{HCV} = 26364 + 5407.5 + 188.2 = 31959.7 \;\text{kJ/kg} $$

**Step 5:** Apply the LCV relation:
$$ \text{LCV} = \text{HCV} - 9 \times H \times 2442 $$
$$ \text{LCV} = 31959.7 - 9 \times 0.05 \times 2442 $$
$$ \text{LCV} = 31959.7 - 1098.9 = 30860.8 \;\text{kJ/kg} $$

**Step 6:** Final answers:
$$ \boxed{\text{HCV} \approx 31\,960 \;\text{kJ/kg}, \quad \text{LCV} \approx 30\,861 \;\text{kJ/kg}} $$

**Discussion (1 mark):** In IC engine calculations, **LCV is used** because the exhaust gases leave the cylinder at high temperatures (typically 500–800 °C), well above the dew point of water. Therefore, the latent heat of condensation of water vapor **cannot be recovered** as useful work — it is lost with the exhaust. Only the *sensible* heat contribution (HCV minus 9H·h_fg) is converted to mechanical energy, which is exactly the LCV.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Conceptual + Numerical Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, PBMET604 Model Paper]**

> **(a)** What is **Octane Number**? Describe the **CFR Engine Test** method with reference to iso-octane and n-heptane. Explain how the octane requirement of an SI engine varies with **compression ratio**, and what additives are commonly used to enhance octane rating. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A certain gasoline has a measured octane number of **88**. The fuel has a calorific value of **44 000 kJ/kg**, and during a test the engine consumes **0.30 kg/hr** of fuel while producing **10 kW** of brake power. Calculate: **(i)** the **brake thermal efficiency**, and **(ii)** the **brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC)**. Comment on whether this efficiency is typical for an SI engine. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(a) — Octane Number & CFR Test (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):** Octane Number is a dimensionless index that quantifies the **knock resistance** of a spark-ignition engine fuel. A fuel of octane number X knocks with the same intensity as a blend of X% iso-octane (assigned ON = 100, very high knock resistance) and (100−X)% n-heptane (assigned ON = 0, knocks severely).

**CFR Engine Test (3 marks):**
- Test apparatus: A **single-cylinder, variable-compression-ratio CFR engine** with a continuously adjustable cylinder head.
- The engine is operated at standard conditions: **600 rpm**, intake air at 52 °C, coolant at 100 °C, spark advance set by a knock-meter.
- **Procedure:** The test fuel's compression ratio is increased (or decreased) until *standard knock intensity* is reached (defined as a knock-meter reading of 55 mm on a 0–100 scale).
- Then, a blend of iso-octane + n-heptane is tested under identical conditions; the % of iso-octane in the matching blend = the **Octane Number** of the test fuel.
- Two ratings are reported: **RON (Research Octane Number)** — test at 600 rpm, mild conditions; **MON (Motor Octane Number)** — test at 900 rpm, more severe. **Anti-Knock Index (AKI)** in India = (RON + MON)/2.

**Octane vs Compression Ratio (1 mark):** As the **compression ratio (r)** of an SI engine increases, peak cylinder temperature and pressure rise sharply, making the end-gas more prone to auto-ignition. Hence, higher r demands *higher* ON fuel (e.g., r = 8:1 needs ~87 ON, r = 12:1 needs ~98 ON). This is why high-performance engines (e.g., sports cars) mandate premium (95+) fuel.

**Additives to enhance ON (1 mark):** Historically, **tetraethyl lead (TEL)** was used. Modern BS-VI regulations have banned leaded petrol. Current alternatives include:
- **MTBE (Methyl tert-butyl ether)** — raises ON by 7–10 points.
- **Ethanol (E10, E20)** — raises ON by 3–6 points.
- **Toluene / Xylene / Aromatics** — high ON blending components.
- **MMT (Methylcyclopentadienyl manganese tricarbonyl)** — metallic anti-knock.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(b) — Performance Calculations (7 marks)**

**Given:** Fuel CV = 44 000 kJ/kg; fuel consumption $\dot{m}_f$ = 0.30 kg/hr; brake power $P_b$ = 10 kW.

**Step 1 (i):** Compute brake thermal efficiency:
$$ \eta_{b,\text{thermal}} = \frac{\text{Useful work output per second}}{\text{Energy input per second}} = \frac{P_b}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV}} $$

**Step 2:** Convert fuel rate to kg/s:
$$ \dot{m}_f = \frac{0.30}{3600} = 8.333 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{kg/s} $$

**Step 3:** Energy input rate:
$$ \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV} = 8.333 \times 10^{-5} \times 44\,000 = 3.667 \;\text{kW} $$

**Step 4:** Brake thermal efficiency:
$$ \eta_{b,\text{thermal}} = \frac{10}{3.667} = 2.727 \;\text{or}\;\boxed{27.27\%} $$

**Step 5 (ii):** Compute BSFC:
$$ \text{BSFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b} = \frac{0.30 \;\text{kg/hr}}{10 \;\text{kW}} = 0.03 \;\text{kg/kWh} = 300 \;\text{g/kWh} $$

**Step 6:** Verify unit:
$$ \text{BSFC} = \frac{8.333 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{kg/s}}{10 \;\text{kW}} = 8.333 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{kg/J} \times 3.6 \times 10^{6} = 300 \;\text{g/kWh} $$
$$ \boxed{\text{BSFC} = 300 \;\text{g/kWh}} $$

**Step 7: Comment on typical efficiency (1 mark):**
A brake thermal efficiency of ~27% is **typical for a naturally aspirated SI engine** at part-load. Modern SI engines achieve 25–35% (Otto-cycle theoretical limit at r = 10 is ~60%, but real engines lose much to heat transfer, friction, and incomplete combustion). The BSFC of 300 g/kWh also lies in the normal range (200–400 g/kWh for SI engines). So this engine is operating reasonably, though there is room for improvement via turbocharging or hybridisation.

> **Note:** Students sometimes forget to convert kg/hr → kg/s, leading to dimensional errors. Always write the units explicitly at every step.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in 'Fuels for IC Engines' Problems**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Oxygen Correction in A/F:** When the fuel contains bound O (alcohols, coal, biomass, biodiesel), the term $O/8$ MUST be subtracted. Missing this costs ~1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing HCV and LCV:** Writing the wrong formula (e.g., using HCV for engine efficiency) shows lack of conceptual clarity — full 1 mark deduction.
> 3. **Mixing up Octane vs Cetane:** Many students state "high ON is good for diesel" — this is the *opposite* of correct. Diesel needs **high CN**, which generally corresponds to **low ON**.
> 4. **Unit Mistakes:** Failing to convert kg/hr to kg/s when computing efficiency. Use S.I. units consistently.
> 5. **Ignoring Sulphur in DuLong's formula:** Even small S content (1–2%) contributes; do not drop it.
> 6. **No discussion of real-engine applicability:** Just computing LCV is not enough; always state *why* LCV is used (latent heat of water vapor lost in exhaust).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [x] **IC engine fuel** = substance that releases heat via combustion → converted to mechanical work.
- [x] **Three states:** Solid, Liquid (primary for SI/CI), Gaseous (CNG, LPG, H₂).
- [x] **Calorific Value** = energy released per unit mass/volume. **HCV** includes latent heat of water; **LCV** does not. Use **LCV** for engine work calculations.
- [x] **Octane Number (ON)** = knock resistance for SI engines. Higher ON = better anti-knock. Measured via **CFR engine** against iso-octane (100) and n-heptane (0).
- [x] **Cetane Number (CN)** = ignition quality for CI engines. Higher CN = shorter ignition delay = smoother combustion. Measured against n-cetane (100) and α-methylnaphthalene (0).
- [x] **Flash Point < Fire Point < Auto-ignition Temperature.** Order is critical for safety classifications.
- [x] **Stoichiometric A/F ratio** formula: $(A/F)_s = 11.6C + 34.8(H - O/8) + 4.35S$ — apply oxygen correction when fuel contains bound O.
- [x] **DuLong's formula:** $\text{HCV} = 33800C + 144200(H - O/8) + 9410S$ kJ/kg.
- [x] **$\eta_{\text{thermal}}$** = (work output) / (fuel energy input) — typical SI: 25–35%, CI: 35–45%.
- [x] **BSFC** = fuel rate per unit power (g/kWh or kg/kWh) — lower = more efficient.
- [x] **Alternative fuels** — CNG, LPG, ethanol, biodiesel, hydrogen — each with trade-offs in energy density, storage, and emissions.
- [x] **Aromatics** (benzene, toluene) → high ON, low CN. **Paraffins** (n-alkanes) → low ON, high CN.
- [x] **Sulphur** → forms SO₂ → acid rain → catalyst poisoning → corrosion. Modern regulations (BS-VI / Euro 6) demand < 10 ppm S in diesel.
- [x] **Vapour Lock risk in petrol** (RVP too high in summer) and **waxing in winter diesel** (high pour point) are real-world problems governed by volatility and cold-flow properties.
- [x] **Safety hierarchy:** Petrol (highly volatile, low flash) > Ethanol > Kerosene > Diesel > Fuel Oil. Storage and transport regulations follow this order.
- [x] **BSFC comparison heuristic:** SI engine: 250–400 g/kWh; CI engine: 200–300 g/kWh. CI inherently more fuel-efficient due to higher compression ratio and lean-burn operation.

---

**End of Section 5 — All KTU 2024 Module 4 learning outcomes for 'Fuels for IC Engines' have been addressed.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ignition Limits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ignition Limits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ignition Limits** define the boundary conditions (in terms of temperature, pressure, and fuel-oxidizer concentration) within which a combustible mixture can undergo self-sustaining combustion. Outside these limits, the mixture is either too lean, too rich, too cold, or too close to surfaces that absorb heat faster than it can be released, and therefore fails to ignite or propagate flame.

The three classical "Ignition Limits" discussed in KTU Thermal Engineering are:

1. **Flammability (Concentration) Limits** – The range of fuel-to-air ratios that support combustion.
2. **Ignition (Temperature) Limits** – The minimum temperature at which a mixture spontaneously ignites without an external spark.
3. **Quenching Limits** – The minimum characteristic distance between heat-absorbing surfaces required for flame propagation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL)** is the *minimum* fuel concentration below which combustion cannot occur (mixture too lean).
> **Upper Flammable Limit (UFL)** is the *maximum* fuel concentration above which combustion cannot occur (mixture too rich).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of combustion like lighting a campfire with wet wood:

- **Too little wood (Lean side)**: The flames die because there isn't enough fuel to release heat. The wet wood analogy matches LFL — not enough "combustible stuff" to sustain fire.
- **Too much wood (Rich side)**: The fire chokes because there isn't enough oxygen to react with all the fuel. The flames suffocate. This matches UFL — the "stove is overloaded."
- **Ignition Temperature**: Even if the fuel-air ratio is correct, if the wood is *cold*, sparks cannot start a flame. The mixture must be hot enough so that the rate of heat release exceeds heat loss. This minimum threshold temperature is the **Ignition Temperature**.
- **Quenching Distance**: If you try to light a fire inside a very narrow, cold metal pipe, the metal walls absorb heat faster than the flame can release it. The flame dies. The minimum pipe diameter that still allows flame propagation is the **Quenching Diameter**, and its half is the **Quenching Distance**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value (Methane-Air) |
|-----------|--------|------------------------------|
| Lower Flammable Limit | LFL | **5% (by volume)** |
| Upper Flammable Limit | UFL | **15% (by volume)** |
| Auto-Ignition Temperature | T_ig | **537 °C (≈ 810 K)** |
| Stoichiometric Ratio | (F/A)_st | **0.0583** |
| Quenching Distance | d_q | **2.0 – 2.5 mm** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Ignition Boundaries Explained

#### 2.1.1 Flammability Limits (Concentration Limits)

The mixture is described by the **Equivalence Ratio (φ)**:

$$\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoichiometric}}$$

- **Lean mixture**: φ < 1 (excess air, fuel deficient)
- **Stoichiometric mixture**: φ = 1 (chemically correct ratio)
- **Rich mixture**: φ > 1 (excess fuel, air deficient)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Combustion is possible only within a band **approximately 0.4 ≤ φ ≤ 2.5** for most hydrocarbons in air. The exact range varies by fuel chemistry.

**Why does combustion stop at the LFL?**
At the lower flammable limit, the energy released by the few molecules of fuel reacting is insufficient to heat the surrounding unburned mixture above its ignition temperature. The flame cannot propagate.

**Why does combustion stop at the UFL?**
At the upper flammable limit, there is so much fuel that the available oxygen cannot propagate a self-sustaining reaction. The chain-branching radicals (H•, OH•, O•) become diluted and recombine with the abundant fuel molecules, terminating the chain reaction.

#### 2.1.2 Ignition Temperature (Auto-Ignition Temperature)

Defined as the minimum temperature of a fuel-oxidizer mixture at which combustion becomes self-sustaining without an external ignition source.

The classical **Semenov Thermal Ignition Theory** balances heat generation rate (q_gen) against heat loss rate (q_loss). Ignition occurs when:

$$\frac{d(q_{gen})}{dT} \geq \frac{d(q_{loss})}{dT} \quad \text{at the critical point}$$

This produces the critical condition:

$$T_{ig} = \frac{E}{R \ln\left(\dfrac{Z \cdot V \cdot \rho \cdot C^p}{h \cdot A \cdot R}\right)}$$

Where:
- **E** = Activation Energy (J/mol)
- **R** = Universal Gas Constant (**8.314 J/mol·K**)
- **Z** = Pre-exponential factor
- **V** = Volume of mixture
- **A** = Heat transfer surface area
- **h** = Heat transfer coefficient

#### 2.1.3 Quenching Limit (Quenching Distance)

Flame propagation requires that the flame thickness (δ_f) be greater than the distance between two adjacent heat-absorbing surfaces. The **Quenching Diameter** is:

$$d_q = \frac{2 \cdot \lambda}{c_p \cdot \rho_u \cdot S_L}$$

Where:
- **λ** = Thermal conductivity of unburned mixture
- **c_p** = Specific heat at constant pressure
- **ρ_u** = Density of unburned mixture
- **S_L** = Laminar flame speed

### 2.2 Le Chatelier's Rule for Flammable Mixtures

When two or more flammable gases are mixed, the **LFL** and **UFL** of the mixture are calculated as:

$$LFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{\sum_{i} \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}}$$

$$UFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{\sum_{i} \dfrac{y_i}{UFL_i}}$$

Where **y_i** is the mole percent of component *i* in the fuel mixture.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Equivalence Ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoichiometric}}$ | Defines mixture strength |
| 2 | LFL Mixture (Le Chatelier) | $LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{\sum y_i / LFL_i}$ | Safety design of gas mixtures |
| 3 | UFL Mixture (Le Chatelier) | $UFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{\sum y_i / UFL_i}$ | Safety design of gas mixtures |
| 4 | Adiabatic Flame Temperature | $T_{ad} = T_i + \dfrac{\sum n_{p,i} h_{f,p,i}^0 - \sum n_{r,j} h_{f,r,j}^0}{\sum n_{p,i} c_{p,i}}$ | Maximum theoretical combustion temperature |
| 5 | Ignition Temperature (Semenov) | $T_{ig} = \dfrac{E}{R \ln(\text{function of geometry})}$ | Predicting auto-ignition |
| 6 | Quenching Distance | $d_q = \dfrac{2\lambda}{c_p \rho_u S_L}$ | Flame arrestor design |
| 7 | Heat Release Condition | $q_{gen} \geq q_{loss}$ at flame front | Sustained combustion |
| 8 | Mass Burn Rate | $\dot{m} = \rho_u \cdot A \cdot S_L$ | Combustion chamber sizing |
| 9 | Reynolds Number for Quenching | $Re_q = \dfrac{S_L \cdot d_q}{\nu}$ | Determining flow regime |
| 10 | Spontaneous Ignition Delay | $\tau_{ig} = A \cdot e^{E/RT}$ | Diesel knock, HCCI engines |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU students are **frequently** asked to compute **LFL/UFL using Le Chatelier's Rule** and the **Quenching Distance** using flame speed correlations. These two formulas appear in nearly every 14-mark question on ignition limits.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Boiler & Furnace Design**: Engineers must operate well within flammable limits to avoid flashback or explosion.
- **Internal Combustion Engines**: Spark-ignition engines must have a fuel-air ratio between LFL and UFL for proper ignition.
- **Fire Safety**: Flammability limits dictate ventilation design in mines, petrochemical plants, and LNG carriers.
- **Flame Arrestors**: Quenching distance determines the safe mesh size to prevent flame propagation in pipelines.
- **Rocket & Gas Turbine Combustors**: Ignition temperature determines torch igniter placement and reliability at altitude.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Le Chatelier's Rule (Mixture Flammability Limit)

**Statement**: The lower flammable limit of a gas mixture is reached when the total moles of non-flammable diluent (inert + excess air) reaches the value corresponding to the LFL of any single fuel.

**Step 1 — Define mole fractions**

Let the fuel mixture contain *n* components, each with mole percent **y_i** and lower flammable limit **LFL_i** in air.

**Step 2 — Express inert content at LFL**

At the LFL of component *i*, the mole fraction of *i* in the fuel+air mixture is LFL_i/100. The remaining **100 − LFL_i** mole percent is air (oxygen + nitrogen).

**Step 3 — Sum contributions**

Since combustion proceeds independently in a mixed system (an approximation, but industrially accurate), the equivalent LFL of the mixture is reached when:

$$\frac{100}{LFL_{mix}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{y_i}{LFL_i}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for LFL_mix**

$$\boxed{LFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}}}$$

The identical logic applies to the upper flammable limit (UFL), producing:

$$\boxed{UFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i}{UFL_i}}}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (LFL/UFL of a Natural Gas Substitute)

**Problem**: A fuel gas mixture contains:
- 80% Methane (CH₄), LFL = 5.0%, UFL = 15.0%
- 15% Ethane (C₂H₆), LFL = 3.0%, UFL = 12.5%
- 5% Propane (C₃H₈), LFL = 2.1%, UFL = 9.5%

Find the LFL and UFL of the mixture.

**Step 1 — Calculate LFL**

$$\frac{100}{LFL_{mix}} = \frac{80}{5.0} + \frac{15}{3.0} + \frac{5}{2.1}$$

$$\frac{100}{LFL_{mix}} = 16.00 + 5.00 + 2.381 = 23.381$$

$$LFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{23.381} = 4.28\%$$

**Step 2 — Calculate UFL**

$$\frac{100}{UFL_{mix}} = \frac{80}{15.0} + \frac{15}{12.5} + \frac{5}{9.5}$$

$$\frac{100}{UFL_{mix}} = 5.333 + 1.200 + 0.526 = 7.060$$

$$UFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{7.060} = 14.16\%$$

**Final Answer**: The mixture is flammable in the range **4.28% to 14.16%** by volume in air.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Quenching Distance from Energy Balance

**Step 1 — Geometry**

Consider flame propagation between two parallel plates separated by distance **d**. The flame sheet has thickness equal to **d** at the quenching limit.

**Step 2 — Heat conduction to the wall**

Heat conducted from the flame to the wall (per unit area) is:

$$q_{wall} = \frac{\lambda (T_f - T_u)}{(d/2)}$$

Where **T_f** is the flame temperature and **T_u** is the unburned gas temperature.

**Step 3 — Heat generation rate in the flame**

$$q_{gen} = \rho_u \cdot S_L \cdot c_p \cdot (T_f - T_u)$$

**Step 4 — Equate at the quenching limit**

At the critical limit, heat lost to the wall equals heat generated:

$$\frac{2 \lambda (T_f - T_u)}{d_q} = \rho_u \cdot S_L \cdot c_p \cdot (T_f - T_u)$$

**Step 5 — Cancel (T_f − T_u) and solve**

$$\frac{2 \lambda}{d_q} = \rho_u \cdot S_L \cdot c_p$$

$$\boxed{d_q = \frac{2 \lambda}{\rho_u \cdot c_p \cdot S_L}}$$

This derivation shows that the quenching distance is **inversely proportional to flame speed**. Faster-burning mixtures (e.g., hydrogen) have smaller quenching distances and thus can leak through narrower gaps, a critical safety concern.

---

### 3.4 Effect of Temperature and Pressure on Flammability Limits (Empirical)

The upper and lower flammable limits vary with temperature and pressure according to:

$$LFL_T = LFL_{25} \cdot \left[1 - \frac{T - 25}{600}\right]$$

$$UFL_T = UFL_{25} \cdot \left[1 + \frac{T - 25}{600}\right]$$

Where **T** is in °C and the reference temperature is **25 °C**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trend**: As temperature increases, the flammable range **widens** (LFL decreases, UFL increases). This is why a substance that is "safe" at room temperature may become explosive when heated.

For pressure (typical hydrocarbons in air):

$$LFL_P \approx LFL_{1\,atm} \cdot (1 - C_1 \cdot \Delta P)$$

$$UFL_P \approx UFL_{1\,atm} \cdot (1 + C_2 \cdot \Delta P)$$

Where **C₁ ≈ 0.001 / atm** and **C₂ ≈ 0.01 / atm** are empirical constants. Higher pressure generally widens the flammable range for most hydrocarbons.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
