<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Define the terms **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL)** and **Upper Flammable Limit (UFL)** of a fuel-air mixture. State typical values for methane in air.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Lower Flammable Limit (LFL) is the minimum concentration of fuel in a fuel-air mixture below which combustion cannot propagate because the mixture is too lean to release sufficient energy. The Upper Flammable Limit (UFL) is the maximum concentration of fuel above which combustion cannot propagate because insufficient oxygen is available to sustain the chain reaction.
>
> For methane (CH₄) in air at standard conditions:
> - **LFL = 5.0%** by volume
> - **UFL = 15.0%** by volume
>
> Thus methane-air is combustible only when the methane concentration is between 5% and 15%.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

What is meant by **quenching distance** in flame propagation? State its significance in flame arrestor design.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Quenching distance is the minimum distance between two parallel heat-absorbing surfaces below which a flame cannot propagate. If the gap is smaller than this distance, the heat released by the reacting gas is conducted away to the walls faster than it can heat the adjacent unburned mixture to its ignition temperature, causing the flame to extinguish.
>
> **Significance in flame arrestors**: Flame arrestors installed in pipelines handling flammable gases use perforated plates or wire mesh with characteristic gaps smaller than the quenching distance of the gas. This prevents a flame from one side propagating to the other side, ensuring fire safety in LNG carriers, refineries, and fuel lines. *(3 marks distributed: 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for application)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]*

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Flammability Limits** with a neat sketch showing the lean, stoichiometric, and rich regions of combustion. State any **four** factors that affect flammability limits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fuel gas mixture contains the following by volume: 70% Methane (LFL = 5.0%, UFL = 15.0%), 20% Ethane (LFL = 3.0%, UFL = 12.5%), and 10% Propane (LFL = 2.1%, UFL = 9.5%). Calculate the LFL and UFL of the mixture using **Le Chatelier's Rule**. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) Solution — 7 marks**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Flammability limits are the boundary concentrations of fuel in a fuel-oxidizer mixture between which combustion can occur. Outside this range, the mixture is non-flammable.

**Diagram Description (3 marks):**
A plot of flame temperature or flame speed versus equivalence ratio φ:
- At φ → 0 (pure air): no combustion.
- At φ = LFL (≈ 0.4 for hydrocarbons): combustion begins.
- At φ = 1: stoichiometric — peak flame temperature and peak flame speed.
- At φ = UFL (≈ 2.0–2.5): combustion ceases.
- At φ → ∞ (pure fuel): no combustion.

**Four Factors Affecting Flammability Limits (2 marks):**
1. **Temperature**: Higher temperature widens the flammable range (LFL decreases, UFL increases).
2. **Pressure**: Higher pressure generally widens the range for most hydrocarbons.
3. **Inert diluents (CO₂, N₂, H₂O vapor)**: Narrow the flammable range; can extinguish flame.
4. **Direction of flame propagation**: Limits differ for upward, downward, and horizontal propagation (upward is widest).
5. *(Additional)* Turbulence and igniter strength.

---

**Part (b) Solution — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Write Le Chatelier's formulas (1 mark):**

$$LFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{\sum \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}}, \quad UFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{\sum \dfrac{y_i}{UFL_i}}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate LFL mixture (3 marks):**

$$\frac{100}{LFL_{mix}} = \frac{70}{5.0} + \frac{20}{3.0} + \frac{10}{2.1}$$

$$\frac{100}{LFL_{mix}} = 14.00 + 6.667 + 4.762 = 25.429$$

$$LFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{25.429} = 3.93\%$$

**Step 3 — Calculate UFL mixture (3 marks):**

$$\frac{100}{UFL_{mix}} = \frac{70}{15.0} + \frac{20}{12.5} + \frac{10}{9.5}$$

$$\frac{100}{UFL_{mix}} = 4.667 + 1.600 + 1.053 = 7.320$$

$$UFL_{mix} = \frac{100}{7.320} = 13.66\%$$

**Final Answer**: The fuel mixture is flammable between **3.93%** and **13.66%** by volume in air.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023]*

**(a)** Define **ignition temperature** and explain how it is determined using the **Semenov Thermal Theory** of ignition. Sketch the q_gen and q_loss curves and mark the critical ignition temperature. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **quenching distance** and derive the expression $d_q = \dfrac{2\lambda}{\rho_u c_p S_L}$ for a flame propagating between two parallel plates. If the thermal conductivity of a methane-air mixture is 0.026 W/m·K, density is 1.1 kg/m³, specific heat is 1.05 kJ/kg·K, and laminar flame speed is 0.45 m/s, calculate the quenching distance. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) Solution — 7 marks**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Ignition temperature (auto-ignition temperature) is the minimum temperature to which a fuel-oxidizer mixture must be raised so that combustion becomes self-sustaining without any external ignition source.

**Semenov Theory (4 marks):**
The theory assumes a well-mixed reactor where the entire gas volume is at uniform temperature **T**, and heat is lost only through the walls. The rate of heat generation due to chemical reaction follows an **Arrhenius exponential** form:

$$q_{gen} = Q \cdot Z \cdot e^{-E/RT} \cdot V$$

The rate of heat loss to the surroundings is **Newtonian cooling**:

$$q_{loss} = h \cdot A \cdot (T - T_0)$$

On a plot of q versus T, q_gen is a steep exponential curve and q_loss is a straight line. They intersect at **two points** (low and high T). The lower intersection is **stable** (any perturbation returns to equilibrium), while the upper intersection is **unstable** (perturbation causes runaway). The point of tangency between them is the **critical ignition temperature T_ig**.

**Sketch (1.5 marks):** Standard Semenov curve with q_gen exponential, q_loss linear, two intersections, tangency point labeled T_ig.

---

**Part (b) Solution — 7 marks**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Quenching distance is the minimum gap between two heat-absorbing surfaces below which a flame cannot propagate.

**Derivation (4 marks):** *(As shown in Section 3.3 above.)*
- Heat lost to wall: $q_{wall} = \dfrac{2\lambda(T_f - T_u)}{d_q}$
- Heat generated in flame: $q_{gen} = \rho_u S_L c_p (T_f - T_u)$
- Equating at the quenching limit and cancelling (T_f − T_u) yields: $d_q = \dfrac{2\lambda}{\rho_u c_p S_L}$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Given:
- λ = 0.026 W/m·K
- ρ_u = 1.1 kg/m³
- c_p = 1.05 kJ/kg·K = 1050 J/kg·K
- S_L = 0.45 m/s

$$d_q = \frac{2 \times 0.026}{1.1 \times 1050 \times 0.45}$$

$$d_q = \frac{0.052}{519.75}$$

$$\boxed{d_q = 1.00 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.10 \text{ mm}}$$

This means flame arrestor mesh for methane must have gap sizes less than **0.10 mm** to prevent flame propagation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions**: In Part B(b), students frequently plug in c_p in kJ without converting to J. This produces a 1000× error. Always show units explicitly.
> 2. **Inverted Le Chatelier formula**: Some students write $LFL_{mix} = \sum y_i \cdot LFL_i$ instead of the harmonic form. KTU award **zero marks** for this — the rule is always harmonic.
> 3. **Missing the tangency point**: In Semenov theory questions, simply plotting q_gen and q_loss without labeling the **tangent point T_ig** costs 1–2 marks. Always mark the critical point explicitly.
> 4. **Confusing LFL/LEL terminology**: KTU uses LFL (Lower Flammable Limit). Using LEL is acceptable but stating LFL is preferred.
> 5. **Quenching distance vs. quenching diameter**: Students often forget that **d_q is the half-gap** (single side), while **D_q is the full gap**. The formula $d_q = 2\lambda / (\rho_u c_p S_L)$ gives the **half-gap**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Ignition Limits**

- ☐ **Three types of ignition limits**: Flammability (concentration), Ignition Temperature, and Quenching Distance.
- ☐ **LFL** = Lean limit (5% for CH₄). **UFL** = Rich limit (15% for CH₄).
- ☐ **Le Chatelier Rule** is always **harmonic**: $\dfrac{100}{LFL_{mix}} = \sum \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}$
- ☐ **Equivalence Ratio** $\phi = (F/A)_{actual} / (F/A)_{stoich}$; lean if φ < 1, rich if φ > 1.
- ☐ **Semenov Theory**: q_gen (Arrhenius exponential) vs q_loss (linear). Critical T_ig at the **tangent point**.
- ☐ **Quenching Distance**: $d_q = \dfrac{2\lambda}{\rho_u c_p S_L}$. Inversely proportional to flame speed.
- ☐ **Temperature effect**: Higher T → wider flammable range.
- ☐ **Pressure effect**: Higher P → wider range for hydrocarbons.
- ☐ **Inerts (N₂, CO₂, H₂O)** → narrower flammable range.
- ☐ **Upward flame propagation** gives the widest flammable range; **downward** gives the narrowest.
- ☐ **Application areas**: Engine tuning (SI engines near φ = 1), boiler safety, LNG/petrochem safety, flame arrestor sizing.
- ☐ **Remember unit conversions**: c_p in J/kg·K (not kJ), distances in meters (not mm) for SI consistency.
- ☐ **Methane quenching distance** is approximately **2.0 mm (full gap)** → flame arrestor mesh must have smaller gaps.
- ☐ **Hydrogen has wider limits** (LFL = 4%, UFL = 75%) and **smaller quenching distance** — most dangerous fuel to handle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air-Fuel Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Air-Fuel Ratio # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F ratio)** of a combustible mixture is defined as the **mass of air supplied per unit mass of fuel** introduced into the combustion chamber for the burning process. Mathematically, it is expressed as: $$(A/F) = \frac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}}$$ where: - $m_{\text{air}}$ = Mass of air supplied (in **kg** or **kg/kmol of fuel**) - $m_{\text{fuel}}$ = Mass of fuel burned (in **kg** or **kg/kmol of fuel**) The **Fuel-Air Ratio (F/A)** is simply the reciprocal of A/F and is extensively used in gas turbine and jet propulsion analysis: $$(F/A) = \frac{1}{(A/F)} = \frac{m_{\text{fuel}}}{m_{\text{air}}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stoichiometric (Theoretical) Air-Fuel Ratio:** The chemically correct (minimum) mass of air required to completely burn **1 kg of fuel**, supplying just enough oxygen to oxidise all carbon to CO₂ and all hydrogen to H₂O, with **no oxygen left over**. This is denoted as $(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}$. ## 1.2 Classification of Mixtures | Mixture Type | Condition | Engine Behaviour | |---|---|---| | **Stoichiometric (Chemically Correct)** | A/F = (A/F)ᵢ | Complete combustion, no free O₂ | | **Lean Mixture** | A/F > (A/F)ᵢ | Excess air present, incomplete energy release | | **Rich Mixture** | A/F < (A/F)ᵢ | Insufficient air, CO / unburnt HC present | > [!NOTE] > For petrol (gasoline) engines, the stoichiometric A/F ratio is approximately **14.7 : 1**, while for diesel engines the actual operating A/F ratio is much higher (typically **18 : 1 to 70 : 1**) because excess air is intentionally supplied. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of making a cup of **masala chai**. The tea leaves : water : sugar : milk ratio must be just right. If you pour too much water, the chai becomes watery and insipid (this is your **lean mixture**). If you add too little water, the tea becomes overpowering and the sugar doesn't dissolve properly (this is your **rich mixture**). The *perfect* balance gives the ideal brew — this is your **stoichiometric mixture**. In a combustion chamber, the "water" is the **air (oxygen)**, the "tea leaves" are the **fuel**, and the goal is to release the maximum useful heat. Too much air cools the chamber and dilutes the energy; too little air leaves fuel unburnt, producing toxic carbon monoxide and reducing efficiency. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - Molecular mass of dry air ≈ **28.97 kg/kmol** (often rounded to **29 kg/kmol**) - 1 kmol of O₂ is accompanied by **3.76 kmol of N₂** in atmospheric air - 1 kmol of O₂ has mass = **32 kg**; 1 kmol of N₂ has mass = **28 kg** - Molecular mass of air = $0.21 \times 32 + 0.79 \times 28 = \mathbf{28.84\ kg/kmol}$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical and Actual Air Requirements **Theoretical (Stoichiometric) Oxygen Requirement ($O_{\text{min}}$):** For a hydrocarbon fuel of the general form $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z$: $$O_{\text{min}} = \left(x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2}\right)\ \text{kmol of O}_2 \text{ per kmol of fuel}$$ **Theoretical (Stoichiometric) Air Requirement ($A_{\text{min}}$):** $$A_{\text{min}} = 4.76 \times O_{\text{min}} \ \text{kmol of air per kmol of fuel}$$ where the factor **4.76** comes from $1 + 3.76$ (O₂ + accompanying N₂). **Actual Air Supplied ($A_{\text{act}}$):** $$A_{\text{act}} = A_{\text{min}} + A_{\text{excess}} = A_{\text{min}} \times (1 + e)$$ where $e$ = **fractional excess air** (e.g., $e = 0.25$ means 25% excess air). ## 2.2 Equivalence Ratio (φ) — The KTU Favourite The **equivalence ratio** is the inverse of the relative air-fuel ratio and is widely used in KTU problems: $$\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}} = \frac{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}$$ - $\phi = 1$ → Stoichiometric mixture - $\phi < 1$ → Lean mixture - $\phi > 1$ → Rich mixture ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | A/F Ratio | $(A/F) = m_{\text{air}} / m_{\text{fuel}}$ | Dimensionless | | 2 | F/A Ratio | $(F/A) = 1/(A/F)$ | Dimensionless | | 3 | Stoichiometric O₂ | $O_{\text{min}} = x + y/4 - z/2$ | kmol O₂ / kmol fuel | | 4 | Stoichiometric air | $A_{\text{min}} = 4.76 \times O_{\text{min}}$ | kmol air / kmol fuel | | 5 | Mass of 1 kmol air | $M_{\text{air}} = 28.97$ | kg/kmol | | 6 | Mass of 1 kmol O₂ | $M_{\text{O}_2} = 32$ | kg/kmol | | 7 | N₂/O₂ ratio in air | 3.76 : 1 | — | | 8 | Equivalence ratio | $\phi = (A/F)_{\text{stoich}} / (A/F)_{\text{actual}}$ | — | | 9 | Excess air % | $\%e = \left(\dfrac{A_{\text{act}} - A_{\text{min}}}{A_{\text{min}}}\right) \times 100$ | % | | 10 | Air-Fuel ratio with excess air | $(A/F)_{\text{act}} = (A/F)_{\text{stoich}} \times (1 + e)$ | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL KTU Tip:** When using the vertical-bar symbol in any markdown table row, ALWAYS use `\vert` (e.g., $\phi = 1 \vert$ limit) — never the raw `|` character, as it breaks the table rendering. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The Air-Fuel Ratio is **the single most important operating parameter** in: - **SI Engines (Petrol):** Closed-loop lambda sensors (λ = 1/φ) maintain stoich ratio for catalytic converter efficiency. - **CI Engines (Diesel):** Operate always lean (high A/F) to suppress soot and NOₓ. - **Gas Turbines:** Lean premixed combustion reduces peak flame temperature and NOₓ emissions. - **Industrial Boilers:** Excess air is added (typically 10–20%) to ensure complete combustion, but each 1% excess air drops efficiency by ~0.5%. - **Rocket Propulsion:** F/O ratio dictates specific impulse and combustion temperature. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric A/F for a Generic Hydrocarbon Consider the generic combustion of **1 kmol of fuel** $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z$ with stoichiometric air: $$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z + a(\text{O}_2 + 3.76\text{N}_2) \rightarrow b\,\text{CO}_2 + c\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + d\,\text{N}_2$$ **Step 1: Apply the Carbon Balance (atoms of C conserved)** $$b = x$$ **Step 2: Apply the Hydrogen Balance (atoms of H conserved)** $$2c = y \quad \Rightarrow \quad c = \frac{y}{2}$$ **Step 3: Apply the Oxygen Balance (atoms of O conserved)** $$z + 2a = 2b + c$$ Substituting the values of $b$ and $c$: $$z + 2a = 2x + \frac{y}{2}$$ Solving for $a$: $$a = x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2}$$ This is the **kmol of O₂ required per kmol of fuel**, i.e., $O_{\text{min}}$. **Step 4: Stoichiometric air per kmol of fuel** $$A_{\text{min}} = a \times (1 + 3.76) = 4.76\,a\ \text{kmol of air/kmol of fuel}$$ **Step 5: Convert to Mass Basis** Mass of air = $A_{\text{min}} \times 28.97$ kg Mass of fuel = $(12x + y + 16z)$ kg Therefore: $$(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{4.76\,a \times 28.97}{(12x + y + 16z)}$$ > [!NOTE] > KTU **favourite fuels** to test: Octane $\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}$, Methane $\text{CH}_4$, Ethane $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$, and pure Carbon C / Hydrogen H₂. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Worked Example — Octane (C₈H₁₈) Octane is the reference fuel for petrol engines. **Step 1: Combustion Equation Setup** $$\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + a(\text{O}_2 + 3.76\,\text{N}_2) \rightarrow 8\,\text{CO}_2 + 9\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 3.76a\,\text{N}_2$$ **Step 2: Solve for $a$ (Oxygen Balance)** Oxygen in = Oxygen out $$2a = (2 \times 8) + (1 \times 9) = 16 + 9 = 25$$ $$a = 12.5\ \text{kmol of O}_2$$ **Step 3: Stoichiometric air required** $$A_{\text{min}} = 4.76 \times 12.5 = 59.5\ \text{kmol of air}$$ **Step 4: Masses** $$\text{Mass of air} = 59.5 \times 28.97 = 1723.7\ \text{kg}$$ $$\text{Mass of fuel (octane)} = (8 \times 12) + (18 \times 1) = 96 + 18 = 114\ \text{kg}$$ **Step 5: Stoichiometric A/F Ratio** $$(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{1723.7}{114} = 15.12\ \text{kg air/kg fuel}$$ This is the canonical result KTU expects for octane. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Worked Example — Methane (CH₄) **Step 1: Combustion Equation** $$\text{CH}_4 + 2(\text{O}_2 + 3.76\,\text{N}_2) \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 7.52\,\text{N}_2$$ **Step 2: Oxygen Balance Check** $$2a = 2(1) + 2 = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = 2\ \text{kmol of O}_2 \ \checkmark$$ **Step 3: Masses** $$\text{Mass of air} = (2 \times 32) + (7.52 \times 28) = 64 + 210.56 = 274.56\ \text{kg}$$ $$\text{Mass of fuel} = 12 + 4 = 16\ \text{kg}$$ **Step 4: Stoichiometric A/F** $$(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{274.56}{16} = 17.16\ \text{kg air/kg fuel}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Stoichiometric A/F for Common Fuels | Fuel | Formula | O₂ reqd (kmol) | Mass of air (kg) | Mass of fuel (kg) | (A/F)ᵢ | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Carbon | C | 1.00 | 137.28 | 12.00 | **11.44** | | Hydrogen | H₂ | 0.50 | 68.64 | 2.00 | **34.32** | | Methane | CH₄ | 2.00 | 274.56 | 16.00 | **17.16** | | Ethane | C₂H₆ | 3.50 | 480.48 | 30.00 | **16.02** | | Propane | C₃H₈ | 5.00 | 686.40 | 44.00 | **15.60** | | Octane | C₈H₁₈ | 12.50 | 1723.70 | 114.00 | **15.12** | | Methanol | CH₃OH | 1.50 | 205.92 | 32.00 | **6.43** | | Ethanol | C₂H₅OH | 3.00 | 411.84 | 46.00 | **8.95** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Observation:** Lower hydrocarbons and pure hydrogen have higher stoichiometric A/F ratios because each kg of fuel requires a relatively larger mass of air. Oxygenated fuels (alcohols) have very low A/Fᵢ because they carry their own oxygen. ## 3.5 Derivation 4: Effect of Excess Air Suppose the actual air supplied is **25% in excess** of stoichiometric for octane: $$(A/F)_{\text{act}} = 1.25 \times 15.12 = 18.90$$ **Equivalence ratio:** $$\phi = \frac{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}{(A/F)_{\text{act}}} = \frac{15.12}{18.90} = 0.80\ \ (\text{Lean mixture})$$ **Combustion equation with 25% excess air:** $$\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 1.25 \times 12.5 (\text{O}_2 + 3.76\text{N}_2) \rightarrow 8\,\text{CO}_2 + 9\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 3.125\,\text{O}_2 + 58.75\,\text{N}_2$$ The $3.125$ kmol of free O₂ in the products is the diagnostic of **lean combustion**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Classification of Combustion Mixtures ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Air-Fuel Mixture] --> B{Compare A/F actual with A/F stoich} B -- Equal --> C[Stoichiometric Mixture] B -- Greater --> D[Lean Mixture] B -- Less --> E[Rich Mixture] C --> F[Complete Combustion
No free O2
No unburnt fuel] D --> G[Excess Air
Free O2 in products
Lower flame temp] E --> H[Insufficient O2
CO and unburnt HC
Smoky exhaust] F --> I[Lambda Sensor 1
Petrol Engine stoich] G --> J[CI Diesel Engine
Gas Turbine lean burn] H --> K[Power enrichment
SI Cold start] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — A/F Calculation Procedure ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Fuel Composition CxHyOz] --> B[Compute O2 min
x + y/4 - z/2 kmol] B --> C[Compute A min
4.76 times O2 min] C --> D[Compute Mass Air
4.76 times O2 min times 28.97] A --> E[Compute Mass Fuel
12x + y + 16z] D --> F[Divide by E] E --> F F --> G[Stoichiometric A/F Result] H[Excess Air percent] --> I[Multiply A/F stoich by 1 plus e] G --> I I --> J[Actual A/F for Engine Operation] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Mass Balance Across Combustion Chamber ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Input["INPUT STREAMS"] A1[Fuel
1 kg] B1[Air
A by F actual kg] end subgraph Reaction["COMBUSTION CHAMBER
Steady State Steady Flow"] R[Chemical Reaction
Complete Oxidation] end subgraph Output["OUTPUT FLUE GASES"] O1[CO2] O2[H2O] O3[N2] O4[O2 if Lean] O5[CO if Rich] end A1 --> R B1 --> R R --> O1 R --> O2 R --> O3 R --> O4 R --> O5 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the block diagram:** Mass in must equal mass out (conservation of mass). The ratio of Input-Air mass to Input-Fuel mass is the **A/F ratio** of that particular combustion process. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define the terms: (i) Stoichiometric air, (ii) Excess air, (iii) Equivalence ratio.** **Model Answer:** (i) **Stoichiometric Air:** The minimum mass of air required for complete combustion of a unit mass of fuel, supplying exactly the chemically correct amount of oxygen so that all carbon forms CO₂ and all hydrogen forms H₂O, with no free O₂ remaining. (ii) **Excess Air:** The amount of air supplied beyond the stoichiometric requirement, usually expressed as a percentage of stoichiometric air: $\%e = \dfrac{A_{\text{act}} - A_{\text{min}}}{A_{\text{min}}} \times 100$. (iii) **Equivalence Ratio (φ):** The ratio of actual fuel-air ratio to stoichiometric fuel-air ratio: $$\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}}$$ $\phi = 1$ stoich; $\phi > 1$ rich; $\phi < 1$ lean. **[3 Marks]** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **A fuel has the composition C = 86%, H₂ = 14% by mass. Calculate the stoichiometric air required per kg of fuel.** **Model Answer:** For 1 kg fuel: $C = 0.86$ kg, $H_2 = 0.14$ kg. Moles of C: $0.86/12 = 0.0717$ kmol Moles of H₂: $0.14/2 = 0.0700$ kmol $$O_2\ \text{needed} = 0.0717 + 0.0700/2 = 0.0717 + 0.0350 = 0.1067\ \text{kmol}$$ $$A_{\text{min}} = 4.76 \times 0.1067 = 0.5079\ \text{kmol of air}$$ $$\text{Mass of air} = 0.5079 \times 28.97 = 14.71\ \text{kg/kg fuel}$$ **Stoichiometric A/F ≈ 14.71 : 1** **[3 Marks]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]** **OR** ### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Pattern]` **(a)** Octane $\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}$ is burned with **30% excess air**. Assuming complete combustion, determine: 1. The stoichiometric A/F ratio. 2. The actual A/F ratio. 3. The mole fraction of each component in the wet flue gas products. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A coal sample has the ultimate analysis: C = 78%, H = 5%, O = 8%, N = 2%, S = 1%, Ash = 6% (by mass). Calculate: 1. The minimum air required for complete combustion per kg of coal. 2. The actual A/F ratio if 40% excess air is supplied. 3. The mass of dry flue gas per kg of coal. **(7 Marks)** #### **Solution to Q.A (a):** **1. Stoichiometric A/F for Octane:** From the derivation in Section 3.2: $$(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{4.76 \times 12.5 \times 28.97}{114} = \frac{1723.7}{114} = 15.12$$ **2. Actual A/F with 30% excess air:** $$(A/F)_{\text{act}} = (1 + 0.30) \times 15.12 = 1.30 \times 15.12 = 19.66$$ **3. Mole fractions in wet flue gas:** Stoichiometric O₂ needed = 12.5 kmol Actual O₂ supplied = $1.30 \times 12.5 = 16.25$ kmol Actual N₂ supplied = $16.25 \times 3.76 = 61.10$ kmol Combustion equation: $$\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 16.25(\text{O}_2 + 3.76\text{N}_2) \rightarrow 8\,\text{CO}_2 + 9\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 3.75\,\text{O}_2 + 61.10\,\text{N}_2$$ Total moles of wet products: $$N_{\text{total}} = 8 + 9 + 3.75 + 61.10 = 81.85\ \text{kmol}$$ Mole fractions: $$\begin{aligned} y_{\text{CO}_2} &= 8/81.85 = 0.0977 \\ y_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} &= 9/81.85 = 0.1100 \\ y_{\text{O}_2} &= 3.75/81.85 = 0.0458 \\ y_{\text{N}_2} &= 61.10/81.85 = 0.7465 \\ \end{aligned}$$ Sum check: $0.0977 + 0.1100 + 0.0458 + 0.7465 = 1.0000$ ✓ **[7 Marks]** #### **Solution to Q.A (b):** For 1 kg coal: C = 0.78, H = 0.05, O = 0.08, N = 0.02, S = 0.01, Ash = 0.06 kg. **1. Minimum O₂ required:** $$\begin{aligned} O_2\ \text{from C} &= 0.78/12 = 0.0650\ \text{kmol} \\ O_2\ \text{from H} &= 0.05 \times 8 = 0.4000\ \text{kg} = 0.0125\ \text{kmol} \\ O_2\ \text{from S} &= 0.01/32 = 0.0003125\ \text{kmol} \\ O_2\ \text{already in fuel} &= 0.08/32 = 0.0025\ \text{kmol} \\ \end{aligned}$$ Net O₂ required from air: $$O_{\text{min}} = 0.0650 + 0.0125 + 0.0003125 - 0.0025 = 0.0753\ \text{kmol}$$ Minimum air: $$A_{\text{min}} = 4.76 \times 0.0753 = 0.3585\ \text{kmol/kg coal} = 10.39\ \text{kg/kg coal}$$ **2. Actual A/F with 40% excess air:** $$(A/F)_{\text{act}} = 1.40 \times 10.39 = 14.55\ \text{kg air/kg coal}$$ **3. Mass of dry flue gas per kg coal:** Products (dry basis, per kg coal): CO₂, SO₂, N₂ (from air + from fuel), and free O₂. $$\begin{aligned} m_{\text{CO}_2} &= (0.0650)(44) = 2.860\ \text{kg} \\ m_{\text{SO}_2} &= (0.0003125)(64) = 0.020\ \text{kg} \\ m_{\text{N}_2 \text{ from air}} &= 0.3585 \times 1.40 \times 0.79 \times 28 / 28.97 \text{ (approx.)} \\ &\approx \text{Actual air mass} \times 0.7664 = 14.55 \times 0.7664 = 11.15\ \text{kg (approx)} \\ m_{\text{N}_2 \text{ from fuel}} &= 0.02/28 = 0.000714\ \text{kmol} \times 28 = 0.020\ \text{kg} \\ m_{\text{O}_2 \text{ (excess 40\% of stoich)}} &= 0.40 \times 0.0753 \times 32 = 0.964\ \text{kg} \\ \end{aligned}$$ Total dry flue gas ≈ $2.86 + 0.02 + 11.15 + 0.02 + 0.96 \approx 15.01\ \text{kg/kg coal}$ (Ash is solid, separated before gas analysis.) **[7 Marks]** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]` **(a)** Methane ($\text{CH}_4$) is burned with **150% theoretical air**. For complete combustion, compute: 1. Air-Fuel ratio on a mass basis. 2. Equivalence ratio. 3. Dew point of the products if the total pressure is 101.325 kPa. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A furnace burns fuel oil with the analysis C = 84%, H = 12%, S = 3%, O = 1% (by mass), with **20% excess air**. Calculate: 1. The mass of air supplied per kg of fuel. 2. The mass of dry products per kg of fuel. 3. The Orsat analysis (dry basis mole %). **(7 Marks)** #### **Solution to Q.B (a):** **1. A/F on mass basis:** From Section 3.3, stoichiometric A/F for methane = 17.16. With 150% theoretical air, the air supplied = 1.50 × 17.16. $$(A/F)_{\text{act}} = 1.50 \times 17.16 = 25.74\ \text{kg air/kg CH}_4$$ **2. Equivalence ratio:** $$\phi = \frac{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}{(A/F)_{\text{act}}} = \frac{17.16}{25.74} = 0.667$$ Since $\phi < 1$, mixture is **lean** ✓ **3. Dew point of products:** Combustion equation (per kmol CH₄): $$\text{CH}_4 + 3(\text{O}_2 + 3.76\,\text{N}_2) \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + 11.28\,\text{N}_2$$ (150% air means 1.5 × 2 = 3 kmol O₂, leaving 1 kmol free O₂.) Total moles (wet) = 1 + 2 + 1 + 11.28 = 15.28 Mole fraction of H₂O (water vapour, partial pressure source): $$y_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 2/15.28 = 0.1309$$ Partial pressure of H₂O: $$p_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 0.1309 \times 101.325 = 13.26\ \text{kPa}$$ Dew point = saturation temperature at 13.26 kPa. From steam tables ≈ **52.3 °C**. **[7 Marks]** #### **Solution to Q.B (b):** For 1 kg fuel oil: C = 0.84, H = 0.12, S = 0.03, O = 0.01 kg. **1. Minimum O₂ required:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{For C: } O_2 &= 0.84/12 = 0.0700\ \text{kmol} \\ \text{For H: } O_2 &= 0.12/4 = 0.0300\ \text{kmol} \\ \text{For S: } O_2 &= 0.03/32 = 0.000938\ \text{kmol} \\ \text{Subtract O}_2\ \text{in fuel: } 0.01/32 &= 0.000313\ \text{kmol} \\ \end{aligned}$$ $$O_{\text{min}} = 0.0700 + 0.0300 + 0.000938 - 0.000313 = 0.1006\ \text{kmol}$$ $$A_{\text{min}} = 4.76 \times 0.1006 = 0.4790\ \text{kmol/kg fuel} = 13.88\ \text{kg/kg fuel}$$ **With 20% excess air:** $$\text{Actual air} = 1.20 \times 13.88 = 16.66\ \text{kg/kg fuel}$$ **2. Mass of dry products per kg fuel:** Dry products = CO₂ + SO₂ + N₂ + O₂ (excess) $$\begin{aligned} m_{\text{CO}_2} &= 0.0700 \times 44 = 3.080\ \text{kg} \\ m_{\text{SO}_2} &= 0.000938 \times 64 = 0.060\ \text{kg} \\ m_{\text{N}_2} &= 1.20 \times 0.4790 \times 3.76 \times 28 = 60.50\ \text{kg} \text{ (re-check using mass balance: actual air mass × 0.7664 = 16.66 × 0.7664 = 12.77 kg)} \\ \end{aligned}$$ (Note: the N₂ in actual air = 16.66 × 0.7664 ≈ 12.77 kg, more accurate using mass fractions.) $$m_{\text{O}_2 \text{ (excess)}} = 0.20 \times 0.1006 \times 32 = 0.644\ \text{kg}$$ Total dry products = $3.080 + 0.060 + 12.77 + 0.644 = 16.55\ \text{kg/kg fuel}$ **3. Orsat analysis (mole % dry basis):** Moles of each dry product: $$\begin{aligned} n_{\text{CO}_2} &= 0.0700 \\ n_{\text{SO}_2} &= 0.000938 \\ n_{\text{N}_2} &= 0.4790 \times 1.20 \times 3.76 = 2.161 \\ n_{\text{O}_2} &= 0.20 \times 0.1006 = 0.0201 \\ \end{aligned}$$ Total = $0.0700 + 0.000938 + 2.161 + 0.0201 = 2.252$ kmol $$\begin{aligned} \%\,\text{CO}_2 &= (0.0700/2.252) \times 100 = 3.11\% \\ \%\,\text{SO}_2 &= (0.000938/2.252) \times 100 = 0.04\% \\ \%\,\text{N}_2 &= (2.161/2.252) \times 100 = 95.95\% \\ \%\,\text{O}_2 &= (0.0201/2.252) \times 100 = 0.89\% \\ \end{aligned}$$ Sum ≈ 99.99% (rounding) ✓ **[7 Marks]** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the N₂ in air.** Every kmol of O₂ brings 3.76 kmol of N₂. Skipping this gives a wrong A/F ratio (~1/4.76 of correct value). **Lose ~2 marks.** > 2. **Using 28 instead of 28.97** for air molecular mass — KTU accepts both 28 and 28.97, but **be consistent** and **state** the value used. Mismatch between (A/F) and (F/A) form is a common trap. > 3. **Oxygen in fuel ignored.** For alcohols (CH₃OH, C₂H₅OH) and coal, deduct the O already in the fuel; otherwise O₂ requirement is overestimated. > 4. **Wet vs dry flue gas confusion.** The dew point calculation needs H₂O in the products — but Orsat analysis reports the **dry** composition (water removed). Mixing these two is a guaranteed 3-mark loss. > 5. **Confusing actual A/F with stoichiometric** when excess air is mentioned. The 1+e factor is the most-skipped step. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **A/F = mass of air ÷ mass of fuel** (dimensionless). - **Stoichiometric A/F for octane = 15.12**, methane = 17.16, hydrogen = 34.32, carbon = 11.44. - **Generic stoichiometric O₂ formula:** $O_{\text{min}} = x + y/4 - z/2$ for $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z$. - **Air contains 21% O₂ + 79% N₂ by volume**, so 1 kmol O₂ → 4.76 kmol air (factor to remember forever). - **Molecular mass of air = 28.97 kg/kmol**; **O₂ = 32 kg/kmol**. - **Excess air equation:** $(A/F)_{\text{act}} = (A/F)_{\text{stoich}} \times (1 + e)$. - **Equivalence ratio φ:** $\phi = 1$ stoich, $\phi < 1$ lean, $\phi > 1$ rich. **Lambda (λ) sensor in modern cars = 1/φ.** - **Lean combustion** produces free O₂ in products; **rich combustion** produces CO and unburnt HC. - **In equilibrium problems, mass in = mass out** — use it to cross-check every calculation. - **Wet flue gas** includes H₂O; **dry flue gas / Orsat analysis** excludes H₂O. - **Diesel engines** operate with massive excess air (high A/F); **petrol engines** operate near stoichiometric. - **KTU's most-tested fuels:** Octane, Methane, Ethane, and coal with ultimate analysis.

topics.title: Equivalence Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Equivalence Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Combustion Engineering**, the **Equivalence Ratio** (denoted by the Greek letter $\phi$) is defined as the ratio of the *actual* fuel-to-air mass ratio supplied to a combustion process to the *stoichiometric* fuel-to-air mass ratio required for complete combustion.

$$
\phi \;=\; \frac{\left( F/A \right)_{\text{actual}}}{\left( F/A \right)_{\text{stoichiometric}}}
$$

Equivalently, the parameter can be expressed using the air-to-fuel ratios as:

$$
\phi \;=\; \frac{\left( A/F \right)_{\text{stoichiometric}}}{\left( A/F \right)_{\text{actual}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PBMET604 – Module 4):** The Equivalence Ratio is a fundamental non-dimensional parameter used to classify combustion mixtures and is essential for analysing flame structure, pollutant formation, and performance of internal combustion engines, boilers, and gas turbines.

## 1.2 Classification of Combustion Mixtures

The numerical value of $\phi$ categorises the combustion regime into three fundamental classes:

| Value of $\phi$ | Condition | Combustion Regime |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $\phi < 1$ | Excess air present | **Lean** mixture |
| $\phi = 1$ | Exact stoichiometric air | **Stoichiometric** mixture |
| $\phi > 1$ | Insufficient air | **Rich** mixture |

## 1.3 Relation with Relative Air-Fuel Ratio ($\lambda$)

In European and modern engine control literature, the **relative air-fuel ratio** (also called the *excess air factor*) is used. It is the reciprocal of the equivalence ratio:

$$
\lambda \;=\; \frac{1}{\phi} \;=\; \frac{\left(A/F\right)_{\text{actual}}}{\left(A/F\right)_{\text{stoichiometric}}}
$$

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Cooking Stove

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Real-world Analogy):** Imagine you are cooking rice in a pressure cooker. The recipe book says you need **exactly 2 cups of water** for 1 cup of rice — this is the *stoichiometric* requirement. If you put **3 cups of water**, you are running "lean" ($\phi < 1$) — the rice cooks fine but the extra water is wasted. If you put only **1 cup of water**, you are running "rich" ($\phi > 1$) — the rice burns at the bottom. **Equivalence Ratio** is simply the *book says 2 cups, you actually put X cups* — it is a dimensionless scale that immediately tells the chef (or engineer) how far the mixture deviates from the chemically perfect recipe.

## 1.5 Physical Significance

- A purely mathematical ratio with **no units** (dimensionless).
- Indicates the **chemical character** of the combustion mixture irrespective of the fuel type.
- Critical for evaluating **combustion efficiency, flame temperature, and pollutant emissions** such as CO, unburned hydrocarbons, and NO$_x$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

Combustion is fundamentally a chemical oxidation reaction between a fuel (hydrocarbon $C_xH_y$, hydrogen, carbon, etc.) and oxygen from air. The **stoichiometric air requirement** is the minimum quantity of air needed to provide *exactly* enough oxygen for the complete oxidation of all combustible elements — producing only CO$_2$, H$_2$O, SO$_2$, and N$_2$.

The **actual air supplied** in a real combustion system is often different from the stoichiometric quantity due to:

1. **Practical design considerations** (excess air is supplied to ensure complete combustion).
2. **Mixture preparation limitations** in IC engines.
3. **Operating regime requirements** (rich mixtures for high power, lean for economy/emissions).

## 2.2 Stepwise Logic — Why $\phi$ Matters

- **Step 1:** Determine the chemical composition of the fuel (e.g., CH$_4$, C$_8$H$_{18}$, C$_{12}$H$_{26}$).
- **Step 2:** Calculate the **stoichiometric oxygen** required from the balanced combustion equation.
- **Step 3:** Convert stoichiometric oxygen to **stoichiometric air** using the molar ratio $O_2 + 3.76\,N_2$.
- **Step 4:** Compute the **stoichiometric A/F ratio** by dividing the mass of air by the mass of fuel.
- **Step 5:** Compare the **actual** operating A/F ratio to the stoichiometric value through $\phi$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | **Equivalence Ratio** | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | **Equivalence Ratio (alt form)** | $\phi = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | **Relative Air-Fuel Ratio** | $\lambda = 1/\phi$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | **Percent Excess Air** | $\%EA = (\lambda - 1)\times 100 = \left(\dfrac{1}{\phi} - 1\right)\times 100$ | Percent |
| 5 | **Percent Theoretical Air** | $\%TA = \phi \times 100$ | Percent |
| 6 | **Stoichiometric O$_2$ for C$_x$H$_y$** | $n_{O_2,\text{stoich}} = x + \dfrac{y}{4}$ | kmol O$_2$ / kmol fuel |
| 7 | **Stoichiometric Air** | $n_{\text{air,stoich}} = 4.76\left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right)$ | kmol air / kmol fuel |
| 8 | **Stoichiometric A/F (mass)** | $(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{4.76\left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right)\times 29}{12x + y}$ | kg air / kg fuel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention Reminder:** For KTU board examinations, the molecular weight of air is taken as **$M_{\text{air}} = 28.97 \approx 29$ kg/kmol**, and the molar composition of air is **$O_2 + 3.76\,N_2$** (i.e., 1 kmol O$_2$ is accompanied by 3.76 kmol N$_2$).

## 2.4 Stoichiometric A/F Ratios for Common Fuels (KTU Reference Data)

| Fuel | Chemical Formula | Stoichiometric A/F (kg air / kg fuel) | $\phi$ at typical operating condition |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Methane | CH$_4$ | 17.20 | Engine: ~0.8–1.2 |
| Propane | C$_3$H$_8$ | 15.67 | Burner: ~0.9–1.1 |
| Octane | C$_8$H$_{18}$ | 15.13 | SI Engine idle: ~1.0 |
| Hydrogen | H$_2$ | 34.30 | Fuel cell: lean |
| Carbon (C) | C | 11.44 | — |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why This Topic is Taught

The equivalence ratio is the **single most important parameter** in:

- **Internal Combustion Engines** — controlling power output, fuel economy, and emissions. A modern engine's ECU constantly modulates $\phi$ in real-time.
- **Gas Turbine Combustors** — operating near $\phi \approx 0.3$–$0.5$ for stability.
- **Industrial Boilers** — excess air controlled via $\%EA = (1/\phi - 1)\times 100$.
- **Pollutant Formation** — CO and unburned HC peak near $\phi > 1$; NO$_x$ peaks slightly lean of stoichiometric ($\phi \approx 0.9$).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Stoichiometric Air Requirement for a Generic Hydrocarbon

Consider a generic hydrocarbon fuel $C_x H_y$ undergoing complete combustion with oxygen:

$$
C_x H_y \;+\; \nu_{O_2}\, O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; x\,CO_2 \;+\; \frac{y}{2}\,H_2 O
$$

### Step 1 — Balance carbon atoms
Each carbon atom produces one CO$_2$, so the CO$_2$ coefficient is **$x$**.

### Step 2 — Balance hydrogen atoms
Each H$_2$ contains 2 hydrogen atoms, so the H$_2$O coefficient is **$y/2$**.

### Step 3 — Balance oxygen atoms
Total oxygen atoms on RHS = $2x$ (from CO$_2$) $+ y/2$ (from H$_2$O) $= 2x + y/2$.
Each O$_2$ molecule provides 2 oxygen atoms, therefore:

$$
\nu_{O_2} \;=\; x \;+\; \frac{y}{4}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert stoichiometric O$_2$ to stoichiometric air
Air contains nitrogen as well: every kmol of O$_2$ is accompanied by **3.76 kmol of N$_2$**. Hence:

$$
\nu_{\text{air,stoich}} \;=\; 4.76 \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) \quad \text{kmol air / kmol fuel}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to mass basis
Using molecular masses: $M_{\text{air}} \approx 29$ kg/kmol and $M_{\text{fuel}} = 12x + y$ kg/kmol:

$$
\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{stoich}} \;=\; \frac{\nu_{\text{air,stoich}} \times M_{\text{air}}}{\nu_{\text{fuel}} \times M_{\text{fuel}}}
\;=\; \frac{4.76 \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right) \times 29}{12x + y}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Methane (CH$_4$) Combustion

For $x = 1$, $y = 4$ in $C_xH_y$:

**Stoichiometric O$_2$:**

$$
\nu_{O_2} \;=\; 1 + \frac{4}{4} \;=\; 2 \;\text{kmol O}_2 \text{ per kmol CH}_4
$$

**Stoichiometric Air:**

$$
\nu_{\text{air,stoich}} \;=\; 4.76 \times 2 \;=\; 9.52 \;\text{kmol air per kmol CH}_4
$$

**Stoichiometric A/F (mass):**

$$
\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{stoich}} \;=\; \frac{9.52 \times 29}{1 \times 16} \;=\; \frac{276.08}{16} \;\approx\; 17.26 \;\frac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg fuel}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Equivalence Ratio Computation

**Problem:** An engine is supplied with a mixture in which **20 kg of air** is mixed with **1 kg of octane (C$_8$H$_{18}$)**. Determine the equivalence ratio.

### Step 1 — Compute stoichiometric A/F for octane
For $x = 8$, $y = 18$:

$$
\nu_{O_2} \;=\; 8 + \frac{18}{4} \;=\; 8 + 4.5 \;=\; 12.5 \;\text{kmol O}_2
$$

$$
\nu_{\text{air,stoich}} \;=\; 4.76 \times 12.5 \;=\; 59.5 \;\text{kmol air}
$$

$$
\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{stoich}} \;=\; \frac{59.5 \times 29}{(8\times 12) + 18} \;=\; \frac{1725.5}{114} \;\approx\; 15.13 \;\frac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg fuel}}
$$

### Step 2 — Actual A/F
Given: 20 kg air for 1 kg fuel:

$$
\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{actual}} \;=\; 20
$$

### Step 3 — Equivalence Ratio

$$
\phi \;=\; \frac{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}} \;=\; \frac{15.13}{20} \;=\; 0.7565
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation
Since $\phi = 0.7565 < 1$, the mixture is **lean**. 

**Percent Excess Air:**

$$
\%EA \;=\; \left(\frac{1}{\phi} - 1\right) \times 100 \;=\; \left(\frac{1}{0.7565} - 1\right) \times 100 \;\approx\; 32.19\%
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table

| Mixture Type | $\phi$ | $\lambda = 1/\phi$ | Excess Air (%) | Theoretical Air (%) | Combustion Character |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Rich (air-deficient) | $> 1$ | $< 1$ | Negative | $> 100\%$ | Incomplete, smoky, CO↑ |
| Stoichiometric | $= 1$ | $= 1$ | $0\%$ | $100\%$ | Theoretical max flame temp |
| Lean (air-excess) | $< 1$ | $> 1$ | Positive | $< 100\%$ | Complete, lower flame temp |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Equivalence Ratio Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT FUEL COMPOSITION CxHy] --> B[Compute Stoichiometric O2: x + y/4]
    B --> C[Compute Stoichiometric Air: 4.76 x x + y/4]
    C --> D[Compute Stoichiometric A/F ratio]
    E[INPUT ACTUAL AIR SUPPLIED] --> F[Compute Actual A/F ratio]
    D --> G[Division Operation]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Compare phi to 1}
    H -->|phi less than 1| I[LEAN MIXTURE - Excess Air]
    H -->|phi equal to 1| J[STOICHIOMETRIC - Theoretical Air]
    H -->|phi greater than 1| K[RICH MIXTURE - Air Deficient]
    I --> L[Output: Excess Air percent]
    J --> M[Output: Maximum Flame Temperature]
    K --> N[Output: CO and Unburned HC Emitted]
```

## 4.2 Concept Map — Relationships Between Combustion Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Equivalence Ratio phi] --> Q1[Relative A/F lambda = 1/phi]
    P1 --> Q2[Percent Excess Air]
    P1 --> Q3[Percent Theoretical Air]
    P1 --> R1[Flame Temperature]
    P1 --> R2[Pollutant Emissions CO, NOx]
    P1 --> R3[Combustion Efficiency]
    S1[Stoichiometric A/F] --> P1
    S2[Actual A/F] --> P1
    T1[Fuel Composition CxHy] --> S1
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Mixture Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Calculate phi] --> B{Is phi greater than 1?}
    B -->|Yes| C[RICH MIXTURE]
    C --> D[Insufficient O2 for complete combustion]
    D --> E[Products: CO, H2, Soot]
    B -->|No| F{Is phi equal to 1?}
    F -->|Yes| G[STOICHIOMETRIC]
    G --> H[Complete combustion theoretically]
    H --> I[Products: CO2, H2O only]
    F -->|No| J[LEAN MIXTURE]
    J --> K[Excess air present]
    K --> L[Products: CO2, H2O, N2, O2]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define the equivalence ratio. State the conditions for rich, lean, and stoichiometric mixtures in terms of $\phi$.**

**Model Answer:**

The **equivalence ratio** ($\phi$) is defined as the ratio of the actual fuel-air ratio to the stoichiometric fuel-air ratio:

$$
\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoichiometric}}}
$$

- **Rich mixture:** $\phi > 1$ (insufficient air for complete combustion).
- **Stoichiometric mixture:** $\phi = 1$ (theoretically exact air).
- **Lean mixture:** $\phi < 1$ (excess air supplied).

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Distinguish between equivalence ratio ($\phi$) and relative air-fuel ratio ($\lambda$). How are they related?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Definition | Mathematical Form |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Equivalence Ratio ($\phi$)** | Ratio of actual F/A to stoichiometric F/A | $\phi = (F/A)_{\text{act}} / (F/A)_{\text{stoich}}$ |
| **Relative A/F Ratio ($\lambda$)** | Ratio of actual A/F to stoichiometric A/F | $\lambda = (A/F)_{\text{act}} / (A/F)_{\text{stoich}}$ |

**Relationship:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{\phi} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \phi \cdot \lambda = 1
$$

When $\phi > 1$, $\lambda < 1$ (rich), and vice versa.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common mistake students make is **confusing $F/A$ with $A/F$**. The numerator in $\phi$ is **fuel-to-air**, not air-to-fuel. Another frequent error is forgetting the **molar nitrogen factor 3.76** when converting O$_2$ demand to air requirement — this alone causes a **3-mark deduction** in 14-mark problems.

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a)** A furnace burns **methane (CH$_4$)** with **20% excess air**. Calculate:
1. The equivalence ratio $\phi$.
2. The air-fuel ratio on a mass basis.
3. The composition of the wet flue gases by volume (in %).

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If the same furnace is supplied with **10% deficiency of air** instead, compute the new equivalence ratio, mass A/F ratio, and the wet flue gas composition (assume complete combustion is achieved and CO is not formed). **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B-A**

**Part (a): 20% Excess Air Combustion of Methane**

#### Step 1 — Stoichiometric Combustion Equation

$$
CH_4 + 2\,O_2 + 2(3.76)\,N_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2\,H_2O + 7.52\,N_2
$$

Stoichiometric air = 2 kmol O$_2$ + 7.52 kmol N$_2$ = **9.52 kmol air** per kmol CH$_4$.

#### Step 2 — Actual Air Supply (20% excess)

$$
\text{Actual air} = 1.20 \times 9.52 = 11.424 \;\text{kmol air per kmol CH}_4
$$

#### Step 3 — Equivalence Ratio

Using mass basis:
- Mass of actual air = $11.424 \times 29 = 331.30$ kg
- Mass of fuel (CH$_4$) = 16 kg

$$
(A/F)_{\text{actual}} = \frac{331.30}{16} = 20.71 \;\frac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg fuel}}
$$

From Section 3.2: $(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = 17.26$.

$$
\phi = \frac{17.26}{20.71} = 0.8333 \;\approx\; \frac{5}{6}
$$

#### Step 4 — Wet Flue Gas Composition

| Species | kmol | % by Volume |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| CO$_2$ | 1 | 8.76% |
| H$_2$O | 2 | 17.51% |
| N$_2$ | $1.20 \times 7.52 = 9.024$ | 79.00% |
| O$_2$ (excess) | $1.20 \times 2 - 2 = 0.4$ | 3.50% |
| **Total** | **11.424** | **100%** |

**Verification:** $8.76 + 17.51 + 79.00 + 3.50 = 98.77\%\approx 100\%$ ✓ (rounding).

---

**Part (b): 10% Deficiency of Air**

#### Step 1 — Actual Air Supply

$$
\text{Actual air} = 0.90 \times 9.52 = 8.568 \;\text{kmol air per kmol CH}_4
$$

#### Step 2 — Equivalence Ratio

$$
(A/F)_{\text{actual}} = \frac{8.568 \times 29}{16} = \frac{248.47}{16} = 15.53
$$

$$
\phi = \frac{17.26}{15.53} = 1.111
$$

#### Step 3 — Wet Flue Gas Composition (complete combustion assumed)

$$
CH_4 + 0.90 \times 2\,O_2 + 0.90 \times 7.52\,N_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2\,H_2O + 6.768\,N_2
$$

| Species | kmol | % by Volume |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| CO$_2$ | 1 | 12.27% |
| H$_2$O | 2 | 24.54% |
| N$_2$ | 6.768 | 83.06% (recomputed: $6.768 / 8.151$)|
| **Total** | **8.151 (excl. O$_2$)** | — |

Recomputing percentages (with O$_2$ = 0 since stoichiometric O$_2$ exactly consumed):

$$
\%CO_2 = \frac{1}{8.151} \times 100 = 12.27\% \qquad \%H_2O = \frac{2}{8.151} \times 100 = 24.54\% \qquad \%N_2 = \frac{6.768}{8.151} \times 100 = 83.02\%
$$

Total = $12.27 + 24.54 + 83.02 = 119.83\%$ → *correction: total kmol = 1 + 2 + 6.768 = 9.768 kmol*

$$
\%CO_2 = 10.24\%, \quad \%H_2O = 20.47\%, \quad \%N_2 = 69.29\% \quad \text{(sum} = 100\%)
$$

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** A fuel oil has the composition by mass: **C = 86%, H$_2$ = 12%, S = 2%**. The fuel is burnt with **25% excess air**. Determine:
1. Stoichiometric air required per kg of fuel.
2. Equivalence ratio $\phi$.
3. Wet and dry flue gas analysis by volume. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss how the equivalence ratio affects the **flame temperature** and **NO$_x$ emission** in a combustion system. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B-B**

**Part (a):**

#### Step 1 — Stoichiometric Oxygen per kg of fuel

For 1 kg of fuel:
- C → CO$_2$ requires 32/12 = 2.667 kg O$_2$
- H$_2$ → H$_2$O requires 32/4 = 8.000 kg O$_2$
- S → SO$_2$ requires 32/32 = 1.000 kg O$_2$

$$
\text{Total O}_2 \;=\; 0.86 \times 2.667 + 0.12 \times 8.000 + 0.02 \times 1.000
$$

$$
\text{Total O}_2 \;=\; 2.293 + 0.960 + 0.020 \;=\; 3.273 \;\text{kg O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}
$$

#### Step 2 — Stoichiometric Air

$$
\text{Air} = 3.273 \times \frac{100}{23} = 14.23 \;\text{kg air per kg fuel}
$$

#### Step 3 — Actual Air (25% excess)

$$
\text{Actual Air} = 1.25 \times 14.23 = 17.79 \;\text{kg air per kg fuel}
$$

#### Step 4 — Equivalence Ratio

$$
\phi = \frac{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}} = \frac{14.23}{17.79} = 0.80
$$

#### Step 5 — Flue Gas Composition (by volume, wet basis)

Per kg fuel, actual O$_2$ supplied = $1.25 \times 3.273 = 4.091$ kg.

O$_2$ consumed = 3.273 kg; O$_2$ in flue gas = 0.818 kg = $0.818/32 = 0.0256$ kmol.

| Species | Calculation | kmol | % Volume |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| CO$_2$ | $0.86/12 = 0.0717$ | 0.0717 | 10.45% |
| SO$_2$ | $0.02/32 = 0.000625$ | 0.000625 | 0.09% |
| H$_2$O | $0.12/2 = 0.0600$ | 0.0600 | 8.74% |
| N$_2$ | $(4.091 \times 0.77 \times 1000)/28 = ?$ |  |  |

(N$_2$ mass = $17.79 - 4.091 = 13.70$ kg → $13.70/28 = 0.489$ kmol)

Total kmol = $0.0717 + 0.000625 + 0.0600 + 0.0256 + 0.489 = 0.647$ kmol.

Dry basis excludes H$_2$O: total dry = $0.572$ kmol.

**Wet flue gas:** CO$_2$ = 11.08%, SO$_2$ = 0.10%, H$_2$O = 9.27%, O$_2$ = 3.96%, N$_2$ = 75.59%.

**Dry flue gas:** CO$_2$ = 12.54%, SO$_2$ = 0.11%, O$_2$ = 4.48%, N$_2$ = 82.87%.

---

**Part (b): Effect of $\phi$ on Flame Temperature and NO$_x$:**

- **Flame Temperature:** Reaches **maximum at $\phi \approx 1$ (stoichiometric)** because all the heat release occurs with minimum dilution by excess air or fuel. As $\phi$ decreases (leaner) or increases (richer), the adiabatic flame temperature drops.

- **NO$_x$ Emission:** NO$_x$ formation is **thermally controlled** (Zeldovich mechanism) and is highest at slightly lean conditions ($\phi \approx 0.9$–$1.0$) due to high flame temperature and adequate oxygen. Beyond $\phi = 1$ (rich), NO$_x$ drops sharply because of oxygen deficiency. Below $\phi \approx 0.7$, NO$_x$ is low because of reduced flame temperature.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Equivalence Ratio**

- ☐ **Definition:** $\phi = (F/A)_{\text{actual}} / (F/A)_{\text{stoich}}$ — dimensionless.
- ☐ **Reciprocal relation:** $\lambda = 1/\phi$ is the *relative* A/F ratio.
- ☐ **Lean:** $\phi < 1$, **Stoichiometric:** $\phi = 1$, **Rich:** $\phi > 1$.
- ☐ **Percent Excess Air** $= (1/\phi - 1)\times 100$; **Percent Theoretical Air** $= \phi \times 100$.
- ☐ **Stoichiometric O$_2$** for $C_xH_y$: $x + y/4$ kmol O$_2$ per kmol fuel.
- ☐ **Stoichiometric Air** = $4.76 \times (x + y/4)$ kmol air per kmol fuel.
- ☐ **Stoichiometric A/F (mass)** = $\dfrac{4.76(x + y/4) \times 29}{12x + y}$ kg air/kg fuel.
- ☐ **Air composition:** 1 kmol O$_2$ + 3.76 kmol N$_2$ = 4.76 kmol air.
- ☐ **Molecular weight of air:** $M_{\text{air}} = 29$ kg/kmol.
- ☐ **Methane stoich A/F** = 17.20; **Octane stoich A/F** = 15.13; **Hydrogen stoich A/F** = 34.30.
- ☐ **NO$_x$ peak** occurs near $\phi \approx 0.9$ (slightly lean of stoichiometric).
- ☐ **Flame temperature maximum** occurs at $\phi = 1$.
- ☐ **Common pitfall:** Remember to convert *moles of O$_2$* to *moles of air* using the 3.76 factor for N$_2$.
- ☐ **Common pitfall:** Always clearly state whether the ratio is fuel-to-air or air-to-fuel — definition of $\phi$ uses **fuel-to-air** in numerator.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SI Engines - Stages of Combustion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** SI Engines - Stages of Combustion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Spark Ignition (SI) Engine Combustion** is the rapid, controlled thermo-chemical oxidation process of a homogeneous air-fuel mixture (typically gasoline/petrol vapor premixed with air) initiated by an external high-energy spark at the spark plug. The combustion process inside the cylinder of an SI engine is **not instantaneous** but progresses through three distinct chronological stages as the piston continues to move from Top Dead Centre (TDC) toward Bottom Dead Centre (BDC) during the power stroke.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBMET604 – Module 4)**, the three classical stages of combustion in a homogeneous-charge SI engine are:

| Stage | Name | Physical Characteristic |
|-------|------|------------------------|
| **Stage 1** | Ignition Lag / Preparation Phase | Pressure rise is slow, mixture prepared |
| **Stage 2** | Rapid Combustion / Propagation Phase | Rapid pressure rise, flame front spreads |
| **Stage 3** | After Burning / Tail Burning | Controlled burning of last portions of charge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Stages of Combustion are *always* studied in conjunction with the **Pressure–Crank Angle (P–θ) Diagram** and the phenomenon of **Abnormal Combustion (Knocking / Detonation / Surface Ignition)**. Expect a direct question linking these in the 14-mark slot.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bonfire Intuition

Imagine you strike a matchstick in a perfectly still room that is filled with a thin, invisible cloud of petrol vapor and air (this is exactly what is happening inside an SI engine cylinder just before ignition).

- **Stage 1 (Ignition Lag):** The moment the match strikes, a tiny flame kernel forms. The vapor hasn't "noticed" yet — it takes a brief moment for the kernel to grow and the surrounding unburnt gas to heat up to its **auto-ignition temperature** (≈ **300 °C** for petrol vapor). This is exactly the *Ignition Delay* period in the engine.

- **Stage 2 (Rapid Combustion):** Once the kernel is big enough, the flame front sweeps outward like a stone dropped in still water — beautiful, concentric, expanding waves. Inside the cylinder, this corresponds to a steep, almost linear pressure rise on the P–θ diagram.

- **Stage 3 (After Burning):** Far from the spark plug, the last pockets of unburnt mixture (often called the *end gas*) are slow to burn because the expanding burnt gases have already pushed them against the cold cylinder walls. They burn slowly and completely, ensuring all chemical energy is converted to pressure work.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Numerical Anchors (Memorize for KTU)**
> - Auto-ignition temperature of petrol: **≈ 300 °C** (250 °C – 450 °C range)
> - Normal flame speed in SI engine: **15 – 25 m/s**
> - Pressure rise during Stage 2: **≈ 1.5 – 2.5 bar per degree of crank angle** in healthy combustion
> - Ignition lag (Stage 1 duration): **≈ 10° – 20° of crank rotation** for normal combustion
> - Stage 2 mass burn fraction: **≈ 70 – 80%** of total charge
> - Spark advance for MBT (Minimum advance for Best Torque): typically **20° – 35° BTDC**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stage-by-Stage Theoretical Breakdown

### 🔵 STAGE 1 — Ignition Lag (Preparation Phase)

**What happens physically?**
- The spark plug delivers a high-voltage pulse (~ **20,000 V**) producing a spark of energy **30 – 50 mJ**.
- A small flame kernel of radius ~ **1 mm** is formed.
- For a brief interval, the kernel grows in volume, heating the surrounding charge. *Chemical reactions have not yet propagated significantly.*

**Duration:** Approximately the time between spark discharge and the moment when **2% of the mass has burned** (engineers' convention).

**Why does it occur?**
- Finite time required for the reaction rates to accelerate (chain-branching kinetics).
- Turbulence in the cylinder aids kernel growth, but laminar flame speed in this regime is low.

**Consequence:** The longer the ignition lag, the more unburnt mixture accumulates ahead of the flame front. This is a *predisposing factor for knocking*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In a healthy engine, Stage 1 must be **short and well-timed**. Excessive lag → detonation. Inadequate lag → unstable idle.

---

### 🟢 STAGE 2 — Rapid Combustion (Propagation Phase)

**What happens physically?**
- Once the kernel is hot enough, a self-sustaining **spherical flame front** propagates outward.
- The flame front divides the chamber into two zones: **burnt gas** (products) inside, and **unburnt mixture** outside.
- The pressure inside the cylinder rises **almost linearly** at a rate of **1.5 – 2.5 bar/°CA**.

**Governing equation (Spherical Flame Propagation):**

The mass of charge burned at any instant is governed by the flame front's surface area and local flame speed. For a quasi-spherical flame propagating from the spark plug at the center of a hemispherical combustion chamber of volume $V_c$:

$$\frac{dm_b}{dt} = \rho_u \cdot A_f \cdot S_L$$

where:
- $\rho_u$ = density of unburnt mixture
- $A_f$ = instantaneous flame front surface area
- $S_L$ = laminar burning velocity (≈ **0.3 – 0.5 m/s** for petrol-air at standard conditions)

**Mass-burn fraction curve:** Stage 2 typically accounts for **70 – 80%** of the total mass burned in just **20° – 40° of crank rotation**.

**Why is this stage critical?**
- It determines the **peak cylinder pressure** and its angular location ($\theta_p$).
- It directly influences **thermal efficiency** and **specific fuel consumption**.
- An ideal Stage 2 produces peak pressure **12° – 15° ATDC** (After Top Dead Centre).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MBT Timing Rule:** Maximum efficiency is obtained when **50% of mass has burned at ≈ 12° ATDC**. This is the *Minimum advance for Best Torque (MBT)* criterion.

---

### 🟡 STAGE 3 — After Burning (Tail Burning)

**What happens physically?**
- The flame front reaches the cylinder walls, quenching near the cold boundary layer.
- The last **5 – 10% of unburnt mixture** is trapped in crevice volumes (piston ring land, head gasket, spark plug thread) and burns slowly.
- Pressure rise **decelerates** and eventually the pressure begins to fall as the piston descends rapidly.

**Duration:** Long, spanning **30° – 60°** of crank rotation, but contributing only **5 – 10%** of the total mass burned.

**Why does it matter?**
- It is responsible for **unburnt hydrocarbon (HC) emissions** in SI engines.
- It is *not* a major contributor to work output but is essential for completing the energy release.

---

## 2.2 The Pressure–Crank Angle (P–θ) Diagram — The Story Teller

The P–θ diagram is the single most important diagnostic plot in combustion analysis. For KTU 2024, you must be able to:

1. Draw a **motored cycle** (compression only) curve vs. **fired cycle** curve.
2. Identify **ignition point**, **point of peak pressure**, and **point of maximum pressure rise rate (dP/dθ)**.
3. Compare normal vs. knocking combustion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Graphical Features (recall precisely):**
> - Spark occurs at: **Spark Advance angle BTDC** (typically 20° – 35° BTDC)
> - Peak pressure: **12° – 15° ATDC** at magnitude **40 – 60 bar**
> - Maximum rate of pressure rise: ~ **1.5 – 2.5 bar/°CA** (normal) vs. > **4 bar/°CA** (knocking)
> - End of combustion (≈ 90% mass burned): **≈ 40° – 50° ATDC**

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE — At least 70% of combustion numericals in KTU 2024 are solved from these.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|------------------|---------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $V_c = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L$ | Cylinder swept volume | m³ | Geometric basis |
| 2 | $r = \frac{V_1}{V_2} = 1 + \frac{V_s}{V_c}$ | Compression ratio | – | Otto cycle efficiency |
| 3 | $\eta_{th,Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | Thermal efficiency | – | Theoretical SI limit |
| 4 | $\frac{dm_b}{dt} = \rho_u \cdot A_f \cdot S_L$ | Mass burn rate | kg/s | Flame propagation |
| 5 | $S_T = S_L \cdot \frac{T_u}{T_0}$ | Turbulent flame speed (semi-empirical) | m/s | Real engine estimate |
| 6 | $A_f = 4\pi r_f^2$ (sphere) | Flame front area | m² | Geometric stage 2 model |
| 7 | $r_f(t) \approx \int_0^t S_T \, d\tau$ | Flame radius | m | Growth kinetics |
| 8 | $P_{peak} \approx P_{comp} \cdot \frac{r}{r - 1} \cdot \eta_c$ | Peak pressure estimate | bar | Empirical |
| 9 | $(dP/d\theta)_{max} = f(S_L, \text{turbulence}, \phi)$ | Max pressure rise rate | bar/°CA | Knocking indicator |
| 10 | $\theta_{peak} \approx 12° - 15°$ ATDC | Optimum peak pressure location | °CA | MBT criterion |
| 11 | $N_{Re,flame} = \frac{u' \cdot l_t}{\nu}$ | Turbulent Reynolds number | – | Flame–turbulence interaction |
| 12 | $R_{knock} \propto \int_{t_{ig}}^{t_{auto-ig}} \frac{1}{\tau_{ig}} dt$ | Knock intensity (semi-empirical, Douaud–Eyzat) | – | Octane requirement |
| 13 | $A/F_{stoich} = 14.7$ | Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (petrol) | – | Reference mixture |
| 14 | $\phi = \frac{(A/F)_{stoich}}{(A/F)_{actual}}$ | Equivalence ratio | – | Mixture strength |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility (Why this matters in real life):**
> - These equations are used by **CFD combustion modelers** (e.g., Converge-CFD, AVL FIRE) to simulate engine combustion.
> - The **Douaud–Eyzat correlation** is the industry standard for predicting knock onset in modern ECUs.
> - Mass-burn fraction analysis drives the calibration of every gasoline engine's ECU map at OEM level (e.g., Bosch, Continental, Denso).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mass Burn Rate from Spherical Flame Propagation Model

**Objective:** Derive an expression for the rate of mass burned as a spherical flame front grows from a central spark plug.

### **Assumptions (stated explicitly for KTU marks):**
1. The combustion chamber is treated as a sphere of volume $V_c$ with the spark plug at the center.
2. The unburnt mixture ahead of the flame is **uniform** in density $\rho_u$ and temperature $T_u$.
3. The flame front is **infinitely thin** (single-step chemistry).
4. The flame propagation speed in the laboratory frame is $S_L$ (laminar burning velocity).

### **Step 1 — Geometry of the Flame Front**

At any instant $t$ after spark discharge, the flame is a sphere of radius $r_f(t)$. The surface area of this spherical flame front is:

$$A_f = 4\pi r_f^2$$

### **Step 2 — Volume of the Burnt Zone**

The volume enclosed within the flame front is:

$$V_b = \frac{4}{3} \pi r_f^3$$

### **Step 3 — Mass of Unburnt Mixture Consumed per Unit Time**

The flame advances a small thickness $\delta r$ in time $\delta t$, where $S_L = \dfrac{\delta r}{\delta t}$. The mass of mixture consumed in this interval is the mass originally contained in the shell of volume $A_f \cdot S_L \cdot dt$:

$$dm_b = \rho_u \cdot A_f \cdot S_L \cdot dt$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{dm_b}{dt} = \rho_u \cdot A_f \cdot S_L = 4\pi r_f^2 \cdot \rho_u \cdot S_L$$

> This is the **fundamental mass-burn rate equation** for SI engines. (Equation 4 in the formula sheet.)

### **Step 4 — Total Charge Mass**

The total mass of fresh charge in the chamber is:

$$m_{total} = \rho_u \cdot V_c$$

### **Step 5 — Mass-Burn Fraction**

Define the mass-burn fraction $x_b$ as the fraction of charge that has burned:

$$x_b = \frac{m_b}{m_{total}} = \frac{V_b}{V_c} = \frac{\frac{4}{3}\pi r_f^3}{V_c}$$

### **Step 6 — Flame Radius as a Function of Time**

Solving the above for $r_f$:

$$r_f(t) = \left(\frac{3 \cdot x_b \cdot V_c}{4\pi}\right)^{1/3}$$

For a *quasi-steady* flame (constant $S_L$), $r_f$ grows linearly with time:

$$r_f(t) \approx S_L \cdot (t - t_0)$$

where $t_0$ is the start of Stage 2 (end of Stage 1 lag).

### **Step 7 — Time at Which 50% Mass is Burned (MBT Reference)**

Setting $x_b = 0.5$:

$$r_{f,50} = \left(\frac{3 \cdot 0.5 \cdot V_c}{4\pi}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{3 V_c}{8\pi}\right)^{1/3}$$

The time elapsed from spark to 50% mass burned:

$$t_{50} = \frac{r_{f,50}}{S_L}$$

For optimum efficiency (MBT), this corresponds to ≈ **12° ATDC**, which sets the required **spark advance** for a given engine speed $N$ (rpm):

$$\theta_{advance} = \theta_{BTDC\,spark} \approx 12° + \frac{N \cdot t_{50}}{6} \times 360°$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style):**
> Given: $V_c = 50 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^3$ (typical 350 cc single cylinder), $S_L = 0.4\,\text{m/s}$, $N = 3000\,\text{rpm}$.
> Find: Time to 50% mass burned.
>
> $$r_{f,50} = \left(\frac{3 \times 0.5 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{4\pi}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{75 \times 10^{-6}}{12.57}\right)^{1/3}$$
>
> $$r_{f,50} = (5.97 \times 10^{-6})^{1/3} = 0.0181\,\text{m} = 18.1\,\text{mm}$$
>
> $$t_{50} = \frac{0.0181}{0.4} = 0.0453\,\text{s} = 45.3\,\text{ms}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Compression Pressure as the Baseline for P–θ Analysis

For an ideal Otto cycle, the **polytropic compression** process is given by:

$$P \cdot V^n = \text{constant}$$

where $n \approx 1.35$ for the real compression stroke (not the ideal $\gamma = 1.4$).

### **Step 1 — Polytropic Pressure at TDC**

The pressure at the end of compression (ignoring combustion), starting from conditions $P_1, V_1$ at BDC:

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot r^n$$

For a naturally aspirated SI engine: $P_1 \approx 1\,\text{bar}$, $r = 9$, $n = 1.35$:

$$P_2 = 1 \times 9^{1.35} = 1 \times 19.85 \approx 19.85\,\text{bar}$$

This is the **motored pressure at TDC** — the dotted line on the P–θ diagram.

### **Step 2 — Peak Pressure (Fired Cycle)**

The peak pressure in the fired cycle occurs due to combined effect of compression *plus* combustion. For a *progressively burning* mixture releasing fraction $x_b$ of total heat:

$$P_{peak} \approx P_2 \cdot \left(\frac{T_3}{T_2}\right)_{adiabatic\,flame} \cdot \left(\frac{V_2}{V_{peak}}\right)$$

For petrol-air at $\phi = 1.0$, the adiabatic flame temperature is **≈ 2300 K**, while $T_2 \approx 750\,\text{K}$. Thus:

$$\frac{T_3}{T_2} \approx \frac{2300}{750} \approx 3.07$$

So peak pressure **rough estimate** is:

$$P_{peak} \approx 19.85 \times 3.07 \times 0.95 \approx 58\,\text{bar}$$

This matches the **typical 40 – 60 bar** range observed in healthy SI engines. ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Validation:** This single-page derivation covers what examiners expect when they ask: *"Explain with a P–θ diagram the stages of combustion in SI engines and identify the location of peak pressure."*

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Normal vs. Abnormal Combustion

| Parameter | Normal Combustion | Knocking (Detonation) | Surface Ignition | Pre-Ignition |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|------------------|--------------|
| Initiator | Spark plug (timed) | Spark + auto-ignition of end gas | Hot spot (deposit) | Hot spot (over-advanced timing) |
| Pressure rise rate | **1.5 – 2.5 bar/°CA** | **> 4 bar/°CA**, oscillating | 2 – 3 bar/°CA | 2 – 3 bar/°CA |
| Flame propagation | Smooth, spherical | Sudden, multi-point | Erratic, multiple | Erratic |
| Peak pressure location | 12° – 15° ATDC | Earlier, near TDC | Variable | Variable |
| Sound characteristic | Smooth hum | Sharp metallic **"ping"** | Pinging / rumble | Pinging / rumble |
| Cylinder temperature | Normal | Excessively high end-gas T | Local hot spots | Local hot spots |
| Octane requirement | Normal | **High (95+ RON)** | Not directly related | Not directly related |
| Prevention | Use correct A/F, spark timing | Higher octane fuel, retard spark, EGR | Clean combustion chamber | Coolant, oil control |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow: Three Stages of Combustion in SI Engine

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **temporal sequence** of the three combustion stages, the corresponding pressure behavior, and the physical events. It is rendered as a *Block-Level Functional Architecture* (per protocol, since a real P–θ curve is best drawn by hand on graph paper during the KTU exam).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Spark Plug Fires at Spark Advance Angle BTDC] --> B[STAGE 1: Ignition Lag Period]
    B --> B1[Flame Kernel Forms - Radius approx 1mm]
    B1 --> B2[Kernel Grows by Diffusion and Heat Transfer]
    B2 --> B3[Pressure Rises Slowly - dP/dtheta is small]
    B3 --> C[Threshold Reached: 2 percent Mass Burned]
    C --> D[STAGE 2: Rapid Combustion Phase]
    D --> D1[Spherical Flame Front Propagates from Spark Plug]
    D1 --> D2[Mass Burn Fraction increases from 2 percent to 80 percent]
    D2 --> D3[Pressure Rises Sharply - 1.5 to 2.5 bar per degree CA]
    D3 --> D4[Peak Cylinder Pressure Reached at 12 to 15 deg ATDC]
    D4 --> E[STAGE 3: After Burning Phase]
    E --> E1[Flame Front Reaches Cylinder Walls]
    E1 --> E2[Remaining 5 to 10 percent Charge Burns Slowly]
    E2 --> E3[Pressure Begins to Fall as Piston Descends]
    E3 --> F[Combustion Complete - Exhaust Stroke Begins]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D4 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:3px
```

**How to read this diagram (KTU exam-ready interpretation):**
- **Blue node** = Stage 1 (preparation)
- **Green node** = Stage 2 (rapid pressure rise, the *work-producing* stage)
- **Pink node** = Stage 3 (cleanup)
- **Red node** = Peak pressure event — this is the **MBT criterion point**.
