# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. Define the three stages of combustion in an SI engine.**  *(Model Answer Below)*

**Model Answer:**

The combustion process in a Spark Ignition (SI) engine is divided into three distinct stages based on the rate of pressure rise and the extent of mass burned:

1. **Stage 1 — Ignition Lag (Preparation Phase):** This is the interval between spark discharge and the instant when a measurable fraction (~2%) of the charge has burned. The pressure rise is slow, and a small flame kernel of radius ≈ 1 mm grows by heat conduction and diffusion.

2. **Stage 2 — Rapid Combustion (Propagation Phase):** A self-sustaining spherical flame front propagates outward from the spark plug. About **70–80% of the mass** burns in this stage within 20°–40° of crank rotation. Pressure rises sharply at **1.5–2.5 bar/°CA** and reaches a peak at **12°–15° ATDC** (the MBT criterion).

3. **Stage 3 — After Burning (Tail Burning):** The remaining **5–10% of the charge**, trapped in crevice volumes and near the cold cylinder walls, burns slowly over 30°–60° of crank rotation. It has little effect on work output but is responsible for unburnt hydrocarbon emissions.

> [!NOTE]
> *(3 marks: 1 mark for naming each stage + 0.5 mark for each physical event + 0.5 mark for stage duration/range — answer in clean bullet form for examiner readability.)*

---

### **Q2. What is meant by "Ignition Lag" in an SI engine and what factors affect it?**  *(Model Answer Below)*

**Model Answer:**

**Ignition Lag** is the time delay (in milliseconds or degrees of crank angle) between the instant the spark plug fires and the moment the combustion becomes self-sustaining. It is the duration of **Stage 1** of combustion.

**Factors Affecting Ignition Lag:**

1. **Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F):** Rich or lean mixtures (away from stoichiometric) have higher ignition lag.
2. **Turbulence Intensity:** Higher turbulence shortens the lag by enhancing kernel growth.
3. **Compression Ratio:** Higher CR → higher end-of-compression T → shorter lag.
4. **Spark Energy:** Insufficient spark energy (worn plug) increases lag.
5. **Charge Temperature & Pressure:** Higher T and P reduce lag.
6. **Fuel Properties:** Octane rating, volatility, and self-ignition temperature all affect lag.
7. **Residual Gas Fraction (EGR):** Higher EGR dilutes charge → longer lag.

> [!NOTE]
> *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for factors — list 4–5 factors with brief justification. Examiner rewards breadth.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> The KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) follows an **Internal Choice** model. You are given a choice between two 14-mark questions, each typically divided into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Below are TWO fully worked-out alternative questions.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Stages + P–θ Diagram*

`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, May 2024]`

#### **(a)** With the help of a neat **Pressure–Crank Angle (P–θ) diagram**, explain the **three stages of combustion** in a Spark Ignition engine. Clearly mark the ignition point, peak pressure location, and identify the regions of slow and rapid pressure rise. **(7 Marks)**

#### **(b)** A four-cylinder, four-stroke SI engine has a bore of **80 mm** and stroke of **90 mm**. At a peak pressure of **55 bar**, the corresponding crank angle is **12° ATDC**. The compression ratio is **9:1** and the motored pressure at TDC (without combustion) is **20 bar**. Calculate:
- (i) The percentage increase in peak pressure due to combustion.
- (ii) The average rate of pressure rise between spark timing (25° BTDC) and peak pressure, in **bar/°CA**.
- (iii) Comment on whether this indicates normal or knocking combustion. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION A**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Diagram (describe verbally for the answer script):**

```
   P (bar)
   ↑
55 |          ● ← PEAK (12° ATDC, Stage 2 end)
50 |        ╱  ╲  ← Stage 2 (Rapid Combustion)
45 |      ╱      ╲
40 |    ╱          ╲
35 |  ╱              ╲
30 |╱                 ╲  ← Stage 3 (After Burning)
25 |                    ╲___ Spark fires (25° BTDC)
20 |- - - - - - - - - -●- - - - ← MOTORED CURVE
15 |              ___---╲
10 |        ___---        ╲___
 5 |  ___---                  ╲___
 0 |________________________________→ θ (Crank Angle)
   BDC         BTDC  TDC        ATDC      BDC
              Spark            Peak P
```

**Stages explanation:**

| Stage | Angle Range | Description |
|-------|-------------|-------------|
| **Stage 1: Ignition Lag** | 25° BTDC → ~10° BTDC | Pressure rises slowly from motored curve. Flame kernel grows. 0–2% mass burned. |
| **Stage 2: Rapid Combustion** | ~10° BTDC → 12° ATDC | Steep, near-linear pressure rise. 2% → 80% mass burned. Peak pressure attained at 12° ATDC. |
| **Stage 3: After Burning** | 12° ATDC → 50° ATDC | Pressure falls but stays above motored curve. Last 10% mass burns slowly. |

*(Diagram 3 marks + Stage explanation 3 marks + Marking 1 mark = 7 marks)*

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given Data:**
- $D = 80\,\text{mm} = 0.08\,\text{m}$, $L = 90\,\text{mm} = 0.09\,\text{m}$
- $P_{peak} = 55\,\text{bar}$, $\theta_{peak} = 12°\,\text{ATDC}$
- $P_{motored,TDC} = 20\,\text{bar}$
- Spark timing: $\theta_{spark} = 25°\,\text{BTDC}$

**(i) Percentage increase in peak pressure due to combustion:**

$$\Delta P = P_{peak} - P_{motored,TDC} = 55 - 20 = 35\,\text{bar}$$

$$\%\text{ increase} = \frac{35}{20} \times 100 = \mathbf{175\%}$$

**(ii) Average rate of pressure rise between spark and peak:**

Total crank angle from spark (25° BTDC) to peak (12° ATDC) = 25 + 12 = **37° CA**

$$\left(\frac{dP}{d\theta}\right)_{avg} = \frac{55 - P_{spark}}{37}$$

We need $P_{spark}$. At 25° BTDC, the motored pressure (polytropic $P_2 = P_1 r^n$, with $n = 1.35$) is:

$$\frac{P_{spark}}{P_1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_{spark}}\right)^{1.35}$$

At 25° BTDC, $\theta = 155°$ from BDC. Position $x = \frac{L}{2}(1 - \cos\theta) = 0.045(1 - \cos 155°)$

$\cos 155° = -0.906$

$$x = 0.045 \times 1.906 = 0.0858\,\text{m}$$

Total cylinder volume at this position:

$$V_{spark} = V_c + \frac{\pi}{4}D^2 \cdot x = V_c + A_p \cdot x$$

Compression ratio $r = 9$ means $V_1 = 9 V_c$, so $V_s = 8 V_c$.

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.08)^2(0.09) = 4.524 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3$$
$$V_c = \frac{V_s}{r-1} = \frac{4.524 \times 10^{-4}}{8} = 5.655 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^3$$
$$V_1 = 9 \times 5.655 \times 10^{-5} = 5.09 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3$$

Volume at 25° BTDC:

$$V_{spark} = V_c + A_p \cdot x = 5.655 \times 10^{-5} + (5.027 \times 10^{-3})(0.0858) = 4.879 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3$$

Now:

$$P_{spark} = 1 \times \left(\frac{5.09 \times 10^{-4}}{4.879 \times 10^{-4}}\right)^{1.35} = (1.043)^{1.35} = 1.0587 \times 1 = 1.06\,\text{bar}$$

Hmm — this seems too low. Let me recheck. Actually at 25° BTDC, the piston is very close to TDC, so $P_{spark}$ should be much higher. Let me recompute $x$ correctly.

Recheck: at 25° BTDC, crank angle from TDC = 25°. Using the standard formula:

$$x = \frac{L}{2}\left(1 - \cos(180° - 25°)\right) = \frac{L}{2}\left(1 - \cos(155°)\right)$$

Wait, the correct kinematic relation is:

$$x = r_c\left(\cos\theta - \cos(180°)\right) + \frac{L}{2} - 0$$

Actually the standard form is: position from TDC = $r_c(1 - \cos\theta) + \frac{L}{2}(1 - \sqrt{1 - (r_c/L)^2 \sin^2\theta})$

But for a simple slider-crank with $r_c/L = 0.25$ (typical), at 25° BTDC:

$$x \approx \frac{L}{2}(1 - \cos 25°) = 0.045(1 - 0.906) = 0.045 \times 0.094 = 0.00423\,\text{m}$$

This is the correct distance from TDC at 25° BTDC. So:

$$V_{spark} = V_c + A_p \cdot x = 5.655 \times 10^{-5} + 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.00423 = 5.655 \times 10^{-5} + 2.127 \times 10^{-5} = 7.78 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^3$$

Now:

$$P_{spark} = 1 \times \left(\frac{5.09 \times 10^{-4}}{7.78 \times 10^{-5}}\right)^{1.35} = (6.54)^{1.35} = 12.8\,\text{bar}$$

So:

$$\left(\frac{dP}{d\theta}\right)_{avg} = \frac{55 - 12.8}{37} = \frac{42.2}{37} = \mathbf{1.14\,\text{bar/°CA}}$$

**(iii) Comment on combustion type:**

A maximum rate of pressure rise of **1.14 bar/°CA** (averaged) and a peak pressure of **55 bar** at **12° ATDC** are both consistent with **normal (non-knocking) combustion** in an SI engine. The recommended range for normal combustion is **1.5 – 2.5 bar/°CA** for *maximum* local rate, with the average being lower. Knocking is typically indicated by rates **> 4 bar/°CA** with sharp pressure oscillations visible on a high-speed P–θ trace. The given data shows no evidence of abnormal combustion.

**Mark distribution:** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 4 marks, (iii) 1 mark.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Ignition Lag + Abnormal Combustion*

`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Dec 2023]`

#### **(a)** What is **Ignition Lag** in an SI engine? Explain the physical and chemical factors affecting ignition lag. Discuss how a longer ignition lag influences the susceptibility of the engine to **knocking**. **(7 Marks)**

#### **(b)** Explain the phenomenon of **knocking (detonation)** in SI engines. With a comparative P–θ diagram, differentiate between:
- (i) Normal combustion
- (ii) Knocking combustion

Discuss **four design and operating parameters** that can be modified to suppress knocking. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION B**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Ignition lag is the time interval (typically measured in milliseconds or degrees of crank rotation) between the instant of spark discharge at the spark plug and the moment when a self-sustaining, measurable fraction (conventionally ~2%) of the charge mass has been burned.

**Physical factors affecting ignition lag (3 marks):**

1. **Temperature of the charge:** Higher end-of-compression temperature (T₂) shortens the lag by accelerating the chemical kinetics of the unburnt mixture.
2. **Pressure of the charge:** Higher P₂ increases molecular collision frequency, accelerating the pre-flame reactions.
3. **Turbulence intensity:** Turbulence enhances flame kernel growth by improving heat and mass transfer; highly turbulent SI engines (modern DISI, TGDI) have shorter lags.
4. **Spark plug condition:** A worn spark plug delivers less energy and produces a smaller initial kernel, prolonging the lag.
5. **Residual gas fraction (EGR):** Inert residual gases dilute the charge and lower its specific heat release rate, lengthening the lag.

**Chemical factors affecting ignition lag (2 marks):**

1. **Fuel octane rating:** Higher-octane fuels have higher auto-ignition temperatures and longer ignition lags. This is *intentional* — it is what allows the spark to initiate controlled combustion before auto-ignition can occur.
2. **Air-Fuel ratio:** Mixtures close to stoichiometric have shortest lags; very rich or very lean mixtures have longer lags.
3. **Fuel composition:** Aromatics, paraffins, and oxygenates influence radical pool formation during the pre-flame reactions.

**Relation to knocking (1 mark):**
A longer ignition lag means that more unburnt charge accumulates in the end-gas region *before* the flame front arrives. This accumulated end-gas is exposed to high pressure and high temperature for a longer time, making it more likely to **auto-ignite spontaneously** ahead of the flame front. This spontaneous auto-ignition is the root cause of **knocking**. Therefore, fuels with shorter lag are more knock-resistant *only if* the chemistry is balanced — generally, fuels with the *right amount* of lag (matched to engine design) knock less.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Knocking Phenomenon (2 marks):**
Knocking (or detonation) is the abnormal combustion phenomenon in SI engines characterized by:
- Spontaneous, uncontrolled auto-ignition of the end-gas (unburnt mixture ahead of the flame front).
- Very rapid pressure rise (> 4 bar/°CA) with characteristic high-frequency oscillations in the P–θ trace.
- Audible "ping" or metallic rattling sound.
- Localized extreme cylinder pressures and temperatures that can cause piston, ring, and head damage.

**Comparative P–θ Diagram (2 marks):**

```
   P (bar)                P (bar)
   ↑                       ↑
55 |       ●  (Normal)    65|  ⌒⌒⌒⌒⌒ ← Knocking
50 |     ╱                  60| ╱ ╲ ╱ ╲ ⌒  (Pressure Oscillations)
45 |   ╱                   50|╱   ╲╱   ╲
40 | ╱                     40|       ╲___
30 |╱                      30|          ╲___
20 |- - - - - - - - - - 20|- - - - - - ●- -
                            (Motored)  (Auto-ignition
                                       of end-gas)
   Normal Combustion:       Knocking Combustion:
   - Smooth curve            - Sudden, sharp spike
   - Peak at 12-15° ATDC     - Peak near TDC or earlier
   - No oscillations         - High-frequency oscillations
```

**Four Design/Operating Parameters to Suppress Knocking (3 marks):**

1. **Increase the octane rating of the fuel:** Use premium gasoline (95+ RON) or fuels with anti-knock additives (e.g., MTBE, ethanol blends). Higher octane raises the auto-ignition temperature, preventing end-gas auto-ignition.

2. **Reduce compression ratio:** Lower CR (e.g., from 10:1 to 8:1) reduces the end-of-compression temperature, which is the most direct way to suppress knock. (Trade-off: lower thermal efficiency.)

3. **Retard spark timing:** Moving the spark later (smaller advance) reduces the time available for end-gas to auto-ignite. Modern ECUs use **knock sensors** (piezoelectric accelerometers on the block) to detect knock and retard timing in real time.

4. **Use Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR):** Introducing inert exhaust gases dilutes the unburnt charge, lowering peak flame temperatures and increasing the specific heat capacity of the end-gas, which suppresses auto-ignition.

*Other acceptable parameters: charge cooling (water/methanol injection), direct injection stratified charge, downsizing with turbocharging (to reduce knock tendency by reducing effective CR), use of intercoolers.*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS — READ BEFORE EXAM!**
>
> 1. **Confusing Ignition Lag (SI engine) with Ignition Delay (CI engine):** In SI engines, the lag is *physical-chemical delay* before flame kernel establishes; in CI engines, it is the *physical + chemical* delay between injection and auto-ignition. Many students write SI answers using CI terminology — *immediate loss of 1 mark*.
>
> 2. **Wrong peak pressure location:** Students frequently state that peak pressure occurs *ATDC = 0° (TDC)*. **The correct MBT location is 12°–15° ATDC.** Examiners will deduct 0.5–1 mark for this error.
>
> 3. **Sketching P–θ diagram without the MOTORED curve:** Without the dotted motored curve, you lose 1–2 marks because the area between curves (= net work) cannot be identified.
>
> 4. **Using "detonation" and "knocking" interchangeably without qualification:** Knocking is the *audible symptom*; detonation is the *physical mechanism* (auto-ignition of end gas). The proper phrasing is "Knocking is the audible manifestation of detonation."
>
> 5. **Missing units in the P-θ rate calculation:** Always write **(bar/°CA)** — not just "(rate)". Examiners award 0.5 mark for correct unit.
>
> 6. **Stages of Combustion = only 2 stages listed:** Some students forget Stage 3 (After Burning). This is a 1-mark deduction per stage missed.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLETS — Memorize These 24 Points Before KTU Exam**

- [x] SI engine = **Spark Ignition**, fuel = petrol/gasoline, mixture = **homogeneous** (premixed)
- [x] Auto-ignition temperature of petrol ≈ **300 °C**
- [x] **Three stages** of combustion = Ignition Lag → Rapid Combustion → After Burning
- [x] **Stage 1 (Ignition Lag):** 0 → 2% mass burned, slow pressure rise, kernel forms
- [x] **Stage 2 (Rapid Combustion):** 2% → 80% mass burned in 20°–40° CA
- [x] **Stage 3 (After Burning):** last 5–10% mass, slow, contributes to HC emissions
- [x] Spark energy: **30 – 50 mJ**; spark voltage: **~ 20 kV**
- [x] Laminar flame speed $S_L$ ≈ **0.3 – 0.5 m/s**; turbulent flame speed $S_T$ ≈ **15 – 25 m/s**
- [x] Peak pressure in normal combustion: **40 – 60 bar**
- [x] Peak pressure location: **12° – 15° ATDC** (MBT criterion)
- [x] Max pressure rise rate normal: **1.5 – 2.5 bar/°CA**; knocking: **> 4 bar/°CA**
- [x] Mass burn rate equation: $\frac{dm_b}{dt} = \rho_u A_f S_L$
- [x] Flame front area for spherical propagation: $A_f = 4\pi r_f^2$
- [x] P–θ diagram has both **motored** (dotted) and **fired** (solid) curves
- [x] **Area between curves = net work per cycle**
- [x] **Knocking** = audible symptom of **detonation** (auto-ignition of end-gas)
- [x] Causes of knocking: low octane fuel, high CR, high intake T, advanced timing, low speed
- [x] Anti-knock measures: high octane fuel, lower CR, retard spark, EGR, intercooler
- [x] **Pre-ignition:** ignition by hot spot *before* spark fires
- [x] **Surface ignition:** ignition by hot spot *after* spark fires (can be normal or pre-ignition)
- [x] Stoichiometric A/F for petrol: **14.7:1**
- [x] Equivalence ratio $\phi = (A/F)_{stoich}/(A/F)_{actual}$
- [x] MBT = Minimum advance for Best Torque
- [x] **Douaud–Eyzat correlation** is the industry standard for knock prediction

> **END OF NOTES — SI Engines: Stages of Combustion (PBMET604, Module 4)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ignition Lag
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ignition Lag

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Core Definition (KTU Board Standard)**
> **Ignition Lag** (also called **Ignition Delay** or **Delay Period**) is defined as the time interval, expressed either in **degrees of crank angle (°CA)** or in **milliseconds (ms)**, between the **start of fuel injection** into the combustion chamber and the **start of combustion** (first appearance of a self-sustaining flame kernel) in a Compression Ignition (CI / Diesel) engine.
>
> In a Spark Ignition (SI) engine, the corresponding concept is the time between the **spark discharge** and the **measurable rise in cylinder pressure** due to combustion onset.

Mathematically, for a CI engine:

$$
\theta_{lag} = \theta_{inj} - \theta_{comb}
$$

where $\theta_{lag}$ is the ignition lag, $\theta_{inj}$ is the crank angle at start of injection, and $\theta_{comb}$ is the crank angle at start of combustion.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **The "Stove & Kerosene" Analogy**
> Imagine you are lighting a kerosene stove in a cold room:
> 1. You press the pump (fuel injected), but the wick doesn't catch fire *instantly*.
> 2. The kerosene must first **absorb heat** from the surroundings, **vaporize**, and **mix with air** before a spark or flame can ignite it.
> 3. Only after a small but **finite delay** does the flame become self-sustaining.
>
> This delay between "fuel availability" and "flame onset" is **Ignition Lag**. The same phenomenon occurs inside a diesel engine cylinder — the fuel spray must atomize, vaporize, mix with hot compressed air, and undergo pre-flame chemical reactions before combustion begins.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range (CI Engine) | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Ignition Lag (modern DI Diesel) | **0.5 – 2.0** | ms |
| Ignition Lag in terms of crank angle | **6° – 15°** | °CA |
| Critical reference temperature | **~ 750 K** (auto-ignition threshold) | Kelvin |
| Engine speed range of relevance | **1500 – 4000** | rpm |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Syllabus Highlight**
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4, *Ignition Lag* is treated as a **prerequisite concept** for understanding **Diesel Knock**, **Combustion Chambers**, and **Fuel Injection Systems**. Mastery of this topic is essential to answer full-mark derivations on combustion phasing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Two Sub-Components of Ignition Lag (Physical Lag + Chemical Lag)

The total delay period in a CI engine is broadly divided into **two sequential sub-phases**:

#### 2.1.1 Physical Lag (Delay Period / Preparation Phase)
* The fuel jet enters the combustion chamber at very high pressure (200 – 1000 bar in modern CRDI systems).
* During this phase, the fuel undergoes:
  1. **Atomization** – breakup of the liquid jet into fine droplets.
  2. **Vaporization** – droplets absorb heat from the surrounding hot air and evaporate.
  3. **Mixing** – fuel vapor diffuses and mixes with the compressed air.
  4. **Heating to auto-ignition temperature** – the air–fuel mixture must reach ~ **750 K** to become chemically reactive.
* No chemical reaction of significance occurs during this phase. The duration depends on **fuel physical properties, injection pressure, and air temperature/density**.

> [!NOTE] **Why "Physical"?**
> Because this lag is governed purely by **physical processes** (heat transfer, mass transfer, fluid dynamics) — *no combustion chemistry happens yet*.

#### 2.1.2 Chemical Lag (Pre-flame Reaction Phase)
* Once the local mixture reaches auto-ignition temperature, slow **pre-flame chemical reactions** (cool-flame / blue-flame / two-stage ignition chemistry) begin.
* These are **exothermic low-temperature oxidation reactions** that build up radicals (·OH, ·H, ·O, H₂O₂) until a critical concentration is reached.
* At this critical radical concentration, the mixture **self-ignites** — and the **rapid, high-temperature combustion** phase begins.
* This lag is governed by **activation energy (E)**, **local equivalence ratio (φ)**, and **Arrhenius kinetics**.

> [!NOTE] **Why "Chemical"?**
> Because the controlling phenomenon is **chemical kinetics**, primarily governed by the **Arrhenius equation**.

### 2.2 Total Ignition Lag Relationship

$$
\tau_{total} = \tau_{physical} + \tau_{chemical}
$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\theta_{lag} = \theta_{start\,of\,comb} - \theta_{start\,of\,inj}$ | Crank-angle definition of lag | °CA |
| 2 | $\tau_{lag} = \dfrac{\theta_{lag}}{6 \cdot N}$ | Lag in seconds (N = rpm) | seconds |
| 3 | $\tau_{lag} \propto \dfrac{1}{T_{comp}^{\,a} \cdot P_{comp}^{\,b}}$ | Empirical — decreases with T and P | a ≈ 1–2, b ≈ 1 |
| 4 | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / R_u T}$ | Arrhenius rate constant (chemical lag) | $E_a$ in J/mol |
| 5 | $\tau_{chem} \propto e^{+E_a / R_u T}$ | Chemical lag increases with activation energy | — |
| 6 | $\tau_{lag} \propto \dfrac{1}{CN}$ | Lag **decreases** as Cetane Number **increases** | CN dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Cetane Number} + \text{Cetane Index} \approx \text{Ignition Quality}$ | KTU-tested relation | — |
| 8 | $\rho_{comp} = \rho_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_{comp}}{P_0}\right)^{1/\gamma}$ | Compressed air density | kg/m³ |
| 9 | $T_{comp} = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}$ (ideal) | Adiabatic compression temperature | Kelvin |
| 10 | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | Local equivalence ratio controls chemistry | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Utility**
> Engineers use this formula sheet to:
> * Design **combustion chambers** (open vs. swirl vs. pre-chamber) to minimize lag.
> * Select **fuel injection timing** (advance/injector) to compensate for the lag.
> * Choose **fuels with high Cetane Number** for cleaner, quieter CI combustion.
> * Predict and prevent **Diesel Knock**, which is a direct consequence of an *excessive* ignition lag.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Arrhenius-Based Chemical Delay Period

The chemical delay is governed by the rate of pre-flame oxidation. From chemical kinetics, the **rate constant** $k$ of the pre-flame reactions follows the **Arrhenius equation**:

$$
k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / R_u T}
$$

where:
* $A$ = pre-exponential (frequency) factor, in s⁻¹
* $E_a$ = activation energy of the fuel–air pre-flame reaction, in J/mol
* $R_u$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/mol·K**
* $T$ = absolute temperature of the compressed charge, in K

The **delay time** $\tau_{chem}$ is **inversely proportional** to the reaction rate constant (faster reaction → shorter delay):

$$
\tau_{chem} = \dfrac{C}{k} = \dfrac{C}{A \cdot e^{-E_a / R_u T}}
$$

$$
\tau_{chem} = \dfrac{C}{A} \cdot e^{+E_a / R_u T}
$$

Taking natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\ln(\tau_{chem}) = \ln\left(\dfrac{C}{A}\right) + \dfrac{E_a}{R_u T}
$$

This is the **linearized form** of the chemical delay, which is the basis of the **Wolfer / Henein–Bolt correlation** used in KTU reference textbooks.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Interpretation (KTU Examiner Tip)**
> * Higher $T$ (hotter compressed air) → smaller $E_a / R_u T$ → **shorter chemical delay** ✓
> * Higher $E_a$ (less reactive fuel) → **longer chemical delay** ✗
> * This is why **low-Cetane fuels produce more Diesel Knock**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Empirical Delay Period (Wolfer's Correlation)

The widely cited **Wolfer's empirical formula** for ignition delay in a CI engine is:

$$
\tau_{lag} = \dfrac{0.44}{P^{1.19} \cdot e^{\,4470 / T}} \quad \text{[seconds]}
$$

where $P$ is in atmospheres and $T$ is in Kelvin.

**Step-by-step conversion into crank-angle form:**

The crank angle $\theta_{lag}$ swept during the delay is:

$$
\theta_{lag} = 6 \cdot N \cdot \tau_{lag} \quad \text{[degrees CA]}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\theta_{lag} = 6 \cdot N \cdot \left[ \dfrac{0.44}{P^{1.19} \cdot e^{\,4470 / T}} \right]
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

A diesel engine operates at $N = 1500$ rpm with compression pressure $P = 40$ atm and compressed air temperature $T = 900$ K. Calculate the ignition lag in **°CA** and **milliseconds**.

**Step 1** — Compute the exponent:

$$
\dfrac{4470}{T} = \dfrac{4470}{900} = 4.9667
$$

**Step 2** — Compute the exponential:

$$
e^{4.9667} \approx 143.31
$$

**Step 3** — Compute the pressure term:

$$
P^{1.19} = 40^{1.19}
$$

Using logarithms: $\ln(40) = 3.6889$, so $1.19 \times 3.6889 = 4.3898$, hence $P^{1.19} = e^{4.3898} \approx 80.64$.

**Step 4** — Compute $\tau_{lag}$:

$$
\tau_{lag} = \dfrac{0.44}{80.64 \times 143.31} = \dfrac{0.44}{11556.6} \approx 3.81 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} \approx 0.0381 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5** — Convert to crank angle:

$$
\theta_{lag} = 6 \times 1500 \times 3.81 \times 10^{-5} = 0.343^\circ \text{ CA}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Sanity Check**
> A lag of $\approx 0.34°$ is unusually small — this is because $T = 900$ K is very high. For a typical cold-start condition ($T \approx 600$ K), the lag can be 10–20× larger, producing the characteristic **cold-start diesel rattle** (diesel knock).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effect of Cetane Number on Ignition Lag

Empirically, for a given engine and operating condition:

$$
\tau_{lag} \propto \dfrac{1}{CN}
$$

Or in the form used in KTU textbook problems:

$$
\dfrac{\tau_{1}}{\tau_{2}} = \dfrac{CN_{2}}{CN_{1}}
$$

This shows that **doubling the Cetane Number halves the ignition delay**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cause-Effect Summary

| Change in Parameter | Effect on Physical Lag | Effect on Chemical Lag | Net Effect on $\tau_{lag}$ | Effect on Diesel Knock |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| ↑ Compression Ratio | — (slight ↓) | ↓ (higher T) | **Decreases** | **Reduces** |
| ↑ Injection Pressure | ↓ (better atomization) | ↓ (better mixing) | **Decreases** | **Reduces** |
| ↑ Intake Air Temperature | ↓ (less heating needed) | ↓↓ (Arrhenius) | **Decreases** | **Reduces** |
| ↑ Cetane Number | ↓ | ↓↓ | **Decreases** | **Reduces** |
| ↑ Engine Speed | ↓ (less time available) | ↓ (shorter mixing) | **Decreases (in °CA)** | **Mixed** |
| ↑ Fuel Cetane → Alcohol Blends | ↑ (latent heat) | ↑ (lower reactivity) | **Increases** | **Increases** |
| ↓ Coolant Temperature | ↑ (slower vaporization) | ↑ (slower chemistry) | **Increases** | **Increases** (cold knock) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Phases of Ignition Lag in a CI Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Crank Reaches Injection Timing"] --> B["Start of Fuel Injection"]
    B --> C["Physical Lag Phase"]
    C --> C1["Atomization of Fuel Jet"]
    C1 --> C2["Vaporization of Droplets"]
    C2 --> C3["Mixing with Compressed Air"]
    C3 --> C4["Heating to Auto-Ignition Temperature ~ 750 K"]
    C4 --> D["Chemical Lag Phase"]
    D --> D1["Low-Temperature Pre-Flame Reactions"]
    D1 --> D2["Radical Build-up H, OH, H2O2"]
    D2 --> D3["Critical Radical Concentration Reached"]
    D3 --> E["Start of Combustion"]
    E --> F["Rapid Heat Release Phase"]
    F --> G["After-Burning / Diffusion Flame"]
    G --> H["End of Combustion"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Factors Influencing Ignition Lag

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph F1["Fuel-Side Parameters"]
        F1A["Cetane Number"]
        F1B["Volatility / Viscosity"]
        F1C["Latent Heat of Vaporization"]
    end
    subgraph F2["Engine-Operating Parameters"]
        F2A["Compression Ratio"]
        F2B["Coolant Temperature"]
        F2C["Engine Speed rpm"]
    end
    subgraph F3["Injection System Parameters"]
        F3A["Injection Pressure"]
        F3B["Nozzle Hole Geometry"]
        F3C["Injection Timing Advance"]
    end
    subgraph OUT["Ignition Lag Output"]
        OUTA["Physical Lag ms"]
        OUTB["Chemical Lag ms"]
        OUTC["Total Delay degrees CA"]
    end
    F1 --> OUT
    F2 --> OUT
    F3 --> OUT
    OUTA --> OUTC
    OUTB --> OUTC
    OUTC --> X["Combustion Quality and Knock Tendency"]
```

### 4.3 Pressure–Crank-Angle (p–θ) Trace — Visualizing the Lag

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Start of Injection θ_inj"] -->|"Δθ = θ_lag"| B["Start of Combustion θ_comb"]
    B -->|"Rapid pressure rise dp/dθ"| C["Peak Cylinder Pressure"]
    A2["Compression Curve (Motoring)"] -.->|"Diverges at θ_comb"| B
    B --> D["Heat Release Curve p HRR vs θ"]
```

> [!NOTE] **How to Read This Diagram**
> On an actual $p$–$\theta$ indicator diagram, the **motoring curve** (engine cranked without fuel) and the **fired curve** are identical up to the **start of injection**, then they remain identical until the **start of combustion** (because combustion has not yet begun), and finally the fired curve rises *sharply above* the motoring curve. The **angular gap** between injection and this sharp pressure rise is the **ignition lag in crank-angle degrees**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
> **Define Ignition Lag. How does it affect the performance of a CI engine?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
* **Definition (1.5 marks):** Ignition lag is the time interval between the start of fuel injection and the start of combustion in a CI engine, measured in ms or degrees of crank angle. It has two components: physical lag and chemical lag.
* **Effect on Performance (1.5 marks):** A long ignition lag causes accumulation of fuel inside the cylinder, leading to a sudden and uncontrolled pressure rise (Diesel Knock), increased noise, higher mechanical stress, lower thermal efficiency, and higher NO_x and unburnt hydrocarbon emissions. A short, controlled lag produces smooth combustion and high efficiency.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
> **Differentiate between Physical Lag and Chemical Lag in CI engine combustion.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Physical Lag | Chemical Lag |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Nature** | Physical (heat/mass transfer) | Chemical (reaction kinetics) |
| **Processes** | Atomization, vaporization, mixing, heating | Pre-flame oxidation, radical build-up |
| **Duration** | 0.5 – 1.5 ms (typical) | 0.2 – 1.0 ms (typical) |
| **Controlling factor** | Injection pressure, fuel properties, air temperature | Activation energy, temperature, equivalence ratio |
| **Equation** | Empirical — depends on $\rho$, T, $C_p$, latent heat | $\tau_{chem} \propto e^{+E_a / R_u T}$ |
| **Dominant at** | Cold start, low load | High load, high EGR |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Note for KTU 2024 Students:** As per the 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, Module 4 offers an internal choice. **Attempt ANY ONE** of the following two full questions (Q3A **OR** Q3B).

---

#### **Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat $p$–$\theta$ diagram the phenomenon of **ignition lag** in a CI engine. List **six (6) factors** that affect the ignition lag. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using **Wolfer's empirical formula**, calculate the ignition delay in **milliseconds** and in **degrees of crank angle** for a diesel engine running at **2000 rpm**, with compression pressure **35 atm** and compressed air temperature **800 K**. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

1. **Diagram (2 marks):** A neat $p$–$\theta$ trace should be drawn showing:
   * Compression curve from BDC to TDC.
   * Start of injection marked with a small dot.
   * Motoring and fired curves overlapping up to start of combustion.
   * Sharp pressure rise marking start of combustion.
   * The angular gap labelled $\theta_{lag}$.
2. **Explanation (2 marks):** Define ignition lag, state physical and chemical sub-phases, and mention that a long lag causes Diesel Knock.
3. **Six factors (3 marks):** Compression ratio, intake air temperature, fuel cetane number, injection pressure, engine speed, coolant temperature, fuel volatility, swirl motion, EGR rate. (Any six clearly explained = 3 marks.)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1** — Write Wolfer's formula:

$$
\tau_{lag} = \dfrac{0.44}{P^{1.19} \cdot e^{4470 / T}} \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2** — Substitute $P = 35$ atm, $T = 800$ K, $N = 2000$ rpm.

Compute $P^{1.19} = 35^{1.19}$:
$\ln(35) = 3.5553$, $\times 1.19 = 4.231$, $e^{4.231} = 68.83$.

Compute $e^{4470/800} = e^{5.5875} = 266.4$.

**Step 3** — Compute $\tau_{lag}$:

$$
\tau_{lag} = \dfrac{0.44}{68.83 \times 266.4} = \dfrac{0.44}{18336.5} = 2.40 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 0.024 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4** — Convert to crank angle:

$$
\theta_{lag} = 6 \cdot N \cdot \tau_{lag} = 6 \times 2000 \times 2.40 \times 10^{-5} = 0.288^\circ \text{ CA}
$$

**Step 5** — Comment (1 mark): The delay is very small ($\approx 0.29°$ CA) because of the high compression temperature. For a cold-start at $T = 600$ K, the delay would be 8–10× larger, producing audible knock.

---

#### **Q3B. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between **chemical delay**, **activation energy**, and **compressed air temperature** starting from the Arrhenius equation. Hence explain why a **fuel with low Cetane Number causes Diesel Knock**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two diesel fuels A and B have Cetane Numbers of **55** and **48** respectively. If fuel A gives an ignition delay of **8° CA** at a particular operating condition, estimate the ignition delay of fuel B at the same condition. Comment on the suitability of these fuels for high-speed diesel engines. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Starting from Arrhenius equation:

$$
k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / R_u T}
$$

Since delay $\tau_{chem} \propto 1/k$:

$$
\tau_{chem} = \dfrac{C}{A} \cdot e^{+E_a / R_u T}
$$

Taking logarithms:

$$
\ln(\tau_{chem}) = \text{constant} + \dfrac{E_a}{R_u T}
$$

This shows that:
* $\tau_{chem}$ **decreases exponentially** as $T$ increases.
* $\tau_{chem}$ **increases exponentially** as $E_a$ increases.

**Why low CN causes Diesel Knock (2 marks):**
A low-Cetane fuel has high activation energy $E_a$, so $\tau_{chem}$ is long. During this long delay, more fuel accumulates in the cylinder. When combustion finally starts, all the accumulated fuel burns almost simultaneously, causing a **sharp pressure rise (knock)**. A high-Cetane fuel (low $E_a$) ignites quickly and smoothly.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1** — Use the inverse proportionality:

$$
\dfrac{\tau_A}{\tau_B} = \dfrac{CN_B}{CN_A}
$$

**Step 2** — Substitute:

$$
\dfrac{8^\circ}{\tau_B} = \dfrac{48}{55}
$$

**Step 3** — Solve:

$$
\tau_B = 8 \times \dfrac{55}{48} = 9.17^\circ \text{ CA}
$$

**Step 4** — Suitability comment (2 marks): Fuel A (CN 55) is **excellent** for high-speed diesel engines — it gives a short, controlled delay of 8° CA, ensuring smooth combustion. Fuel B (CN 48) gives a slightly longer 9.17° CA delay; it is acceptable for medium-speed engines but may cause mild knock in high-speed applications. Both are above the **minimum IS-1460 standard of CN 45** for road diesel in India.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Read Carefully!)**
> 1. **Do not confuse "Ignition Lag" with "Flame Propagation Period."** Lag = before combustion starts. Propagation = after combustion has started. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this conflation.
> 2. **Always quote the units** (ms OR °CA), not just "the lag is small." A numerical answer without units = 0.5 mark lost in Part A, 1 mark lost in Part B.
> 3. **Arrhenius derivation must end with the log form** $\ln(\tau) = \text{const} + E_a / (R_u T)$. Many students stop at $\tau = C/A \cdot e^{E_a / R_u T}$ and lose 1 mark.
> 4. **When using Wolfer's formula**, students forget that $P$ is in **atmospheres**, not bar. $1 \text{ atm} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$. This single unit error can cascade and lose 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Cetane Number question trap:** The question may ask "what CN is *required* to halve the delay of CN 50 fuel?" — correct answer is **CN 100** (theoretical), but the next *practical* fuel would be **CN 70–75**, not "100". Examiners expect a sensible practical answer.
> 6. **Diagrams must have labelled axes** ($p$ on Y, $\theta$ on X for $p$–$\theta$ diagram). An unlabelled sketch = 0 marks for the diagram sub-part.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read Before Exam)**

* ⏱️ **Ignition Lag = Injection-to-Combustion interval** (ms or °CA).
* 🧪 **Two components:** Physical Lag (atomize → vaporize → mix → heat) + Chemical Lag (pre-flame reactions → radical build-up → self-ignition).
* 📈 **Wolfer's formula:** $\tau_{lag} = 0.44 / (P^{1.19} \cdot e^{4470/T})$ — $P$ in atm, $T$ in K.
* 🧠 **Arrhenius:** $\tau_{chem} \propto e^{+E_a / R_u T}$ — hot charge = short chemical lag.
* ⛽ **Cetane Number ↑** → Ignition Lag ↓ → Less Diesel Knock.
* 🔥 **Diesel Knock** = consequence of *excessive* ignition lag causing uncontrolled simultaneous burning.
* ⚙️ **Factors that *decrease* lag:** ↑ compression ratio, ↑ injection pressure, ↑ intake temperature, ↑ CN, ↑ engine speed (in °CA), ↓ EGR.
* ⚙️ **Factors that *increase* lag:** ↓ coolant temp, ↓ CN, low volatility, high latent heat, alcohol blending.
* 📐 **Conversion:** $\theta_{lag}(°) = 6 \cdot N(\text{rpm}) \cdot \tau_{lag}(\text{s})$.
* 🎯 **Typical values:** Modern DI Diesel = 0.5–2 ms = 6°–15° CA.
* 🚗 **Real-world impact:** Cold-start rattle, white smoke, NO_x, BS-VI emission compliance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Auto Ignition and Detonation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Auto Ignition and Detonation

# AUTO IGNITION AND DETONATION

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Auto-Ignition (Self-Ignition)

**Definition:** Auto-ignition is the spontaneous ignition of a fuel-air mixture **without any external spark or ignition source**, occurring purely as a result of the charge reaching a critical temperature due to compression alone. The temperature at which this occurs is called the **auto-ignition temperature (AIT)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Auto-ignition is the fundamental reason why diesel engines (CI engines) work — the air is compressed to such a high pressure and temperature that when fuel is injected, it self-ignites. Conversely, in SI engines, auto-ignition before the spark is a *catastrophic* phenomenon.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine placing a cotton ball in a microwave. You don't touch it with a flame, but as the microwave heats it up internally, the temperature of the cotton rises until it bursts into flame on its own. This is auto-ignition. In a diesel engine, the piston acts like the microwave — it compresses air so violently that the air gets hot enough to ignite fuel the moment fuel touches it. No spark plug is needed.

### 1.2 Detonation (Engine Knock)

**Definition:** Detonation is an **abnormal, uncontrolled combustion phenomenon** in Spark Ignition (SI) engines where the end gas (the unburned mixture ahead of the flame front) auto-ignites almost instantaneously, releasing energy in the form of a high-pressure shock wave that collides with the cylinder walls, producing the characteristic metallic "pinging" or "knocking" sound.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of lighting a trail of gunpowder with a match. Normally, the flame travels smoothly along the trail. But if the gunpowder has been sitting in the sun and is already very hot, then instead of a smooth flame, the *entire remaining length* of the powder explodes at once with a sharp "crack" — sending a shockwave through the air. This sudden explosion is detonation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently asked in KTU):**
> - **Auto-ignition** is a *desired* phenomenon in CI engines and an *undesired* phenomenon in SI engines.
> - **Detonation/Knock** is the *consequence* of unwanted auto-ignition of the end gas in SI engines.

### 1.3 Related Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Knock** | Audible sharp sound due to detonation; colloquial term |
| **Pre-ignition** | Ignition of charge **before** the spark occurs (caused by hot spots) |
| **Surface Ignition** | Ignition by any hot surface other than the spark plug |
| **End Gas** | Unburned mixture ahead of the propagating flame front |
| **Octane Number** | Measure of a fuel's resistance to knock |
| **Cetane Number** | Measure of a fuel's ignition delay in CI engines |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Pre-ignition and Detonation are NOT the same. Pre-ignition occurs *before* the spark (earlier in the cycle), while detonation occurs *after* the spark (later, due to auto-ignition of end gas). Both can occur simultaneously and worsen each other.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Mechanism of Normal Combustion in SI Engines

1. Spark plug fires at the end of compression stroke.
2. A spherical flame kernel forms and grows.
3. Flame front propagates outward at a velocity of **15–25 m/s** during normal combustion.
4. Pressure rises smoothly and reaches peak just after TDC.
5. End gas (remaining charge) gets heated and compressed by the advancing flame front and the rising piston.
6. Ideally, the flame front consumes the end gas before it can self-ignite.

## 2.2 Mechanism of Detonation (Step-by-Step)

1. Flame front propagates normally from spark plug.
2. End gas ahead of flame front experiences **rising temperature and pressure**.
3. If end gas temperature exceeds its **auto-ignition temperature** before flame arrival → spontaneous ignition.
4. The entire end gas burns almost instantaneously (in microseconds), instead of milliseconds.
5. This creates a **localized pressure spike** of up to **100 bar** in microseconds.
6. Two pressure waves collide, forming a high-amplitude **detonation wave** (~1000 m/s).
7. This wave strikes the cylinder walls producing the audible "knock".

## 2.3 Two Stages of Abnormal Combustion

**Stage 1 — Pre-ignition:**
- Caused by overheated components (exhaust valve, carbon deposits, spark plug electrodes).
- Charge ignites *before* the spark.
- Results in very high peak pressures and possible engine damage.

**Stage 2 — Detonation (Knock):**
- Caused by auto-ignition of end gas *after* the spark.
- Results in pressure oscillations and characteristic knock sound.
- More common and more frequently tested in KTU exams.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are essential for KTU Numerical/Analytical questions.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Value | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|--------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Octane Number (ON) | $ON = \left(\dfrac{V_{iso}}{V_{total}}\right)_{test} \times 100$ | where iso-octane = 100, n-heptane = 0 |
| 2 | Cetane Number (CN) | $CN = \left(\dfrac{V_{n-C16}}{V_{total}}\right)_{test} \times 100$ | where n-cetane = 100, α-methyl naphthalene = 0 |
| 3 | Compression Ratio relation (SI) | Higher CR $\Rightarrow$ Higher ON required | Anti-knock design parameter |
| 4 | End gas temperature relation | $T_{end} = T_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$ | Adiabatic compression of end gas |
| 5 | Flame velocity (normal) | $V_f \approx 15 - 25$ | m/s |
| 6 | Detonation wave velocity | $V_d \approx 1000 - 2000$ | m/s (shock wave) |
| 7 | Auto-ignition temperature (gasoline) | $A_{gasoline} \approx 250 - 280$ | °C (varies with conditions) |
| 8 | Auto-ignition temperature (diesel) | $A_{diesel} \approx 210 - 260$ | °C (lower → easier self-ignition) |
| 9 | Ignition Delay (CI engines) | $\tau_{id}$ = time between injection and start of combustion | typically 0.001–0.003 s |
| 10 | Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP) | Higher RVP $\Rightarrow$ Higher knock tendency | Affects vaporization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Anti-Break Rule:** Within this formula table, mathematical absolute-value bars are written using the LaTeX `\vert` command (e.g., $\vert P_2 - P_1 \vert$) to avoid breaking the markdown pipe syntax. The vertical bar character `|` is **NEVER** used directly inside table cells.

## 2.5 Factors Affecting Knock / Detonation in SI Engines

### A. Engine Design Factors

1. **Compression Ratio (CR):** Higher CR → higher end-gas temperature → more knock. This is why high-performance engines use high-octane fuels.
2. **Combustion Chamber Geometry:** Compact, squish-type chambers reduce knock (shorter flame travel distance).
3. **Spark Plug Location:** Centrally located plug shortens flame travel → less knock.
4. **Valve Overlap:** Increased overlap increases residual exhaust gas → dilution → may *reduce* knock slightly.

### B. Operating Condition Factors

1. **Load:** Higher load → higher temperature and pressure → more knock.
2. **Speed:** Higher speed → less time for end-gas auto-ignition → less knock (in some cases). However, very low speed increases knock.
3. **Spark Advance:** More advanced spark → peak pressure closer to TDC → more knock.
4. **Intake Temperature:** Higher temperature → higher end-gas temperature → more knock.

### C. Fuel-Related Factors

1. **Octane Rating:** Higher octane → more knock-resistant.
2. **Chemical Structure:** 
   - Branched-chain paraffins > straight-chain paraffins
   - Aromatics > Naphthenes > Paraffins (in knock resistance)
3. **Molecular Weight:** Higher molecular weight → better knock resistance.
4. **Self-Ignition Temperature:** Higher AIT → better knock resistance.

## 2.6 Practical Engineering Utility

- **Fuel Refining Industry:** Octane rating drives refining processes like catalytic reforming, alkylation, and isomerization.
- **Engine Design:** Determines the maximum compression ratio allowable for a given fuel.
- **Emission Control:** Detonation increases NOx and unburned hydrocarbon emissions.
- **CI Engine Calibration:** Cetane number influences injection timing and combustion quality.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: End-Gas Temperature During Combustion

### Problem Setup
Consider the unburned end gas trapped between the advancing flame front and the cylinder head/piston. As combustion proceeds, this gas is compressed both by the rising piston and by the expanding burned gases.

### Assumptions
- End gas behaves as an **ideal gas**.
- Compression of end gas is **adiabatic** (no heat transfer to flame front yet).
- Process follows $PV^{\gamma} = \text{constant}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

For an adiabatic process on the end gas:

$$P_1 V_1^{\gamma} = P_2 V_2^{\gamma}$$

Rearranging for the volume ratio:

$$\frac{V_1}{V_2} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{1}{\gamma}}$$

Using the ideal gas relation $\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{P_1 V_1}$, we get:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{P_2}{P_1} \cdot \frac{V_2}{V_1}$$

Substituting $\frac{V_2}{V_1} = \left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{\frac{1}{\gamma}}$:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{P_2}{P_1} \cdot \left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{\frac{1}{\gamma}}$$

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1 - \frac{1}{\gamma}}$$

Using the identity $1 - \dfrac{1}{\gamma} = \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}$:

$$\boxed{T_{end} = T_1 \cdot \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}}$$

### Physical Interpretation
- As combustion progresses, $P_2 \uparrow$ rapidly → $T_{end}$ rises sharply.
- When $T_{end}$ exceeds the **auto-ignition temperature** of the fuel → detonation occurs.
- The exponent $\dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}$ (≈ 0.286 for $\gamma = 1.4$) governs the rate of temperature rise.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Knock vs. Pre-Ignition Comparison

| Parameter | Pre-Ignition | Detonation (Knock) |
|-----------|--------------|--------------------|
| **When does it occur?** | Before spark | After spark |
| **Trigger** | Hot spots (overheated components, carbon deposits) | Auto-ignition of end gas |
| **Combustion type** | Premature, single-stage | Normal flame + sudden end-gas explosion |
| **Pressure rise** | Extremely high, gradual | Oscillatory, with sharp spikes |
| **Audible sound** | Usually silent / loss of power | Sharp metallic "ping" |
| **Damage potential** | Severe (melting of piston/crown) | Moderate to severe |
| **Effect on power** | Significant loss | Slight loss |
| **Detection** | Difficult without instrumentation | Easy by sound |
| **Primary remedy** | Cool deposits, replace plugs, retard timing | Higher octane fuel, retard timing, lower CR |

## 3.3 Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Question:** A gasoline engine has an intake temperature of 60 °C and compression ratio of 9:1. The pressure at the start of compression is 1 bar. Assuming polytropic compression with $\gamma = 1.35$, find the temperature of the end gas when its pressure reaches 25 bar. Take auto-ignition temperature of the fuel as 380 °C. Will detonation occur?

### Solution

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 60 + 273 = 333$ K
- $P_1 = 1$ bar
- $P_2 = 25$ bar
- $\gamma = 1.35$
- $A_{fuel} = 380 + 273 = 653$ K

**Calculation:**

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$$

$$T_2 = 333 \cdot \left(\frac{25}{1}\right)^{\frac{1.35 - 1}{1.35}}$$

$$\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.35}{1.35} = 0.2593$$

$$T_2 = 333 \cdot (25)^{0.2593}$$

$$\ln(25) = 3.2189 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 25^{0.2593} = e^{3.2189 \times 0.2593} = e^{0.8347} = 2.304$$

$$T_2 = 333 \times 2.304 = 767.4 \text{ K}$$

$$T_2 = 767.4 - 273 = 494.4 \text{ °C}$$

**Comparison:**
$$T_2 = 494.4 \text{ °C} > A_{fuel} = 380 \text{ °C}$$

**Conclusion:** Yes, **detonation WILL occur**, because the end-gas temperature (494.4 °C) significantly exceeds the auto-ignition temperature (380 °C). The remedy would be to use a higher-octane fuel or reduce the compression ratio.

## 3.4 Octane Number Computation (Reference Method)

A test fuel in a CFR engine gives the same knock intensity as a blend of **60% iso-octane and 40% n-heptane**. The octane number of the fuel is:

$$ON = \frac{V_{iso-octane}}{V_{iso-octane} + V_{n-heptane}} \times 100 = \frac{60}{100} \times 100 = 60$$

**Interpretation:** The fuel has the same knock resistance as a 60% iso-octane blend. A regular gasoline (RON 91) would be more knock-resistant; a racing fuel (RON 100+) would be far superior.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Flow: Cause–Effect Chain of Detonation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Spark Ignition at Spark Plug] --> B[Flame Kernel Forms]
    B --> C[Flame Front Propagates Outward]
    C --> D[End Gas Ahead of Flame Front]
    D --> E[End Gas Compressed by Piston and Burned Gases]
    E --> F{End Gas Temperature vs Auto-Ignition Temperature}
    F -->|Below AIT| G[Normal Combustion - No Knock]
    F -->|Exceeds AIT| H[Auto-Ignition of End Gas]
    H --> I[Sudden Energy Release in Microseconds]
    I --> J[High Pressure Shock Wave ~1000 m/s]
    J --> K[Collision with Cylinder Walls]
    K --> L[Audible KNOCK / PING Sound]
    L --> M[Engine Damage, Power Loss, NOx Increase]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Distinguishing Combustion Abnormalities

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Abnormal Combustion in SI Engine] --> B[Pre-Ignition]
    A --> C[Detonation Knocking]
    B --> B1[Trigger: Hot Spots]
    B --> B2[Timing: Before Spark]
    B --> B3[Silent / Power Loss]
    C --> C1[Trigger: End Gas Auto-Ignition]
    C --> C2[Timing: After Spark]
    C --> C3[Audible PING Sound]
    B2 --> D[Both may Coexist]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[Severe Engine Damage]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Octane Rating Comparison Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reference Fuel Iso-Octane ON 100] --> D[CFR Engine Standard Test]
    B[Reference Fuel n-Heptane ON 0] --> D
    C[Test Fuel Unknown] --> D
    D --> E{Same Knock Intensity?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Match Blend Percentage]
    E -->|No| G[Adjust Blend Ratio]
    F --> H[Calculate Octane Number]
    G --> D
    H --> I[ON reported as RON or MON]
```

## 4.4 Pressure–Crank Angle Diagram (Conceptual Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Normal Combustion] --> A1[Smooth Pressure Rise]
    A1 --> A2[Peak Near TDC]
    B[Detonation] --> B1[Normal Flame Starts]
    B1 --> B2[Sudden Pressure Spike]
    B2 --> B3[Oscillations After TDC]
    B3 --> B4[Knock Sound]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Block Diagram:** In a normal P–θ diagram, the pressure curve rises smoothly and peaks shortly after TDC. In a detonating cycle, a *secondary pressure spike* appears well after TDC due to end-gas auto-ignition, accompanied by high-frequency oscillations. This is the signature KTU examiners expect students to identify.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define auto-ignition temperature. How does it differ for gasoline and diesel? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Auto-ignition temperature (AIT)** is the minimum temperature at which a fuel-air mixture spontaneously ignites *without any external spark or flame*. It is a fundamental property of the fuel and depends on pressure, mixture ratio, and chamber geometry.

- **Gasoline AIT:** approximately **250–280 °C** (higher).
- **Diesel AIT:** approximately **210–260 °C** (lower).

**Key Insight:** Diesel has a *lower* auto-ignition temperature than gasoline. This is precisely why diesel engines (CI engines) can use compression alone to ignite the fuel, whereas SI engines must use a spark plug and use fuels with *higher* AIT to avoid pre-ignition.

*(Mark Distribution: 1M Definition + 1M Comparison + 1M Engineering reason)*

### Question 2: Distinguish between detonation and pre-ignition in SI engines. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Pre-Ignition | Detonation |
|--------|--------------|------------|
| Timing | Occurs **before** the spark | Occurs **after** the spark |
| Cause | Hot spots in combustion chamber | Auto-ignition of end gas |
| Detection | Difficult; loss of power only | Easy by audible "knock" |
| Remedy | Cool deposits, retard ignition | Higher octane fuel, retard timing |

*(Mark Distribution: 1M each for cause/timing and 1M for distinction/remedy)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the phenomenon of detonation (knocking) in SI engines. Discuss the factors affecting it. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A fuel gives the same knock intensity as a mixture of 75% iso-octane and 25% n-heptane in a CFR engine. Determine the octane number of the fuel. If the same fuel shows slightly less knock at a higher engine speed, justify the observation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Phenomenon of Detonation (7 Marks):**

**Definition (1M):** Detonation is the abnormal combustion in SI engines where the unburned end gas ahead of the flame front auto-ignites spontaneously, producing a sharp pressure rise and the characteristic knock sound.

**Mechanism (3M):**
1. Spark ignites the mixture; flame front propagates from spark plug.
2. End gas is heated and compressed by the flame front and rising piston.
3. When end-gas temperature exceeds the auto-ignition temperature, it ignites spontaneously.
4. The entire end gas burns in microseconds, releasing energy rapidly.
5. Two pressure waves collide, forming a detonation wave (~1000 m/s).
6. This wave strikes cylinder walls, causing the audible "knock".

**Factors Affecting (3M):**
- *Engine Design:* Compression ratio (↑ → more knock), combustion chamber shape, spark plug location.
- *Operating Conditions:* Load (↑ → more knock), speed, spark advance, intake air temperature.
- *Fuel Properties:* Octane rating (↑ → less knock), chemical structure, molecular weight.

---

**(b) Octane Number Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given:** Blend = 75% iso-octane + 25% n-heptane

**Formula (2M):**

$$ON = \left(\frac{V_{iso-octane}}{V_{total}}\right) \times 100$$

**Calculation (3M):**

$$ON = \frac{75}{100} \times 100 = 75$$

**Result:** The octane number of the test fuel is **75**.

**Justification – Effect of Engine Speed (2M):**
At higher engine speeds, the time available for end-gas auto-ignition is reduced (cycle is shorter). Also, higher turbulence improves flame propagation, ensuring the flame front consumes the end gas *before* it can self-ignite. Hence, the same fuel shows slightly *less* knock at higher speeds.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** What is octane rating? Explain the CFR test method for determining octane number. Why are branched-chain hydrocarbons better knock-resistant than straight-chain ones? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An SI engine compresses a fuel-air mixture from 1 bar, 65 °C to a pressure of 20 bar. Assuming $\gamma = 1.35$, calculate the end-gas temperature. Given the auto-ignition temperature of the fuel is 400 °C, comment on the possibility of detonation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Octane Rating & CFR Test (7 Marks):**

**Octane Rating (2M):** Octane number is a numerical measure of a fuel's resistance to knock. Iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane) is assigned ON = 100 (very knock-resistant), and n-heptane is assigned ON = 0 (highly knocking).

**CFR Test Method (3M):**
1. A Cooperative Fuel Research (CFR) engine is operated under standard conditions.
2. The test fuel's knock intensity is matched to that of a reference blend of iso-octane and n-heptane.
3. By varying the compression ratio, the **Knock Limited Compression Ratio** is determined.
4. The percentage of iso-octane in the reference blend that gives the same knock intensity is the **Octane Number** of the test fuel.

**Branched vs. Straight Chain (2M):** Branched-chain hydrocarbons have higher auto-ignition temperatures because the branching creates steric hindrance, making the molecule more stable and resistant to free-radical chain reactions during pre-flame oxidation. Straight-chain paraffins oxidize more readily, producing more peroxides and shorter ignition delay → more knock.

---

**(b) End-Gas Temperature Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 1$ bar, $T_1 = 65 + 273 = 338$ K
- $P_2 = 20$ bar, $\gamma = 1.35$
- $A_{fuel} = 400 + 273 = 673$ K

**Formula (2M):**

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$$

**Calculation (3M):**

$$\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.35}{1.35} = 0.2593$$

$$T_2 = 338 \times (20)^{0.2593}$$

$$\ln(20) = 2.9957 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 20^{0.2593} = e^{2.9957 \times 0.2593} = e^{0.7769} = 2.174$$

$$T_2 = 338 \times 2.174 = 734.8 \text{ K}$$

$$T_2 = 734.8 - 273 = 461.8 \text{ °C}$$

**Comment (2M):**
$$T_2 = 461.8 \text{ °C} > A_{fuel} = 400 \text{ °C}$$

Since the end-gas temperature (461.8 °C) exceeds the auto-ignition temperature (400 °C) by about **62 °C**, **detonation is highly likely**. Remedies include using higher-octane fuel, retarding spark timing, or reducing compression ratio.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin** in the end-gas temperature formula — *loses 1–2 marks instantly*.
> 2. **Confusing detonation with pre-ignition** in definitions — examiners are strict on this; use the words "after spark" vs "before spark" clearly.
> 3. **Using the wrong exponent:** Some students mistakenly use $\frac{1}{\gamma}$ instead of $\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}$ in the temperature formula.
> 4. **Ignoring units in octane number calculation:** Always state the result as a number with no units (dimensionless).
> 5. **Not justifying "why branched chains resist knock better":** Simply stating it is insufficient; mention *auto-ignition temperature* and *free-radical stability*.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read Before Exam:**

- ☐ **Auto-ignition** = spontaneous ignition without spark; key parameter is **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)**.
- ☐ Diesel has lower AIT than gasoline → enables CI engine operation.
- ☐ **Detonation (Knock)** = abnormal combustion in SI engines caused by auto-ignition of *end gas*.
- ☐ Detonation occurs **after spark**; Pre-ignition occurs **before spark** — never confuse these two.
- ☐ End-gas temperature formula: $T_2 = T_1 \cdot (P_2 / P_1)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$ — must convert °C → K before substitution.
- ☐ Detonation produces a high-pressure shock wave (~1000 m/s) and audible "ping".
- ☐ **Higher compression ratio → more knock** → requires higher octane fuel.
- ☐ **Higher octane number = more knock-resistant fuel.**
- ☐ Iso-octane (branched) → ON = 100; n-heptane (straight) → ON = 0.
- ☐ **Cetane number** is the CI-engine equivalent of octane number (higher CN = shorter ignition delay).
- ☐ Branched hydrocarbons > straight-chain in knock resistance (steric hindrance & higher AIT).
- ☐ Aromatics > Naphthenes > Paraffins in knock resistance.
- ☐ Remedies for knock: higher-octane fuel, retard spark timing, lower compression ratio, better chamber design, lower intake temperature.
- ☐ Remedies for pre-ignition: clean carbon deposits, replace faulty spark plugs, ensure adequate cooling.
- ☐ Effects of knock: power loss, engine damage (piston/crown/head), increased NOx, overheating.
- ☐ CFR engine is the standard reference engine for octane rating determination.
- ☐ Always state physical constants and reference temperatures in **bold** when writing KTU answers.
- ☐ In numerical problems, end the answer with an explicit **comparison statement** (e.g., "T_end > AIT, hence detonation occurs") to fetch full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effects of Engine Variables on Detonation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Effects of Engine Variables on Detonation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Detonation (Engine Knock)

In the context of Spark Ignition (SI) engines, **Detonation** — commonly termed **engine knock** or **pinking** — is the uncontrolled, spontaneous auto-ignition of the unburned end-gas (the charge ahead of the propagating flame front) inside the combustion chamber, occurring *after* the normal spark-ignited flame has initiated combustion. This secondary, near-instantaneous pressure spike collides with the primary flame front, producing the characteristic metallic "pinging" sound and, if sustained, catastrophic mechanical damage.

Mathematically, detonation can be identified on an indicator diagram as a sharp **abrupt discontinuity** in the pressure–crank angle (P–θ) trace, occurring typically 10°–30° after Top Dead Center (aTDC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Detonation is the rapid, uncontrolled combustion of the unburned mixture in the end-gas region of an SI engine, caused by the auto-ignition of charge due to excessive pressure and temperature rise ahead of the advancing flame front.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crowded Room" Intuition

Imagine a busy lecture hall (the **combustion chamber**) where students are exiting through a single front door (the **flame front**). The orderly exit represents normal combustion. Now suppose the crowd ahead of the door (the **end-gas**) gets squeezed — pressure rises, nerves fray, and someone in the back spontaneously panics and shouts, triggering a chaotic stampede. This rogue, premature outburst is **detonation** — the end-gas "auto-ignites" while the orderly exit is still happening. The result is a violent, destructive pressure wave instead of smooth progression.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Octane Number | ON | 0 – 100 | Anti-knock resistance of fuel |
| Critical Compression Ratio | CCR | 8 – 14 (dimensionless) | Compression limit before knock |
| Flame Front Velocity | $V_f$ | **15 – 30 m/s** (normal), > **300 m/s** (detonation) | Distinguishes deflagration from detonation |
| End-gas Auto-ignition Delay | $\tau_{ig}$ | **2 – 10 ms** | Time available before spontaneous ignition |
| Detonation Pressure Spike | $\Delta P_{det}$ | **2 – 5 MPa** above normal | Damaging pressure differential |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Detonation Matters in KTU Examinations:** Detonation is the single most important abnormal combustion phenomenon covered under Module 4 of Thermal Engineering. Almost every KTU question bank (Dec 2018, June 2019, Dec 2022, June 2023) has either a 7-mark or 14-mark question dedicated to the variables affecting detonation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Major Engine Variables Affecting Detonation

### 2.1.1 Effect of Compression Ratio (CR)

As compression ratio rises:
- End-gas temperature ($T_{end}$) and pressure ($P_{end}$) increase sharply.
- The end-gas crosses the auto-ignition temperature threshold *before* the flame front arrives.
- **Detonation tendency increases monotonically with CR.**

$$\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$$

where $r$ = compression ratio. Higher $r$ gives higher efficiency but lower knock resistance — a fundamental design trade-off.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Compression Ratio (CCR):** The compression ratio at which a given fuel just begins to knock under standard reference conditions. It is inversely related to octane number.

### 2.1.2 Effect of Air–Fuel Ratio (Equivalence Ratio $\phi$)

Detonation is maximum near **chemically correct mixture** ($\phi \approx 1.0$).

- **Rich mixtures ($\phi < 1$):** Excess fuel absorbs heat, dilutes the charge, lowers end-gas temperature → reduced knock.
- **Lean mixtures ($\phi > 1$):** Excess air absorbs heat and slows combustion → reduced knock, but flame becomes unstable and **miss-fire** occurs if too lean.
- **Stoichiometric / slightly rich (φ = 0.85 – 1.0):** Peak flame temperature → maximum knock tendency.

$$\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoichiometric}}$$

### 2.1.3 Effect of Ignition Timing (Spark Advance)

- **Advanced spark** → longer time for pressure rise → higher peak pressure and end-gas temperature → **more knock**.
- **Retarded spark** → lower peak pressure/temperature → **less knock**, but at the cost of efficiency (heat loss to walls).

### 2.1.4 Effect of Engine Speed (RPM)

- Higher RPM → shorter time for combustion → flame travels less distance per crank degree.
- However, residual gas and turbulence effects dominate.
- **Net effect:** Knock tendency generally **decreases at higher RPM** because end-gas has less residence time before flame arrival.

### 2.1.5 Effect of Engine Load

- Higher load → more throttle opening → higher manifold pressure → more charge mass per cycle → higher peak pressures and temperatures → **increased knock**.
- At part-load, residual gas dilution suppresses knock.

### 2.1.6 Effect of Inlet Air Temperature and Pressure

- Higher $T_{inlet}$ → higher end-gas temperature → **more knock**.
- Higher $P_{inlet}$ (supercharging/turbocharging) → higher compression end conditions → **more knock**.
- **Altitude** (lower $P_{atm}$) reduces knock.

### 2.1.7 Effect of Combustion Chamber Design

| Design Factor | Effect on Knock |
|---|---|
| Compact chamber | Shorter flame travel → less knock |
| Central spark plug | Shorter flame path → less knock |
| High swirl/tumble | Faster flame → less knock |
| Cool walls | Heat extraction → less knock |
| Hot spots | Local auto-ignition → more knock |

### 2.1.8 Effect of Fuel Properties — Octane Number

- **Octane Number (ON)** is the standard measure of a fuel's resistance to knock.
- Iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane) = 100 ON, n-heptane = 0 ON.
- Higher ON → greater knock resistance → higher allowable CR.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Variables | Units | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{end} = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1} \cdot \frac{P_{end}}{P_1}$ | $T_1$, $P_1$ = initial; $r$, $\gamma$ | K, Pa | End-gas temperature rise |
| 2 | $\tau_{ig} = A \cdot \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{R_u T_{end}}\right) [O_2]^{-m} [F]^{-n}$ | Arrhenius-type | s, J/mol | Auto-ignition delay |
| 3 | $\eta_{otto} = 1 - r^{1-\gamma}$ | $\gamma = C_p/C_v$ | dimensionless | Efficiency vs CR trade-off |
| 4 | $\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | equivalence ratio | dimensionless | Mixture strength |
| 5 | $K_{factor} \propto \frac{[O_2] \cdot [Fuel]}{[Diluent]^2}$ | chemical concentrations | dimensionless | Knock intensity index |
| 6 | $CCR \approx 13 - 0.06 \cdot (RON)$ (empirical) | empirical | — | Empirical CR limit |
| 7 | $P_{det} \approx 2 P_{max}$ | pressure spike | Pa | Damage threshold |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid using pipe `|` inside formulas in your answer sheets.** The KTU digital valuation portal parses markdown; use $\vert$ or $\mid$ if you must display absolute value.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fuel Refining:** Petroleum engineers tune refinery processes (catalytic cracking, isomerization, alkylation) to produce high-octane gasoline. The octane number directly dictates the allowable CR and hence the thermal efficiency of consumer vehicles.
- **Engine ECU Calibration:** Modern Electronic Control Units (ECUs) use knock sensors (piezoelectric accelerometers mounted on the cylinder block) to detect vibration signatures at ~**7 kHz**, then retard spark timing in real time by 1°–3° per cycle until knock ceases.
- **Aviation & High-Performance Engines:** Aero piston engines use 100+ MON fuels and superchargers; designers must balance detonation margins against altitude performance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: End-Gas Temperature Rise with Compression Ratio

Consider an ideal Otto cycle with compression ratio $r$. Using isentropic compression relations:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = r^{\gamma - 1}$$

Step 1: Write the in-cylinder state after compression (point 2):

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}$$

Step 2: The end-gas is not just compressed isentropically — it is also being heated by conduction from the flame-front region and the advancing combustion. Add a heat-receipt term $\Delta T_{rad}$:

$$T_{end} = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1} + \Delta T_{rad}$$

Step 3: Compare $T_{end}$ with the auto-ignition temperature $T_{ai}$ of the fuel. Detonation occurs if:

$$T_{end} \geq T_{ai}$$

Step 4: Substitute $T_{end}$ into the auto-ignition delay expression. For an Arrhenius-type chemistry:

$$\tau_{ig} = A \cdot \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{R_u T_{end}}\right) \cdot [O_2]^{-m} [Fuel]^{-n}$$

Step 5: Knock occurs when the **time available for flame propagation** ($t_{prop}$) equals or exceeds $\tau_{ig}$:

$$t_{prop} = \frac{x_{flame}}{V_f} \geq \tau_{ig}$$

This is the *fundamental knock criterion*. For a centrally located spark plug in a hemispherical chamber of bore $D$:

$$t_{prop} \approx \frac{D}{2 V_f}$$

Step 6: Using the polytropic relation for a real cycle with heat loss, we replace $\gamma$ with an effective polytropic index $n \approx 1.30$ to 1.35:

$$T_{end,real} = T_1 \cdot r^{n - 1}$$

Step 7: The knock limit can be expressed as a **critical compression ratio**:

$$r_{crit} = \left(\frac{T_{ai} - \Delta T_{rad}}{T_1}\right)^{\frac{1}{n-1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a high-yield KTU derivation. The examiner frequently tests the relationship between $r_{crit}$, $T_{ai}$, and $n$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Directional Effects Summary

The following matrix summarizes the directional effect of each variable on detonation tendency. This is a **must-revise** KTU exam table.

| Engine Variable | Increase in Variable | Effect on Detonation | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compression Ratio ($r$) | Increase | **Increases ↑↑** | Higher end-gas T and P |
| Air–Fuel Ratio (going richer) | Toward stoichiometric | **Increases ↑** | Peak flame temperature |
| Spark Advance | Advance timing | **Increases ↑↑** | Longer pre-flame heating |
| Engine Speed (RPM) | Increase | **Decreases ↓** | Less time for auto-ignition chemistry |
| Engine Load | Increase | **Increases ↑** | Higher in-cylinder pressure |
| Inlet Air Temperature | Increase | **Increases ↑** | Higher initial end-gas T |
| Inlet Air Pressure | Increase | **Increases ↑** | Higher density, faster chemistry |
| Octane Number (RON) | Increase | **Decreases ↓↓** | Higher auto-ignition temperature |
| Cooling Water T | Increase | **Increases ↑** | Higher wall temperature |
| Combustion Chamber Wall Temp | Increase | **Increases ↑** | Reduced heat loss |
| Spark Plug Location (central) | Move to center | **Decreases ↓** | Shorter flame path |
| Swirl/Tumble | Increase | **Decreases ↓** | Faster flame, more turbulence |
| Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR) | Increase | **Decreases ↓** | Dilutes charge, lowers T |
| Humidity (moisture) | Increase | **Decreases ↓** | Water vapor is diluent |
| Altitude (lower P) | Increase altitude | **Decreases ↓** | Lower atmospheric pressure |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** An SI engine operates at $r = 9$, $\phi = 1.05$, with $T_1 = 350$ K, $P_1 = 100$ kPa, and $\gamma = 1.35$ (effective). The fuel's auto-ignition temperature is $T_{ai} = 580$ K. Determine if the engine will knock, assuming negligible heat addition from flame-front radiation ($\Delta T_{rad} \approx 0$).

**Step 1:** Compute end-gas temperature:

$$T_{end} = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1} = 350 \cdot 9^{0.35}$$

Step 2: Evaluate $9^{0.35}$:

$$9^{0.35} = e^{0.35 \cdot \ln 9} = e^{0.35 \cdot 2.1972} = e^{0.7690} = 2.158$$

Step 3: Substitute:

$$T_{end} = 350 \cdot 2.158 = 755.3 \text{ K}$$

Step 4: Compare with $T_{ai}$:

$$T_{end} = 755.3 \text{ K} \quad \text{vs.} \quad T_{ai} = 580 \text{ K}$$

Step 5: Since $T_{end} \gg T_{ai}$, **the engine will knock severely**.

Step 6: Find the critical compression ratio:

$$r_{crit} = \left(\frac{T_{ai}}{T_1}\right)^{\frac{1}{\gamma - 1}} = \left(\frac{580}{350}\right)^{\frac{1}{0.35}} = (1.657)^{2.857}$$

Step 7: Evaluate:

$$\ln(1.657) = 0.5050; \quad 0.5050 \cdot 2.857 = 1.443 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_{crit} = e^{1.443} = 4.23$$

Step 8: Conclusion — Any $r > 4.23$ will cause knock. The engine at $r = 9$ is dangerously close to severe knock.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Interpretation:** A typical modern gasoline engine runs $r = 10$–$12$ without knock *only* because it uses high-octane fuel (RON 95–98). The mathematics above assumes a low-octane reference fuel ($T_{ai} = 580$ K).

## 3.4 The Knock Tendency Equation (Semi-Empirical)

A widely used empirical correlation in KTU textbooks:

$$K_t \propto \frac{1}{ON} \cdot \frac{r^{n}}{T_{inlet}} \cdot \frac{P_{inlet}}{\phi} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{R_u T_{end}}\right)$$

where $K_t$ is the knock tendency index. Higher $K_t$ ⇒ more knock.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Causal Map of Engine Variables on Detonation

The following mermaid diagram maps the **causal relationships** between the major engine variables and the resulting detonation tendency. It is constructed using only alphanumeric node identifiers and clean uppercase labels to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rendering safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["COMPRESSION RATIO INCREASE"]

    nodeE["INLET AIR TEMPERATURE INCREASE"]
    nodeF["ENGINE LOAD INCREASE"]

    nodeH["OCTANE NUMBER DECREASE"]
    nodeI["COOLING INEFFECTIVE OR HOT WALLS"]
    nodeJ["EXCESS EGR ABSENT OR LOW HUMIDITY"]
    nodeK["END GAS AUTO IGNITION DELAY EXPIRED"]
    nodeL["UNCONTROLLED PRESSURE SPIKE OF 2 TO 5 MPA"]
    nodeM["DAMAGING SHOCK WAVE COLLIDES WITH FLAME FRONT"]
    nodeN["ENGINE KNOCK AND MECHANICAL FAILURE"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeC --> nodeB
    nodeD --> nodeB
    nodeE --> nodeB
    nodeF --> nodeB

    nodeG --> nodeK
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK

    style nodeN fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#900,color:#fff
    style nodeL fill:#ffa500,stroke:#a60,color:#000
```
