
## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Functional Architecture of a Knock-Control System

This diagram illustrates how a modern SI engine's Electronic Control Unit (ECU) **detects and mitigates** detonation in real time. It serves as a "block-level functional architecture flow" as required by the protocol for topics requiring complex physical schematics.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS["SPARK PLUG IGNITES MIXTURE"]
    nodeF["FLAME FRONT ADVANCES THROUGH CHARGE"]
    nodeE["END GAS COMPRESSED AND HEATED"]
    nodeK["AUTO IGNITION OF END GAS OCCURS"]
    nodeV["VIBRATION AT 7 KHZ PRODUCED"]
    nodeP["PIEZOELECTRIC KNOCK SENSOR"]
    nodeU["ECU SIGNAL CONDITIONING"]
    nodeD["DETECTION OF KNOCK EVENT"]
    nodeR["RETARD SPARK TIMING BY 1 TO 3 DEGREES"]
    nodeM["MONITOR UNTIL KNOCK CEASES"]
    nodeX["RESTORE OPTIMAL TIMING"]

    nodeS --> nodeF --> nodeE --> nodeK --> nodeV --> nodeP --> nodeU --> nodeD --> nodeR --> nodeM --> nodeX

    style nodeK fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#900,color:#fff
    style nodeR fill:#9ccc65,stroke:#060,color:#000
    style nodeX fill:#4fc3f7,stroke:#036,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Trade-off Between Efficiency and Knock Resistance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["HIGHER COMPRESSION RATIO"]
    nodeB["HIGHER THERMAL EFFICIENCY"]
    nodeC["HIGHER END GAS TEMPERATURE"]
    nodeD["INCREASED KNOCK TENDENCY"]
    nodeE["NEED HIGHER OCTANE FUEL"]
    nodeF["HIGHER FUEL COST"]
    nodeG["DESIGN OPTIMUM AT R EQUALS 10 TO 12 WITH RON 95"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeA --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeG

    style nodeG fill:#4fc3f7,stroke:#036,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Set A]
**Define detonation in SI engines. List any four engine variables that affect detonation.**

**Model Answer:**

Detonation (also called knock or pinging) is the uncontrolled, spontaneous auto-ignition of the unburned end-gas mixture in the combustion chamber of an SI engine, occurring after the normal spark-ignited flame has been initiated. The auto-ignition produces a sudden localized pressure spike of 2–5 MPa that collides with the advancing flame front, producing a metallic knocking sound and potential mechanical damage.

Four engine variables affecting detonation are:

1. **Compression ratio** — higher CR increases end-gas temperature, increasing detonation tendency.
2. **Air–fuel ratio** — detonation peaks near stoichiometric mixtures ($\phi \approx 1.0$).
3. **Spark advance** — excessive advancement increases pre-flame pressure rise.
4. **Engine load** — higher loads produce higher in-cylinder pressures and temperatures.

*(Any four valid variables accepted; 3 marks = 1 for definition + 0.5 each for the four variables.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Set B]
**Explain why an SI engine is more prone to knock at low speeds than at high speeds.**

**Model Answer:**

At low engine speeds, the time available for the end-gas to undergo auto-ignition chemistry (the auto-ignition delay $\tau_{ig}$) is much longer than at high speeds, because the crank spends more time at each angular position near TDC. The end-gas therefore has sufficient residence time at high temperature and pressure for spontaneous ignition to occur before the advancing flame front reaches it.

At high speeds, the flame front traverses the chamber in less time, and the end-gas has less time for pre-ignition chemistry. Although in-cylinder turbulence increases at high RPM (which speeds up flame propagation), the dominant effect is the reduced residence time, which **decreases knock tendency**.

Hence, SI engines are most knock-prone during **low-speed, high-load** operation — for example, when climbing a hill in top gear at 1500 RPM. *(3 marks: 1 for low-speed explanation + 1 for high-speed explanation + 1 for concluding statement.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Knock Questions:**
> 1. Students frequently **confuse detonation with pre-ignition**. Detonation is auto-ignition of end-gas *after* spark; pre-ignition is auto-ignition *before* spark. Always state this distinction.
> 2. Many candidates write *"higher compression ratio reduces detonation"* — this is **the opposite of the truth**. Always emphasize the monotonic increase.
> 3. When asked about octane number, do not just write "increases octane reduces knock" — you must mention that octane measures **resistance to auto-ignition**, and link it to higher allowable compression ratio and efficiency.
> 4. Do not forget to mention the role of **end-gas** — many students write about "the mixture" generally, but examiners specifically look for the word *end-gas*.

### PART B — Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the phenomenon of detonation in SI engines. Discuss the difference between detonation and pre-ignition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the effect of the following engine variables on detonation:
- (i) Compression ratio
- (ii) Air–fuel ratio
- (iii) Spark timing
- (iv) Engine speed and load **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Phenomenon of Detonation (7 marks):**

In a normal SI engine cycle, combustion begins at the spark plug and propagates as a smooth, subsonic flame front across the combustion chamber at a velocity of approximately **15–30 m/s**. As combustion proceeds, the unburned charge ahead of the flame front — called the **end-gas** — is compressed and heated by the rising pressure and temperature of the burned gases. Under normal conditions, the flame front reaches the end of the chamber before the end-gas temperature exceeds its auto-ignition temperature.

**Detonation** occurs when the end-gas auto-ignites spontaneously, *before* the flame front arrives. This creates a second, nearly instantaneous reaction front traveling at **300 m/s or higher** — comparable to a true detonation wave. The two reaction fronts collide, generating a sharp local pressure spike of **2–5 MPa** above the normal peak pressure. On the P–θ indicator diagram, this appears as a high-frequency oscillation superimposed on the normal pressure curve, typically 10°–30° aTDC.

**Sketch description (to be drawn in exam):**

- Combustion chamber with spark plug at one side.
- Normal flame front drawn as a smooth curve advancing across the chamber.
- End-gas region shaded ahead of the flame.
- A second spontaneous ignition point marked inside the end-gas, with arrows showing two opposing reaction fronts colliding.
- A P–θ diagram showing the normal smooth curve with a sudden spike marked "detonation."

**Difference between Detonation and Pre-Ignition:**

| Aspect | Pre-Ignition | Detonation |
|---|---|---|
| Timing | Occurs **before** spark | Occurs **after** spark |
| Cause | Hot spot / overheated component | End-gas auto-ignition from pressure/temperature rise |
| Frequency of pressure rise | Single abnormal rise | Oscillatory high-frequency spikes |
| Detection | Engine runs rough at all loads | Knocking occurs only at high load |

**(b) Effects of Engine Variables on Detonation (7 marks):**

**(i) Compression Ratio (2 marks):**
Higher compression ratio → higher end-gas temperature and pressure → reduced auto-ignition delay → **increased detonation**. The relationship is monotonic and governed by $T_{end} = T_1 r^{\gamma - 1}$. The Critical Compression Ratio (CCR) defines the upper limit for a given fuel. Higher-octane fuels permit higher CCR.

**(ii) Air–Fuel Ratio (2 marks):**
Detonation peaks at slightly rich-to-stoichiometric mixtures ($\phi = 0.85$–$1.05$). Both very rich and very lean mixtures reduce knock: rich mixtures absorb heat via excess fuel vaporization; lean mixtures dilute with excess air. However, very lean operation causes **flame instability** and **miss-fire**. Peak knock occurs near $\phi = 1.0$.

**(iii) Spark Timing (1.5 marks):**
Advanced spark timing gives the end-gas more time to heat and reach auto-ignition temperature before the flame arrives, **increasing detonation**. Retarded spark reduces peak pressure and end-gas temperature, suppressing knock but reducing thermal efficiency. Modern ECUs dynamically retard timing by 1°–3° per cycle when knock is detected by the piezoelectric sensor.

**(iv) Engine Speed and Load (1.5 marks):**
- **Speed:** Lower RPM → longer residence time → more knock. Higher RPM → less knock.
- **Load:** Higher load → higher manifold pressure → higher in-cylinder pressure/temperature → more knock. Part-load reduces knock due to throttle-induced pressure drop and residual gas dilution.

---

### PART B — Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam — June 2019]

**(a)** What is octane number? Explain how it relates to detonation resistance in SI engines. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a P–θ diagram, explain how detonation manifests on an indicator diagram. Describe the following methods to suppress detonation in SI engines: **(7 marks)**
- (i) Use of high-octane fuel
- (ii) Retarding spark timing
- (iii) Effective cooling
- (iv) EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation)

---

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Octane Number (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Octane Number (ON) is a numerical measure of a fuel's resistance to auto-ignition (knocking) in an SI engine, determined by comparing the fuel's knocking tendency with that of a blend of iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane, assigned ON = 100) and n-heptane (assigned ON = 0). The Research Octane Number (RON) is measured under conditions of 600 RPM inlet air temperature; the Motor Octane Number (MON) is measured at 900 RPM with more severe conditions.

**Relationship to Detonation Resistance (5 marks):**
- Higher octane number ⇒ higher auto-ignition temperature ⇒ greater resistance to detonation ⇒ higher allowable compression ratio.
- A fuel with RON = 95 behaves like a 95% iso-octane / 5% n-heptane blend under standard test conditions.
- Higher ON fuels permit operation at higher compression ratios, yielding higher thermal efficiency per the Otto cycle.
- Conversely, low-octane fuels (e.g., straight-run naphtha with RON ≈ 60) detonate severely at CR = 7–8.
- Aromatics (e.g., toluene, xylene) and branched alkanes (iso-octane) have high ON; straight-chain alkanes (n-heptane) have low ON.
- Refineries use **catalytic reforming**, **alkylation**, and **isomerization** to boost octane.

**(b) P–θ Diagram and Knock-Suppression Methods (7 marks):**

**P–θ Diagram (2 marks):**
A normal Otto cycle indicator diagram has a smooth rising compression curve, an abrupt pressure rise at ignition, and a smooth expansion curve. When detonation occurs, a sharp high-frequency pressure oscillation (typically at 5–10 kHz) appears superimposed on the expansion curve, occurring 5°–30° aTDC. The peak pressure may be 1–3 MPa higher than expected. This is the signature of knock on an indicator diagram.

**Suppression Methods (5 marks — 1.25 each):**

**(i) High-Octane Fuel:** Increases the auto-ignition temperature of the charge, raising the knock threshold. Allows higher compression ratios without knock.

**(ii) Retarded Spark Timing:** Reduces pre-flame heat-receipt time in the end-gas, lowering peak pressure and temperature. Mild retardation of 5°–10° can suppress knock but reduces efficiency by 2–4%.

**(iii) Effective Cooling:** Maintains cylinder wall temperature at 100°C–150°C. Lower wall temperatures extract heat from the end-gas, increasing its auto-ignition delay. Water-cooled jackets are standard; oil-cooled pistons and sodium-cooled exhaust valves are used in high-performance engines.

**(iv) EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation):** Introducing 5–15% inert exhaust gas into the intake charge dilutes the air–fuel mixture, lowering peak flame temperature and slowing reaction kinetics. This effectively raises the knock limit. EGR is also a primary NOx-reduction technique.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Module 4, Detonation**

- [x] **Detonation** = spontaneous auto-ignition of **end-gas** *after* spark.
- [x] **Pre-ignition** = auto-ignition *before* spark (often due to hot spots).
- [x] Detonation pressure spike = **2–5 MPa**; velocity of auto-ignition front = **300+ m/s**.
- [x] **Higher CR** → higher $T_{end}$ → **more knock**.
- [x] **Stoichiometric mixture** → maximum knock; lean or rich → less knock.
- [x] **Advanced spark** → more knock; retarded spark → less knock but lower efficiency.
- [x] **Low RPM + high load** → worst knock condition.
- [x] **Higher octane number** → higher auto-ignition temperature → less knock.
- [x] Knock sensor detects **~7 kHz** vibration; ECU retards timing by **1°–3°**.
- [x] Critical Compression Ratio (CCR) is inversely related to octane number.
- [x] EGR, swirl, central spark plug, effective cooling, and humid/cool intake air all **reduce** knock.
- [x] Empirical formula to remember: $T_{end} = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}$.
- [x] Knock-prone operating envelope: low speed, high load, high ambient T, advanced timing, low-octane fuel.
- [x] Always mention "end-gas" explicitly in KTU exam answers — examiners check for this keyword.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When a KTU question asks *"How does X affect detonation?"* — always structure the answer as: **(1) Direction of effect (↑ or ↓), (2) Quantitative or formula-based reasoning, (3) Practical example or operating condition**. This three-part structure consistently scores full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Octane Rating of Fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Octane Rating of Fuels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 4: Octane Rating of Fuels

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Octane Rating** (also called Octane Number, ON) is a standard measure of the **resistance of a fuel to knock (auto-ignition) during combustion in a Spark Ignition (SI) reciprocating engine**. It is a dimensionless scalar quantity determined experimentally by comparing the knocking tendency of a fuel sample against a binary blend of two reference hydrocarbons:

- **Normal Heptane (n-C₇H₁₆)** → assigned octane rating **0**
- **Iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane, C₈H₁₈)** → assigned octane rating **100**

By definition, if a fuel produces the same knock intensity as a blend containing **X % iso-octane** and **(100 − X) % n-heptane**, its octane rating is **X**.

$$
\text{Octane Rating of Fuel} = \text{Vol \% of iso-octane in reference blend that matches knock intensity}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PBMET604):** Octane rating is fundamentally a *comparative knock-resistance index*, NOT a measure of energy content (calorific value). A fuel with a higher octane rating simply resists premature auto-ignition more effectively.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a classroom of students (fuel molecules) waiting for the teacher to ask a question (the spark plug). Some over-enthusiastic students blurt out the answer *before* being called upon (these are **low-octane, knock-prone molecules**). The "octane rating" is essentially a measure of **discipline and patience** of the molecules:

- **Low octane fuel** (e.g., n-heptane, ON = 0) → molecules auto-ignite prematurely under heat and pressure → **knocking**
- **High octane fuel** (e.g., iso-octane, ON = 100) → molecules wait patiently until the spark arrives → **smooth combustion**

Just as a *disciplined* classroom is more orderly, a *high-octane* fuel provides a smoother, more controlled burn — critical for high-compression engines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used:**
> - Auto-ignition temperature of n-heptane ≈ **215 °C (488 K)**
> - Auto-ignition temperature of iso-octane ≈ **447 °C (720 K)**
> - Reference engine speed (RON test): **600 rpm**
> - Reference engine speed (MON test): **900 rpm**
> - Standard inlet air temperature: **38 °C (311 K)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Origin of the Problem — Engine Knock

In a Spark Ignition (SI) engine, the air–fuel mixture is *intended* to ignite via the spark plug. However, toward the end of the compression stroke, the unburned end-gas ahead of the flame front is subjected to extreme **pressure** and **temperature**. If the temperature exceeds the **auto-ignition temperature (AIT)** of the fuel, spontaneous, uncontrolled combustion occurs — this is **knock** (or "pinging").

Knock is undesirable because it:
1. Produces the characteristic metallic "ping" sound.
2. Causes localized pressure spikes that can damage pistons, rings, and bearings.
3. Reduces thermal efficiency and power output.
4. Increases heat transfer to cooling surfaces.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic — How Octane Rating is Determined

1. **Reference Engine Used:** A standardized **Cooperative Fuel Research (CFR) single-cylinder, variable-compression-ratio engine**.
2. **Test Fuel Sample** is run under controlled conditions; the compression ratio is gradually increased until a **standard knock intensity** is recorded (via a **Knockmeter** — typically a "Bounce Pin" or "DIB — Detonation Intensity Board").
3. **Reference Blends** of iso-octane and n-heptane are then tested to find the blend producing the *same* knock intensity at the *same* compression ratio.
4. The **volume percentage of iso-octane** in this matching blend is reported as the **Octane Number**.

## 2.3 Two Standard Test Methods — RON vs MON

| Feature | **Research Octane Number (RON)** | **Motor Octane Number (MON)** |
|---|---|---|
| Standard | ASTM D2699 / IS 14500 | ASTM D2700 / IS 1448 |
| Engine Speed | **600 rpm** | **900 rpm** |
| Inlet Air Temp. | **38 °C (311 K)** (warmed) | **149 °C (422 K)** (warm) |
| Ignition Timing | 13° BTDC (fixed) | Variable (19°–26° BTDC, advanced to onset of knock) |
| Severity | Mild conditions → **higher ON reading** | Severe conditions → **lower ON reading** |
| Typical Use | Lab characterization, fuel specs | Simulates high-speed / high-load driving |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Relationship:** For almost all real fuels: **RON > MON**. The difference is called **Sensitivity**.

$$
\text{Sensitivity} = \text{RON} - \text{MON}
$$

A high sensitivity indicates the fuel performs much better under mild (low-load) conditions than severe (high-load) conditions. Paraffinic fuels typically have low sensitivity; aromatic and olefinic fuels have high sensitivity.

## 2.4 Anti-Knock Index (AKI) — Pump Octane

In many countries (notably the **USA, Canada, Brazil**), the number printed on the petrol pump (e.g., 87, 91, 95) is the **Anti-Knock Index**, defined as the arithmetic mean of RON and MON:

$$
\boxed{\text{AKI} = \frac{\text{RON} + \text{MON}}{2}}
$$

In **Europe, India, and most of Asia**, the displayed octane is the **RON**.

## 2.5 Performance Number (P.N.) — For Fuels with ON > 100

When a fuel resists knock better than pure iso-octane, the simple iso-octane scale is extended using the **Performance Number** (developed by the **Ethyl Corporation**). A reference fuel of iso-octane + tetraethyl lead (TEL) is used as the extrapolated standard above 100.

$$
\text{P.N.} = \frac{(\text{O.N. of isooctane + TEL blend} - 100)}{0.04935} + 100 \quad (\text{for MON conditions})
$$

For RON conditions, the divisor is **0.05128**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Relation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Octane Number definition | $\text{ON} = \% \text{ iso-octane by volume in matching reference blend}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Sensitivity | $\Delta = \text{RON} - \text{MON}$ | Dimensionless, typically 5–12 |
| 3 | Anti-Knock Index | $\text{AKI} = \dfrac{\text{RON} + \text{MON}}{2}$ | Pump rating in USA |
| 4 | RON test speed | $N_{\text{RON}} = 600$ | **rpm** |
| 5 | MON test speed | $N_{\text{MON}} = 900$ | **rpm** |
| 6 | RON inlet temp | $T_{\text{air,RON}} = 311$ | **K** (38 °C) |
| 7 | MON inlet temp | $T_{\text{air,MON}} = 422$ | **K** (149 °C) |
| 8 | Performance Number (MON) | $\text{P.N.} = \dfrac{\text{ON}_{\text{ref}} - 100}{0.04935} + 100$ | for fuels with ON > 100 |
| 9 | Performance Number (RON) | $\text{P.N.} = \dfrac{\text{ON}_{\text{ref}} - 100}{0.05128} + 100$ | for fuels with ON > 100 |
| 10 | Compression Ratio relation | $r_k = r_s \cdot \left( \dfrac{\text{ON}_{\text{fuel}} + \text{C}}{\text{ON}_{\text{refuel}} + \text{C}} \right)$ | Empirical; C ≈ const. |
| 11 | Knocking tendency (qualitative) | $\propto \dfrac{1}{\text{Octane Number}}$ | Inverse relation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - Octane rating directly dictates the **maximum usable compression ratio** $r_k$ of an engine.
> - A higher octane fuel permits a higher $r_k$, which yields higher **thermal efficiency** per the **Otto cycle**: $\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$.
> - This is why **premium petrols (RON 95–98)** are mandated for **turbocharged and high-performance engines**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation — Matching Blend Composition from Knock Intensity

A fuel sample produces a standard knock intensity on the CFR engine. Two reference blends are tested:

- **Blend A:** 80 % iso-octane + 20 % n-heptane → produces a *milder* knock than the sample.
- **Blend B:** 90 % iso-octane + 10 % n-heptane → produces a *heavier* knock than the sample.

We interpolate linearly between A and B to find the matching percentage.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ON}_{\text{sample}} &= \text{ON}_A + (\text{ON}_B - \text{ON}_A) \cdot \frac{\text{Knock}_s - \text{Knock}_A}{\text{Knock}_B - \text{Knock}_A} \\[6pt]
&= 80 + (90 - 80) \cdot \frac{\text{Knock}_s - \text{Knock}_A}{\text{Knock}_B - \text{Knock}_A}
\end{aligned}
$$

The bracketed ratio is the **bracket interpolation factor**, universally applied in CFR octane testing.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Computing Sensitivity and AKI

A premium gasoline sample yields the following test results:

- $\text{RON} = 98$
- $\text{MON} = 86$

**Find (a) Sensitivity and (b) Anti-Knock Index.**

### Solution (a) — Sensitivity

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sensitivity} &= \text{RON} - \text{MON} \\[4pt]
&= 98 - 86 \\[4pt]
&= 12
\end{aligned}
$$

A sensitivity of **12** is high, indicating an aromatic-rich fuel — typical of modern reformulated gasoline.

### Solution (b) — Anti-Knock Index

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AKI} &= \frac{\text{RON} + \text{MON}}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{98 + 86}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{184}{2} \\[4pt]
&= 92
\end{aligned}
$$

The **pump octane (AKI)** would be displayed as **92**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Comparing Two Fuels via Otto Efficiency

Engine X uses **Fuel P** (RON = 91) at a maximum knock-free compression ratio $r_P = 9.5 : 1$.
Engine Y uses **Fuel Q** (RON = 98) and can safely run at $r_Q = 11.0 : 1$.

Given $\gamma = 1.4$ for the combustion products, compute the **percentage gain in Otto-cycle thermal efficiency** when switching from Fuel P to Fuel Q.

### Solution — Otto Cycle Thermal Efficiency

The Otto efficiency formula is:

$$
\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}
$$

For Fuel P ($r_P = 9.5$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_P &= 1 - \frac{1}{(9.5)^{1.4 - 1}} \\[4pt]
&= 1 - \frac{1}{(9.5)^{0.4}} \\[4pt]
&= 1 - \frac{1}{2.5398} \\[4pt]
&= 1 - 0.3937 \\[4pt]
&= 0.6063 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 60.63\%
\end{aligned}
$$

For Fuel Q ($r_Q = 11.0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_Q &= 1 - \frac{1}{(11.0)^{0.4}} \\[4pt]
&= 1 - \frac{1}{2.6028} \\[4pt]
&= 1 - 0.3842 \\[4pt]
&= 0.6158 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 61.58\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Percentage Gain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{Gain} &= \frac{\eta_Q - \eta_P}{\eta_P} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.6158 - 0.6063}{0.6063} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.0095}{0.6063} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 1.567 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Even a modest 7-point jump in RON (91 → 98) unlocks a higher compression ratio and delivers roughly **1.57 % more thermal efficiency** — a meaningful gain in BSFC and fuel economy.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Fuel Structure on Octane Rating

| Hydrocarbon Family | Example | Approx. Octane Rating (RON) | Knock Resistance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **n-Paraffins** (straight chain) | n-heptane | 0 | Very poor |
| **n-Paraffins** (lighter) | n-hexane | ~25 | Poor |
| **Isoparaffins** (branched) | iso-octane | 100 | Excellent |
| **Olefins** (alkenes) | 1-hexene | ~76 | Good |
| **Naphthenes** (cycloalkanes) | cyclohexane | ~43 | Moderate |
| **Aromatics** | toluene | ~120 | Excellent |
| **Alcohols** | ethanol | ~108–110 | Excellent |
| **Ethers** | MTBE | ~118 | Excellent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Modern refineries use **catalytic reforming** and **isomerization** units specifically to convert low-octane n-paraffins and naphthenes into high-octane iso-paraffins and aromatics — directly raising the fuel's octane rating to meet market demand.

---

## 3.5 Anti-Knock Additives — Mathematical Equivalence

The lead-based anti-knock **tetraethyl lead (TEL)** historically boosted octane ratings dramatically. A rough empirical correlation:

$$
\Delta\text{ON}_{\text{lead}} \approx k_{\text{TEL}} \cdot m_{\text{TEL}}
$$

where $m_{\text{TEL}}$ is the lead concentration in grams per litre and $k_{\text{TEL}}$ is the **lead susceptibility** (typically 4–6 ON per g/L of Pb for MON, 8–12 ON per g/L of Pb for RON in sensitive fuels).

Due to toxicity, modern fuels use **oxygenated additives** like **MTBE, ETBE, ethanol** — these have **inherent octane value** rather than acting as catalysts.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Octane Rating Determination Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Fuel Sample Received"] --> B["Mount in CFR Engine"]
    B --> C["Vary Compression Ratio"]
    C --> D["Record Knock Intensity via Knockmeter"]
    D --> E{"Standard Knock Reached?"}
    E -- "No" --> C
    E -- "Yes" --> F["Fix Compression Ratio r_k"]
    F --> G["Test Reference Blend A: Lower % Iso-octane"]
    G --> H["Test Reference Blend B: Higher % Iso-octane"]
    H --> I["Bracket Interpolation"]
    I --> J["Compute Octane Number"]
    J --> K{"RON or MON Test?"}
    K -- "RON 600 rpm 311 K" --> L["Report Research Octane Number"]
    K -- "MON 900 rpm 422 K" --> M["Report Motor Octane Number"]
    L --> N["Compute Sensitivity and AKI"]
    M --> N
    N --> O["Final Fuel Specification Sheet"]
```

```mermaid
    end
```
