
## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Octane Rating Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root["Octane Rating of Fuels"]
    Root --> R1["Test Methods"]
    Root --> R2["Derived Indices"]
    Root --> R3["Knock Chemistry"]
    Root --> R4["Enhancement Strategies"]

    R1 --> R1a["RON ASTM D2699"]
    R1 --> R1b["MON ASTM D2700"]

    R2 --> R2a["Sensitivity"]
    R2 --> R2b["Anti Knock Index AKI"]
    R2 --> R2c["Performance Number"]

    R3 --> R3a["End Gas Auto Ignition"]
    R3 --> R3b["Flame Front Propagation"]
    R3 --> R3c["Hydrocarbon Structure Effect"]

    R4 --> R4a["Refining Isomerization"]
    R4 --> R4b["Catalytic Reforming"]
    R4 --> R4c["Oxygenate Addition Ethanol MTBE"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define octane rating of a SI engine fuel. Why is normal heptane assigned a value of zero and iso-octane assigned a value of 100?

**Model Answer:**

Octane rating is the measure of the **resistance of a fuel to knock** (auto-ignition) in a spark ignition engine under standard test conditions.

**Why n-heptane → 0:** Normal heptane (n-C₇H₁₆) is a straight-chain paraffin that auto-ignites *very easily* under compression. It produces the *maximum* knock intensity among common hydrocarbons — hence chosen as the **lower reference** with the value **0**.

**Why iso-octane → 100:** Iso-octane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane, C₈H₁₈) is a heavily branched paraffin whose branched molecular structure raises its auto-ignition temperature. It shows *excellent* knock resistance under the same conditions — hence chosen as the **upper reference** with the value **100**.

By bracketing an unknown fuel between these two, its octane rating is reported as the **volume percentage of iso-octane** in the blend that matches its knock intensity. **[3 marks]**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q:** Distinguish between RON and MON of a fuel. What is meant by "sensitivity"?

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | RON (Research Octane Number) | MON (Motor Octane Number) |
|---|---|---|
| Engine speed | 600 rpm | 900 rpm |
| Inlet air temperature | 38 °C (311 K) | 149 °C (422 K) |
| Conditions | Mild | Severe (high-load) |
| Typical result | Higher value | Lower value |

**Sensitivity** is defined as the difference between RON and MON:

$$
\text{Sensitivity} = \text{RON} - \text{MON}
$$

It quantifies how much worse a fuel performs under severe conditions compared to mild ones. Aromatic and olefinic fuels exhibit high sensitivity (8–12); paraffinic fuels exhibit low sensitivity (3–6). **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### ➤ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]`

**Q (a)** Explain in detail the working of the **CFR engine** used for determining the octane rating of a fuel. Discuss both the RON and MON test methods, including the standard test conditions. **[7 marks]**

**Q (b)** A gasoline sample has $\text{RON} = 95$ and $\text{MON} = 85$. Compute its **(i)** Sensitivity, **(ii)** Anti-Knock Index, and **(iii)** the percentage increase in Otto-cycle thermal efficiency if the engine's compression ratio can be raised from **8.5 : 1** to **10.5 : 1** by virtue of this fuel's high rating. Take $\gamma = 1.4$. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A (a)

The **Cooperative Fuel Research (CFR) engine** is the **standard ASTM/IS-approved single-cylinder, four-stroke, variable-compression-ratio engine** used universally for octane rating.

**Salient features of the CFR engine:**

1. **Single cylinder** with continuously variable compression ratio (typically 4:1 to 18:1) — varied by moving the cylinder head up/down via a worm-gear mechanism while keeping combustion chamber volume adjustable.
2. **Carburetor** for precise air-fuel mixture control.
3. **Knockmeter** — usually a **bounce-pin type** sensor or modern pressure transducer — that converts cylinder head vibrations into a readable knock intensity on a "DIB" (Detonation Intensity Board).
4. **Standardized cooling, lubrication, and ignition systems** to ensure test reproducibility.

**RON test (ASTM D2699):**
- Engine speed: **600 rpm**
- Inlet air temperature: **38 °C (311 K)**
- Ignition timing: **13° BTDC** (fixed)
- Spark advance: not varied

**MON test (ASTM D2700):**
- Engine speed: **900 rpm**
- Inlet air temperature: **149 °C (422 K)**
- Ignition timing: **Varied from 19° to 26° BTDC** depending on compression ratio (advanced until incipient knock)
- Spark advance: adjusted

**Procedure (bracket method):**
1. Mount the test fuel and adjust compression ratio upward until a **standard knock intensity** is reached.
2. Without changing the compression ratio, run reference blends of iso-octane and n-heptane.
3. The blend matching the fuel's knock intensity defines the **Octane Number** as the volume % of iso-octane.

*Any reference fuel test bracket typically spans ~2 ON points (e.g., 90 and 92); linear interpolation yields the result.* **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A (b)

**Given:**
- $\text{RON} = 95$, $\text{MON} = 85$
- $r_1 = 8.5 : 1$, $r_2 = 10.5 : 1$
- $\gamma = 1.4$

**(i) Sensitivity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sensitivity} &= \text{RON} - \text{MON} \\
&= 95 - 85 \\
&= 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Anti-Knock Index:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AKI} &= \frac{\text{RON} + \text{MON}}{2} \\
&= \frac{95 + 85}{2} \\
&= 90
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Otto-cycle thermal efficiency comparison:**

Using $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ with $\gamma - 1 = 0.4$:

For $r_1 = 8.5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_1 &= 1 - \frac{1}{(8.5)^{0.4}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{1}{2.4423} \\
&= 1 - 0.4094 \\
&= 0.5906 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 59.06\%
\end{aligned}
$$

For $r_2 = 10.5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_2 &= 1 - \frac{1}{(10.5)^{0.4}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{1}{2.5453} \\
&= 1 - 0.3929 \\
&= 0.6071 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 60.71\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Percentage increase:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{ Increase} &= \frac{\eta_2 - \eta_1}{\eta_1} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{0.6071 - 0.5906}{0.5906} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{0.0165}{0.5906} \times 100 \\
&= 2.79\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Results:**
- Sensitivity = **10**
- AKI = **90**
- Efficiency gain = **≈ 2.79 %** **[7 marks]**

---

### ➤ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]`

**Q (a)** Explain the **structural effect of hydrocarbons** on octane rating. Discuss the role of **refining processes** (isomerization and catalytic reforming) and **oxygenate additives** in enhancing octane rating. **[7 marks]**

**Q (b)** A fuel sample produces the same standard knock intensity as a reference blend containing **92 % iso-octane** and **8 % n-heptane** under RON test conditions, and **86 % iso-octane + 14 % n-heptane** under MON conditions. Determine the **(i)** RON, **(ii)** MON, **(iii)** Sensitivity, and **(iv)** Anti-Knock Index of the fuel. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (a)

**Effect of hydrocarbon structure on octane rating:**

Octane rating is strongly influenced by molecular architecture:

1. **Branching:** Branched-chain paraffins (iso-paraffins) have **higher** octane ratings than straight-chain (normal) paraffins of the same carbon count. Branching raises the auto-ignition temperature by stabilizing the molecule against pre-flame oxidation reactions. *Example:* n-hexane (RON ~25) vs 2,2-dimethylbutane (RON ~94).
2. **Aromaticity:** Aromatic rings are extremely knock-resistant (toluene RON ~120) because their ring structure has high bond-dissociation energy and resists radical-chain auto-ignition.
3. **Olefins:** Unsaturated hydrocarbons show moderate-to-good knock resistance (1-hexene RON ~76).
4. **Naphthenes:** Cycloalkanes have moderate octane ratings (cyclohexane RON ~43).
5. **Chain length:** Within a given family, **shorter chains generally have higher octane ratings** than longer chains.

**Refining processes that enhance octane:**

1. **Isomerization:** Converts low-octane n-paraffins (RON 25–65) into higher-octane iso-paraffins (RON 80–95) by rearranging the carbon skeleton using a platinum/chloride-alumina catalyst at ~150 °C.
2. **Catalytic Reforming:** Converts low-octane naphthenes and n-paraffins into high-octane aromatics (benzene, toluene, xylenes — collectively **BTX**) using platinum-rhenium catalysts at ~500 °C. This is the **primary octane-boosting unit** in modern refineries.
3. **Alkylation:** Combines light olefins (C₃, C₄) with isobutane to produce high-octane alkylate (RON ~94–97), a premium blending stock.

**Oxygenate additives:**

Modern fuels use oxygenated compounds instead of toxic TEL. Common ones include:

- **MTBE (Methyl tert-butyl ether):** RON ≈ 118, blending value ~115
- **ETBE (Ethyl tert-butyl ether):** RON ≈ 118
- **Ethanol (C₂H₅OH):** RON ≈ 108–110
- **Methanol:** RON ≈ 106

These additives also promote more complete combustion, reducing CO and unburned hydrocarbon emissions. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (b)

**Given (direct interpretation):**
- Reference blend matching fuel's RON condition: 92 % iso-octane → $\text{RON} = 92$
- Reference blend matching fuel's MON condition: 86 % iso-octane → $\text{MON} = 86$

**(i) RON:**

$$
\text{RON} = 92
$$

**(ii) MON:**

$$
\text{MON} = 86
$$

**(iii) Sensitivity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sensitivity} &= \text{RON} - \text{MON} \\
&= 92 - 86 \\
&= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Anti-Knock Index:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AKI} &= \frac{\text{RON} + \text{MON}}{2} \\
&= \frac{92 + 86}{2} \\
&= 89
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Results:**
- RON = **92**
- MON = **86**
- Sensitivity = **6** (moderately sensitive — typical of isomerate-rich blends)
- AKI = **89** **[7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
>
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students write "Octane number = calorific value of fuel" — **WRONG**. Octane number is *knock resistance*, not energy content. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Reporting MON as equal to or greater than RON — **logically impossible** for hydrocarbon fuels since MON conditions are always more severe.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to specify whether the displayed octane at a petrol pump is RON (India/Europe) or AKI (USA) — KTU expects this contextual awareness.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** In Otto-efficiency calculation, students incorrectly use $\gamma - 1$ as $0.4$ but write the exponent as $(\gamma - 1)$ symbolically and skip numerical evaluation. **Always substitute $\gamma = 1.4$ explicitly.**
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** Treating octane number as a linear function of compression ratio. The relationship is **empirical and nonlinear**; in problems, treat given compression ratios as fixed design values.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read before every exam):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Octane rating = volume % of iso-octane in a blend of iso-octane + n-heptane that matches the fuel's knock intensity on a CFR engine.
- ✅ **Reference fuels:** n-heptane (ON = 0), iso-octane (ON = 100).
- ✅ **CFR Engine:** Single-cylinder, variable compression ratio, with knockmeter (bounce pin / pressure transducer).
- ✅ **RON** = ASTM D2699, 600 rpm, 38 °C inlet air, fixed timing 13° BTDC.
- ✅ **MON** = ASTM D2700, 900 rpm, 149 °C inlet air, variable timing.
- ✅ **Always RON > MON** for hydrocarbon fuels.
- ✅ **Sensitivity = RON − MON** (aromatics high, paraffins low).
- ✅ **AKI = (RON + MON)/2** — pump rating in USA; India uses RON.
- ✅ **Performance Number** is used for fuels with ON > 100 (aviation gasoline, racing fuels).
- ✅ **Octane ↑ → Auto-ignition temperature ↑ → Knock resistance ↑ → Higher compression ratio allowed → Higher Otto efficiency.**
- ✅ **Otto efficiency formula:** $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ with $\gamma \approx 1.4$.
- ✅ **Hydrocarbon structure ranking (low → high ON):** n-paraffin < naphthene < olefin < iso-paraffin < aromatic.
- ✅ **Refinery processes that boost octane:** isomerization, catalytic reforming, alkylation.
- ✅ **Modern anti-knock additives:** MTBE, ETBE, ethanol (TEL banned due to lead toxicity).
- ✅ **Anti-Knock Index (AKI)** is also called **Pump Octane Number (PON)**.
- ✅ **Knocking** is *not* the same as **pre-ignition**; knock occurs in the end-gas after spark, pre-ignition occurs before spark.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CI Engines - Stages of Combustion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** CI Engines - Stages of Combustion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
A **Compression Ignition (CI) Engine**, also known as a **Diesel Engine**, is an internal combustion engine in which ignition of the fuel is achieved by the high temperature of the compressed air charge, without the need for an external spark. The fuel is injected into the combustion chamber at or near the end of the compression stroke, where it mixes with the hot compressed air and self-ignites. The process of combustion in a CI engine is heterogeneous, non-premixed, and occurs in **four distinct stages** that are observable on a pressure-crank angle (p–θ) diagram.

The four stages of combustion in a CI engine are:
1. **Ignition Delay Period** (also called Ignition Lag)
2. **Rapid Combustion / Uncontrolled Combustion / Premixed Combustion Phase**
3. **Slow / Controlled / Diffusion Combustion Phase**
4. **After-Burning Phase** (sometimes considered part of stage 3)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students must be able to sketch and label the stages on a p–θ diagram and identify the crank angle positions (θ₁, θ₂, θ₃) demarcating each stage. Questions frequently ask for differences between SI and CI engine combustion stages.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine trying to light a campfire by spraying fuel onto hot charcoal. The fuel does not burst into flame the instant it touches the charcoal — there is a tiny moment of hesitation while the fuel droplets warm up, vaporize, mix with air, and finally reach the **auto-ignition temperature** (~250 °C for diesel). After that delay, the fuel vapor and air mixture burns almost explosively (like a controlled "whoosh"), followed by a steady, sustained burn of the remaining fuel as it continues to mix with air. CI engine combustion behaves in exactly the same way — first a brief **delay**, then a **violent but short burst**, and finally a **slow, controlled burn**.

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Auto-ignition temperature of diesel:** approximately **250 °C** to **300 °C**
- **Cetane number of standard diesel:** **45 to 55**
- **Typical compression ratio of CI engines:** **14:1 to 22:1**
- **Ignition delay duration:** approximately **0.001 to 0.003 seconds** (crank-angle equivalent: ~8° to 12° of crank rotation)
- **Peak cylinder pressure:** approximately **60 to 80 bar** in naturally aspirated CI engines
- **Maximum rate of pressure rise (dP/dθ) during rapid combustion:** should be limited to **4 to 6 bar/degree** to ensure smooth engine operation

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Auto-Ignition:** Auto-ignition is the spontaneous combustion of a fuel-air mixture at a critical temperature, *without* the need for an external ignition source. In a CI engine, the air is compressed to a temperature well above the auto-ignition point of the fuel, ensuring reliable self-ignition upon injection.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physical Sequence of Events in a CI Combustion Cycle

A CI engine completes its four-stroke cycle (or two-stroke cycle) with combustion occurring over a portion of the power stroke. The fuel injector typically begins injection **10° to 20° before TDC** (Top Dead Center) so that the pressure peak occurs slightly after TDC, providing the most efficient torque output.

The combustion process is best understood by breaking it down chronologically:

---

### 2.2 Stage 1: Ignition Delay Period (Preparation Phase)

This is the interval between the **start of fuel injection (θᵢ)** and the **start of combustion (θ₁)**, i.e., the point where the pressure trace first deviates upward from the motoring (non-firing) compression curve.

$$ \theta_{\text{delay}} = \theta_1 - \theta_i \;\; (\text{typically } 8° \text{ to } 12° \text{ of crank angle}) $$

The ignition delay is divided into two sub-phases:

#### (a) Physical Delay
- Duration: Fuel injection → fuel atomization → fuel vaporization → mixing with air.
- During this period, the fuel is being physically prepared to burn.
- The fuel temperature must rise to the local mixture temperature.

#### (b) Chemical Delay
- Duration: Time taken for the pre-combustion chemical reactions (oxidation) to occur.
- The fuel molecules react with oxygen to form peroxides and other intermediate species until self-ignition.
- This is the dominant portion of the total delay (typically 60–80 % of the total).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Factors Affecting Ignition Delay:**
> - **Fuel property:** Higher **cetane number** → shorter chemical delay (better auto-ignition quality).
> - **Compression ratio:** Higher compression → higher temperature → shorter delay.
> - **Intake air temperature and pressure:** Higher T and P → shorter delay.
> - **Engine speed:** Higher speed → shorter absolute delay time (in seconds), but crank-angle equivalent may be similar.
> - **Fuel spray quality:** Better atomization → shorter physical delay.

**Wolfer's Correlation (often cited in KTU textbooks):**
An empirical relation for ignition delay in crank angle degrees:

$$ \theta_d = \frac{0.44}{n \cdot p^{1.19}} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{4650}{T}\right) $$

where:
- $\theta_d$ = ignition delay (seconds)
- $n$ = engine speed (rev/s)
- $p$ = compression pressure (bar)
- $T$ = compression temperature (K)

---

### 2.3 Stage 2: Rapid Combustion (Premixed Combustion / Uncontrolled Combustion)

This stage starts at **θ₁** (start of pressure rise) and ends at **θ₂** (peak pressure point). During the ignition delay, fuel was being injected, vaporized, and mixed with air, but had not yet ignited. As soon as the first batch of fuel-air mixture ignites, *all* the accumulated premixed fuel burns almost simultaneously, causing a sharp, near-explosive rise in pressure.

- **Rate of pressure rise:** very high → 4 to 6 bar/degree (ideal) but can spike to 10 bar/degree or higher in extreme cases.
- **Heat release rate:** extremely high in a short crank-angle window.
- This phase produces the characteristic **diesel knock** (rough running) if too much fuel accumulates during the delay.

> [!WARNING]
> **Diesel Knock Cause:** If the ignition delay is too long, an excessive amount of fuel is injected and premixed before ignition. The subsequent rapid combustion of this large mass produces a sharp, audible pressure wave, perceived as **diesel knock**, which is mechanically damaging.

The fraction of total fuel burned in the rapid combustion stage:

$$ f_1 = \frac{m_{f,1}}{m_{f,\text{total}}} = 0.7 \text{ to } 0.85 $$

---

### 2.4 Stage 3: Controlled Combustion (Diffusion Combustion / Slow Combustion)

This stage begins at **θ₂** (peak pressure) and ends at **θ₃** (end of injection or end of rapid heat release). Here, the rate of combustion is governed by the rate at which the **incoming fuel droplets mix with the remaining air**.

- The pressure drops from the peak value smoothly.
- The rate of heat release is much lower than in stage 2.
- Combustion is **diffusion-controlled** (mixing-limited), not kinetics-limited.

The fraction of fuel burned in the controlled combustion stage:

$$ f_2 = \frac{m_{f,2}}{m_{f,\text{total}}} = 0.15 \text{ to } 0.30 $$

---

### 2.5 Stage 4: After-Burning

- A small fraction of the fuel (~2–5 %) continues to burn after the injector has stopped delivering fuel and after the peak pressure is past.
- Caused by incomplete mixing during stage 3.
- After-burning extends the heat release into the expansion stroke, reducing engine efficiency.

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ignition delay (angle) | $\theta_d = \theta_1 - \theta_i$ | degrees of crank rotation | Typical: 8°–12° |
| Ignition delay (time) | $\theta_d = \dfrac{0.44}{n\,p^{1.19}}\,\exp\!\left(\dfrac{4650}{T}\right)$ | seconds | Wolfer's correlation |
| Fuel mass fraction (Stage 2) | $f_1 = m_{f,1}/m_{f,\text{total}}$ | dimensionless | Range: 0.70–0.85 |
| Fuel mass fraction (Stage 3) | $f_2 = m_{f,2}/m_{f,\text{total}}$ | dimensionless | Range: 0.15–0.30 |
| Mass of fuel burned in Stage 2 | $m_{f,1} = f_1 \cdot m_{f,\text{total}}$ | kg | Premixed phase |
| Mass of fuel burned in Stage 3 | $m_{f,2} = f_2 \cdot m_{f,\text{total}}$ | kg | Diffusion phase |
| Heat released in Stage 2 | $Q_1 = m_{f,1} \cdot \text{CV}$ | kJ | CV = calorific value |
| Heat released in Stage 3 | $Q_2 = m_{f,2} \cdot \text{CV}$ | kJ | Diffusion heat release |
| Rate of pressure rise | $(dP/d\theta)_{\max}$ | bar/deg | Limit: $\leq 4$–$6$ bar/deg |
| Compression end temperature | $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}$ | K | Ideal cycle reference |
| Compression end pressure | $p_2 = p_1 \cdot r^{\gamma}$ | bar | Ideal cycle reference |
| Cetane number (CN) | Empirical fuel rating | dimensionless | Higher CN $\Rightarrow$ shorter delay |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The four-stage model is used in engine simulation software (e.g., GT-Power, AVL FIRE) to model in-cylinder pressure and predict NOₓ emissions. A well-designed CI engine aims to minimize stage-2 pressure rise (to reduce noise and mechanical stress) while maximizing stage-3 efficiency (for fuel economy).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Heat Release Rate During Rapid Combustion Stage

In the **rapid combustion (stage 2)** of a CI engine, a large fraction $f_1$ of the injected fuel mass burns in a very short crank-angle interval $\Delta\theta = \theta_2 - \theta_1$. We derive the **average rate of heat release** for this stage.

**Given:**
- Total fuel injected per cycle: $m_f$ (kg)
- Mass fraction burned in stage 2: $f_1$
- Calorific value of fuel: $\text{CV}$ (kJ/kg)
- Crank-angle duration of stage 2: $\Delta\theta_2 = \theta_2 - \theta_1$ (degrees)
- Engine speed: $N$ (rpm)

**Step 1 — Mass of fuel burned in stage 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{f,2} &= f_1 \cdot m_f
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* By definition, the mass fraction $f_1$ of the total injected fuel is consumed during the rapid combustion phase.

**Step 2 — Total heat released in stage 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_2 &= m_{f,2} \cdot \text{CV} = f_1 \cdot m_f \cdot \text{CV}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Each kilogram of fuel releases its calorific value (CV) of chemical energy upon complete combustion.

**Step 3 — Time duration of stage 2:**

The crank rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, i.e., $360 N$ degrees per minute. Hence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t_2 &= \frac{\Delta\theta_2}{360\,N/60} = \frac{\Delta\theta_2}{6\,N} \;\; \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Crank angle converted to time using the rotational speed relationship.

**Step 4 — Average rate of heat release in stage 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{dQ}{dt}\right)_{2,\text{avg}} &= \frac{Q_2}{\Delta t_2} = \frac{f_1 \cdot m_f \cdot \text{CV} \cdot 6\,N}{\Delta\theta_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Divide total heat by the time interval to obtain the mean heat-release power in this stage.

**Final compact form:**

$$
\boxed{\;\left(\frac{dQ}{dt}\right)_{2,\text{avg}} = \frac{6\,N \cdot f_1 \cdot m_f \cdot \text{CV}}{\Delta\theta_2}\;}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Average Rate of Pressure Rise in Stage 2

The **rate of pressure rise** $(dP/d\theta)$ is the critical parameter that distinguishes smooth from rough CI engine operation.

**Given:**
- Peak pressure: $p_{\max}$ (bar)
- Compression end pressure (motoring pressure at θ₁): $p_c$ (bar)
- Crank-angle interval: $\Delta\theta_2 = \theta_2 - \theta_1$ (degrees)

**Step 1 — Net pressure rise during stage 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta p &= p_{\max} - p_c
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* The pressure climbs from the motoring curve value $p_c$ at the start of combustion to the peak $p_{\max}$ at θ₂.

**Step 2 — Average rate of pressure rise:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{dP}{d\theta}\right)_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{\Delta p}{\Delta\theta_2} = \frac{p_{\max} - p_c}{\theta_2 - \theta_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Average rate of change of pressure with respect to crank angle.

**KTU Benchmark:** For acceptable smoothness, this rate should be in the range of **4 to 6 bar per degree**. Values above **10 bar/degree** produce audible diesel knock and may damage the bearings.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Ignition Delay from Wolfer's Correlation

Wolfer (1938) experimentally correlated ignition delay with engine operating conditions. Given operating data, we can compute the delay:

**Step 1 — Identify engine conditions:**
- Engine speed: $n$ rev/s (= $N$/60)
- Compression end pressure: $p$ bar
- Compression end temperature: $T$ K

**Step 2 — Apply Wolfer's correlation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_d &= \frac{0.44}{n \cdot p^{1.19}} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{4650}{T}\right) \;\; \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to crank-angle degrees:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{d,\text{deg}} &= \theta_d \cdot 6N \;\; \text{degrees}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Multiply the time delay by the rotational speed to obtain the crank-angle equivalent.

**Worked Numerical Example:**

**Given:** $N = 1500$ rpm, $p = 35$ bar, $T = 900$ K
**Step 1:** $n = 1500/60 = 25$ rev/s

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_d &= \frac{0.44}{25 \cdot (35)^{1.19}} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{4650}{900}\right) \;\; \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $(35)^{1.19}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
(35)^{1.19} &\approx 60.84
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the exponential:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\exp\!\left(\frac{4650}{900}\right) &= \exp(5.1667) \approx 175.2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Combine:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_d &= \frac{0.44}{25 \cdot 60.84} \cdot 175.2 = \frac{0.44 \cdot 175.2}{1521} = \frac{77.09}{1521} \approx 0.0507 \;\text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to degrees:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{d,\text{deg}} &= 0.0507 \cdot 6 \cdot 1500 = 0.0507 \cdot 9000 \approx 456° \;\; \text{(this exceeds a full revolution, indicating Wolfer's value is dimensional)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The above numerical example reveals that Wolfer's original form is dimensionally inconsistent. The exam-friendly KTU version is used only for **qualitative comparison** between operating conditions. Students should be cautious in numerical substitution and may use the simplified Arrhenius-type form for comparative analysis.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Stage-Wise Comparison

| Parameter | Stage 1: Ignition Delay | Stage 2: Rapid Combustion | Stage 3: Controlled Combustion | Stage 4: After-Burning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Crank-angle interval | $\theta_i \rightarrow \theta_1$ | $\theta_1 \rightarrow \theta_2$ | $\theta_2 \rightarrow \theta_3$ | $\theta_3 \rightarrow \theta_f$ |
| Fuel injection status | Injecting | Still injecting (end) | Still injecting (tail) | No injection |
| Mixture state | Preparing (vaporization, mixing) | Premixed, auto-ignited | Diffusion-controlled | Tail-end unburned fuel |
| Rate of pressure rise | ≈ 0 (no combustion) | Very high (4–10 bar/deg) | Moderate, near zero or negative | Slight, decaying |
| Heat release rate | ≈ 0 | Very high peak | Moderate, sustained | Low, decaying |
| Duration (typical) | 8°–12° of crank | 30°–60° of crank | 30°–50° of crank | Extends into expansion |
| Mass fraction burned | ≈ 0 | $f_1$ = 0.70–0.85 | $f_2$ = 0.15–0.30 | ≈ 0.02–0.05 |
| Governing physics | Kinetics + atomization | Kinetics (autocatalytic) | Mixing (turbulent diffusion) | Slow oxidation of residuals |
| Noise/knock potential | None | **Maximum** (diesel knock) | Low | Negligible |
| NOₓ formation | None | **Peak** (high T) | Continued | Low |
| Soot/PM formation | None | Low | **Peak** (fuel-rich pockets) | Continued |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Stage-by-Stage Combustion Process

The mermaid diagram below maps the sequential flow of events in CI engine combustion, including the sub-phases and key outputs of each stage.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Fuel Injection Starts at theta_i] --> B[STAGE 1 IGNITION DELAY]
    B --> B1[Sub-Phase A Physical Delay]
    B1 --> B2[Fuel Atomization and Vaporization]
    B2 --> B3[Sub-Phase B Chemical Delay]
    B3 --> B4[Pre-Combustion Oxidation Reactions]
    B4 --> C[STAGE 2 RAPID COMBUSTION]
    C --> C1[Premixed Fuel-Air Burns Explosively]
    C1 --> C2[Sharp Pressure Rise]
    C2 --> C3[Peak Pressure at theta_2]
    C3 --> D[STAGE 3 CONTROLLED COMBUSTION]
    D --> D1[Diffusion Limited Mixing]
    D1 --> D2[Moderate Heat Release]
    D2 --> D3[Pressure Begins to Fall at theta_3]
    D3 --> E[STAGE 4 AFTER BURNING]
    E --> E1[Residual Fuel Burns Slowly]
    E1 --> F[Combustion Ends at theta_f]
    C2 -. Diesel Knock Risk .-> G[CAUTION High dP by dtheta]
    C1 -. NO_x Peak .-> H[CAUTION High Local Temperature]
    D1 -. Soot Peak .-> I[CAUTION Fuel Rich Zones]
```

### 4.2 Pressure-Crank Angle Diagram (Conceptual Map)

The four stages of CI combustion are best visualized on a **p–θ diagram**, which plots cylinder pressure against crank angle. Below is a block-level schematic representation:

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph TDC[TDC Region]
        A1[theta_i] -->|Injection Start| A2[theta_1]
    end
    subgraph Stage1[STAGE 1 IGNITION DELAY]
        A2 -->|theta_d about 8 to 12 deg| A3[theta_1 End of Delay]
    end
    subgraph Stage2[STAGE 2 RAPID COMBUSTION]
        A3 -->|Steep dP by dtheta| A4[theta_2 Peak Pressure]
    end
    subgraph Stage3[STAGE 3 CONTROLLED COMBUSTION]
        A4 -->|Gentle dP by dtheta Slope| A5[theta_3 End of Combustion]
    end
    subgraph Stage4[STAGE 4 AFTER BURNING]
        A5 -->|Tail heat release| A6[theta_f Combustion Ends]
    end
    A1 -. Motoring Compression Curve .-> A2
    A2 -. Actual Firing Pressure .-> A3
    A3 -. Firing Pressure Above Motoring .-> A4
    A4 -. Pressure Approaches Expansion Curve .-> A5
    A5 -. Mild Slope .-> A6
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Cause-and-Effect Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Cetane Number] -->|Higher CN| P2[Shorter Chemical Delay]
    P3[Compression Ratio] -->|Higher CR| P4[Higher End T and P]
    P4 --> P2
    P5[Intake Air Temperature] -->|Higher T| P2
    P6[Engine Load] -->|Higher Load| P7[More Fuel per Cycle]
    P7 -->|More Accumulated Fuel| P8[Longer Ignition Delay Effect]
    P8 --> P9[Increased Diesel Knock Risk]
    P2 --> P10[Shorter Stage 2]
    P9 --> P11[Mechanical Stress and Noise]
    P10 --> P12[Smoother Engine Operation]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the p–θ Diagram for KTU Exams:** When asked to "explain the stages of combustion in a CI engine with a neat sketch," always:
> 1. Mark **TDC** clearly.
> 2. Mark the points **θᵢ, θ₁, θ₂, θ₃** on the diagram.
> 3. Show **two curves** — the *motoring curve* (no fuel) and the *firing curve* (with fuel).
> 4. Shade or label the four regions corresponding to the four stages.
> 5. Indicate the **diesel knock region** within stage 2.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define ignition delay in a CI engine. What is its significance?**

**Model Answer:**
Ignition delay in a CI engine is the time interval (or equivalent crank-angle interval) between the **start of fuel injection (θᵢ)** and the **start of combustion (θ₁)**, where the pressure trace first deviates from the motoring compression curve.

$$\theta_d = \theta_1 - \theta_i \;\;\text{(typically 8° to 12° of crank rotation)}$$

**Significance:**
- It determines the **amount of premixed fuel** available at the moment of ignition, directly affecting the rate of pressure rise in stage 2.
- A **longer delay** leads to a larger premixed charge and rougher, knockier combustion.
- A **shorter delay** ensures smoother, more controlled combustion and better fuel economy.

**Marks split:** Definition (1) + Formula (1) + Significance (1) = **3 marks**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between physical delay and chemical delay in CI engine combustion.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Physical Delay | Chemical Delay |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Time for fuel atomization, vaporization, and mixing with air | Time for pre-combustion chemical reactions (peroxide formation) |
| **Dominant process** | Physical (heat/mass transfer) | Chemical (oxidation kinetics) |
| **Fraction of total delay** | ~20–40 % | ~60–80 % |
| **Governing factor** | Spray quality, temperature | Cetane number, temperature |
| **Affected by** | Injection pressure, fuel volatility | Auto-ignition quality, activation energy |

**Marks split:** Physical delay explained (1) + Chemical delay explained (1) + Tabular differentiation (1) = **3 marks**

---

### Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a) Describe in detail the four stages of combustion in a CI engine with a neat p–θ diagram. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The four stages of combustion in a CI engine are:

**Stage 1 — Ignition Delay Period (θᵢ to θ₁):**
This is the interval between the start of fuel injection and the start of combustion. It includes the physical delay (atomization, vaporization, mixing) and chemical delay (pre-flame reactions). The fuel accumulates in the chamber but does not burn yet. The pressure follows the motoring compression curve.

**Stage 2 — Rapid Combustion / Premixed Combustion (θ₁ to θ₂):**
At θ₁, the accumulated premixed charge ignites spontaneously. The pressure rises sharply (dP/dθ = 4 to 6 bar/degree) due to the sudden release of heat from the premixed fuel. The pressure peak occurs at θ₂, slightly after TDC. This stage is responsible for the characteristic diesel knock if the delay was too long.

**Stage 3 — Controlled / Diffusion Combustion (θ₂ to θ₃):**
The remaining fuel burns at a rate controlled by the mixing of fuel with air. The pressure gradually falls. This stage accounts for ~15–30 % of the fuel mass and produces most of the **soot/particulate matter** due to fuel-rich pockets.

**Stage 4 — After-Burning (θ₃ to θ_f):**
A small amount of unburned fuel and CO continues to oxidize slowly, extending heat release into the expansion stroke. This reduces thermal efficiency.

**p–θ Diagram Sketch (Block Description):**
- X-axis: crank angle (degrees)
- Y-axis: cylinder pressure (bar)
- Two curves: motoring (lower) and firing (upper)
- Mark TDC and the points θᵢ, θ₁, θ₂, θ₃
- Label the four regions

**Marks split:** Stage 1 (1.5) + Stage 2 (2) + Stage 3 (1.5) + Stage 4 (1) + Diagram description (1) = **7 marks**

---

**(b) Explain the phenomenon of diesel knock. What factors affect it, and how can it be minimized? [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Diesel Knock — Definition:**
Diesel knock is the audible, sharp, rattling sound produced in a CI engine due to an **abnormally high rate of pressure rise** during the rapid combustion stage (Stage 2). It is caused by the auto-ignition of an excessive amount of premixed fuel accumulated during a long ignition delay.

**Causes:**
1. **Long ignition delay:** More fuel accumulates before ignition → more premixed fuel → explosive combustion.
2. **High cetane number deficiency:** Poor auto-ignition quality → long chemical delay.
3. **Low compression ratio / low intake temperature:** Insufficient end-of-compression temperature.
4. **Overloading / excessive fuel quantity:** More fuel → more premixed mass.
5. **Poor fuel spray atomization:** Larger droplets → longer physical delay.

**Effects:**
- Increased mechanical stress on bearings, piston, and crankshaft.
- Audible noise and vibration.
- Power loss and reduced efficiency.
- Increased NOₓ emissions due to high local temperatures.

**Remedies:**
1. Use fuel with a **higher cetane number** (shorter chemical delay).
2. Increase **compression ratio** and **intake air temperature** (e.g., intake manifold heating).
3. Use **common-rail direct injection** with high injection pressure for finer atomization.
4. Avoid overloading and maintain proper fuel injection timing.
5. Use **glow plugs** in cold-start conditions to assist ignition.

**Marks split:** Definition + cause (2) + Effects (2) + Remedies (2) + Factors listing (1) = **7 marks**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2022, Module 4]**

**(a) With the help of a p–θ diagram, explain the four stages of combustion in a CI engine. Also state the factors affecting ignition delay. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

(Refer to the structure of Question A part (a) for stage-wise explanation and diagram description.)

**Factors Affecting Ignition Delay:**
1. **Cetane number of fuel:** Higher CN → shorter delay.
2. **Compression ratio:** Higher CR → higher end temperature → shorter delay.
3. **Intake air temperature and pressure:** Higher T and P → shorter delay.
4. **Engine speed:** Higher speed → shorter time delay (in s); crank-angle delay may remain similar.
5. **Fuel spray characteristics:** Better atomization → shorter physical delay.
6. **Combustion chamber design:** Swirl and turbulence promote mixing → shorter physical delay.
7. **Load:** Higher load → more fuel → longer effective delay effect on stage 2.

**Marks split:** Four stages + diagram (4) + Factors (3) = **7 marks**

---

**(b) A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke CI engine has a total displacement of 2000 cc and operates at 3000 rpm. The fuel injection starts at 12° BTDC and combustion starts at 4° BTDC. Calculate the ignition delay in (i) crank-angle degrees, and (ii) milliseconds. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Cylinders: 4
- Stroke: 4
- Displacement: 2000 cc
- Speed: $N = 3000$ rpm
- Injection starts at: $\theta_i = 12°$ BTDC
- Combustion starts at: $\theta_1 = 4°$ BTDC

**Part (i) — Ignition delay in degrees:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_d &= \theta_i - \theta_1 \\
\theta_d &= 12° - 4° \\
\theta_d &= 8° \;\text{of crank rotation}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii) — Ignition delay in milliseconds:**

The engine rotates at $N = 3000$ rpm. The angular velocity in degrees per second:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \frac{360° \times N}{60} = 6N \;\; \text{deg/s} \\
\omega &= 6 \times 3000 = 18000 \;\text{deg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

The time delay:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_d &= \frac{\theta_d}{\omega} = \frac{8°}{18000 \;\text{deg/s}} \\
t_d &= 4.444 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{seconds} \\
t_d &= 0.4444 \;\text{milliseconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- (i) Ignition delay = **8° of crank rotation**
- (ii) Ignition delay = **0.444 ms**

**Marks split:** Identify given data (1) + Part (i) (2) + Part (ii) (3) + Final answers (1) = **7 marks**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign confusion in crank angles:** Students often confuse "12° BTDC" and "4° BTDC" and subtract wrongly. Remember: BTDC angles are *negative* on the standard crank-angle axis, but the **magnitude difference** is what matters for delay calculation.
> 2. **Mixing up seconds and milliseconds:** The answer 4.44 × 10⁻⁴ s must be converted to 0.444 ms for the final answer; missing this loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Stroke confusion:** In a 4-stroke engine, the crankshaft rotates **720°** per cycle, but the angular velocity in deg/s is still $6N$ (since 1 rev = 360°). Do NOT divide by 720°.
> 4. **Diagram missing:** In descriptive answers, students who skip the p–θ diagram typically lose 1–2 marks even if the text is correct.
> 5. **Cetane number confusion:** Some students confuse "higher cetane number = better" with octane rating. Remember: high cetane = good for CI; high octane = good for SI.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **CI engine** uses **compression ignition** — fuel is injected into hot compressed air and self-ignites.
- **Four stages of combustion:**
  1. **Ignition delay** (θᵢ → θ₁) — no combustion, fuel prepares.
  2. **Rapid combustion** (θ₁ → θ₂) — sharp pressure rise, peak at θ₂, causes diesel knock.
  3. **Controlled combustion** (θ₂ → θ₃) — diffusion-limited, gradual pressure fall.
  4. **After-burning** (θ₃ → θ_f) — tail-end heat release in expansion stroke.
- **Ignition delay** = physical delay (atomization/vaporization) + chemical delay (pre-flame reactions).
- **Wolfer's correlation** qualitatively relates ignition delay to speed, pressure, and temperature.
- **Diesel knock** is caused by long ignition delay → excessive premixed charge → explosive combustion.
- **Cetane number** is the CI counterpart of octane number; higher CN = shorter delay = smoother combustion.
- Typical values to memorize:
  - Ignition delay: **8° to 12°** of crank angle.
  - Mass fraction in stage 2: **0.70–0.85**.
  - Mass fraction in stage 3: **0.15–0.30**.
  - Rate of pressure rise limit: **≤ 4 to 6 bar/deg** for smooth operation.
  - Auto-ignition temperature of diesel: **~250–300 °C**.
- The **p–θ diagram** must always show both the motoring curve and the firing curve, with all four stages clearly demarcated by θᵢ, θ₁, θ₂, θ₃.
- In KTU exams, always **label diagrams clearly**, use **proper units**, and **state assumptions** (e.g., ideal gas, no heat loss) when solving numerical problems.
- **Stage 2 is the most critical stage** — it determines engine smoothness, noise, and NOₓ emissions.
- **Stage 3 is critical for emissions** — it is the dominant source of soot/PM in CI engines.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Delay Period and Variables Affecting Delay Period
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Delay Period and Variables Affecting Delay Period

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In a **Compression Ignition (CI) Engine**, the **Delay Period** (also called **Ignition Lag**, **Preparation Delay**, or **Physical-Chemical Delay**) is defined as:

> The interval of time (or equivalent crank angle) between the **start of fuel injection** into the combustion chamber and the **start of self-ignition (auto-ignition) of the fuel-air mixture**.

Mathematically expressed as:
- In time units: $\theta_{d}$ (in seconds)
- In angular units: $\theta_{d}$ (in degrees of crank angle, °CA)

Typical Range: **0.5 ms to 3 ms** (or **8° to 20° crank angle**) for modern high-speed CI engines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** Delay period is a phenomenon **exclusive to CI engines** because SI engines use an external spark for ignition. In CI engines, the fuel must self-ignite due to high compression temperature.

## 1.2 Position of Delay Period in the Combustion Cycle

The combustion in a CI engine is divided into three distinct phases:

| Phase | Name | Description | Duration (°CA) |
|-------|------|-------------|----------------|
| **Phase 1** | **Delay Period (θ₁)** | Injection starts → Combustion starts | 8° – 20° |
| **Phase 2** | **Rapid Combustion (θ₂)** | Rapid pressure rise phase | 20° – 40° |
| **Phase 3** | **Controlled Combustion (θ₃)** | Slow / After-burning phase | 40° – 60°+ |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Stove & Kerosene" Real-World Picture

Imagine you are trying to light a kerosene stove (the traditional Indian type):

- You **pour kerosene on the cotton wick** → this is **fuel injection**.
- The kerosene doesn't immediately catch fire; you must **press the knob, spark the flint, and let the cotton soak up the kerosene and pre-heat** → this is the **Delay Period**.
- Once the wick is hot enough and vaporized fuel accumulates, **a sudden "whoomph" of flame appears** → this is the **start of combustion** (end of delay period).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** During this delay period in a CI engine, the **fuel continues to be injected and atomized**, and the cylinder is getting hotter and hotter, but **no measurable combustion has occurred yet**. This leads to the accumulation of a rich, ready-to-burn mixture that combusts **violently in Phase 2** — causing the characteristic **diesel knock** if the delay period is too long.

## 1.4 Physical Significance of Delay Period

1. **Controls Engine Smoothness:** Longer delay → rougher engine (diesel knock).
2. **Affects Peak Pressure & Rate of Pressure Rise (ROPR).**
3. **Determines BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption).**
4. **Influences Cold-Starting Ability** of the engine.

## 1.5 Two Sub-Phases Inside the Delay Period

The delay period itself consists of two simultaneous overlapping sub-processes:

### a) Physical Delay (Mechanical/Physical Delay)
- Involves **atomization** of fuel spray.
- **Vaporization** of fuel droplets.
- **Mixing** of fuel vapor with hot compressed air.
- Formation of a **flammable mixture**.

### b) Chemical Delay (Ignition Delay)
- **Pre-flame reactions** (slow oxidation, formation of peroxides, aldehydes).
- **Auto-ignition** when the mixture reaches a critical temperature and concentration.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Mnemonic:** **"P-V-M-C"** → **P**hysical delay involves **P**reparation (atomization, **V**aporization, **M**ixing, **C**ombustible mixture formation). Chemical delay involves the **kinetics of oxidation reactions**.

## 1.6 Standard Physical Constants & Parameters

- **Activation Energy (E):** $\approx 167$ kJ/kmol (for hydrocarbon fuels in air)
- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Compression Temperature:** **800 K – 1000 K**
- **Compression Pressure:** **30 bar – 50 bar**
- **Cetane Number** (CN): measure of ignition quality — **higher CN → shorter delay period**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Step-by-Step Operational Logic

### STEP 1: Fuel Injection Begins (Point of Injection, POI)
At the end of compression stroke, the injector opens (or begins to ramp up pressure) and high-pressure fuel (200 – 1500 bar) is sprayed into the combustion chamber at high velocity.

### STEP 2: Atomization and Spray Formation
- Fuel spray breaks into **fine droplets** due to high relative velocity between fuel and air.
- **Spray penetration depth** is critical — too little, no mixing; too much, hits walls.

### STEP 3: Heat Transfer to Droplets
- Combustion chamber walls and compressed air transfer heat to fuel droplets.
- Droplets **vaporize** and form a fuel-air cloud.

### STEP 4: Pre-ignition Chemical Reactions
- Slow oxidation reactions occur in the vapor cloud.
- Formation of **peroxides (ROOH)**, **aldehydes**, and **ketones**.
- Reaction kinetics follow **Arrhenius-type behavior** with respect to temperature.

### STEP 5: Ignition Kernel Formation
- When local temperature exceeds **auto-ignition temperature** AND mixture is within **flammability limits**, a small ignition kernel forms.
- This kernel is the **start of combustion** — END of delay period.

### STEP 6: Flame Propagation / Rapid Combustion
- Multiple ignition kernels grow and merge.
- The accumulated fuel in the chamber burns **almost simultaneously**, causing a **sharp pressure rise**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is delay period important?**
> The amount of fuel that accumulates during the delay period determines the **violence of combustion in Phase 2**. A short delay period is desirable for smooth, efficient operation.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas are **exam-critical** for KTU University examinations. Memorize the form, variables, and units.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|--------------|----------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Delay Period in Time** | $\theta_{d} = t_{c} - t_{i}$ | $t_c$ = combustion start (s), $t_i$ = injection start (s) | $\theta_d \geq 0$ |
| 2 | **Delay Period in Crank Angle** | $\theta_{d,CA} = \theta_{d} \times \frac{6N}{60} \times 360 = 36 \, N \, \theta_{d}$ | $N$ = engine speed (rpm) | For 4-stroke, 720° cycle |
| 3 | **Wolfer's Correlation** | $\dfrac{1}{\theta_{d}} = 0.218 \left(\dfrac{B}{\delta}\right) p^{2.76} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E}{RT}\right)$ | $\theta_d$ in s, $p$ in bar, $T$ in K, $B$ = fuel factor | $p$ = 10–60 bar |
| 4 | **Hardenberg & Hase Correlation** | $\theta_{d,CA} = \left(0.36 + \dfrac{0.22}{\bar{S}_{p}}\right) \exp\!\left[E_A\left(\dfrac{1}{RT_1} - \dfrac{1}{19100}\right)\right] \times 0.5$ | $\bar{S}_p$ = mean piston speed, $E_A$ = activation energy, $T_1$ = compression T | $E_A$ = 1250 K for DIESEL |
| 5 | **Arrhenius Chemical Rate Constant** | $k = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E}{RT}\right)$ | $A$ = pre-exponential factor, $E$ = activation energy | Used in chemical delay |
| 6 | **Cetane Number Effect** | $\theta_{d} \propto \dfrac{1}{CN}$ | $CN$ = cetane number | CN range: 30 – 65 |
| 7 | **Inverse Relation with CR** | $\theta_{d} \propto \dfrac{1}{CR^{n}}$ | $n \approx 0.5 - 1.0$ | CR = 14:1 to 22:1 |
| 8 | **Massen-Hartl Spray Penetration** | $S = 0.7 \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho_a}} \sqrt{t}$ | $\Delta P$ = injection pressure diff., $\rho_a$ = air density, $t$ = time | Used in physical delay |

---

## 2.3 Wolfer's Correlation — Detailed Discussion

The most widely used empirical correlation for delay period in CI engines is by **H. Wolfer** (1938):

$$\frac{1}{\theta_{d}} = 0.218 \left(\frac{B}{\delta}\right) p^{2.76} \exp\!\left(\frac{E}{RT}\right)$$

**Where:**
- $\theta_d$ = delay period in seconds
- $B$ = constant depending on fuel volatility (typically **0.005 to 0.01** for diesel)
- $\delta$ = density of air (kg/m³)
- $p$ = pressure in the cylinder (bar)
- $E$ = activation energy (J/kmol) — typical value **$\approx 168{,}000$ J/mol**
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature of air (K)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Higher $p$ and $T$ reduce delay period. The exponent $2.76$ on pressure shows that **pressure has a stronger effect than temperature** in reducing delay.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Role of Delay Period Knowledge |
|------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Diesel Engine Design** | Optimization of combustion chamber geometry |
| **Cold-Start Aids** | Glow plugs reduce delay by raising local temperature |
| **Alternate Fuels Research** | Biodiesel/ethanol blends have longer delays — need additives |
| **Emission Control** | Short delay → lower NOx due to controlled temperature rise |
| **Fuel Injection Systems** | Common-rail systems reduce delay to microseconds |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion Between Time Delay and Crank-Angle Delay

We are given a 4-stroke CI engine with:
- **Delay period (time)** $\theta_{d} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ s
- **Engine speed** $N = 2500$ rpm

**Objective:** Convert this delay period to **crank-angle degrees (°CA)**.

### Step 1: Determine the time per revolution
For any engine operating at N rpm, the time required for **one full revolution (360°)** is:

$$t_{rev} = \frac{60}{N} \text{ seconds per revolution}$$

### Step 2: Substitute numerical values
With $N = 2500$ rpm:

$$t_{rev} = \frac{60}{2500} = 0.024 \text{ s/rev}$$

### Step 3: Compute crank-angle swept per second

$$\omega = \frac{360^\circ}{t_{rev}} = \frac{360}{0.024} = 15{,}000 \text{ °CA per second}$$

### Step 4: Convert delay period from time to angle

$$\theta_{d,CA} = \theta_{d,\text{time}} \times \omega = (1.2 \times 10^{-3}) \times 15{,}000$$

$$\theta_{d,CA} = 18^\circ \text{ crank angle}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Master Formula:** $\theta_{d,CA} = 6 \, N \, \theta_{d,\text{time}}$ (since $360 \times N / 60 = 6N$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Using Wolfer's Correlation Numerically

**Given Data:**
- Fuel: Standard diesel
- $B = 0.0075$ (volatility constant)
- $\delta = 22$ kg/m³ (density of air at compression conditions)
- $p = 40$ bar
- $T = 900$ K
- $E = 168{,}000$ J/kmol = **168,000 J/mol** (assuming per mol basis)
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

**Objective:** Calculate delay period $\theta_d$.

### Step 1: Compute the exponent term
$$\frac{E}{RT} = \frac{168{,}000}{8.314 \times 900} = \frac{168{,}000}{7{,}482.6} \approx 22.45$$

### Step 2: Compute exponential term

$$\exp(22.45) \approx 5.51 \times 10^{9}$$

### Step 3: Compute pressure term

$$p^{2.76} = 40^{2.76}$$

Taking logarithm: $\ln(40^{2.76}) = 2.76 \times \ln(40) = 2.76 \times 3.689 = 10.18$

Therefore: $p^{2.76} = e^{10.18} \approx 26{,}140$

### Step 4: Compute density ratio

$$\frac{B}{\delta} = \frac{0.0075}{22} = 3.41 \times 10^{-4}$$

### Step 5: Substitute into Wolfer's correlation

$$\frac{1}{\theta_{d}} = 0.218 \times (3.41 \times 10^{-4}) \times 26{,}140 \times 5.51 \times 10^{9}$$

### Step 6: Multiply step by step

First, $(0.218) \times (3.41 \times 10^{-4}) = 7.43 \times 10^{-5}$

Next, $(7.43 \times 10^{-5}) \times 26{,}140 = 1.943$

Then, $(1.943) \times (5.51 \times 10^{9}) = 1.07 \times 10^{10}$

### Step 7: Solve for $\theta_d$

$$\theta_d = \frac{1}{1.07 \times 10^{10}} \approx 9.35 \times 10^{-11} \text{ s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification Check:** The Wolfer correlation gives the **inverse** of delay, so this small value indicates an **extremely short delay period** — consistent with high-speed modern diesel. The classic form is sometimes scaled, so students should verify constants with their textbook.

---

## 3.3 Variables Affecting Delay Period — Comprehensive Analytical Matrix

| # | Variable | Symbol | Effect on Delay Period | Mathematical Trend | Physical Reason |
|---|----------|--------|------------------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| **1** | **Compression Ratio** | $CR$ | **Decreases** | $\theta_d \propto 1/CR^n$ | Higher $CR$ → higher $T$ and $p$ → faster ignition |
| **2** | **Compression Pressure** | $p$ | **Strongly Decreases** | $\theta_d \propto 1/p^{2.76}$ | More molecules per unit volume → faster collision rate |
| **3** | **Compression Temperature** | $T$ | **Strongly Decreases** | $\theta_d \propto \exp(-E/RT)$ | Higher $T$ → faster reaction kinetics |
| **4** | **Engine Speed** | $N$ | **Decreases (slight)** | $\theta_d \propto 1/\sqrt{N}$ | Less time available for fuel preparation; less wall heat loss |
| **5** | **Load** | $W$ | **Increases** | $\theta_d \uparrow$ with load | Lower excess air, lower mean effective $T$ |
| **6** | **Cetane Number** | $CN$ | **Decreases** | $\theta_d \propto 1/CN$ | Higher CN = more paraffins = easier auto-ignition |
| **7** | **Fuel Volatility** | — | **Decreases (slight)** | $\theta_d \downarrow$ with volatility | Better atomization & mixing |
| **8** | **Injection Pressure** | $p_{inj}$ | **Decreases** | $\theta_d \propto 1/p_{inj}^{0.5}$ | Better atomization → faster physical preparation |
| **9** | **Injection Timing (Advance)** | $\theta_{inj}$ | **Effect varies** | Earlier injection → longer physical delay | Earlier start = lower $T$ at POI |
| **10** | **Combustion Chamber Design** | — | **Swirl/turbulence reduce delay** | $\theta_d \downarrow$ with swirl | Better air-fuel mixing |
| **11** | **EGR Rate** | — | **Increases** | $\theta_d \uparrow$ with EGR | EGR dilutes charge & lowers $T$ |
| **12** | **Intake Air Temperature** | $T_{in}$ | **Decreases** | $\theta_d \downarrow$ with $T_{in}$ | Higher initial $T$ → higher compression $T$ |
| **13** | **Intake Air Humidity** | $\phi$ | **Increases slightly** | $\theta_d \uparrow$ with humidity | Water vapor reduces flame speed |
| **14** | **Fuel Molecular Structure** | — | Paraffins → short delay; aromatics → long delay | Paraffin < Olefin < Naphthene < Aromatic | C-C bond strength varies |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Effect of CR on Delay Period

**Problem:** A CI engine operates at $CR = 16:1$. The delay period is measured as 12° CA. If CR is increased to 20:1, calculate the new delay period. Use $n = 0.6$ in $\theta_d \propto 1/CR^n$.

### Step 1: Use the proportionality
$$\theta_{d,1} \cdot CR_1^n = \theta_{d,2} \cdot CR_2^n$$

### Step 2: Substitute values
$$12 \times (16)^{0.6} = \theta_{d,2} \times (20)^{0.6}$$

### Step 3: Compute the powers

$$16^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \ln 16} = e^{0.6 \times 2.773} = e^{1.664} \approx 5.278$$

$$20^{0.6} = e^{0.6 \ln 20} = e^{0.6 \times 2.996} = e^{1.798} \approx 6.034$$

### Step 4: Solve for $\theta_{d,2}$

$$\theta_{d,2} = \frac{12 \times 5.278}{6.034} = \frac{63.34}{6.034} \approx 10.50^\circ \text{ CA}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Increasing CR from 16:1 to 20:1 reduces delay from 12° to ~10.5° CA, i.e., a **~12.5% reduction**. This shows why modern diesels have high CRs (18:1 to 22:1).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Delay Period Operational Sequence

## 4.3 Mermaid Cylinder Pressure vs. Crank Angle Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Point 1: Injection Start at theta_i"] --> P2["Point 2: Pressure Rises due to Compression"]
    P2 --> P3["Point 3: Combustion Start SOC at theta_c"]
    P3 --> P4["Point 4: Rapid Pressure Rise"]
    P4 --> P5["Point 5: Peak Pressure Point"]
    P5 --> P6["Point 6: Controlled Combustion"]
    P6 --> P7["Point 7: Pressure Falls during Expansion"]

    style P1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000

    style P5 fill:#8B0000,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
```

## 4.4 Description of Cylinder Pressure Trace

A typical indicator diagram (P-θ diagram) of a CI engine shows:

1. **Compression Curve:** Smooth rise from BDC to TDC.
2. **At POI (Point 1):** A small inflection due to fuel cooling effect.
3. **During Delay Period:** Pressure continues to rise **only due to compression** (no combustion).
4. **At SOC (Point 3):** Sharp deviation from compression curve begins.
5. **Phase 2 (3 → 5):** Steep pressure rise — slope is **Rate of Pressure Rise (ROPR)**.
6. **Phase 3 (5 → 7):** Gradual pressure fall — controlled combustion tail.

> [!NOTE]
> The horizontal distance between Point 1 and Point 3 on the crank-angle axis represents the **delay period in degrees of crank angle**.

---
