# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation I — Converting ppm (volumetric) to Brake-Specific Emission (g/kWh)

**Given:**
- Engine brake power: $P_b = 50 \text{ kW}$
- Fuel consumption: $\dot{m}_f = 12 \text{ kg/h}$
- CO concentration in dry exhaust: $c_{\text{CO}} = 1.5\% = 15000 \text{ ppm}$
- Molecular weight of dry exhaust: $M_{\text{exh}} = 28.97 \text{ kg/kmol}$
- Molecular weight of CO: $M_{\text{CO}} = 28 \text{ kg/kmol}$

**Find:** Brake-specific CO emission in g/kWh.

### Step 1 — Compute the exhaust mass flow rate (kg/h)

Assume the engine runs lean (CI). Approximate exhaust mass flow as:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{exh}} = \dot{m}_f + \dot{m}_{\text{air}} \approx \dot{m}_f \times (1 + \text{AFR})$$

For typical CI engine, AFR $\approx 18:1$ (mass basis). So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{air}} &= \dot{m}_f \times \text{AFR} = 12 \times 18 = 216 \text{ kg/h} \\
\dot{m}_{\text{exh}} &= 12 + 216 = 228 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert volumetric fraction to mass fraction

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{\text{CO}} &= c_{\text{CO}, \%} = 0.015 \\
x_{\text{CO}} &= y_{\text{CO}} \times \frac{M_{\text{CO}}}{M_{\text{exh}}} = 0.015 \times \frac{28}{28.97}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{\text{CO}} &= 0.015 \times 0.9665 = 0.014497 \quad \text{(mass fraction)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Mass flow rate of CO (kg/h)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{CO}} &= x_{\text{CO}} \times \dot{m}_{\text{exh}} = 0.014497 \times 228 \\
\dot{m}_{\text{CO}} &= 3.3053 \text{ kg/h} = 3305.3 \text{ g/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Brake-Specific Emission (g/kWh)

$$
\begin{aligned}
BS_{\text{CO}} &= \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{CO}} \times 3600}{P_b \times 1000} = \frac{3305.3 \times 3600}{50 \times 1000} \\
BS_{\text{CO}} &= \frac{11{,}899{,}080}{50{,}000} = 237.98 \text{ g/kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** Typical BS-VI limits for CO in CI engines are around 1.5 g/kWh. A raw value of 238 g/kWh is impossibly high — this confirms the exercise is teaching the **methodology** of conversion rather than representing a real engine. In practice, the CO % would be ~0.05% for a modern CI engine, not 1.5%.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Zeldovich NO Rate (Simplified First Reaction)

**Reaction:** $\text{O} + \text{N}_2 \xrightarrow{k_1} \text{NO} + \text{N}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d[\text{NO}]}{dt} &\approx 2 k_1 [\text{O}] [\text{N}_2] \\
\text{where} \quad k_1 &= A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R_u T)} \\
A &= 1.8 \times 10^8 \text{ m}^3/(\text{kmol·s}) \\
E_a &= 319{,}000 \text{ kJ/kmol} \\
R_u &= 8.314 \text{ kJ/(kmol·K)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Given:** $[\text{O}] = 0.001 \text{ kmol/m}^3$, $[\text{N}_2] = 7.5 \text{ kmol/m}^3$, $T = 2200 \text{ K}$.

### Step 1 — Calculate $E_a / (R_u T)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{E_a}{R_u T} &= \frac{319{,}000}{8.314 \times 2200} = \frac{319{,}000}{18{,}290.8} = 17.44
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Calculate $k_1$

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= 1.8 \times 10^8 \times e^{-17.44} = 1.8 \times 10^8 \times 2.74 \times 10^{-8} \\
k_1 &\approx 4.93 \text{ m}^3/(\text{kmol·s})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate the rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d[\text{NO}]}{dt} &= 2 \times 4.93 \times 0.001 \times 7.5 \\
\frac{d[\text{NO}]}{dt} &= 0.0739 \text{ kmol/(m}^3\text{·s)} \approx 7.39 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kmol/(m}^3\text{·s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to practical units (g/m³·s)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{r}_{\text{NO}} &= 0.0739 \times 30{,}000 = 2217 \text{ g/(m}^3\text{·s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a peak rate at the flame front. Integrating over the entire combustion duration (typically 2–5 ms in SI, slightly longer in CI) gives the *total* NO mass produced. The integration typically uses the "**Fenimore–Hanson NO correlation**" in real engine cycle simulations.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — SI vs CI Pollutant Comparison

| Pollutant | SI Engine | CI Engine | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CO** | **High** (especially at cold start / full load) | **Low** | CI burns lean; SI operates near stoich. |
| **HC** | **High** | **Low to Moderate** | SI has crevices + wall quenching; CI has high EGT to oxidise. |
| **NOx** | Moderate | **High** | CI has higher peak $T$ and $P$. |
| **PM (Soot)** | Low (PFI) / Moderate (GDI) | **High** | Heterogeneous mixture in CI → fuel-rich zones. |
| **CO₂** | Moderate | High (per kg fuel) | Diesel has higher C content. |
| **SOx** | Very low | Moderate | Diesel fuel has higher S content (now <10 ppm in BS-VI). |
| **Aldehydes** | Moderate (cold start) | Low | Rich mixture zones in SI. |
| **Lead (Pb)** | Historical (PFI petrol) | None | TEL was added to petrol only. |

---

## 3.4 Derivation III — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Diesel (C₁₂H₂₃)

**Combustion reaction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{23} + a(\text{O}_2 + 3.76 \text{N}_2) &\rightarrow 12 \text{CO}_2 + \frac{23}{2} \text{H}_2\text{O} + a \cdot 3.76 \text{N}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Balance C and H:**

Carbon: 12 C atoms → 12 CO₂
Hydrogen: 23 H atoms → 23/2 H₂O = 11.5 H₂O

**Step 2 — Balance O:**

O needed = $2 \times 12 + 11.5 = 24 + 11.5 = 35.5$ moles O
$a = 35.5 / 2 = 17.75$ mol O₂

**Step 3 — Calculate mass of O₂:**

$m_{\text{O}_2} = 17.75 \times 32 = 568 \text{ kg}$

**Step 4 — Calculate mass of air:**

$m_{\text{air}} = 17.75 \times (32 + 3.76 \times 28) = 17.75 \times (32 + 105.28) = 17.75 \times 137.28 = 2436.7 \text{ kg}$

**Step 5 — Mass of fuel:**

$m_f = 12 \times 12 + 23 \times 1 = 144 + 23 = 167 \text{ kg}$

**Step 6 — Stoichiometric AFR (mass basis):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} &= \frac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_f} = \frac{2436.7}{167} \approx 14.59
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Real diesel AFR at full load ≈ 18–22 (lean); at idle ≈ 25–30. Excess air helps suppress CO but raises NOx and PM trade-off concerns.

---

## 3.5 Derivation IV — Fuel Composition & Emission Impact

**Average emission per kg fuel burned** (for typical SI petrol C₈H₁₈):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 12.5(\text{O}_2 + 3.76 \text{N}_2) &\rightarrow 8 \text{CO}_2 + 9 \text{H}_2\text{O} \\
\text{CO}_2 \text{ produced} &= 8 \text{ mol} \times 44 \text{ g/mol} = 352 \text{ g CO}_2 \\
\text{Per kg fuel: } \quad 1000 \text{ g} / (114 \text{ g/mol C}_8\text{H}_{18}) &= 8.77 \text{ mol fuel} \\
\text{CO}_2 \text{ per kg fuel} &= 8.77 \times 352 = 3088 \text{ g} = 3.088 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kg fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In real engines, only ~25–35% of fuel's chemical energy becomes useful work, so the **practical CO₂ per kWh** of mechanical work is much higher. This is why BS-VI and CAFE norms mandate **CO₂ targets** for OEMs.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Pollutant Formation Pathways in SI & CI Engines

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Tank Supply] --> B[Air Intake and Mixing]
    B --> C{Engine Type}
    C --> D1[SI Engine: Homogeneous Charge]
    C --> D2[CI Engine: Heterogeneous Spray]

    D1 --> E1[Spark Ignition at TDC]
    D2 --> E2[Auto-Ignition via Compression]

    E1 --> F1[Flame Propagation]
    E2 --> F2[Premixed and Diffusion Phases]

    F1 --> G1[Combustion Products]
    F2 --> G2[Combustion Products]

    G1 --> H1[CO from Rich Zones]
    G1 --> H2[HC from Crevices and Wall Quenching]
    G1 --> H3[NOx from Flame Front Temperatures]
    G1 --> H4[CO2 from Complete Combustion]

    G2 --> H3
    G2 --> H5[PM Soot from Rich Spray Core]
    G2 --> H6[SOx from Fuel Sulphur]
    G2 --> H7[HC from Wall Wetting and Quench]
    G2 --> H4

    H1 --> I[Exhaust Manifold]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    H5 --> I
    H6 --> I
    H7 --> I

    I --> J1[Three Way Catalyst TWC for SI]
    I --> J2[DOC plus DPF plus SCR for CI]
    J1 --> K[Tailpipe to Atmosphere]
    J2 --> K
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — After-Treatment Mapping for Major Pollutants

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pollutant1[CO] --> remedy1[Oxidation Catalyst TWC or DOC converts to CO2]
    pollutant2[HC] --> remedy1
    pollutant3[NOx] --> remedy2[Three Way Catalyst or SCR or LNT]
    pollutant4[PM Soot] --> remedy3[DPF traps and regenerates]
    pollutant5[SOx] --> remedy4[Low Sulphur Fuel plus DOC]
    pollutant6[CO2] --> remedy5[Improved Efficiency and Biofuels]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Fuel Property vs Pollutant Trade-Off

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Increase Combustion Temperature] --> Q1[NOx Rises]
    P1 --> Q2[CO and HC Drop]
    P2[Increase Mixture Richness] --> Q3[CO Rises]
    P2 --> Q4[HC Rises]
    P2 --> Q5[NOx Drops at Very Rich]
    P3[Use Higher Sulphur Fuel] --> Q6[SOx and Sulphate PM Rise]
    P4[Increase Injection Pressure in CI] --> Q7[PM Soot Drops]
    P4 --> Q8[NOx may rise slightly]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Every pollutant control action in an engine involves a *trade-off*. Lower NOx by EGR → higher PM. Lower PM by retarded injection → higher fuel consumption and CO₂. The engine control unit (ECU) optimises within a regulatory envelope using **calibration maps**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (CO1, KTU University Exam – May 2024)
**Q:** List any **four** major pollutants from SI engines. Briefly explain the formation mechanism of **Carbon Monoxide** in SI engines.

**Model Answer:**

The four major pollutants from SI engines are:
1. **Carbon Monoxide (CO)**
2. **Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC)**
3. **Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx)**
4. **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)**

**Formation of CO in SI Engines:**
CO is formed due to **incomplete combustion** of carbon in the fuel. In fuel-rich regions where the local equivalence ratio $\phi > 1$, there is insufficient oxygen to fully oxidise carbon to CO₂. The reaction proceeds only as far as:

$$2\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{CO}$$

The further oxidation of CO to CO₂ requires additional oxygen and sufficient residence time at high temperature. In SI engines, especially during cold start, full load, and transient operation, the cylinder gas temperature drops rapidly during expansion, **kinetically freezing the CO** before it can be converted to CO₂. Hence, CO levels in SI engines are highest at **rich mixtures** and **low engine speeds**.

*(Word count target: 120–150 words for a 3-mark answer.)*

---

### Question 2 (CO1, KTU University Exam – Dec 2023)
**Q:** Differentiate between **Prompt NOx** and **Thermal NOx** in internal combustion engines.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Thermal NOx (Zeldovich) | Prompt NOx (Fenimore) |
|---|---|---|
| **Where it forms** | In the post-flame gases, behind the flame front. | In the flame front itself, via hydrocarbon radicals. |
| **Temperature sensitivity** | Extremely high sensitivity (Arrhenius $E_a \approx 319 \text{ kJ/mol}$). Significant above **1800 K**. | Mild temperature sensitivity. Forms even at moderate temperatures. |
| **Equivalence-ratio sensitivity** | Peaks near stoichiometric ($\phi \approx 1$). | Favours **fuel-rich** mixtures ($\phi > 1$). |
| **Magnitude in SI/CI** | Dominant NOx mechanism in **both** SI and CI engines. | Minor contributor (~5% of total) in most engines. |
| **Time-scale** | Forms in the post-flame region, slow kinetics. | Fast — completes within flame transit time. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "Prompt NOx is the *main* NOx" — this is **wrong**. Thermal NOx dominates in almost all practical engines. Prompt NOx is significant only in very rich mixtures like in heavily stratified gasoline or HCCI engines.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

### Question A — Module 4 Choice 1 (CO2, KTU University Exam – Dec 2023)

**Q (a)** Explain in detail the **mechanisms of formation of oxides of nitrogen (NOx)** in SI engines. Discuss the effect of equivalence ratio, peak temperature, and load on NOx formation. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **formation of soot (particulate matter)** in CI engines. Discuss how fuel properties and injection parameters affect soot formation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
NOx is a generic term for NO + NO₂. In SI engines, **~95% of NOx is NO**, and only ~5% is NO₂. The dominant formation mechanism in SI engines is the **Extended Zeldovich (Thermal) Mechanism**.

**2. Zeldovich Mechanism (2 marks):**

The three reversible reactions are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i)} \quad \text{O} + \text{N}_2 &\rightleftharpoons \text{NO} + \text{N} \\
\text{(ii)} \quad \text{N} + \text{O}_2 &\rightleftharpoons \text{NO} + \text{O} \\
\text{(iii)} \quad \text{N} + \text{OH} &\rightleftharpoons \text{NO} + \text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

- Reaction (i) is **rate-limiting** due to the high activation energy required to break the N≡N triple bond.
- The rate constant follows Arrhenius form: $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / R T}$.
- Significant NO formation requires $T > 1800 \text{ K}$.

**3. Effect of Equivalence Ratio (1 mark):**
- NOx is **maximum near stoichiometric** ($\phi \approx 1$).
- This is because at $\phi = 1$, both [O] and [N₂] are reasonably high.
- For lean mixtures ($\phi < 1$), temperature is lower → less NOx.
- For rich mixtures ($\phi > 1$), [O] is depleted → less NO.

**4. Effect of Peak Temperature (1 mark):**
- NOx has an **exponential sensitivity** to peak in-cylinder temperature.
- A 100 K rise in peak $T$ can double the NO formation rate.
- SI engine peak temperature is ~2400–2800 K in the unburnt gas ahead of the flame.

**5. Effect of Load (1 mark):**
- Higher load → more fuel → higher $T_{peak}$ → **more NOx**.
- Throttle closes at part load → less air mass → lower $T_{peak}$ → less NOx.
- Hence, **NOx peaks near full load**.

**6. Other Mechanisms (1 mark):**
- **Prompt NOx** (Fenimore): CH radicals attack N₂ → HCN → NO. Favours rich mixtures.
- **Fuel-bound NOx**: nitrogen in fuel; negligible for petrol/diesel.
- **N₂O pathway**: small contribution at moderate temperatures.

---

#### Model Solution for Q (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Soot Definition (0.5 marks):**
Particulate matter in CI engines is a complex mixture of elemental carbon (soot), soluble organic fraction (SOF), sulphates, and trace ash.

**2. Schematic of Soot Formation (1 mark):**

*(Use a flowchart: Fuel → Pyrolysis → PAH → Nucleation → Surface Growth → Coagulation → Oxidation → Exhaust PM)*

**3. Stages of Soot Formation (2.5 marks):**

**Stage 1 — Nucleation:**
In the rich, hot core of the fuel spray (inside individual droplets), heavy hydrocarbons undergo **pyrolysis and dehydrogenation** to form **Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAH)** — primarily pyrene and coronene. These PAH stack into the first solid nuclei (~1–2 nm).

**Stage 2 — Surface Growth:**
PAH and acetylene add to the surface of existing nuclei. Particles grow to ~10–30 nm spherical primary particles. This is the dominant mass-addition stage.

**Stage 3 — Coagulation / Agglomeration:**
Particles collide due to Brownian motion and form chain-like agglomerates (50–500 nm). The total number of particles decreases but total mass stays the same.

**Stage 4 — Oxidation:**
In the presence of O₂ and OH radicals, soot can be oxidised back to CO and CO₂. The final emission is the **net result** of formation and oxidation. Black smoke appears when oxidation is starved (insufficient O₂ or low local temperature).

**4. Effect of Fuel Properties (1.5 marks):**
- **Higher cetane number** → shorter ignition delay → less time for over-mixing → less soot.
- **Higher aromatic content** (e.g., naphthalene) → more soot precursors → more PM.
- **Higher fuel density / viscosity** → poor atomisation → larger droplets → rich zones → more soot.
- **Higher sulphur** → more sulphate PM.
- **Oxygenated fuels** (biodiesel, ethanol blends) → soot drops significantly.

**5. Effect of Injection Parameters (1.5 marks):**
- **Higher injection pressure** → finer atomisation → better air–fuel mixing → **less soot**.
- **Smaller nozzle hole diameter** → finer spray → less soot.
- **Optimised injection timing** → too retarded → fuel injected late → less time for combustion → more soot.
- **Multiple-pulse injection** (pilot + main) → premixed combustion → less soot, but more NOx.
- **Higher EGR** → lowers flame temperature → more soot.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently describe soot as just "unburnt carbon." It is **not** simply unburnt fuel — it is a complex **multi-stage solid-phase formation and oxidation** process. You must mention **PAH nucleation, surface growth, coagulation, and oxidation** for full marks. Also, students forget that **soot is not eliminated** — it is the *balance* of formation and oxidation.

---

### Question B — Module 4 Choice 2 (CO2, KTU University Exam – May 2024)

**Q (a)** Compare SI and CI engines with respect to their **major pollutant emissions**. Tabulate the typical emission levels (g/kWh) for CO, HC, NOx, and PM for both engine types. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** An SI engine operates at the following conditions:
- Brake power = 45 kW
- BSFC = 0.32 kg/kWh
- CO in dry exhaust = 0.6% by volume
- HC in dry exhaust = 200 ppm (as CH₁.₈₅)
- Molecular weight of dry exhaust = 29.0 kg/kmol
- A/F ratio = 14.5
- Molecular weight of fuel = 114 kg/kmol

**Calculate:**
(i) The mass of CO emitted per hour (kg/h).
(ii) The mass of HC emitted per hour (kg/h).
(iii) The brake-specific CO emission in g/kWh.
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q (a) — 7 Marks

**Tabulated Comparison (5 marks):**

| Pollutant | SI Engine (typical) | CI Engine (typical) | BS-VI Limit (CI heavy-duty) | Why Different? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CO** | **10 – 50 g/kWh** | **1 – 5 g/kWh** | 1.5 g/kWh | CI burns very lean; SI near stoich. |
| **HC** | **2 – 8 g/kWh** | **0.5 – 2 g/kWh** | 0.13 g/kWh | SI crevices + wall quenching; CI mostly oxidised. |
| **NOx** | **2 – 10 g/kWh** | **5 – 15 g/kWh** | 0.46 g/kWh | CI has higher peak $T$ and $P$. |
| **PM** | **0.05 – 0.1 g/kWh** (PFI) | **0.1 – 0.5 g/kWh** | 0.01 g/kWh | Heterogeneous mixture in CI. |
| **CO₂** | ~650 g/kWh | ~600 g/kWh | (CO₂ fleet norms) | Diesel is more efficient. |

**Key Reasons for Differences (2 marks):**
1. **SI** uses a **homogeneous, near-stoichiometric** mixture → rich regions give CO and HC; lower peak T limits NOx and PM.
2. **CI** uses a **heterogeneous, very lean** mixture → excellent oxidation of CO/HC, but fuel-rich spray cores form soot, and high peak T produces NOx.
3. **SI** has crevices, oil absorption, and wall quenching → higher HC.
4. **CI** operates at higher compression ratio → higher $P_{peak}$ and $T_{peak}$ → more NOx.

---

#### Model Solution for Q (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- $P_b = 45 \text{ kW}$
- $\text{BSFC} = 0.32 \text{ kg/kWh}$
- $c_{\text{CO}} = 0.6\%$
- $c_{\text{HC}} = 200 \text{ ppm} = 0.02\%$
- $M_{\text{exh}} = 29.0 \text{ kg/kmol}$
- $M_{\text{CO}} = 28 \text{ kg/kmol}$
- $M_{\text{HC}} = 13.85 \text{ kg/kmol}$ (as CH₁.₈₅)
- $\text{A/F} = 14.5$
- $M_f = 114 \text{ kg/kmol}$

### Step 1 — Fuel mass flow rate (kg/h)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_f &= \text{BSFC} \times P_b = 0.32 \times 45 = 14.4 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Air mass flow rate (kg/h)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{air}} &= \dot{m}_f \times \text{A/F} = 14.4 \times 14.5 = 208.8 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Exhaust mass flow rate (kg/h)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{exh}} &= \dot{m}_f + \dot{m}_{\text{air}} = 14.4 + 208.8 = 223.2 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (i) Mass of CO emitted per hour (kg/h) — 2 marks

**Convert volumetric fraction to mass fraction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{\text{CO}} &= y_{\text{CO}} \times \frac{M_{\text{CO}}}{M_{\text{exh}}} = 0.006 \times \frac{28}{29.0} \\
x_{\text{CO}} &= 0.006 \times 0.9655 = 0.005793
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mass flow of CO:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{CO}} &= x_{\text{CO}} \times \dot{m}_{\text{exh}} = 0.005793 \times 223.2 \\
\dot{m}_{\text{CO}} &= 1.293 \text{ kg/h} = 1293 \text{ g/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (ii) Mass of HC emitted per hour (kg/h) — 2 marks

**Convert 200 ppm to mass fraction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{\text{HC}} &= 200 \text{ ppm} = 200 \times 10^{-6} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \\
x_{\text{HC}} &= y_{\text{HC}} \times \frac{M_{\text{HC}}}{M_{\text{exh}}} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{13.85}{29.0} \\
x_{\text{HC}} &= 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.4776 = 9.55 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mass flow of HC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{HC}} &= x_{\text{HC}} \times \dot{m}_{\text{exh}} = 9.55 \times 10^{-5} \times 223.2 \\
\dot{m}_{\text{HC}} &= 0.02132 \text{ kg/h} = 21.32 \text{ g/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (iii) Brake-Specific CO emission (g/kWh) — 2 marks

$$
\begin{aligned}
BS_{\text{CO}} &= \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{CO}}}{P_b} = \frac{1293 \text{ g/h}}{45 \text{ kW}} = 28.73 \text{ g/kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Mark Allocation Summary
- Step 1 & 2: 1 mark
- Step 3: 1 mark
- Part (i) conversion + calculation: 2 marks
- Part (ii) conversion + calculation: 2 marks
- Part (iii) BSCO calculation: 1 mark

**Final Answers:**
- $\dot{m}_{\text{CO}} = 1.293 \text{ kg/h}$
- $\dot{m}_{\text{HC}} = 0.0213 \text{ kg/h}$
- $BS_{\text{CO}} = 28.73 \text{ g/kWh}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is to forget the **molecular weight correction** when converting volumetric to mass fractions. Students often write $\dot{m}_x = c \times \dot{m}_{\text{exh}}$ directly, which is **dimensionally wrong** (mixing volume fraction with mass flow). The correct procedure is the two-step: $y \to x$ using $M_x / M_{\text{exh}}$, *then* multiply by $\dot{m}_{\text{exh}}$. A second common error is to use $M_{\text{HC}} = 14$ (of CH₄) when the question explicitly says **CH₁.₈₅** — you must use $M_{\text{HC}} = 13.85$ as given.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

### A. Key Pollutants & Their Origin
- **CO** → Rich mixture, incomplete combustion, low $T$ post-flame (SI dominant).
- **HC** → Crevices, wall quenching, oil absorption (SI), wall wetting (CI).
- **NOx** → Extended Zeldovich (Thermal); peaks at high $T$, near stoich (CI dominant).
- **PM** → PAH → nucleation → surface growth → coagulation → oxidation (CI dominant; GDI-SI also affected).
- **CO₂** → Complete combustion; greenhouse gas.
- **SOx** → Fuel sulphur; nearly eliminated in BS-VI diesel.
- **Aldehydes & PAH** → Toxic, partial-oxidation byproducts.
- **Lead (Pb)** → From TEL; phased out in India.

### B. Critical Numerical Conversions
- **ppm ↔ %:** $1\% = 10{,}000 \text{ ppm}$.
- **ppm → mass flow:** $\dot{m}_x = c \times (M_x / M_{\text{exh}}) \times \dot{m}_{\text{exh}}$.
- **BSFC → fuel rate:** $\dot{m}_f = \text{BSFC} \times P_b$.
- **Exhaust mass flow:** $\dot{m}_{\text{exh}} = \dot{m}_f \times (1 + \text{A/F})$.
- **Brake-specific emission:** $BS_x = \dot{m}_x / P_b$ (with unit conversion).

### C. Fundamental Mechanisms
- **Zeldovich mechanism** — 3 reversible reactions; reaction (i) is rate-limiting.
- **Soot formation** — 4 stages; remember **nucleation → growth → coagulation → oxidation**.
- **Equivalence ratio ($\phi$)** — CO peaks at $\phi \approx 1.2$; NOx peaks at $\phi \approx 1.0$; PM peaks at $\phi > 2.0$ (in spray core).

### D. SI vs CI — Always Remember
- SI → **more CO, more HC**, less NOx, less PM.
- CI → **less CO, less HC**, more NOx, more PM, more CO₂ per kg of fuel.

### E. Engineering Control Strategies
- **EGR** (Exhaust Gas Recirculation) → reduces NOx, increases PM.
- **TWC** (Three-Way Catalyst) → for SI; reduces CO, HC, NOx simultaneously.
- **DOC + DPF + SCR** → for CI; oxidation, soot-trap, and NOx-reduction.
- **Retarded timing** → reduces NOx, increases HC, CO, BSFC.
- **Higher injection pressure** → reduces PM, may slightly increase NOx.
- **Lean-burn SI** → reduces CO, HC, NOx but increases heat-loss and unburnt HC.
- **Low-sulphur fuel** → reduces SOx and sulphate PM (mandatory in BS-VI).

### F. Common KTU Exam Traps
1. Confusing **ppm (vol)** with **mg/m³ (mass)** — they differ by $M_x / M_{\text{exh}}$.
2. Forgetting the **rate-limiting reaction** in Zeldovich mechanism.
3. Saying "soot = unburnt fuel" — *wrong*; it is a separate chemical pathway.
4. Treating **CO₂ as toxic** — it is a **greenhouse** gas, not directly toxic.
5. Using $M_{\text{HC}} = 14$ when the question specifies **CH₁.₈₅** ($M = 13.85$).
6. Forgetting that **BSFC × Power = Fuel Rate** in kg/h.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Exhaust Emissions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Exhaust Emissions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Exhaust Emissions** are the byproducts of incomplete or complete combustion of hydrocarbon fuels in internal combustion (IC) engines and combustion systems that are released into the atmosphere through the exhaust manifold. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, the measurement of exhaust emissions refers to the systematic quantification of regulated pollutants—primarily **Carbon Monoxide (CO), Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC), Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx), Carbon Dioxide (CO₂), Particulate Matter (PM), and Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)**—using standardized analytical instruments that comply with national and international regulatory frameworks such as **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)**, **Euro VI**, and **EPA Tier 4** norms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The focus of this module is on the *instrumentation and analytical techniques* used to quantify exhaust pollutants from SI and CI engines, NOT on combustion chemistry. Students must understand the working principle, sample handling, and limitations of each analyzer.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your kitchen gas stove. When the blue flame is perfectly adjusted, you get only **carbon dioxide and water vapor** (clean combustion). But if the flame turns yellow due to insufficient air, you produce **soot and carbon monoxide** (toxic, incomplete combustion). Now scale this up to a 100 kW diesel engine burning fuel 2000 times faster—you get a continuous stream of exhaust that must be **sampled, conditioned, and analyzed** in real time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Context:** A modern heavy-duty diesel truck emits roughly **2.6 g of NOx per km** and **0.01 g of PM per km** under Euro VI norms. Forty years ago, these same trucks emitted over **15× more pollutants**. The instruments that enabled this reduction are the same instruments you will study in this module.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The regulated emission limits are universally expressed in:
- **g/km** (for light-duty vehicles, used in European and Indian standards)
- **g/kWh** (for heavy-duty engines, used in BS-VI and EPA standards)
- **ppm (parts per million)** or **% by volume** (raw analyzer output)
- **Opacity in % (Hartridge scale: 0 to 100)** or **Bosch smoke number (0 to 10)**
- **FSN (Filter Smoke Number)** ranging from 0 (no smoke) to 10 (very dense black smoke)

The key physical constants relevant to emission analysis:
- **Molecular weight of air** = **28.97 g/mol**
- **Molar volume of an ideal gas at STP (0 °C, 1 atm)** = **22.414 L/mol**
- **Molecular weight of CO** = **28.01 g/mol**
- **Molecular weight of NO** = **30.01 g/mol**
- **Molecular weight of NO₂** = **46.01 g/mol**
- **Stoichiometric air–fuel ratio for gasoline** = **14.7:1**
- **Stoichiometric air–fuel ratio for diesel** = **14.5:1**
- **Speed of light (c)** = **3 × 10⁸ m/s** (used in NDIR detector geometry)

## 1.4 Classification of Exhaust Emissions

| Pollutant | Chemical Symbol | Toxicity/Impact | Typical Concentration |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon Monoxide | CO | Highly toxic (binds Hb) | 0.5–10 % vol (SI engines) |
| Unburnt Hydrocarbons | HC | Smog, carcinogenic | 50–1000 ppm |
| Oxides of Nitrogen | NOx | Acid rain, smog | 100–2000 ppm |
| Carbon Dioxide | CO₂ | Greenhouse gas | 5–15 % vol |
| Particulate Matter | PM | Respiratory disease | 0.01–0.5 g/m³ |
| Sulphur Dioxide | SO₂ | Acid rain | 10–200 ppm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** In CI (diesel) engines, **PM and NOx dominate**; in SI (petrol) engines, **CO and HC dominate**. This is why the analyzer selection differs—an SI engine test cell prioritizes a 5-gas bench analyzer, while a CI engine test cell prioritizes a smoke meter and a chemiluminescent NOx analyzer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework

The measurement of exhaust emissions is grounded in three physical principles:

1. **Absorption Spectroscopy** — molecules selectively absorb infrared (IR) or ultraviolet (UV) light at characteristic wavelengths.
2. **Flame Ionization** — hydrocarbons ionize in a hydrogen flame, producing a measurable current.
3. **Chemiluminescence** — NO reacts with ozone (O₃) to produce a chemiluminescent reaction that emits light proportional to NO concentration.
4. **Opacity/Extinction** — particulate-laden exhaust attenuates a light beam; the reduction in intensity is correlated to smoke density.
5. **Electrochemical / Paramagnetic Sensing** — O₂ measurement for combustion air–fuel ratio calculation.

The general sequence in a standard exhaust gas analysis is:
1. **Sample Extraction** — probe inserted into the tailpipe or exhaust duct.
2. **Sample Conditioning** — moisture removal, particulate filtration, temperature stabilization.
3. **Sample Distribution** — manifold sends gas to multiple analyzers in parallel.
4. **Detection** — physical/chemical interaction of gas with the detector.
5. **Signal Conditioning & Calibration** — analog-to-digital conversion, span gas calibration.

## 2.2 The Six Major Analyzers — Operational Principles

### 2.2.1 Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR) Analyzer — for CO, CO₂, HC

**Working Principle:** Each exhaust gas molecule (CO, CO₂, HC) has a unique infrared absorption fingerprint due to its vibrational–rotational energy levels. When IR light passes through a sample cell containing the exhaust gas, the specific wavelength absorbed is proportional to the gas concentration, following the **Beer–Lambert Law**.

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha(\nu) \cdot C \cdot L}$$

where:
- $I$ = transmitted light intensity
- $I_0$ = incident light intensity
- $\alpha(\nu)$ = absorption coefficient at frequency $\nu$
- $C$ = gas concentration
- $L$ = optical path length

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** NDIR analyzers use a **Luft detector** (a microflow detector with a sealed reference gas chamber) for high selectivity. The detector converts the differential pressure between the sample and reference chambers into an electrical signal. The two-beam optical design (sample vs. reference) cancels out common-mode noise and drift.

### 2.2.2 Flame Ionization Detector (FID) — for HC

**Working Principle:** A sample of exhaust gas is mixed with **hydrogen fuel** and **zero air**, then burned in a small jet flame. Carbon-containing compounds ionize in the flame, producing a current between two electrodes. The current is proportional to the number of carbon atoms oxidized per unit time.

$$I_{FID} \propto \sum_{i} n_i \cdot C_i$$

where $n_i$ is the number of carbon atoms in hydrocarbon species $i$, and $C_i$ is its concentration.

The FID response is **carbon-number normalized** — it is calibrated using **propane (C₃H₈)** as the span gas, and the result is reported as **ppmC (parts per million as carbon)**.

### 2.2.3 Chemiluminescent NOx Analyzer — for NO and NO₂

**Working Principle:** This is a **two-stage** analyzer:
- **Stage 1:** NO reacts with ozone (O₃) in a reaction chamber to produce NO₂ in an excited electronic state, which decays by emitting a photon in the 600–875 nm range (red/infrared):

$$NO + O_3 \rightarrow NO_2^* + O_2$$
$$NO_2^* \rightarrow NO_2 + h\nu \quad (\lambda \approx 600\text{–}875 \text{ nm})$$

- **Stage 2 (for NO₂):** A **molybdenum converter** at 350 °C reduces NO₂ to NO first, then the gas is sent to the same reaction chamber. The difference between Stage 1 and Stage 2 readings gives NO₂.

The emitted photon flux is measured by a **photomultiplier tube (PMT)**, and the signal is proportional to NO concentration.

### 2.2.4 Smoke Meters — Hartridge and Bosch

**Hartridge Smoke Meter (Opacimeter):**
- A light source and detector are placed on opposite sides of the exhaust duct.
- The exhaust stream flows through the optical path continuously.
- Opacity $N$ (%) is defined as the percentage of light absorbed:

$$N = \left(1 - \frac{I}{I_0}\right) \times 100\%$$

- Typical range: **0 to 100 %**; response time: **~0.4 s**.

**Bosch Smoke Meter:**
- A measured volume of exhaust is drawn through a **white filter paper**.
- The soot deposited stains the paper; its **reflectance** is measured photoelectrically.
- The Bosch smoke number $R_B$ is calculated as:

$$R_B = 10 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{R_s}{R_w}\right)$$

where $R_s$ is the reflectance of the stained paper and $R_w$ is the reflectance of the clean (white) paper.

### 2.2.5 Particulate Matter (PM) Measurement — Gravimetric Method

The most regulatory-accepted method is the **Constant Volume Sampling (CVS)** technique:
1. Exhaust is diluted with filtered ambient air in a dilution tunnel.
2. A sample is drawn through a **Teflon-coated filter paper** of known mass.
3. The filter is weighed after sampling under controlled humidity.
4. PM mass = (Final mass – Initial mass), reported in **mg/km** or **mg/kWh**.

### 2.2.6 Oxygen Analyzer — for AFR Calculation

A **paramagnetic oxygen sensor** exploits the high magnetic susceptibility of O₂ (it is the only common exhaust gas that is strongly paramagnetic). The differential pressure or force produced in a magnetic field is proportional to O₂ concentration. The exhaust O₂ measurement is critical for the **air–fuel ratio (AFR)** calculation:

$$AFR = \frac{\text{Actual air mass supplied}}{\text{Fuel mass supplied}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Excess Air Coefficient (λ):** Defined as the ratio of actual AFR to stoichiometric AFR.
> - $\lambda = 1$ → stoichiometric (chemically correct mixture)
> - $\lambda < 1$ → rich mixture (insufficient air)
> - $\lambda > 1$ → lean mixture (excess air)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $I = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha C L}$ | Beer–Lambert Law (NDIR) | $I$ in arbitrary units |
| $N = (1 - I/I_0) \times 100$ | Opacity (Hartridge) | % |
| $R_B = 10 \cdot (1 - R_s/R_w)$ | Bosch Smoke Number | dimensionless, 0–10 |
| $I_{FID} \propto \sum n_i C_i$ | FID response (carbon count) | amps, proportional to ppmC |
| $NO + O_3 \to NO_2^* + O_2$ | Chemiluminescent reaction (NO measurement) | emits $h\nu$ at 600–875 nm |
| $AFR = m_{air}/m_{fuel}$ | Air–Fuel Ratio | kg/kg |
| $\lambda = AFR_{actual}/AFR_{stoich}$ | Excess air coefficient | dimensionless |
| $V_{molar} = 22.414$ L/mol | Molar volume at STP | used in ppm ↔ mg/m³ conversion |
| $C_{mass} = C_{ppm} \cdot \frac{M \cdot P}{R \cdot T}$ | ppm to mass concentration | $M$ = molar mass |
| $BSFC = \dot{m}_f / P_{brake}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | kg/kWh |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Tip (ppm ↔ mg/m³):**
> $$C_{mg/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \frac{M_{gas}}{V_{molar}} \times \frac{P}{P_{std}} \times \frac{T_{std}}{T}$$
> This conversion is essential when emission regulations are quoted in different units.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These analyzers are used in:
- **Engine development test cells** (R&D centers of Tata Motors, Ashok Leyland, Mahindra).
- **Type-approval certification laboratories** (ARAI Pune, iCAT Manesar).
- **In-service vehicle inspection** (PUC — Pollution Under Control certificate centers).
- **On-board diagnostics (OBD-II)** — simplified versions of NDIR and electrochemical sensors are embedded in the vehicle ECU.
- **Industrial stack monitoring** for power plant and boiler compliance with **CPCB norms**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: NDIR Analyzer Sensitivity from Beer–Lambert Law

The Beer–Lambert Law relates the transmitted light intensity to gas concentration:

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha(\nu) \cdot C \cdot L}$$

**Step 1: Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\alpha(\nu) \cdot C \cdot L$$

**Step 2: Recognize that absorbance $A$ is defined as $\log_{10}(I_0/I)$.**

$$\frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-\alpha C L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \frac{\alpha C L}{2.303}$$

**Step 3: Define absorbance $A$.**

$$A = \alpha' \cdot C \cdot L$$

where $\alpha' = \alpha / 2.303$ is the decadic absorption coefficient (units: ppm⁻¹·m⁻¹ or atm⁻¹·cm⁻¹).

**Step 4: Solve for concentration $C$.**

$$C = \frac{A}{\alpha' \cdot L}$$

**Interpretation:** The minimum detectable concentration is limited by the **minimum detectable absorbance** $\Delta A_{min}$, which is set by detector noise. For a typical NDIR analyzer, $\Delta A_{min} \approx 10^{-4}$. With $\alpha' = 1.5$ atm⁻¹·cm⁻¹ at 4.6 µm (CO absorption band) and $L = 20$ cm, the **minimum detectable CO concentration** is:

$$C_{min} = \frac{10^{-4}}{1.5 \times 20} = 3.33 \times 10^{-6} \text{ atm} = 3.33 \text{ ppm}$$

This explains why NDIR analyzers are well-suited to ppm-level pollutant detection.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bosch Smoke Number from Reflectance Measurements

The Bosch smoke meter measures the reflectance of a soiled filter paper. The reflected intensity follows a linear relationship with soot density under the assumption of low optical thickness.

**Step 1: Define reflectance of clean paper (white reference).**

$$R_w = \frac{I_{clean}}{I_{incident}}$$

**Step 2: Define reflectance of soiled paper.**

$$R_s = \frac{I_{soiled}}{I_{incident}}$$

**Step 3: Define smoke number as the fractional loss in reflectance, scaled to a 0–10 scale.**

$$R_B = 10 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{R_s}{R_w}\right)$$

**Step 4: Verify endpoints.**

- When the paper is clean: $R_s = R_w$ → $R_B = 0$ ✓ (no smoke)
- When the paper is fully black: $R_s = 0$ → $R_B = 10$ ✓ (very dense smoke)

**Step 5: Convert Bosch smoke number to opacity (Hartridge) for cross-reference.**

Empirically established correlation (approximate):

$$N_{Hartridge} \approx 0.5 \cdot R_B + 5 \quad (\text{at low smoke levels})$$

This is an empirical linearization valid for $R_B < 5$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Conversion of ppm to mg/m³ for NOx

**Given:** A chemiluminescent analyzer reads $C_{ppm} = 800$ ppm of NO at exhaust conditions of $T = 450$ °C = 723 K and $P = 1$ atm.

**Step 1: Recall the ideal gas relation.**

$$PV = nRT \quad \Rightarrow \quad n = \frac{PV}{RT}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the molar volume at exhaust conditions.**

$$V_{molar} = \frac{RT}{P} = \frac{0.0821 \, \text{L·atm/mol·K} \times 723 \, \text{K}}{1 \, \text{atm}} = 59.36 \, \text{L/mol}$$

**Step 3: Calculate mass concentration from ppm.**

$$C_{mg/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \frac{M_{NO} \times 10^3}{V_{molar}}$$

where the $10^3$ factor converts L to m³, and we multiply by molar mass to get mass.

**Step 4: Substitute values. $M_{NO} = 30.01$ g/mol.**

$$C_{mg/m^3} = 800 \times \frac{30.01 \times 1000}{59.36 \times 1000} = 800 \times \frac{30.01}{59.36}$$

**Step 5: Evaluate.**

$$C_{mg/m^3} = 800 \times 0.5056 = 404.5 \, \text{mg/m}^3$$

**Step 6: Cross-check at STP ($T = 273$ K, $V_{molar} = 22.414$ L/mol).**

$$C_{mg/m^3, STP} = 800 \times \frac{30.01}{22.414} = 800 \times 1.339 = 1071.4 \, \text{mg/m}^3 \text{ (at STP)}$$

**Engineering Insight:** Regulators specify emission limits **at STP** (25 °C, 1 atm) to ensure comparability. Thus, raw analyzer readings at elevated exhaust temperature must be corrected to standard conditions before comparison with regulatory limits.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Excess Air Coefficient (λ) from Exhaust O₂ Measurement

**Given:** An SI engine operates with the following dry exhaust composition (volumetric): **O₂ = 2.5 %, CO₂ = 12 %, CO = 1 %, HC = 1000 ppmC, N₂ = 84 % (balance)**. Determine λ.

**Step 1: Apply a carbon balance for stoichiometric reference.**

For a fuel approximated as C₈H₁₇ (octane), the stoichiometric combustion reaction is:

$$C_8H_{17} + 12 \, O_2 \rightarrow 8 \, CO_2 + 8.5 \, H_2O$$

The stoichiometric O₂ requirement is 12 mol per mol of fuel.

**Step 2: Write the actual (lean) combustion with excess air factor $\lambda$.**

$$C_8H_{17} + 12\lambda \, (O_2 + 3.76 \, N_2) \rightarrow 8 \, CO_2 + 8.5 \, H_2O + 12(\lambda-1) \, O_2 + 12\lambda \times 3.76 \, N_2$$

**Step 3: Calculate the dry exhaust O₂ mole fraction.**

After removing water, the dry products are: $8 \, CO_2 + 12(\lambda-1) \, O_2 + 12\lambda \times 3.76 \, N_2$

$$X_{O_2, dry} = \frac{12(\lambda-1)}{8 + 12(\lambda-1) + 45.12\lambda}$$

**Step 4: Set $X_{O_2, dry} = 0.025$ and solve for $\lambda$.**

$$0.025 = \frac{12(\lambda-1)}{8 + 12\lambda - 12 + 45.12\lambda}$$

$$0.025 \times (57.12\lambda - 4) = 12(\lambda-1)$$

$$1.428\lambda - 0.1 = 12\lambda - 12$$

$$-0.1 + 12 = 12\lambda - 1.428\lambda$$

$$11.9 = 10.572\lambda$$

$$\lambda = \frac{11.9}{10.572} = 1.125$$

**Step 5: Compute actual AFR.**

$$AFR_{stoich} = 14.7 \quad \Rightarrow \quad AFR_{actual} = 1.125 \times 14.7 = 16.54$$

**Result:** The engine is operating at a **lean mixture** of 16.54:1, with 12.5% excess air.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Emission vs. Air–Fuel Ratio (SI Engine)

| AFR | $\lambda$ | CO (%) | HC (ppm) | NOx (ppm) | CO₂ (%) | Combustion Quality |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 9:1 (very rich) | 0.61 | 7.0 | 1200 | 200 | 8.0 | Incomplete, smoky |
| 12:1 (rich) | 0.82 | 4.5 | 700 | 800 | 11.5 | High power, high CO |
| 14.7:1 (stoich) | 1.00 | 0.5 | 250 | 1500 | 13.5 | Three-way catalyst efficient |
| 16:1 (lean) | 1.09 | 0.2 | 200 | 1100 | 13.0 | Lean burn, lower NOx |
| 18:1 (very lean) | 1.22 | 0.1 | 250 | 600 | 12.0 | Misfire risk |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three-Way Catalyst (TWC) Operating Window:** A TWC requires $\lambda = 1.00 \pm 0.01$ to simultaneously reduce CO, HC, and NOx by >95%. The lambda sensor (oxygen sensor) in the exhaust manifold provides closed-loop feedback to the ECU to maintain this tight window.

## 3.6 Comparison Matrix of Analyzers

| Analyzer | Target Gas | Principle | Range | Response Time | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| NDIR | CO, CO₂, HC | IR absorption | 0–10% / 0–5000 ppm | 1–2 s | **Very High** |
| FID | HC (total) | Flame ionization | 0–10000 ppmC | < 1 s | **Very High** |
| Chemiluminescent | NO, NO₂, NOx | O₃ reaction + PMT | 0–10000 ppm | 2–5 s | **High** |
| Hartridge | Smoke | Light extinction | 0–100% opacity | 0.4 s | **High** |
| Bosch | Smoke | Filter reflectance | 0–10 BSU | 30 s/pump | **Medium** |
| Paramagnetic | O₂ | Magnetic susceptibility | 0–25% | 3 s | **Medium** |
| Electrochemical | O₂, NOx | Redox cell | 0–5000 ppm | 5–15 s | **Low** |
| FID + Methanizer | HC (speciated) | FID post-conversion | sub-ppb | 2 s | **Low** |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Exhaust Gas Analysis Train

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Under Test] --> B[Exhaust Manifold]
    B --> C[Sample Probe with Filter]
    C --> D[Sample Conditioning Unit]
    D --> E[Water Removal - Chiller]
    E --> F[Particulate Filter - 5 micron]
    F --> G[Distribution Manifold]
    G --> H1[NDIR Analyzer - CO and CO2]
    G --> H2[FID - Total HC]
    G --> H3[Chemiluminescent - NOx]
    G --> H4[Paramagnetic - O2]
    G --> H5[Hartridge Smoke Meter]
    H1 --> I1[Signal Conditioner]
    H2 --> I2[Signal Conditioner]
    H3 --> I3[Signal Conditioner]
    H4 --> I4[Signal Conditioner]
    H5 --> I5[Signal Conditioner]
    I1 --> J[Data Acquisition System - DAS]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    I4 --> J
    I5 --> J
    J --> K[Display and Logging PC]
    J --> L[Calibration Span Gas Inputs]
```

## 4.2 Working Schematic — NDIR Analyzer (Luft Detector)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Src[IR Source - Heated Filament] --> M1[Chopper Motor]
    M1 --> SC[Sample Cell - Gas Inlet and Outlet]
    M1 --> RC[Reference Cell - Sealed N2]
    SC --> DF[Detector Front Chamber - CO Filled]
    RC --> DB[Detector Back Chamber - CO Filled]
    DF --> CM[Capacitive Microphone Membrane]
    DB --> CM
    CM --> EA[Electronic Amplifier]
    EA --> DSP[Signal Processing and Linearization]
    DSP --> OUT[Concentration Output - ppm]
    SP[Span Gas Calibration Input] --> DSP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanation of Operation:** The chopper alternately directs IR light through the sample and reference cells. The CO in the detector chambers absorbs IR at 4.6 µm. When the gas in the sample cell also contains CO, less light reaches the front chamber, creating a pressure differential that flexes the capacitive membrane. This AC signal is amplified and linearized to give CO concentration.

## 4.3 Process Flow — Chemiluminescent NOx Analyzer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Sample Inlet] --> V1[Three Way Valve]
    V1 --> MFC1[Molybdenum Converter at 350C]
    V1 --> DC1[Direct Path - NO only]
    MFC1 --> RC[Reaction Chamber with O3 Generator]
    DC1 --> RC
    RC --> PMT[Photomultiplier Tube]
    PMT[Photomultiplier Tube] --> AMPL[Current Amplifier]
    AMPL --> COMP[Signal Comparator]
    COMP --> CALC[NOx minus NO equals NO2]
    CALC --> DISP[Display - NO, NO2, NOx]
    O2[O2 Supply] --> OZG[Ozone Generator]
    OZG --> RC
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Smoke Measurement Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    EX[Exhaust Pipe] --> PROBE[Opacimeter Probe Pair]
    LS[Light Source - LED or Tungsten] --> LEN1[Collimating Lens]
    LEN1 --> PROBE
    PROBE --> LEN2[Focusing Lens]
    LEN2 --> PD[Photodiode Detector]
    PD --> AMP[Transimpedance Amplifier]
    AMP --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> DSP[Microcontroller - Calibration and Linearization]
    DSP --> LCD[LCD Display - Percent Opacity]
    DSP --> UART[Serial Output to Data Logger]
    REFL[Bosch Filter Paper In] --> SUBS[Bosch Reflectance Sensor]
    SUBS --> ADC2[ADC Channel 2]
    ADC2 --> DSP
    DSP --> CALC[R_B equals 10 times 1 minus R_s over R_w]
    CALC --> BSM[Bosch Smoke Number Display]
```

## 4.5 Decision Flow — Selecting the Right Analyzer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Engine Type?}
    Q1 -->|SI - Petrol| P1{Application?}
    Q1 -->|CI - Diesel| P2{Application?}
    P1 -->|Type Approval| P3[5-Gas Bench: NDIR for CO, CO2, HC, FID for HC, Chemiluminescent for NOx, Paramagnetic for O2]
    P1 -->|PUC Center| P4[NDIR for CO and HC, Electrochemical for O2]
    P2 -->|Type Approval| P5[NDIR for CO and CO2, FID for HC, Chemiluminescent for NOx, CVS for PM, Hartridge for Opacity]
    P2 -->|In-Use Compliance| P6[Hartridge Opacimeter only]
    P5 --> P7[BS-VI or Euro VI Compliant]
    P6 --> P7
    P3 --> P7
    P4 --> P7
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the working of a Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR) analyzer for the measurement of CO in exhaust gases.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The NDIR analyzer works on the principle of **infrared absorption by specific gas molecules**. The key components are:
1. **IR source** — a heated filament that emits broadband infrared light.
2. **Chopper wheel** — modulates the IR beam into a pulsed signal.
3. **Sample cell** — the exhaust gas flows through this cell, where CO molecules absorb IR radiation at **4.6 µm** (a characteristic absorption band of CO).
4. **Reference cell** — sealed with N₂ (non-absorbing) to provide a baseline.
5. **Luft detector** — a two-chamber detector filled with CO, fitted with a capacitive microphone that senses the pressure differential caused by differential IR absorption.
6. **Amplifier & display** — converts the detector signal into CO concentration in ppm.

The concentration is calculated from the **Beer–Lambert Law**: $C = A / (\alpha' \cdot L)$. The dual-beam (sample vs. reference) design cancels out source intensity fluctuations, giving high accuracy (± 1% of full scale).

> [!TIP]
> **Key Marks Distributor:** Naming the IR source (½), sample cell (½), detector (½), and Beer–Lambert Law (1 mark) typically fetches full marks. Missing the wavelength (4.6 µm) costs ½ mark.

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the various exhaust emission measuring instruments used in IC engines. State the principle of measurement for any two.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The various exhaust emission measuring instruments are:
1. **NDIR analyzer** — for CO, CO₂, HC (IR absorption).
2. **Flame Ionization Detector (FID)** — for total HC (flame ionization).
3. **Chemiluminescent analyzer** — for NO and NO₂ (ozone reaction with photomultiplier detection).
4. **Hartridge smoke meter** — for smoke opacity (light extinction).
5. **Bosch smoke meter** — for smoke (filter paper reflectance).
6. **Paramagnetic oxygen analyzer** — for O₂ (magnetic susceptibility).
7. **Particulate sampler (CVS)** — for PM (gravimetric).
8. **Electrochemical sensor** — for O₂ in PUC centers (redox cell).

**Two principles:**

**NDIR (CO measurement):** The exhaust gas is passed through an infrared beam. CO molecules absorb IR at 4.6 µm. The reduction in transmitted light intensity is proportional to CO concentration, following the Beer–Lambert Law. A Luft detector converts the pressure differential (caused by selective IR absorption) into an electrical signal.

**Chemiluminescent (NOx):** NO in the sample reacts with ozone in a reaction chamber:
$$NO + O_3 \rightarrow NO_2^* + O_2$$
The excited NO₂* emits photons in the 600–875 nm range. A photomultiplier tube measures the photon flux, which is proportional to NO concentration. NO₂ is measured separately by reducing it to NO in a molybdenum converter.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners expect you to list at least 4–5 instruments (1 mark) and explain principles of any 2 in detail (1 mark each). Always state the **specific wavelength** or **chemical equation** for full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) (7 marks)** With a neat schematic, explain the working of a Flame Ionization Detector (FID) used for measurement of unburnt hydrocarbons in IC engine exhaust. Discuss its advantages and limitations.

**(b) (7 marks)** A chemiluminescent NOx analyzer gives a reading of 850 ppm NO (at exhaust conditions of 200 °C and 1 atm). The exhaust also contains 120 ppm NO₂. Calculate the total NOx concentration in mg/m³ at STP (0 °C, 1 atm). The molar mass of NO = 30.01 g/mol and NO₂ = 46.01 g/mol.

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — FID (7 marks)**

**Working Principle:** The FID is a **carbon-counting** detector. Exhaust sample is mixed with a **hydrogen carrier gas** and **zero air** (purified air free of hydrocarbons), and burned in a small jet flame. The hydrogen flame ionizes the carbon atoms in the hydrocarbon molecules:

$$C_nH_m \xrightarrow{H_2 \text{ flame, air}} CO_2 + H_2O + e^- + \text{ions}$$

A pair of **polarizing electrodes** (one in the flame, one as a collector) collects the ions, producing a current proportional to the **total number of carbon atoms** ionized per second.

**Schematic (textual):**
- Sample inlet → Mixing chamber (H₂ + zero air) → Burner jet → Hydrogen flame → Collector electrode (positively charged) → Polarizer electrode (in flame) → Picoammeter → Amplifier → Display
- Make-up gas (N₂ or H₂) maintains flame stability.

**Calibration:** The FID is calibrated using **propane (C₃H₈) in N₂ span gas**. The **Effective Carbon Number (ECN)** is used to convert ppmC of propane to ppmC of the measured HC:

$$C_{sample} = \frac{ECN_{propane} \times C_{span}}{ECN_{sample}}$$

**Advantages (3 marks worth of content):**
1. **Carbon-number normalized** response — linear over a wide range (sub-ppb to 10,000 ppm).
2. **Fast response time** (< 1 second) — suitable for transient cycle testing.
3. **High sensitivity** — detection limit down to **1 ppbC** for methane.
4. **Universal response** to all hydrocarbons, including methane (which NDIR cannot detect).
5. **Non-destructive** to the sample (water and CO₂ are also non-interfering).

**Limitations:**
1. **No response to non-hydrocarbons** — cannot detect CO, CO₂, NOx.
2. **Requires fuel gases** — H₂ and zero air must be supplied continuously.
3. **Cannot speciate** — gives total HC only, not individual components (e.g., benzene vs. toluene).
4. **Saturated response** at very high HC concentrations (> 1% by volume).
5. **Condensation in sample line** can cause loss of heavy HC fractions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Note:** A clean schematic is worth 2 marks, working principle 2 marks, advantages 1.5 marks, limitations 1.5 marks.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $C_{NO}$ = 850 ppm (at 200 °C = 473 K, 1 atm)
- $C_{NO_2}$ = 120 ppm (at 200 °C = 473 K, 1 atm)
- Convert to mg/m³ at STP: $T_{STP} = 273$ K, $P = 1$ atm
- $M_{NO} = 30.01$ g/mol, $M_{NO_2} = 46.01$ g/mol

**Step 1: Calculate molar volume at exhaust conditions.**

$$V_{exhaust} = \frac{RT_{exhaust}}{P} = \frac{0.0821 \times 473}{1} = 38.83 \, \text{L/mol}$$

**Step 2: Convert ppm to mg/m³ at exhaust conditions.**

For NO:
$$C_{NO, exhaust} = 850 \times \frac{30.01}{38.83 \times 10^{-3}} \times 10^{-3} = 850 \times 0.7729 = 657.0 \, \text{mg/m}^3$$

For NO₂:
$$C_{NO_2, exhaust} = 120 \times \frac{46.01}{38.83 \times 10^{-3}} \times 10^{-3} = 120 \times 1.1849 = 142.2 \, \text{mg/m}^3$$

**Step 3: Apply the ideal gas correction to STP.**

Using the combined gas law: $\frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$ and mass concentration scaling:

$$C_{STP} = C_{exhaust} \times \frac{T_{exhaust}}{T_{STP}} = C_{exhaust} \times \frac{473}{273}$$

For NO at STP:
$$C_{NO, STP} = 657.0 \times \frac{473}{273} = 657.0 \times 1.7326 = 1138.3 \, \text{mg/m}^3$$

For NO₂ at STP:
$$C_{NO_2, STP} = 142.2 \times \frac{473}{273} = 142.2 \times 1.7326 = 246.4 \, \text{mg/m}^3$$

**Step 4: Calculate total NOx (expressed as NO₂ equivalent, per regulatory convention).**

Regulatory bodies often report NOx as NO₂ equivalent mass:
$$C_{NOx} = C_{NO} \times \frac{M_{NO_2}}{M_{NO}} + C_{NO_2}$$

$$C_{NOx} = 1138.3 \times \frac{46.01}{30.01} + 246.4$$

$$C_{NOx} = 1138.3 \times 1.5332 + 246.4 = 1745.4 + 246.4 = 1991.8 \, \text{mg/m}^3$$

**Final Answer (as NO₂ equivalent at STP):** $C_{NOx} \approx 1991.8 \, \text{mg/m}^3 \approx 1.99 \, \text{g/m}^3$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes:** (1) Forgetting to convert from exhaust to STP conditions (loses 2 marks). (2) Reporting NOx as a simple sum of NO and NO₂ concentrations in ppm rather than the regulatory NO₂-equivalent mass. (3) Using the wrong molar volume (22.4 L/mol is for STP, not exhaust conditions). (4) Mixing up the sign of the temperature ratio.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) (7 marks)** Describe the working principle of a chemiluminescent NOx analyzer with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of the molybdenum converter and the photomultiplier tube.

**(b) (7 marks)** With a sketch, explain the working of a Hartridge smoke meter. A Hartridge smoke meter reads an opacity of 35% for a diesel engine. The transmittance reading on the photodiode is 2.1 V. What is the incident light intensity (in volts), assuming linear photodiode response?

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Chemiluminescent NOx Analyzer (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram (textual representation, refer to Section 4.3):**
1. Sample inlet → 3-way solenoid valve → (Path A: direct) OR (Path B: molybdenum converter at 350 °C) → Reaction chamber (where O₃ is generated from O₂ via corona discharge) → Optical filter (600–875 nm bandpass) → Photomultiplier tube (PMT) → Current-to-voltage amplifier → A/D converter → Microprocessor → Display.
2. O₂ supply feeds the **ozone generator** continuously.
3. Span gas (NO in N₂) is used for daily calibration.

**Working Principle:**

The analyzer operates in **two stages**:

**Stage 1 — NO measurement (direct path):**
The sample gas bypasses the converter and enters the reaction chamber, where it mixes with ozone:
$$NO + O_3 \rightarrow NO_2^*(excited) + O_2$$
$$NO_2^* \rightarrow NO_2(ground) + h\nu \quad (\lambda \approx 600\text{–}875 \text{ nm})$$

The emitted photons pass through a bandpass filter and strike the photocathode of the PMT. The PMT multiplies the photoelectrons via a cascade of dynodes, producing a measurable anode current proportional to NO concentration.

**Stage 2 — NO₂ measurement (converter path):**
The 3-way valve now directs the sample through the **molybdenum converter** at 350 °C, which reduces NO₂ to NO via:
$$3 \, NO_2 + Mo \xrightarrow{350°C} MoO_3 + 3 \, NO$$
The converted gas enters the same reaction chamber, and the PMT now measures the sum (NO + reduced NO₂ = NOx). The difference gives NO₂.

**Role of Molybdenum Converter (1.5 marks):**
- Catalytically reduces NO₂ to NO at 350 °C with >95% conversion efficiency.
- Allows sequential measurement of NO and NOx in the same reaction chamber.
- Eliminates the need for a second PMT, reducing cost and complexity.

**Role of Photomultiplier Tube (1.5 marks):**
- Detects single photons from the chemiluminescent reaction.
- Internal electron multiplication (typically 10⁶ gain) allows detection of very low photon fluxes.
- Provides the sensitivity needed for ppm-level NO measurement.
- Operates in photon-counting mode for very low concentrations (< 1 ppm).

**Advantages:**
1. Very high sensitivity (sub-ppb NO detection).
2. Highly specific to NO (no cross-interference from CO₂ or H₂O).
3. Real-time measurement (response time < 2 s).

**Limitations:**
1. Requires high-purity O₂ and ozone generator maintenance.
2. Molybdenum converter degrades over time and must be replaced periodically.
3. Cannot directly measure NO₂ without the converter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write that the PMT "amplifies light" — it actually amplifies **photoelectrons** via secondary emission at the dynodes. Using the term "electron multiplication" or "dynode cascade" fetches full marks.

**Part (b) — Hartridge Smoke Meter & Numerical (7 marks)**

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
The Hartridge smoke meter is a real-time, in-stream opacimeter. It measures the **reduction in light intensity** as a collimated light beam passes through the exhaust stream.

**Construction (textual sketch, refer to Section 4.4):**
- A light source (typically a high-intensity LED or tungsten bulb) is mounted on one side of the exhaust pipe.
- A **collimating lens** focuses the light into a parallel beam.
- A photodiode (or photomultiplier for high accuracy) is mounted on the opposite side.
- The exhaust stream flows continuously through the optical path between the two units.
- A reference path with a known neutral density filter is used for span calibration.

**Working:**
1. The light source emits light of intensity $I_0$.
2. As the light passes through the soot-laden exhaust, soot particles scatter and absorb the light.
3. The transmitted intensity $I$ at the photodiode is reduced.
4. **Opacity $N$ (%)** is defined as:

$$N = \left(1 - \frac{I}{I_0}\right) \times 100\%$$

5. Typical range: 0% (clean air) to 100% (opaque exhaust). Response time: ~0.4 s.
6. Advantages over Bosch smoke meter: real-time continuous reading; can resolve individual strokes of the engine.

**Numerical Solution (3 marks):**

**Given:** Opacity $N = 35\%$, transmitted photodiode voltage $V_T = 2.1$ V (proportional to $I$).

**Step 1: Use the opacity definition.**

$$N = \left(1 - \frac{I}{I_0}\right) \times 100$$

$$35 = \left(1 - \frac{2.1}{V_0}\right) \times 100$$

where $V_0$ is the incident photodiode voltage (proportional to $I_0$).

**Step 2: Solve for $V_0$.**

$$0.35 = 1 - \frac{2.1}{V_0}$$

$$\frac{2.1}{V_0} = 1 - 0.35 = 0.65$$

$$V_0 = \frac{2.1}{0.65} = 3.231 \, \text{V}$$

**Final Answer:** The incident light intensity (in voltage equivalent) is **$V_0 \approx 3.23$ V**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Errors:** (1) Confusing transmittance $I/I_0$ with absorbance. (2) Using the Bosch smoke number formula for a Hartridge problem (lose 2 marks). (3) Forgetting to convert percentage to decimal (lose 1 mark). (4) Reporting voltage without units.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Drawing a vague block diagram without labeling components** — always label every block (e.g., "Luft Detector," "Molybdenum Converter," "PMT"). Unlabeled diagrams lose 50% of diagram marks.
> 2. **Confusing NDIR with UV spectroscopy** — CO and CO₂ are detected using IR, not UV. NO₂ can be detected by UV (at 400–450 nm), but for NOx we use chemiluminescence.
> 3. **Skipping the converter explanation in chemiluminescent analyzers** — the molybdenum converter is a separate sub-component worth 1–2 marks. Always explain its role in detail.
> 4. **Unit conversion errors in numericals** — the ppm ↔ mg/m³ conversion is the most common source of computational mistakes. Always write the formula, the standard conditions, and the gas constant explicitly.
> 5. **Reporting Bosch smoke number and Hartridge opacity interchangeably** — they are NOT the same scale. Bosch is 0–10, Hartridge is 0–100%. Conversions require empirical correlations.
> 6. **Forgetting to state temperature and pressure** in numerical problems — regulatory comparisons must be at STP. Forgetting this loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — Read before entering the exam hall:**

- [ ] **Six major analyzers** to memorize: **NDIR, FID, Chemiluminescent, Hartridge, Bosch, Paramagnetic**.
- [ ] **Target gases**: NDIR → CO, CO₂, HC; FID → total HC; Chemiluminescent → NO/NO₂; Hartridge/Bosch → smoke; Paramagnetic → O₂.
- [ ] **CO absorption wavelength** = **4.6 µm** (in IR); **NO chemiluminescence emission** = 600–875 nm (red-IR).
- [ ] **Beer–Lambert Law** is the foundation of NDIR: $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha C L}$.
- [ ] **FID is calibrated with propane (C₃H₈)**; result reported as **ppmC** (parts per million as carbon).
- [ ] **Chemiluminescent reaction:** $NO + O_3 \rightarrow NO_2^* + O_2 \rightarrow NO_2 + h\nu$.
- [ ] **Molybdenum converter** reduces NO₂ to NO at **350 °C** for sequential NOx measurement.
- [ ] **Hartridge opacity:** $N = (1 - I/I_0) \times 100\%$; **Bosch smoke number:** $R_B = 10(1 - R_s/R_w)$.
- [ ] **Emission limits at STP** (0 °C, 1 atm) for regulatory reporting.
- [ ] **Three-Way Catalyst (TWC) requires** $\lambda = 1.00 \pm 0.01$ for simultaneous CO, HC, NOx reduction.
- [ ] **NOx reported as NO₂-equivalent** in mass-based regulations.
- [ ] **SI engine dominant pollutants:** CO, HC; **CI engine dominant pollutants:** NOx, PM.
- [ ] **Conversion formula (critical for numericals):** $C_{mg/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times (M \times 10^3) / V_{molar}$.
- [ ] **PM measurement** uses **Constant Volume Sampling (CVS)** with gravimetric filter weighing.
- [ ] **Lambda ($\lambda$)** = actual AFR / stoichiometric AFR; $\lambda > 1$ = lean, $\lambda < 1$ = rich.
- [ ] **Regulatory standards** to know: **Euro VI / BS-VI / EPA Tier 4**.
- [ ] **Sample conditioning** is critical: chillers for water removal, 5 µm filters for particulates.
- [ ] **PMT** multiplies **photoelectrons** via dynode cascade (NOT "light amplification").

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 4):** Emission control techniques are mandatory study items covering both Spark Ignition (SI) and Compression Ignition (CI) engine exhaust after-treatment. Mastery of **Catalytic Converters, Particulate Traps, Thermal Reactors, and EGR** is required for Part A and Part B questions.

## 1.1 Introduction to Engine Emission Control

An **Internal Combustion (IC) engine** exhaust stream contains several pollutants. The major regulated pollutants are:

- **Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC)** – from incomplete combustion and crevice volumes.
- **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** – from incomplete oxidation of carbon.
- **Oxides of Nitrogen ($\text{NO}_x$)** – from high in-cylinder peak temperatures (>$1800\,\text{K}$).
- **Particulate Matter (PM)** – primarily from diesel engines (soot + soluble organic fraction).
- **Lead compounds, $\text{SO}_2$, aldehydes** – secondary pollutants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> *Emission control techniques* are engineered systems—either in-cylinder modifications or exhaust after-treatment devices—designed to reduce the concentration of regulated pollutants in IC engine exhaust to meet **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** emission norms, which align with **Euro 6** standards.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor Visit" Intuition

Think of an engine as a **patient with a multi-organ disorder**:

| Engine Component | Doctor Analogy | Pollutant Treated |
|---|---|---|
| **Catalytic Converter** | The "lung doctor" – chemically converts harmful gases into harmless ones (like a respiratory therapist). | CO, HC, $\text{NO}_x$ |
| **Particulate Trap (DPF)** | The "kidney dialyzer" – physically filters soot particles from the bloodstream. | PM (Soot) |
| **Thermal Reactor** | The "fever reducer" – adds extra heat/oxygen to burn residual HC and CO completely. | CO, HC |
| **EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation)** | The "dieting coach" – dilutes intake with inert gas to reduce fever (combustion temperature). | $\text{NO}_x$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Aid:**
> **C**atalytic → Cures CO & HC (oxidation) + $\text{NO}_x$ (reduction)
> **P**articulate → Pulls PM
> **T**hermal → Treats (burns) HC & CO via heat
> **E**GR → Eases $\text{NO}_x$ by Exhaust gas recirculation

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters for KTU

In the 2024 scheme, this topic typically appears as:
- **2-mark short questions** (define terms, draw sketches).
- **7-mark sub-parts** in Part B (working of catalytic converter, advantages of EGR).
- **14-mark full questions** comparing techniques or describing construction/working of a specific device.

The standard legislative limits in India (BS-VI) are:
- **CO** = $1.0\,\text{g/km}$
- **HC + $\text{NO}_x$** = $0.10\,\text{g/km}$ (Petrol)
- **$\text{NO}_x$** = $0.08\,\text{g/km}$ (Diesel)
- **PM** = $0.0045\,\text{g/km}$ (Diesel)

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)

### 2.1.1 Construction

A catalytic converter consists of a **ceramic monolith honeycomb substrate** (typically cordierite – $2\text{MgO} \cdot 2\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot 5\text{SiO}_2$) coated with a **washcoat** of alumina ($\gamma\text{-Al}_2\text{O}_3$) on which **noble metal catalysts** are impregnated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Noble Metals Used (in order of activity):**
> 1. **Platinum (Pt)** – primary oxidation catalyst for CO and HC.
> 2. **Palladium (Pd)** – oxidation, cheaper than Pt.
> 3. **Rhodium (Rh)** – the only highly effective **reduction** catalyst for $\text{NO}_x$.

**Loading density:** $1.0 - 1.5\,\text{g/litre}$ of substrate volume (Pt, Pd) and $0.2 - 0.3\,\text{g/litre}$ (Rh).

### 2.1.2 Three Simultaneous Reactions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (TWC):** A *Three-Way Catalyst* is a single device in which three chemical reactions—**oxidation of CO, oxidation of HC, and reduction of $\text{NO}_x$**—occur simultaneously in the presence of suitable catalysts.

The reactions catalyzed are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Oxidation of CO:} \quad & 2\text{CO} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt/Pd}} 2\text{CO}_2 \\[4pt]
\text{Oxidation of HC:} \quad & \text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Pt/Pd}} x\text{CO}_2 + \frac{y}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} \\[4pt]
\text{Reduction of NO}_x\text{:} \quad & 2\text{NO} \xrightarrow{\text{Rh}} \text{N}_2 + \text{O}_2 \quad \text{or} \\[4pt]
& 2\text{NO} + 2\text{CO} \xrightarrow{\text{Rh}} \text{N}_2 + 2\text{CO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.1.3 The Lambda ($\lambda$) Window

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept:** The TWC works only within a narrow **air-fuel ratio window** of $\lambda = 1.00 \pm 0.01$. Outside this window, conversion efficiencies drop sharply.

Where:
$$
\lambda = \frac{(\text{A/F})_{\text{actual}}}{(\text{A/F})_{\text{stoichiometric}}}
$$

- $\lambda = 1$: Stoichiometric (14.7:1 for petrol)
- $\lambda < 1$: Rich mixture → high CO, HC, low $\text{NO}_x$
- $\lambda > 1$: Lean mixture → high $\text{NO}_x$, low CO, HC

This is why a **closed-loop EFI system** with an **oxygen (lambda) sensor** is mandatory for TWC operation.

### 2.1.4 Light-Off Temperature

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The *light-off temperature* is the temperature at which the catalyst achieves **50% conversion efficiency** of the pollutant.
> - For Pt/Pd: $\approx 250 - 300\,°\text{C}$
> - Operating temperature range: $400 - 800\,°\text{C}$
> - Deactivation temperature: $> 900\,°\text{C}$ (sintering of catalyst)

## 2.2 Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC)

In CI engines, since combustion is always lean ($\lambda > 1$), the **TWC cannot reduce $\text{NO}_x$**. Instead, a **Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC)** oxidizes:
- CO → $\text{CO}_2$
- HC (SOF) → $\text{CO}_2$ + $\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- NO → $\text{NO}_2$ (which then helps burn soot in DPF continuously)

## 2.3 Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF) / Particulate Trap

### 2.3.1 Construction

A wall-flow filter made of **silicon carbide (SiC)** or cordierite, with **alternately plugged channels**:

> [!TIP]
> **Working Principle:** Exhaust gas enters the open channels, is forced through the **porous ceramic walls** (pore size $\approx 10 - 20\,\mu\text{m}$), and exits from adjacent channels. Soot particles are trapped on the wall.

### 2.3.2 Regeneration (Critical Concept)

The trapped soot must be periodically burned off; otherwise, **back pressure** rises and engine performance drops.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Spontaneous regeneration:} \quad & \text{C (soot)} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad (\text{needs} > 600\,°\text{C}) \\[4pt]
\text{Forced (active) regeneration methods:} \quad & \\
\text{(a) Burner regeneration:} \quad & \text{Electric burner raises exhaust to } 600\,°\text{C} \\[4pt]
\text{(b) Catalyst-coated DPF:} \quad & \text{Lowers ignition to } 350 - 450\,°\text{C} \\[4pt]
\text{(c) Fuel additive:} \quad & \text{Ce-based fuel-borne catalyst (FBC) reduces soot ignition T} \\[4pt]
\text{(d) $\text{NO}_2$-assisted:} \quad & \text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{DOC}} \text{NO}_2 \\[2pt]
& \text{NO}_2 + \text{C} \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{CO} \quad (\text{at} \sim 250\,°\text{C})
\end{aligned}
$$

Filter efficiency is typically **$> 95\%$** for PM.

## 2.4 Thermal Reactor

### 2.4.1 Concept

A thermal reactor is an **afterburner chamber** placed close to the exhaust valve. It maintains high temperature ($> 700\,°\text{C}$) and turbulent mixing to **thermally oxidize** residual CO and HC.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net oxidation reaction:} \quad & \text{HC} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Heat} \\[4pt]
\text{Catalyst-aided:} \quad & \text{Pt/Pd coating on reactor walls lowers T to } 400 - 500\,°\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The thermal reactor works on the principle of *quenching the exhaust gas reactions*. Hot exhaust enters a larger chamber where it mixes with secondary air supplied by an **air injection system** (often driven by an air pump or pulse from exhaust pressure).

### 2.4.2 Requirements for Effective Thermal Reactor

1. **High temperature** ($> 600\,°\text{C}$) – requires exhaust valve insulation.
2. **Sufficient residence time** ($\tau \ge 0.1 - 0.3\,\text{s}$).
3. **Turbulent mixing** of exhaust with secondary air.
4. **Excess oxygen** – secondary air pump mandatory.

**Residence time equation:**
$$
\tau = \frac{V_{\text{reactor}}}{\dot{V}_{\text{exhaust}}}
$$

Where $V_{\text{reactor}}$ is reactor volume and $\dot{V}_{\text{exhaust}}$ is the volumetric exhaust flow rate.

## 2.5 Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)

### 2.5.1 Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU):** *Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)* is a technique in which a portion of the exhaust gas is routed back to the engine intake (or supplied directly to the cylinder) to dilute the fresh charge, lower peak flame/cylinder temperature, and thereby suppress the **thermal (Zeldovich) mechanism** of $\text{NO}_x$ formation.

### 2.5.2 The Zeldovich Mechanism (Why EGR Works)

$\text{NO}_x$ forms predominantly via the **extended Zeldovich mechanism**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{O} + \text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{N} \\[4pt]
\text{N} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O} \\[4pt]
\text{N} + \text{OH} \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" of EGR:** The first reaction has a very high activation energy ($\approx 318\,\text{kJ/mol}$). It is *extremely temperature sensitive*. A drop of just $100\,\text{K}$ in peak flame temperature can reduce $\text{NO}_x$ by up to **50%**. EGR dilutes intake with inert $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, which absorb heat and lower adiabatic flame temperature.

### 2.5.3 EGR Rate and its Effect

$$
\text{EGR Rate} (\%) = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{EGR}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{air}} + \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}}} \times 100
$$

Typical EGR rates:
- Gasoline: $5 - 15\%$
- Diesel: $15 - 40\%$ (modern diesels with cooled EGR)

### 2.5.4 Effect of EGR on Engine Performance

| Parameter | Effect of EGR |
|---|---|
| Peak flame temperature | ↓ Decreases |
| $\text{NO}_x$ emission | ↓↓ Significantly reduces |
| BSFC | ↑ Slightly increases |
| HC emission | ↑ Slightly increases (quench effect) |
| CO emission | ↑ Slightly increases (less O₂) |
| Smoke (diesel) | ↑ Increases at high EGR |
| Power output | ↓ Slightly decreases |

### 2.5.5 Types of EGR

1. **Internal EGR** – residual gases trapped by negative valve overlap (no external piping). Used in HCCI / Gasoline.
2. **External EGR** – external pipe + control valve + cooler.
   - **Hot EGR** – recirculate without cooling.
   - **Cooled EGR** – recirculate through EGR cooler; reduces cylinder temperature most effectively and allows higher EGR rates.

### 2.5.6 EGR Cooler

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In modern BS-VI diesels, **cooled EGR** is mandatory. The exhaust gas is passed through a heat exchanger cooled by engine coolant, reducing the EGR temperature from $\approx 400 - 500\,°\text{C}$ down to $\approx 100 - 150\,°\text{C}$. This boosts charge density and allows higher EGR rates without knock/smoke penalties.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Symbol / Equation | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Air-Fuel Ratio Equivalence | $\lambda = (\text{A/F})_{\text{act}} / (\text{A/F})_{\text{stoich}}$ | $\lambda = 1$ (stoich) |
| Stoichiometric A/F (Petrol) | $\text{A/F}_{\text{stoich}}$ | $14.7:1$ |
| Stoichiometric A/F (Diesel, C₁₂H₂₃) | – | $\approx 14.5:1$ |
| EGR Rate | $\dot{m}_{\text{EGR}} / (\dot{m}_{\text{air}} + \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}})$ | $5 - 40\%$ |
| Reactor residence time | $\tau = V_{\text{reactor}} / \dot{V}_{\text{exhaust}}$ | $0.1 - 0.3\,\text{s}$ |
| TWC operating window | $\lambda = 1.00 \pm 0.01$ | – |
| Light-off temperature (Pt) | $50\%$ conversion | $250 - 300\,°\text{C}$ |
| DPF regeneration T (catalyst-aided) | – | $350 - 450\,°\text{C}$ |
| Zeldovich activation energy | $E_a$ | $\approx 318\,\text{kJ/mol}$ |
| $\text{NO}_x$ reduction per 100 K drop | – | $\sim 50\%$ |
| TWC catalyst loading | Pt, Pd / Rh | $1.0$ / $0.2\,\text{g/L}$ |
| DPF filtration efficiency | – | $95 - 99\%$ |
| EGR cooler outlet T | – | $100 - 150\,°\text{C}$ |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Petrol cars (BS-VI)** → TWC + lambda sensor + secondary air injection.
- **Diesel cars (BS-VI)** → DOC + DPF + SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction) + EGR + ASC (Ammonia Slip Catalyst).
- **Motorcycles** → Small TWC (since BS-IV).
- **Heavy-duty trucks** → Larger DPF + SCR for $\text{NO}_x$ (AdBlue/DEF injection).
- **Hybrid vehicles** → Cold-start emissions still remain a major challenge; close-coupled catalysts are placed within $30\,\text{cm}$ of exhaust port.
