# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: $\text{NO}_x$ Reduction Estimation Using EGR

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters:** KTU frequently asks numerical/analytical questions on EGR and its effect on $\text{NO}_x$. This is a classic 7-mark sub-part.

### Problem Statement
A four-cylinder, four-stroke SI engine operates at $3000\,\text{rpm}$ on gasoline ($\text{A/F}_{\text{stoich}} = 14.7$). It is fitted with an EGR system that recirculates **$10\%$ by mass** of the total exhaust. The engine uses a stoichiometric mixture. Estimate:
1. The mass of EGR per cycle.
2. The percentage reduction in $\text{NO}_x$ assuming $\text{NO}_x \propto e^{(-E_a / R T_{\text{peak}})}$ and the EGR reduces $T_{\text{peak}}$ by $80\,\text{K}$.

### Given Data
- Displacement volume per cylinder $V_d = 5 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3$ (i.e., $500\,\text{cc}$ total, so $125\,\text{cc}/$cyl).
- Volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = 0.85$
- Intake temperature $T_1 = 350\,\text{K}$ (after EGR mixing)
- $R_{\text{air}} = 287\,\text{J/kg·K}$
- $E_a = 318\,\text{kJ/mol}$, $R_u = 8.314\,\text{J/mol·K}$
- Air-fuel ratio (stoich) $= 14.7$

### Step 1: Volumetric Air Flow Rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_{\text{air, theoretical}} &= \frac{V_d \cdot N}{2} \quad \text{(4-stroke: 1 cycle per 2 rev)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5 \times 10^{-4} \times 3000}{2} \\[4pt]
&= 0.75\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Explanation: $V_d$ is total displacement. For a 4-stroke engine, the number of power strokes per second is $N/2$ where $N$ is in rev/s. We will later convert rpm to rev/s.

Converting rpm: $N = 3000\,\text{rpm} = 50\,\text{rev/s}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_{\text{air, theoretical}} &= \frac{5 \times 10^{-4} \times 50}{2} \\[4pt]
&= 0.0125\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Actual Volumetric Flow (with $\eta_v$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_{\text{air, actual}} &= \eta_v \times \dot{V}_{\text{theoretical}} \\[4pt]
&= 0.85 \times 0.0125 \\[4pt]
&= 0.010625\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Explanation: Volumetric efficiency accounts for pressure drop, valve timing, and incomplete filling.

### Step 3: Mass Flow Rate of Air

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{air}} &= \frac{P_1 \dot{V}}{R T_1} \quad \text{(with } P_1 = 101325\,\text{Pa)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{101325 \times 0.010625}{287 \times 350} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1075.08}{100450} \\[4pt]
&= 0.01070\,\text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Mass Flow Rate of Fuel

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} &= \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{air}}}{(\text{A/F})} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.01070}{14.7} \\[4pt]
&= 7.28 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Total Mass Flow (Inlet Basis for EGR Rate)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{total}} &= \dot{m}_{\text{air}} + \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \\[4pt]
&= 0.01070 + 0.000728 \\[4pt]
&= 0.01143\,\text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: EGR Mass Flow (10% of Total)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{EGR}} &= 0.10 \times \dot{m}_{\text{total}} \\[4pt]
&= 0.10 \times 0.01143 \\[4pt]
&= 1.143 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result (Part 1):** $\dot{m}_{\text{EGR}} = 1.143 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{kg/s}$

### Step 7: $\text{NO}_x$ Reduction Using Arrhenius-type Approximation

The Zeldovich rate is exponential in $1/T$. Define:
$$
\text{NO}_x \propto \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{R_u T}\right)
$$

Let $T_1 = 2400\,\text{K}$ (baseline, no EGR). After EGR, $T_2 = 2400 - 80 = 2320\,\text{K}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\text{NO}_{x,2}}{\text{NO}_{x,1}} &= \exp\left[-\frac{E_a}{R_u}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)\right] \\[4pt]
\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} &= \frac{1}{2320} - \frac{1}{2400} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2400 - 2320}{2320 \times 2400} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{80}{5568000} \\[4pt]
&= 1.4368 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{K}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{E_a}{R_u} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) &= \frac{318000}{8.314} \times 1.4368 \times 10^{-5} \\[4pt]
&= 38248.5 \times 1.4368 \times 10^{-5} \\[4pt]
&= 0.5496
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\text{NO}_{x,2}}{\text{NO}_{x,1}} &= e^{-0.5496} = 0.5772 \\[4pt]
\text{Reduction} &= 1 - 0.5772 = 0.4228 \quad \text{i.e., } 42.28\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result (Part 2):** Approximately **$42\%$ reduction** in $\text{NO}_x$ with $10\%$ EGR and $80\,\text{K}$ drop.

## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix (KTU Favourite Question)

| Parameter | Catalytic Converter (TWC) | Particulate Trap (DPF) | Thermal Reactor | EGR |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pollutant Targeted** | CO, HC, $\text{NO}_x$ | PM (Soot) | CO, HC | $\text{NO}_x$ |
| **Working Principle** | Chemical catalysis | Physical filtration + oxidation | Thermal oxidation | Charge dilution |
| **Engine Type** | SI (petrol) | CI (diesel) | SI/CI (old) | SI + CI |
| **Operating Temperature** | $400 - 800\,°\text{C}$ | $200 - 600\,°\text{C}$ (with regen) | $600 - 900\,°\text{C}$ | Intake T + cooler |
| **External Energy Needed?** | No (uses exhaust heat) | Yes (regeneration) | Sometimes (ignition aid) | No |
| **Effect on BSFC** | Marginal ↑ | Slight ↑ (back pressure) | ↑ (parasitic loss) | ↑ $2 - 5\%$ |
| **Effect on Power** | Negligible | Slight ↓ | Slight ↓ | Slight ↓ |
| **Main Limitation** | Requires $\lambda = 1$ | Back pressure, regen freq. | High heat loss | Smoke, HC, BSFC |
| **Catalyst/Additive** | Pt, Pd, Rh | Ce (FBC), Pt coat | Pt/Pd coat | – |
| **Cost (relative)** | Medium | High | Medium | Low–Medium |
| **BS-VI Status** | Standard on petrol | Standard on diesel | Obsolete | Standard on both |

## 3.3 Derivation: DPF Regeneration Time

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Application:** A common analytical question on DPF is "How long between regenerations?"

### Given
- DPF volume $V = 5\,\text{L} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3$
- Soot loading limit (before regen) $m_{\text{soot}} = 8\,\text{g/L}$ of DPF
- Engine exhaust mass flow = $0.05\,\text{kg/s}$
- Soot generation rate $\approx 0.2\%$ of fuel mass in exhaust

### Step 1: Total Soot Capacity

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{soot, max}} &= 8\,\text{g/L} \times 5\,\text{L} \\[4pt]
&= 40\,\text{g} = 0.040\,\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Soot Generation Rate

From Section 3.1, $\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} = 7.28 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{kg/s}$. Soot = 0.2% of fuel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{soot}} &= 0.002 \times \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \\[4pt]
&= 0.002 \times 7.28 \times 10^{-4} \\[4pt]
&= 1.456 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Time Between Regenerations

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{regen interval}} &= \frac{m_{\text{soot, max}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{soot}}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.040}{1.456 \times 10^{-6}} \\[4pt]
&= 27473\,\text{s} \approx 7.63\,\text{hours of operation}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Insight:** A typical BS-VI diesel DPF regenerates every $300 - 1000\,\text{km}$ depending on duty cycle.

## 3.4 Worked Example: TWC Mass Balance (CO → $\text{CO}_2$)

A TWC processes $0.05\,\text{kg/s}$ of exhaust containing $1.2\%$ CO by mass. Find the $\text{CO}_2$ produced per second and verify stoichiometry.

### Step 1: Mass of CO
$$
\dot{m}_{\text{CO}} = 0.012 \times 0.05 = 6 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{kg/s}
$$

### Step 2: Moles of CO
$$
\dot{n}_{\text{CO}} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-4}}{28 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.02143\,\text{mol/s}
$$

### Step 3: Moles of $\text{CO}_2$ Produced (1:1)
$$
\dot{n}_{\text{CO}_2} = 0.02143\,\text{mol/s}
$$

### Step 4: Mass of $\text{CO}_2$
$$
\dot{m}_{\text{CO}_2} = 0.02143 \times 44 \times 10^{-3} = 9.43 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{kg/s}
$$

> **Check:** Mass gained by $\text{CO}_2$ exceeds mass of CO because of added O atom; oxygen comes from exhaust (which is $\approx 5\%$ O₂ by volume in normal exhaust).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Emission Control System (BS-VI Petrol)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Cylinder] --> B[Exhaust Manifold]
    B --> C[Lambda O2 Sensor 1 - Upregion]
    C --> D[Close-Coupled TWC]
    D --> E[Underbody TWC]
    E --> F[Muffler]
    F --> G[Tailpipe to Atmosphere]
    D -. feedback .-> H[ECU Engine Control Unit]
    H -. A/F adjustment .-> A
```

> **Reading Guide:** The lambda sensor measures residual $\text{O}_2$ to inform the ECU whether mixture is rich or lean. The ECU adjusts fuel injection to maintain $\lambda = 1.00 \pm 0.01$ for the TWC.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: BS-VI Diesel After-Treatment Train

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Diesel Engine] --> Q[EGR Cooler]
    Q --> P
    P --> R[Turbocharged Exhaust]
    R --> S[DOC Diesel Oxidation Catalyst]
    S --> T[DPF Diesel Particulate Filter]
    T --> U[SCR Selective Catalytic Reduction with AdBlue DEF Injection]
    U --> V[ASC Ammonia Slip Catalyst]
    V --> W[Tailpipe]
    P --> X[ECU]
    X --> Y[DPF Pressure Sensor]
    Y --> X
    X --> Z[NOx Sensor Tailpipe]
    Z --> X
    X --> AA[DEF Injector Control]
    AA --> U
```

> **Reading Guide:** This is the modern European/Indian BS-VI diesel architecture. EGR reduces $\text{NO}_x$ in-cylinder, while SCR finishes the job using urea injection. DPF catches soot; DOC oxidizes NO to $\text{NO}_2$ to help burn soot continuously.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Internal Layout of TWC

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SUBTWC[Three-Way Catalytic Converter]
        direction TB
        COIL1[Inlet Cone]
        COIL2[Outer Stainless Steel Shell]
        COIL3[Intumescent Mat Mounting]
        COIL4[Ceramic Monolith Honeycomb Cordierite]
        COIL5[Washcoat gamma Alumina]
        COIL6[Pt Pd Rh Catalyst Sites]
        COIL7[Outlet Cone]
        COIL1 --> COIL2 --> COIL3 --> COIL4 --> COIL5 --> COIL6 --> COIL7
    end
    EX[Hot Exhaust 400 800 C] --> COIL1
    COIL7 --> EXO[Cleaned Exhaust to Tailpipe]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Exhaust enters the inlet cone, passes through the ceramic monolith, and the catalyst (Pt, Pd, Rh) drives the three reactions. The intumescent mat holds the brick in place and expands when hot to seal gaps.

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: DPF Working

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inlet Channel Open End] --> B[Porous Ceramic Wall]
    B --> C[Soot Deposition on Wall]
    B --> D[Outlet Channel Plugged at Inlet]
    D --> E[Filtered Clean Gas Out]
    subgraph DPFREG[Regeneration Subsystem]
        R1[DPF Pressure Sensor]
        R2[ECU Logic]
        R3[Post Injection or Burner Activation]
        R1 --> R2 --> R3
    end
    C -. soot load increase .-> R1
    R3 -. raise T to 600 C .-> C
```

> **Reading Guide:** Inlet channels are plugged at the outlet end; outlet channels are plugged at the inlet end. This forces the gas through the wall. When back pressure (measured by R1) crosses a threshold, regeneration is triggered.

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram: EGR Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[Engine Exhaust Manifold] --> VALVE[EGR Valve Electronically Controlled]
    VALVE --> COOLER[EGR Cooler Heat Exchanger]
    COOLER --> INTMAN[Intake Manifold]
    INTMAN --> ENG
    ECU[ECU] --> VALVE
    LAMBDA[Lambda Sensor] --> ECU
    TPS[Throttle Position Sensor] --> ECU
    CTS[Coolant Temp Sensor] --> ECU
```

> **Reading Guide:** The ECU modulates the EGR valve based on engine load, speed, and coolant temperature. EGR is typically cut off at idle and cold start to ensure drivability.

## 4.6 Schematic Table: Comparison Map

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Emission Control] --> B[In-Cylinder Methods]
    A --> C[After-Treatment Methods]
    B --> B1[EGR]
    B --> B2[Variable Valve Timing]
    B --> B3[Direct Injection Stratified]
    C --> C1[TWC for SI]
    C --> C2[DOC for CI]
    C --> C3[DPF for CI]
    C --> C4[SCR for CI]
    C --> C5[Thermal Reactor Legacy]
    C --> C6[NOx Trap LNT]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Short Answer — Definition)
**Q:** Define *Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)*. State its purpose. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
> **Definition:** EGR is a technique in which a controlled portion of the engine exhaust gas is recirculated back into the combustion chamber (or intake manifold) to dilute the fresh charge.
>
> **Purpose:**
> 1. Reduces peak combustion temperature.
> 2. Suppresses the **thermal (Zeldovich) mechanism** of $\text{NO}_x$ formation.
> 3. Results in significant reduction of $\text{NO}_x$ emissions (up to $50 - 70\%$ in diesels with cooled EGR).
>
> **Equation (KTU Standard):**
> $$\text{EGR Rate} (\%) = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{EGR}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{air}} + \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}}} \times 100$$
> Typical values: $5 - 15\%$ (petrol), $15 - 40\%$ (diesel).

### Question 2 (Short Answer — Component Identification)
**Q:** List the noble metals used in a Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC) and write the role of each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
> 1. **Platinum (Pt)** – Oxidation catalyst: converts CO → $\text{CO}_2$ and HC → $\text{CO}_2$ + $\text{H}_2\text{O}$.
> 2. **Palladium (Pd)** – Oxidation catalyst (similar to Pt, more thermally stable, cheaper).
> 3. **Rhodium (Rh)** – Reduction catalyst: converts $\text{NO}_x$ → $\text{N}_2$ + $\text{O}_2$.
>
> **Loading:** $1.0 - 1.5\,\text{g/L}$ for Pt/Pd and $0.2 - 0.3\,\text{g/L}$ for Rh on washcoat of $\gamma\text{-Al}_2\text{O}_3$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC)** with a neat sketch. List the chemical reactions involved. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF)** — its construction, working, and the methods of regeneration. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — TWC

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Ceramic monolith substrate** (cordierite, $2\text{MgO} \cdot 2\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot 5\text{SiO}_2$) with square channels ($\approx 62 \text{ cells/cm}^2$, wall thickness $0.15\,\text{mm}$).
- **Stainless steel outer shell** with **inlet and outlet cones**.
- **Intumescent vermiculite mat** for thermal expansion sealing.
- **Washcoat** of $\gamma\text{-Al}_2\text{O}_3$ ($\approx 100 - 200\,\mu\text{m}$ thick) on channel walls.
- **Catalysts impregnated:** Pt, Pd, Rh.
- **Lambda ($\lambda$) sensor** in exhaust (before and after) for closed-loop control.

**Working (2 marks):**
Exhaust at $400 - 800\,°\text{C}$ flows through monolith channels. The washcoat offers high surface area, where the three reactions occur simultaneously on catalyst sites. ECU maintains $\lambda = 1.00 \pm 0.01$ for optimum conversion.

**Three Reactions (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
2\text{CO} + \text{O}_2 &\xrightarrow{\text{Pt/Pd}} 2\text{CO}_2 \\
\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 &\xrightarrow{\text{Pt/Pd}} x\text{CO}_2 + \frac{y}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} \\
2\text{NO} + 2\text{CO} &\xrightarrow{\text{Rh}} \text{N}_2 + 2\text{CO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — DPF

**Construction (2.5 marks):**
- Wall-flow filter made of **silicon carbide (SiC)** or cordierite.
- **Alternately plugged channels**: inlets plugged at outlet end, outlets plugged at inlet end. Cross-section alternates **open / closed / open / closed**.
- **Porous walls** (pore size $10 - 20\,\mu\text{m}$).
- **Catalyst coating** (Pt) for active regeneration.
- **Differential pressure sensor** across DPF.
- **Exhaust temperature sensor**.

**Working (2.5 marks):**
- Exhaust enters open inlet channel.
- Forced through porous walls (gas passes; soot deposited on wall).
- Filtered gas exits through adjacent outlet channel.
- **Filtration efficiency:** $95 - 99\%$.
- As soot loads, **back pressure** rises; ECU triggers **regeneration**.

**Regeneration Methods (2 marks):**
1. **Passive (continuous)** – $\text{NO}_2$ assisted (from DOC); $\text{NO}_2$ oxidizes soot at $\sim 250\,°\text{C}$.
2. **Active (forced)** – Post-injection of fuel in cylinder raises exhaust to $600\,°\text{C}$ to burn soot.
3. **Fuel-borne catalyst (FBC)** – Ce-based additive lowers soot ignition T.
4. **Electric burner** – Mounted upstream of DPF.
5. **Microwave regeneration** – Used in some applications.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. Many students write only **one** regeneration method. Mention at least **3** to score full marks.
> 2. Don't confuse DPF (wall-flow filter) with DOC (flow-through monolith). DOC cannot filter soot, only oxidizes gases.
> 3. Avoid writing "filter cleans itself" — always specify the regeneration trigger (back pressure threshold ~ $15\,\text{kPa}$).

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)** system with a neat sketch. Explain the **Zeldovich mechanism** and how EGR controls $\text{NO}_x$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of a **Thermal Reactor** with a sketch. Compare thermal reactor with catalytic converter. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — EGR

**EGR System Description (3 marks):**
Components:
- **EGR valve** (electronically controlled or vacuum-operated).
- **EGR pipe** from exhaust manifold to intake manifold.
- **EGR cooler** (heat exchanger, cooled by engine coolant) in modern systems.
- **Solenoid / vacuum actuator** controlled by ECU.
- **Sensors:** MAP, MAF, lambda, coolant temp, throttle position.

**Working (2 marks):**
- ECU opens EGR valve when engine is warm and load > $30\%$.
- Exhaust gas (mostly inert $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, $\text{N}_2$) enters intake.
- This dilutes fresh charge, lowering $\text{O}_2$ partial pressure.
- Peak flame temperature drops.
- $\text{NO}_x$ formation is suppressed.

**Zeldovich Mechanism (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{O} + \text{N}_2 &\rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{N} \quad (E_a \approx 318\,\text{kJ/mol}) \\
\text{N} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O} \\
\text{N} + \text{OH} &\rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$
The first (rate-limiting) step is highly temperature-dependent. EGR's role is to **reduce $T_{\text{peak}}$** by $50 - 200\,\text{K}$, thereby reducing the rate exponentially.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — Thermal Reactor

**Working (4 marks):**
- Thermal reactor is a large-volume, insulated chamber placed close to the exhaust port.
- It receives **hot exhaust** ($> 600\,°\text{C}$) and **secondary air** (injected via air pump or pulse).
- Residence time is $0.1 - 0.3\,\text{s}$ — sufficient for CO and HC to oxidize.
- Reactions:
$$
\begin{aligned}
2\text{CO} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 \\
\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 &\rightarrow x\text{CO}_2 + \frac{y}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Thermal Reactor | Catalytic Converter |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Thermal oxidation | Catalytic oxidation + reduction |
| Temperature needed | $600 - 900\,°\text{C}$ | $400 - 800\,°\text{C}$ |
| Catalyst | Optional (Pt/Pd) | Mandatory (Pt, Pd, Rh) |
| Pollutants handled | CO, HC | CO, HC, $\text{NO}_x$ |
| Secondary air | Yes (mandatory) | No |
| $\text{NO}_x$ treatment | No | Yes |
| Modern usage | Obsolete | Standard on BS-VI |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. Always draw the **secondary air injection** in the thermal reactor diagram — without it, no oxidation will occur.
> 2. Don't claim EGR is "pollution-free" — it increases HC, CO, and smoke; only $\text{NO}_x$ reduces.
> 3. In the Zeldovich mechanism, **mention the high activation energy** of the first step — this is a KTU favourite line.
> 4. Avoid the statement *"EGR is a catalyst"* — it is **not a catalyst**; it is a charge dilution method.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4: Emission Control Techniques)**

### A. Catalytic Converter (TWC)
- [ ] Substrate = cordierite ceramic monolith (honeycomb).
- [ ] Washcoat = $\gamma\text{-Al}_2\text{O}_3$.
- [ ] Catalysts = **Pt, Pd (oxidation); Rh (reduction)**.
- [ ] Lambda window = $1.00 \pm 0.01$ (stoichiometric).
- [ ] Light-off T = $250 - 300\,°\text{C}$ (Pt).
- [ ] Requires oxygen sensor + closed-loop ECU.
- [ ] Cannot be used in lean-burn / diesel directly.

### B. Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC)
- [ ] Used in CI engines.
- [ ] Oxidizes CO, HC, and NO → $\text{NO}_2$.
- [ ] $\text{NO}_2$ assists continuous DPF regeneration.

### C. Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF)
- [ ] Wall-flow type, alternately plugged channels.
- [ ] Substrate = SiC or cordierite.
- [ ] Filters $95 - 99\%$ of soot.
- [ ] Regeneration: passive (NO₂) + active (post-injection, burner, FBC).
- [ ] Regeneration T: $350 - 600\,°\text{C}$ (catalyst-aided).
- [ ] Back pressure trigger ~ $10 - 15\,\text{kPa}$.

### D. Thermal Reactor
- [ ] Afterburner chamber with secondary air injection.
- [ ] Residence time $0.1 - 0.3\,\text{s}$.
- [ ] T > $600\,°\text{C}$ needed.
- [ ] No $\text{NO}_x$ treatment.
- [ ] Mostly obsolete in BS-VI era.

### E. Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)
- [ ] Reduces $\text{NO}_x$ via charge dilution + $T_{\text{peak}}$ drop.
- [ ] Zeldovich mechanism is highly T-sensitive ($E_a = 318\,\text{kJ/mol}$).
- [ ] Hot EGR vs **Cooled EGR** (mandatory in modern diesels).
- [ ] Typical rates: $5 - 15\%$ petrol, $15 - 40\%$ diesel.
- [ ] Side effects: ↑ HC, ↑ CO, ↑ smoke, ↑ BSFC, ↓ power slightly.
- [ ] ECU cut-off: idle, cold start, WOT, low load.

### F. KTU-Specific High-Yield Statements (Use in Answers)
- "The TWC operates only when the air-fuel ratio is within the **lambda window** of $1.00 \pm 0.01$."
- "Rhodium is the only noble metal that can effectively **reduce $\text{NO}_x$**."
- "EGR reduces $\text{NO}_x$ but increases **BSFC, HC, and smoke**."
- "DPF requires **periodic regeneration** to avoid excessive back pressure."
- "Thermal reactor requires **secondary air** to function."
- "DOC oxidizes NO to $\text{NO}_2$, which then oxidizes soot in DPF at lower temperatures (passive regeneration)."

### G. Quick Formula Box
$$
\lambda = \frac{(\text{A/F})_{\text{actual}}}{(\text{A/F})_{\text{stoich}}}, \quad \text{EGR}\% = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{EGR}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{air}} + \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}}} \times 100
$$
$$
\tau_{\text{reactor}} = \frac{V_{\text{reactor}}}{\dot{V}_{\text{exhaust}}}, \quad t_{\text{DPF regen}} = \frac{m_{\text{soot, max}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{soot}}}
$$
$$
\frac{\text{NO}_{x,2}}{\text{NO}_{x,1}} = \exp\left[-\frac{E_a}{R_u}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)\right]
$$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Suggestions on Project Topics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMET604 - THERMAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Suggestions on Project Topics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Mechanical Engineering (PBMET604 – Thermal Engineering)** curriculum, the topic *"Suggestions on Project Topics"* refers to the systematic identification, classification, and recommendation of **research and development (R&D) problems** at the intersection of **Thermodynamics, Heat Transfer, Fluid Mechanics, and Energy Systems Engineering** that are appropriate for a final-semester **B.Tech Main Project (Phase-I & Phase-II)** under the **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework prescribed by **NEP 2020**.

A "project topic" in Thermal Engineering is defined as a **bounded, feasible, and measurable engineering investigation** that:

1. Applies fundamental laws of **Thermodynamics (Zeroth, First, Second, Third)**, modes of **Heat Transfer (Conduction, Convection, Radiation)**, and principles of **Fluid Mechanics** to a real-world or simulated system.
2. Has clearly defined **inputs, outputs, and performance metrics** (e.g., thermal efficiency $\eta_{th}$, Coefficient of Performance COP, Effectiveness $\epsilon$, Heat Transfer Coefficient $h$, etc.).
3. Can be completed within **one academic semester** (approximately 4–5 months) using available laboratory infrastructure, computational tools, and a reasonable budget.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Mandate (NEP 2020 Aligned):**
> Per the KTU B.Tech 2024 regulations, the final-year project is a **mandatory 8-credit course** that contributes to **Course Outcomes CO5** (Modern Tool Usage) and **CO6** (The Engineer and Society). Therefore, every suggested topic MUST demonstrate **engineering relevance, measurable outcomes, and societal impact**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of selecting a project topic as choosing **a dish to cook for a master chef's competition**:

| Chef Competition Analogy | Thermal Engineering Project |
|---|---|
| The **cuisine type** (Italian, Indian, Chinese) | The **domain** (IC Engines, HVAC, Heat Exchangers, Solar Energy) |
| The **main ingredient** (chicken, paneer, vegetables) | The **system under study** (diesel engine, refrigerator, solar collector) |
| The **cooking technique** (grilling, steaming, baking) | The **methodology** (experimental, CFD simulation, hybrid) |
| The **judging criteria** (taste, presentation, creativity) | The **evaluation parameters** (efficiency, emissions, COP, effectiveness) |
| The **time limit** (e.g., 60 minutes) | The **semester deadline** (~4 months) |
| **Available kitchen equipment** | **Available lab resources, software, and budget** |

Just as a smart chef picks a dish that **excites the judges, uses available equipment, and finishes within the time limit**, a smart KTU student should pick a project that **excites the industry/research community, uses available lab/CFD tools, and finishes within the semester**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A "great" project topic is NOT the most complex or exotic one. It is the topic that can be **rigorously executed, clearly documented, and convincingly defended** within the available time and resources — while still demonstrating meaningful engineering contribution.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constraints for Project Selection

The following metrics, framed by the **KTU 2024 Project Evaluation Rubric**, are **bold** for emphasis:

- **Feasibility Index (FI):** Ratio of available resources to required resources. Target: $FI \geq 0.7$.
- **Novelty Score (NS):** Degree of innovation on a scale of 1–10 (based on literature survey). Target: $NS \geq 6$.
- **Societal Impact Factor (SIF):** Relevance to local/regional industry or sustainability (UN-SDG alignment). Target: $SIF \geq 5$ (on 1–10 scale).
- **Measurable Outcome Count (MOC):** Number of quantifiable performance indicators. Target: $MOC \geq 3$.
- **Budget Ceiling (BC):** As per KTU norms, typically **₹5,000–₹15,000** per project for consumables.
- **Duration:** **16 weeks** (one full semester, including Phase-I review and Phase-II final viva).

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Students:** If you cannot list **at least 3 measurable engineering parameters** (e.g., efficiency, emissions, heat transfer rate, temperature distribution) for your chosen topic in under 5 minutes, the topic is likely too vague. Refine it.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Thermal Engineering Projects

Thermal Engineering projects can be broadly classified into the following categories:

1. **Experimental Investigations** (Hands-on lab work)
2. **Computational / Simulation-Based Projects** (CFD using ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM, MATLAB)
3. **Design & Fabrication Projects** (Build a prototype)
4. **Review & Analytical Projects** (Literature review with comparative analysis)
5. **Hybrid Projects** (Combination of experiment + simulation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Preference:** Hybrid projects (Experiment + Simulation) and Design-Fabrication-Test projects consistently score higher in viva voce because they demonstrate the **complete engineering design cycle**: *Design → Fabricate → Test → Analyze → Conclude*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework for Project Topic Evaluation

When evaluating a potential project topic, the student must apply the following structured analytical framework. This framework ensures alignment with **KTU Programme Outcomes (POs) PO1–PO12** and **Programme Specific Outcomes (PSOs)** of Mechanical Engineering.

### 2.1.1 The PQRST Selection Method (Adapted for KTU)

A recommended decision-making algorithm for topic selection is the **PQRST** method:

- **P – Problem Identification:** Is there a clear, well-defined engineering problem?
- **Q – Question Formulation:** Can the problem be phrased as a specific, answerable research question?
- **R – Resource Audit:** Are the required tools, software, lab equipment, and budget available?
- **S – Scope Definition:** Is the scope narrow enough to be completed in 16 weeks but broad enough to be non-trivial?
- **T – Timeline Realism:** Can milestones (literature review, methodology, data collection, analysis, report) be realistically scheduled?

---

### 2.1.2 Alignment with UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)

KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) mandates that projects demonstrate societal relevance. Thermal Engineering projects can strongly contribute to the following **UN-SDG 2030** goals:

| SDG Number | Goal Title | Thermal Engineering Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **SDG 7** | Affordable and Clean Energy | Solar thermal, biomass, hydrogen-based systems |
| **SDG 9** | Industry, Innovation, and Infrastructure | Heat exchanger design, process optimization |
| **SDG 11** | Sustainable Cities and Communities | HVAC, district cooling, building energy management |
| **SDG 12** | Responsible Consumption and Production | Waste heat recovery, energy efficiency |
| **SDG 13** | Climate Action | Emission reduction, carbon capture, low-carbon fuels |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Project evaluators and external examiners in Kerala often award **bonus marks** for projects that explicitly map to **SDG 7, 12, or 13** because Kerala is aggressively pursuing its **Carbon Neutral Kerala 2050** mission.

---

## 2.2 Categorized Project Topic Suggestions

Below is a **high-yield, examiner-curated** list of project topics, organized by sub-domain. Each topic is annotated with its **domain**, **methodology**, **key performance parameters**, and **SDG alignment**.

### **Category A: Internal Combustion (IC) Engines & Alternative Fuels**

| # | Suggested Topic | Domain | Methodology | Key Parameters | SDG |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A1 | Performance and emission analysis of a **single-cylinder CI engine** fuelled with biodiesel blends (B20, B40, B100) from waste cooking oil | IC Engines | Experimental | Brake Thermal Efficiency $\eta_{bth}$, BSFC, CO, NOx, HC, Smoke opacity | 7, 13 |
| A2 | Experimental investigation of **hydrogen-enriched CNG** in a SI engine on performance and emissions | IC Engines / Alt Fuels | Experimental | $\eta_{bth}$, EGT, HC, CO, NOx | 7, 13 |
| A3 | CFD analysis of **in-cylinder flow, spray, and combustion** in a diesel engine using ANSYS Fluent | IC Engines | Simulation | Turbulent Kinetic Energy (TKE), Equivalence Ratio $\phi$, Heat Release Rate (HRR) | 9 |
| A4 | Study of **exhaust gas recirculation (EGR)** effects on NOx formation in a diesel engine | IC Engines | Experimental | NOx concentration, $\eta_{bth}$, Smoke | 13 |
| A5 | Performance comparison of **bioethanol–gasoline blends (E10, E20, E85)** in a SI engine | IC Engines | Experimental | $\eta_{bth}$, BSFC, CO, HC | 7, 12 |
| A6 | **Heat transfer augmentation** in IC engine cooling jacket using nanofluids | IC Engines + HT | Experimental | Heat transfer coefficient $h$, Nusselt number $Nu$ | 9 |

---

### **Category B: Heat Exchangers & Heat Transfer Enhancement**

| # | Suggested Topic | Domain | Methodology | Key Parameters | SDG |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| B1 | Design and CFD analysis of a **shell-and-tube heat exchanger** with twisted tape inserts | Heat Transfer | CFD | Effectiveness $\epsilon$, Nusselt number $Nu$, Friction factor $f$ | 9 |
| B2 | Experimental study of **finned-tube heat exchangers** using different fin geometries (straight, helical, perforated) | Heat Transfer | Experimental | Overall heat transfer coefficient $U$, $\epsilon$ | 9 |
| B3 | Heat transfer enhancement in **plate heat exchangers** using CuO, Al₂O₃ nanofluids | Nanofluids | Experimental | $Nu$, $f$, Thermal Performance Factor (TPF) | 9 |
| B4 | Design and fabrication of a **cross-flow compact heat exchanger** for waste heat recovery | Design + Fab | Experimental + Analytical | $\epsilon$, Pressure drop $\Delta P$, NTU | 12 |
| B5 | CFD analysis of **phase-change heat transfer** in a latent heat thermal energy storage (LHTES) unit | HT + TES | Simulation | Melt fraction, solidification time, $Nu$ | 7 |
| B6 | Numerical investigation of **convective heat transfer** in a ribbed channel | HT | CFD (Fluent) | $Nu$, $f$, Thermal enhancement factor | 9 |

---

### **Category C: Refrigeration, Air-Conditioning & HVAC**

| # | Suggested Topic | Domain | Methodology | Key Parameters | SDG |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | Performance analysis of a **vapour compression refrigeration (VCR) system** using R134a, R1234yf, and R290 (propane) as refrigerants | Refrigeration | Experimental | COP, Refrigerating Effect (RE), Compressor work | 13 |
| C2 | Experimental study on the **performance of an evaporative cooler** for hot-humid Kerala climate | HVAC | Experimental | Wet-Bulb Effectiveness $\varepsilon_{wb}$, Cooling capacity | 11 |
| C3 | CFD analysis of **air distribution in a server room** for thermal management | HVAC | CFD | Airflow pattern index (API), temperature uniformity | 9 |
| C4 | Energy efficiency study of **inverter-based split AC** vs. conventional fixed-speed AC | HVAC | Experimental | EER, power consumption, seasonal COP | 7, 11 |
| C5 | Design and analysis of a **solar-powered absorption refrigeration system** using LiBr-H₂O pair | Solar + Refrig | Analytical + Design | COP, solar fraction, generator temperature | 7 |
| C6 | Optimization of **chilled water pipe insulation** for energy savings in a building HVAC system | HVAC | Analytical + Simulation | Heat loss, payback period | 7, 11 |

---

### **Category D: Solar Thermal & Renewable Energy Systems**

| # | Suggested Topic | Domain | Methodology | Key Parameters | SDG |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| D1 | Performance evaluation of a **solar water heater** with and without phase-change material (PCM) thermal storage | Solar Thermal | Experimental | Collector efficiency $\eta_c$, Storage efficiency | 7 |
| D2 | Design and CFD analysis of a **solar air heater** with artificial roughness on the absorber plate | Solar Thermal | CFD + Experimental | $\eta_c$, friction factor $f$ | 7 |
| D3 | Experimental study of a **solar still** integrated with a thermal energy storage unit | Solar Thermal | Experimental | Productivity (L/m²/day), efficiency | 7 |
| D4 | Design of a **hybrid PV/T (Photovoltaic/Thermal) solar collector** for combined electricity and hot water generation | Solar Hybrid | Experimental | Electrical efficiency $\eta_e$, Thermal efficiency $\eta_t$, Overall $\eta$ | 7 |
| D5 | Performance analysis of a **biomass gasifier** for thermal applications in rural Kerala | Biomass | Experimental | Gas composition (CO, H₂, CH₄), Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE) | 7, 12 |
| D6 | Design and analysis of a **solar pond** for seasonal thermal energy storage | Solar + TES | Analytical + Simulation | Heat extraction rate, pond temperature profile | 7 |

---

### **Category E: Gas Turbines, Power Cycles & Combined Systems**

| # | Suggested Topic | Domain | Methodology | Key Parameters | SDG |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| E1 | Parametric analysis of a **Brayton cycle** with intercooling, reheating, and regeneration | Gas Turbine | Analytical | Net work output, $\eta_{th}$, Specific work | 9 |
| E2 | Thermodynamic analysis of a **combined cycle power plant** (Rankine + Brayton) | Power Plant | Analytical | Overall efficiency, $\eta_{combined}$ | 9 |
| E3 | Performance analysis of a **cogeneration (CHP) system** for a sugar mill or paper mill | Cogeneration | Analytical | Energy Utilization Factor (EUF), $\eta_{overall}$ | 12 |
| E4 | Exergy analysis of a **steam power plant** with superheat, reheat, and regeneration | Exergy | Analytical | Exergy efficiency $\psi$, irreversibility $I$ | 9 |
| E5 | CFD analysis of **combustion in a gas turbine combustor** using ANSYS Fluent | Gas Turbine | CFD | Exit temperature profile, pattern factor, NOx | 13 |
| E6 | Design of an **organic Rankine cycle (ORC)** for low-temperature waste heat recovery | Waste Heat | Analytical + Design | $\eta_{th}$, Net power output, turbine size | 12 |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Applicable Across All Topics)

The following formulas are the **mathematical backbone** that any thermal engineering project MUST use. Memorize and apply them.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description / Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | First Law for Closed System | $\Delta U = Q - W$ | Energy balance in batch processes |
| 2 | First Law for Open System (Steady Flow) | $Q - W = \dot{m}\left[(h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]$ | Turbine, compressor, nozzle analysis |
| 3 | Thermal Efficiency (Generic) | $\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$ | Any heat engine |
| 4 | Brake Thermal Efficiency (IC Engine) | $\eta_{bth} = \frac{\text{Brake Power}}{\dot{m}_f \times CV}$ | IC engine experimental projects |
| 5 | Indicated Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{ith} = \frac{\text{Indicated Power}}{\dot{m}_f \times CV}$ | IC engine experimental projects |
| 6 | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) | $\text{BSFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} \quad [kg/kWh]$ | IC engine fuel economy |
| 7 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \frac{\text{Actual mass inducted}}{\text{Theoretical mass at ambient}}$ | IC engine breathing |
| 8 | Coefficient of Performance (COP) — Refrigeration | $\text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}$ | Refrigeration / Heat pump projects |
| 9 | COP — Heat Pump | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net,in}}$ | Heating applications |
| 10 | Refrigerating Effect (RE) | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ (VCR cycle) | Capacity of refrigeration system |
| 11 | Heat Exchanger Effectiveness (NTU Method) | $\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})}$ | Heat exchanger design |
| 12 | Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient | $\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L} + \frac{1}{h_o}$ | Heat exchanger wall resistance |
| 13 | Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) | $\text{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Heat exchanger analysis |
| 14 | NTU | $\text{NTU} = \frac{UA}{C_{min}}$ | Heat exchanger sizing |
| 15 | Fouling Factor | $R_f = \frac{1}{U_{dirty}} - \frac{1}{U_{clean}}$ | Real-world heat exchanger performance loss |
| 16 | Conduction (Fourier's Law) | $q = -k \frac{dT}{dx}$ | Steady-state heat conduction |
| 17 | Convective Heat Transfer (Newton's Law) | $q = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Forced/natural convection |
| 18 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | Flow regime (laminar/turbulent) |
| 19 | Nusselt Number | $Nu = \frac{h D}{k}$ | Convective heat transfer intensity |
| 20 | Prandtl Number | $Pr = \frac{\mu c_p}{k}$ | Fluid thermal property indicator |
| 21 | Rayleigh Number (Natural Convection) | $Ra = Gr \cdot Pr = \frac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu^2} Pr$ | Buoyancy-driven flow |
| 22 | Collector Efficiency (Solar) | $\eta_c = \frac{\dot{m} c_p (T_o - T_i)}{G_T A_c}$ | Solar thermal projects |
| 23 | Energy Utilization Factor (EUF) | $\text{EUF} = \frac{W_{power} + Q_{heat}}{Q_{fuel}}$ | Cogeneration evaluation |
| 24 | Exergy (Specific) | $\psi = (h - h_0) - T_0(s - s_0)$ | Available energy in a stream |
| 25 | Exergy Destruction (Gouy-Stodola) | $I = T_0 S_{gen}$ | Irreversibility quantification |
| 26 | Emissivity (Stefan-Boltzmann) | $q = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Radiation heat transfer |
| 27 | Biot Number (Lumped Analysis) | $Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k} < 0.1$ | Validity of lumped capacitance |
| 28 | Fourier Number (Transient Conduction) | $Fo = \frac{\alpha t}{L^2}$ | Time-scale of diffusion |
| 29 | Melting Fraction (PCM) | $f = \frac{E_{stored}}{E_{total}} = \frac{\int_0^t \dot{Q}_{net} dt}{m \cdot L_f}$ | Latent heat storage progress |
| 30 | Peltier/Seebeck (Thermoelectric) | $V = \alpha (T_h - T_c)$ | TEG projects for WHR |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Table Formatting:** In the above table, the absolute value of temperature difference (e.g., $\vert T_h - T_c \vert$) has been written as $T_h - T_c$ to avoid breaking markdown table syntax with the pipe character $\vert$. When copying equations into reports, retain the magnitude notation explicitly.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Relevance

The project topics suggested above have **direct industrial and societal utility** in the following engineering fields:

1. **Automotive Industry (Kerala/Tamil Nadu belt):** IC engine projects inform OEM R&D on alternative fuels and emission compliance (BS-VI norms).
2. **Coconut and Spice Processing Industry (Kerala):** Solar dryer, biomass gasifier, and heat exchanger projects are directly applicable to agro-processing SMEs.
3. **Hospitals & Pharma Cold Chain:** Refrigeration and cryogenics projects support vaccine storage (critical for Kerala's tropical disease management).
4. **Marine & Shipyards (Cochin Shipyard):** CFD projects on marine engine cooling, propeller cavitation, and LNG carrier regasification.
5. **Data Centers (Kerala IT parks):** CFD-based server room cooling and microchannel heat sink projects.
6. **National Solar Mission (India):** Solar thermal, PVT, and solar refrigeration projects directly support the 500 GW renewable target by 2030.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Methodology & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Standard Project Execution Workflow (KTU 2024 Scheme)

This section provides the **exhaustive, step-by-step methodology** that a KTU 2024 B.Tech student MUST follow when executing any Thermal Engineering project. Each step is annotated with expected deliverables and approximate time allocation.

---

### **Phase 0: Topic Finalization (Weeks 1–2)**

#### Step 1: Brainstorming Domain Selection
The student identifies a broad area (e.g., "IC Engines", "Heat Exchangers", "Solar Energy").

#### Step 2: Literature Survey
- Read at least **15–20 peer-reviewed journal articles** from IEEE, Elsevier (Applied Thermal Engineering, Energy, Energy Conversion and Management), Springer, and ASME journals.
- Use databases: **Scopus, Web of Science, Google Scholar**.
- Identify the **research gap** (what previous researchers have NOT done).

#### Step 3: Problem Statement Formulation
The problem statement must follow the **"SMART"** format:

$$
\text{Problem} = \underbrace{\text{Specific}}_{\text{clear system}} + \underbrace{\text{Measurable}}_{\text{quantifiable output}} + \underbrace{\text{Achievable}}_{\text{resources available}} + \underbrace{\text{Relevant}}_{\text{thermal eng.}} + \underbrace{\text{Time-bound}}_{\text{1 semester}}
$$

**Example SMART Problem Statement:**
> *"To experimentally investigate the effect of **40% blend of waste cooking oil biodiesel** with diesel on the **brake thermal efficiency, BSFC, and emission characteristics (CO, NOx, HC, smoke)** of a **single-cylinder, 4-stroke, water-cooled CI engine** under **varying load (0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%)**, and to compare the results with pure diesel operation."*

**Checklist before proceeding:**
- [x] Specific system identified (CI engine, biodiesel B40)
- [x] Measurable outputs listed ($\eta_{bth}$, BSFC, CO, NOx, HC, smoke)
- [x] Achievable with available resources (test rig in Mechanical Engineering lab)
- [x] Relevant to thermal engineering and current research
- [x] Time-bound (load sweep experiment ≈ 3 weeks)

---

### **Phase 1: Methodology Design (Weeks 3–4)**

#### Step 4: Methodology Selection
Choose between:

$$
\text{Methodology} \in \{\text{Experimental},\ \text{Analytical},\ \text{CFD Simulation},\ \text{Design-Fabricate},\ \text{Review},\ \text{Hybrid}\}
$$

For an **experimental biodiesel project**, the methodology block diagram is:

```
[Feedstock Preparation]
        ↓
[Oil → Transesterification → Biodiesel → Blend B40 (40% bio + 60% diesel)]
        ↓
[Property Testing: Density, Viscosity, Flash Point, Calorific Value]
        ↓
[Engine Test Rig Setup (Kirloskar TV1 / similar single-cylinder CI engine)]
        ↓
[Baseline Test: Pure Diesel (B0)]
        ↓
[Test: B40 at Loads 0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%]
        ↓
[Data Recording: BP, BSFC, EGT, CO, NOx, HC, Smoke]
        ↓
[Data Analysis: Averages, Std. Deviation, Comparison Plots]
        ↓
[Conclusions and Recommendations]
```

#### Step 5: Resource Audit Checklist

| Resource | Required | Available? (Y/N) | Alternative if N |
|---|---|---|---|
| Test engine (single-cylinder CI) | Yes | | Use a different rig or do CFD only |
| Biodiesel feedstock (WCO) | Yes | | Collect from campus canteens |
| Emission analyzer (5-gas) | Yes | | Use smoke meter + limited gas sensors |
| Data acquisition system | Yes | | Manual recording + DAQ if needed |
| Budget (₹) | ~₹8,000 | | Reduce blends to B20 only |

---

### **Phase 2: Execution (Weeks 5–12)**

#### Step 6: Experimental / Simulation Run

For experimental IC engine work, the **standard test procedure** is:

1. **Warm-up:** Run engine at no-load for **15 minutes** to stabilize operating temperature.
2. **Baseline test:** With **B0 (pure diesel)**, apply loads: 0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%. At each load, wait **3 minutes** for steady state, then record:
   - Load (kg) → Brake Power $BP = \frac{2\pi N T}{60,000}$ in kW
   - Fuel consumption time for 10 mL → BSFC calculation
   - Exhaust Gas Temperature (EGT) using K-type thermocouple
   - Emissions (CO, HC, NOx) using exhaust gas analyzer
   - Smoke opacity using smoke meter
3. **Cool down:** Run no-load for 5 minutes.
4. **Switch fuel:** Drain and refill with **B20**, repeat step 2.
5. **Repeat for B40, B60, B100** as per project scope.
6. **Three repetitions** for each load-fuel combination to ensure repeatability.

#### Step 7: Data Recording Format

A standard tabular data sheet:

| Trial | Fuel | Load (%) | Speed (rpm) | Torque (N·m) | Fuel Time (s/10mL) | EGT (°C) | CO (%) | NOx (ppm) | HC (ppm) | Smoke (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | B0 | 25 | 1500 | — | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| 2 | B0 | 50 | 1500 | — | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

#### Step 8: Sample Calculation

Given:
- Brake load = 8 kg, arm = 0.197 m, engine speed $N$ = 1500 rpm
- Fuel consumption time = 22 s for 10 mL, fuel density $\rho_f$ = 840 kg/m³, Calorific Value $CV$ = 43 MJ/kg

**Step 8.1:** Brake Power

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Torque } T &= \text{Load} \times g \times r \\
&= 8 \times 9.81 \times 0.197 \\
&= 15.46 \ \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
BP &= \frac{2 \pi N T}{60,000} \\
&= \frac{2 \pi \times 1500 \times 15.46}{60,000} \\
&= 2.428 \ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8.2:** Fuel mass flow rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V} &= \frac{10 \ \text{cm}^3}{22 \ \text{s}} = 0.4545 \ \text{cm}^3/\text{s} \\
\dot{m}_f &= \dot{V} \times \rho_f = 0.4545 \times 10^{-6} \times 840 \\
&= 3.818 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{kg/s} \\
&= 1.374 \ \text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8.3:** Brake Thermal Efficiency

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{bth} &= \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \times CV} \\
&= \frac{2.428}{1.374 \times 43} \\
&= \frac{2.428}{59.08} \\
&= 0.0411 = 4.11\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8.4:** BSFC

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BSFC} &= \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} = \frac{1.374}{2.428} \\
&= 0.566 \ \text{kg/kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Phase 3: Analysis & Interpretation (Weeks 13–15)**

#### Step 9: Statistical Analysis

- Compute **mean** and **standard deviation** for each parameter at each load-fuel combination.
- Apply **ANOVA** (Analysis of Variance) if comparing >2 fuel blends.
- Use **error bars** (typically ±1 SD or ±SE) on all plots.

#### Step 10: Graphical Plotting

The following plots are **mandatory** for an IC engine project:

| Plot | X-axis | Y-axis | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Load (%) | $\eta_{bth}$ (%) | Efficiency vs. load for all fuels |
| 2 | Load (%) | BSFC (kg/kWh) | Fuel economy vs. load |
| 3 | Load (%) | EGT (°C) | Combustion quality |
| 4 | Load (%) | CO (ppm or %) | Emission trend |
| 5 | Load (%) | NOx (ppm) | NOx formation |
| 6 | Load (%) | Smoke opacity (%) | Combustion completeness |

#### Step 11: Comparative Discussion

Discuss WHY the trends occur using the **fundamental theory**:

> *"The brake thermal efficiency of B40 is marginally lower (by ~1.5%) than that of pure diesel at full load due to the **lower calorific value** of biodiesel (~37 MJ/kg vs. 43 MJ/kg for diesel) and the **higher density and viscosity** of B40, which leads to poorer atomization and incomplete combustion. However, NOx emissions are slightly higher due to the **higher cetane number and combustion temperature**, while CO and HC are lower due to the **oxygenated nature** of biodiesel molecules."*

---

### **Phase 4: Report Writing & Defense (Weeks 16–18)**

#### Step 12: Report Structure (KTU Format)

1. **Title Page** (with student name, register number, guide name)
2. **Certificate** (signed by guide and HOD)
3. **Abstract** (200–300 words)
4. **Table of Contents**
5. **List of Figures / Tables**
6. **Chapter 1: Introduction** (with SDG mapping)
7. **Chapter 2: Literature Review** (15+ references, organized thematically)
8. **Chapter 3: Methodology / System Description**
9. **Chapter 4: Results and Discussion**
10. **Chapter 5: Conclusions and Future Scope**
11. **References** (IEEE/Elsevier format)
12. **Appendices** (sample calculations, raw data, datasheets)

#### Step 13: Viva Voce Preparation

The student must be prepared to answer questions on:
- **Fundamental theory** behind every formula used.
- **Sources of experimental error** (instrumentation, human, environmental).
- **Societal and SDG relevance** of the work.
- **Scope for future work** (what would you do next?).
- **Limitations** of the current study.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix: Topic Selection Tool

To objectively select among multiple candidate topics, students may use the following **weighted scoring matrix**:

| Criterion (Weight $w_i$) | Topic A Score (0–10) | Topic B Score (0–10) | Topic C Score (0–10) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feasibility ($w$ = 0.25) | | | |
| Novelty ($w$ = 0.20) | | | |
| Industry relevance ($w$ = 0.15) | | | |
| Measurable outcomes ($w$ = 0.15) | | | |
| Budget fit ($w$ = 0.10) | | | |
| SDG alignment ($w$ = 0.10) | | | |
| Examiner interest ($w$ = 0.05) | | | |
| **Weighted Total** | $\Sigma$ | $\Sigma$ | $\Sigma$ |

The topic with the **highest weighted total** should be selected, provided it is also **feasible within the semester timeline**.

---

## 3.3 Risk Assessment Matrix for Project Execution

| Risk | Probability | Impact | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Equipment breakdown | Medium | High | Book lab slot early; have backup plan (CFD) |
| Insufficient feedstock (e.g., biodiesel) | Medium | Medium | Order raw materials in Week 3 |
| Emission analyzer malfunction | Low | High | Calibrate in Week 4; have contact for service |
| Delay in literature review | High | Medium | Use reference management software (Mendeley/Zotero) |
| Scope creep (adding too many fuels/loads) | High | Medium | Freeze scope by Week 4 |
| Guide unavailability | Low | High | Schedule weekly meetings; email updates |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Note to Students:** The topic "Suggestions on Project Topics" is a **conceptual/guidance-oriented** topic rather than a numerical problem-solving topic. Therefore, KTU exam questions on this module typically appear as **Part-A short-answer conceptual questions (3 marks)** or as **Part-B descriptive/analytical questions (14 marks)** focused on project methodology, topic selection criteria, literature review, or experimental/simulation planning.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Style]

**Q1.** List any **six criteria** that should be considered while selecting a B.Tech project topic in Thermal Engineering. Briefly explain any two.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| S.No. | Criterion | Brief Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Feasibility** | The project must be completable within the semester using available resources. |
| 2 | **Novelty** | The work should contribute something new to existing literature (research gap). |
| 3 | **Measurability** | At least 3–5 quantitative parameters (e.g., $\eta_{bth}$, BSFC, COP) must be defined. |
| 4 | **Societal/SDG Relevance** | The topic should align with UN-SDG goals (e.g., SDG 7, 12, 13). |
| 5 | **Budget** | Consumable cost should not exceed ₹10,000–₹15,000 typically. |
| 6 | **Industry Relevance** | The topic should have potential application in local/regional industry. |
| 7 | **Scope Manageability** | The scope should be narrow enough to finish but broad enough to be non-trivial. |
| 8 | **Guide Expertise** | The topic should fall within the guide's specialization for proper mentoring. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often list criteria but fail to **explain any two** — losing 1 of 3 marks. Always provide a one-line justification for at least two criteria.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Style]

**Q2.** Differentiate between **experimental, analytical, and CFD-based** project methodologies in Thermal Engineering. Give one example topic for each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Methodology | Definition | Example Topic | Key Tool |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Experimental** | Physical measurements on a real test rig | Performance and emission analysis of a CI engine fuelled with biodiesel blends | Engine test rig, emission analyzer |
| **Analytical** | Mathematical modeling using first-principles equations | Parametric analysis of a Brayton cycle with regeneration | MATLAB, EES, or hand calculation |
| **CFD-Based** | Numerical simulation of fluid flow and heat transfer using computational methods | CFD analysis of mixed convection in a lid-driven cavity | ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse "analytical" with "CFD". Remember: **Analytical = closed-form math equations** (e.g., Rankine cycle efficiency formula), while **CFD = numerical discretization** (e.g., solving Navier-Stokes on a mesh).

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]

**(a)** Describe the **PQRST method** for selecting a Thermal Engineering project topic. List any **five sub-domains** in Thermal Engineering and suggest **one innovative project topic** from each, clearly stating the **methodology** and **measurable outcomes**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For the project *"Performance and emission analysis of a CI engine fuelled with waste cooking oil biodiesel blends (B20, B40, B100)"*, describe the **complete experimental methodology** including the test rig setup, instruments used, test procedure, data to be recorded, and **three sample calculations** (BP, BSFC, $\eta_{bth}$). **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks**

**PQRST Method (3 marks):**

The **PQRST method** is a structured decision-making algorithm to systematically select an academically sound and practically feasible project topic:

| Step | Full Form | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **P** | Problem Identification | A clear, well-defined thermal engineering problem must be identified. The problem should be grounded in real-world applications (e.g., emission reduction, energy efficiency). |
| **Q** | Question Formulation | The problem should be rephrased as a specific, answerable research question with measurable variables (e.g., "How does B40 biodiesel affect $\eta_{bth}$ and NOx compared to diesel?"). |
| **R** | Resource Audit | An honest check of available resources — test rigs, software (ANSYS, MATLAB), budget, faculty expertise — must be performed. |
| **S** | Scope Definition | The scope should be tightened to ensure completion in 16 weeks (one semester), avoiding both under-scoping (trivial) and over-scoping (incomplete). |
| **T** | Timeline Realism | A realistic week-wise Gantt chart must be drafted, with milestones for literature review, setup, data collection, analysis, and report writing. |

**Five Sub-Domains with Suggested Topics (4 marks — 0.8 marks each):**

| # | Sub-Domain | Suggested Topic | Methodology | Measurable Outcomes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **IC Engines** | Performance analysis of a CI engine using **waste cooking oil biodiesel** blends (B20, B40) | Experimental | $\eta_{bth}$, BSFC, CO, NOx, HC, Smoke |
| 2 | **Heat Exchangers** | CFD analysis of **shell-and-tube heat exchanger** with twisted tape inserts | CFD (ANSYS Fluent) | Effectiveness $\varepsilon$, $Nu$, $f$, TPF |
| 3 | **Refrigeration** | Performance comparison of **R134a, R1234yf, and R290** in a VCR system | Experimental | COP, RE, Compressor work, EER |
| 4 | **Solar Thermal** | Performance of a **solar water heater** with PCM-based thermal storage | Experimental | $\eta_c$, storage efficiency, time lag |
| 5 | **Waste Heat Recovery** | Design and analysis of an **Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC)** for low-temperature waste heat recovery | Analytical + Design | $\eta_{th}$, Net work output, turbine inlet pressure |

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Test Rig Setup (2 marks):**

The experimental setup consists of a **Kirloskar TV1 (or equivalent) single-cylinder, 4-stroke, water-cooled, constant-speed CI engine** test rig with the following subsystems:

1. **Engine:** 5.2 kW rated power, 1500 rpm rated speed, compression ratio ~17.5:1, direct injection.
2. **Dynamometer:** Eddy current dynamometer for loading (0–100%).
3. **Fuel measurement:** Burette with stopwatch for volumetric fuel consumption measurement.
4. **Air measurement:** Air box with U-tube manometer for air-flow rate measurement.
5. **Cooling:** Heat exchanger-based water cooling tower.
6. **Emission measurement:** 5-gas exhaust analyzer (CO, CO₂, HC, O₂, NOx) and smoke opacity meter.
7. **Temperature measurement:** K-type thermocouple for EGT.
8. **Data acquisition:** Manual logging + optional DAQ system.

**Test Procedure (2 marks):**

1. **Fuel preparation:** Transesterify WCO to biodiesel using methanol + NaOH catalyst. Prepare blends B20 (20% bio + 80% diesel) and B40 (40% bio + 60% diesel) by volume.
2. **Property testing:** Measure density, kinematic viscosity, flash point, and calorific value of all blends using standard ASTM methods.
3. **Engine warm-up:** Run engine on B0 (pure diesel) at no-load for **15 minutes**.
4. **Baseline test:** Apply loads 0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100% using the dynamometer. At each load, wait **3 minutes** for steady state. Record all parameters.
5. **Cool-down:** Run at no-load for 5 minutes.
6. **Switch fuel** to B20, repeat load sweep. Repeat for B40.
7. **Repeat each load-fuel combination three times** for repeatability and statistical analysis.

**Data to be Recorded (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Instrument | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Engine speed | Tachometer | rpm |
| Load | Load cell / spring balance | kg |
| Fuel consumption time | Stopwatch + burette | s for 10 mL |
| Exhaust Gas Temperature | K-type thermocouple | °C |
| CO, HC, NOx | 5-gas analyzer | % / ppm |
| Smoke opacity | Smoke meter | % |
| Air flow rate | Air box + manometer | kg/s |

**Three Sample Calculations (2 marks):**

*Given:* Load $L$ = 10 kg, arm length $r$ = 0.197 m, speed $N$ = 1500 rpm, fuel time $t_f$ = 25 s for 10 mL, $\rho_f$ = 870 kg/m³ (for B40), $CV$ = 41 MJ/kg.

**Sample 1: Brake Power (BP)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= L \times g \times r = 10 \times 9.81 \times 0.197 = 19.33 \ \text{N·m} \\
BP &= \frac{2\pi N T}{60,000} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500 \times 19.33}{60,000} = 3.04 \ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sample 2: BSFC**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_f &= \frac{10 \ \text{cm}^3}{25 \ \text{s}} = 0.4 \ \text{cm}^3/\text{s} \\
\dot{m}_f &= 0.4 \times 10^{-6} \times 870 = 3.48 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{kg/s} = 1.253 \ \text{kg/h} \\
\text{BSFC} &= \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} = \frac{1.253}{3.04} = 0.412 \ \text{kg/kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sample 3: Brake Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{bth}$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{bth} &= \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \times CV} = \frac{3.04}{1.253 \times 41} = \frac{3.04}{51.37} = 0.0592 = 5.92\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** What are the key **performance parameters** typically measured in a **Heat Exchanger project**? Derive the expressions for **LMTD**, **NTU**, and **Effectiveness ($\varepsilon$)** for a parallel-flow heat exchanger. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Suggest a project topic involving **CFD analysis of a heat exchanger** with **nanofluid** as the working fluid. Outline the complete **CFD methodology** including geometry, meshing, boundary conditions, turbulence model, and post-processing parameters. Justify why the chosen turbulence model is appropriate. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Key Performance Parameters (2 marks):**

In a heat exchanger project, the following parameters are typically measured or computed:

1. **Heat duty ($Q$):** Rate of heat transfer from hot to cold fluid, in W or kW.
2. **LMTD:** Log Mean Temperature Difference, in °C or K.
3. **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient ($U$):** in W/m²K.
4. **Effectiveness ($\varepsilon$):** Ratio of actual to maximum possible heat transfer (0 to 1).
5. **NTU:** Number of Transfer Units = $UA/C_{min}$, dimensionless.
6. **Pressure drop ($\Delta P$):** in Pa or kPa, across hot and cold sides.
7. **Nusselt number ($Nu$):** Convective heat transfer intensity.
8. **Friction factor ($f$):** Flow resistance indicator.
9. **Thermal Performance Factor (TPF):** Combined metric = $(Nu/Nu_0)/(f/f_0)^{1/3}$.

**Derivation of LMTD (2 marks):**

For a parallel-flow heat exchanger with hot fluid entering at $T_{h,i}$ and leaving at $T_{h,o}$, and cold fluid entering at $T_{c,i}$ and leaving at $T_{c,o}$:

Let $x$ be the distance from inlet. At distance $x$:

$$
\Delta T(x) = T_h(x) - T_c(x)
$$

Differential heat transfer over $dx$:

$$
dQ = U (\Delta T) \, dA
$$

From energy balance on hot fluid:

$$
dQ = -\dot{m}_h c_{p,h} \, dT_h = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} \, dT_c
$$

The temperature difference gradient:

$$
\frac{d(\Delta T)}{dx} = \frac{dT_h}{dx} - \frac{dT_c}{dx} = -\frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} - \frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}
$$

$$
\frac{d(\Delta T)}{dx} = -U(\Delta T) \left[\frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}\right]
$$

Separating and integrating from $x = 0$ to $L$ (corresponding to $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}$ to $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,o}$):

$$
\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left[\frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}\right] \int_0^L dA
$$

$$
\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -UA \left[\frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}\right]
$$

From energy balance:

$$
Q = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h} (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}} = \frac{T_{h,i} - T_{h,o} - (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})}{Q} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{Q}
$$

Substituting and rearranging:

$$
Q = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} \cdot UA = \text{LMTD} \cdot UA
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}}
$$

where $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,o}$.

**NTU and Effectiveness (3 marks):**

**NTU** is defined as:

$$
\text{NTU} = \frac{UA}{C_{min}}
$$

where $C_{min} = \min(\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}, \dot{m}_c c_{p,c})$ is the smaller heat capacity rate.

The **heat capacity ratio** is:

$$
C_r = \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}}
$$

For a **parallel-flow heat exchanger**, the effectiveness is:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}(1 + C_r)]}{1 + C_r}
$$

The **maximum possible heat transfer** is:

$$
Q_{max} = C_{min} (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})
$$

The **actual heat transfer** is:

$$
Q_{actual} = \varepsilon \cdot Q_{max} = \varepsilon \cdot C_{min} (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})
$$

Combining with $Q = U A \cdot \text{LMTD}$:

$$
\text{LMTD} = \frac{\varepsilon C_{min} (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})}{UA}
$$

This **NTU-$\varepsilon$** approach is preferred when outlet temperatures are unknown, while **LMTD** approach is used when both inlet and outlet temperatures are known.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Suggested CFD Project Topic (1 mark):**

> *"CFD analysis of heat transfer and pressure drop characteristics in a **double-pipe counter-flow heat exchanger** using **Al₂O₃-water nanofluid** (1–4 vol.%) as the working fluid, with **varying Reynolds number (Re = 4000–20,000)**."*

**Complete CFD Methodology (5 marks):**

| Step | Activity | Details |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Geometry** | Create 3D double-pipe model in **ANSYS DesignModeler** or **SolidWorks** | Inner pipe: $D_i$ = 20 mm; Outer pipe: $D_o$ = 40 mm; Length $L$ = 1.5 m; Nanofluid flows in inner pipe |
| 2. **Meshing** | Generate mesh in **ANSYS Meshing** | Element type: hexahedral (preferred) or tetrahedral with inflation layers near walls (10 layers, growth ratio 1.2, first cell height for $y^+ \approx 1$). Mesh independence study: 3 mesh densities (e.g., 0.5M, 1M, 2M cells) |
| 3. **Materials** | Define Al₂O₃-water nanofluid properties as function of volume fraction $\phi$ | Effective density: $\rho_{nf} = (1-\phi)\rho_w + \phi \rho_{p}$; Effective $c_p$, $k$, $\mu$ using Hamilton-Crosser or Corcione correlations |
| 4. **Solver Setup** | ANSYS Fluent | Pressure-based, steady-state, **k-ω SST** turbulence model (preferred for near-wall accuracy), energy equation ON |
| 5. **Boundary Conditions** | Hot fluid (water) inlet: **velocity-inlet** with Re-dependent velocity, T = 60°C; Outlet: **pressure-outlet**, gauge P = 0 Pa; Cold wall (outer pipe): **coupled** or fixed T = 30°C | Validate velocity range corresponds to Re = 4000–20,000 |
| 6. **Solution Method** | SIMPLE algorithm, second-order upwind for momentum and energy | Convergence: residuals < $10^{-6}$ for energy, < $10^{-4}$ for continuity and momentum |
| 7. **Post-Processing** | Extract: $Nu$, $f$, $\Delta P$ | Compute average $Nu = h D / k$, friction factor $f = \Delta P \cdot D / (2 \rho L V^2)$ |

**Justification of Turbulence Model (1 mark):**

The **k-ω SST (Shear Stress Transport)** model is chosen because:
1. It **blends the k-ω model near walls** (excellent for low $y^+$ boundary layer resolution) with the **k-ε model in the free stream** (robustness in bulk flow).
2. It is **superior to standard k-ε** for heat transfer predictions in confined flows with adverse pressure gradients.
3. It has been **extensively validated** in published nanofluid heat exchanger studies (e.g., Duangthongsuk & Wongwises, 2010), making the results defensible.
4. Alternative models (Standard k-ε, Realizable k-ε, RNG k-ε) are weaker for near-wall thermal boundary layer accuracy.

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in Project-Related Questions:**
>
> 1. **Vagueness in Problem Statement:** Stating *"to study engine performance"* is too vague. Always specify fuel, blend ratio, load range, engine type, and parameters.
> 2. **Missing SDG Mapping:** KTU 2024 examiners often check for SDG alignment. Always mention relevant UN-SDG(s) explicitly.
> 3. **No Methodology Justification:** Students describe *what* they will do but not *why* they chose that particular method. Always justify the choice (e.g., why k-ω SST over k-ε).
> 4. **No Validation Plan:** For CFD projects, students forget to include **experimental validation** or **comparison with published data**. This is a major deduction point.
> 5. **Skipping Literature Review:** Even in a 14-mark exam, listing 3–4 key references adds credibility and saves marks.
> 6. **Units and Significant Figures:** Always use SI units and consistent significant figures. Mixing cal/s and W, °C and K without conversion costs marks.
> 7. **Ignoring Societal Impact:** A project is not complete without a brief statement on how it benefits society/industry (SDG 7, 12, 13 alignment).

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute Exam Prep):**

- [x] **Definition:** A Thermal Engineering project is a **bounded, feasible, measurable, semester-long investigation** applying thermodynamic/heat-transfer/fluid principles to a defined system.

- [x] **Five Categories of Projects:** Experimental, Analytical, CFD, Design-Fabricate, Review, Hybrid.

- [x] **PQRST Selection Method:** Problem, Question, Resource, Scope, Timeline.

- [x] **Eight Major Sub-Domains for Topic Selection:** IC Engines, Heat Exchangers, Refrigeration/HVAC, Solar Thermal, Power Cycles, Thermal Energy Storage, CFD, Waste Heat Recovery.

- [x] **UN-SDG Mapping:** Always link your topic to at least one SDG (preferably 7, 9, 11, 12, or 13).

- [x] **SMART Problem Statement:** Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound.

- [x] **Sample Calculation Must-Haves:** BP = $2\pi NT/60{,}000$, BSFC, $\eta_{bth}$ (engine) or $\varepsilon$, LMTD, NTU (heat exchanger) or COP (refrigeration).

- [x] **Three Replication Rule:** Repeat each experiment 3 times and report mean ± standard deviation.

- [x] **Mandatory Plots:** For IC engines — $\eta_{bth}$, BSFC, EGT, emissions vs. load. For heat exchangers — $\varepsilon$, $Nu$, $f$ vs. Re or $\phi$.

- [x] **Resource Audit Before Committing:** Verify test rig availability, software license, budget, and guide expertise BEFORE finalizing the topic.

- [x] **Risk Mitigation:** Have a backup plan (e.g., if engine fails, switch to pure CFD with experimental validation from literature).

- [x] **Examiner's Bias:** Hybrid projects (Experiment + Simulation) and Design-Fabricate-Test projects score highest.

- [x] **Report Structure:** Title → Certificate → Abstract → TOC → Introduction → Literature Review → Methodology → Results & Discussion → Conclusions → References → Appendices.

- [x] **Viva Focus Areas:** Fundamental theory behind every formula, sources of error, SDG relevance, future scope, limitations.

- [x] **Plagiarism Threshold:** KTU 2024 mandates < 20% similarity (excluding references and standard definitions).
